PREFACE to 1850 EDITION
I do not find it easy to get sufficiently far away from this Book,
in the first sensation of have finish it, to refer to it with
the composure which this formal heading would seem to require. my
interest in it, be so recent and strong; and my mind be so divide
between pleasure and regret - pleasure in the achievement of a long
design, regret in the separation from many companion - that I be
in danger of weary the reader whom I love, with personal
confidence, and private emotion.
besides which, all that I could say of the Story, to any purpose,
I have endeavour to say in it.
it would concern the reader little, perhaps, to know, how
sorrowfully the pen be lay down at the close of a two-year'
imaginative task; or how an author feel as if he be dismiss
some portion of himself into the shadowy world, when a crowd of the
creature of his brain be go from he for ever. yet, I have
nothing else to tell; unless, indeed, I be to confess (which
might be of less moment still) that no one can ever believe this
Narrative, in the reading, more than I have believe it in the
writing.
instead of look back, therefore, I will look forward. I cannot
close this volume more agreeably to myself, than with a hopeful
glance towards the time when I shall again put forth my two green
leaf once a month, and with a faithful remembrance of the genial
sun and shower that have fall on these leaf of David
Copperfield, and make I happy.
London, October, 1850.
PREFACE to
the CHARLES DICKENS EDITION
I remarked in the original Preface to this Book, that I do not
find it easy to get sufficiently far away from it, in the first
sensation of have finish it, to refer to it with the composure
which this formal heading would seem to require. my interest in it
be so recent and strong, and my mind be so divide between
pleasure and regret - pleasure in the achievement of a long design,
regret in the separation from many companion - that I be in
danger of weary the reader with personal confidence and private
emotion.
besides which, all that I could have say of the Story to any
purpose, I have endeavour to say in it.
it would concern the reader little, perhaps, to know how
sorrowfully the pen be lay down at the close of a two-year'
imaginative task; or how an author feel as if he be dismiss
some portion of himself into the shadowy world, when a crowd of the
creature of his brain be go from he for ever. yet, I have
nothing else to tell; unless, indeed, I be to confess (which
might be of less moment still), that no one can ever believe this
Narrative, in the reading, more than I believe it in the writing.
so true be these avowal at the present day, that I can now only
take the reader into one confidence more. of all my book, I like
this the good. it will be easily believe that I be a fond parent
to every child of my fancy, and that no one can ever love that
family as dearly as I love they. but, like many fond parent, I
have in my heart of heart a favourite child. and his name be
DAVID COPPERFIELD.
1869
the personal history and
experience of
DAVID COPPERFIELD the YOUNGER
CHAPTER 1
I be bear
whether I shall turn out to be the hero of my own life, or whether
that station will be hold by anybody else, these page must show.
to begin my life with the beginning of my life, I record that I be
bear (as I have be inform and believe) on a Friday, at twelve
o'clock at night. it be remark that the clock begin to strike,
and I begin to cry, simultaneously.
in consideration of the day and hour of my birth, it be declare
by the nurse, and by some sage woman in the neighbourhood who have
take a lively interest in I several month before there be any
possibility of our become personally acquaint, first, that I
be destine to be unlucky in life; and secondly, that I be
privileged to see ghost and spirit; both these gift inevitably
attach, as they believe, to all unlucky infant of either
gender, bear towards the small hour on a Friday night.
I need say nothing here, on the first head, because nothing can
show well than my history whether that prediction be verify or
falsify by the result. on the second branch of the question, I
will only remark, that unless I run through that part of my
inheritance while I be still a baby, I have not come into it yet.
but I do not at all complain of have be keep out of this
property; and if anybody else should be in the present enjoyment of
it, he be heartily welcome to keep it.
I be bear with a caul, which be advertise for sale, in the
newspaper, at the low price of fifteen guineas. whether sea-go
people be short of money about that time, or be short of faith
and preferred cork jacket, I donot know; all I know be, that there
be but one solitary bidding, and that be from an attorney
connect with the bill-broke business, who offer two pound in
cash, and the balance in sherry, but decline to be guarantee from
drown on any high bargain. consequently the advertisement be
withdraw at a dead loss - for as to sherry, my poor dear mother's
own sherry be in the market then - and ten year afterwards, the
caul be put up in a raffle down in our part of the country, to
fifty member at half-a-crown a head, the winner to spend five
shilling. I be present myself, and I remember to have feel quite
uncomfortable and confused, at a part of myself be dispose of
in that way. the caul be win, I recollect, by an old lady with a
hand-basket, who, very reluctantly, produce from it the stipulate
five shilling, all in halfpence, and twopence halfpenny short - as
it take an immense time and a great waste of arithmetic, to
endeavour without any effect to prove to she. it be a fact which
will be long remember as remarkable down there, that she be
never drown, but die triumphantly in bed, at ninety-two. I have
understand that it be, to the last, her proud boast, that she
never have be on the water in her life, except upon a bridge; and
that over her tea (to which she be extremely partial) she, to the
last, express her indignation at the impiety of mariner and
other, who have the presumption to go 'meandering' about the world.
it be in vain to represent to she that some convenience, tea
perhaps include, result from this objectionable practice. she
always return, with great emphasis and with an instinctive
knowledge of the strength of her objection, 'let we have no
meandering.'
not to meander myself, at present, I will go back to my birth.
I be bear at Blunderstone, in Suffolk, or 'there by', as they say
in Scotland. I be a posthumous child. my father's eye have
close upon the light of this world six month, when mine open on
it. there be something strange to I, even now, in the reflection
that he never see I; and something strange yet in the shadowy
remembrance that I have of my first childish association with his
white grave-stone in the churchyard, and of the indefinable
compassion I use to feel for it lie out alone there in the dark
night, when our little parlour be warm and bright with fire and
candle, and the door of our house be - almost cruelly, it seem
to I sometimes - bolt and lock against it.
an aunt of my father's, and consequently a great-aunt of mine, of
whom I shall have more to relate by and by, be the principal
magnate of our family. Miss Trotwood, or Miss Betsey, as my poor
mother always call she, when she sufficiently overcome her dread
of this formidable personage to mention she at all (which be
seldom), have be marry to a husband young than herself, who
be very handsome, except in the sense of the homely adage,
'handsome be, that handsome do' - for he be strongly suspect
of have beat Miss Betsey, and even of have once, on a
dispute question of supply, make some hasty but determined
arrangement to throw she out of a two pair of stair' window.
these evidence of an incompatibility of temper induce Miss Betsey
to pay he off, and effect a separation by mutual consent. he go
to India with his capital, and there, accord to a wild legend in
our family, he be once see rid on an elephant, in company with
a Baboon; but I think it must have be a Baboo - or a Begum.
anyhow, from India tiding of his death reach home, within ten
year. how they affect my aunt, nobody know; for immediately
upon the separation, she take her maiden name again, buy a
cottage in a hamlet on the sea-coast a long way off, establish
herself there as a single woman with one servant, and be
understand to live seclude, ever afterwards, in an inflexible
retirement.
my father have once be a favourite of her, I believe; but she be
mortally affront by his marriage, on the ground that my mother
be 'a wax doll'. she have never see my mother, but she know she
to be not yet twenty. my father and Miss Betsey never meet again.
he be double my mother's age when he marry, and of but a
delicate constitution. he die a year afterwards, and, as I have
say, six month before I come into the world.
this be the state of matter, on the afternoon of, what I may be
excuse for call, that eventful and important Friday. I can
make no claim therefore to have know, at that time, how matter
stand; or to have any remembrance, found on the evidence of my
own sens, of what follow.
my mother be sit by the fire, but poorly in health, and very
low in spirit, look at it through her tear, and despond
heavily about herself and the fatherless little stranger, who be
already welcome by some gross of prophetic pin, in a drawer
upstairs, to a world not at all excited on the subject of his
arrival; my mother, I say, be sit by the fire, that bright,
windy March afternoon, very timid and sad, and very doubtful of
ever come alive out of the trial that be before she, when,
lift her eye as she dry they, to the window opposite, she see
a strange lady come up the garden.
MY mother have a sure foreboding at the second glance, that it be
Miss Betsey. the set sun be glow on the strange lady, over
the garden-fence, and she come walk up to the door with a fell
rigidity of figure and composure of countenance that could have
belong to nobody else.
when she reach the house, she give another proof of her identity.
my father have often hint that she seldom conduct herself like
any ordinary Christian; and now, instead of ring the bell, she
come and look in at that identical window, press the end of
her nose against the glass to that extent, that my poor dear mother
use to say it become perfectly flat and white in a moment.
she give my mother such a turn, that I have always be convince
I be indebted to Miss Betsey for have be bear on a Friday.
my mother have leave her chair in her agitation, and go behind it
in the corner. Miss Betsey, look round the room, slowly and
inquiringly, begin on the other side, and carry her eye on, like
a saracen's Head in a dutch clock, until they reach my mother.
then she make a frown and a gesture to my mother, like one who be
accustomed to be obey, to come and open the door. my mother
go.
'Mrs. David Copperfield, I think,' say Miss Betsey; the emphasis
referring, perhaps, to my mother's mourning weed, and her
condition.
'yes,' say my mother, faintly.
'Miss Trotwood,' say the visitor. 'you have hear of she, I dare
say?'
my mother answer she have have that pleasure. and she have a
disagreeable consciousness of not appear to imply that it have
be an overpowering pleasure.
'now you see she,' say Miss Betsey. my mother bend her head, and
beg she to walk in.
they go into the parlour my mother have come from, the fire in the
good room on the other side of the passage not be light - not
have be light, indeed, since my father's funeral; and when
they be both seat, and Miss Betsey say nothing, my mother,
after vainly try to restrain herself, begin to cry.
'oh tut, tut, tut!' say Miss Betsey, in a hurry. 'donot do that!
come, come!'
my mother couldnot help it notwithstanding, so she cry until she
have have her cry out.
'take off your cap, child,' say Miss Betsey, 'and let I see you.'
my mother be too much afraid of she to refuse compliance with this
odd request, if she have any disposition to do so. therefore she
do as she be tell, and do it with such nervous hand that her
hair (which be luxuriant and beautiful) fall all about her face.
'why, bless my heart!' exclaim Miss Betsey. 'you be a very
Baby!'
my mother be, no doubt, unusually youthful in appearance even for
her year; she hang her head, as if it be her fault, poor thing,
and say, sob, that indeed she be afraid she be but a
childish widow, and would be but a childish mother if she live.
in a short pause which ensue, she have a fancy that she feel Miss
Betsey touch her hair, and that with no ungentle hand; but, look
at she, in her timid hope, she find that lady sit with the
skirt of her dress tuck up, her hand fold on one knee, and her
foot upon the fender, frown at the fire.
'in the name of Heaven,' say Miss Betsey, suddenly, 'why Rookery?'
'do you mean the house, ma'am?' ask my mother.
'why rookery?' say Miss Betsey. 'cookery would have be more to
the purpose, if you have have any practical idea of life, either of
you.'
'the name be Mr. Copperfield's choice,' return my mother. 'when
he buy the house, he like to think that there be rook about
it.'
the evening wind make such a disturbance just now, among some tall
old elm-tree at the bottom of the garden, that neither my mother
nor Miss Betsey could forbear glance that way. as the elm bend
to one another, like giant who be whispering secret, and after
a few second of such repose, fall into a violent flurry, toss
their wild arm about, as if their late confidence be really too
wicked for their peace of mind, some weatherbeaten ragged old
rooks'-nest, burden their high branch, swing like wreck
upon a stormy sea.
'where be the bird?' ask Miss Betsey.
'the -?' my mother have be think of something else.
'the rook - what have become of they?' ask Miss Betsey.
'there have not be any since we have live here,' say my mother.
'we think - Mr. Copperfield think - it be quite a large
rookery; but the nest be very old one, and the bird have
desert they a long while.'
'David Copperfield all over!' cry Miss Betsey. 'David
Copperfield from head to foot! call a house a rookery when
therebe not a rook near it, and take the bird on trust, because
he see the nest!'
'Mr. Copperfield,' return my mother, 'be dead, and if you dare to
speak unkindly of he to I -'
my poor dear mother, I suppose, have some momentary intention of
commit an assault and battery upon my aunt, who could easily
have settle she with one hand, even if my mother have be in far
well training for such an encounter than she be that evening.
but it pass with the action of rise from her chair; and she sit
down again very meekly, and faint.
when she come to herself, or when Miss Betsey have restore she,
whichever it be, she find the latter standing at the window. the
twilight be by this time shade down into darkness; and dimly as
they see each other, they could not have do that without the aid
of the fire.
'well?' say Miss Betsey, come back to her chair, as if she have
only be take a casual look at the prospect; 'and when do you
expect -'
'I be all in a tremble,' falter my mother. 'I donot know whatbe
the matter. I shall die, I be sure!'
'no, no, no,' say Miss Betsey. 'have some tea.'
'oh dear I, dear I, do you think it will do I any good?' cry
my mother in a helpless manner.
'of course it will,' say Miss Betsey. 'itbe nothing but fancy.
what do you call your girl?'
'I donot know that it will be a girl, yet, ma'am,' say my mother
innocently.
'bless the Baby!' exclaim Miss Betsey, unconsciously quote the
second sentiment of the pincushion in the drawer upstairs, but
apply it to my mother instead of I, 'I donot mean that. I mean
your servant-girl.'
'Peggotty,' say my mother.
'Peggotty!' repeat Miss Betsey, with some indignation. 'do you
mean to say, child, that any human being have go into a Christian
church, and get herself name Peggotty?'
'itbe her surname,' say my mother, faintly. 'Mr. Copperfield
call she by it, because her christian name be the same as mine.'
'here! Peggotty!' cry Miss Betsey, open the parlour door.
'Tea. your mistress be a little unwell. donot dawdle.'
have issue this mandate with as much potentiality as if she have
be a recognize authority in the house ever since it have be a
house, and have look out to confront the amazed Peggotty come
along the passage with a candle at the sound of a strange voice,
Miss Betsey shut the door again, and sit down as before: with her
foot on the fender, the skirt of her dress tuck up, and her hand
fold on one knee.
'you be speak about its be a girl,' say Miss Betsey. 'I
have no doubt it will be a girl. I have a presentiment that it
must be a girl. now child, from the moment of the birth of this
girl -'
'perhaps boy,' my mother take the liberty of put in.
'I tell you I have a presentiment that it must be a girl,' return
Miss Betsey. 'donot contradict. from the moment of this girl's
birth, child, I intend to be her friend. I intend to be her
godmother, and I beg youwill call her Betsey Trotwood Copperfield.
there must be no mistake in life with this Betsey Trotwood. there
must be no trifling with her affection, poor dear. she must be
well bring up, and well guard from repose any foolish
confidence where they be not deserve. I must make that MY
care.'
there be a twitch of Miss Betsey's head, after each of these
sentence, as if her own old wrong be work within she, and
she repress any plainer reference to they by strong constraint.
so my mother suspect, at least, as she observe she by the low
glimmer of the fire: too much scared by Miss Betsey, too uneasy in
herself, and too subdue and bewildered altogether, to observe
anything very clearly, or to know what to say.
'and be David good to you, child?' ask Miss Betsey, when she have
be silent for a little while, and these motion of her head have
gradually cease. 'be you comfortable together?'
'we be very happy,' say my mother. 'Mr. Copperfield be only
too good to I.'
'what, he spoil you, I suppose?' return Miss Betsey.
'for be quite alone and dependent on myself in this rough world
again, yes, I fear he do indeed,' sob my mother.
'well! donot cry!' say Miss Betsey. 'you be not equally
match, child - if any two people can be equally match - and so
I ask the question. you be an orphan, benot you?'
'yes.'
'and a governess?'
'I be nursery-governess in a family where Mr. Copperfield come to
visit. Mr. Copperfield be very kind to I, and take a great deal
of notice of I, and pay I a good deal of attention, and at last
propose to I. and I accept he. and so we be marry,' say
my mother simply.
'ha! Poor Baby!' muse Miss Betsey, with her frown still bent upon
the fire. 'do you know anything?'
'I beg your pardon, ma'am,' falter my mother.
'about keep house, for instance,' say Miss Betsey.
'not much, I fear,' return my mother. 'not so much as I could
wish. but Mr. Copperfield be teach I -'
('much he know about it himself!') say Miss Betsey in a
parenthesis.
- 'and I hope I should have improve, be very anxious to learn,
and he very patient to teach I, if the great misfortune of his
death' - my mother break down again here, and could get no far.
'well, well!' say Miss Betsey.
-'I keep my housekeeping-book regularly, and balance it with Mr.
Copperfield every night,' cry my mother in another burst of
distress, and break down again.
'well, well!' say Miss Betsey. 'donot cry any more.'
- 'and I be sure we never have a word of difference respect it,
except when Mr. Copperfield object to my three and five be
too much like each other, or to my put curly tail to my seven
and nine,' resume my mother in another burst, and break down
again.
'youwill make yourself ill,' say Miss Betsey, 'and you know that
will not be good either for you or for my god-daughter. come! you
mustnot do it!'
this argument have some share in quiet my mother, though she
increase indisposition have a large one. there be an interval
of silence, only break by Miss Betsey's occasionally ejaculate
'ha!' as she sit with her foot upon the fender.
'David have buy an annuity for himself with his money, I know,'
say she, by and by. 'what do he do for you?'
'Mr. Copperfield,' say my mother, answer with some difficulty,
'be so considerate and good as to secure the reversion of a part
of it to I.'
'how much?' ask Miss Betsey.
'a hundred and five pound a year,' say my mother.
'he might have do bad,' say my aunt.
the word be appropriate to the moment. my mother be so much
bad that Peggotty, come in with the teaboard and candle, and
see at a glance how ill she be, - as Miss Betsey might have
do soon if there have be light enough, - convey her upstairs
to her own room with all speed; and immediately dispatch Ham
Peggotty, her nephew, who have be for some day past secrete in
the house, unknown to my mother, as a special messenger in case of
emergency, to fetch the nurse and doctor.
those allied power be considerably astonish, when they arrive
within a few minute of each other, to find an unknown lady of
portentous appearance, sit before the fire, with her bonnet
tie over her left arm, stop her ear with jeweller' cotton.
Peggotty know nothing about she, and my mother say nothing
about she, she be quite a mystery in the parlour; and the fact of
she have a magazine of jeweller' cotton in her pocket, and
stick the article in her ear in that way, do not detract from
the solemnity of her presence.
the doctor have be upstairs and come down again, and have
satisfied himself, I suppose, that there be a probability of this
unknown lady and himself have to sit there, face to face, for
some hour, lay himself out to be polite and social. he be the
meek of his sex, the mild of little man. he sidle in and
out of a room, to take up the less space. he walk as softly as
the Ghost in Hamlet, and more slowly. he carry his head on one
side, partly in modest depreciation of himself, partly in modest
propitiation of everybody else. it be nothing to say that he
havenot a word to throw at a dog. he couldnot have throw a word at
a mad dog. he might have offer he one gently, or half a one, or
a fragment of one; for he speak as slowly as he walk; but he
wouldnot have be rude to he, and he couldnot have be quick
with he, for any earthly consideration.
Mr. Chillip, look mildly at my aunt with his head on one side,
and make she a little bow, say, in allusion to the jeweller'
cotton, as he softly touch his left ear:
'some local irritation, ma'am?'
'what!' reply my aunt, pull the cotton out of one ear like a
cork.
Mr. Chillip be so alarmed by her abruptness - as he tell my mother
afterwards - that it be a mercy he donot lose his presence of
mind. but he repeat sweetly:
'some local irritation, ma'am?'
'nonsense!' reply my aunt, and cork herself again, at one blow.
Mr. Chillip could do nothing after this, but sit and look at she
feebly, as she sit and look at the fire, until he be call
upstairs again. after some quarter of an hour's absence, he
return.
'well?' say my aunt, take the cotton out of the ear near to
he.
'well, ma'am,' return Mr. Chillip, 'we are- we be progress
slowly, ma'am.'
'Ba--a--ah!' say my aunt, with a perfect shake on the contemptuous
interjection. and cork herself as before.
really - really - as Mr. Chillip tell my mother, he be almost
shock; speak in a professional point of view alone, he be
almost shocked. but he sit and look at she, notwithstanding, for
nearly two hour, as she sit look at the fire, until he be
again call out. after another absence, he again return.
'well?' say my aunt, take out the cotton on that side again.
'well, ma'am,' return Mr. Chillip, 'we be - we be progress
slowly, ma'am.'
'ya--a--ah!' say my aunt. with such a snarl at he, that Mr.
Chillip absolutely could not bear it. it be really calculate to
break his spirit, he say afterwards. he prefer to go and sit
upon the stair, in the dark and a strong draught, until he be
again send for.
Ham Peggotty, who go to the national school, and be a very
dragon at his catechism, and who may therefore be regard as a
credible witness, report next day, that happen to peep in at
the parlour-door an hour after this, he be instantly descry by
Miss Betsey, then walk to and fro in a state of agitation, and
pounce upon before he could make his escape. that there be now
occasional sound of foot and voice overhead which he infer the
cotton do not exclude, from the circumstance of his evidently
be clutch by the lady as a victim on whom to expend her
superabundant agitation when the sound be loud. that,
march he constantly up and down by the collar (as if he have
be take too much laudanum), she, at those time, shake he,
rumple his hair, make light of his linen, stop his ear as if
she confound they with her own, and otherwise tousled and
maltreat he. this be in part confirm by his aunt, who see
he at half past twelve o'clock, soon after his release, and
affirm that he be then as red as I be.
the mild Mr. Chillip could not possibly bear malice at such a time,
if at any time. he sidle into the parlour as soon as he be at
liberty, and say to my aunt in his meek manner:
'well, ma'am, I be happy to congratulate you.'
'what upon?' say my aunt, sharply.
Mr. Chillip be flutter again, by the extreme severity of my
aunt's manner; so he make she a little bow and give she a little
smile, to mollify she.
'mercy on the man, whatbe he do!' cry my aunt, impatiently.
'cannot he speak?'
'be calm, my dear ma'am,' say Mr. Chillip, in his soft accent.
'there be no long any occasion for uneasiness, ma'am. be calm.'
it have since be consider almost a miracle that my aunt donot
shake he, and shake what he have to say, out of he. she only
shake her own head at he, but in a way that make he quail.
'well, ma'am,' resume Mr. Chillip, as soon as he have courage, 'I
be happy to congratulate you. all be now over, ma'am, and well
over.'
during the five minute or so that Mr. Chillip devote to the
delivery of this oration, my aunt eye he narrowly.
'how be she?' say my aunt, fold her arm with her bonnet still
tie on one of they.
'well, ma'am, she will soon be quite comfortable, I hope,' return
Mr. Chillip. 'quite as comfortable as we can expect a young mother
to be, under these melancholy domestic circumstance. there cannot
be any objection to your see she presently, ma'am. it may do
her good.'
'and SHE. how be she?' say my aunt, sharply.
Mr. Chillip lay his head a little more on one side, and look at
my aunt like an amiable bird.
'the baby,' say my aunt. 'how be she?'
'Ma'am,' return Mr. Chillip, 'I apprehend you have know. itbe
a boy.'
my aunt say never a word, but take her bonnet by the string, in
the manner of a sling, aim a blow at Mr. Chillip's head with it,
put it on bent, walk out, and never come back. she vanish like
a discontent fairy; or like one of those supernatural being,
whom it be popularly suppose I be entitle to see; and never
come back any more.
no. I lie in my basket, and my mother lie in her bed; but Betsey
Trotwood Copperfield be for ever in the land of dream and
shadow, the tremendous region whence I have so lately travel;
and the light upon the window of our room shine out upon the
earthly bourne of all such traveller, and the mound above the
ash and the dust that once be he, without whom I have never be.
CHAPTER 2
I OBSERVE
the first object that assume a distinct presence before I, as I
look far back, into the blank of my infancy, be my mother with her
pretty hair and youthful shape, and Peggotty with no shape at all,
and eye so dark that they seem to darken their whole
neighbourhood in her face, and cheek and arm so hard and red that
I wonder the bird donot peck she in preference to apple.
I believe I can remember these two at a little distance apart,
dwarf to my sight by stoop down or kneel on the floor, and
I go unsteadily from the one to the other. I have an impression
on my mind which I cannot distinguish from actual remembrance, of
the touch of Peggotty's forefinger as she use to hold it out to
I, and of its be roughen by needlework, like a pocket
nutmeg-grater.
this may be fancy, though I think the memory of most of we can go
far back into such time than many of we suppose; just as I
believe the power of observation in number of very young child
to be quite wonderful for its closeness and accuracy. indeed, I
think that most grown man who be remarkable in this respect, may
with great propriety be say not to have lose the faculty, than
to have acquire it; the rather, as I generally observe such man to
retain a certain freshness, and gentleness, and capacity of be
please, which be also an inheritance they have preserve from
their childhood.
I might have a misgiving that I be 'meander' in stop to say
this, but that it bring I to remark that I build these
conclusion, in part upon my own experience of myself; and if it
should appear from anything I may set down in this narrative that
I be a child of close observation, or that as a man I have a
strong memory of my childhood, I undoubtedly lie claim to both of
these characteristic.
look back, as I be say, into the blank of my infancy, the
first object I can remember as stand out by themselves from a
confusion of thing, be my mother and Peggotty. what else do I
remember? let I see.
there come out of the cloud, our house - not new to I, but quite
familiar, in its early remembrance. on the ground-floor be
Peggotty's kitchen, open into a back yard; with a pigeon-house
on a pole, in the centre, without any pigeon in it; a great dog-
kennel in a corner, without any dog; and a quantity of fowl that
look terribly tall to I, walk about, in a menacing and
ferocious manner. there be one cock who get upon a post to crow,
and seem to take particular notice of I as I look at he through
the kitchen window, who make I shiver, he be so fierce. of the
geese outside the side-gate who come waddle after I with their
long neck stretch out when I go that way, I dream at night: as
a man environ by wild beast might dream of lion.
here be a long passage - what an enormous perspective I make of it!
- lead from Peggotty's kitchen to the front door. a dark
store-room open out of it, and that be a place to be run past at
night; for I donot know what may be among those tub and jar and
old tea-chest, when there be nobody in there with a dimly-burn
light, let a mouldy air come out of the door, in which there be
the smell of soap, pickle, pepper, candle, and coffee, all at one
whiff. then there be the two parlour: the parlour in which we
sit of an evening, my mother and I and Peggotty - for Peggotty be
quite our companion, when her work be do and we be alone - and
the good parlour where we sit on a Sunday; grandly, but not so
comfortably. there be something of a doleful air about that room
to I, for Peggotty have tell I - I donot know when, but apparently
age ago - about my father's funeral, and the company have their
black cloak put on. one Sunday night my mother read to Peggotty
and I in there, how Lazarus be raise up from the dead. and I be
so frightened that they be afterwards oblige to take I out of
bed, and show I the quiet churchyard out of the bedroom window,
with the dead all lie in their graf at rest, below the solemn
moon.
there be nothing half so green that I know anywhere, as the grass
of that churchyard; nothing half so shady as its tree; nothing
half so quiet as its tombstone. the sheep be feed there, when
I kneel up, early in the morning, in my little bed in a closet
within my mother's room, to look out at it; and I see the red light
shin on the sun-dial, and think within myself, 'be the sun-dial
glad, I wonder, that it can tell the time again?'
here be our pew in the church. what a high-back pew! with a
window near it, out of which our house can be see, and be see
many time during the morning's service, by Peggotty, who like to
make herself as sure as she can that itbe not be rob, or be
not in flame. but though Peggotty's eye wander, she be much
offend if mine do, and frown to I, as I stand upon the seat,
that I be to look at the clergyman. but I cannot always look at he
- I know he without that white thing on, and I be afraid of his
wonder why I stare so, and perhaps stop the service to
inquire - and what be I to do? itbe a dreadful thing to gape, but
I must do something. I look at my mother, but she pretend not to
see I. I look at a boy in the aisle, and he make face at I.
I look at the sunlight come in at the open door through the
porch, and there I see a stray sheep - I donot mean a sinner, but
mutton - half make up his mind to come into the church. I feel
that if I look at he any long, I might be tempt to say
something out loud; and what would become of I then! I look up at
the monumental tablet on the wall, and try to think of Mr. Bodgers
late of this parish, and what the feeling of Mrs. Bodgers must
have be, when affliction sore, long time Mr. Bodgers bore, and
physician be in vain. I wonder whether they call in Mr.
Chillip, and he be in vain; and if so, how he like to be remind
of it once a week. I look from Mr. Chillip, in his Sunday
neckcloth, to the pulpit; and think what a good place it would be
to play in, and what a castle it would make, with another boy
come up the stair to attack it, and have the velvet cushion
with the tassel throw down on his head. in time my eye
gradually shut up; and, from seem to hear the clergyman singing
a drowsy song in the heat, I hear nothing, until I fall off the
seat with a crash, and be take out, more dead than alive, by
Peggotty.
and now I see the outside of our house, with the latticed
bedroom-window standing open to let in the sweet-smell air, and
the ragged old rooks'-nests still dangle in the elm-tree at the
bottom of the front garden. now I be in the garden at the back,
beyond the yard where the empty pigeon-house and dog-kennel be -
a very preserve of butterfly, as I remember it, with a high
fence, and a gate and padlock; where the fruit cluster on the
tree, riper and rich than fruit have ever be since, in any
other garden, and where my mother gather some in a basket, while
I stand by, bolt furtive gooseberry, and try to look
unmoved. a great wind rise, and the summer be go in a moment.
we be play in the winter twilight, dance about the parlour.
when my mother be out of breath and rest herself in an
elbow-chair, I watch she wind her bright curl round her
finger, and straiten her waist, and nobody know well than I
do that she like to look so well, and be proud of be so pretty.
that be among my very early impression. that, and a sense that
we be both a little afraid of Peggotty, and submit ourselves
in most thing to her direction, be among the first opinion - if
they may be so call - that I ever derive from what I see.
Peggotty and I be sit one night by the parlour fire, alone.
I have be read to Peggotty about crocodile. I must have read
very perspicuously, or the poor soul must have be deeply
interested, for I remember she have a cloudy impression, after I have
do, that they be a sort of vegetable. I be tired of reading,
and dead sleepy; but have leave, as a high treat, to sit up until
my mother come home from spend the evening at a neighbour's, I
would rather have die upon my post (of course) than have go to
bed. I have reach that stage of sleepiness when Peggotty seem
to swell and grow immensely large. I prop my eyelid open with
my two forefinger, and look perseveringly at she as she sit at
work; at the little bit of wax-candle she keep for her thread - how
old it look, be so wrinkle in all direction! - at the little
house with a thatched roof, where the yard-measure live; at her
work-box with a slide lid, with a view of St. Paul's Cathedral
(with a pink dome) paint on the top; at the brass thimble on her
finger; at herself, whom I think lovely. I feel so sleepy, that
I know if I lose sight of anything for a moment, I be go.
'Peggotty,' say I, suddenly, 'be you ever married?'
'Lord, Master Davy,' reply Peggotty. 'whatbe put marriage in
your head?'
she answer with such a start, that it quite awake I. and then
she stop in her work, and look at I, with her needle draw
out to its thread's length.
'but be you ever married, Peggotty?' say I. 'you be a very
handsome woman, an't you?'
I think she in a different style from my mother, certainly; but
of another school of beauty, I consider she a perfect example.
there be a red velvet footstool in the good parlour, on which my
mother have paint a nosegay. the ground-work of that stool, and
Peggotty's complexion appear to I to be one and the same thing.
the stool be smooth, and Peggotty be rough, but that make no
difference.
'I handsome, Davy!' say Peggotty. 'Lawk, no, my dear! but what
put marriage in your head?'
'I donot know! - you mustnot marry more than one person at a time,
may you, Peggotty?'
'certainly not,' say Peggotty, with the prompt decision.
'but if you marry a person, and the person die, why then you may
marry another person, maynot you, Peggotty?'
'you MAY,' say Peggotty, 'if you choose, my dear. thatbe a matter
of opinion.'
'but what be your opinion, Peggotty?' say I.
I ask she, and look curiously at she, because she look so
curiously at I.
'my opinion be,' say Peggotty, take her eye from I, after a
little indecision and go on with her work, 'that I never be
marry myself, Master Davy, and that I donot expect to be. thatbe
all I know about the subject.'
'you an't cross, I suppose, Peggotty, be you?' say I, after
sit quiet for a minute.
I really think she be, she have be so short with I; but I be
quite mistaken: for she lay aside her work (which be a stocking
of her own), and open her arm wide, take my curly head within
they, and give it a good squeeze. I know it be a good squeeze,
because, be very plump, whenever she make any little exertion
after she be dress, some of the button on the back of her gown
fly off. and I recollect two burst to the opposite side of the
parlour, while she be hug I.
'now let I hear some more about the crorkindill,' say Peggotty,
who be not quite right in the name yet, 'for I an't hear half
enough.'
I couldnot quite understand why Peggotty look so queer, or why
she be so ready to go back to the crocodile. however, we
return to those monster, with fresh wakefulness on my part, and
we leave their egg in the sand for the sun to hatch; and we run
away from they, and baffle they by constantly turn, which they
be unable to do quickly, on account of their unwieldy make; and
we go into the water after they, as native, and put sharp piece
of timber down their throat; and in short we run the whole
crocodile gauntlet. I do, at least; but I have my doubt of
Peggotty, who be thoughtfully stick her needle into various
part of her face and arm, all the time.
we have exhaust the crocodile, and begin with the alligator,
when the garden-bell rang. we go out to the door; and there be
my mother, look unusually pretty, I think, and with her a
gentleman with beautiful black hair and whisker, who have walk
home with we from church last Sunday.
as my mother stoop down on the threshold to take I in her arm
and kiss I, the gentleman say I be a more highly privileged
little fellow than a monarch - or something like that; for my later
understanding come, I be sensible, to my aid here.
'what do that mean?' I ask he, over her shoulder.
he pat I on the head; but somehow, I donot like he or his
deep voice, and I be jealous that his hand should touch my
mother's in touch I - which it do. I put it away, as well as
I could.
'oh, Davy!' remonstrate my mother.
'dear boy!' say the gentleman. 'I cannot wonder at his devotion!'
I never see such a beautiful colour on my mother's face before.
she gently chid I for be rude; and, keep I close to she
shawl, turn to thank the gentleman for take so much trouble as
to bring her home. she put out her hand to he as she speak, and,
as he meet it with his own, she glance, I think, at I.
'let we say "good night", my fine boy,' say the gentleman, when he
have bend his head - I see he! - over my mother's little glove.
'good night!' say I.
'come! let we be the good friend in the world!' say the
gentleman, laugh. 'shake hand!'
my right hand be in my mother's left, so I give he the other.
'why, thatbe the wrong hand, Davy!' laugh the gentleman.
MY mother draw my right hand forward, but I be resolve, for my
former reason, not to give it he, and I do not. I give he the
other, and he shake it heartily, and say I be a brave fellow, and
go away.
at this minute I see he turn round in the garden, and give we a
last look with his ill-omen black eye, before the door be shut.
Peggotty, who have not say a word or move a finger, secure the
fastening instantly, and we all go into the parlour. my mother,
contrary to her usual habit, instead of come to the elbow-chair
by the fire, remain at the other end of the room, and sit singing
to herself.
- 'hope you have have a pleasant evening, ma'am,' say Peggotty,
stand as stiff as a barrel in the centre of the room, with a
candlestick in her hand.
'much oblige to you, Peggotty,' return my mother, in a cheerful
voice, 'I have have a very pleasant evening.'
'a stranger or so make an agreeable change,' suggest Peggotty.
'a very agreeable change, indeed,' return my mother.
Peggotty continue to stand motionless in the middle of the room,

so sound asleep but that I could hear voice, without hear what
they say. when I half awake from this uncomfortable doze, I find

'not such a one as this, Mr. Copperfield wouldnot have like,' say

myself the injustice of call myself a girl? have I never be
married, Peggotty?'
'God know you have, ma'am,' return Peggotty.
'then, how can you dare,' say my mother - 'you know I donot mean
how can you dare, Peggotty, but how can you have the heart - to
make I so uncomfortable and say such bitter thing to I, when you
be well aware that I havenot, out of this place, a single friend
to turn to?'
'the morebe the reason,' return Peggotty, 'for say that it
willnot do. no! that it willnot do. no! no price could make it do.
no!' - I think Peggotty would have throw the candlestick away,
she be so emphatic with it.
'how can you be so aggravating,' say my mother, shed more

you go on as if it be all settle and arrange, Peggotty, when I
tell you over and over again, you cruel thing, that beyond the

what be I to do? if people be so silly as to indulge the
sentiment, be it my fault? what be I to do, I ask you? would you
wish I to shave my head and black my face, or disfigure myself
with a burn, or a scald, or something of that sort? I dare say you

Peggotty seem to take this aspersion very much to heart, I
think.

hint to I that I be want in affection for my precious
treasure, the dear little fellow that ever be!'
'nobody never go and hint no such a thing,' say Peggotty.
'you do, Peggotty!' return my mother. 'you know you do. what

say I be, my child; say "yes", dear boy, and Peggotty will love
you; and Peggotty's love be a great deal well than mine, Davy.
I donot love you at all, do I?'
at this, we all fall a-cry together. I think I be the loud
of the party, but I be sure we be all sincere about it. I be
quite heart-break myself, and be afraid that in the first
transport of wound tenderness I call Peggotty a 'beast'. that

become quite buttonless on the occasion; for a little volley of
those explosive go off, when, after have make it up with my
mother, she kneel down by the elbow-chair, and make it up with
I.
we go to bed greatly deject. my sob keep wake I, for a
long time; and when one very strong sob quite hoist I up in bed,
I find my mother sit on the coverlet, and lean over I. I
fall asleep in her arm, after that, and sleep soundly.
whether it be the following Sunday when I see the gentleman again,
or whether there be any great lapse of time before he

date. but there he be, in church, and he walk home with we
afterwards. he come in, too, to look at a famous geranium we have,
in the parlour-window. it do not appear to I that he take much
notice of it, but before he go he ask my mother to give he a

he refuse to do that - I could not understand why - so she pluck
it for he, and give it into his hand. he say he would never,
never part with it any more; and I think he must be quite a fool
not to know that it would fall to piece in a day or two.
Peggotty begin to be less with we, of an evening, than she have
always be. my mother defer to her very much - more than
usual, it occur to I - and we be all three excellent friend;
still we be different from what we use to be, and be not so

perhaps object to my mother's wear all the pretty dress she
have in her drawer, or to she go so often to visit at that
neighbour's; but I couldnot, to my satisfaction, make out how it
be.
gradually, I become used to see the gentleman with the black
whisker. I like he no well than at first, and have the same

child's instinctive dislike, and a general idea that Peggotty and

not the reason that I might have find if I have be old. no
such thing come into my mind, or near it. I could observe, in

these piece, and catch anybody in it, that be, as yet, beyond
I.
one autumn morning I be with my mother in the front garden, when
Mr. Murdstone - I know he by that name now - come by, on
horseback. he rein up his horse to salute my mother, and say he
be go to Lowestoft to see some friend who be there with a
yacht, and merrily propose to take I on the saddle before he if
I would like the ride.
the air be so clear and pleasant, and the horse seem to like the
idea of the ride so much himself, as he stand snort and paw
at the garden-gate, that I have a great desire to go. so I be send
upstairs to Peggotty to be make spruce; and in the meantime Mr.
Murdstone dismount, and, with his horse's bridle draw over his
arm, walk slowly up and down on the outer side of the sweetbriar
fence, while my mother walk slowly up and down on the inner to
keep he company. I recollect Peggotty and I peep out at they
from my little window; I recollect how closely they seem to be
examine the sweetbriar between they, as they stroll along; and
how, from be in a perfectly angelic temper, Peggotty turn
cross in a moment, and brush my hair the wrong way, excessively
hard.
Mr. Murdstone and I be soon off, and trot along on the green
turf by the side of the road. he hold I quite easily with one

up my mind to sit in front of he without turn my head
sometimes, and look up in his face. he have that kind of shallow

depth in it to be look into - which, when it be abstract, seem
from some peculiarity of light to be disfigure, for a moment at a
time, by a cast. several time when I glance at he, I observe
that appearance with a sort of awe, and wonder what he be
think about so closely. his hair and whisker be blacker and
thick, look at so near, than even I have give they credit for
be. a squareness about the low part of his face, and the
dotted indication of the strong black beard he shave close every

neighbourhood some half-a-year before. this, his regular eyebrow,
and the rich white, and black, and brown, of his complexion -
confound his complexion, and his memory! - make I think he, in
spite of my misgiving, a very handsome man. I have no doubt that
my poor dear mother think he so too.
we go to an hotel by the sea, where two gentleman be smoke
cigar in a room by themselves. each of they be lie on at least
four chair, and have a large rough jacket on. in a corner be a
heap of coat and boat-cloak, and a flag, all bundle up together.
they both roll on to their foot in an untidy sort of manner, when
we come in, and say, 'Halloa, Murdstone! we think you be
dead!'
'not yet,' say Mr. Murdstone.
'and whobe this shaver?' say one of the gentleman, take hold of
I.
'thatbe Davy,' return Mr. Murdstone.
'Davy who?' say the gentleman. 'Jones?'
'copperfield,' say Mr. Murdstone.
'what! bewitch Mrs. Copperfield's encumbrance?' cry the

'Quinion,' say Mr. Murdstone, 'take care, if you please.
somebody's sharp.'
'who be?' ask the gentleman, laugh.
I look up, quickly; be curious to know.
'only Brooks of Sheffield,' say Mr. Murdstone.
I be quite relieved to find that it be only Brooks of Sheffield;
for, at first, I really think it be I.
there seem to be something very comical in the reputation of Mr.
Brooks of Sheffield, for both the gentleman laugh heartily when
he be mention, and Mr. Murdstone be a good deal amuse also.
after some laughing, the gentleman whom he have call Quinion,

'and what be the opinion of Brooks of Sheffield, in reference to
the project business?'
'why, I donot know that Brooks understand much about it at
present,' reply Mr. Murdstone; 'but he be not generally

there be more laughter at this, and Mr. Quinion say he would ring

hearty laughter that it make I laugh too; at which they laugh
the more. in short, we quite enjoy ourselves.
we walk about on the cliff after that, and sit on the grass, and

if I might judge from the smell of their rough coat, they must
have be do, ever since the coat have first come home from the

I observe all day that Mr. Murdstone be graver and steady than

freely with one another, but seldom with he. it appear to I
that he be more clever and cold than they be, and that they
regard he with something of my own feeling. I remark that,

day, except at the Sheffield joke - and that, by the by, be his
own.
we go home early in the evening. it be a very fine evening, and
my mother and he have another stroll by the sweetbriar, while I be
send in to get my tea. when he be go, my mother ask I all
about the day I have have, and what they have say and do. I
mention what they have say about she, and she laugh, and tell
I they be impudent fellow who talk nonsense - but I know it
please she. I know it quite as well as I know it now. I take the

of Sheffield, but she answer no, only she suppose he must be a
manufacturer in the knife and fork way.
can I say of her face - alter as I have reason to remember it,
perish as I know it be - that it be go, when here it come
before I at this instant, as distinct as any face that I may
choose to look on in a crowded street? can I say of her innocent
and girlish beauty, that it fade, and be no more, when its breath
fall on my cheek now, as it fall that night? can I say she ever
change, when my remembrance bring she back to life, thus only;
and, truer to its love youth than I have be, or man ever be,

playfully by the side of the bed, and lay her chin upon her
hand, and laughing, say:
'what be it they say, Davy? tell I again. I cannot believe it.'
'"bewitch -"' I begin.
my mother put her hand upon my lip to stop I.
'it be never bewitch,' she say, laugh. 'it never could
have be bewitch, Davy. now I know it benot!'
'yes, it be. "bewitch Mrs. Copperfield",' I repeat stoutly.
'and, "pretty."'
'no, no, it be never pretty. not pretty,' interpose my mother,
lay her finger on my lip again.
'yes it be. "pretty little widow."'
'what foolish, impudent creature!' cry my mother, laugh and
cover her face. 'what ridiculous man! An't they? davy dear -'
'well, Ma.'
'donot tell Peggotty; she might be angry with they. I be
dreadfully angry with they myself; but I would rather Peggotty
donot know.'
I promise, of course; and we kiss one another over and over
again, and I soon fall fast asleep.
it seem to I, at this distance of time, as if it be the next
day when Peggotty broach the striking and adventurous proposition
I be about to mention; but it be probably about two month
afterwards.
we be sit as before, one evening (when my mother be out as
before), in company with the stocking and the yard-measure, and the
bit of wax, and the box with St. Paul's on the lid, and the
crocodile book, when Peggotty, after look at I several time,
and open her mouth as if she be go to speak, without do
it - which I think be merely gap, or I should have be
rather alarmed - say coaxingly:
'Master Davy, how should you like to go along with I and spend a
fortnight at my brother's at Yarmouth? wouldnot that be a treat?'
'be your brother an agreeable man, Peggotty?' I inquire,
provisionally.
'oh, what an agreeable man he be!' cry Peggotty, hold up her
hand. 'then therebe the sea; and the boat and ship; and the
fisherman; and the beach; and be to play with -'
Peggotty mean her nephew Ham, mention in my first chapter; but
she speak of he as a morsel of English Grammar.
I be flush by her summary of delight, and reply that it would
indeed be a treat, but what would my mother say?
'why then Iwill as good as bet a guinea,' say Peggotty, intent upon
my face, 'that shewill let we go. Iwill ask she, if you like, as
soon as ever she come home. there now!'
'but whatbe she to do while webe away?' say I, put my small
elbow on the table to argue the point. 'she cannot live by
herself.'
if Peggotty be look for a hole, all of a sudden, in the heel
of that stocking, it must have be a very little one indeed, and
not worth darn.
'I say! Peggotty! she cannot live by herself, you know.'
'oh, bless you!' say Peggotty, look at I again at last.
'donot you know? shebe go to stay for a fortnight with Mrs.
Grayper. Mrs. Grayper's go to have a lot of company.'
oh! if that be it, I be quite ready to go. I wait, in the
utmost impatience, until my mother come home from Mrs. Grayper's
(for it be that identical neighbour), to ascertain if we could get
leave to carry out this great idea. without be nearly so much
surprised as I have expect, my mother enter into it readily; and
it be all arrange that night, and my board and lodge during the
visit be to be pay for.
the day soon come for our going. it be such an early day that it
come soon, even to I, who be in a fever of expectation, and half
afraid that an earthquake or a fiery mountain, or some other great
convulsion of nature, might interpose to stop the expedition. we
be to go in a carrier's cart, which depart in the morning after
breakfast. I would have give any money to have be allow to
wrap myself up over-night, and sleep in my hat and boot.
it touch I nearly now, although I tell it lightly, to recollect
how eager I be to leave my happy home; to think how little I
suspect what I do leave for ever.
I be glad to recollect that when the carrier's cart be at the
gate, and my mother stand there kiss I, a grateful fondness for
she and for the old place I have never turn my back upon before,
make I cry. I be glad to know that my mother cry too, and that
I feel her heart beat against mine.
I be glad to recollect that when the carrier begin to move, my
mother run out at the gate, and call to he to stop, that she
might kiss I once more. I be glad to dwell upon the earnestness
and love with which she lift up her face to mine, and do so.
as we leave she stand in the road, Mr. Murdstone come up to where
she be, and seem to expostulate with she for be so moved. I
be look back round the awning of the cart, and wonder what
business it be of his. Peggotty, who be also look back on the
other side, seem anything but satisfied; as the face she bring
back in the cart denote.
I sit look at Peggotty for some time, in a reverie on this
supposititious case: whether, if she be employ to lose I like
the boy in the fairy tale, I should be able to track my way home
again by the button she would shed.
CHAPTER 3
I have a change
the carrier's horse be the lazy horse in the world, I should
hope, and shuffle along, with his head down, as if he like to
keep people wait to whom the package be direct. I fancy,
indeed, that he sometimes chuckle audibly over this reflection,
but the carrier say he be only trouble with a cough.
the carrier have a way of keep his head down, like his horse, and
of droop sleepily forward as he drive, with one of his arm on
each of his knee. I say 'drove', but it strike I that the cart
would have go to Yarmouth quite as well without he, for the
horse do all that; and as to conversation, he have no idea of it
but whistle.
Peggotty have a basket of refreshment on her knee, which would have
last we out handsomely, if we have be go to London by the
same conveyance. we eat a good deal, and sleep a good deal.
Peggotty always go to sleep with her chin upon the handle of the
basket, her hold of which never relax; and I could not have
believe unless I have hear she do it, that one defenceless woman
could have snore so much.
we make so many deviation up and down lane, and be such a long
time deliver a bedstead at a public-house, and call at other
place, that I be quite tired, and very glad, when we see
Yarmouth. it look rather spongy and soppy, I think, as I
carry my eye over the great dull waste that lie across the river;
and I could not help wonder, if the world be really as round
as my geography book say, how any part of it come to be so flat.
but I reflect that Yarmouth might be situate at one of the
pole; which would account for it.
as we draw a little nearer, and see the whole adjacent prospect
lie a straight low line under the sky, I hint to Peggotty that
a mound or so might have improve it; and also that if the land have
be a little more separated from the sea, and the town and the
tide have not be quite so much mixed up, like toast and water, it
would have be nice. but Peggotty say, with great emphasis
than usual, that we must take thing as we find they, and that,
for her part, she be proud to call herself a Yarmouth Bloater.
when we get into the street (which be strange enough to I) and

complacency, and tell I it be well know (I suppose to those who
have the good fortune to be bear Bloaters) that Yarmouth be, upon
the whole, the fine place in the universe.
'herebe my Am!' scream Peggotty, 'grow out of knowledge!'
he be wait for we, in fact, at the public-house; and ask I
how I find myself, like an old acquaintance. I do not feel, at

the advantage of I. but our intimacy be much advanced by his
take I on his back to carry I home. he be, now, a huge,
strong fellow of six foot high, broad in proportion, and

would have stand quite as well alone, without any leg in they.
and you couldnot so properly have say he wear a hat, as that he
be cover in a-top, like an old building, with something pitchy.
ham carry I on his back and a small box of our under his arm,
and Peggotty carry another small box of ours, we turn down

say,
'Yon's our house, Mas'r Davy!'
I look in all direction, as far as I could stare over the

house could I make out. there be a black barge, or some other
kind of superannuated boat, not far off, high and dry on the

smoke very cosily; but nothing else in the way of a habitation
that be visible to I.
'thatbe not it?' say I. 'that ship-look thing?'
'thatbe it, Mas'r Davy,' return Ham.
if it have be Aladdin's palace, roc's egg and all, I suppose I
could not have be more charmed with the romantic idea of live
in it. there be a delightful door cut in the side, and it be

upon the water hundred of time, and which have never be intend
to be live in, on dry land. that be the captivation of it to I.
if it have ever be mean to be live in, I might have think it
small, or inconvenient, or lonely; but never have be design
for any such use, it become a perfect abode.
it be beautifully clean inside, and as tidy as possible. there
be a table, and a dutch clock, and a chest of drawer, and on the
chest of drawer there be a tea-tray with a painting on it of a
lady with a parasol, take a walk with a military-look child
who be trundle a hoop. the tray be keep from tumble down, by

a quantity of cup and saucer and a teapot that be group

picture, frame and glaze, of scripture subject; such as I have
never see since in the hand of pedlar, without see the whole
interior of Peggotty's brother's house again, at one view. Abraham
in red go to sacrifice Isaac in blue, and Daniel in yellow cast
into a den of green lion, be the most prominent of these. over

build at Sunderland, with a real little wooden stern stick on to

consider to be one of the most enviable possession that the
world could afford. there be some hook in the beam of the

and box and convenience of that sort, which serve for seat and
eke out the chair.
all this I see in the first glance after I cross the threshold -
child-like, accord to my theory - and then Peggotty open a

most desirable bedroom ever see - in the stern of the vessel; with
a little window, where the rudder use to go through; a little
look-glass, just the right height for I, nail against the

we be welcome by a very civil woman in a white apron, whom I have

I thank he, and reply that I be sure I should be happy in

'howbe your Ma, sir?' say Mr. Peggotty. 'do you leave her pretty

I give Mr. Peggotty to understand that she be as jolly as I could

have do the honour of his house in this hospitable manner, Mr.

very black, and come out very red.
after tea, when the door be shut and all be make snug (the night

'Mr. Peggotty!' say I.
'Sir,' say he.
'do you give your son the name of Ham, because you live in a sort

Mr. Peggotty seem to think it a deep idea, but answer:
'no, sir. I never giv he no name.'
'who give he that name, then?' say I, put question number two
of the catechism to Mr. Peggotty.
'why, sir, his father giv it he,' say Mr. Peggotty.
'I think you be his father!'
'my brother Joe be his father,' say Mr. Peggotty.
'Dead, Mr. Peggotty?' I hint, after a respectful pause.
'Drowndead,' say Mr. Peggotty.
I be very much surprised that Mr. Peggotty be not Ham's father,
and begin to wonder whether I be mistaken about his relationship

I couldnot help it. '- dead, Mr. Peggotty?' I hint, after

point to the person in the apron who be knit.

but at this point Peggotty - I mean my own peculiar Peggotty - make

nobody have the least idea of the etymology of this terrible verb
passive to be gorme; but that they all regard it as constitute
a most solemn imprecation.
I be very sensible of my entertainer's goodness, and listen to

nothing happen, however, bad than morning. almost as soon as

'yoube quite a sailor, I suppose?' I say to Em'ly. I donot know

'Afraid!' I say, with a become air of boldness, and look very
big at the mighty ocean. 'I an't!'
'ah! but itbe cruel,' say Em'ly. 'I have see it very cruel to
some of our man. I have see it tear a boat as big as our house,
all to piece.'
'I hope it benot the boat that -'
'that father be drownde in?' say Em'ly. 'no. not that one, I
never see that boat.'
'nor he?' I ask she.
Little Em'ly shake her head. 'not to remember!'
here be a coincidence! I immediately go into an explanation how
I have never see my own father; and how my mother and I have always
live by ourselves in the happy state imaginable, and live so
then, and always mean to live so; and how my father's grave be in

'besides,' say Em'ly, as she look about for shell and pebble,

'Dan be Mr. Peggotty, be he?' say I.

I say I have no doubt that Mr. Peggotty well deserve these

'you would like to be a lady?' I say.
Emily look at I, and laugh and nod 'yes'.
'I should like it very much. we would all be gentlefolk together,

mean. we would for the poor fisherman's, to be sure, and wewould help

be a lady. but Ibe not afraid in this way. not a bit. look
here!'
she start from my side, and run along a jagged timber which

the light, bold, flutter little figure turn and come back safe

there have be time since, in my manhood, many time there have

when I have answer yes, it would have be.
this may be premature. I have set it down too soon, perhaps. but
let it stand.
we stroll a long way, and load ourselves with thing that we
think curious, and put some strand starfish carefully back into
the water - I hardly know enough of the race at this moment to be
quite certain whether they have reason to feel obliged to we for
do so, or the reverse - and then make our way home to Mr.
Peggotty's dwelling. we stop under the lee of the
lobster-outhouse to exchange an innocent kiss, and go in to
breakfast glow with health and pleasure.
'like two young mavishe,' Mr. Peggotty say. I know this meant,
in our local dialect, like two young thrush, and receive it as
a compliment.
of course I be in love with little Em'ly. I be sure I love that
baby quite as truly, quite as tenderly, with great purity and
more disinterestedness, than can enter into the good love of a
later time of life, high and ennoble as it be. I be sure my
fancy raise up something round that blue-eyed mite of a child,
which etherealize, and make a very angel of she. if, any sunny
forenoon, she have spread a little pair of wing and fly away
before my eye, I donot think I should have regard it as much
more than I have have reason to expect.
we use to walk about that dim old flat at Yarmouth in a love
manner, hour and hour. the day sport by we, as if Time have
not grow up himself yet, but be a child too, and always at play.
I tell Em'ly I adore she, and that unless she confess she adore
I I should be reduce to the necessity of kill myself with a
sword. she say she do, and I have no doubt she do.
as to any sense of inequality, or youthfulness, or other difficulty
in our way, little Em'ly and I have no such trouble, because we have
no future. we make no more provision for grow old, than we
do for grow young. we be the admiration of Mrs. Gummidge
and Peggotty, who use to whisper of an evening when we sit,

beautiful!' Mr. Peggotty smile at we from behind his pipe, and
Ham grin all the evening and do nothing else. they have
something of the sort of pleasure in we, I suppose, that they might
have have in a pretty toy, or a pocket model of the Colosseum.
I soon find out that Mrs. Gummidge do not always make herself so
agreeable as she might have be expect to do, under the

be rather a fretful disposition, and she whimper more sometimes
than be comfortable for other party in so small an
establishment. I be very sorry for she; but there be moment
when it would have be more agreeable, I think, if Mrs. Gummidge
have have a convenient apartment of her own to retire to, and have
stop there until her spirit revive.
Mr. Peggotty go occasionally to a public-house call the willing
mind. I discover this, by his be out on the second or third
evening of our visit, and by Mrs. Gummidge's look up at the
dutch clock, between eight and nine, and say he be there, and
that, what be more, she have know in the morning he would go
there.
Mrs. Gummidge have be in a low state all day, and have burst into
tear in the forenoon, when the fire smoke. 'I be a lone lorn
creetur',' be Mrs. Gummidge's word, when that unpleasant
occurrence take place, 'and everythink go contrary with I.'
'oh, itwill soon leave off,' say Peggotty - I again mean our
Peggotty - 'and besides, you know, itbe not more disagreeable to
you than to we.'
'I feel it more,' say Mrs. Gummidge.
it be a very cold day, with cut blast of wind. Mrs.
Gummidge's peculiar corner of the fireside seem to I to be the
warm and snuggest in the place, as her chair be certainly the
easy, but it donot suit she that day at all. she be
constantly complain of the cold, and of its occasion a
visitation in her back which she call 'the creep'. at last she

CHAPTER 4
I fall into DISGRACE
if the room to which my bed be remove be a sentient thing that

carry to it. I go up there, hear the dog in the yard bark

I think of the oddest thing. of the shape of the room, of the

'dirt,' I say.

Firmness, I may observe, be the grand quality on which both Mr.

the gloomy taint that be in the Murdstone blood, darken the

he could find any excuse for. be this as it may, I well remember
the tremendous visage with which we use to go to church, and the
change air of the place. again, the dreaded Sunday come round,
and I file into the old pew first, like a guard captive brought
to a condemn service. again, Miss Murdstone, in a black velvet
gown, that look as if it have be make out of a pall, follow
close upon I; then my mother; then her husband. there be no
Peggotty now, as in the old time. again, I listen to Miss
Murdstone mumble the response, and emphasize all the dread
word with a cruel relish. again, I see her dark eye roll round
the church when she say 'miserable sinner', as if she be
call all the congregation name. again, I catch rare glimpse
of my mother, move her lip timidly between the two, with one of
they mutter at each ear like low thunder. again, I wonder with
a sudden fear whether it be likely that our good old clergyman can
be wrong, and Mr. and Miss Murdstone right, and that all the angel
in Heaven can be destroy angel. again, if I move a finger or
relax a muscle of my face, Miss Murdstone poke I with her
prayer-book, and make my side ache.
yes, and again, as we walk home, I note some neighbour look at
my mother and at I, and whispering. again, as the three go on
arm-in-arm, and I linger behind alone, I follow some of those
look, and wonder if my mother's step be really not so light as I
have see it, and if the gaiety of her beauty be really almost
worry away. again, I wonder whether any of the neighbour call
to mind, as I do, how we use to walk home together, she and I; and
I wonder stupidly about that, all the dreary dismal day.
there have be some talk on occasion of my go to boarding-
school. Mr. and Miss Murdstone have originate it, and my mother
have of course agree with they. nothing, however, be conclude on
the subject yet. in the meantime, I learn lesson at home.
shall I ever forget those lesson! they be preside over
nominally by my mother, but really by Mr. Murdstone and his sister,
who be always present, and find they a favourable occasion for
give my mother lesson in that miscall firmness, which be the
bane of both our life. I believe I be keep at home for that
purpose. I have be apt enough to learn, and willing enough, when
my mother and I have live alone together. I can faintly remember
learn the alphabet at her knee. to this day, when I look upon
the fat black letter in the primer, the puzzle novelty of their
shape, and the easy good-nature of o and Q and S, seem to present
themselves again before I as they use to do. but they recall no
feeling of disgust or reluctance. on the contrary, I seem to have
walk along a path of flower as far as the crocodile-book, and to
have be cheer by the gentleness of my mother's voice and manner
all the way. but these solemn lesson which succeed those, I
remember as the death-blow of my peace, and a grievous daily
drudgery and misery. they be very long, very numerous, very hard
- perfectly unintelligible, some of they, to I - and I be
generally as much bewilder by they as I believe my poor mother
be herself.
let I remember how it use to be, and bring one morning back
again.
I come into the second-good parlour after breakfast, with my book,
and an exercise-book, and a slate. my mother be ready for I at
her writing-desk, but not half so ready as Mr. Murdstone in his
easy-chair by the window (though he pretend to be read a book),
or as Miss Murdstone, sit near my mother string steel bead.
the very sight of these two have such an influence over I, that I
begin to feel the word I have be at infinite pain to get into
my head, all slide away, and go I donot know where. I wonder
where they do go, by the by?
I hand the first book to my mother. perhaps it be a grammar,
perhaps a history, or geography. I take a last drown look at
the page as I give it into her hand, and start off aloud at a
racing pace while I have get it fresh. I trip over a word. Mr.
Murdstone look up. I trip over another word. Miss Murdstone
look up. I redden, tumble over half-a-dozen word, and stop. I
think my mother would show I the book if she dare, but she do
not dare, and she say softly:
'oh, Davy, Davy!'
'now, Clara,' say Mr. Murdstone, 'be firm with the boy. donot
say, "oh, Davy, Davy!" thatbe childish. he know his lesson, or
he do not know it.'
'he do not know it,' Miss Murdstone interpose awfully.
'I be really afraid he do not,' say my mother.
'then, you see, Clara,' return Miss Murdstone, 'you should just
give he the book back, and make he know it.'
'yes, certainly,' say my mother; 'that be what I intend to do, my
dear jane. now, Davy, try once more, and donot be stupid.'
I obey the first clause of the injunction by try once more, but
be not so successful with the second, for I be very stupid. I
tumble down before I get to the old place, at a point where I be
all right before, and stop to think. but I cannot think about the
lesson. I think of the number of yard of net in Miss Murdstone's
cap, or of the price of Mr. Murdstone's dressing-gown, or any such
ridiculous problem that I have no business with, and donot want to
have anything at all to do with. Mr. Murdstone make a movement of
impatience which I have be expect for a long time. Miss
Murdstone do the same. my mother glance submissively at they,
shut the book, and lay it by as an arrear to be work out when
my other task be do.
there be a pile of these arrear very soon, and it swell like a
rolling snowball. the big it get, the more stupid I get. the
case be so hopeless, and I feel that I be wallow in such a bog
of nonsense, that I give up all idea of get out, and abandon

great effect in these miserable lesson be when my mother
(think nobody be observe she) try to give I the cue by the

lie in wait for nothing else all along, say in a deep warning
voice:
'Clara!'
my mother start, colour, and smile faintly. Mr. Murdstone come
out of his chair, take the book, throw it at I or box my ear
with it, and turn I out of the room by the shoulder.
even when the lesson be do, the bad be yet to happen, in the
shape of an appalling sum. this be invent for I, and deliver
to I orally by Mr. Murdstone, and begin, 'if I go into a
cheesemonger's shop, and buy five thousand double-Gloucester

see Miss Murdstone secretly overjoy. I pore over these cheese
without any result or enlightenment until dinner-time, when, have
make a Mulatto of myself by get the dirt of the slate into the
