cheese, and be consider in disgrace for the rest of the evening.
it seem to I, at this distance of time, as if my unfortunate
study generally take this course. I could have do very well if
I have be without the Murdstones; but the influence of the
Murdstones upon I be like the fascination of two snake on a
wretched young bird. even when I do get through the morning with
tolerable credit, there be not much gain but dinner; for Miss
Murdstone never could endure to see I untasked, and if I rashly
make any show of be unemployed, call her brother's attention
to I by say, 'Clara, my dear, therebe nothing like work - give
your boy an exercise'; which cause I to be clap down to some
new labour, there and then. as to any recreation with other
child of my age, I have very little of that; for the gloomy
theology of the Murdstones make all child out to be a swarm of
little viper (though there be a child once set in the midst of
the Disciples), and hold that they contaminate one another.
the natural result of this treatment, continue, I suppose, for
some six month or more, be to make I sullen, dull, and dog.
I be not make the less so by my sense of be daily more and more
shut out and alienate from my mother. I believe I should have
be almost stupefied but for one circumstance.
it be this. my father have leave a small collection of book in a
little room upstairs, to which I have access (for it adjoin my
own) and which nobody else in our house ever trouble. from that
bless little room, Roderick Random, Peregrine Pickle, Humphrey
Clinker, Tom Jones, the Vicar of Wakefield, Don Quixote, Gil Blas,
and Robinson Crusoe, come out, a glorious host, to keep I company.
they keep alive my fancy, and my hope of something beyond that
place and time, - they, and the Arabian Nights, and the Tales of
the Genii, - and do I no harm; for whatever harm be in some of
they be not there for I; I know nothing of it. it be astonishing
to I now, how I find time, in the midst of my poring and
blundering over heavy theme, to read those book as I do. it
be curious to I how I could ever have console myself under my
small trouble (which be great trouble to I), by impersonate
my favourite character in they - as I do - and by put Mr. and
Miss Murdstone into all the bad one - which I do too. I have
be Tom Jones (a child's Tom Jones, a harmless creature) for a
week together. I have sustain my own idea of Roderick Random for
a month at a stretch, I verily believe. I have a greedy relish for
a few volume of Voyages and Travels - I forget what, now - that
be on those shelf; and for day and day I can remember to have
go about my region of our house, arm with the centre-piece out
of an old set of boot-tree - the perfect realization of Captain
Somebody, of the Royal British Navy, in danger of be beset by
savage, and resolve to sell his life at a great price. the
Captain never lose dignity, from have his ear box with the
Latin Grammar. I do; but the Captain be a captain and a hero, in
despite of all the grammar of all the language in the world, dead
or alive.
this be my only and my constant comfort. when I think of it, the
picture always rise in my mind, of a summer evening, the boy at
play in the churchyard, and I sit on my bed, read as if for
life. every barn in the neighbourhood, every stone in the church,
and every foot of the churchyard, have some association of its own,
in my mind, connect with these book, and stand for some locality
make famous in they. I have see Tom Pipes go climb up the
church-steeple; I have watch Strap, with the knapsack on his
back, stop to rest himself upon the wicket-gate; and I know
that Commodore Trunnion hold that club with Mr. Pickle, in the
parlour of our little village alehouse.
the reader now understand, as well as I do, what I be when I come
to that point of my youthful history to which I be now come
again.
one morning when I go into the parlour with my book, I find my
mother look anxious, Miss Murdstone look firm, and Mr.
Murdstone bind something round the bottom of a cane - a lithe
and limber cane, which he leave off binding when I come in, and
poise and switch in the air.
'I tell you, Clara,' say Mr. Murdstone, 'I have be often flog
myself.'
'to be sure; of course,' say Miss Murdstone.
'certainly, my dear jane,' falter my mother, meekly. 'but - but
do you think it do Edward good?'
'do you think it do Edward harm, Clara?' ask Mr. Murdstone,
gravely.
'thatbe the point,' say his sister.
to this my mother return, 'certainly, my dear jane,' and say no
more.
I feel apprehensive that I be personally interested in this
dialogue, and seek Mr. Murdstone's eye as it light on mine.
'now, David,' he say - and I see that cast again as he say it -
'you must be far more careful today than usual.' he give the cane
another poise, and another switch; and have finish his
preparation of it, lay it down beside he, with an impressive
look, and take up his book.
this be a good freshener to my presence of mind, as a beginning.
I feel the word of my lesson slip off, not one by one, or
line by line, but by the entire page; I try to lay hold of they;
but they seem, if I may so express it, to have put skate on, and
to skim away from I with a smoothness there be no checking.
we begin badly, and go on bad. I have come in with an idea of
distinguish myself rather, conceive that I be very well
prepare; but it turn out to be quite a mistake. book after book
be add to the heap of failure, Miss Murdstone be firmly
watchful of we all the time. and when we come at last to the five
thousand cheese (cane he make it that day, I remember), my mother
burst out cry.
'Clara!' say Miss Murdstone, in her warning voice.
'I be not quite well, my dear Jane, I think,' say my mother.
I see he wink, solemnly, at his sister, as he rise and say,
take up the cane:
'why, Jane, we can hardly expect Clara to bear, with perfect
firmness, the worry and torment that David have occasion she
today. that would be stoical. Clara be greatly strengthen and
improve, but we can hardly expect so much from she. David, you
and I will go upstairs, boy.'
as he take I out at the door, my mother run towards we. Miss
Murdstone say, 'Clara! be you a perfect fool?' and interfere.
I see my mother stop her ear then, and I hear her cry.
he walk I up to my room slowly and gravely - I be certain he have
a delight in that formal parade of execute justice - and when we
get there, suddenly twist my head under his arm.
'Mr. Murdstone! Sir!' I cry to he. 'donot! pray donot beat
I! I have try to learn, sir, but I cannot learn while you and
Miss Murdstone be by. I cannot indeed!'
'cannot you, indeed, David?' he say. 'wewill try that.'
he have my head as in a vice, but I twin round he somehow, and
stop he for a moment, entreat he not to beat I. it be
only a moment that I stop he, for he cut I heavily an instant
afterwards, and in the same instant I catch the hand with which he
hold I in my mouth, between my tooth, and bite it through. it set
my tooth on edge to think of it.
he beat I then, as if he would have beat I to death. above all
the noise we make, I hear they run up the stair, and cry
out - I hear my mother cry out - and Peggotty. then he be
go; and the door be lock outside; and I be lie, fevere and
hot, and tear, and sore, and rag in my puny way, upon the floor.
how well I recollect, when I become quiet, what an unnatural
stillness seem to reign through the whole house! how well I
remember, when my smart and passion begin to cool, how wicked I
begin to feel!
I sit listen for a long while, but there be not a sound. I
crawl up from the floor, and see my face in the glass, so
swollen, red, and ugly that it almost frighten I. my stripe
be sore and stiff, and make I cry afresh, when I move; but they
be nothing to the guilt I feel. it lie heavy on my breast than
if I have be a most atrocious criminal, I dare say.
it have begin to grow dark, and I have shut the window (I have be
lie, for the most part, with my head upon the sill, by turn
cry, dozing, and look listlessly out), when the key be
turn, and Miss Murdstone come in with some bread and meat, and
milk. these she put down upon the table without a word, glare at
I the while with exemplary firmness, and then retire, lock the
door after she.
long after it be dark I sit there, wonder whether anybody else
would come. when this appear improbable for that night, I
undressed, and go to bed; and, there, I begin to wonder fearfully
what would be do to I. whether it be a criminal act that I have
commit? whether I should be take into custody, and send to
prison? whether I be at all in danger of be hang?
I never shall forget the waking, next morning; the be cheerful
and fresh for the first moment, and then the being weigh down by
the stale and dismal oppression of remembrance. Miss Murdstone
reappear before I be out of bed; tell I, in so many word, that
I be free to walk in the garden for half an hour and no long;
and retire, leave the door open, that I might avail myself of
that permission.
I do so, and do so every morning of my imprisonment, which last
five day. if I could have see my mother alone, I should have
go down on my knee to she and besought her forgiveness; but I
see no one, Miss Murdstone except, during the whole time - except
at evening prayer in the parlour; to which I be escort by Miss
Murdstone after everybody else be place; where I be station,
a young outlaw, all alone by myself near the door; and whence I be
solemnly conduct by my jailer, before any one arise from the
devotional posture. I only observe that my mother be as far off
from I as she could be, and keep she face another way so that I
never see it; and that Mr. Murdstone's hand be bind up in a large
linen wrapper.
the length of those five day I can convey no idea of to any one.
they occupy the place of year in my remembrance. the way in which
I listen to all the incident of the house that make themselves
audible to I; the ringing of bell, the opening and shutting of
door, the murmuring of voice, the footstep on the stair; to any
laughing, whistling, or singing, outside, which seem more dismal
than anything else to I in my solitude and disgrace - the
uncertain pace of the hour, especially at night, when I would wake
think it be morning, and find that the family be not yet go
to bed, and that all the length of night have yet to come - the
depressed dream and nightmare I have - the return of day, noon,
afternoon, evening, when the boy play in the churchyard, and I
watch they from a distance within the room, be ashamed to show
myself at the window lest they should know I be a prisoner - the
strange sensation of never hear myself speak - the fleeting
interval of something like cheerfulness, which come with eating
and drinking, and go away with it - the setting in of rain one
evening, with a fresh smell, and its come down fast and fast
between I and the church, until it and gathering night seem to
quench I in gloom, and fear, and remorse - all this appear to
have go round and round for year instead of day, it be so
vividly and strongly stamp on my remembrance.
on the last night of my restraint, I be awaken by hear my own
name speak in a whisper. I start up in bed, and put out my
arm in the dark, say:
'be that you, Peggotty?'
there be no immediate answer, but presently I hear my name again,
in a tone so very mysterious and awful, that I think I should have
go into a fit, if it have not occur to I that it must have
come through the keyhole.
I grope my way to the door, and put my own lip to the
keyhole, whisper: 'be that you, Peggotty dear?'
'yes, my own precious Davy,' she reply. 'be as soft as a mouse,
or the Cat'll hear we.'
I understand this to mean Miss Murdstone, and be sensible of the
urgency of the case; her room be close by.
'howbe mama, dear Peggotty? be she very angry with I?'
I could hear Peggotty cry softly on her side of the keyhole, as
I be do on mine, before she answer. 'no. not very.'
'what be go to be do with I, Peggotty dear? do you know?'
'School. near London,' be Peggotty's answer. I be oblige to
get she to repeat it, for she speak it the first time quite down my
throat, in consequence of my have forget to take my mouth away
from the keyhole and put my ear there; and though her word tickle
I a good deal, I donot hear they.
'when, Peggotty?'
'tomorrow.'
'be that the reason why Miss Murdstone take the clothe out of my
drawer?' which she have do, though I have forget to mention
it.
'yes,' say Peggotty. 'Box.'
'shallnot I see mama?'
'yes,' say Peggotty. 'Morning.'
then Peggotty fit her mouth close to the keyhole, and deliver
these word through it with as much feeling and earnestness as a
keyhole have ever be the medium of communicating, I will venture
to assert: shoot in each broken little sentence in a convulsive
little burst of its own.
'Davy, dear. if I benot be azackly as intimate with you.
lately, as I use to be. it benot because I donot love you. just
as well and more, my pretty poppet. itbe because I think it
well for you. and for someone else besides. Davy, my darling,
be you listen? can you hear?'
'ye-ye-ye-yes, Peggotty!' I sob.
'my own!' say Peggotty, with infinite compassion. 'what I want to
say, be. that you must never forget I. for Iwill never forget
you. and Iwill take as much care of your mama, Davy. as ever I
take of you. and I willnot leave she. the day may come when shewill
be glad to lay her poor head. on her stupid, cross old Peggotty's
arm again. and Iwill write to you, my dear. though I benot no
scholar. and Iwill - Iwill -' Peggotty fall to kiss the keyhole,
as she couldnot kiss I.
'thank you, dear Peggotty!' say I. 'oh, thank you! thank you!
will you promise I one thing, Peggotty? will you write and tell
Mr. Peggotty and little Em'ly, and Mrs. Gummidge and Ham, that I be
not so bad as they might suppose, and that I send them all my love
- especially to little Em'ly? will you, if you please, Peggotty?'
the kind soul promise, and we both of we kiss the keyhole with
the great affection - I pat it with my hand, I recollect, as
if it have be her honest face - and part. from that night there
grow up in my breast a feeling for Peggotty which I cannot very
well define. she do not replace my mother; no one could do that;
but she come into a vacancy in my heart, which close upon she, and
I feel towards her something I have never feel for any other human
being. it be a sort of comical affection, too; and yet if she have
die, I cannot think what I should have do, or how I should have
act out the tragedy it would have be to I.
in the morning Miss Murdstone appear as usual, and tell I I be
go to school; which be not altogether such news to I as she
suppose. she also inform I that when I be dress, I be to
come downstairs into the parlour, and have my breakfast. there, I
find my mother, very pale and with red eye: into whose arm I
run, and beg her pardon from my suffering soul.
'oh, Davy!' she say. 'that you could hurt anyone I love! try to
be well, pray to be well! I forgive you; but I be so grieved,
Davy, that you should have such bad passion in your heart.'
they have persuade she that I be a wicked fellow, and she be more
sorry for that than for my go away. I feel it sorely. I try
to eat my parting breakfast, but my tear drop upon my bread-
and-butter, and trickle into my tea. I see my mother look at I
sometimes, and then glance at the watchful Miss Murdstone, and than
look down, or look away.
'Master Copperfield's box there!' say Miss Murdstone, when wheel
be hear at the gate.
I look for Peggotty, but it be not she; neither she nor Mr.
Murdstone appear. my former acquaintance, the carrier, be at
the door. the box be take out to his cart, and lift in.
'Clara!' say Miss Murdstone, in her warning note.

be go for your own good. good-bye, my child. you will come
home in the holiday, and be a well boy.'
'Clara!' Miss Murdstone repeat.
'certainly, my dear jane,' reply my mother, who be hold I.
'I forgive you, my dear boy. God bless you!'
'Clara!' Miss Murdstone repeat.
Miss Murdstone be good enough to take I out to the cart, and to
say on the way that she hop I would repent, before I come to a
bad end; and then I get into the cart, and the lazy horse walk
off with it.
CHAPTER 5
I be send away from HOME
we might have go about half a mile, and my pocket-handkerchief
be quite wet through, when the carrier stop short. look out
to ascertain for what, I see, to MY amazement, Peggotty burst from
a hedge and climb into the cart. she take I in both her arm, and
squeeze I to her stay until the pressure on my nose be
extremely painful, though I never think of that till afterwards
when I find it very tender. not a single word do Peggotty speak.
release one of her arm, she put it down in her pocket to the
elbow, and bring out some paper bag of cake which she cram
into my pocket, and a purse which she put into my hand, but not
one word do she say. after another and a final squeeze with both
arm, she get down from the cart and run away; and, my belief be,
and have always be, without a solitary button on her gown. I
pick up one, of several that be roll about, and treasure it
as a keepsake for a long time.
the carrier look at I, as if to inquire if she be come back.
I shake my head, and say I think not. 'then come up,' say the
carrier to the lazy horse; who come up accordingly.
have by this time cry as much as I possibly could, I begin to
think it be of no use cry any more, especially as neither
Roderick Random, nor that Captain in the Royal British Navy, have
ever cry, that I could remember, in try situation. the
carrier, see I in this resolution, propose that my pocket-
handkerchief should be spread upon the horse's back to dry. I
thank he, and assent; and particularly small it look, under
those circumstance.
I have now leisure to examine the purse. it be a stiff leather
purse, with a snap, and have three bright shilling in it, which
Peggotty have evidently polish up with whitening, for my great
delight. but its most precious content be two half-crown
fold together in a bit of paper, on which be write, in my
mother's hand, 'for Davy. with my love.' I be so overcome by
this, that I ask the carrier to be so good as to reach I my
pocket-handkerchief again; but he say he think I have well do
without it, and I think I really have, so I wipe my eye on my
sleeve and stop myself.
for good, too; though, in consequence of my previous emotion, I
be still occasionally seize with a stormy sob. after we have
jog on for some little time, I ask the carrier if he be go
all the way.
'all the way where?' inquire the carrier.
'there,' I say.
'wherebe there?' inquire the carrier.
'Near London,' I say.
'why that horse,' say the carrier, jerk the rein to point he
out, 'would be dead than pork afore he get over half the ground.'
'be you only go to Yarmouth then?' I ask.
'thatbe about it,' say the carrier. 'and there I shall take you
to the stage-cutch, and the stage-cutch thatwill take you to -
wherever it be.'
as this be a great deal for the carrier (whose name be Mr.
Barkis) to say - he be, as I observe in a former chapter, of a
phlegmatic temperament, and not at all conversational - I offer
he a cake as a mark of attention, which he eat at one gulp,
exactly like an elephant, and which make no more impression on his
big face than it would have do on an elephant's.
'do she make them, now?' say Mr. Barkis, always lean forward,
in his slouching way, on the footboard of the cart with an arm on
each knee.
'Peggotty, do you mean, sir?'
'ah!' say Mr. Barkis. 'she.'
'yes. she make all our pastry, and do all our cooking.'
'do she though?' say Mr. Barkis.
he make up his mouth as if to whistle, but he donot whistle. he
sit look at the horse's ear, as if he see something new there;
and sit so, for a considerable time. by and by, he say:
'no sweetheart, I b'lieve?'
'sweetmeat do you say, Mr. Barkis?' for I think he want
something else to eat, and have pointedly allude to that
description of refreshment.
'Hearts,' say Mr. Barkis. 'sweet heart; no person walk with
she!'
'with Peggotty?'
'ah!' he say. 'she.'
'oh, no. she never have a sweetheart.'
'donot she, though!' say Mr. Barkis.
again he make up his mouth to whistle, and again he donot whistle,
but sit look at the horse's ear.
'so she make,' say Mr. Barkis, after a long interval of
reflection, 'all the apple parstie, and doos all the cooking, do
she?'
I reply that such be the fact.
'well. Iwill tell you what,' say Mr. Barkis. 'p'rap you might be
writin' to she?'
'I shall certainly write to she,' I rejoin.
'ah!' he say, slowly turn his eye towards I. 'well! if you
be writin' to she, p'raps youwould recollect to say that Barkis be
willin'; would you?'
'that Barkis be willing,' I repeat, innocently. 'be that all the
message?'
'ye-es,' he say, consider. 'ye-es. Barkis be willin'.'
'but you will be at Blunderstone again tomorrow, Mr. Barkis,' I
say, falter a little at the idea of my be far away from it
then, and could give your own message so much well.'
as he repudiate this suggestion, however, with a jerk of his head,
and once more confirm his previous request by say, with
profound gravity, 'barkis be willin'. thatbe the message,' I
readily undertake its transmission. while I be wait for the
coach in the hotel at Yarmouth that very afternoon, I procure a
sheet of paper and an inkstand, and write a note to Peggotty, which
run thus: 'my dear Peggotty. I have come here safe. Barkis be
willing. my love to mama. your affectionately. P.S. he say he
particularly want you to know - BARKIS be willing.'
when I have take this commission on myself prospectively, Mr.
Barkis relapse into perfect silence; and I, feel quite worn out
by all that have happen lately, lie down on a sack in the cart and
fall asleep. I sleep soundly until we get to Yarmouth; which be
so entirely new and strange to I in the inn-yard to which we
drive, that I at once abandon a latent hope I have have of meeting
with some of Mr. Peggotty's family there, perhaps even with little
Em'ly herself.
the coach be in the yard, shin very much all over, but without
any horse to it as yet; and it look in that state as if nothing
be more unlikely than its ever go to London. I be think
this, and wonder what would ultimately become of my box, which
Mr. Barkis have put down on the yard-pavement by the pole (he have
drive up the yard to turn his cart), and also what would
ultimately become of I, when a lady look out of a bow-window
where some fowl and joint of meat be hang up, and say:
'be that the little gentleman from Blunderstone?'
'yes, ma'am,' I say.
'what name?' inquire the lady.
'Copperfield, ma'am,' I say.
'that willnot do,' return the lady. 'nobody's dinner be pay for
here, in that name.'
'be it Murdstone, ma'am?' I say.
'if yoube Master Murdstone,' say the lady, 'why do you go and
give another name, first?'
I explain to the lady how it be, who than rang a bell, and
call out, 'William! show the coffee-room!' upon which a waiter
come run out of a kitchen on the opposite side of the yard to
show it, and seem a good deal surprise when he be only to show
it to I.
it be a large long room with some large map in it. I doubt if I
could have feel much strange if the map have be real foreign
country, and I cast away in the middle of they. I feel it be
take a liberty to sit down, with my cap in my hand, on the corner
of the chair nearest the door; and when the waiter lay a cloth on
purpose for I, and put a set of castor on it, I think I must have
turn red all over with modesty.
he bring I some chop, and vegetable, and take the cover off
in such a bounce manner that I be afraid I must have give he
some offence. but he greatly relieve my mind by put a chair
for I at the table, and say, very affably, 'now, six-foot! come
on!'
I thank he, and take my seat at the board; but find it
extremely difficult to handle my knife and fork with anything like
dexterity, or to avoid splash myself with the gravy, while he
be stand opposite, star so hard, and make I blush in the
most dreadful manner every time I catch his eye. after watch
I into the second chop, he say:
'therebe half a pint of ale for you. will you have it now?'
I thank he and say, 'yes.' upon which he pour it out of a
jug into a large tumbler, and hold it up against the light, and
make it look beautiful.
'my eye!' he say. 'it seem a good deal, donot it?'
'it do seem a good deal,' I answer with a smile. for it be
quite delightful to I, to find he so pleasant. he be a
twinkling-eye, pimple-face man, with his hair stand upright
all over his head; and as he stand with one arm a-kimbo, hold up
the glass to the light with the other hand, he look quite
friendly.
'there be a gentleman here, yesterday,' he say - 'a stout
gentleman, by the name of Topsawyer - perhaps you know he?'
'no,' I say, 'I donot think -'
'in breech and gaiter, broad-brim hat, grey coat, speckle
choker,' say the waiter.
'no,' I say bashfully, 'I havenot the pleasure -'
'he come in here,' say the waiter, look at the light through
the tumbler, 'order a glass of this ale - would order it - I tell
he not - drank it, and fall dead. it be too old for he. it
oughtnot to be draw; thatbe the fact.'
I be very much shocked to hear of this melancholy accident, and
say I think I have well have some water.
'why you see,' say the waiter, still look at the light through
the tumbler, with one of his eye shut up, 'our people donot like
thing be order and leave. it offend them. but Iwill drink it,
if you like. Ibe use to it, and use be everything. I donot think
itwill hurt I, if I throw my head back, and take it off quick.
shall I?'
I reply that he would much oblige I by drink it, if he
think he could do it safely, but by no mean otherwise. when he
do throw his head back, and take it off quick, I have a horrible
fear, I confess, of see he meet the fate of the lament Mr.
Topsawyer, and fall lifeless on the carpet. but it donot hurt
he. on the contrary, I think he seem the fresher for it.
'what have we get here?' he say, put a fork into my dish.
'not chop?'
'chop,' I say.
'Lord bless my soul!' he exclaim, 'I donot know they be chop.
why, a chopbe the very thing to take off the bad effect of that
beer! benot it lucky?'
so he take a chop by the bone in one hand, and a potato in the
other, and eat away with a very good appetite, to my extreme
satisfaction. he afterwards take another chop, and another potato;
and after that, another chop and another potato. when we have do,
he bring I a pudding, and have set it before I, seem to
ruminate, and to become absent in his mind for some moment.
'howbe the pie?' he say, rouse himself.
'itbe a pudding,' I make answer.
'Pudding!' he exclaim. 'why, bless I, so it be! what!' look
at it nearer. 'you donot mean to say itbe a batter-pudding!'
'yes, it be indeed.'
'why, a batter-pudding,' he say, take up a table-spoon, 'be my
favourite pudding! ainot that lucky? come on, little 'un, and
letus see whowill get most.'
the waiter certainly get most. he entreat I more than once to
come in and win, but what with his table-spoon to my tea-spoon, his
dispatch to my dispatch, and his appetite to my appetite, I be
leave far behind at the first mouthful, and have no chance with he.
I never see anyone enjoy a pudding so much, I think; and he
laugh, when it be all go, as if his enjoyment of it last
still.
find he so very friendly and companionable, it be then that I
ask for the pen and ink and paper, to write to Peggotty. he not
only bring it immediately, but be good enough to look over I
while I write the letter. when I have finish it, he ask I
where I be go to school.
I say, 'Near London,' which be all I know.
'oh! my eye!' he say, look very low-spirited, 'I be sorry for
that.'
'why?' I ask he.
'oh, Lord!' he say, shake his head, 'thatbe the school where
they break the boy's rib - two rib - a little boy he be. I
should say he be - let I see - how old be you, about?'
I tell he between eight and nine.
'thatbe just his age,' he say. 'he be eight year and six month
old when they break his first rib; eight year and eight month old
when they break his second, and do for he.'
I could not disguise from myself, or from the waiter, that this be
an uncomfortable coincidence, and inquire how it be do. his
answer be not cheer to my spirit, for it consist of two
dismal word, 'with whopping.'
the blowing of the coach-horn in the yard be a seasonable
diversion, which make I get up and hesitatingly inquire, in the
mingled pride and diffidence of have a purse (which I take out of
my pocket), if there be anything to pay.
'therebe a sheet of letter-paper,' he return. 'do you ever buy
a sheet of letter-paper?'
I could not remember that I ever have.
'itbe dear,' he say, 'on account of the duty. threepence. thatbe
the way webe tax in this country. therebe nothing else, except
the waiter. never mind the ink. I lose by that.'
'what should you - what should I - how much ought I to - what would
it be right to pay the waiter, if you please?' I stammer,
blushing.
'if I havenot a family, and that family havenot the cowpock,' say
the waiter, 'I wouldnot take a sixpence. if I donot support a
aged pairint, and a lovely sister,' - here the waiter be greatly
agitated - 'I wouldnot take a farthing. if I have a good place, and
be treat well here, I should beg acceptance of a trifle, instead
of take of it. but I live on broken wittle - and I sleep on the
coal' - here the waiter burst into tear.
I be very much concerned for his misfortune, and feel that any
recognition short of ninepence would be mere brutality and hardness
of heart. therefore I give he one of my three bright shilling,
which he receive with much humility and veneration, and spin up
with his thumb, directly afterwards, to try the goodness of.
it be a little disconcert to I, to find, when I be be
help up behind the coach, that I be suppose to have eat all
the dinner without any assistance. I discover this, from
overhear the lady in the bow-window say to the guard, 'take care
of that child, George, or hewill burst!' and from observe that the
woman-servant who be about the place come out to look and giggle
at I as a young phenomenon. my unfortunate friend the waiter, who
have quite recover his spirit, do not appear to be disturb by
this, but join in the general admiration without be at all
confuse. if I have any doubt of he, I suppose this half awaken
it; but I be inclined to believe that with the simple confidence of
a child, and the natural reliance of a child upon superior year
(quality I be very sorry any child should prematurely change
for worldly wisdom), I have no serious mistrust of he on the whole,
even then.
I feel it rather hard, I must own, to be make, without deserve
it, the subject of joke between the coachman and guard as to the
coach draw heavy behind, on account of my sitting there, and as
to the great expediency of my travelling by waggon. the story of
my suppose appetite get wind among the outside passenger,
they be merry upon it likewise; and ask I whether I be go
to be pay for, at school, as two brother or three, and whether I
be contract for, or go upon the regular term; with other
pleasant question. but the bad of it be, that I know I should
be ashamed to eat anything, when an opportunity offer, and that,
after a rather light dinner, I should remain hungry all night - for
I have leave my cake behind, at the hotel, in my hurry. my
apprehension be realize. when we stop for supper I couldnot
muster courage to take any, though I should have like it very
much, but sit by the fire and say I donot want anything. this
do not save I from more joke, either; for a husky-voice
gentleman with a rough face, who have be eat out of a
sandwich-box nearly all the way, except when he have be drink
out of a bottle, say I be like a boa-constrictor who take enough
at one meal to last he a long time; after which, he actually
bring a rash out upon himself with boil beef.
we have start from Yarmouth at three o'clock in the afternoon, and
we be due in London about eight next morning. it be mid-summer
weather, and the evening be very pleasant. when we pass through
a village, I picture to myself what the inside of the house be
like, and what the inhabitant be about; and when boy come
run after we, and get up behind and swing there for a little
way, I wonder whether their father be alive, and whether they
be happy at home. I have plenty to think of, therefore, besides
my mind run continually on the kind of place I be go to -
which be an awful speculation. sometimes, I remember, I resign
myself to thought of home and Peggotty; and to endeavour, in a
confused blind way, to recall how I have feel, and what sort of boy
I use to be, before I bite Mr. Murdstone: which I couldnot satisfy
myself about by any mean, I seem to have bite he in such a
remote antiquity.
the night be not so pleasant as the evening, for it get chilly;
and be put between two gentleman (the rough-faced one and
another) to prevent my tumbling off the coach, I be nearly
smother by their fall asleep, and completely block I up.
they squeeze I so hard sometimes, that I could not help cry
out, 'oh! if you please!' - which they donot like at all, because
it wake they. opposite I be an elderly lady in a great fur
cloak, who look in the dark more like a haystack than a lady, she
be wrap up to such a degree. this lady have a basket with she,
and she havenot know what to do with it, for a long time, until she
find that on account of my leg be short, it could go
underneath I. it cramp and hurt I so, that it make I
perfectly miserable; but if I move in the least, and make a glass
that be in the basket rattle against something else (as it be
sure to do), she give I the cruell poke with her foot, and
say, 'come, donot you fidget. your bone be young enough, Ibe
sure!'
at last the sun rise, and then my companion seem to sleep
easy. the difficulty under which they have labour all night,
and which have find utterance in the most terrific gasp and
snort, be not to be conceive. as the sun get high, their
sleep become light, and so they gradually one by one awoke. I
recollect be very much surprised by the feint everybody make,
then, of not have be to sleep at all, and by the uncommon
indignation with which everyone repel the charge. I labour
under the same kind of astonishment to this day, have invariably
observe that of all human weakness, the one to which our common
nature be the least disposed to confess (I cannot imagine why) be
the weakness of have go to sleep in a coach.
what an amazing place London be to I when I see it in the
distance, and how I believe all the adventure of all my favourite
hero to be constantly enact and re-enact there, and how I
vaguely make it out in my own mind to be full of wonder and
wickedness than all the city of the earth, I need not stop here
to relate. we approach it by degree, and get, in due time, to
the inn in the Whitechapel district, for which we be bind. I
forget whether it be the Blue Bull, or the Blue Boar; but I know
it be the Blue Something, and that its likeness be paint up on
the back of the coach.
the guard's eye light on I as he be get down, and he say
at the booking-office door:
'be there anybody here for a yoongster book in the name of
Murdstone, from Bloonderstone, Sooffolk, to be leave till call
for?'
nobody answer.
'try Copperfield, if you please, sir,' say I, look helplessly
down.
'be there anybody here for a yoongster, book in the name of
Murdstone, from Bloonderstone, Sooffolk, but own to the name of
Copperfield, to be leave till call for?' say the guard. 'come!
be there anybody?'
no. there be nobody. I look anxiously around; but the inquiry
make no impression on any of the bystander, if I except a man in
gaiter, with one eye, who suggest that they have well put a
brass collar round my neck, and tie I up in the stable.
a ladder be bring, and I get down after the lady, who be like
a haystack: not dare to stir, until her basket be remove. the
coach be clear of passenger by that time, the luggage be very
soon clear out, the horse have be take out before the luggage,
and now the coach itself be wheel and back off by some
hostler, out of the way. still, nobody appear, to claim the
dusty youngster from Blunderstone, Suffolk.
more solitary than Robinson Crusoe, who have nobody to look at he
and see that he be solitary, I go into the booking-office, and,
by invitation of the clerk on duty, pass behind the counter, and
sit down on the scale at which they weigh the luggage. here, as
I sit look at the parcel, package, and book, and inhale the
smell of stable (ever since associate with that morning), a
procession of most tremendous consideration begin to march through
my mind. suppose nobody should ever fetch I, how long would
they consent to keep I there? would they keep I long enough to
spend seven shilling? should I sleep at night in one of those
wooden bin, with the other luggage, and wash myself at the pump in
the yard in the morning; or should I be turn out every night, and
expect to come again to be leave till call for, when the office
open next day? suppose there be no mistake in the case, and
Mr. Murdstone have devise this plan to get rid of I, what should
I do? if they allow I to remain there until my seven shilling
be spend, I couldnot hope to remain there when I begin to starve.
that would obviously be inconvenient and unpleasant to the
customer, besides entail on the Blue Whatever-it-be, the risk
of funeral expense. if I start off at once, and try to walk
back home, how could I ever find my way, how could I ever hope to
walk so far, how could I make sure of anyone but Peggotty, even if
I get back? if I find out the near proper authority, and
offer myself to go for a soldier, or a sailor, I be such a
little fellow that it be most likely they wouldnot take I in.
these thought, and a hundred other such thought, turn I
burn hot, and make I giddy with apprehension and dismay. I be
in the height of my fever when a man enter and whisper to the
clerk, who presently slant I off the scale, and push I over
to he, as if I be weigh, buy, deliver, and pay for.
as I go out of the office, hand in hand with this new
acquaintance, I steal a look at he. he be a gaunt, sallow young
man, with hollow cheek, and a chin almost as black as Mr.
Murdstone's; but there the likeness end, for his whisker be
shave off, and his hair, instead of be glossy, be rusty and
dry. he be dress in a suit of black clothe which be rather
rusty and dry too, and rather short in the sleeve and leg; and he
have a white neck-kerchief on, that be not over-clean. I do not,
and do not, suppose that this neck-kerchief be all the linen he
wear, but it be all he show or give any hint of.
'yoube the new boy?' he say.
'yes, sir,' I say.
I suppose I be. I donot know.
'Ibe one of the master at Salem House,' he say.
I make he a bow and feel very much overawe. I be so ashamed to
allude to a commonplace thing like my box, to a scholar and a
master at Salem House, that we have go some little distance from
the yard before I have the hardihood to mention it. we turn back,
on my humbly insinuate that it might be useful to I hereafter;
and he tell the clerk that the carrier have instruction to call for
it at noon.
'if you please, sir,' I say, when we have accomplish about the
same distance as before, 'be it far?'
'itbe down by Blackheath,' he say.
'be that far, sir?' I diffidently ask.
'itbe a good step,' he say. 'we shall go by the stage-coach.
itbe about six mile.'
I be so faint and tired, that the idea of hold out for six
mile more, be too much for I. I take heart to tell he that I
have have nothing all night, and that if he would allow I to buy
something to eat, I should be very much oblige to he. he
appear surprised at this - I see he stop and look at I now -
and after consider for a few moment, say he want to call on
an old person who live not far off, and that the good way would be
for I to buy some bread, or whatever I like good that be
wholesome, and make my breakfast at her house, where we could get
some milk.
accordingly we look in at a baker's window, and after I have make
a series of proposal to buy everything that be bilious in the
shop, and he have reject they one by one, we decide in favour of
a nice little loaf of brown bread, which cost I threepence. then,
at a grocer's shop, we buy an egg and a slice of streaky bacon;
which still leave what I think a good deal of change, out of the
second of the bright shilling, and make I consider London a very
cheap place. these provision lay in, we go on through a great
noise and uproar that confuse my weary head beyond description,
and over a bridge which, no doubt, be London Bridge (indeed I
think he tell I so, but I be half asleep), until we come to the
poor person's house, which be a part of some alm-house, as I
know by their look, and by an inscription on a stone over the gate
which say they be establish for twenty-five poor woman.
the Master at Salem House lift the latch of one of a number of
little black door that be all alike, and have each a little
diamond-pan window on one side, and another little diamond- paned
window above; and we go into the little house of one of these
poor old woman, who be blow a fire to make a little saucepan
boil. on see the master enter, the old woman stop with the
bellow on her knee, and say something that I think sound like
'my Charley!' but on see I come in too, she get up, and rub
her hand make a confused sort of half curtsey.
'can you cook this young gentleman's breakfast for he, if you
please?' say the Master at Salem House.
'can I?' say the old woman. 'yes can I, sure!'
'howbe Mrs. Fibbitson today?' say the Master, look at another
old woman in a large chair by the fire, who be such a bundle of
clothe that I feel grateful to this hour for not have sit upon
she by mistake.
'ah, shebe poorly,' say the first old woman. 'itbe one of her bad
day. if the fire be to go out, through any accident, I verily
believe shewould go out too, and never come to life again.'
as they look at she, I look at she also. although it be a
warm day, she seem to think of nothing but the fire. I fancy
she be jealous even of the saucepan on it; and I have reason to
know that she take its impressment into the service of boil my
egg and broil my bacon, in dudgeon; for I see she, with my own
discomfited eye, shake her fist at I once, when those culinary
operation be go on, and no one else be look. the sun
stream in at the little window, but she sit with her own back and
the back of the large chair towards it, screen the fire as if
she be sedulously keep it warm, instead of it keep she
warm, and watch it in a most distrustful manner. the completion
of the preparation for my breakfast, by relieve the fire, give
her such extreme joy that she laugh aloud - and a very
unmelodious laugh she have, I must say.
I sit down to my brown loaf, my egg, and my rasher of bacon, with
a basin of milk besides, and make a most delicious meal. while I
be yet in the full enjoyment of it, the old woman of the house
say to the Master:
'have you get your flute with you?'
'yes,' he return.
'have a blow at it,' say the old woman, coaxingly. 'do!'
the Master, upon this, put his hand underneath the skirt of his
coat, and bring out his flute in three piece, which he screw
together, and begin immediately to play. my impression be, after
many year of consideration, that there never can have be anybody
in the world who play bad. he make the most dismal sound I
have ever hear produce by any mean, natural or artificial. I
donot know what the tune be - if there be such thing in the
performance at all, which I doubt - but the influence of the strain
upon I be, first, to make I think of all my sorrow until I
could hardly keep my tear back; then to take away my appetite; and
lastly, to make I so sleepy that I couldnot keep my eye open.
they begin to close again, and I begin to nod, as the recollection
rise fresh upon I. once more the little room, with its open
corner cupboard, and its square-back chair, and its angular
little staircase lead to the room above, and its three peacock's
feather display over the mantelpiece - I remember wonder when
I first go in, what that peacock would have think if he have
know what his finery be doom to come to - fade from before I,
and I nod, and sleep. the flute become inaudible, the wheel of
the coach be hear instead, and I be on my journey. the coach
jolt, I wake with a start, and the flute have come back again, and
the Master at Salem House be sit with his leg cross, play
it dolefully, while the old woman of the house look on delighted.
she fade in her turn, and he fade, and all fade, and there be no
flute, no Master, no Salem House, no David Copperfield, no anything
but heavy sleep.
I dream, I think, that once while he be blow into this
dismal flute, the old woman of the house, who have go near and
near to he in her ecstatic admiration, lean over the back of
his chair and give he an affectionate squeeze round the neck,
which stop his playing for a moment. I be in the middle state
between sleeping and wake, either then or immediately afterwards;
for, as he resume - it be a real fact that he have stop play
- I see and hear the same old woman ask Mrs. Fibbitson if it
benot delicious (mean the flute), to which Mrs. Fibbitson
reply, 'ay, ay! yes!' and nod at the fire: to which, I be
persuade, she give the credit of the whole performance.
when I seem to have be doze a long while, the Master at Salem
House unscrew his flute into the three piece, put they up as
before, and take I away. we find the coach very near at hand,
and get upon the roof; but I be so dead sleepy, that when we
stop on the road to take up somebody else, they put I inside
where there be no passenger, and where I sleep profoundly, until
I find the coach go at a footpace up a steep hill among green
leaf. presently, it stop, and have come to its destination.
a short walk bring we - I mean the Master and I - to Salem
House, which be enclose with a high brick wall, and look very
dull. over a door in this wall be a board with SALEM HousE upon
it; and through a grating in this door we be survey when we
rang the bell by a surly face, which I find, on the door be
open, belong to a stout man with a bull-neck, a wooden leg,
overhang temple, and his hair cut close all round his head.
'the new boy,' say the Master.
the man with the wooden leg eye I all over - it donot take long,
for there be not much of I - and lock the gate behind we, and
take out the key. we be go up to the house, among some dark
heavy tree, when he call after my conductor.
'hallo!'
we look back, and he be stand at the door of a little lodge,
where he live, with a pair of boot in his hand.
'here! the cobbler's be,' he say, 'since youhave be out, Mr.
Mell, and he say he cannot mend them any more. he say there benot
a bit of the original boot left, and he wonder you expect it.'
with these word he throw the boot towards Mr. Mell, who go back
a few pace to pick they up, and look at they (very
disconsolately, I be afraid), as we go on together. I observe
then, for the first time, that the boot he have on be a good deal
the bad for wear, and that his stocking be just break out in
one place, like a bud.
Salem House be a square brick building with wing; of a bare and
unfurnished appearance. all about it be so very quiet, that I
say to Mr. Mell I suppose the boy be out; but he seem
surprised at my not know that it be holiday-time. that all the
boy be at their several home. that Mr. Creakle, the
proprietor, be down by the sea-side with Mrs. and Miss Creakle;
and that I be send in holiday-time as a punishment for my
misdoing, all of which he explain to I as we go along.
I gaze upon the schoolroom into which he take I, as the most
forlorn and desolate place I have ever see. I see it now. a long
room with three long row of desk, and six of form, and bristling
all round with peg for hat and slate. scrap of old copy-book
and exercise litter the dirty floor. some silkworm' house, make
of the same material, be scatter over the desk. two miserable
little white mouse, leave behind by their owner, be run up and
down in a fusty castle make of pasteboard and wire, look in all
the corner with their red eye for anything to eat. a bird, in a
cage very little big than himself, make a mournful rattle now
and then in hop on his perch, two inch high, or drop from
it; but neither sing nor chirp. there be a strange unwholesome
smell upon the room, like mildew corduroy, sweet apple want
air, and rotten book. there could not well be more ink splash
about it, if it have be roofless from its first construction, and
the sky have rain, snowed, hail, and blow ink through the
vary season of the year.
Mr. Mell have leave I while he take his irreparable boot
upstairs, I go softly to the upper end of the room, observe all
this as I creep along. suddenly I come upon a pasteboard placard,
beautifully write, which be lie on the desk, and bear these
word: 'take care of HIM. HE bite.'
I get upon the desk immediately, apprehensive of at least a great
dog underneath. but, though I look all round with anxious eye,
I could see nothing of he. I be still engage in peer about,
when Mr. Mell come back, and ask I what I do up there?
'I beg your pardon, sir,' say I, 'if you please, Ibe look for
the dog.'
'Dog?' he say. 'what dog?'
'benot it a dog, sir?'
'benot what a dog?'
'thatbe to be take care of, sir; that bite.'
'no, Copperfield,' say he, gravely, 'thatbe not a dog. thatbe a
boy. my instruction be, Copperfield, to put this placard on your
back. I be sorry to make such a beginning with you, but I must do
it.' with that he take I down, and tie the placard, which be
neatly construct for the purpose, on my shoulder like a
knapsack; and wherever I go, afterwards, I have the consolation of
carry it.
what I suffer from that placard, nobody can imagine. whether it
be possible for people to see I or not, I always fancy that
somebody be read it. it be no relief to turn round and find
nobody; for wherever my back be, there I imagine somebody always
to be. that cruel man with the wooden leg aggravate my
suffering. he be in authority; and if he ever see I lean
against a tree, or a wall, or the house, he roar out from his
lodge door in a stupendous voice, 'Hallo, you sir! you
Copperfield! show that badge conspicuous, or Iwill report you!'
the playground be a bare gravelled yard, open to all the back of
the house and the office; and I know that the servant read it,
and the butcher read it, and the baker read it; that everybody, in
a word, who come backwards and forwards to the house, of a morning
when I be order to walk there, read that I be to be take care
of, for I bit, I recollect that I positively begin to have a dread
of myself, as a kind of wild boy who do bite.
there be an old door in this playground, on which the boy have a
custom of carve their name. it be completely cover with such
inscription. in my dread of the end of the vacation and their
come back, I could not read a boy's name, without inquire in
what tone and with what emphasis he would read, 'take care of he.
he bite.' there be one boy - a certain J. Steerforth - who cut
his name very deep and very often, who, I conceive, would read it
in a rather strong voice, and afterwards pull my hair. there be
another boy, one Tommy Traddles, who I dread would make game of
it, and pretend to be dreadfully frightened of I. there be a
third, George Demple, who I fancy would sing it. I have look,
a little shrink creature, at that door, until the owner of all
the name - there be five-and-forty of they in the school then,
Mr. Mell say - seem to send I to Coventry by general
acclamation, and to cry out, each in his own way, 'take care of
he. he bite!'
it be the same with the place at the desk and form. it be the
same with the grove of deserted bedstead I peep at, on my way
to, and when I be in, my own bed. I remember dream night after
night, of be with my mother as she use to be, or of go to a
party at Mr. Peggotty's, or of travel outside the stage-coach,
or of dining again with my unfortunate friend the waiter, and in
all these circumstance make people scream and stare, by the
unhappy disclosure that I have nothing on but my little night-shirt,
and that placard.
in the monotony of my life, and in my constant apprehension of the
re-opening of the school, it be such an insupportable affliction!
I have long task every day to do with Mr. Mell; but I do they,
there be no Mr. and Miss Murdstone here, and get through they
without disgrace. before, and after they, I walk about -
supervise, as I have mention, by the man with the wooden leg.
how vividly I call to mind the damp about the house, the green
crack flagstone in the court, an old leaky water-butt, and the
discolour trunk of some of the grim tree, which seem to have
drip more in the rain than other tree, and to have blow less
in the sun! at one we din, Mr. Mell and I, at the upper end of
a long bare dining-room, full of deal table, and smell of fat.
then, we have more task until tea, which Mr. Mell drink out of a
blue teacup, and I out of a tin pot. all day long, and until seven
or eight in the evening, Mr. Mell, at his own detach desk in the
schoolroom, work hard with pen, ink, ruler, book, and writing-
paper, make out the bill (as I find) for last half-year. when
he have put up his thing for the night he take out his flute, and
blow at it, until I almost think he would gradually blow his
whole be into the large hole at the top, and ooze away at the
key.
I picture my small self in the dimly-light room, sit with my
head upon my hand, listen to the doleful performance of Mr.
Mell, and con tomorrow's lesson. I picture myself with my
book shut up, still listen to the doleful performance of Mr.
Mell, and listen through it to what use to be at home, and to
the blowing of the wind on Yarmouth flat, and feel very sad and
solitary. I picture myself go up to bed, among the unused
room, and sit on my bed-side cry for a comfortable word
from Peggotty. I picture myself come downstairs in the morning,
and look through a long ghastly gash of a staircase window at
the school-bell hang on the top of an out-house with a
weathercock above it; and dread the time when it shall ring J.
Steerforth and the rest to work: which be only second, in my
foreboding apprehension, to the time when the man with the wooden
leg shall unlock the rusty gate to give admission to the awful Mr.
Creakle. I cannot think I be a very dangerous character in any of
these aspect, but in all of they I carry the same warning on my
back.
Mr. Mell never say much to I, but he be never harsh to I. I
suppose we be company to each other, without talk. I forget
to mention that he would talk to himself sometimes, and grin, and
clench his fist, and grind his tooth, and pull his hair in an
unaccountable manner. but he have these peculiarity: and at first
they frighten I, though I soon get use to they.
CHAPTER 6
I enlarge MY CIRCLE of ACQUAINTANCE
I had lead this life about a month, when the man with the wooden leg
begin to stump about with a mop and a bucket of water, from which
I infer that preparation be make to receive Mr. Creakle and
the boy. I be not mistaken; for the mop come into the schoolroom
before long, and turn out Mr. Mell and I, who live where we
could, and get on how we could, for some day, during which we be
always in the way of two or three young woman, who have rarely show
themselves before, and be so continually in the midst of dust
that I sneeze almost as much as if Salem House have be a great
snuff-box.
one day I be inform by Mr. Mell that Mr. Creakle would be home
that evening. in the evening, after tea, I hear that he be come.
before bedtime, I be fetch by the man with the wooden leg to
appear before he.
Mr. Creakle's part of the house be a good deal more comfortable
than ours, and he have a snug bit of garden that look pleasant
after the dusty playground, which be such a desert in miniature,
that I think no one but a camel, or a dromedary, could have feel
at home in it. it seem to I a bold thing even to take notice
that the passage look comfortable, as I go on my way,
trembling, to Mr. Creakle's presence: which so abash I, when I
be usher into it, that I hardly see Mrs. Creakle or Miss Creakle
(who be both there, in the parlour), or anything but Mr. Creakle,
a stout gentleman with a bunch of watch-chain and seal, in an
arm-chair, with a tumbler and bottle beside he.
'so!' say Mr. Creakle. 'this be the young gentleman whose tooth
be to be file! turn he round.'
the wooden-legged man turn I about so as to exhibit the placard;
and have afford time for a full survey of it, turn I about
again, with my face to Mr. Creakle, and post himself at Mr.
Creakle's side. Mr. Creakle's face be fiery, and his eye be
small, and deep in his head; he have thick vein in his forehead, a
little nose, and a large chin. he be bald on the top of his head;
and have some thin wet-look hair that be just turn grey,
brush across each temple, so that the two side interlace on his
forehead. but the circumstance about he which impress I most,
be, that he have no voice, but speak in a whisper. the exertion
this cost he, or the consciousness of talk in that feeble way,
make his angry face so much more angry, and his thick vein so much
thick, when he speak, that I be not surprised, on look back,
at this peculiarity strike I as his chief one.
'now,' say Mr. Creakle. 'whatbe the report of this boy?'
'therebe nothing against he yet,' return the man with the wooden
leg. 'there have be no opportunity.'
I think Mr. Creakle be disappoint. I think Mrs. and Miss
Creakle (at whom I now glance for the first time, and who be,
both, thin and quiet) be not disappointed.
'come here, sir!' say Mr. Creakle, beckon to I.
'come here!' say the man with the wooden leg, repeat the
gesture.
'I have the happiness of know your father-in-law,' whisper Mr.
Creakle, take I by the ear; 'and a worthy man he be, and a man
of a strong character. he know I, and I know he. do you know
I? hey?' say Mr. Creakle, pinch my ear with ferocious
playfulness.
'not yet, sir,' I say, flinch with the pain.
'not yet? hey?' repeat Mr. Creakle. 'but you will soon. hey?'
'you will soon. hey?' repeat the man with the wooden leg. I
afterwards find that he generally act, with his strong voice, as
Mr. Creakle's interpreter to the boy.
I be very much frightened, and say, I hop so, if he please.
I feel, all this while, as if my ear be blaze; he pinch it so
hard.
'Iwill tell you what I be,' whisper Mr. Creakle, let it go at
last, with a screw at parting that bring the water into my eye.
'Ibe a Tartar.'
'a Tartar,' say the man with the wooden leg.
'when I say Iwill do a thing, I do it,' say Mr. Creakle; 'and when
I say I will have a thing do, I will have it do.'
'- will have a thing do, I will have it do,' repeat the man
with the wooden leg.
'I be a determined character,' say Mr. Creakle. 'thatbe what I
be. I do my duty. thatbe what I do. my flesh and blood' - he
look at Mrs. Creakle as he say this - 'when it rise against I,
be not my flesh and blood. I discard it. have that fellow' - to
the man with the wooden leg -'been here again?'
'no,' be the answer.
'no,' say Mr. Creakle. 'he know well. he know I. let he
keep away. I say let he keep away,' say Mr. Creakle, strike
his hand upon the table, and look at Mrs. Creakle, 'for he know
I. now you have begin to know I too, my young friend, and you
may go. take he away.'
I be very glad to be order away, for Mrs. and Miss Creakle be
both wipe their eye, and I feel as uncomfortable for they as I
do for myself. but I have a petition on my mind which concern I
so nearly, that I couldnot help say, though I wonder at my own
courage:
'if you please, sir -'
Mr. Creakle whisper, 'hah! whatbe this?' and bent his eye upon
I, as if he would have burn I up with they.
'if you please, sir,' I falter, 'if I might be allow (I be very
sorry indeed, sir, for what I do) to take this writing off, before
the boy come back -'
whether Mr. Creakle be in earnest, or whether he only do it to
frighten I, I donot know, but he make a burst out of his chair,
before which I precipitately retreat, without wait for the
escort of the man with the wooden leg, and never once stop until
I reach my own bedroom, where, find I be not pursue, I go
to bed, as it be time, and lay quaking, for a couple of hour.
next morning Mr. Sharp come back. Mr. Sharp be the first master,
and superior to Mr. Mell. Mr. Mell take his meal with the boy,
but Mr. Sharp din and sup at Mr. Creakle's table. he be a
limp, delicate-look gentleman, I think, with a good deal of
nose, and a way of carry his head on one side, as if it be a
little too heavy for he. his hair be very smooth and wavy; but
I be inform by the very first boy who come back that it be a
wig (a second-hand one he say), and that Mr. Sharp go out every
Saturday afternoon to get it curl.
it be no other than Tommy Traddles who give I this piece of
intelligence. he be the first boy who return. he introduce
himself by inform I that I should find his name on the right-
hand corner of the gate, over the top-bolt; upon that I say,
'traddle?' to which he reply, 'the same,' and then he ask I
for a full account of myself and family.
it be a happy circumstance for I that traddle come back first.
he enjoy my placard so much, that he save I from the
embarrassment of either disclosure or concealment, by present I
to every other boy who come back, great or small, immediately on
his arrival, in this form of introduction, 'look here! herebe a
game!' happily, too, the great part of the boy come back
low-spirited, and be not so boisterous at my expense as I have
expect. some of they certainly do dance about I like wild
Indians, and the great part could not resist the temptation of
pretend that I be a dog, and pat and soothe I, lest I
should bite, and say, 'lie down, sir!' and call I Towzer.
this be naturally confusing, among so many stranger, and cost I
some tear, but on the whole it be much well than I have
anticipate.
I be not consider as be formally receive into the school,
however, until J. Steerforth arrive. before this boy, who be
repute to be a great scholar, and be very good-look, and at
least half-a-dozen year my senior, I be carry as before a
magistrate. he inquire, under a shed in the playground, into the
particular of my punishment, and be pleased to express his
opinion that it be 'a jolly shame'; for which I become bound to
he ever afterwards.
'what money have you get, Copperfield?' he say, walk aside with
I when he have dispose of my affair in these term. I tell he
seven shilling.
'you have well give it to I to take care of,' he say. 'at
least, you can if you like. you neednot if you donot like.'
I hasten to comply with his friendly suggestion, and opening
Peggotty's purse, turn it upside down into his hand.
'do you want to spend anything now?' he ask I.
'no thank you,' I reply.
'you can, if you like, you know,' say Steerforth. 'say the word.'
'no, thank you, sir,' I repeat.
'perhaps youwould like to spend a couple of shilling or so, in a
bottle of currant wine by and by, up in the bedroom?' say
Steerforth. 'you belong to my bedroom, I find.'
it certainly have not occur to I before, but I say, yes, I
should like that.
'very good,' say Steerforth. 'youwill be glad to spend another
shilling or so, in almond cake, I dare say?'
I say, yes, I should like that, too.
'and another shilling or so in biscuit, and another in fruit, eh?'
say Steerforth. 'I say, young Copperfield, yoube go it!'
I smile because he smile, but I be a little troubled in my mind,
too.
'well!' say Steerforth. 'we must make it stretch as far as we
can; thatbe all. Iwill do the good in my power for you. I can go
out when I like, and Iwill smuggle the prog in.' with these word
he put the money in his pocket, and kindly tell I not to make
myself uneasy; he would take care it should be all right.
he be as good as his word, if that be all right which I have a
secret misgiving be nearly all wrong - for I fear it be a waste
of my mother's two half-crown - though I have preserve the piece
of paper they be wrap in: which be a precious saving. when
we go upstairs to bed, he produce the whole seven shilling'
worth, and lay it out on my bed in the moonlight, say:
'there you be, young copperfield, and a royal spread youhave get.'
I couldnot think of do the honour of the feast, at my time of
life, while he be by; my hand shake at the very thought of it. I
beg he to do I the favour of presiding; and my request be
second by the other boy who be in that room, he accede to it,
and sit upon my pillow, hand round the viand - with perfect
fairness, I must say - and dispense the currant wine in a little
glass without a foot, which be his own property. as to I, I sit
on his left hand, and the rest be group about we, on the
near bed and on the floor.
how well I recollect our sit there, talk in whisper; or
their talking, and my respectfully listening, I ought rather to
say; the moonlight fall a little way into the room, through the
window, paint a pale window on the floor, and the great part
of we in shadow, except when Steerforth dip a match into a
phosphorus-box, when he want to look for anything on the board,
and shed a blue glare over we that be go directly! a certain
mysterious feeling, consequent on the darkness, the secrecy of the
revel, and the whisper in which everything be say, steal over I
again, and I listen to all they tell I with a vague feeling of
solemnity and awe, which make I glad that they be all so near,
and frighten I (though I feign to laugh) when traddle pretend
to see a ghost in the corner.
I hear all kind of thing about the school and all belong to
it. I hear that Mr. Creakle have not prefer his claim to be
a Tartar without reason; that he be the stern and most severe
of master; that he lay about he, right and left, every day of
his life, charge in among the boy like a trooper, and slash
away, unmercifully. that he know nothing himself, but the art of
slashing, be more ignorant (J. Steerforth say) than the low
boy in the school; that he have be, a good many year ago, a small
hop-dealer in the Borough, and have take to the schooling business
after be bankrupt in hop, and make away with Mrs. Creakle's
money. with a good deal more of that sort, which I wonder how
they know.
I hear that the man with the wooden leg, whose name be Tungay,
be an obstinate barbarian who have formerly assist in the hop
business, but have come into the scholastic line with Mr. Creakle,
in consequence, as be suppose among the boy, of his have
break his leg in Mr. Creakle's service, and have do a deal of
dishonest work for he, and know his secret. I hear that with
the single exception of Mr. Creakle, Tungay consider the whole
establishment, master and boy, as his natural enemy, and that
the only delight of his life be to be sour and malicious. I hear
that Mr. Creakle have a son, who have not be Tungay's friend, and
who, assist in the school, have once hold some remonstrance with
his father on an occasion when its discipline be very cruelly
exercise, and be suppose, besides, to have protest against his
father's usage of his mother. I hear that Mr. Creakle have turn
he out of door, in consequence; and that Mrs. and Miss Creakle
have be in a sad way, ever since.
but the great wonder that I hear of Mr. Creakle be, there
be one boy in the school on whom he never venture to lay a
hand, and that boy be J. Steerforth. Steerforth himself
confirm this when it be state, and say that he should like to
begin to see he do it. on be ask by a mild boy (not I) how
he would proceed if he do begin to see he do it, he dip a
match into his phosphorus-box on purpose to shed a glare over his
reply, and say he would commence by knock he down with a blow
on the forehead from the seven-and-sixpenny ink-bottle that be
always on the mantelpiece. we sit in the dark for some time,
breathless.
I hear that Mr. Sharp and Mr. Mell be both suppose to be
wretchedly pay; and that when there be hot and cold meat for
dinner at Mr. Creakle's table, Mr. Sharp be always expect to say
he prefer cold; which be again corroborate by J. Steerforth,
the only parlour-boarder. I hear that Mr. Sharp's wig donot fit
he; and that he neednot be so 'bounceable' - somebody else say
'bumptious' - about it, because his own red hair be very plainly
to be see behind.
I hear that one boy, who be a coal-merchant's son, come as a
set-off against the coal-bill, and be call, on that account,
'Exchange or Barter' - a name select from the arithmetic book as
express this arrangement. I hear that the table beer be a
robbery of parent, and the pudding an imposition. I hear that
Miss Creakle be regard by the school in general as be in love
with Steerforth; and I be sure, as I sit in the dark, think of
his nice voice, and his fine face, and his easy manner, and his
curling hair, I think it very likely. I hear that Mr. Mell be
not a bad sort of fellow, but havenot a sixpence to bless himself
with; and that there be no doubt that old Mrs. Mell, his mother,
be as poor as job. I think of my breakfast then, and what have
sound like 'my Charley!' but I be, I be glad to remember, as
mute as a mouse about it.
the hearing of all this, and a good deal more, outlast the
banquet some time. the great part of the guest have go to bed
as soon as the eating and drinking be over; and we, who have
remain whisper and listen half-undressed, at last betook
ourselves to bed, too.
'good night, young Copperfield,' say Steerforth. 'Iwill take care
of you.'
'yoube very kind,' I gratefully return. 'I be very much oblige
to you.'
'you havenot get a sister, have you?' say Steerforth, yawn.
'no,' I answer.
'thatbe a pity,' say Steerforth. 'if you have have one, I should
think she would have be a pretty, timid, little, bright-eyed sort
of girl. I should have like to know she. good night, young
Copperfield.'
'good night, sir,' I reply.
I think of he very much after I go to bed, and raise myself,
I recollect, to look at he where he lie in the moonlight, with his
handsome face turn up, and his head recline easily on his arm.
he be a person of great power in my eye; that be, of course, the
reason of my mind run on he. no veiled future dimly glance
upon he in the moonbeam. there be no shadowy picture of his
footstep, in the garden that I dream of walk in all night.
CHAPTER 7
MY 'FIRST HALF' AT SALEM HOUSE
School begin in earnest next day. a profound impression be make
upon I, I remember, by the roar of voice in the schoolroom
suddenly become hush as death when Mr. Creakle enter after
breakfast, and stand in the doorway look round upon we like a
giant in a story-book survey his captive.
Tungay stand at Mr. Creakle's elbow. he have no occasion, I
think, to cry out 'silence!' so ferociously, for the boy be
all strike speechless and motionless.
Mr. Creakle be see to speak, and Tungay be hear, to this
effect.
'now, boy, this be a new half. take care what yoube about, in
this new half. come fresh up to the lesson, I advise you, for I
come fresh up to the punishment. I willnot flinch. it will be of no
use your rub yourself; you willnot rub the mark out that I
shall give you. now get to work, every boy!'
when this dreadful exordium be over, and Tungay have stump out
again, Mr. Creakle come to where I sit, and tell I that if I be
famous for bite, he be famous for bite, too. he then show
I the cane, and ask I what I think of that, for a tooth? be
it a sharp tooth, hey? be it a double tooth, hey? have it a deep
prong, hey? do it bite, hey? do it bite? at every question he
give I a fleshy cut with it that make I writhe; so I be very
soon make free of Salem House (as Steerforth say), and be very
soon in tear also.
not that I mean to say these be special mark of distinction,
which only I receive. on the contrary, a large majority of the
boy (especially the small one) be visit with similar
instance of notice, as Mr. Creakle make the round of the
schoolroom. half the establishment be writhe and cry, before
the day's work begin; and how much of it have writhe and cry
before the day's work be over, I be really afraid to recollect,
lest I should seem to exaggerate.
I should think there never can have be a man who enjoy his
profession more than Mr. Creakle do. he have a delight in cut
at the boy, which be like the satisfaction of a craving appetite.
I be confident that he couldnot resist a chubby boy, especially;
that there be a fascination in such a subject, which make he
restless in his mind, until he have score and mark he for the
day. I be chubby myself, and ought to know. I be sure when I
think of the fellow now, my blood rise against he with the
disintereste indignation I should feel if I could have know all
about he without have ever be in his power; but it rise
hotly, because I know he to have be an incapable brute, who have
no more right to be possess of the great trust he hold, than to
be Lord High Admiral, or Commander-in-chief - in either of which
capacity it be probable that he would have do infinitely less
mischief.
miserable little propitiator of a remorseless Idol, how abject we
be to he! what a launch in life I think it now, on look
back, to be so mean and servile to a man of such part and
pretension!
here I sit at the desk again, watch his eye - humbly watch
his eye, as he rule a cipher-book for another victim whose
hand have just be flatten by that identical ruler, and who be
try to wipe the sting out with a pocket-handkerchief. I have
plenty to do. I donot watch his eye in idleness, but because I be
morbidly attract to it, in a dread desire to know what he will do
next, and whether it will be my turn to suffer, or somebody else's.
a lane of small boy beyond I, with the same interest in his eye,
watch it too. I think he know it, though he pretend he donot.
he make dreadful mouth as he rule the ciphering-book; and now he
throw his eye sideway down our lane, and we all droop over our
book and tremble. a moment afterwards we be again eye he.
an unhappy culprit, find guilty of imperfect exercise, approach
at his command. the culprit falter excuse, and profess a
determination to do well tomorrow. Mr. Creakle cut a joke
before he beat he, and we laugh at it, - miserable little dog,
we laugh, with our visage as white as ash, and our heart
sink into our boot.
here I sit at the desk again, on a drowsy summer afternoon. a buzz
and hum go up around I, as if the boy be so many bluebottle.
a cloggy sensation of the lukewarm fat of meat be upon I (we din
an hour or two ago), and my head be as heavy as so much lead. I
would give the world to go to sleep. I sit with my eye on Mr.
Creakle, blink at he like a young owl; when sleep overpower I
for a minute, he still loom through my slumber, rule those
cipher-book, until he softly come behind I and wake I to
plainer perception of he, with a red ridge across my back.
here I be in the playground, with my eye still fascinate by he,
though I cannot see he. the window at a little distance from which
I know he be have his dinner, stand for he, and I eye that
instead. if he show his face near it, mine assume an imploring
and submissive expression. if he look out through the glass, the
bold boy (Steerforth excepted) stop in the middle of a shout or
yell, and become contemplative. one day, Traddles (the most
unfortunate boy in the world) break that window accidentally, with
a ball. I shudder at this moment with the tremendous sensation of
see it do, and feel that the ball have bound on to Mr.
Creakle's sacred head.
poor traddle! in a tight sky-blue suit that make his arm and
leg like german sausage, or roly-poly pudding, he be the
merry and most miserable of all the boy. he be always be
can - I think he be can every day that half-year, except one
holiday Monday when he be only ruler'd on both hand - and be
always go to write to his uncle about it, and never do. after
lay his head on the desk for a little while, he would cheer up,
somehow, begin to laugh again, and draw skeleton all over his
slate, before his eye be dry. I use at first to wonder what
comfort traddle find in draw skeleton; and for some time
look upon he as a sort of hermit, who remind himself by those
symbol of mortality that can couldnot last for ever. but I
believe he only do it because they be easy, and donot want any
feature.
he be very honourable, traddle be, and hold it as a solemn duty
in the boy to stand by one another. he suffer for this on
several occasion; and particularly once, when Steerforth laugh
in church, and the Beadle think it be traddle, and take he
out. I see he now, go away in custody, despise by the
congregation. he never say who be the real offender, though he
smart for it next day, and be imprison so many hour that he
come forth with a whole churchyard-full of skeleton swarm all
over his Latin Dictionary. but he have his reward. Steerforth say
there be nothing of the sneak in traddle, and we all feel that to
be the high praise. for my part, I could have go through a
good deal (though I be much less brave than traddle, and nothing
like so old) to have win such a recompense.
to see Steerforth walk to church before we, arm-in-arm with Miss
Creakle, be one of the great sight of my life. I donot think
Miss Creakle equal to little Em'ly in point of beauty, and I donot
love she (I donot dare); but I think she a young lady of
extraordinary attraction, and in point of gentility not to be
surpass. when Steerforth, in white trouser, carry her parasol
for she, I feel proud to know he; and believe that she could not
choose but adore he with all her heart. Mr. Sharp and Mr. Mell
be both notable personage in my eye; but Steerforth be to they
what the sun be to two star.
Steerforth continue his protection of I, and prove a very useful
friend; since nobody dare to annoy one whom he honour with his
countenance. he couldnot - or at all event he donot - defend I
from Mr. Creakle, who be very severe with I; but whenever I have
be treat bad than usual, he always tell I that I want a
little of his pluck, and that he wouldnot have stand it himself;
which I feel he intend for encouragement, and consider to be
very kind of he. there be one advantage, and only one that I
know of, in Mr. Creakle's severity. he find my placard in his way
when he come up or down behind the form on which I sit, and want
to make a cut at I in pass; for this reason it be soon take
off, and I see it no more.
an accidental circumstance cement the intimacy between Steerforth
and I, in a manner that inspire I with great pride and
satisfaction, though it sometimes lead to inconvenience. it
happen on one occasion, when he be do I the honour of
talk to I in the playground, that I hazard the observation
that something or somebody - I forget what now - be like something
or somebody in Peregrine Pickle. he say nothing at the time; but
when I be go to bed at night, ask I if I have get that book?
I tell he no, and explain how it be that I have read it, and all
those other book of which I have make mention.
'and do you recollect they?' Steerforth say.
'oh yes,' I reply; I have a good memory, and I believe I
recollect they very well.
'then I tell you what, young copperfield,' say Steerforth, 'you
shall tell them to I. I cannot get to sleep very early at night,
and I generally wake rather early in the morning. wewill go over
them one after another. wewill make some regular Arabian Nights of
it.'
I feel extremely flatter by this arrangement, and we commence
carry it into execution that very evening. what ravage I
commit on my favourite author in the course of my
interpretation of they, I be not in a condition to say, and should
be very unwilling to know; but I have a profound faith in they, and
I have, to the good of my belief, a simple, earnest manner of
narrate what I do narrate; and these quality go a long way.
the drawback be, that I be often sleepy at night, or out of
