spirit and indispose to resume the story; and then it be rather
hard work, and it must be do; for to disappoint or to displease
Steerforth be of course out of the question. in the morning, too,
when I feel weary, and should have enjoy another hour's repose
very much, it be a tiresome thing to be rouse, like the Sultana
Scheherazade, and force into a long story before the get-up
bell ring; but Steerforth be resolute; and as he explain to I,
in return, my sum and exercise, and anything in my task that be
too hard for I, I be no loser by the transaction. let I do
myself justice, however. I be move by no interested or selfish
motive, nor be I move by fear of he. I admire and love he,
and his approval be return enough. it be so precious to I that
I look back on these trifle, now, with an aching heart.
Steerforth be considerate, too; and show his consideration, in
one particular instance, in an unflinche manner that be a little
tantalizing, I suspect, to poor traddle and the rest. Peggotty's
promise letter - what a comfortable letter it be! - arrive
before 'the half' be many week old; and with it a cake in a
perfect nest of orange, and two bottle of cowslip wine. this
treasure, as in duty bind, I lay at the foot of Steerforth, and
beg he to dispense.
'now, Iwill tell you what, young copperfield,' say he: 'the wine
shall be keep to wet your whistle when you be story-telling.'
I blush at the idea, and beg he, in my modesty, not to think
of it. but he say he have observe I be sometimes hoarse - a
little roopy be his exact expression - and it should be, every
drop, devote to the purpose he have mention. accordingly, it be
lock up in his box, and draw off by himself in a phial, and
administer to I through a piece of quill in the cork, when I be
suppose to be in want of a restorative. sometimes, to make it a
more sovereign specific, he be so kind as to squeeze orange juice
into it, or to stir it up with ginger, or dissolve a peppermint
drop in it; and although I cannot assert that the flavour be
improve by these experiment, or that it be exactly the compound
one would have choose for a stomachic, the last thing at night and
the first thing in the morning, I drink it gratefully and be very
sensible of his attention.
we seem, to I, to have be month over Peregrine, and month more
over the other story. the institution never flag for want of
a story, I be certain; and the wine last out almost as well as
the matter. poor traddle - I never think of that boy but with a
strange disposition to laugh, and with tear in my eye - be a
sort of chorus, in general; and affect to be convulse with mirth
at the comic part, and to be overcome with fear when there be any
passage of an alarming character in the narrative. this rather put
I out, very often. it be a great jest of his, I recollect, to
pretend that he couldnot keep his tooth from chattering, whenever
mention be make of an Alguazill in connexion with the adventure
of Gil Blas; and I remember that when Gil Blas meet the captain of
the robber in Madrid, this unlucky joker counterfeit such an
ague of terror, that he be overhear by Mr. Creakle, who be
prowl about the passage, and handsomely flog for disorderly
conduct in the bedroom.
whatever I have within I that be romantic and dreamy, be
encourage by so much story-telling in the dark; and in that
respect the pursuit may not have be very profitable to I. but
the be cherish as a kind of playthe in my room, and the
consciousness that this accomplishment of mine be bruit about
among the boy, and attract a good deal of notice to I though I
be the young there, stimulate I to exertion. in a school
carry on by sheer cruelty, whether it be preside over by a dunce
or not, there be not likely to be much learn. I believe our boy
be, generally, as ignorant a set as any schoolboy in existence;
they be too much troubled and knock about to learn; they could
no more do that to advantage, than any one can do anything to
advantage in a life of constant misfortune, torment, and worry.
but my little vanity, and Steerforth's help, urge I on somehow;
and without save I from much, if anything, in the way of
punishment, make I, for the time I be there, an exception to the
general body, insomuch that I do steadily pick up some crumb of
knowledge.
in this I be much assist by Mr. Mell, who have a liking for I
that I be grateful to remember. it always give I pain to observe
that Steerforth treat he with systematic disparagement, and
seldom lose an occasion of wound his feeling, or induce
other to do so. this trouble I the more for a long time,
because I have soon tell Steerforth, from whom I could no more keep
such a secret, than I could keep a cake or any other tangible
possession, about the two old woman Mr. Mell have take I to see;
and I be always afraid that Steerforth would let it out, and twit
he with it.
we little thought, any one of we, I dare say, when I eat my
breakfast that first morning, and go to sleep under the shadow of
the peacock's feather to the sound of the flute, what consequence
would come of the introduction into those alm-house of my
insignificant person. but the visit have its unforeseen
consequence; and of a serious sort, too, in their way.
one day when Mr. Creakle keep the house from indisposition, which
naturally diffuse a lively joy through the school, there be a
good deal of noise in the course of the morning's work. the great
relief and satisfaction experience by the boy make they difficult
to manage; and though the dread Tungay bring his wooden leg in
twice or thrice, and take note of the principal offender' name,
no great impression be make by it, as they be pretty sure of
get into trouble tomorrow, do what they would, and think it
wise, no doubt, to enjoy themselves today.
it be, properly, a half-holiday; be Saturday. but as the noise
in the playground would have disturb Mr. Creakle, and the weather
be not favourable for go out walk, we be order into
school in the afternoon, and set some light task than usual,
which be make for the occasion. it be the day of the week on
which Mr. Sharp go out to get his wig curl; so Mr. Mell, who
always do the drudgery, whatever it be, keep school by himself.
if I could associate the idea of a bull or a bear with anyone so
mild as Mr. Mell, I should think of he, in connexion with that
afternoon when the uproar be at its height, as of one of those
animal, bait by a thousand dog. I recall he bend his
aching head, support on his bony hand, over the book on his desk,
and wretchedly endeavour to get on with his tiresome work,
amidst an uproar that might have make the Speaker of the House of
Commons giddy. boy start in and out of their place, play at
puss in the corner with other boy; there be laugh boy,
singing boy, talk boy, dance boy, howl boy; boy
shuffle with their foot, boy whirl about he, grin, make
face, mimic he behind his back and before his eye; mimic
his poverty, his boot, his coat, his mother, everything belong
to he that they should have have consideration for.
'silence!' cry Mr. Mell, suddenly rise up, and strike his
desk with the book. 'what do this mean! itbe impossible to bear
it. itbe madden. how can you do it to I, boy?'
it be my book that he strike his desk with; and as I stand beside
he, follow his eye as it glance round the room, I see the boy
all stop, some suddenly surprised, some half afraid, and some sorry
perhaps.
Steerforth's place be at the bottom of the school, at the opposite
end of the long room. he be lounge with his back against the
wall, and his hand in his pocket, and look at Mr. Mell with his
mouth shut up as if he be whistle, when Mr. Mell look at he.
'Silence, Mr. Steerforth!' say Mr. Mell.
'silence yourself,' say Steerforth, turn red. 'whom be you
talk to?'
'sit down,' say Mr. Mell.
'sit down yourself,' say Steerforth, 'and mind your business.'
there be a titter, and some applause; but Mr. Mell be so white,
that silence immediately succeed; and one boy, who have dart out
behind he to imitate his mother again, change his mind, and
pretend to want a pen mend.
'if you think, Steerforth,' say Mr. Mell, 'that I be not
acquaint with the power you can establish over any mind here' -
he lay his hand, without consider what he do (as I suppose),
upon my head - 'or that I have not observe you, within a few
minute, urge your junior on to every sort of outrage against
I, you be mistaken.'
'I donot give myself the trouble of think at all about you,'
say Steerforth, coolly; 'so Ibe not mistaken, as it happen.'
'and when you make use of your position of favouritism here, sir,'
pursue Mr. Mell, with his lip tremble very much, 'to insult a
gentleman -'
'a what? - where be he?' say Steerforth.
here somebody cry out, 'Shame, J. Steerforth! too bad!' it be
traddle; whom Mr. Mell instantly discomfit by bid he hold
his tongue.
- 'to insult one who be not fortunate in life, sir, and who never
give you the least offence, and the many reason for not insult
whom you be old enough and wise enough to understand,' say Mr.
Mell, with his lip tremble more and more, 'you commit a mean and
base action. you can sit down or stand up as you please, sir.
copperfield, go on.'
'Young Copperfield,' say Steerforth, come forward up the room,
'stop a bit. I tell you what, Mr. Mell, once for all. when you
take the liberty of call I mean or base, or anything of that
sort, you be an impudent beggar. you be always a beggar, you
know; but when you do that, you be an impudent beggar.'
I be not clear whether he be go to strike Mr. Mell, or Mr. Mell
be go to strike he, or there be any such intention on either
side. I see a rigidity come upon the whole school as if they have
be turn into stone, and find Mr. Creakle in the midst of we,
with Tungay at his side, and Mrs. and Miss Creakle look in at
the door as if they be frightened. Mr. Mell, with his elbow on
his desk and his face in his hand, sit, for some moment, quite
still.
'Mr. Mell,' say Mr. Creakle, shake he by the arm; and his
whisper be so audible now, that Tungay feel it unnecessary to
repeat his word; 'you have not forget yourself, I hope?'
'no, sir, no,' return the Master, show his face, and shake
his head, and rub his hand in great agitation. 'no, sir. no.
I have remember myself, I - no, Mr. Creakle, I have not forget
myself, I - I have remember myself, sir. I - I - could wish you
have remember I a little soon, Mr. Creakle. it - it - would
have be more kind, sir, more just, sir. it would have save I
something, sir.'
Mr. Creakle, look hard at Mr. Mell, put his hand on Tungay's
shoulder, and get his foot upon the form close by, and sit upon the
desk. after still look hard at Mr. Mell from his throne, as he
shake his head, and rub his hand, and remain in the same
state of agitation, Mr. Creakle turn to Steerforth, and say:
'now, sir, as he donot condescend to tell I, what be this?'
Steerforth evade the question for a little while; look in scorn
and anger on his opponent, and remain silent. I could not help
think even in that interval, I remember, what a noble fellow he
be in appearance, and how homely and plain Mr. Mell look opposed
to he.
'what do he mean by talk about favourite, then?' say
Steerforth at length.
'Favourites?' repeat Mr. Creakle, with the vein in his forehead
swell quickly. 'who talk about favourite?'
'he do,' say Steerforth.
'and pray, what do you mean by that, sir?' demand Mr. Creakle,
turn angrily on his assistant.
'I mean, Mr. Creakle,' he return in a low voice, 'as I say;
that no pupil have a right to avail himself of his position of
favouritism to degrade I.'
'to degrade you?' say Mr. Creakle. 'my star! but give I leave
to ask you, Mr. What's-your-name'; and here Mr. Creakle fold his
arm, cane and all, upon his chest, and make such a knot of his
brow that his little eye be hardly visible below they;
'whether, when you talk about favourite, you show proper respect
to I? to I, sir,' say Mr. Creakle, dart his head at he
suddenly, and draw it back again, 'the principal of this
establishment, and your employer.'
'it be not judicious, sir, I be willing to admit,' say Mr. Mell.
'I should not have do so, if I have be cool.'
here Steerforth strike in.
'then he say I be mean, and then he say I be base, and then I
call he a beggar. if I have be cool, perhaps I shouldnot have
call he a beggar. but I do, and I be ready to take the
consequence of it.'
without consider, perhaps, whether there be any consequence
to be take, I feel quite in a glow at this gallant speech. it
make an impression on the boy too, for there be a low stir among
they, though no one speak a word.
'I be surprised, Steerforth - although your candour do you
honour,' say Mr. Creakle, 'do you honour, certainly - I be
surprised, Steerforth, I must say, that you should attach such an
epithet to any person employ and pay in Salem House, sir.'
Steerforth give a short laugh.
'thatbe not an answer, sir,' say Mr. Creakle, 'to my remark. I
expect more than that from you, Steerforth.'
if Mr. Mell look homely, in my eye, before the handsome boy, it
would be quite impossible to say how homely Mr. Creakle look.
'let he deny it,' say Steerforth.
'deny that he be a beggar, Steerforth?' cry Mr. Creakle. 'why,
where do he go a-beg?'
'if he be not a beggar himself, his near relation's one,' say
Steerforth. 'itbe all the same.'
he glance at I, and Mr. Mell's hand gently pat I upon the
shoulder. I look up with a flush upon my face and remorse in my
heart, but Mr. Mell's eye be fix on Steerforth. he continue
to pat I kindly on the shoulder, but he look at he.
'since you expect I, Mr. Creakle, to justify myself,' say
Steerforth, 'and to say what I mean, - what I have to say be, that
his mother live on charity in an alms-house.'
Mr. Mell still look at he, and still pat I kindly on the
shoulder, and say to himself, in a whisper, if I hear right:
'yes, I think so.'
Mr. Creakle turn to his assistant, with a severe frown and
laboured politeness:
'now, you hear what this gentleman say, Mr. Mell. have the
goodness, if you please, to set he right before the assemble
school.'
'he be right, sir, without correction,' return Mr. Mell, in the
midst of a dead silence; 'what he have say be true.'
'be so good then as declare publicly, will you,' say Mr. Creakle,
put his head on one side, and roll his eye round the
school, 'whether it ever come to my knowledge until this moment?'
'I believe not directly,' he return.
'why, you know not,' say Mr. Creakle. 'donot you, man?'
'I apprehend you never suppose my worldly circumstance to be very
good,' reply the assistant. 'you know what my position be, and
always have be, here.'
'I apprehend, if you come to that,' say Mr. Creakle, with his
vein swell again big than ever, 'that youhave be in a wrong
position altogether, and mistook this for a charity school. Mr.
Mell, wewill part, if you please. the soon the well.'
'there be no time,' answer Mr. Mell, rise, 'like the present.'
'Sir, to you!' say Mr. Creakle.
'I take my leave of you, Mr. Creakle, and all of you,' say Mr.
Mell, glance round the room, and again pat I gently on the
shoulder. 'James Steerforth, the good wish I can leave you be
that you may come to be ashamed of what you have do today. at
present I would prefer to see you anything rather than a friend, to
I, or to anyone in whom I feel an interest.'
once more he lay his hand upon my shoulder; and then take his
flute and a few book from his desk, and leave the key in it for
his successor, he go out of the school, with his property under
his arm. Mr. Creakle then make a speech, through Tungay, in which
he thank Steerforth for assert (though perhaps too warmly) the
independence and respectability of Salem House; and which he wind
up by shake hand with Steerforth, while we give three cheer -
I do not quite know what for, but I suppose for Steerforth, and
so join in they ardently, though I feel miserable. Mr. Creakle
then can Tommy Traddles for be discover in tear, instead of
cheer, on account of Mr. Mell's departure; and go back to his
sofa, or his bed, or wherever he have come from.
we be leave to ourselves now, and look very blank, I recollect,
on one another. for myself, I feel so much self-reproach and
contrition for my part in what have happen, that nothing would
have enable I to keep back my tear but the fear that Steerforth,
who often look at I, I see, might think it unfriendly - or, I
should rather say, consider our relative age, and the feeling
with which I regard he, undutiful - if I show the emotion
which distress I. he be very angry with traddle, and say he
be glad he have catch it.
Poor traddle, who have pass the stage of lie with his head upon
the desk, and be relieve himself as usual with a burst of
skeleton, say he donot care. Mr. Mell be ill-use.
'who have ill-use he, you girl?' say Steerforth.
'why, you have,' return traddle.
'what have I do?' say Steerforth.
'what have you do?' retort traddle. 'hurt his feeling, and
lose he his situation.'
'his feeling?' repeat Steerforth disdainfully. 'his feeling
will soon get the well of it, Iwill be bind. his feeling be
not like your, Miss Traddles. as to his situation - which be a
precious one, benot it? - do you suppose I be not go to write
home, and take care that he get some money? polly?'
we think this intention very noble in Steerforth, whose mother
be a widow, and rich, and would do almost anything, it be say,
that he ask she. we be all extremely glad to see traddle so
put down, and exalt Steerforth to the sky: especially when he
tell we, as he condescend to do, that what he have do have be
do expressly for we, and for our cause; and that he have confer
a great boon upon we by unselfishly do it.
but I must say that when I be go on with a story in the dark
that night, Mr. Mell's old flute seem more than once to sound
mournfully in my ear; and that when at last Steerforth be tired,
and I lie down in my bed, I fancy it play so sorrowfully
somewhere, that I be quite wretched.
I soon forget he in the contemplation of Steerforth, who, in an
easy amateur way, and without any book (he seem to I to know
everything by heart), take some of his class until a new master
be find. the new master come from a grammar school; and before
he enter on his duty, din in the parlour one day, to be
introduce to Steerforth. Steerforth approve of he highly, and
tell we he be a Brick. without exactly understand what learn
distinction be mean by this, I respect he greatly for it, and
have no doubt whatever of his superior knowledge: though he never
take the pain with I - not that I be anybody - that Mr. Mell have
take.
there be only one other event in this half-year, out of the daily
school-life, that make an impression upon I which still survive.
it survive for many reason.
one afternoon, when we be all harass into a state of dire
confusion, and Mr. Creakle be lay about he dreadfully, Tungay
come in, and call out in his usual strong way: 'visitor for
Copperfield!'
a few word be interchange between he and Mr. Creakle, as, who
the visitor be, and what room they be to be show into; and
then I, who have, accord to custom, stand up on the announcement
be make, and feel quite faint with astonishment, be tell to go
by the back stair and get a clean frill on, before I repair to
the dining-room. these order I obey, in such a flutter and
hurry of my young spirit as I have never know before; and when I
get to the parlour door, and the thought come into my head that it
might be my mother - I have only thought of Mr. or Miss Murdstone
until then - I draw back my hand from the lock, and stop to have
a sob before I go in.
at first I see nobody; but feel a pressure against the door, I
look round it, and there, to my amazement, be Mr. Peggotty and
Ham, duck at I with their hat, and squeeze one another
against the wall. I could not help laugh; but it be much more
in the pleasure of see they, than at the appearance they make.
we shake hand in a very cordial way; and I laugh and laugh,
until I pull out my pocket-handkerchief and wipe my eye.
Mr. Peggotty (who never shut his mouth once, I remember, during the
visit) show great concern when he see I do this, and nudge Ham
to say something.
'cheer up, Mas'r Davy bor'!' say Ham, in his simper way. 'why,
how you have grow!'
'be I grow?' I say, dry my eye. I be not cry at anything
in particular that I know of; but somehow it make I cry, to see
old friend.
'grow, mas'r Davy bor'? benot he grow!' say Ham.
'benot he grow!' say Mr. Peggotty.
they make I laugh again by laugh at each other, and then we all
three laugh until I be in danger of cry again.
'do you know how mama be, Mr. Peggotty?' I say. 'and how my dear,
dear, old Peggotty be?'
'Oncommon,' say Mr. Peggotty.
'and little Em'ly, and Mrs. Gummidge?'
'on - common,' say Mr. Peggotty.
there be a silence. Mr. Peggotty, to relieve it, take two
prodigious lobster, and an enormous crab, and a large canvas bag
of shrimp, out of his pocket, and pile they up in Ham's arm.
'you see,' say Mr. Peggotty, 'know as you be partial to a
little relish with your wittle when you be along with we, we take
the liberty. the old Mawther bile them, she do. Mrs. Gummidge
bile them. yes,' say Mr. Peggotty, slowly, who I think appear
to stick to the subject on account of have no other subject
ready, 'Mrs. Gummidge, I do assure you, she bile them.'
I express my thank; and Mr. Peggotty, after look at Ham, who
stand smile sheepishly over the shellfish, without make any
attempt to help he, say:
'we come, you see, the wind and tide making in our favour, in one
of our Yarmouth lug to Gravesen'. my sister she write to I the
name of this here place, and write to I as if ever I chance to
come to Gravesen', I be to come over and inquire for Mas'r Davy
and give her dooty, humbly wish he well and reporting of the
fam'ly as they be oncommon toe-be-sure. little Em'ly, you see,
shewill write to my sister when I go back, as I see you and as you
be similarly oncommon, and so we make it quite a merry-
go-rounder.'
I be oblige to consider a little before I understand what Mr.
Peggotty mean by this figure, expressive of a complete circle of
intelligence. I then thank he heartily; and say, with a
consciousness of redden, that I suppose little Em'ly be
alter too, since we use to pick up shell and pebble on the
beach?
'shebe get to be a woman, thatbe wot shebe get to be,' say
Mr. Peggotty. 'ask HIM.'
he mean Ham, who beam with delight and assent over the bag of
shrimp.
'her pretty face!' say Mr. Peggotty, with his own shin like a
light.
'her learning!' say Ham.
'her writing!' say Mr. Peggotty. 'why itbe as black as jet! and
so large it be, you might see it anywhere.'
it be perfectly delightful to behold with what enthusiasm Mr.
Peggotty become inspire when he think of his little favourite.
he stand before I again, his bluff hairy face irradiate with a
joyful love and pride, for which I can find no description. his
honest eye fire up, and sparkle, as if their depth be stir
by something bright. his broad chest heave with pleasure. his
strong loose hand clench themselves, in his earnestness; and he
emphasize what he say with a right arm that show, in my pigmy
view, like a sledge-hammer.
Ham be quite as earnest as he. I dare say they would have say
much more about she, if they have not be abash by the unexpected
come in of Steerforth, who, see I in a corner speak with
two stranger, stop in a song he be sing, and say: 'I
donot know you be here, young Copperfield!' (for it be not the
usual visit room) and cross by we on his way out.
I be not sure whether it be in the pride of have such a friend
as Steerforth, or in the desire to explain to he how I come to
have such a friend as Mr. Peggotty, that I call to he as he be
go away. but I say, modestly - Good Heaven, how it all come
back to I this long time afterwards! -
'donot go, Steerforth, if you please. these be two Yarmouth
boatman - very kind, good people - who be relation of my nurse,
and have come from Gravesend to see I.'
'aye, aye?' say Steerforth, return. 'I be glad to see they.
how be you both?'
there be an ease in his manner - a gay and light manner it be,
but not swagger - which I still believe to have bear a kind of
enchantment with it. I still believe he, in virtue of this
carriage, his animal spirit, his delightful voice, his handsome
face and figure, and, for aught I know, of some inborn power of
attraction besides (which I think a few people possess), to have
carry a spell with he to which it be a natural weakness to
yield, and which not many person could withstand. I could not but
see how pleased they be with he, and how they seem to open
their heart to he in a moment.
'you must let they know at home, if you please, Mr. Peggotty,' I
say, 'when that letter be send, that Mr. Steerforth be very kind
to I, and that I donot know what I should ever do here without
he.'
'nonsense!' say Steerforth, laugh. 'you mustnot tell they
anything of the sort.'
'and if Mr. Steerforth ever come into Norfolk or Suffolk, Mr.
Peggotty,' I say, 'while I be there, you may depend upon it I
shall bring he to Yarmouth, if he will let I, to see your house.
you never see such a good house, Steerforth. itbe make out of a
boat!'
'make out of a boat, be it?' say Steerforth. 'itbe the right sort
of a house for such a thorough-build boatman.'
'so 'tis, sir, so 'tis, sir,' say Ham, grin. 'yoube right,
young gen'l'm'n! mas'r Davy bor', gen'l'm'n's right. a thorough-
build boatman! Hor, hor! thatbe what he be, too!'
Mr. Peggotty be no less pleased than his nephew, though his
modesty forbid he to claim a personal compliment so vociferously.
'well, sir,' he say, bow and chuckle, and tuck in the end
of his neckerchief at his breast: 'I thankee, sir, I thankee! I do
my endeavour in my line of life, sir.'
'the good of man can do no more, Mr. Peggotty,' say Steerforth.
he have get his name already.
'Iwill pound it, itbe wot you do yourself, sir,' say Mr. Peggotty,
shake his head, 'and wot you do well - right well! I thankee,
sir. Ibe obleege to you, sir, for your welcome manner of I.
Ibe rough, sir, but Ibe ready - least way, I hope Ibe ready, you
unnerstand. my house benot much for to see, sir, but itbe hearty
at your service if ever you should come along with Mas'r Davy to
see it. Ibe a reg'lar dodman, I be,' say Mr. Peggotty, by which
he mean snail, and this be in allusion to his be slow to go,
for he have attempt to go after every sentence, and have somehow or
other come back again; 'but I wish you both well, and I wish you
happy!'
Ham echo this sentiment, and we part with they in the heartiest
manner. I be almost tempt that evening to tell Steerforth about
pretty little Em'ly, but I be too timid of mention her name,
and too much afraid of his laugh at I. I remember that I
think a good deal, and in an uneasy sort of way, about Mr.
Peggotty have say that she be get on to be a woman; but I
decide that be nonsense.
we transport the shellfish, or the 'relish' as Mr. Peggotty have
modestly call it, up into our room unobserved, and make a great
supper that evening. but traddle couldnot get happily out of it.
he be too unfortunate even to come through a supper like anybody
else. he be take ill in the night - quite prostrate he be - in
consequence of Crab; and after be drug with black draught
and blue pill, to an extent which Demple (whose father be a
doctor) say be enough to undermine a horse's constitution,
receive a can and six chapter of Greek Testament for refuse
to confess.
the rest of the half-year be a jumble in my recollection of the
daily strife and struggle of our life; of the wan summer and
the change season; of the frosty morning when we be ring out
of bed, and the cold, cold smell of the dark night when we be
ring into bed again; of the evening schoolroom dimly light and
indifferently warm, and the morning schoolroom which be nothing
but a great shivering-machine; of the alternation of boil beef
with roast beef, and boil mutton with roast mutton; of clod of
bread-and-butter, dog's-eare lesson-book, crack slate,
tear-blot copy-book, caning, rulering, hair-cutting, rainy
Sundays, suet-pudding, and a dirty atmosphere of ink, surround
all.
I well remember though, how the distant idea of the holiday, after
seem for an immense time to be a stationary speck, begin to come
towards we, and to grow and grow. how from counting month, we
come to week, and then to day; and how I then begin to be afraid
that I should not be send for and when I learn from Steerforth
that I have be send for, and be certainly to go home, have dim
foreboding that I might break my leg first. how the breaking-up
day change its place fast, at last, from the week after next to
next week, this week, the day after tomorrow, tomorrow, today,
tonight - when I be inside the Yarmouth mail, and go home.
I have many a broken sleep inside the Yarmouth mail, and many an
incoherent dream of all these thing. but when I awake at
interval, the ground outside the window be not the playground of
Salem House, and the sound in my ear be not the sound of Mr.
Creakle give it to traddle, but the sound of the coachman
touch up the horse.
CHAPTER 8
MY holiday. especially one HAPPY AFTERNOON
when we arrive before day at the inn where the mail stop, which
be not the inn where my friend the waiter live, I be show up to
a nice little bedroom, with dolphin paint on the door. very cold
I be, I know, notwithstanding the hot tea they have give I before
a large fire downstairs; and very glad I be to turn into the
Dolphin's bed, pull the dolphin's blanket round my head, and go to
sleep.
Mr. Barkis the carrier be to call for I in the morning at nine
o'clock. I get up at eight, a little giddy from the shortness of
my night's rest, and be ready for he before the appoint time.
he receive I exactly as if not five minute have elapse since we
be last together, and I have only be into the hotel to get
change for sixpence, or something of that sort.
as soon as I and my box be in the cart, and the carrier seat,
the lazy horse walk away with we all at his accustomed pace.
'you look very well, Mr. Barkis,' I say, think he would like to
know it.
Mr. Barkis rub his cheek with his cuff, and then look at his
cuff as if he expect to find some of the bloom upon it; but make
no other acknowledgement of the compliment.
'I give your message, Mr. Barkis,' I say: 'I write to Peggotty.'
'ah!' say Mr. Barkis.
Mr. Barkis seem gruff, and answer drily.
'benot it right, Mr. Barkis?' I ask, after a little hesitation.
'why, no,' say Mr. Barkis.
'not the message?'
'the message be right enough, perhaps,' say Mr. Barkis; 'but it
come to an end there.'
not understand what he mean, I repeat inquisitively: 'come to
an end, Mr. Barkis?'
'nothing come of it,' he explain, look at I sideways. 'no
answer.'
'there be an answer expect, be there, Mr. Barkis?' say I,
open my eye. for this be a new light to I.
'when a man say hebe willin',' say Mr. Barkis, turn his glance
slowly on I again, 'itbe as much as to say, that man's a-waitin'
for a answer.'
'well, Mr. Barkis?'
'well,' say Mr. Barkis, carry his eye back to his horse's
ear; 'that manbe be a-waitin' for a answer ever since.'
'have you tell she so, Mr. Barkis?'
'no - no,' growl Mr. Barkis, reflect about it. 'I benot get
no call to go and tell she so. I never say six word to she
myself, I benot a-go to tell she so.'
'would you like I to do it, Mr. Barkis?' say I, doubtfully.
'you might tell she, if you would,' say Mr. Barkis, with another
slow look at I, 'that Barkis be a-waitin' for a answer. say you
- what name be it?'
'her name?'
'ah!' say Mr. Barkis, with a nod of his head.
'Peggotty.'
'Chrisen name? or nat'ral name?' say Mr. Barkis.
'oh, itbe not her christian name. her christian name be Clara.'
'be it though?' say Mr. Barkis.
he seem to find an immense fund of reflection in this
circumstance, and sit ponder and inwardly whistle for some
time.
'well!' he resume at length. 'say you, "Peggotty! Barkis be
waitin' for a answer." say she, perhaps, "answer to what?" say
you, "to what I tell you." "what be that?" say she. "Barkis be
willin'," say you.'
this extremely artful suggestion Mr. Barkis accompany with a
nudge of his elbow that give I quite a stitch in my side. after
that, he slouch over his horse in his usual manner; and make no
other reference to the subject except, half an hour afterwards,
take a piece of chalk from his pocket, and write up, inside the
tilt of the cart, 'Clara Peggotty' - apparently as a private
memorandum.
ah, what a strange feeling it be to be go home when it be not
home, and to find that every object I look at, remind I of the
happy old home, which be like a dream I could never dream again!
the day when my mother and I and Peggotty be all in all to one
another, and there be no one to come between we, rise up before I
so sorrowfully on the road, that I be not sure I be glad to be
there - not sure but that I would rather have remain away, and
forget it in Steerforth's company. but there I be; and soon I
be at our house, where the bare old elm-tree wring their many
hand in the bleak wintry air, and shred of the old rooks'-nests
drift away upon the wind.
the carrier put my box down at the garden-gate, and leave I. I
walk along the path towards the house, glance at the window,
and fear at every step to see Mr. Murdstone or Miss Murdstone
lower out of one of they. no face appear, however; and be
come to the house, and know how to open the door, before dark,
without knock, I go in with a quiet, timid step.
God know how infantine the memory may have be, that be awaken
within I by the sound of my mother's voice in the old parlour,
when I set foot in the hall. she be sing in a low tone. I
think I must have lain in her arm, and hear she singing so to I
when I be but a baby. the strain be new to I, and yet it be so
old that it fill my heart brim-full; like a friend come back from
a long absence.
I believe, from the solitary and thoughtful way in which my mother
murmur her song, that she be alone. and I go softly into the
room. she be sit by the fire, suckle an infant, whose tiny
hand she hold against her neck. her eye be look down upon
its face, and she sit sing to it. I be so far right, that she
have no other companion.
I speak to she, and she start, and cry out. but see I, she
call I her dear Davy, her own boy! and come half across the
room to meet I, kneel down upon the ground and kiss I, and
lay my head down on her bosom near the little creature that be
nestle there, and put its hand to my lip.
I wish I have die. I wish I have die then, with that feeling in my
heart! I should have be more fit for Heaven than I ever have
be since.
'he be your brother,' say my mother, fondle I. 'Davy, my
pretty boy! my poor child!' then she kiss I more and more, and
clasp I round the neck. this she be do when Peggotty come
run in, and bounce down on the ground beside we, and go mad
about we both for a quarter of an hour.
it seem that I have not be expect so soon, the carrier be
much before his usual time. it seem, too, that Mr. and Miss
Murdstone have go out upon a visit in the neighbourhood, and would
not return before night. I have never hop for this. I have never
think it possible that we three could be together undisturbed,
once more; and I feel, for the time, as if the old day be come
back.
we din together by the fireside. Peggotty be in attendance to
wait upon we, but my mother wouldnot let she do it, and make she
dine with we. I have my own old plate, with a brown view of a
man-of-war in full sail upon it, which Peggotty have hoard
somewhere all the time I have be away, and would not have have
break, she say, for a hundred pound. I have my own old mug with
David on it, and my own old little knife and fork that wouldnot
cut.
while we be at table, I think it a favourable occasion to tell
Peggotty about Mr. Barkis, who, before I have finish what I have to
tell she, begin to laugh, and throw her apron over her face.
'Peggotty,' say my mother. 'whatbe the matter?'
Peggotty only laugh the more, and hold her apron tight over she
face when my mother try to pull it away, and sit as if her head
be in a bag.
'what be you do, you stupid creature?' say my mother,
laugh.
'oh, drat the man!' cry Peggotty. 'he want to marry I.'
'it would be a very good match for you; wouldnot it?' say my
mother.
'oh! I donot know,' say Peggotty. 'donot ask I. I wouldnot
have he if he be make of gold. nor I wouldnot have anybody.'
'then, why donot you tell he so, you ridiculous thing?' say my
mother.
'tell he so,' retort Peggotty, look out of her apron. 'he
have never say a word to I about it. he know well. if he be
to make so bold as say a word to I, I should slap his face.'
her own be as red as ever I see it, or any other face, I think;
but she only cover it again, for a few moment at a time, when
she be take with a violent fit of laughter; and after two or
three of those attack, go on with her dinner.
I remark that my mother, though she smile when Peggotty look
at she, become more serious and thoughtful. I have see at first
that she be change. her face be very pretty still, but it
look careworn, and too delicate; and her hand be so thin and
white that it seem to I to be almost transparent. but the
change to which I now refer be superadded to this: it be in her
manner, which become anxious and fluttered. at last she say,
put out her hand, and lay it affectionately on the hand of
her old servant,
'peggotty, dear, you be not go to be marry?'
'Me, ma'am?' return Peggotty, star. 'Lord bless you, no!'
'not just yet?' say my mother, tenderly.
'never!' cry Peggotty.
my mother take her hand, and say:
'donot leave I, Peggotty. stay with I. it will not be for long,
perhaps. what should I ever do without you!'
'I leave you, my precious!' cry Peggotty. 'not for all the
world and his wife. why, whatbe put that in your silly little
head?' - for Peggotty have be use of old to talk to my mother
sometimes like a child.
but my mother make no answer, except to thank she, and Peggotty
go run on in her own fashion.
'I leave you? I think I see myself. Peggotty go away from you?
I should like to catch she at it! no, no, no,' say Peggotty,
shake her head, and fold her arm; 'not she, my dear. it
benot that there benot some Cats that would be well enough please
if she do, but they sha'n't be pleased. they shall be aggravate.
Iwill stay with you till I be a cross cranky old woman. and when
Ibe too deaf, and too lame, and too blind, and too mumbly for want
of tooth, to be of any use at all, even to be find fault with,
than I shall go to my Davy, and ask he to take I in.'
'and, Peggotty,' say I, 'I shall be glad to see you, and Iwill make
you as welcome as a queen.'
'bless your dear heart!' cry Peggotty. 'I know you will!' and
she kiss I beforehand, in grateful acknowledgement of my
hospitality. after that, she cover her head up with her apron
again and have another laugh about Mr. Barkis. after that, she take
the baby out of its little cradle, and nurse it. after that, she
clear the dinner table; after that, come in with another cap on,
and her work-box, and the yard-measure, and the bit of wax-candle,
all just the same as ever.
we sit round the fire, and talk delightfully. I tell they what
a hard master Mr. Creakle be, and they pity I very much. I
tell they what a fine fellow Steerforth be, and what a patron of
mine, and Peggotty say she would walk a score of mile to see he.
I take the little baby in my arm when it be awake, and nurse it
lovingly. when it be asleep again, I creep close to my mother's
side accord to my old custom, break now a long time, and sit
with my arm embrace her waist, and my little red cheek on her
shoulder, and once more feel her beautiful hair droop over I -
like an angel's wing as I use to think, I recollect - and be very
happy indeed.
while I sit thus, look at the fire, and see picture in the
red-hot coal, I almost believe that I have never be away; that
Mr. and Miss Murdstone be such picture, and would vanish when
the fire get low; and that there be nothing real in all that I
remember, save my mother, Peggotty, and I.
Peggotty darn away at a stocking as long as she could see, and
then sit with it draw on her left hand like a glove, and her
needle in her right, ready to take another stitch whenever there
be a blaze. I cannot conceive whose stocking they can have be
that Peggotty be always darn, or where such an unfailing supply
of stocking in want of darning can have come from. from my
early infancy she seem to have be always employ in that
class of needlework, and never by any chance in any other.
'I wonder,' say Peggotty, who be sometimes seize with a fit of
wonder on some most unexpected topic, 'whatbe become of Davy's
great-aunt?'
'Lor, Peggotty!' observe my mother, rouse herself from a
reverie, 'what nonsense you talk!'
'well, but I really do wonder, ma'am,' say Peggotty.
'what can have put such a person in your head?' inquire my mother.
'be there nobody else in the world to come there?'
'I donot know how it be,' say Peggotty, 'unless itbe on account of
be stupid, but my head never can pick and choose its people.
they come and they go, and they donot come and they donot go, just
as they like. I wonder whatbe become of she?'
'how absurd you be, Peggotty!' return my mother. 'one would
suppose you want a second visit from she.'
'Lord forbid!' cry Peggotty.
'well then, donot talk about such uncomfortable thing, therebe a
good soul,' say my mother. 'Miss Betsey be shut up in her cottage
by the sea, no doubt, and will remain there. at all event, she be
not likely ever to trouble we again.'
'no!' muse Peggotty. 'no, that benot likely at all. - I wonder,
if she be to die, whether shewould leave Davy anything?'
'good gracious I, Peggotty,' return my mother, 'what a
nonsensical woman you be! when you know that she take offence at
the poor dear boybe ever be bear at all.'
'I suppose she wouldnot be incline to forgive he now,' hint
Peggotty.
'why should she be incline to forgive he now?' say my mother,
rather sharply.
'now that hebe get a brother, I mean,' say Peggotty.
my mother immediately begin to cry, and wonder how Peggotty dare
to say such a thing.
'as if this poor little innocent in its cradle have ever do any
harm to you or anybody else, you jealous thing!' say she. 'you
have much well go and marry Mr. Barkis, the carrier. why donot
you?'
'I should make Miss Murdstone happy, if I be to,' say Peggotty.
'what a bad disposition you have, Peggotty!' return my mother.
'you be as jealous of Miss Murdstone as it be possible for a
ridiculous creature to be. you want to keep the key yourself, and
give out all the thing, I suppose? I shouldnot be surprised if
you do. when you know that she only do it out of kindness and
the good intention! you know she do, Peggotty - you know it
well.'
Peggotty mutter something to the effect of 'bother the good
intention!' and something else to the effect that there be a
little too much of the good intention go on.
'I know what you mean, you cross thing,' say my mother. 'I
understand you, Peggotty, perfectly. you know I do, and I wonder
you donot colour up like fire. but one point at a time. Miss
Murdstone be the point now, Peggotty, and you sha'n't escape from
it. havenot you hear she say, over and over again, that she
think I be too thoughtless and too - a - a -'
'pretty,' suggest Peggotty.
'well,' return my mother, half laugh, 'and if she be so silly
as to say so, can I be blame for it?'
'no one say you can,' say Peggotty.
'no, I should hope not, indeed!' return my mother. 'havenot you
hear she say, over and over again, that on this account she wish
to spare I a great deal of trouble, which she think I be not
suit for, and which I really donot know myself that I be suit
for; and benot she up early and late, and go to and fro
continually - and donot she do all sort of thing, and grope
into all sort of place, coal-hole and pantry and I donot know
where, that cannot be very agreeable - and do you mean to insinuate
that there be not a sort of devotion in that?'
'I donot insinuate at all,' say Peggotty.
'you do, Peggotty,' return my mother. 'you never do anything
else, except your work. you be always insinuate. you revel in
it. and when you talk of Mr. Murdstone's good intention -'
'I never talk of them,' say Peggotty.
'no, Peggotty,' return my mother, 'but you insinuate. thatbe
what I tell you just now. thatbe the bad of you. you will
insinuate. I say, at the moment, that I understand you, and you
see I do. when you talk of Mr. Murdstone's good intention, and
pretend to slight they (for I donot believe you really do, in your
heart, Peggotty), you must be as well convinced as I be how good
they be, and how they actuate he in everything. if he seem to
have be at all stern with a certain person, Peggotty - you
understand, and so I be sure do Davy, that I be not allude to
anybody present - it be solely because he be satisfied that it be
for a certain person's benefit. he naturally love a certain
person, on my account; and act solely for a certain person's good.
he be well able to judge of it than I be; for I very well know
that I be a weak, light, girlish creature, and that he be a firm,
grave, serious man. and he take,' say my mother, with the tear
which be engender in her affectionate nature, steal down her
face, 'he take great pain with I; and I ought to be very
thankful to he, and very submissive to he even in my thought;
and when I be not, Peggotty, I worry and condemn myself, and feel
doubtful of my own heart, and donot know what to do.'
Peggotty sit with her chin on the foot of the stocking, look
silently at the fire.
'there, Peggotty,' say my mother, change her tone, 'donot let we
fall out with one another, for I couldnot bear it. you be my true
friend, I know, if I have any in the world. when I call you a
ridiculous creature, or a vexatious thing, or anything of that
sort, Peggotty, I only mean that you be my true friend, and always
have be, ever since the night when Mr. Copperfield first bring
I home here, and you come out to the gate to meet I.'
Peggotty be not slow to respond, and ratify the treaty of
friendship by give I one of her good hug. I think I have some
glimpse of the real character of this conversation at the time;
but I be sure, now, that the good creature originate it, and take
her part in it, merely that my mother might comfort herself with
the little contradictory summary in which she have indulge. the

at ease during the rest of the evening, and that Peggotty observe
she less.
when we have have our tea, and the ash be throw up, and the
candle snuff, I read Peggotty a chapter out of the Crocodile
Book, in remembrance of old time - she take it out of her pocket:
I donot know whether she have keep it there ever since - and then we
talk about Salem House, which bring I round again to
Steerforth, who be my great subject. we be very happy; and that
evening, as the last of its race, and destine evermore to close
that volume of my life, will never pass out of my memory.
it be almost ten o'clock before we hear the sound of wheel. we
all get up then; and my mother say hurriedly that, as it be so
late, and Mr. and Miss Murdstone approve of early hour for young
people, perhaps I have well go to bed. I kiss she, and go
upstairs with my candle directly, before they come in. it appear
to my childish fancy, as I ascend to the bedroom where I have be
imprison, that they bring a cold blast of air into the house
which blow away the old familiar feeling like a feather.
I feel uncomfortable about go down to breakfast in the morning,
as I have never set eye on Mr. Murdstone since the day when I
commit my memorable offence. however, as it must be do, I
go down, after two or three false start half-way, and as many
run back on tiptoe to my own room, and present myself in the
parlour.
he be stand before the fire with his back to it, while Miss
Murdstone make the tea. he look at I steadily as I enter, but
make no sign of recognition whatever.
I go up to he, after a moment of confusion, and say: 'I beg
your pardon, sir. I be very sorry for what I do, and I hope you
will forgive I.'
'I be glad to hear you be sorry, David,' he reply.
the hand he give I be the hand I have bite. I could not
restrain my eye from rest for an instant on a red spot upon it;
but it be not so red as I turn, when I meet that sinister
expression in his face.
'how do you do, ma'am?' I say to Miss Murdstone.
'ah, dear I!' sigh Miss Murdstone, give I the tea-caddy scoop
instead of her finger. 'how long be the holiday?'
'a month, ma'am.'
'count from when?'
'from today, ma'am.'
'oh!' say Miss Murdstone. 'then herebe one day off.'
she keep a calendar of the holiday in this way, and every morning
check a day off in exactly the same manner. she do it gloomily
until she come to ten, but when she get into two figure she become
more hopeful, and, as the time advance, even jocular.
it be on this very first day that I have the misfortune to throw
she, though she be not subject to such weakness in general, into
