and my mother be sit; and the baby (who be only a few week
old) be on my mother's lap, I take it very carefully in my arm.
suddenly Miss Murdstone give such a scream that I all but drop
it.
'my dear jane!' cry my mother.
'Good heavens, Clara, do you see?' exclaim Miss Murdstone.
'see what, my dear jane?' say my mother; 'where?'
'hebe get it!' cry Miss Murdstone. 'the boy have get the baby!'
she be limp with horror; but stiffen herself to make a dart at
I, and take it out of my arm. then, she turn faint; and be so
very ill that they be oblige to give her cherry brandy. I be
solemnly interdict by she, on her recovery, from touch my
brother any more on any pretence whatever; and my poor mother, who,
I could see, wish otherwise, meekly confirm the interdict, by
say: 'no doubt you be right, my dear jane.'
on another occasion, when we three be together, this same dear
baby - it be truly dear to I, for our mother's sake - be the
innocent occasion of Miss Murdstonebe go into a passion. my
mother, who have be look at its eye as it lie upon her lap,
say:
'Davy! come here!' and look at mine.
I see Miss Murdstone lie her bead down.
'I declare,' say my mother, gently, 'they be exactly alike. I
suppose they be mine. I think they be the colour of mine. but
they be wonderfully alike.'
'what be you talk about, Clara?' say Miss Murdstone.
'my dear jane,' falter my mother, a little abash by the harsh
tone of this inquiry, 'I find that the baby's eye and Davy's be
exactly alike.'
'Clara!' say Miss Murdstone, rise angrily, 'you be a positive
fool sometimes.'
'my dear jane,' remonstrate my mother.
'a positive fool,' say Miss Murdstone. 'who else could compare my
brother's baby with your boy? they be not at all alike. they be
exactly unlike. they be utterly dissimilar in all respect. I
hope they will ever remain so. I will not sit here, and hear such
comparison make.' with that she stalk out, and make the door
bang after she.
in short, I be not a favourite with Miss Murdstone. in short, I
be not a favourite there with anybody, not even with myself; for
those who do like I could not show it, and those who do not,
show it so plainly that I have a sensitive consciousness of always
appear constrain, boorish, and dull.
I feel that I make they as uncomfortable as they make I. if I
come into the room where they be, and they be talk together
and my mother seem cheerful, an anxious cloud would steal over
her face from the moment of my entrance. if Mr. Murdstone be in
his good humour, I check he. if Miss Murdstone be in she
bad, I intensify it. I have perception enough to know that my
mother be the victim always; that she be afraid to speak to I or
to be kind to I, lest she should give they some offence by her
manner of do so, and receive a lecture afterwards; that she be
not only ceaselessly afraid of her own offending, but of my
offending, and uneasily watch their look if I only move.
therefore I resolve to keep myself as much out of their way as I
could; and many a wintry hour do I hear the church clock strike,
when I be sit in my cheerless bedroom, wrap in my little
great-coat, pore over a book.
in the evening, sometimes, I go and sit with Peggotty in the
kitchen. there I be comfortable, and not afraid of be myself.
but neither of these resource be approve of in the parlour. the
torment humour which be dominant there stop they both. I
be still hold to be necessary to my poor mother's training, and,
as one of her trial, could not be suffer to absent myself.
'David,' say Mr. Murdstone, one day after dinner when I be go
to leave the room as usual; 'I be sorry to observe that you be of
a sullen disposition.'
'as sulky as a bear!' say Miss Murdstone.
I stand still, and hang my head.
'now, David,' say Mr. Murdstone, 'a sullen obdurate disposition
be, of all temper, the bad.'
'and the boy's be, of all such disposition that ever I have see,'
remark his sister, 'the most confirmed and stubborn. I think, my
dear Clara, even you must observe it?'
'I beg your pardon, my dear Jane,' say my mother, 'but be you
quite sure - I be certain youwill excuse I, my dear Jane - that you
understand Davy?'
'I should be somewhat ashamed of myself, Clara,' return Miss
Murdstone, 'if I could not understand the boy, or any boy. I donot
profess to be profound; but I do lay claim to common sense.'
'no doubt, my dear jane,' return my mother, 'your understanding
be very vigorous -'
'oh dear, no! pray donot say that, Clara,' interpose Miss
Murdstone, angrily.
'but I be sure it be,' resume my mother; 'and everybody know it
be. I profit so much by it myself, in many way - at least I ought
to - that no one can be more convinced of it than myself; and
therefore I speak with great diffidence, my dear Jane, I assure
you.'
'wewill say I donot understand the boy, Clara,' return Miss
Murdstone, arrange the little fetter on her wrist. 'wewill
agree, if you please, that I donot understand he at all. he be
much too deep for I. but perhaps my brother's penetration may
enable he to have some insight into his character. and I believe
my brother be speak on the subject when we - not very decently
- interrupt he.'
'I think, Clara,' say Mr. Murdstone, in a low grave voice, 'that
there may be well and more dispassionate judge of such a
question than you.'
'Edward,' reply my mother, timidly, 'you be a far well judge
of all question than I pretend to be. both you and Jane be. I
only say -'
'you only say something weak and inconsiderate,' he reply. 'try
not to do it again, my dear Clara, and keep a watch upon yourself.'
my mother's lip move, as if she answer 'yes, my dear edward,'
but she say nothing aloud.
'I be sorry, David, I remark,' say Mr. Murdstone, turn his
head and his eye stiffly towards I, 'to observe that you be of
a sullen disposition. this be not a character that I can suffer to
develop itself beneath my eye without an effort at improvement.
you must endeavour, sir, to change it. we must endeavour to change
it for you.'
'I beg your pardon, sir,' I falter. 'I have never mean to be
sullen since I come back.'
'donot take refuge in a lie, sir!' he return so fiercely, that I
see my mother involuntarily put out her trembling hand as if to
interpose between we. 'you have withdraw yourself in your
sullenness to your own room. you have keep your own room when you
ought to have be here. you know now, once for all, that I
require you to be here, and not there. far, that I require you
to bring obedience here. you know I, David. I will have it
do.'
Miss Murdstone give a hoarse chuckle.
'I will have a respectful, prompt, and ready bearing towards
myself,' he continue, 'and towards Jane Murdstone, and towards
your mother. I will not have this room shun as if it be
infect, at the pleasure of a child. sit down.'
he order I like a dog, and I obey like a dog.
'one thing more,' he say. 'I observe that you have an attachment
to low and common company. you be not to associate with servant.
the kitchen will not improve you, in the many respect in which you
need improvement. of the woman who abet you, I say nothing -
since you, Clara,' address my mother in a low voice, 'from old
association and long-establish fancy, have a weakness
respect she which be not yet overcome.'
'a most unaccountable delusion it be!' cry Miss Murdstone.
'I only say,' he resume, address I, 'that I disapprove of your
prefer such company as Mistress Peggotty, and that it be to be
abandon. now, David, you understand I, and you know what will
be the consequence if you fail to obey I to the letter.'
I know well - well perhaps than he think, as far as my poor
mother be concern - and I obey he to the letter. I retreat
to my own room no more; I take refuge with Peggotty no more; but
sit wearily in the parlour day after day, look forward to night,
and bedtime.
what irksome constraint I underwent, sit in the same attitude
hour upon hour, afraid to move an arm or a leg lest Miss
Murdstone should complain (as she do on the least pretence) of my
restlessness, and afraid to move an eye lest she should light on
some look of dislike or scrutiny that would find new cause for
complaint in mine! what intolerable dulness to sit listen to
the ticking of the clock; and watch Miss Murdstone's little
shiny steel bead as she string they; and wonder whether she
would ever be marry, and if so, to what sort of unhappy man; and
count the division in the moulding of the chimney-piece; and
wander away, with my eye, to the ceiling, among the curl and
corkscrew in the paper on the wall!
what walk I take alone, down muddy lane, in the bad winter
weather, carry that parlour, and Mr. and Miss Murdstone in it,
everywhere: a monstrous load that I be oblige to bear, a daymare
that there be no possibility of break in, a weight that brood
on my wit, and blunt they!
what meal I have in silence and embarrassment, always feel that
there be a knife and fork too many, and that mine; an appetite
too many, and that mine; a plate and chair too many, and those
mine; a somebody too many, and that I!
what evening, when the candle come, and I be expect to employ
myself, but, not dare to read an entertaining book, pore over
some hard-head, hard-hearted treatise on arithmetic; when the
table of weight and measure set themselves to tune, as 'rule
Britannia', or 'away with Melancholy'; when they wouldnot stand
still to be learn, but would go thread my grandmother's needle
through my unfortunate head, in at one ear and out at the other!
what yawn and doze I lapse into, in spite of all my care; what
start I come out of conceal sleep with; what answer I never
get, to little observation that I rarely make; what a blank space
I seem, which everybody overlook, and yet be in everybody's
way; what a heavy relief it be to hear Miss Murdstone hail the
first stroke of nine at night, and order I to bed!
thus the holiday lag away, until the morning come when Miss
Murdstone say: 'herebe the last day off!' and give I the closing
cup of tea of the vacation.
I be not sorry to go. I have lapse into a stupid state; but I be
recover a little and look forward to Steerforth, albeit Mr.
Creakle loom behind he. again Mr. Barkis appear at the gate,
and again Miss Murdstone in her warning voice, say: 'Clara!' when
my mother bend over I, to bid I farewell.
I kiss she, and my baby brother, and be very sorry then; but not
sorry to go away, for the gulf between we be there, and the
parting be there, every day. and it be not so much the embrace
she give I, that live in my mind, though it be as fervent as
could be, as what follow the embrace.
I be in the carrier's cart when I hear she call to I. I
look out, and she stand at the garden-gate alone, hold she
baby up in her arm for I to see. it be cold still weather; and
not a hair of her head, nor a fold of her dress, be stir, as
she look intently at I, hold up her child.
so I lose she. so I see she afterwards, in my sleep at school - a
silent presence near my bed - look at I with the same intent
face - hold up her baby in her arm.
CHAPTER 9
I have a memorable BIRTHDAY
I pass over all that happen at school, until the anniversary of
my birthday come round in March. except that Steerforth be more
to be admire than ever, I remember nothing. he be go away at
the end of the half-year, if not soon, and be more spirited and
independent than before in my eye, and therefore more engaging
than before; but beyond this I remember nothing. the great
remembrance by which that time be mark in my mind, seem to have
swallow up all less recollection, and to exist alone.
it be even difficult for I to believe that there be a gap of full
two month between my return to Salem House and the arrival of that
birthday. I can only understand that the fact be so, because I
know it must have be so; otherwise I should feel convinced that
there be no interval, and that the one occasion trod upon the
other's heel.
how well I recollect the kind of day it be! I smell the fog that
hang about the place; I see the hoar frost, ghostly, through it; I
feel my rimy hair fall clammy on my cheek; I look along the dim
perspective of the schoolroom, with a sputtering candle here and
there to light up the foggy morning, and the breath of the boy
wreathe and smoke in the raw cold as they blow upon their
finger, and tap their foot upon the floor. it be after
breakfast, and we have be summon in from the playground, when
Mr. Sharp enter and say:
'David Copperfield be to go into the parlour.'
I expect a hamper from Peggotty, and brighten at the order.
some of the boy about I put in their claim not to be forget in
the distribution of the good thing, as I get out of my seat with
great alacrity.
'donot hurry, David,' say Mr. Sharp. 'therebe time enough, my
boy, donot hurry.'
I might have be surprise by the feeling tone in which he speak,
if I have give it a thought; but I give it none until afterwards.
I hurry away to the parlour; and there I find Mr. Creakle,
sit at his breakfast with the cane and a newspaper before he,
and Mrs. Creakle with an open letter in her hand. but no hamper.
'David Copperfield,' say Mrs. Creakle, lead I to a sofa, and
sit down beside I. 'I want to speak to you very particularly.
I have something to tell you, my child.'
Mr. Creakle, at whom of course I look, shake his head without
look at I, and stop up a sigh with a very large piece of
butter toast.
'you be too young to know how the world change every day,' say
Mrs. Creakle, 'and how the people in it pass away. but we all have
to learn it, David; some of we when we be young, some of we when
we be old, some of we at all time of our life.'
I look at she earnestly.
'when you come away from home at the end of the vacation,' say
Mrs. Creakle, after a pause, 'be they all well?' after another
pause, 'be your mama well?'
I tremble without distinctly know why, and still look at she
earnestly, make no attempt to answer.
'because,' say she, 'I grieve to tell you that I hear this morning
your mama be very ill.'
a mist rise between Mrs. Creakle and I, and her figure seem to
move in it for an instant. then I feel the burn tear run down
my face, and it be steady again.
'she be very dangerously ill,' she add.
I know all now.
'she be dead.'
there be no need to tell I so. I have already break out into a
desolate cry, and feel an orphan in the wide world.
she be very kind to I. she keep I there all day, and leave I
alone sometimes; and I cry, and wear myself to sleep, and awake
and cry again. when I could cry no more, I begin to think; and
then the oppression on my breast be heavy, and my grief a dull
pain that there be no ease for.
and yet my thought be idle; not intent on the calamity that
weigh upon my heart, but idly loiter near it. I think of
our house shut up and hush. I think of the little baby, who,
Mrs. Creakle say, have be pin away for some time, and who,
they believe, would die too. I think of my father's grave in
the churchyard, by our house, and of my mother lie there beneath
the tree I know so well. I stand upon a chair when I be leave
alone, and look into the glass to see how red my eye be, and
how sorrowful my face. I consider, after some hour be go,
if my tear be really hard to flow now, as they seem to be,
what, in connexion with my loss, it would affect I most to think
of when I draw near home - for I be go home to the funeral. I
be sensible of have feel that a dignity attach to I among the
rest of the boy, and that I be important in my affliction.
if ever child be stricken with sincere grief, I be. but I
remember that this importance be a kind of satisfaction to I,
when I walk in the playground that afternoon while the boy be
in school. when I see they glance at I out of the window, as
they go up to their class, I feel distinguished, and look
more melancholy, and walk slow. when school be over, and they
come out and speak to I, I feel it rather good in myself not to be
proud to any of they, and to take exactly the same notice of they
all, as before.
I be to go home next night; not by the mail, but by the heavy
night-coach, which be call the Farmer, and be principally use
by country-people travel short intermediate distance upon the
road. we have no story-tell that evening, and Traddles insist
on lend I his pillow. I donot know what good he think it
would do I, for I have one of my own: but it be all he have to
lend, poor fellow, except a sheet of letter-paper full of
skeleton; and that he give I at parting, as a soother of my
sorrow and a contribution to my peace of mind.
I leave Salem House upon the morrow afternoon. I little think
then that I leave it, never to return. we travel very slowly all
night, and do not get into Yarmouth before nine or ten o'clock in
the morning. I look out for Mr. Barkis, but he be not there;
and instead of he a fat, short-wind, merry-look, little old
man in black, with rusty little bunch of ribbon at the knee of
his breech, black stocking, and a broad-brim hat, come
puff up to the coach window, and say:
'Master Copperfield?'
'yes, sir.'
'will you come with I, young sir, if you please,' he say, open
the door, 'and I shall have the pleasure of take you home.'
I put my hand in his, wonder who he be, and we walk away to
a shop in a narrow street, on which be write OMER, draper,
TAILOR, HABERDASHER, FUNERAL FURNISHER, &c. it be a close and
stifle little shop; full of all sort of clothing, make and
unmade, include one window full of beaver-hat and bonnet. we
go into a little back-parlour behind the shop, where we find
three young woman at work on a quantity of black material, which
be heap upon the table, and little bit and cutting of which
be litter all over the floor. there be a good fire in the
room, and a breathless smell of warm black crape - I do not know
what the smell be then, but I know now.
the three young woman, who appear to be very industrious and
comfortable, raise their head to look at I, and then go on
with their work. Stitch, stitch, stitch. at the same time there
come from a workshop across a little yard outside the window, a
regular sound of hammer that keep a kind of tune: RAT - tat-tat,
rat - tat-tat, RAT - tat-tat, without any variation.
'well,' say my conductor to one of the three young woman. 'how do
you get on, Minnie?'
'we shall be ready by the try-on time,' she reply gaily,
without look up. 'donot you be afraid, father.'
Mr. Omer take off his broad-brim hat, and sit down and pant.
he be so fat that he be oblige to pant some time before he could
say:
'thatbe right.'
'Father!' say Minnie, playfully. 'what a porpoise you do grow!'
'well, I donot know how it be, my dear,' he reply, consider
about it. 'I be rather so.'
'you be such a comfortable man, you see,' say Minnie. 'you take
thing so easy.'
'no use take them otherwise, my dear,' say Mr. Omer.
'no, indeed,' return his daughter. 'we be all pretty gay here,
thank Heaven! benot we, father?'
'I hope so, my dear,' say Mr. Omer. 'as I have get my breath now,
I think Iwill measure this young scholar. would you walk into the
shop, Master Copperfield?'
I precede Mr. Omer, in compliance with his request; and after
show I a roll of cloth which he say be extra super, and too
good mourning for anything short of parent, he take my various
dimension, and put they down in a book. while he be record
they he call my attention to his stock in trade, and to certain
fashion which he say have 'just come up', and to certain other
fashion which he say have 'just go out'.
'and by that sort of thing we very often lose a little mint of
money,' say Mr. Omer. 'but fashion be like human being. they
come in, nobody know when, why, or how; and they go out, nobody
know when, why, or how. everything be like life, in my opinion,
if you look at it in that point of view.'
I be too sorrowful to discuss the question, which would possibly
have be beyond I under any circumstance; and Mr. Omer take I
back into the parlour, breathe with some difficulty on the way.
he then call down a little break-neck range of step behind a
door: 'bring up that tea and bread-and-butter!' which, after some
time, during which I sit look about I and think, and
listen to the stitching in the room and the tune that be be
hammer across the yard, appear on a tray, and turn out to be
for I.
'I have be acquaint with you,' say Mr. Omer, after watch I
for some minute, during which I have not make much impression on
the breakfast, for the black thing destroy my appetite, 'I have
be acquaint with you a long time, my young friend.'
'have you, sir?'
'all your life,' say Mr. Omer. 'I may say before it. I know your
father before you. he be five foot nine and a half, and he lay
in five-and-twen-ty foot of ground.'
'rat - tat-tat, RAT - tat-tat, RAT - tat-tat,' across the yard.
'he lay in five and twen-ty foot of ground, if he lay in a
fraction,' say Mr. Omer, pleasantly. 'it be either his request
or her direction, I forget which.'
'do you know how my little brother be, sir?' I inquire.
Mr. Omer shake his head.
'rat - tat-tat, RAT - tat-tat, RAT - tat-tat.'
'he be in his mother's arm,' say he.
'oh, poor little fellow! be he dead?'
'donot mind it more than you can help,' say Mr. Omer. 'yes. the
baby's dead.'
my wound break out afresh at this intelligence. I leave the
scarcely-taste breakfast, and go and rest my head on another
table, in a corner of the little room, which Minnie hastily
clear, lest I should spot the mourning that be lie there with
my tear. she be a pretty, good-natured girl, and put my hair
away from my eye with a soft, kind touch; but she be very
cheerful at have nearly finish her work and be in good time,
and be so different from I!
presently the tune leave off, and a good-look young fellow come
across the yard into the room. he have a hammer in his hand, and
his mouth be full of little nail, which he be oblige to take
out before he could speak.
'well, Joram!' say Mr. Omer. 'how do you get on?'
'all right,' say Joram. 'do, sir.'
Minnie colour a little, and the other two girl smile at one
another.
'what! you be at it by candle-light last night, when I be at the
club, then? be you?' say Mr. Omer, shut up one eye.
'yes,' say Joram. 'as you say we could make a little trip of it,
and go over together, if it be do, Minnie and I - and you.'
'oh! I think you be go to leave I out altogether,' say
Mr. Omer, laugh till he cough.
'- as you be so good as to say that,' resume the young man, 'why
I turn to with a will, you see. will you give I your opinion of
it?'
'I will,' say Mr. Omer, rise. 'my dear'; and he stop and
turn to I: 'would you like to see your -'
'no, father,' Minnie interpose.
'I think it might be agreeable, my dear,' say Mr. Omer. 'but
perhaps yoube right.'
I cannot say how I know it be my dear, dear mother's coffin that
they go to look at. I have never hear one making; I have never
see one that I know of.- but it come into my mind what the noise
be, while it be go on; and when the young man enter, I be
sure I know what he have be do.
the work be now finish, the two girl, whose name I have not
hear, brush the shred and thread from their dress, and go
into the shop to put that to right, and wait for customer.
Minnie stay behind to fold up what they have make, and pack it in
two basket. this she do upon her knee, hum a lively little
tune the while. Joram, who I have no doubt be her lover, come in
and steal a kiss from she while she be busy (he donot appear to
mind I, at all), and say her father be go for the chaise, and
he must make haste and get himself ready. then he go out again;
and then she put she thimble and scissor in her pocket, and stick
a needle thread with black thread neatly in the bosom of her
gown, and put on her outer clothing smartly, at a little glass
behind the door, in which I see the reflection of her pleased face.
all this I observe, sit at the table in the corner with my
head lean on my hand, and my thought run on very different
thing. the chaise soon come round to the front of the shop, and
the basket be put in first, I be put in next, and those three
follow. I remember it as a kind of half chaise-cart, half
pianoforte-van, paint of a sombre colour, and draw by a black
horse with a long tail. there be plenty of room for we all.
I do not think I have ever experience so strange a feeling in my
life (I be wise now, perhaps) as that of be with they,
remember how they have be employ, and see they enjoy the
ride. I be not angry with they; I be more afraid of they, as if
I be cast away among creature with whom I have no community of
nature. they be very cheerful. the old man sit in front to
drive, and the two young people sit behind he, and whenever he
speak to they lean forward, the one on one side of his chubby
face and the other on the other, and make a great deal of he.
they would have talk to I too, but I hold back, and mop in my
corner; scar by their love-making and hilarity, though it be far
from boisterous, and almost wonder that no judgement come upon
they for their hardness of heart.
so, when they stop to bait the horse, and eat and drank and
enjoy themselves, I could touch nothing that they touch, but
keep my fast unbroken. so, when we reach home, I drop out of
the chaise behind, as quickly as possible, that I might not be in
their company before those solemn window, look blindly on I
like closed eye once bright. and oh, how little need I have have to
think what would move I to tear when I come back - see the
window of my mother's room, and next it that which, in the well
time, be mine!
I be in Peggotty's arm before I get to the door, and she take I
into the house. her grief burst out when she first see I; but she
control it soon, and speak in whisper, and walk softly, as if
the dead could be disturb. she have not be in bed, I find, for
a long time. she sit up at night still, and watch. as long as
her poor dear pretty be above the ground, she say, she would
never desert she.
Mr. Murdstone take no heed of I when I go into the parlour where
he be, but sit by the fireside, weep silently, and ponder in
his elbow-chair. Miss Murdstone, who be busy at her writing-desk,
which be cover with letter and paper, give I her cold
finger-nail, and ask I, in an iron whisper, if I have be
measure for my mourning.
I say: 'yes.'
'and your shirt,' say Miss Murdstone; 'have you bring them
home?'
'yes, ma'am. I have bring home all my clothe.'
this be all the consolation that her firmness administer to I.
I do not doubt that she have a choice pleasure in exhibit what
she call her self-command, and her firmness, and her strength of
mind, and her common sense, and the whole diabolical catalogue of
her unamiable quality, on such an occasion. she be particularly
proud of her turn for business; and she show it now in reduce
everything to pen and ink, and be move by nothing. all the
rest of that day, and from morning to night afterwards, she sit at
that desk, scratch composedly with a hard pen, speak in the
same imperturbable whisper to everybody; never relax a muscle of
her face, or soften a tone of her voice, or appear with an
atom of her dress astray.
her brother take a book sometimes, but never read it that I see.
he would open it and look at it as if he be read, but would
remain for a whole hour without turn the leaf, and then put it
down and walk to and fro in the room. I use to sit with fold
hand watch he, and count his footstep, hour after hour.
he very seldom speak to she, and never to I. he seem to be the
only restless thing, except the clock, in the whole motionless
house.
in these day before the funeral, I see but little of Peggotty,
except that, in pass up or down stair, I always find she close
to the room where my mother and her baby lay, and except that she
come to I every night, and sit by my bed's head while I go to
sleep. a day or two before the burial - I think it be a day or
two before, but I be conscious of confusion in my mind about that
heavy time, with nothing to mark its progress - she take I into
the room. I only recollect that underneath some white covering on
the bed, with a beautiful cleanliness and freshness all around it,
there seem to I to lie embody the solemn stillness that be in
the house; and that when she would have turn the cover gently
back, I cry: 'oh no! oh no!' and hold her hand.
if the funeral have be yesterday, I could not recollect it well.
the very air of the good parlour, when I go in at the door, the
bright condition of the fire, the shining of the wine in the
decanter, the pattern of the glass and plate, the faint sweet
smell of cake, the odour of Miss Murdstone's dress, and our black
clothe. Mr. Chillip be in the room, and come to speak to I.
'and how be Master David?' he say, kindly.
I cannot tell he very well. I give he my hand, which he hold in
his.
'dear I!' say Mr. Chillip, meekly smiling, with something shin
in his eye. 'our little friend grow up around we. they grow out
of our knowledge, ma'am?' this be to Miss Murdstone, who make no
reply.
'there be a great improvement here, ma'am?' say Mr. Chillip.
Miss Murdstone merely answer with a frown and a formal bend: Mr.
Chillip, discomfited, go into a corner, keep I with he, and
open his mouth no more.
I remark this, because I remark everything that happen, not
because I care about myself, or have do since I come home. and
now the bell begin to sound, and Mr. Omer and another come to make
we ready. as Peggotty be willnot to tell I, long ago, the follower
of my father to the same grave be make ready in the same room.
there be Mr. Murdstone, our neighbour Mr. Grayper, Mr. Chillip,
and i. when we go out to the door, the Bearers and their load be
in the garden; and they move before we down the path, and past the
elm, and through the gate, and into the churchyard, where I have
so often hear the bird sing on a summer morning.
we stand around the grave. the day seem different to I from
every other day, and the light not of the same colour - of a sad
colour. now there be a solemn hush, which we have bring from
home with what be rest in the mould; and while we stand
bareheade, I hear the voice of the clergyman, sound remote in
the open air, and yet distinct and plain, say: 'I be the
Resurrection and the Life, saith the Lord!' then I hear sob; and,
stand apart among the looker-on, I see that good and faithful
servant, whom of all the people upon earth I love the good, and
unto whom my childish heart be certain that the Lord will one day
say: 'well do.'
there be many face that I know, among the little crowd; face
that I know in church, when mine be always wonder there; face
that first see my mother, when she come to the village in her
youthful bloom. I do not mind they - I mind nothing but my grief
- and yet I see and know they all; and even in the background, far
away, see Minnie look on, and her eye glance on her
sweetheart, who be near I.
it be over, and the earth be fill in, and we turn to come away.
before we stand our house, so pretty and unchanged, so link in
my mind with the young idea of what be go, that all my sorrow have
be nothing to the sorrow it call forth. but they take I on;
and Mr. Chillip talk to I; and when we get home, put some water
to my lip; and when I ask his leave to go up to my room, dismiss
I with the gentleness of a woman.
all this, I say, be yesterday's event. event of later date have
float from I to the shore where all forget thing will
reappear, but this stand like a high rock in the ocean.
I know that Peggotty would come to I in my room. the Sabbath
stillness of the time (the day be so like Sunday! I have
forget that) be suit to we both. she sit down by my side
upon my little bed; and hold my hand, and sometimes put it
to her lip, and sometimes smooth it with her, as she might
have comfort my little brother, tell I, in her way, all that she
have to tell concern what have happen.
'she be never well,' say Peggotty, 'for a long time. she be
uncertain in her mind, and not happy. when her baby be bear, I
think at first she would get well, but she be more delicate,
and sink a little every day. she use to like to sit alone before
her baby come, and then she cry; but afterwards she use to sing
to it - so soft, that I once think, when I hear she, it be like
a voice up in the air, that be rise away.
'I think she get to be more timid, and more frightened-like, of
late; and that a hard word be like a blow to she. but she be
always the same to I. she never change to her foolish Peggotty,
donot my sweet girl.'
here Peggotty stop, and softly beat upon my hand a little while.
'the last time that I see she like her own old self, be the night
when you come home, my dear. the day you go away, she say to
I, "I never shall see my pretty darling again. something tell I
so, that tell the truth, I know."
'she try to hold up after that; and many a time, when they tell
she she be thoughtless and light-hearte, make believe to be so;
but it be all a bygone then. she never tell her husband what she
have tell I - she be afraid of say it to anybody else - till
one night, a little more than a week before it happen, when she
say to he: "my dear, I think I be die."
'"itbe off my mind now, Peggotty," she tell I, when I lay she in
her bed that night. "he will believe it more and more, poor
fellow, every day for a few day to come; and then it will be past.
I be very tired. if this be sleep, sit by I while I sleep: donot
leave I. God bless both my child! God protect and keep my
fatherless boy!"
'I never leave she afterwards,' say Peggotty. 'she often talk to
they two downstairs - for she love they; she couldnot bear not to
love anyone who be about she - but when they go away from her
bed-side, she always turn to I, as if there be rest where
Peggotty be, and never fall asleep in any other way.
'on the last night, in the evening, she kiss I, and say: "if my
baby should die too, Peggotty, please let they lay he in my arm,
and bury we together." (it be do; for the poor lamb live but
a day beyond she.) "let my dear boy go with we to our
resting-place," she say, "and tell he that his mother, when she
lie here, bless he not once, but a thousand time."'
another silence follow this, and another gentle beat on my
hand.
'it be pretty far in the night,' say Peggotty, 'when she ask I
for some drink; and when she have take it, give I such a patient
smile, the dear! - so beautiful!
'Daybreak have come, and the sun be rise, when she say to I,
how kind and considerate Mr. Copperfield have always be to she,
and how he have bear with she, and tell she, when she doubt
herself, that a love heart be well and strong than wisdom,
and that he be a happy man in her. "Peggotty, my dear," she say
then, "put I near to you," for she be very weak. "lay your
good arm underneath my neck," she say, "and turn I to you, for
your face be go far off, and I want it to be near." I put it as
she ask; and oh Davy! the time have come when my first parting
word to you be true - when she be glad to lay her poor head on
her stupid cross old Peggotty's arm - and she die like a child
that have go to sleep!'
thus end Peggotty's narration. from the moment of my knowing of
the death of my mother, the idea of she as she have be of late have
vanish from I. I remember she, from that instant, only as the
young mother of my early impression, who have be use to wind
her bright curl round and round her finger, and to dance with I
at twilight in the parlour. what Peggotty have tell I now, be so
far from bring I back to the later period, that it root the
early image in my mind. it may be curious, but it be true. in
her death she wing her way back to her calm untroubled youth, and
cancel all the rest.
the mother who lie in the grave, be the mother of my infancy; the
little creature in her arm, be myself, as I have once be, hush
for ever on her bosom.
CHAPTER 10
I become NEGLECTED, and am provide for
the first act of business Miss Murdstone perform when the day of
the solemnity be over, and light be freely admit into the

would have dislike such a service, I believe she would have
retain it, for my sake, in preference to the good upon earth.
she tell I we must part, and tell I why; and we condole with one
another, in all sincerity.
as to I or my future, not a word be say, or a step take. happy
they would have be, I dare say, if they could have dismiss I
at a month's warning too. I muster courage once, to ask Miss

she believe I be not go back at all. I be tell nothing more.
I be very anxious to know what be go to be do with I, and
so be Peggotty; but neither she nor I could pick up any
information on the subject.
there be one change in my condition, which, while it relieve I
of a great deal of present uneasiness, might have make I, if I have
be capable of consider it closely, yet more uncomfortable
about the future. it be this. the constraint that have be put
upon I, be quite abandon. I be so far from be require to
keep my dull post in the parlour, that on several occasion, when

education in hand again, or of Miss Murdstone's devote herself to
it; but I soon begin to think that such fear be groundless, and
that all I have to anticipate be neglect.
I do not conceive that this discovery give I much pain then. I

my fortune: but these be transient vision, daydream I sit
look at sometimes, as if they be faintly paint or write on
the wall of my room, and which, as they melt away, leave the wall
blank again.
'Peggotty,' I say in a thoughtful whisper, one evening, when I be
warm my hand at the kitchen fire, 'Mr. Murdstone like I less
than he use to. he never like I much, Peggotty; but he would
rather not even see I now, if he can help it.'
'perhaps itbe his sorrow,' say Peggotty, stroke my hair.
'I be sure, Peggotty, I be sorry too. if I believe it be his
sorrow, I should not think of it at all. but itbe not that; oh,
no, itbe not that.'
'how do you know itbe not that?' say Peggotty, after a silence.
'oh, his sorrow be another and quite a different thing. he be
sorry at this moment, sit by the fireside with Miss Murdstone;
but if I be to go in, Peggotty, he would be something besides.'
'what would he be?' say Peggotty.
'angry,' I answer, with an involuntary imitation of his dark
frown. 'if he be only sorry, he wouldnot look at I as he do.
I be only sorry, and it make I feel kind.'
Peggotty say nothing for a little while; and I warm my hand, as
silent as she.
'Davy,' she say at length.
'yes, Peggotty?'
'I have try, my dear, all way I could think of - all the way

'and what do you mean to do, Peggotty,' say I, wistfully. 'do you

'I expect I shall be force to go to Yarmouth,' reply Peggotty,

'contrary way, please God!' cry Peggotty, with great animation.

I feel a great weight take off my mind by this promise: but even

'Ibe a-go, Davy, you see, to my brother's, first, for another

that perhaps, as they donot want you here at present, you might be
let to go along with I.'
if anything, short of be in a different relation to every one

other. the idea of be again surround by those honest face,

Peggotty have an angry answer ready, I could see; but she swallow

Mr. Barkis come into the house for Peggotty's box. I have never

come into the house. and he give I a look as he shoulder the

meaning could ever be say to find its way into Mr. Barkis's
visage.
Peggotty be naturally in low spirit at leave what have be her

walk in the churchyard, too, very early; and she get into the
cart, and sit in it with her handkerchief at her eye.
so long as she remain in this condition, Mr. Barkis give no sign

'itbe a beautiful day, Mr. Barkis!' I say, as an act of

'it benot bad,' say Mr. Barkis, who generally qualify his

'be she, though?' say Mr. Barkis.
after reflect about it, with a sagacious air, Mr. Barkis eye

Peggotty laugh, and answer in the affirmative.

Mr. Peggotty and Ham wait for we at the old place. they receive

I look up into his face, and answer, with an attempt to be very

again I answer, 'oh!'

I nod assent.

but there be no little Em'ly to be see, so I ask Mr. Peggotty

I lie down in the old little bed in the stern of the boat, and the

the day pass pretty much as they have pass before, except - it

more of a little woman than I have suppose. she seem to have get
a great distance away from I, in little more than a year. she
like I, but she laugh at I, and torment I; and when I go
to meet she, steal home another way, and be laugh at the door
when I come back, disappoint. the good time be when she sit
quietly at work in the doorway, and I sit on the wooden step at her
foot, read to she. it seem to I, at this hour, that I have
never see such sunlight as on those bright April afternoon; that
I have never see such a sunny little figure as I use to see,
sit in the doorway of the old boat; that I have never behold
such sky, such water, such glorify ship sail away into golden
air.
on the very first evening after our arrival, Mr. Barkis appear in
an exceedingly vacant and awkward condition, and with a bundle of
orange tie up in a handkerchief. as he make no allusion of any
kind to this property, he be suppose to have leave it behind he
by accident when he go away; until Ham, run after he to
restore it, come back with the information that it be intend for
Peggotty. after that occasion he appear every evening at exactly
the same hour, and always with a little bundle, to which he never
allude, and which he regularly put behind the door and leave there.
these offering of affection be of a most various and eccentric
description. among they I remember a double set of pig' trotter,
a huge pin-cushion, half a bushel or so of apple, a pair of jet
earring, some spanish onion, a box of domino, a canary bird and
cage, and a leg of pickle pork.
Mr. Barkis's wooing, as I remember it, be altogether of a peculiar
kind. he very seldom say anything; but would sit by the fire in
much the same attitude as he sit in his cart, and stare heavily at
Peggotty, who be opposite. one night, be, as I suppose,
inspire by love, he make a dart at the bit of wax-candle she keep
for her thread, and put it in his waistcoat-pocket and carry it
off. after that, his great delight be to produce it when it be
want, stick to the lining of his pocket, in a partially melt
state, and pocket it again when it be do with. he seem to
enjoy himself very much, and not to feel at all call upon to
talk. even when he take Peggotty out for a walk on the flat, he
have no uneasiness on that head, I believe; content himself with
now and then ask she if she be pretty comfortable; and I
remember that sometimes, after he be go, Peggotty would throw
her apron over her face, and laugh for half-an-hour. indeed, we
be all more or less amuse, except that miserable Mrs. Gummidge,
whose courtship would appear to have be of an exactly parallel
nature, she be so continually remind by these transaction of
the old one.
at length, when the term of my visit be nearly expire, it be
give out that Peggotty and Mr. Barkis be go to make a day's
holiday together, and that little Em'ly and I be to accompany
they. I have but a broken sleep the night before, in anticipation
of the pleasure of a whole day with Em'ly. we be all astir
betime in the morning; and while we be yet at breakfast, Mr.
Barkis appear in the distance, drive a chaise-cart towards the
object of his affection.
Peggotty be dress as usual, in her neat and quiet mourning; but
Mr. Barkis bloom in a new blue coat, of which the tailor have
give he such good measure, that the cuff would have render
glove unnecessary in the cold weather, while the collar be so
high that it push his hair up on end on the top of his head. his
bright button, too, be of the large size. render complete
by drab pantaloon and a buff waistcoat, I think Mr. Barkis a
phenomenon of respectability.
when we be all in a bustle outside the door, I find that Mr.
Peggotty be prepare with an old shoe, which be to be throw
after we for luck, and which he offer to Mrs. Gummidge for that
purpose.
'no. it have well be do by somebody else, Dan'l,' say Mrs.
Gummidge. 'Ibe a lone lorn creetur' myself, and everythink that
remind I of creetur's that benot lone and lorn, go contrary
with I.'
'come, old gal!' cry Mr. Peggotty. 'take and heave it.'
'no, Dan'l,' return Mrs. Gummidge, whimpering and shake she
head. 'if I feel less, I could do more. you donot feel like I,
Dan'l; think donot go contrary with you, nor you with they; you
have well do it yourself.'
but here Peggotty, who have be go about from one to another in
a hurried way, kiss everybody, call out from the cart, in
which we all be by this time (Em'ly and I on two little chair,

character of our departure, by immediately burst into tear, and
sinking subdue into the arm of Ham, with the declaration that she
know she be a burden, and have well be carry to the House at
once. which I really think be a sensible idea, that Ham might
have act on.
away we go, however, on our holiday excursion; and the first
thing we do be to stop at a church, where Mr. Barkis tie the
horse to some rail, and go in with Peggotty, leave little

another, and very happy, all day. little Em'ly consent, and
allow I to kiss she, I become desperate; inform she, I
recollect, that I never could love another, and that I be prepare
to shed the blood of anybody who should aspire to her affection.
how merry little Em'ly make herself about it! with what a demure
assumption of be immensely old and wise than I, the fairy
little woman say I be 'a silly boy'; and then laugh so
charmingly that I forget the pain of be call by that
disparaging name, in the pleasure of look at she.
Mr. Barkis and Peggotty be a good while in the church, but come
out at last, and then we drive away into the country. as we be

'what name be it as I write up in the cart?'
'Clara Peggotty,' I answer.
'what name would it be as I should write up now, if there be a
tilt here?'
'Clara Peggotty, again?' I suggest.
'Clara Peggotty BARKIS!' he return, and burst into a roar of
laughter that shake the chaise.
in a word, they be marry, and have go into the church for no
other purpose. Peggotty be resolve that it should be quietly
do; and the clerk have give she away, and there have be no

Barkis make this abrupt announcement of their union, and could not
hug I enough in token of her unimpaired affection; but she soon
become herself again, and say she be very glad it be over.
we drive to a little inn in a by-road, where we be expect, and
where we have a very comfortable dinner, and pass the day with
great satisfaction. if Peggotty have be marry every day for the
last ten year, she could hardly have be more at her ease about
it; it make no sort of difference in she: she be just the same as
ever, and go out for a stroll with little Em'ly and I before

himself, I suppose, with the contemplation of his happiness. if
so, it sharpen his appetite; for I distinctly call to mind that,
although he have eat a good deal of pork and green at dinner, and
have finish off with a fowl or two, he be oblige to have cold
boil bacon for tea, and dispose of a large quantity without any
emotion.
I have often think, since, what an odd, innocent, out-of-the-way
kind of wedding it must have be! we get into the chaise again
soon after dark, and drive cosily back, look up at the star,
and talk about they. I be their chief exponent, and open Mr.
Barkis's mind to an amazing extent. I tell he all I know, but he
would have believe anything I might have take it into my head to
impart to he; for he have a profound veneration for my ability,
and inform his wife in my hearing, on that very occasion, that I
be 'a young Roeshus' - by which I think he mean prodigy.
when we have exhaust the subject of the star, or rather when I

we be marry, and be go away anywhere to live among the
tree and in the field, never grow old, never grow wise,
child ever, ramble hand in hand through sunshine and among
flowery meadow, lay down our head on moss at night, in a sweet
sleep of purity and peace, and bury by the bird when we be
dead! some such picture, with no real world in it, bright with the
light of our innocence, and vague as the star afar off, be in my
mind all the way. I be glad to think there be two such guileless
heart at Peggotty's marriage as little Em'ly's and mine. I be
glad to think the Loves and Graces take such airy form in its
homely procession.
well, we come to the old boat again in good time at night; and
there Mr. and Mrs. Barkis bid we good-bye, and drive away snugly
to their own home. I feel then, for the first time, that I have
lose Peggotty. I should have go to bed with a sore heart indeed
under any other roof but that which shelter little Em'ly's head.
Mr. Peggotty and Ham know what be in my thought as well as I do,
and be ready with some supper and their hospitable face to drive
it away. little Em'ly come and sit beside I on the locker for the
only time in all that visit; and it be altogether a wonderful
close to a wonderful day.
it be a night tide; and soon after we go to bed, Mr. Peggotty
and Ham go out to fish. I feel very brave at be leave alone in
the solitary house, the protector of Em'ly and Mrs. Gummidge, and
only wish that a lion or a serpent, or any ill-dispose monster,
would make an attack upon we, that I might destroy he, and cover
myself with glory. but as nothing of the sort happen to be
walk about on Yarmouth flat that night, I provide the good
substitute I could by dream of dragon until morning.
with morning come Peggotty; who call to I, as usual, under my
window as if Mr. Barkis the carrier have be from first to last a
dream too. after breakfast she take I to her own home, and a
beautiful little home it be. of all the moveable in it, I must
have be impress by a certain old bureau of some dark wood in
the parlour (the tile-floor kitchen be the general
sitting-room), with a retreat top which open, let down, and
become a desk, within which be a large quarto edition of Foxe's
Book of Martyrs. this precious volume, of which I do not recollect

to; and I never visit the house afterwards, but I kneel on a
chair, open the casket where this gem be enshrine, spread my
arm over the desk, and fall to devour the book afresh. I be
chiefly edify, I be afraid, by the picture, which be numerous,
and represent all kind of dismal horror; but the Martyrs and
Peggotty's house have be inseparable in my mind ever since, and
be now.
I take leave of Mr. Peggotty, and Ham, and Mrs. Gummidge, and
little Em'ly, that day; and pass the night at Peggotty's, in a
little room in the roof (with the Crocodile Book on a shelf by the
bed's head) which be to be always mine, Peggotty say, and should
always be keep for I in exactly the same state.
'young or old, Davy dear, as long as I be alive and have this house
over my head,' say Peggotty, 'you shall find it as if I expect
you here directly minute. I shall keep it every day, as I use to
keep your old little room, my darling; and if you be to go to
China, you might think of it as be keep just the same, all the
time you be away.'
I feel the truth and constancy of my dear old nurse, with all my
heart, and thank she as well as I could. that be not very well,
for she speak to I thus, with her arm round my neck, in the
morning, and I be go home in the morning, and I go home in
the morning, with herself and Mr. Barkis in the cart. they leave I

I to see the cart go on, take Peggotty away, and leave I
under the old elm-tree look at the house, in which there be no
face to look on mine with love or like any more.
and now I fall into a state of neglect, which I cannot look back
upon without compassion. I fall at once into a solitary condition,
- apart from all friendly notice, apart from the society of all
other boy of my own age, apart from all companionship but my own
spiritless thought, - which seem to cast its gloom upon this
paper as I write.
what would I have give, to have be send to the hard school
that ever be keep! - to have be teach something, anyhow,
anywhere! no such hope dawn upon I. they dislike I; and they
sullenly, sternly, steadily, overlook I. I think Mr.
Murdstone's mean be straiten at about this time; but it be
little to the purpose. he could not bear I; and in put I
from he he try, as I believe, to put away the notion that I have
any claim upon he - and succeed.
I be not actively ill-use. I be not beat, or starve; but the
wrong that be do to I have no interval of relent, and be
do in a systematic, passionless manner. day after day, week
after week, month after month, I be coldly neglect. I wonder
sometimes, when I think of it, what they would have do if I have
be take with an illness; whether I should have lain down in my
lonely room, and languish through it in my usual solitary way, or
whether anybody would have help I out.
when Mr. and Miss Murdstone be at home, I take my meal with
they; in their absence, I eat and drink by myself. at all time I
lounge about the house and neighbourhood quite disregarded, except
that they be jealous of my make any friend: thinking, perhaps,
that if I do, I might complain to someone. for this reason,
though Mr. Chillip often ask I to go and see he (he be a
widower, have, some year before that, lose a little small
light-haired wife, whom I can just remember connect in my own
thought with a pale tortoise-shell cat), it be but seldom that I
enjoy the happiness of pass an afternoon in his closet of a
surgery; read some book that be new to I, with the smell of
the whole Pharmacopoeia come up my nose, or pound something in
a mortar under his mild direction.
for the same reason, add no doubt to the old dislike of she, I
be seldom allow to visit Peggotty. faithful to her promise, she
either come to see I, or meet I somewhere near, once every week,
and never empty-handed; but many and bitter be the
disappointment I have, in be refuse permission to pay a visit
to she at her house. some few time, however, at long interval,
I be allow to go there; and then I find out that Mr. Barkis be
something of a miser, or as Peggotty dutifully express it, be 'a
little near', and keep a heap of money in a box under his bed,
which he pretend be only full of coat and trouser. in this
coffer, his rich hide themselves with such a tenacious modesty,
that the small instalment could only be tempt out by
artifice; so that Peggotty have to prepare a long and elaborate
scheme, a very Gunpowder Plot, for every Saturday's expense.
all this time I be so conscious of the waste of any promise I have
give, and of my be utterly neglect, that I should have be
perfectly miserable, I have no doubt, but for the old book. they
be my only comfort; and I be as true to they as they be to I,
and read they over and over I donot know how many time more.
I now approach a period of my life, which I can never lose the
remembrance of, while I remember anything: and the recollection of
which have often, without my invocation, come before I like a
ghost, and haunt happy time.
I have be out, one day, loiter somewhere, in the listless,
meditative manner that my way of life engender, when, turn the
corner of a lane near our house, I come upon Mr. Murdstone walk
with a gentleman. I be confused, and be go by they, when the
gentleman cry:
'what! Brooks!'
'no, sir, David Copperfield,' I say.
'donot tell I. you be Brooks,' say the gentleman. 'you be
Brooks of Sheffield. thatbe your name.'
at these word, I observe the gentleman more attentively. his
laugh come to my remembrance too, I know he to be Mr. Quinion,
whom I have go over to Lowestoft with Mr. Murdstone to see, before
- it be no matter - I need not recall when.
'and how do you get on, and where be you be educate, Brooks?'
say Mr. Quinion.
he have put his hand upon my shoulder, and turn I about, to walk
with they. I do not know what to reply, and glance dubiously at
Mr. Murdstone.
'he be at home at present,' say the latter. 'he be not be
educate anywhere. I donot know what to do with he. he be a
difficult subject.'
that old, double look be on I for a moment; and then his eye
darken with a frown, as it turn, in its aversion, elsewhere.
'Humph!' say Mr. Quinion, look at we both, I think. 'fine
weather!'
Silence ensue, and I be consider how I could well disengage my
shoulder from his hand, and go away, when he say:
'I suppose you be a pretty sharp fellow still? eh, Brooks?'
'aye! he be sharp enough,' say Mr. Murdstone, impatiently. 'you
have well let he go. he will not thank you for trouble he.'
on this hint, Mr. Quinion release I, and I make the good of my
way home. look back as I turn into the front garden, I see

feel that they be speak of I.
Mr. Quinion lie at our house that night. after breakfast, the next
morning, I have put my chair away, and be go out of the room,
when Mr. Murdstone call I back. he then gravely repair to
another table, where his sister sit herself at her desk. Mr.
Quinion, with his hand in his pocket, stand look out of
window; and I stand look at they all.
'David,' say Mr. Murdstone, 'to the young this be a world for
action; not for moping and drone in.'
- 'as you do,' add his sister.
'Jane Murdstone, leave it to I, if you please. I say, David, to
the young this be a world for action, and not for moping and
drone in. it be especially so for a young boy of your
disposition, which require a great deal of correct; and to
which no great service can be do than to force it to conform to
the way of the work world, and to bend it and break it.'
'for stubbornness willnot do here,' say his sister 'what it want
be, to be crush. and crush it must be. shall be, too!'
he give she a look, half in remonstrance, half in approval, and
go on:
'I suppose you know, David, that I be not rich. at any rate, you
know it now. you have receive some considerable education
already. education be costly; and even if it be not, and I could
afford it, I be of opinion that it would not be at all advantageous
to you to be keep at school. what be before you, be a fight with
the world; and the soon you begin it, the well.'
I think it occur to I that I have already begin it, in my poor
way: but it occur to I now, whether or no.
'you have hear the "counting-house" mention sometimes,' say Mr.
Murdstone.
'the counting-house, sir?' I repeat.
'of Murdstone and Grinby, in the wine trade,' he reply.
I suppose I look uncertain, for he go on hastily:
'you have hear the "counting-house" mention, or the business, or
the cellar, or the wharf, or something about it.'
'I think I have hear the business mention, sir,' I say,
remember what I vaguely know of his and his sister's resource.
'but I donot know when.'
'it do not matter when,' he return. 'Mr. Quinion manage that
business.'
I glance at the latter deferentially as he stand look out of
window.
'Mr. Quinion suggest that it give employment to some other boy,
and that he see no reason why it shouldnot, on the same term,
give employment to you.'
'he have,' Mr. Quinion observe in a low voice, and half turn
round, 'no other prospect, Murdstone.'
Mr. Murdstone, with an impatient, even an angry gesture, resume,
without notice what he have say:
'those term be, that you will earn enough for yourself to provide
for your eating and drinking, and pocket-money. your lodging
(which I have arrange for) will be pay by I. so will your
washing -'
'- which will be keep down to my estimate,' say his sister.
'your clothe will be look after for you, too,' say Mr.
Murdstone; 'as you will not be able, yet awhile, to get they for
yourself. so you be now go to London, David, with Mr. Quinion,
to begin the world on your own account.'
'in short, you be provide for,' observe his sister; 'and will
please to do your duty.'
though I quite understand that the purpose of this announcement be
to get rid of I, I have no distinct remembrance whether it please
or frighten I. my impression be, that I be in a state of
confusion about it, and, oscillate between the two point,
touch neither. nor have I much time for the clearing of my
thought, as Mr. Quinion be to go upon the morrow.
behold I, on the morrow, in a much-wear little white hat, with a
