hard, stiff corduroy trouser - which Miss Murdstone consider the
good armour for the leg in that fight with the world which be now
to come off. behold I so attired, and with my little worldly all
before I in a small trunk, sitting, a lone lorn child (as Mrs.
Gummidge might have say), in the post-chaise that be carry Mr.
Quinion to the London coach at Yarmouth! see, how our house and
church be lessen in the distance; how the grave beneath the
tree be blot out by intervene object; how the spire point
upwards from my old playground no more, and the sky be empty!
CHAPTER 11
I begin life on my OWN account, and don't like it
I know enough of the world now, to have almost lose the capacity of
be much surprised by anything; but it be matter of some surprise
to I, even now, that I can have be so easily throw away at such
an age. a child of excellent ability, and with strong power of
observation, quick, eager, delicate, and soon hurt bodily or
mentally, it seem wonderful to I that nobody should have make any
sign in my behalf. but none be make; and I became, at ten year
old, a little labour hind in the service of Murdstone and
Grinby.
Murdstone and Grinby's warehouse be at the waterside. it be down
in Blackfriars. modern improvement have alter the place; but it
be the last house at the bottom of a narrow street, curve down
hill to the river, with some stair at the end, where people take
boat. it be a crazy old house with a wharf of its own, abut
on the water when the tide be in, and on the mud when the tide be
out, and literally overrun with rat. its panel room,
discolour with the dirt and smoke of a hundred year, I dare say;
its decay floor and staircase; the squeaking and scuffling of
the old grey rat down in the cellar; and the dirt and rottenness
of the place; be thing, not of many year ago, in my mind, but of
the present instant. they be all before I, just as they be in
the evil hour when I go among they for the first time, with my
trembling hand in Mr. Quinion's.
Murdstone and Grinby's trade be among a good many kind of people,
but an important branch of it be the supply of wine and spirit
to certain packet ship. I forget now where they chiefly go, but
I think there be some among they that make voyage both to the
East and West Indies. I know that a great many empty bottle be
one of the consequence of this traffic, and that certain man and
boy be employ to examine they against the light, and reject
those that be flawed, and to rinse and wash they. when the empty
bottle run short, there be label to be paste on full one, or
cork to be fit to they, or seal to be put upon the cork, or
finish bottle to be pack in cask. all this work be my work,
and of the boy employ upon it I be one.
there be three or four of we, count I. my work place be
establish in a corner of the warehouse, where Mr. Quinion could
see I, when he choose to stand up on the bottom rail of his stool
in the counting-house, and look at I through a window above the
desk. hither, on the first morning of my so auspiciously begin
life on my own account, the old of the regular boy be summon
to show I my business. his name be Mick Walker, and he wear a
ragged apron and a paper cap. he inform I that his father be
a bargeman, and walk, in a black velvet head-dress, in the Lord
Mayor's Show. he also inform I that our principal associate
would be another boy whom he introduce by the - to I -
extraordinary name of Mealy Potatoes. I discover, however, that
this youth have not be christen by that name, but that it have
be bestow upon he in the warehouse, on account of his
complexion, which be pale or mealy. Mealy's father be a
waterman, who have the additional distinction of be a fireman,
and be engage as such at one of the large theatre; where some
young relation of Mealy's - I think his little sister - do Imps in
the Pantomimes.
no word can express the secret agony of my soul as I sunk into
this companionship; compare these henceforth everyday associate
with those of my happy childhood - not to say with Steerforth,
traddle, and the rest of those boy; and feel my hope of grow
up to be a learn and distinguished man, crush in my bosom. the
deep remembrance of the sense I have, of be utterly without hope
now; of the shame I feel in my position; of the misery it be to my
young heart to believe that day by day what I have learn, and
thought, and delight in, and raise my fancy and my emulation up
by, would pass away from I, little by little, never to be bring
back any more; cannot be write. as often as Mick Walker go
away in the course of that forenoon, I mingle my tear with the
water in which I be wash the bottle; and sob as if there
be a flaw in my own breast, and it be in danger of burst.
the counting-house clock be at half past twelve, and there be
general preparation for go to dinner, when Mr. Quinion tap at
the counting-house window, and beckon to I to go in. I go in,
and find there a stoutish, middle-aged person, in a brown surtout
and black tight and shoe, with no more hair upon his head (which
be a large one, and very shin) than there be upon an egg, and
with a very extensive face, which he turn full upon I. his
clothe be shabby, but he have an impose shirt-collar on. he
carry a jaunty sort of a stick, with a large pair of rusty
tassel to it; and a quizzing-glass hang outside his coat, - for
ornament, I afterwards find, as he very seldom look through it,
and couldnot see anything when he do.
'this,' say Mr. Quinion, in allusion to myself, 'be he.'
'this,' say the stranger, with a certain condescending roll in his
voice, and a certain indescribable air of do something genteel,
which impress I very much, 'be Master Copperfield. I hope I see
you well, sir?'
I say I be very well, and hop he be. I be sufficiently ill
at ease, Heaven know; but it be not in my nature to complain much
at that time of my life, so I say I be very well, and hop he
be.
'I be,' say the stranger, 'thank Heaven, quite well. I have
receive a letter from Mr. Murdstone, in which he mention that he
would desire I to receive into an apartment in the rear of my
house, which be at present unoccupied - and be, in short, to be let
as a - in short,' say the stranger, with a smile and in a burst of
confidence, 'as a bedroom - the young beginner whom I have now the
pleasure to -' and the stranger wave his hand, and settle his
chin in his shirt-collar.
'this be Mr. Micawber,' say Mr. Quinion to I.
'Ahem!' say the stranger, 'that be my name.'
'Mr. Micawber,' say Mr. Quinion, 'be know to Mr. Murdstone. he
take order for we on commission, when he can get any. he have
be write to by Mr. Murdstone, on the subject of your lodging,
and he will receive you as a lodger.'
'my address,' say Mr. Micawber, 'be Windsor Terrace, City Road.
I - in short,' say Mr. Micawber, with the same genteel air, and in
another burst of confidence - 'I live there.'
I make he a bow.
'under the impression,' say Mr. Micawber, 'that your
peregrination in this metropolis have not as yet be extensive,
and that you might have some difficulty in penetrate the arcana
of the Modern Babylon in the direction of the City Road, - in
short,' say Mr. Micawber, in another burst of confidence, 'that
you might lose yourself - I shall be happy to call this evening,
and install you in the knowledge of the near way.'
I thank he with all my heart, for it be friendly in he to
offer to take that trouble.
'at what hour,' say Mr. Micawber, 'shall I -'
'at about eight,' say Mr. Quinion.
'at about eight,' say Mr. Micawber. 'I beg to wish you good day,
Mr. Quinion. I will intrude no long.'
so he put on his hat, and go out with his cane under his arm:
very upright, and hum a tune when he be clear of the
counting-house.
Mr. Quinion then formally engage I to be as useful as I could in
the warehouse of Murdstone and Grinby, at a salary, I think, of six
shilling a week. I be not clear whether it be six or seven. I
be inclined to believe, from my uncertainty on this head, that it
be six at first and seven afterwards. he pay I a week down
(from his own pocket, I believe), and I give Mealy sixpence out of
it to get my trunk carry to Windsor Terrace that night: it be
too heavy for my strength, small as it be. I pay sixpence more
for my dinner, which be a meat pie and a turn at a neighbouring
pump; and pass the hour which be allow for that meal, in
walk about the street.
at the appoint time in the evening, Mr. Micawber reappear. I
wash my hand and face, to do the great honour to his
gentility, and we walk to our house, as I suppose I must now call
it, together; Mr. Micawber impress the name of street, and the
shape of corner house upon I, as we go along, that I might
find my way back, easily, in the morning.
arrive at this house in Windsor Terrace (which I notice be
shabby like himself, but also, like himself, make all the show it
could), he present I to Mrs. Micawber, a thin and faded lady,
not at all young, who be sit in the parlour (the first floor
be altogether unfurnished, and the blind be keep down to delude
the neighbour), with a baby at her breast. this baby be one of
twin; and I may remark here that I hardly ever, in all my
experience of the family, see both the twin detach from Mrs.
Micawber at the same time. one of they be always take
refreshment.
there be two other child; Master Micawber, aged about four,
and Miss Micawber, aged about three. these, and a
dark-complexion young woman, with a habit of snort, who be
servant to the family, and inform I, before half an hour have
expire, that she be 'a orfling', and come from St. Luke's
workhouse, in the neighbourhood, complete the establishment. my
room be at the top of the house, at the back: a close chamber;
stencil all over with an ornament which my young imagination
represent as a blue muffin; and very scantily furnish.
'I never think,' say Mrs. Micawber, when she come up, twin and
all, to show I the apartment, and sit down to take breath, 'before
I be marry, when I live with papa and mama, that I should ever
find it necessary to take a lodger. but Mr. Micawber be in
difficulty, all consideration of private feeling must give way.'
I say: 'yes, ma'am.'
'Mr. Micawber's difficulty be almost overwhelming just at
present,' say Mrs. Micawber; 'and whether it be possible to bring
he through they, I donot know. when I live at home with papa and
mama, I really should have hardly understand what the word mean,
in the sense in which I now employ it, but experientia do it, -
as papa use to say.'
I cannot satisfy myself whether she tell I that Mr. Micawber have
be an officer in the Marines, or whether I have imagine it. I
only know that I believe to this hour that he be in the Marines
once upon a time, without know why. he be a sort of town
traveller for a number of miscellaneous house, now; but make
little or nothing of it, I be afraid.
'if Mr. Micawber's creditor will not give he time,' say Mrs.
Micawber, 'they must take the consequence; and the soon they
bring it to an issue the well. blood cannot be obtain from a
stone, neither can anything on account be obtain at present (not
to mention law expense) from Mr. Micawber.'
I never can quite understand whether my precocious self-dependence
confuse Mrs. Micawber in reference to my age, or whether she be
so full of the subject that she would have talk about it to the
very twin if there have be nobody else to communicate with, but
this be the strain in which she begin, and she go on accordingly
all the time I know she.
Poor Mrs. Micawber! she say she have try to exert herself, and
so, I have no doubt, she have. the centre of the street door be
perfectly cover with a great brass-plate, on which be engrave
'Mrs. Micawber's Boarding Establishment for Young lady': but I
never find that any young lady have ever be to school there; or
that any young lady ever come, or propose to come; or that the
least preparation be ever make to receive any young lady. the
only visitor I ever see, or hear of, be creditor. they use
to come at all hour, and some of they be quite ferocious. one
dirty-faced man, I think he be a boot-maker, use to edge himself
into the passage as early as seven o'clock in the morning, and call
up the stair to Mr. Micawber - 'Come! you benot out yet, you
know. pay we, will you? donot hide, you know; thatbe mean. I
wouldnot be mean if I be you. pay we, will you? you just pay we,
d'ye hear? come!' receive no answer to these taunt, he would
mount in his wrath to the word 'swindler' and 'robber'; and
these be ineffectual too, would sometimes go to the extremity of
cross the street, and roar up at the window of the second
floor, where he know Mr. Micawber be. at these time, Mr.
Micawber would be transport with grief and mortification, even to
the length (as I be once make aware by a scream from his wife) of
make motion at himself with a razor; but within half-an-hour
afterwards, he would polish up his shoe with extraordinary pain,
and go out, hum a tune with a great air of gentility than
ever. Mrs. Micawber be quite as elastic. I have know she to be
throw into fainting fit by the king's taxis at three o'clock, and
to eat lamb chop, bread, and drink warm ale (pay for with two
tea-spoon that have go to the pawnbroker's) at four. on one
occasion, when an execution have just be put in, come home
through some chance as early as six o'clock, I see she lying (of
course with a twin) under the grate in a swoon, with her hair all
tear about her face; but I never know she more cheerful than she
be, that very same night, over a veal cutlet before the kitchen
fire, tell I story about her papa and mama, and the company
they use to keep.
in this house, and with this family, I pass my leisure time. my
own exclusive breakfast of a penny loaf and a pennyworth of milk,
I provide myself. I keep another small loaf, and a modicum of
cheese, on a particular shelf of a particular cupboard, to make my
supper on when I come back at night. this make a hole in the six
or seven shilling, I know well; and I be out at the warehouse all
day, and have to support myself on that money all the week. from
Monday morning until Saturday night, I have no advice, no counsel,
no encouragement, no consolation, no assistance, no support, of any
kind, from anyone, that I can call to mind, as I hope to go to
heaven!
I be so young and childish, and so little qualified - how could I
be otherwise? - to undertake the whole charge of my own existence,
that often, in go to Murdstone and Grinby's, of a morning, I
could not resist the stale pastry put out for sale at half-price at
the pastrycook' door, and spend in that the money I should have
keep for my dinner. then, I go without my dinner, or buy a
roll or a slice of pudding. I remember two pudding shop, between
which I be divide, accord to my finance. one be in a court
close to St. Martin's Church - at the back of the church, - which
be now remove altogether. the pudding at that shop be make of
currant, and be rather a special pudding, but be dear,
twopennyworth not be large than a pennyworth of more ordinary
pudding. a good shop for the latter be in the Strand - somewhere
in that part which have be rebuild since. it be a stout pale
pudding, heavy and flabby, and with great flat raisin in it, stick
in whole at wide distance apart. it come up hot at about my time
every day, and many a day do I dine off it. when I din
regularly and handsomely, I have a saveloy and a penny loaf, or a
fourpenny plate of red beef from a cook's shop; or a plate of bread
and cheese and a glass of beer, from a miserable old public-house
opposite our place of business, call the Lion, or the Lion and
something else that I have forget. once, I remember carry my
own bread (which I have bring from home in the morning) under my
arm, wrap in a piece of paper, like a book, and go to a
famous alamode beef-house near Drury Lane, and order a 'small
plate' of that delicacy to eat with it. what the waiter think of
such a strange little apparition come in all alone, I donot know;
but I can see he now, star at I as I eat my dinner, and
bring up the other waiter to look. I give he a halfpenny for
himself, and I wish he havenot take it.
we have half-an-hour, I think, for tea. when I have money enough, I
use to get half-a-pint of ready-make coffee and a slice of bread
and butter. when I have none, I use to look at a venison shop in
Fleet Street; or I have stroll, at such a time, as far as Covent
Garden Market, and star at the pineapple. I be fond of
wander about the adelphi, because it be a mysterious place,
with those dark arch. I see myself emerge one evening from
some of these arch, on a little public-house close to the river,
with an open space before it, where some coal-heaver be dance;
to look at whom I sit down upon a bench. I wonder what they
think of I!
I be such a child, and so little, that frequently when I go into
the bar of a strange public-house for a glass of ale or porter, to
moisten what I have have for dinner, they be afraid to give it I.
I remember one hot evening I go into the bar of a public-house,
and say to the landlord:
'what be your good - your very well - ale a glass?' for it be a
special occasion. I donot know what. it may have be my
birthday.
'twopence-halfpenny,' say the landlord, 'be the price of the
Genuine stunning ale.'
'then,' say I, produce the money, 'just draw I a glass of the
Genuine Stunning, if you please, with a good head to it.'
the landlord look at I in return over the bar, from head to
foot, with a strange smile on his face; and instead of draw the
beer, look round the screen and say something to his wife. she
come out from behind it, with her work in her hand, and join he
in survey I. here we stand, all three, before I now. the
landlord in his shirt-sleeve, lean against the bar
window-frame; his wife look over the little half-door; and I, in
some confusion, look up at they from outside the partition.
they ask I a good many question; as, what my name be, how old
I be, where I live, how I be employ, and how I come there. to
all of which, that I might commit nobody, I invent, I be afraid,
appropriate answer. they serve I with the ale, though I suspect
it be not the genuine Stunning; and the landlord's wife, open
the little half-door of the bar, and bend down, give I my money
back, and give I a kiss that be half admiring and half
compassionate, but all womanly and good, I be sure.
I know I do not exaggerate, unconsciously and unintentionally, the
scantiness of my resource or the difficulty of my life. I know
that if a shilling be give I by Mr. Quinion at any time, I
spend it in a dinner or a tea. I know that I work, from morning
until night, with common man and boy, a shabby child. I know that
I lounge about the street, insufficiently and unsatisfactorily
feed. I know that, but for the mercy of God, I might easily have
be, for any care that be take of I, a little robber or a
little vagabond.
yet I hold some station at Murdstone and Grinby's too. besides
that Mr. Quinion do what a careless man so occupy, and deal
with a thing so anomalous, could, to treat I as one upon a
different footing from the rest, I never say, to man or boy, how
it be that I come to be there, or give the least indication of
be sorry that I be there. that I suffer in secret, and that
I suffer exquisitely, no one ever know but i. how much I
suffer, it be, as I have say already, utterly beyond my power to
tell. but I keep my own counsel, and I do my work. I know from
the first, that, if I could not do my work as well as any of the
rest, I could not hold myself above slight and contempt. I soon
become at least as expeditious and as skilful as either of the
other boy. though perfectly familiar with they, my conduct and
manner be different enough from theirs to place a space between
we. they and the man generally speak of I as 'the little gent',
or 'the young Suffolker.' a certain man name Gregory, who be
foreman of the packer, and another name Tipp, who be the carman,
and wear a red jacket, use to address I sometimes as 'David': but
I think it be mostly when we be very confidential, and when I
have make some effort to entertain they, over our work, with some
result of the old reading; which be fast perish out of my
remembrance. Mealy Potatoes uprose once, and rebel against my
be so distinguished; but Mick Walker settle he in no time.
my rescue from this kind of existence I consider quite hopeless,
and abandon, as such, altogether. I be solemnly convinced that
I never for one hour be reconcile to it, or be otherwise than
miserably unhappy; but I bear it; and even to Peggotty, partly for
the love of she and partly for shame, never in any letter (though
many pass between we) reveal the truth.
Mr. Micawber's difficulty be an addition to the distressed
state of my mind. in my forlorn state I become quite attach to
the family, and use to walk about, busy with Mrs. Micawber's
calculation of way and mean, and heavy with the weight of Mr.
Micawber's debt. on a Saturday night, which be my grand treat,
- partly because it be a great thing to walk home with six or
seven shilling in my pocket, look into the shop and think
what such a sum would buy, and partly because I go home early, -
Mrs. Micawber would make the most heart-rend confidence to I;
also on a Sunday morning, when I mix the portion of tea or coffee
I have buy over-night, in a little shaving-pot, and sit late at
my breakfast. it be nothing at all unusual for Mr. Micawber to
sob violently at the beginning of one of these Saturday night
conversation, and sing about jack's delight be his lovely Nan,
towards the end of it. I have know he come home to supper with
a flood of tear, and a declaration that nothing be now leave but
a jail; and go to bed make a calculation of the expense of
put bow-window to the house, 'in case anything turn up',
which be his favourite expression. and Mrs. Micawber be just the
same.
a curious equality of friendship, originating, I suppose, in our
respective circumstance, spring up between I and these people,
notwithstanding the ludicrous disparity in our year. but I never
allow myself to be prevail upon to accept any invitation to eat
and drink with they out of their stock (know that they get on
badly with the butcher and baker, and have often not too much for
themselves), until Mrs. Micawber take I into her entire
confidence. this she do one evening as follow:
'Master Copperfield,' say Mrs. Micawber, 'I make no stranger of
you, and therefore do not hesitate to say that Mr. Micawber's
difficulty be come to a crisis.'
it make I very miserable to hear it, and I look at Mrs.
Micawber's red eye with the utmost sympathy.
'with the exception of the heel of a dutch cheese - which be not
adapt to the want of a young family' - say Mrs. Micawber,
'there be really not a scrap of anything in the larder. I be
accustomed to speak of the larder when I live with papa and mama,
and I use the word almost unconsciously. what I mean to express
be, that there be nothing to eat in the house.'
'dear I!' I say, in great concern.
I have two or three shilling of my week's money in my pocket - from
which I presume that it must have be on a Wednesday night when we
hold this conversation - and I hastily produce they, and with
heartfelt emotion beg Mrs. Micawber to accept of they as a loan.
but that lady, kiss I, and make I put they back in my
pocket, reply that she couldnot think of it.
'no, my dear Master Copperfield,' say she, 'far be it from my
thought! but you have a discretion beyond your year, and can
render I another kind of service, if you will; and a service I
will thankfully accept of.'
I beg Mrs. Micawber to name it.
'I have part with the plate myself,' say Mrs. Micawber. 'six
tea, two salt, and a pair of sugar, I have at different time
borrow money on, in secret, with my own hand. but the twin be
a great tie; and to I, with my recollection, of papa and mama,
these transaction be very painful. there be still a few trifle
that we could part with. Mr. Micawber's feeling would never allow
he to dispose of they; and Clickett' - this be the girl from the
workhouse - 'being of a vulgar mind, would take painful liberty
if so much confidence be repose in she. Master Copperfield, if
I might ask you -'
I understand Mrs. Micawber now, and beg she to make use of I to
any extent. I begin to dispose of the more portable article of
property that very evening; and go out on a similar expedition
almost every morning, before I go to Murdstone and Grinby's.
Mr. Micawber have a few book on a little chiffoni, which he
call the library; and those go first. I carry they, one
after another, to a bookstall in the City Road - one part of which,
near our house, be almost all bookstall and bird shop then - and
sell they for whatever they would bring. the keeper of this
bookstall, who live in a little house behind it, use to get tipsy
every night, and to be violently scold by his wife every morning.
More than once, when I go there early, I have audience of he in
a turn-up bedstead, with a cut in his forehead or a black eye,
bear witness to his excess over-night (I be afraid he be
quarrelsome in his drink), and he, with a shake hand,
endeavour to find the needful shilling in one or other of the
pocket of his clothe, which lie upon the floor, while his wife,
with a baby in her arm and her shoe down at heel, never leave off
rat he. sometimes he have lose his money, and then he would ask
I to call again; but his wife have always get some - have take his,
I dare say, while he be drunk - and secretly complete the bargain
on the stair, as we go down together.
at the pawnbroker's shop, too, I begin to be very well know. the
principal gentleman who officiate behind the counter, take a good
deal of notice of I; and often get I, I recollect, to decline a
Latin noun or adjective, or to conjugate a latin verb, in his ear,
while he transact my business. after all these occasion Mrs.
Micawber make a little treat, which be generally a supper; and
there be a peculiar relish in these meal which I well remember.
at last Mr. Micawber's difficulty come to a crisis, and he be
arrest early one morning, and carry over to the King's Bench
Prison in the Borough. he tell I, as he go out of the house,
that the God of day have now go down upon he - and I really
think his heart be break and mine too. but I hear,
afterwards, that he be see to play a lively game at skittle,
before noon.
on the first Sunday after he be take there, I be to go and see
he, and have dinner with he. I be to ask my way to such a
place, and just short of that place I should see such another
place, and just short of that I should see a yard, which I be to
cross, and keep straight on until I see a turnkey. all this I do;
and when at last I do see a turnkey (poor little fellow that I
be!), and think how, when Roderick Random be in a debtor'
prison, there be a man there with nothing on he but an old rug,
the turnkey swam before my dimmed eye and my beat heart.
Mr. Micawber be wait for I within the gate, and we go up to
his room (top story but one), and cry very much. he solemnly
conjure I, I remember, to take warning by his fate; and to
observe that if a man have twenty pound a-year for his income, and
spend nineteen pound nineteen shilling and sixpence, he would be
happy, but that if he spend twenty pound one he would be
miserable. after which he borrow a shilling of I for porter,
give I a write order on Mrs. Micawber for the amount, and put
away his pocket-handkerchief, and cheer up.
we sit before a little fire, with two brick put within the rusted
grate, one on each side, to prevent its burning too many coal;
until another debtor, who share the room with Mr. Micawber, come
in from the bakehouse with the loin of mutton which be our
joint-stock repast. then I be send up to 'Captain Hopkins' in the
room overhead, with Mr. Micawber's compliment, and I be his young
friend, and would Captain Hopkins lend I a knife and fork.
Captain Hopkins lend I the knife and fork, with his compliment to
Mr. Micawber. there be a very dirty lady in his little room, and
two wan girl, his daughter, with shock head of hair. I think
it be well to borrow Captain Hopkins's knife and fork, than
Captain Hopkins's comb. the Captain himself be in the last
extremity of shabbiness, with large whisker, and an old, old brown
great-coat with no other coat below it. I see his bed roll up in
a corner; and what plate and dish and pot he have, on a shelf;
and I divine (God know how) that though the two girl with the
shock head of hair be Captain Hopkins's child, the dirty lady
be not married to Captain Hopkins. my timid station on his
threshold be not occupy more than a couple of minute at most;
but I come down again with all this in my knowledge, as surely as
the knife and fork be in my hand.
there be something gipsy-like and agreeable in the dinner, after
all. I take back Captain Hopkins's knife and fork early in the
afternoon, and go home to comfort Mrs. Micawber with an account
of my visit. she faint when she see I return, and make a little
jug of egg-hot afterwards to console we while we talk it over.
I donot know how the household furniture come to be sell for the
family benefit, or who sell it, except that I do not. sell it
be, however, and carry away in a van; except the bed, a few
chair, and the kitchen table. with these possession we encamp,
as it be, in the two parlour of the emptied house in Windsor
Terrace; Mrs. Micawber, the child, the orfling, and myself; and
live in those room night and day. I have no idea for how long,
though it seem to I for a long time. at last Mrs. Micawber
resolve to move into the prison, where Mr. Micawber have now
secure a room to himself. so I take the key of the house to the
landlord, who be very glad to get it; and the bed be send over
to the King's Bench, except mine, for which a little room be hire
outside the wall in the neighbourhood of that Institution, very
much to my satisfaction, since the Micawbers and I have become too
use to one another, in our trouble, to part. the Orfling be
likewise accommodate with an inexpensive lodging in the same
neighbourhood. mine be a quiet back-garret with a sloping roof,
command a pleasant prospect of a timberyard; and when I take
possession of it, with the reflection that Mr. Micawber's trouble
have come to a crisis at last, I think it quite a paradise.
all this time I be work at Murdstone and Grinby's in the same
common way, and with the same common companion, and with the same
sense of unmerited degradation as at first. but I never, happily
for I no doubt, make a single acquaintance, or speak to any of the
many boy whom I see daily in go to the warehouse, in come
from it, and in prowl about the street at meal-time. I lead
the same secretly unhappy life; but I lead it in the same lonely,
self-reliant manner. the only change I be conscious of be,
firstly, that I have grow more shabby, and secondly, that I be now
relieve of much of the weight of Mr. and Mrs. Micawber's care;
for some relative or friend have engage to help they at their
present pass, and they live more comfortably in the prison than
they have live for a long while out of it. I use to breakfast
with they now, in virtue of some arrangement, of which I have
forget the detail. I forget, too, at what hour the gate be
open in the morning, admit of my go in; but I know that I
be often up at six o'clock, and that my favourite lounging-place
in the interval be old London Bridge, where I be willnot to sit in
one of the stone recess, watch the people go by, or to look
over the balustrade at the sun shin in the water, and lighting
up the golden flame on the top of the Monument. the Orfling meet I
here sometimes, to be tell some astonishing fiction respect the
wharf and the Tower; of which I can say no more than that I hope
I believe they myself. in the evening I use to go back to the
prison, and walk up and down the parade with Mr. Micawber; or play
casino with Mrs. Micawber, and hear reminiscence of her papa and
mama. whether Mr. Murdstone know where I be, I be unable to say.
I never tell they at Murdstone and Grinby's.
Mr. Micawber's affair, although past their crisis, be very much
involve by reason of a certain 'deed', of which I use to hear a
great deal, and which I suppose, now, to have be some former
composition with his creditor, though I be so far from be
clear about it then, that I be conscious of have confound it
with those demoniacal parchment which be hold to have, once upon
a time, obtain to a great extent in Germany. at last this
document appear to be get out of the way, somehow; at all event
it cease to be the rock-ahead it have be; and Mrs. Micawber
inform I that 'her family' have decide that Mr. Micawber should
apply for his release under the Insolvent Debtors Act, which would
set he free, she expect, in about six week.
'and then,' say Mr. Micawber, who be present, 'I have no doubt I
shall, please Heaven, begin to be beforehand with the world, and to
live in a perfectly new manner, if - in short, if anything turn
up.'
by way of go in for anything that might be on the card, I call
to mind that Mr. Micawber, about this time, compose a petition to
the House of Commons, pray for an alteration in the law of
imprisonment for debt. I set down this remembrance here, because
it be an instance to myself of the manner in which I fit my old
book to my altered life, and make story for myself, out of the
street, and out of man and woman; and how some main point in the
character I shall unconsciously develop, I suppose, in write my
life, be gradually form all this while.
there be a club in the prison, in which Mr. Micawber, as a
gentleman, be a great authority. Mr. Micawber have state his idea
of this petition to the club, and the club have strongly approve of
the same. wherefore Mr. Micawber (who be a thoroughly
good-natured man, and as active a creature about everything but his
own affair as ever exist, and never so happy as when he be busy
about something that could never be of any profit to he) set to
work at the petition, invent it, engross it on an immense sheet
of paper, spread it out on a table, and appoint a time for all
the club, and all within the wall if they choose, to come up to his
room and sign it.
when I hear of this approach ceremony, I be so anxious to see
they all come in, one after another, though I know the great part
of they already, and they I, that I get an hour's leave of absence
from Murdstone and Grinby's, and establish myself in a corner for
that purpose. as many of the principal member of the club as
could be get into the small room without fill it, support Mr.
Micawber in front of the petition, while my old friend Captain
Hopkins (who have wash himself, to do honour to so solemn an
occasion) station himself close to it, to read it to all who be
unacquainted with its content. the door be then throw open, and
the general population begin to come in, in a long file: several
wait outside, while one enter, affix his signature, and go
out. to everybody in succession, Captain Hopkins say: 'have you
read it?' - 'no.' - 'would you like to hear it read?' if he
weakly show the least disposition to hear it, Captain Hopkins, in
a loud sonorous voice, give he every word of it. the Captain
would have read it twenty thousand time, if twenty thousand people
would have hear he, one by one. I remember a certain luscious
roll he give to such phrase as 'the people's representative in
Parliament assemble,' 'your petitioner therefore humbly approach
your honourable house,' 'his gracious Majesty's unfortunate
subject,' as if the word be something real in his mouth, and
delicious to taste; Mr. Micawber, meanwhile, listen with a
little of an author's vanity, and contemplating (not severely) the
spike on the opposite wall.
as I walk to and fro daily between Southwark and Blackfriars, and
lounge about at meal-time in obscure street, the stone of which
may, for anything I know, be wear at this moment by my childish
foot, I wonder how many of these people be want in the crowd

the SEQUEL of MY resolution

my late experience with Mr. and Mrs. Micawber suggest to I that

I go up the next by-street, take off my waistcoat, roll it

'if you please, sir,' I say, 'I be to sell this for a fair price.'

there, and ultimately say:
'what do you call a price, now, for this here little weskit?'
'oh! you know well, sir,' I return modestly.
'I cannot be buyer and seller too,' say Mr. Dolloby. 'put a price
on this here little weskit.'
'would eighteenpence be?'- I hint, after some hesitation.
Mr. Dolloby roll it up again, and give it I back. 'I should rob

this be a disagreeable way of put the business; because it

indeed, I foresaw pretty clearly that my jacket would go next, and

a plan have occur to I for pass the night, which I be go

although the boy would know nothing of my be there, and the

I have have a hard day's work, and be pretty well jade when I come

of first lie down, without a roof above my head!
Sleep come upon I as it come on many other outcast, against whom

when I little expect that any eye would ever see I the wayfarer
I be now, upon it.
what a different Sunday morning from the old Sunday morning at
Yarmouth! in due time I hear the church-bell ring, as I
plod on; and I meet people who be go to church; and I pass
a church or two where the congregation be inside, and the sound

but the peace and rest of the old Sunday morning be on
everything, except I. that be the difference. I feel quite
wicked in my dirt and dust, with my tangled hair. but for the
quiet picture I have conjure up, of my mother in her youth and
beauty, weep by the fire, and my aunt relent to she, I hardly
think I should have have the courage to go on until next day. but
it always go before I, and I follow.
I get, that Sunday, through three-and-twenty mile on the straight
road, though not very easily, for I be new to that kind of toil.
I see myself, as evening close in, come over the bridge at
