for Travellers', hang out, have tempt I; but I be afraid of
spend the few penny I have, and be even more afraid of the
vicious look of the tramper I have meet or overtaken. I seek no
shelter, therefore, but the sky; and toil into Chatham, - which,
in that night's aspect, be a mere dream of chalk, and drawbridge,
and mastless ship in a muddy river, roof like Noah's ark, -
creep, at last, upon a sort of grass-grow battery overhang a
lane, where a sentry be walk to and fro. here I lie down, near
a cannon; and, happy in the society of the sentry's footstep,
though he know no more of my be above he than the boy at Salem
House have know of my lying by the wall, sleep soundly until
morning.
very stiff and sore of foot I be in the morning, and quite daze
by the beating of drum and marching of troop, which seem to hem
I in on every side when I go down towards the long narrow
street. feel that I could go but a very little way that day, if
I be to reserve any strength for get to my journey's end, I
resolve to make the sale of my jacket its principal business.
accordingly, I take the jacket off, that I might learn to do
without it; and carry it under my arm, begin a tour of
inspection of the various slop-shop.
it be a likely place to sell a jacket in; for the dealer in
second-hand clothe be numerous, and be, generally speak, on
the look-out for customer at their shop door. but as most of
they have, hang up among their stock, an officer's coat or two,
epaulette and all, I be render timid by the costly nature of
their dealing, and walk about for a long time without offer
my merchandise to anyone.
this modesty of mine direct my attention to the marine-store
shop, and such shop as Mr. Dolloby's, in preference to the
regular dealer. at last I find one that I think look
promising, at the corner of a dirty lane, end in an enclosure
full of stinging-nettle, against the paling of which some
second-hand sailor' clothe, that seem to have overflow the
shop, be flutter among some cot, and rusty gun, and oilskin
hat, and certain tray full of so many old rusty key of so many
size that they seem various enough to open all the door in the
world.
into this shop, which be low and small, and which be darken
rather than light by a little window, overhang with clothe, and
be descend into by some step, I go with a palpitating heart;
which be not relieve when an ugly old man, with the low part of
his face all cover with a stubbly grey beard, rush out of a
dirty den behind it, and seize I by the hair of my head. he be
a dreadful old man to look at, in a filthy flannel waistcoat, and
smell terribly of rum. his bedstead, cover with a tumbled and
ragged piece of patchwork, be in the den he have come from, where
another little window show a prospect of more stinging-nettle,
and a lame donkey.
'oh, what do you want?' grin this old man, in a fierce,
monotonous whine. 'oh, my eye and limb, what do you want? oh,
my lung and liver, what do you want? oh, goroo, goroo!'
I be so much dismay by these word, and particularly by the
repetition of the last unknown one, which be a kind of rattle in
his throat, that I could make no answer; hereupon the old man,
still hold I by the hair, repeat:
'oh, what do you want? oh, my eye and limb, what do you want?
oh, my lung and liver, what do you want? oh, goroo!' - which he
screw out of himself, with an energy that make his eye start in
his head.
'I want to know,' I say, tremble, 'if you would buy a jacket.'
'oh, letus see the jacket!' cry the old man. 'oh, my heart on
fire, show the jacket to we! oh, my eye and limb, bring the
jacket out!'
with that he take his trembling hand, which be like the claw of
a great bird, out of my hair; and put on a pair of spectacle, not
at all ornamental to his inflame eye.
'oh, how much for the jacket?' cry the old man, after examine
it. 'oh - goroo! - how much for the jacket?'
'half-a-crown,' I answer, recover myself.
'oh, my lung and liver,' cry the old man, 'no! oh, my eye, no!
oh, my limb, no! Eighteenpence. Goroo!'
every time he utter this ejaculation, his eye seem to be in
danger of start out; and every sentence he speak, he deliver
in a sort of tune, always exactly the same, and more like a gust of
wind, which begin low, mount up high, and fall again, than any
other comparison I can find for it.
'well,' say I, glad to have close the bargain, 'Iwill take
eighteenpence.'
'oh, my liver!' cry the old man, throw the jacket on a shelf.
'get out of the shop! oh, my lung, get out of the shop! oh, my
eye and limb - goroo! - donot ask for money; make it an
exchange.' I never be so frightened in my life, before or since;
but I tell he humbly that I want money, and that nothing else
be of any use to I, but that I would wait for it, as he desire,
outside, and have no wish to hurry he. so I go outside, and sit
down in the shade in a corner. and I sit there so many hour, that
the shade become sunlight, and the sunlight become shade again, and
still I sit there wait for the money.
there never be such another drunken madman in that line of
business, I hope. that he be well know in the neighbourhood, and
enjoy the reputation of have sell himself to the devil, I soon
understand from the visit he receive from the boy, who
continually come skirmish about the shop, shout that legend,
and call to he to bring out his gold. 'you benot poor, you
know, Charley, as you pretend. bring out your gold. bring out
some of the gold you sell yourself to the devil for. come! itbe
in the lining of the mattress, Charley. rip it open and letus have
some!' this, and many offer to lend he a knife for the purpose,
exasperate he to such a degree, that the whole day be a
succession of rush on his part, and flight on the part of the
boy. sometimes in his rage he would take I for one of they, and
come at I, mouth as if he be go to tear I in piece;
then, remember I, just in time, would dive into the shop, and
lie upon his bed, as I think from the sound of his voice, yell
in a frantic way, to his own windy tune, the 'Death of Nelson';
with an oh! before every line, and innumerable Goroos intersperse.
as if this be not bad enough for I, the boy, connect I with
the establishment, on account of the patience and perseverance with
which I sit outside, half-dress, pelt I, and use I very ill
all day.
he make many attempt to induce I to consent to an exchange; at
one time come out with a fishing-rod, at another with a fiddle,
at another with a cocked hat, at another with a flute. but I
resist all these overture, and sit there in desperation; each
time ask he, with tear in my eye, for my money or my jacket.
at last he begin to pay I in halfpence at a time; and be full two
hour get by easy stage to a shilling.
'oh, my eye and limb!' he then cry, peep hideously out of
the shop, after a long pause, 'will you go for twopence more?'
'I cannot,' I say; 'I shall be starve.'
'oh, my lung and liver, will you go for threepence?'
'I would go for nothing, if I could,' I say, 'but I want the money
badly.'
'oh, go-roo!' (it be really impossible to express how he twist
this ejaculation out of himself, as he peep round the door-post
at I, show nothing but his crafty old head); 'will you go for
fourpence?'
I be so faint and weary that I close with this offer; and take
the money out of his claw, not without tremble, go away more
hungry and thirsty than I have ever be, a little before sunset.
but at an expense of threepence I soon refresh myself completely;
and, be in well spirit then, limp seven mile upon my road.
my bed at night be under another haystack, where I rest
comfortably, after have wash my blister foot in a stream, and
dress they as well as I be able, with some cool leaf. when I
take the road again next morning, I find that it lie through a
succession of hop-ground and orchard. it be sufficiently late
in the year for the orchard to be ruddy with ripe apple; and in
a few place the hop-picker be already at work. I think it
all extremely beautiful, and make up my mind to sleep among the
hop that night: imagine some cheerful companionship in the long
perspective of pole, with the graceful leaf twin round they.
the tramper be bad than ever that day, and inspire I with a
dread that be yet quite fresh in my mind. some of they be most
ferocious-look ruffian, who star at I as I go by; and
stop, perhaps, and call after I to come back and speak to
they, and when I take to my heel, stone I. I recollect one
young fellow - a tinker, I suppose, from his wallet and brazi -
who have a woman with he, and who face about and star at I
thus; and then roar to I in such a tremendous voice to come
back, that I halt and look round.
'come here, when yoube call,' say the tinker, 'or Iwill rip your
young body open.'
I think it well to go back. as I draw near to they, try to
propitiate the tinker by my look, I observe that the woman have a
black eye.
'where be you go?' say the tinker, grip the bosom of my
shirt with his blackened hand.
'I be go to Dover,' I say.
'where do you come from?' ask the tinker, give his hand another
turn in my shirt, to hold I more securely.
'I come from London,' I say.
'what lie be you upon?' ask the tinker. 'be you a prig?'
'N-no,' I say.
'benot you, by g--? if you make a brag of your honesty to I,'
say the tinker, 'Iwill knock your brain out.'
with his disengage hand he make a menace of strike I, and then
look at I from head to foot.
'have you get the price of a pint of beer about you?' say the
tinker. 'if you have, out with it, afore I take it away!'
I should certainly have produce it, but that I meet the woman's
look, and see she very slightly shake her head, and form 'no!' with
her lip.
'I be very poor,' I say, attempt to smile, 'and have get no
money.'
'why, what do you mean?' say the tinker, look so sternly at I,
that I almost fear he see the money in my pocket.
'Sir!' I stammer.
'what do you mean,' say the tinker, 'by wear my brother's silk
handkerchief! give it over here!' and he have mine off my neck in
a moment, and toss it to the woman.
the woman burst into a fit of laughter, as if she think this a
joke, and toss it back to I, nod once, as slightly as before,
and make the word 'go!' with her lip. before I could obey,
however, the tinker seize the handkerchief out of my hand with a
roughness that throw I away like a feather, and put it loosely
round his own neck, turn upon the woman with an oath, and knock
she down. I never shall forget see her fall backward on the
hard road, and lie there with her bonnet tumble off, and her hair
all whiten in the dust; nor, when I look back from a distance,
see she sit on the pathway, which be a bank by the
roadside, wipe the blood from her face with a corner of her
shawl, while he go on ahead.
this adventure frighten I so, that, afterwards, when I see any
of these people come, I turn back until I could find a
hiding-place, where I remain until they have go out of sight;
which happen so often, that I be very seriously delay. but
under this difficulty, as under all the other difficulty of my
journey, I seem to be sustain and lead on by my fanciful picture
of my mother in her youth, before I come into the world. it always
keep I company. it be there, among the hop, when I lie down to
sleep; it be with I on my waking in the morning; it go before
I all day. I have associate it, ever since, with the sunny
street of Canterbury, doze as it be in the hot light; and with
the sight of its old house and gateway, and the stately, grey
Cathedral, with the rook sail round the tower. when I come,
at last, upon the bare, wide down near Dover, it relieve the
solitary aspect of the scene with hope; and not until I reach
that first great aim of my journey, and actually set foot in the
town itself, on the sixth day of my flight, do it desert I. but
then, strange to say, when I stand with my ragged shoe, and my
dusty, sunburnt, half-clothe figure, in the place so long desire,
it seem to vanish like a dream, and to leave I helpless and
dispirit.
I inquire about my aunt among the boatman first, and receive
various answer. one say she live in the South Foreland Light,
and have sing her whisker by do so; another, that she be make
fast to the great buoy outside the harbour, and could only be
visit at half-tide; a third, that she be lock up in Maidstone
jail for child-stealing; a fourth, that she be see to mount a
broom in the last high wind, and make direct for Calais. the
fly-driver, among whom I inquire next, be equally jocose and
equally disrespectful; and the shopkeeper, not like my
appearance, generally reply, without hear what I have to say,
that they have get nothing for I. I feel more miserable and
destitute than I have do at any period of my run away. my
money be all go, I have nothing leave to dispose of; I be hungry,
thirsty, and wear out; and seem as distant from my end as if I
have remain in London.
the morning have wear away in these inquiry, and I be sit on
the step of an empty shop at a street corner, near the
market-place, deliberate upon wander towards those other
place which have be mention, when a fly-driver, come by with
his carriage, drop a horsecloth. something good-natured in the
man's face, as I hand it up, encourage I to ask he if he could
tell I where Miss Trotwood live; though I have ask the question
so often, that it almost die upon my lip.
'trotwood,' say he. 'let I see. I know the name, too. old
lady?'
'yes,' I say, 'rather.'
'pretty stiff in the back?' say he, make himself upright.
'yes,' I say. 'I should think it very likely.'
'carry a bag?' say he - 'bag with a good deal of room in it - be
gruffish, and come down upon you, sharp?'
my heart sink within I as I acknowledge the undoubted accuracy of
this description.
'why then, I tell you what,' say he. 'if you go up there,'
point with his whip towards the height, 'and keep right on till
you come to some house face the sea, I think youwill hear of she.
my opinion be she willnot stand anything, so herebe a penny for you.'
I accept the gift thankfully, and buy a loaf with it.
dispatch this refreshment by the way, I go in the direction my
friend have indicate, and walk on a good distance without come
to the house he have mention. at length I see some before I;
and approach they, go into a little shop (it be what we use
to call a general shop, at home), and inquire if they could have
the goodness to tell I where Miss Trotwood live. I address
myself to a man behind the counter, who be weigh some rice for
a young woman; but the latter, take the inquiry to herself,
turn round quickly.
'my mistress?' she say. 'what do you want with she, boy?'
'I want,' I reply, 'to speak to she, if you please.'
'to beg of she, you mean,' retort the damsel.
'no,' I say, 'indeed.' but suddenly remember that in truth I
come for no other purpose, I hold my peace in confusion, and feel
my face burn.
MY aunt's handmaid, as I suppose she be from what she have say,
put her rice in a little basket and walk out of the shop; tell
I that I could follow she, if I want to know where Miss Trotwood
live. I need no second permission; though I be by this time in
such a state of consternation and agitation, that my leg shake
under I. I follow the young woman, and we soon come to a very
neat little cottage with cheerful bow-window: in front of it, a
small square gravelled court or garden full of flower, carefully
tend, and smell deliciously.
'this be Miss Trotwood's,' say the young woman. 'now you know;
and thatbe all I have get to say.' with which word she hurry
into the house, as if to shake off the responsibility of my
appearance; and leave I stand at the garden-gate, look
disconsolately over the top of it towards the parlour window, where
a muslin curtain partly undrawn in the middle, a large round green
screen or fan fasten on to the windowsill, a small table, and a
great chair, suggest to I that my aunt might be at that moment
seat in awful state.
my shoe be by this time in a woeful condition. the sol have
shed themselves bit by bit, and the upper leather have break and
burst until the very shape and form of shoe have depart from
they. my hat (which have serve I for a night-cap, too) be so
crush and bent, that no old battered handleless saucepan on a
dunghill need have be ashamed to vie with it. my shirt and
trouser, stain with heat, dew, grass, and the kentish soil on
which I have sleep - and tear besides - might have frighten the
bird from my aunt's garden, as I stand at the gate. my hair have
know no comb or brush since I leave London. my face, neck, and
hand, from unaccustomed exposure to the air and sun, be burn to
a berry-brown. from head to foot I be powder almost as white
with chalk and dust, as if I have come out of a lime-kiln. in this
plight, and with a strong consciousness of it, I wait to
introduce myself to, and make my first impression on, my formidable
aunt.
the unbroken stillness of the parlour window lead I to infer,
after a while, that she be not there, I lift up my eye to the
window above it, where I see a florid, pleasant-look gentleman,
with a grey head, who shut up one eye in a grotesque manner, nod
his head at I several time, shake it at I as often, laugh, and
go away.
I have be discompose enough before; but I be so much the more
discompose by this unexpected behaviour, that I be on the point
of slink off, to think how I have well proceed, when there come
out of the house a lady with her handkerchief tie over her cap,
and a pair of gardening glove on her hand, wear a gardening
pocket like a toll-man's apron, and carry a great knife. I know
she immediately to be Miss Betsey, for she come stalk out of the
house exactly as my poor mother have so often describe her stalk
up our garden at Blunderstone Rookery.
'go away!' say Miss Betsey, shake her head, and make a distant
chop in the air with her knife. 'go along! no boy here!'
I watch she, with my heart at my lip, as she march to a corner
of her garden, and stoop to dig up some little root there. then,
without a scrap of courage, but with a great deal of desperation,
I go softly in and stand beside she, touch she with my finger.
'if you please, ma'am,' I begin.
she start and look up.
'if you please, aunt.'
'EH?' exclaim Miss Betsey, in a tone of amazement I have never
hear approach.
'if you please, aunt, I be your nephew.'
'oh, Lord!' say my aunt. and sit flat down in the garden-path.
'I be David Copperfield, of Blunderstone, in Suffolk - where you
come, on the night when I be bear, and see my dear mama. I have
be very unhappy since she die. I have be slight, and teach
nothing, and throw upon myself, and put to work not fit for I.
it make I run away to you. I be rob at first set out, and
have walk all the way, and have never sleep in a bed since I
begin the journey.' here my self-support give way all at once; and
with a movement of my hand, intend to show she my ragged state,
and call it to witness that I have suffer something, I break into
a passion of crying, which I suppose have be pen up within I all
the week.
my aunt, with every sort of expression but wonder discharge from
her countenance, sit on the gravel, star at I, until I begin to
cry; when she get up in a great hurry, collar I, and take I
into the parlour. her first proceeding there be to unlock a tall
press, bring out several bottle, and pour some of the content of
each into my mouth. I think they must have be take out at
random, for I be sure I taste aniseed water, anchovy sauce, and
salad dressing. when she have administer these restorative, as
I be still quite hysterical, and unable to control my sob, she
put I on the sofa, with a shawl under my head, and the
handkerchief from her own head under my foot, lest I should sully
the cover; and then, sit herself down behind the green fan or
screen I have already mention, so that I could not see her face,
ejaculate at interval, 'mercy on we!' let those exclamation
off like minute gun.
after a time she ring the bell. 'janet,' say my aunt, when her
servant come in. 'go upstairs, give my compliment to Mr. Dick,
and say I wish to speak to he.'
Janet look a little surprised to see I lie stiffly on the sofa
(I be afraid to move lest it should be displease to my aunt),
but go on her errand. my aunt, with her hand behind she, walk
up and down the room, until the gentleman who have squint at I
from the upper window come in laugh.
'Mr. Dick,' say my aunt, 'donot be a fool, because nobody can be
more discreet than you can, when you choose. we all know that. so
donot be a fool, whatever you be.'
the gentleman be serious immediately, and look at I, I think,
as if he would entreat I to say nothing about the window.
'Mr. Dick,' say my aunt, 'you have hear I mention David
Copperfield? now donot pretend not to have a memory, because you
and I know well.'
'David Copperfield?' say Mr. Dick, who do not appear to I to
remember much about it. 'David Copperfield? oh yes, to be sure.
David, certainly.'
'well,' say my aunt, 'this be his boy - his son. he would be as
like his father as itbe possible to be, if he be not so like his
mother, too.'
'his son?' say Mr. Dick. 'David's son? indeed!'
'yes,' pursue my aunt, 'and he have do a pretty piece of
business. he have run away. ah! his sister, Betsey Trotwood,
never would have run away.' my aunt shake her head firmly,
confident in the character and behaviour of the girl who never be
bear.
'oh! you think she wouldnot have run away?' say Mr. Dick.
'bless and save the man,' exclaim my aunt, sharply, 'how he
talk! donot I know she wouldnot? she would have live with her
god-mother, and we should have be devote to one another. where,
in the name of wonder, should his sister, Betsey Trotwood, have run
from, or to?'
'nowhere,' say Mr. Dick.
'well then,' return my aunt, soften by the reply, 'how can you
pretend to be wool-gathering, Dick, when you be as sharp as a
surgeon's lancet? now, here you see young David Copperfield, and
the question I put to you be, what shall I do with he?'
'what shall you do with he?' say Mr. Dick, feebly, scratch his
head. 'oh! do with he?'
'yes,' say my aunt, with a grave look, and her forefinger hold up.
'come! I want some very sound advice.'
'why, if I be you,' say Mr. Dick, consider, and look
vacantly at I, 'I should -' the contemplation of I seem to
inspire he with a sudden idea, and he add, briskly, 'I should
wash he!'
'janet,' say my aunt, turn round with a quiet triumph, which I
do not then understand, 'Mr. Dick set we all right. heat the
bath!'
although I be deeply interested in this dialogue, I could not help
observe my aunt, Mr. Dick, and Janet, while it be in progress,
and complete a survey I have already be engage in making of the
room.
my aunt be a tall, hard-feature lady, but by no means
ill-looking. there be an inflexibility in her face, in her voice,
in her gait and carriage, amply sufficient to account for the
effect she have make upon a gentle creature like my mother; but she
feature be rather handsome than otherwise, though unbending and
austere. I particularly notice that she have a very quick, bright
eye. her hair, which be grey, be arrange in two plain
division, under what I believe would be call a mob-cap; I mean
a cap, much more common then than now, with side-piece fasten
under the chin. her dress be of a lavender colour, and perfectly
neat; but scantily make, as if she desire to be as little
encumbered as possible. I remember that I think it, in form,
more like a riding-habit with the superfluous skirt cut off, than
anything else. she wear at her side a gentleman's gold watch, if
I might judge from its size and make, with an appropriate chain and
seal; she have some linen at her throat not unlike a shirt-collar,
and thing at her wrist like little shirt-wristband.
Mr. Dick, as I have already say, be grey-head, and florid: I
should have say all about he, in say so, have not his head be
curiously bow - not by age; it remind I of one of Mr.
Creakle's boy' head after a beating - and his grey eye prominent
and large, with a strange kind of watery brightness in they that
make I, in combination with his vacant manner, his submission to
my aunt, and his childish delight when she praise he, suspect he
of be a little mad; though, if he be mad, how he come to be
there puzzle I extremely. he be dress like any other ordinary
gentleman, in a loose grey morning coat and waistcoat, and white
trouser; and have his watch in his fob, and his money in his
pocket: which he rattle as if he be very proud of it.
Janet be a pretty bloom girl, of about nineteen or twenty, and
a perfect picture of neatness. though I make no further
observation of she at the moment, I may mention here what I do not
discover until afterwards, namely, that she be one of a series of
protegee whom my aunt have take into her service expressly to
educate in a renouncement of mankind, and who have generally
complete their abjuration by marry the baker.
the room be as neat as Janet or my aunt. as I lay down my pen,
a moment since, to think of it, the air from the sea come blow
in again, mix with the perfume of the flower; and I see the
old-fashioned furniture brightly rub and polished, my aunt's
inviolable chair and table by the round green fan in the
bow-window, the drugget-cover carpet, the cat, the kettle-holder,
the two canary, the old china, the punchbowl full of dry
rose-leaf, the tall press guard all sort of bottle and pot,
and, wonderfully out of keep with the rest, my dusty self upon
the sofa, take note of everything.
Janet have go away to get the bath ready, when my aunt, to my
great alarm, become in one moment rigid with indignation, and have
hardly voice to cry out, 'Janet! donkey!'
upon which, Janet come run up the stair as if the house be
in flame, dart out on a little piece of green in front, and
warn off two saddle-donkey, lady-ride, that have presume to
set hoof upon it; while my aunt, rush out of the house, seize
the bridle of a third animal laden with a bestriding child, turn
he, lead he forth from those sacred precinct, and box the ear
of the unlucky urchin in attendance who have dare to profane that
hallowed ground.
to this hour I donot know whether my aunt have any lawful right of
way over that patch of green; but she have settle it in her own
mind that she have, and it be all the same to she. the one great
outrage of her life, demand to be constantly avenge, be the
passage of a donkey over that immaculate spot. in whatever
occupation she be engage, however interesting to her the
conversation in which she be take part, a donkey turn the
current of her idea in a moment, and she be upon he straight.
Jugs of water, and watering-pot, be keep in secret place ready
to be discharge on the offend boy; stick be lay in ambush
behind the door; sally be make at all hour; and incessant war
prevail. perhaps this be an agreeable excitement to the
donkey-boy; or perhaps the more sagacious of the donkey,
understand how the case stand, delight with constitutional
obstinacy in come that way. I only know that there be three
alarm before the bath be ready; and that on the occasion of the
last and most desperate of all, I see my aunt engage,
single-handed, with a sandy-head lad of fifteen, and bump his
sandy head against her own gate, before he seem to comprehend
what be the matter. these interruption be of the more
ridiculous to I, because she be give I broth out of a
table-spoon at the time (have firmly persuade herself that I be
actually starve, and must receive nourishment at first in very
small quantity), and, while my mouth be yet open to receive the
spoon, she would put it back into the basin, cry 'Janet! donkey!'
and go out to the assault.
the bath be a great comfort. for I begin to be sensible of acute
pain in my limb from lie out in the field, and be now so
tired and low that I could hardly keep myself awake for five
minute together. when I have bath, they (I mean my aunt and
Janet) enrobe I in a shirt and a pair of trouser belong to
Mr. Dick, and tie I up in two or three great shawl. what sort
of bundle I look like, I donot know, but I feel a very hot one.
feel also very faint and drowsy, I soon lie down on the sofa
again and fall asleep.
it might have be a dream, originate in the fancy which have
occupy my mind so long, but I awake with the impression that my
aunt have come and bent over I, and have put my hair away from my
face, and lay my head more comfortably, and have then stand look
at I. the word, 'pretty fellow,' or 'poor fellow,' seem to be
in my ear, too; but certainly there be nothing else, when I
awake, to lead I to believe that they have be utter by my aunt,
who sit in the bow-window gazing at the sea from behind the green
fan, which be mount on a kind of swivel, and turn any way.
we din soon after I awake, off a roast fowl and a pudding; I
sit at table, not unlike a truss bird myself, and move my
arm with considerable difficulty. but as my aunt have swathe I
up, I make no complaint of be inconvenience. all this time I
be deeply anxious to know what she be go to do with I; but
she take her dinner in profound silence, except when she
occasionally fix her eye on I sit opposite, and say,
'mercy upon we!' which do not by any mean relieve my anxiety.
the cloth be draw, and some sherry put upon the table (of which
I have a glass), my aunt send up for Mr. Dick again, who join we,
and look as wise as he could when she request he to attend to
my story, which she elicit from I, gradually, by a course of
question. during my recital, she keep her eye on Mr. Dick, who
I think would have go to sleep but for that, and who,
whensoever he lapse into a smile, be check by a frown from my
aunt.
'whatever possess that poor unfortunate Baby, that she must go
and be marry again,' say my aunt, when I have finish, 'I cannot
conceive.'
'perhaps she fall in love with her second husband,' Mr. Dick
suggest.
'fall in love!' repeat my aunt. 'what do you mean? what
business have she to do it?'
'perhaps,' Mr. Dick simper, after think a little, 'she do it
for pleasure.'
'pleasure, indeed!' reply my aunt. 'a mighty pleasure for the
poor Baby to fix her simple faith upon any dog of a fellow, certain
to ill-use she in some way or other. what do she propose to
herself, I should like to know! she have have one husband. she have
see David Copperfield out of the world, who be always run
after wax doll from his cradle. she have get a baby - oh, there
be a pair of baby when she give birth to this child sit
here, that Friday night! - and what more do she want?'
Mr. Dick secretly shake his head at I, as if he think there be
no getting over this.
'she couldnot even have a baby like anybody else,' say my aunt.
'where be this child's sister, Betsey Trotwood? not forthcoming.
donot tell I!'
Mr. Dick seem quite frightened.
'that little man of a doctor, with his head on one side,' say my
aunt, 'Jellips, or whatever his name be, what be he about? all
he could do, be to say to I, like a robin redbreast - as he be -
"itbe a boy." a boy! yah, the imbecility of the whole set of
them!'
the heartiness of the ejaculation startle Mr. Dick exceedingly;
and I, too, if I be to tell the truth.
'and then, as if this be not enough, and she have not stand
sufficiently in the light of this child's sister, Betsey Trotwood,'
say my aunt, 'she marry a second time - go and marry a
Murderer - or a man with a name like it - and stand in this
child's light! and the natural consequence be, as anybody but a
baby might have foresee, that he prowl and wander. hebe as like
Cain before he be grow up, as he can be.'
Mr. Dick look hard at I, as if to identify I in this character.
'and then therebe that woman with the Pagan name,' say my aunt,
'that Peggotty, she go and get marry next. because she have
not see enough of the evil attend such thing, she go and
get marry next, as the child relate. I only hope,' say my
aunt, shake her head, 'that her husband be one of those Poker
husband who abound in the newspaper, and will beat she well with
one.'
I could not bear to hear my old nurse so decry, and make the
subject of such a wish. I tell my aunt that indeed she be
mistaken. that Peggotty be the good, the true, the most
faithful, most devoted, and most self-deny friend and servant in
the world; who have ever love I dearly, who have ever love my
mother dearly; who have hold my mother's die head upon her arm, on
whose face my mother have imprint her last grateful kiss. and my
remembrance of they both, choke I, I break down as I be try
to say that her home be my home, and that all she have be mine,
and that I would have go to she for shelter, but for her humble
station, which make I fear that I might bring some trouble on her
- I break down, I say, as I be try to say so, and lay my face
in my hand upon the table.
'well, well!' say my aunt, 'the child be right to stand by those
who have stand by he - Janet! donkey!'
I thoroughly believe that but for those unfortunate donkey, we
should have come to a good understanding; for my aunt have lay she
hand on my shoulder, and the impulse be upon I, thus embolden,
to embrace she and beseech her protection. but the interruption,
and the disorder she be throw into by the struggle outside, put
an end to all soft idea for the present, and keep my aunt
indignantly declaim to Mr. Dick about her determination to
appeal for redress to the law of her country, and to bring action
for trespass against the whole donkey proprietorship of Dover,
until tea-time.
after tea, we sit at the window - on the look-out, as I imagine,
from my aunt's sharp expression of face, for more invader - until
dusk, when Janet set candle, and a backgammon-board, on the table,
and pull down the blind.
'now, Mr. Dick,' say my aunt, with her grave look, and her
forefinger up as before, 'I be go to ask you another question.
look at this child.'
'David's son?' say Mr. Dick, with an attentive, puzzled face.
'exactly so,' return my aunt. 'what would you do with he, now?'
'do with David's son?' say Mr. Dick.
'ay,' reply my aunt, 'with David's son.'
'oh!' say Mr. Dick. 'yes. do with - I should put he to bed.'
'janet!' cry my aunt, with the same complacent triumph that I have
remark before. 'Mr. Dick set we all right. if the bed be
ready, wewill take he up to it.'
Janet report it to be quite ready, I be take up to it; kindly,
but in some sort like a prisoner; my aunt go in front and janet
bring up the rear. the only circumstance which give I any new
hope, be my aunt's stopping on the stair to inquire about a smell
of fire that be prevalent there; and janet's replying that she have
be make tinder down in the kitchen, of my old shirt. but there
be no other clothe in my room than the odd heap of thing I
wear; and when I be leave there, with a little taper which my aunt
forewarn I would burn exactly five minute, I hear they lock my
door on the outside. turn these thing over in my mind I deem
it possible that my aunt, who could know nothing of I, might
suspect I have a habit of run away, and take precaution, on
that account, to have I in safe keeping.
the room be a pleasant one, at the top of the house, overlook
the sea, on which the moon be shin brilliantly. after I have
say my prayer, and the candle have burn out, I remember how I
still sit look at the moonlight on the water, as if I could hope
to read my fortune in it, as in a bright book; or to see my mother
with her child, come from Heaven, along that shin path, to
look upon I as she have look when I last see her sweet face. I
remember how the solemn feeling with which at length I turn my
eye away, yield to the sensation of gratitude and rest which the
sight of the white-curtain bed - and how much more the lying
softly down upon it, nestle in the snow-white sheet! - inspire.
I remember how I think of all the solitary place under the night
sky where I have sleep, and how I pray that I never might be
houseless any more, and never might forget the houseless. I
remember how I seem to float, then, down the melancholy glory of
that track upon the sea, away into the world of dream.
CHAPTER 14
MY AUNT make up her MIND about I
on go down in the morning, I find my aunt musing so profoundly
over the breakfast table, with her elbow on the tray, that the
content of the urn have overflow the teapot and be lay the
whole table-cloth under water, when my entrance put her meditation
to flight. I feel sure that I have be the subject of her
reflection, and be more than ever anxious to know her intention
towards I. yet I dare not express my anxiety, lest it should
give her offence.
my eye, however, not be so much under control as my tongue,
be attract towards my aunt very often during breakfast. I
never could look at she for a few moment together but I find she
look at I - in an odd thoughtful manner, as if I be an
immense way off, instead of be on the other side of the small
round table. when she have finish her breakfast, my aunt very
deliberately lean back in her chair, knit her brow, fold
her arm, and contemplate I at her leisure, with such a fixedness
of attention that I be quite overpower by embarrassment. not
have as yet finish my own breakfast, I attempt to hide my
confusion by proceed with it; but my knife tumble over my fork,
my fork trip up my knife, I chip bit of bacon a surprising
height into the air instead of cut they for my own eating, and
choke myself with my tea, which persist in go the wrong way
instead of the right one, until I give in altogether, and sit
blush under my aunt's close scrutiny.
'Hallo!' say my aunt, after a long time.
I look up, and meet her sharp bright glance respectfully.
'I have write to he,' say my aunt.
'to -?'
'to your father-in-law,' say my aunt. 'I have send he a letter
that Iwill trouble he to attend to, or he and I will fall out, I
can tell he!'
'do he know where I be, aunt?' I inquire, alarm.
'I have tell he,' say my aunt, with a nod.
'shall I - be - give up to he?' I falter.
'I donot know,' say my aunt. 'we shall see.'
'oh! I cannot think what I shall do,' I exclaim, 'if I have to go
back to Mr. Murdstone!'
'I donot know anything about it,' say my aunt, shake her head.
'I cannot say, I be sure. we shall see.'
my spirit sink under these word, and I become very downcast and
heavy of heart. my aunt, without appear to take much heed of
I, put on a coarse apron with a bib, which she take out of the
press; wash up the teacup with her own hand; and, when
everything be wash and set in the tray again, and the cloth
fold and put on the top of the whole, rang for Janet to remove
it. she next sweep up the crumb with a little broom (put on
a pair of glove first), until there do not appear to be one
microscopic speck leave on the carpet; next dust and arrange the
room, which be dust and arrange to a hair's breadth already.
when all these task be perform to her satisfaction, she take
off the glove and apron, fold they up, put they in the
particular corner of the press from which they have be take,
bring out her work-box to her own table in the open window, and
sit down, with the green fan between she and the light, to work.
'I wish youwould go upstairs,' say my aunt, as she thread she
needle, 'and give my compliment to Mr. Dick, and Iwill be glad to
know how he get on with his Memorial.'
I rise with all alacrity, to acquit myself of this commission.
'I suppose,' say my aunt, eye I as narrowly as she have eye
the needle in thread it, 'you think Mr. Dick a short name, eh?'
'I think it be rather a short name, yesterday,' I confess.
'you be not to suppose that he havenot get a long name, if he
choose to use it,' say my aunt, with a lofty air. 'Babley - Mr.
Richard Babley - thatbe the gentleman's true name.'
I be go to suggest, with a modest sense of my youth and the
familiarity I have be already guilty of, that I have well give
he the full benefit of that name, when my aunt go on to say:
'but donot you call he by it, whatever you do. he cannot bear his
name. thatbe a peculiarity of his. though I donot know that itbe
much of a peculiarity, either; for he have be ill-use enough, by
some that bear it, to have a mortal antipathy for it, Heaven know.
Mr. Dick be his name here, and everywhere else, now - if he ever
go anywhere else, which he donot. so take care, child, you donot
call he anything but Mr. Dick.'
I promise to obey, and go upstairs with my message; think, as
I go, that if Mr. Dick have be work at his Memorial long, at
the same rate as I have see he work at it, through the open
door, when I come down, he be probably get on very well
indeed. I find he still drive at it with a long pen, and his
head almost lay upon the paper. he be so intent upon it, that I
have ample leisure to observe the large paper kite in a corner, the
confusion of bundle of manuscript, the number of pen, and, above
all, the quantity of ink (which he seem to have in, in
half-gallon jar by the dozen), before he observe my being
present.
'ha! Phoebus!' say Mr. Dick, lay down his pen. 'how do the
world go? Iwill tell you what,' he add, in a low tone, 'I
shouldnot wish it to be mention, but itbe a -' here he beckon
to I, and put his lip close to my ear - 'itbe a mad world. mad
as Bedlam, boy!' say Mr. Dick, take snuff from a round box on
the table, and laugh heartily.
without presume to give my opinion on this question, I deliver
my message.
'well,' say Mr. Dick, in answer, 'my compliment to she, and I -
I believe I have make a start. I think I have make a start,' say
Mr. Dick, pass his hand among his grey hair, and cast
anything but a confident look at his manuscript. 'you have be to
school?'
'yes, sir,' I answer; 'for a short time.'
'do you recollect the date,' say Mr. Dick, look earnestly at
I, and take up his pen to note it down, 'when King Charles the
First have his head cut off?'
I say I believe it happen in the year sixteen hundred and
forty-nine.
'well,' return Mr. Dick, scratch his ear with his pen, and
look dubiously at I. 'so the book say; but I donot see how
that can be. because, if it be so long ago, how could the people
about he have make that mistake of put some of the trouble out
of his head, after it be take off, into mine?'
I be very much surprised by the inquiry; but could give no
information on this point.
'itbe very strange,' say Mr. Dick, with a despondent look upon his
paper, and with his hand among his hair again, 'that I never can
get that quite right. I never can make that perfectly clear. but
no matter, no matter!' he say cheerfully, and rouse himself,
'therebe time enough! my compliment to Miss Trotwood, I be
get on very well indeed.'
I be go away, when he direct my attention to the kite.
'what do you think of that for a kite?' he say.
I answer that it be a beautiful one. I should think it must
have be as much as seven foot high.
'I make it. wewill go and fly it, you and I,' say Mr. Dick. 'do
you see this?'
he show I that it be cover with manuscript, very closely and
laboriously write; but so plainly, that as I look along the
line, I think I see some allusion to King Charles the First's
head again, in one or two place.
'therebe plenty of string,' say Mr. Dick, 'and when it fly high,
it take the fact a long way. thatbe my manner of diffuse them.
I donot know where they may come down. itbe accord to
circumstance, and the wind, and so forth; but I take my chance of
that.'
his face be so very mild and pleasant, and have something so
reverend in it, though it be hale and hearty, that I be not sure
but that he be have a good-humour jest with I. so I laugh,
and he laugh, and we part the good friend possible.
'well, child,' say my aunt, when I go downstairs. 'and what of
Mr. Dick, this morning?'
I inform she that he send his compliment, and be get on
very well indeed.
'what do you think of he?' say my aunt.
I have some shadowy idea of endeavour to evade the question, by
reply that I think he a very nice gentleman; but my aunt be
not to be so put off, for she lay her work down in her lap, and
say, fold her hand upon it:
'come! your sister Betsey Trotwood would have tell I what she
think of anyone, directly. be as like your sister as you can,
and speak out!'
'be he - be Mr. Dick - I ask because I donot know, aunt - be he at
all out of his mind, then?' I stammer; for I feel I be on
dangerous ground.
'not a morsel,' say my aunt.
'oh, indeed!' I observe faintly.
'if there be anything in the world,' say my aunt, with great
decision and force of manner, 'that Mr. Dick be not, itbe that.'
I have nothing well to offer, than another timid, 'oh, indeed!'
'he have be call mad,' say my aunt. 'I have a selfish pleasure
in say he have be call mad, or I should not have have the
benefit of his society and advice for these last ten year and
upwards - in fact, ever since your sister, Betsey Trotwood,
disappoint I.'
'so long as that?' I say.
'and nice people they be, who have the audacity to call he mad,'
pursue my aunt. 'Mr. Dick be a sort of distant connexion of mine
- it donot matter how; I neednot enter into that. if it havenot
be for I, his own brother would have shut he up for life.
thatbe all.'
I be afraid it be hypocritical in I, but see that my aunt feel
strongly on the subject, I try to look as if I feel strongly too.
'a proud fool!' say my aunt. 'because his brother be a little
eccentric - though he be not half so eccentric as a good many
people - he donot like to have he visible about his house, and
send he away to some private asylum-place: though he have be leave
to his particular care by their deceased father, who think he
almost a natural. and a wise man he must have be to think so!
mad himself, no doubt.'
again, as my aunt look quite convinced, I endeavour to look
quite convinced also.
'so I step in,' say my aunt, 'and make he an offer. I say,
"your brother's sane - a great deal more sane than you be, or ever
will be, it be to be hop. let he have his little income, and
come and live with I. I be not afraid of he, I be not proud, I
be ready to take care of he, and shall not ill-treat he as some
people (besides the asylum-folk) have do." after a good deal of
squabbling,' say my aunt, 'I get he; and he have be here ever
since. he be the most friendly and amenable creature in existence;
and as for advice! - but nobody know what that man's mind be,
except myself.'
my aunt smooth her dress and shake her head, as if she smooth
defiance of the whole world out of the one, and shake it out of the
other.
'he have a favourite sister,' say my aunt, 'a good creature, and
very kind to he. but she do what they all do - take a husband.
and he do what they all do - make she wretched. it have such an
effect upon the mind of Mr. Dick (thatbe not madness, I hope!)
that, combine with his fear of his brother, and his sense of his
unkindness, it throw he into a fever. that be before he come to
I, but the recollection of it be oppressive to he even now. do
he say anything to you about King Charles the First, child?'
'yes, aunt.'
'ah!' say my aunt, rub her nose as if she be a little vexed.
'thatbe his allegorical way of express it. he connect his
illness with great disturbance and agitation, naturally, and thatbe
the figure, or the simile, or whatever itbe call, which he
choose to use. and why shouldnot he, if he think proper!'
I say: 'certainly, aunt.'
'itbe not a business-like way of speak,' say my aunt, 'nor a

insist upon it, that there shallnot be a word about it in his
Memorial.'
'be it a Memorial about his own history that he be write, aunt?'
'yes, child,' say my aunt, rub her nose again. 'he be
memorialize the Lord Chancellor, or the Lord Somebody or other -
one of those people, at all event, who be pay to be memorialize
- about his affair. I suppose it will go in, one of these day.
he havenot be able to draw it up yet, without introduce that
mode of express himself; but it donot signify; it keep he
employ.'
in fact, I find out afterwards that Mr. Dick have be for upwards
of ten year endeavour to keep King Charles the First out of the
Memorial; but he have be constantly get into it, and be there
now.
'I say again,' say my aunt, 'nobody know what that man's mind be
except myself; and hebe the most amenable and friendly creature in
existence. if he like to fly a kite sometimes, what of that!
Franklin use to fly a kite. he be a Quaker, or something of that
sort, if I be not mistaken. and a Quaker fly a kite be a much
more ridiculous object than anybody else.'
if I could have suppose that my aunt have recount these
particular for my especial behoof, and as a piece of confidence in

augur favourably from such a mark of her good opinion. but I
could hardly help observe that she have launch into they,
chiefly because the question be raise in her own mind, and with
very little reference to I, though she have address herself to I
in the absence of anybody else.
at the same time, I must say that the generosity of her
championship of poor harmless Mr. Dick, not only inspire my young
breast with some selfish hope for myself, but warm it unselfishly
towards she. I believe that I begin to know that there be
something about my aunt, notwithstanding her many eccentricity
and odd humour, to be honour and trust in. though she be

about the donkey just as often, and be throw into a tremendous
state of indignation, when a young man, go by, ogle Janet at a
window (which be one of the grave misdemeanour that could be
commit against my aunt's dignity), she seem to I to command
more of my respect, if not less of my fear.
the anxiety I underwent, in the interval which necessarily elapse
before a reply could be receive to her letter to Mr. Murdstone,
be extreme; but I make an endeavour to suppress it, and to be as
agreeable as I could in a quiet way, both to my aunt and Mr. Dick.
the latter and I would have go out to fly the great kite; but
that I have still no other clothe than the anything but ornamental

which confine I to the house, except for an hour after dark, when
my aunt, for my health's sake, parade I up and down on the cliff

count the time, flushed and heat by the conflict of sink

sight of the gloomy face, whose non-arrival startle I every

my aunt be a little more imperious and stern than usual, but I

when she give a sudden alarm of donkey, and to my consternation

deliberately over the sacred piece of green, and stop in front of
the house, look about she.
'go along with you!' cry my aunt, shake her head and her fist
at the window. 'you have no business there. how dare you
trespass? go along! oh! you bold-face thing!'
my aunt be so exasperate by the coolness with which Miss
Murdstone look about she, that I really believe she be
motionless, and unable for the moment to dart out accord to
custom. I seize the opportunity to inform she who it be; and
that the gentleman now come near the offender (for the way up be
very steep, and he have drop behind), be Mr. Murdstone himself.
'I donot care who it be!' cry my aunt, still shake her head and
gesticulate anything but welcome from the bow-window. 'I willnot
be trespass upon. I willnot allow it. go away! janet, turn he
round. lead he off!' and I see, from behind my aunt, a sort of
hurried battle-piece, in which the donkey stand resist
everybody, with all his four leg plant different way, while

several boy, who have come to see the engagement, shout
vigorously. but my aunt, suddenly descry among they the young
malefactor who be the donkey's guardian, and who be one of the
most inveterate offender against she, though hardly in his teen,
rush out to the scene of action, pounce upon he, capture he,
drag he, with his jacket over his head, and his heel grind
the ground, into the garden, and, call upon Janet to fetch the
constable and justice, that he might be take, try, and
execute on the spot, hold he at bay there. this part of the

expert at a variety of feint and dodge, of which my aunt have no
conception, soon go whooping away, leave some deep impression
of his nail boot in the flower-bed, and take his donkey in
triumph with he.
Miss Murdstone, during the latter portion of the contest, have
dismount, and be now wait with her brother at the bottom of

aunt, a little ruffle by the combat, march past they into the

'shall I go away, aunt?' I ask, tremble.
'no, sir,' say my aunt. 'certainly not!' with which she push

be a prison or a bar of justice. this position I continue to
occupy during the whole interview, and from it I now see Mr. and
Miss Murdstone enter the room.
'oh!' say my aunt, 'I be not aware at first to whom I have the
pleasure of object. but I donot allow anybody to ride over that
turf. I make no exception. I donot allow anybody to do it.'
'your regulation be rather awkward to stranger,' say Miss
Murdstone.
'be it!' say my aunt.
Mr. Murdstone seem afraid of a renewal of hostility, and
interposing begin:
'Miss Trotwood!'
'I beg your pardon,' observe my aunt with a keen look. 'you be
the Mr. Murdstone who marry the widow of my late nephew, David
Copperfield, of Blunderstone Rookery! - though why Rookery, I donot
know!'
'I be,' say Mr. Murdstone.
'youwill excuse my saying, sir,' return my aunt, 'that I think it
would have be a much well and happy thing if you have leave
that poor child alone.'
'I so far agree with what Miss Trotwood have remark,' observe
Miss Murdstone, bridle, 'that I consider our lamented Clara to
have be, in all essential respect, a mere child.'
'it be a comfort to you and I, ma'am,' say my aunt, 'who be
get on in life, and be not likely to be make unhappy by our

'no doubt!' return Miss Murdstone, though, I think, not with a
very ready or gracious assent. 'and it certainly might have be,
as you say, a well and happy thing for my brother if he have
never enter into such a marriage. I have always be of that

'I have no doubt you have,' say my aunt. 'janet,' ring the
bell, 'my compliment to Mr. Dick, and beg he to come down.'
until he come, my aunt sit perfectly upright and stiff, frown at
the wall. when he come, my aunt perform the ceremony of

'Mr. Dick. an old and intimate friend. on whose judgement,' say
my aunt, with emphasis, as an admonition to Mr. Dick, who be

Mr. Dick take his finger out of his mouth, on this hint, and stand
among the group, with a grave and attentive expression of face.
my aunt incline her head to Mr. Murdstone, who go on:
'Miss Trotwood: on the receipt of your letter, I consider it an
act of great justice to myself, and perhaps of more respect to
you-'
'thank you,' say my aunt, still eye he keenly. 'you neednot
mind I.'
'to answer it in person, however inconvenient the journey,' pursue
Mr. Murdstone, 'rather than by letter. this unhappy boy who have
run away from his friend and his occupation -'
'and whose appearance,' interpose his sister, direct general
attention to I in my indefinable costume, 'be perfectly scandalous
and disgraceful.'
'Jane Murdstone,' say her brother, 'have the goodness not to
interrupt I. this unhappy boy, Miss Trotwood, have be the
occasion of much domestic trouble and uneasiness; both during the
lifetime of my late dear wife, and since. he have a sullen,
rebellious spirit; a violent temper; and an untoward, intractable
disposition. both my sister and myself have endeavour to correct
his vice, but ineffectually. and I have feel - we both have feel,
I may say; my sister be fully in my confidence - that it be
right you should receive this grave and dispassionate assurance
from our lip.'
'it can hardly be necessary for I to confirm anything state by my
brother,' say Miss Murdstone; 'but I beg to observe, that, of all
the boy in the world, I believe this be the bad boy.'
'strong!' say my aunt, shortly.
'but not at all too strong for the fact,' return Miss Murdstone.
'ha!' say my aunt. 'well, sir?'
'I have my own opinion,' resume Mr. Murdstone, whose face
darken more and more, the more he and my aunt observe each
other, which they do very narrowly, 'as to the good mode of
bring he up; they be found, in part, on my knowledge of he,
and in part on my knowledge of my own mean and resource. I be
responsible for they to myself, I act upon they, and I say no more
about they. it be enough that I place this boy under the eye of a
friend of my own, in a respectable business; that it do not
please he; that he run away from it; make himself a common
vagabond about the country; and come here, in rag, to appeal to
you, Miss Trotwood. I wish to set before you, honourably, the
exact consequence - so far as they be within my knowledge - of
your abet he in this appeal.'
'but about the respectable business first,' say my aunt. 'if he
have be your own boy, you would have put he to it, just the same,
I suppose?'
'if he have be my brother's own boy,' return Miss Murdstone,
strike in, 'his character, I trust, would have be altogether
different.'
'or if the poor child, his mother, have be alive, he would still
have go into the respectable business, would he?' say my aunt.
'I believe,' say Mr. Murdstone, with an inclination of his head,
'that Clara would have dispute nothing which myself and my sister
Jane Murdstone be agree be for the good.'
Miss Murdstone confirm this with an audible murmur.
'Humph!' say my aunt. 'unfortunate baby!'
Mr. Dick, who have be rattle his money all this time, be

'the poor child's annuity die with she?'
'die with she,' reply Mr. Murdstone.

'merely this, Miss Trotwood,' he return. 'I be here to take

away. be he ready to go? if he be not - and you tell I he be
not; on any pretence; it be indifferent to I what - my door be

open to he.'
to this address, my aunt have listen with the close attention,

I answer no, and entreat she not to let I go. I say that

Miss Betsey, without take the least notice of the interruption,

'allow I to inquire, Miss Trotwood,' interpose Miss Murdstone,

he have stand by the door, all this while, observant of she with a

it would require a painter, and no common painter too, to depict my

no attempt at defiance be make, however, her face gradually

'yes, to be sure. yes. Trotwood Copperfield,' say Mr. Dick, a

my aunt take so kindly to the notion, that some ready-make clothe,

thus I begin my new life, in a new name, and with everything new

want of hope, that I have never have the courage even to examine how
long I be doom to lead it. whether it last for a year, or
more, or less, I do not know. I only know that it be, and cease
to be; and that I have write, and there I leave it.
CHAPTER 15
I make another beginning
Mr. Dick and I soon become the good of friend, and very often,
when his day's work be do, go out together to fly the great
kite. every day of his life he have a long sitting at the Memorial,
which never make the least progress, however hard he labour, for
King Charles the First always stray into it, soon or later, and
then it be throw aside, and another one begin. the patience and
hope with which he bear these perpetual disappointment, the mild
perception he have that there be something wrong about King Charles
the First, the feeble effort he make to keep he out, and the
certainty with which he come in, and tumble the Memorial out of
all shape, make a deep impression on I. what Mr. Dick suppose
would come of the Memorial, if it be complete; where he think
it be to go, or what he think it be to do; he know no more than
anybody else, I believe. nor be it at all necessary that he
should trouble himself with such question, for if anything be
certain under the sun, it be certain that the Memorial never would
be finish. it be quite an affect sight, I use to think, to
see he with the kite when it be up a great height in the air.
what he have tell I, in his room, about his belief in its
disseminate the statement paste on it, which be nothing but
old leaf of abortive Memorials, might have be a fancy with he
sometimes; but not when he be out, look up at the kite in the
sky, and feel it pull and tug at his hand. he never look so
serene as he do then. I use to fancy, as I sit by he of an
evening, on a green slope, and see he watch the kite high in the
quiet air, that it lift his mind out of its confusion, and bore
it (such be my boyish thought) into the sky. as he wound the
string in and it come low and low down out of the beautiful
light, until it flutter to the ground, and lie there like a dead
thing, he seem to wake gradually out of a dream; and I remember
to have see he take it up, and look about he in a lost way, as
if they have both come down together, so that I pity he with all
my heart.
while I advance in friendship and intimacy with Mr. Dick, I do
not go backward in the favour of his staunch friend, my aunt. she
take so kindly to I, that, in the course of a few week, she
shorten my adopt name of trotwood into Trot; and even
encourage I to hope, that if I go on as I have begin, I might
take equal rank in her affection with my sister Betsey Trotwood.
'trot,' say my aunt one evening, when the backgammon-board be

education.'
this be my only subject of anxiety, and I feel quite delighted by
she refer to it.
'should you like to go to school at Canterbury?' say my aunt.
I reply that I should like it very much, as it be so near she.

be already no stranger to the general rapidity of my aunt's

I be greatly elate by these order; but my heart smite I for my

she be much gratify; and both her hand be occupy, pat

I ask for no more information about Mr. Wickfield, as she offer

at length we stop before a very old house bulge out over the

when the pony-chaise stop at the door, and my eye be intent

'Mr. Wickfield's at home, ma'am,' say Uriah Heep, 'if youwill

his hair be quite white now, though his eyebrow be still black.

and corner; and in every nook and corner there be some queer

other, that make I think there be not such another good corner in
the room; until I look at the next one, and find it equal to it,
if not well. on everything there be the same air of retirement
and cleanliness that mark the house outside.
Mr. Wickfield tap at a door in a corner of the panel wall,
and a girl of about my own age come quickly out and kiss he. on
her face, I see immediately the placid and sweet expression of the
lady whose picture have look at I downstairs. it seem to my
imagination as if the portrait have grow womanly, and the original
remain a child. although her face be quite bright and happy,
there be a tranquillity about it, and about she - a quiet, good,
calm spirit - that I never have forget; that I shall never
forget. this be his little housekeeper, his daughter Agnes, Mr.
Wickfield say. when I hear how he say it, and see how he hold
her hand, I guess what the one motive of his life be.
she have a little basket-trifle hang at her side, with key in
it; and she look as staid and as discreet a housekeeper as the
old house could have. she listen to her father as he tell she
about I, with a pleasant face; and when he have conclude, propose
to my aunt that we should go upstairs and see my room. we all go
together, she before we: and a glorious old room it be, with more
oak beam, and diamond pane; and the broad balustrade go all
the way up to it.
I cannot call to mind where or when, in my childhood, I have see a
stain glass window in a church. nor do I recollect its subject.
but I know that when I see her turn round, in the grave light of
the old staircase, and wait for we, above, I think of that
window; and I associate something of its tranquil brightness with
Agnes Wickfield ever afterwards.
my aunt be as happy as I be, in the arrangement make for I; and
we go down to the drawing-room again, well pleased and gratify.
as she would not hear of stay to dinner, lest she should by any
chance fail to arrive at home with the grey pony before dark; and
as I apprehend Mr. Wickfield know she too well to argue any point
with she; some lunch be provide for she there, and Agnes go
back to her governess, and Mr. Wickfield to his office. so we be
leave to take leave of one another without any restraint.
she tell I that everything would be arrange for I by Mr.
Wickfield, and that I should want for nothing, and give I the
kind word and the good advice.
'trot,' say my aunt in conclusion, 'be a credit to yourself, to
I, and Mr. Dick, and Heaven be with you!'
I be greatly overcome, and could only thank she, again and again,
and send my love to Mr. Dick.
'never,' say my aunt, 'be mean in anything; never be false; never
be cruel. avoid those three vice, Trot, and I can always be
hopeful of you.'
I promise, as well as I could, that I would not abuse her kindness
or forget her admonition.
'the pony's at the door,' say my aunt, 'and I be off! stay here.'
with these word she embrace I hastily, and go out of the room,
shut the door after she. at first I be startle by so abrupt
a departure, and almost fear I have displease she; but when I
look into the street, and see how dejectedly she get into the
chaise, and drive away without look up, I understand she well
and do not do she that injustice.
by five o'clock, which be Mr. Wickfield's dinner-hour, I have
muster up my spirit again, and be ready for my knife and fork.
the cloth be only lay for we two; but Agnes be wait in the
drawing-room before dinner, go down with her father, and sit
opposite to he at table. I doubt whether he could have din
without she.
we do not stay there, after dinner, but come upstairs into the
drawing-room again: in one snug corner of which, Agnes set glass
for her father, and a decanter of port wine. I think he would
have miss its usual flavour, if it have be put there for he by
any other hand.
there he sit, take his wine, and take a good deal of it, for
two hour; while Agnes play on the piano, work, and talk to
he and I. he be, for the most part, gay and cheerful with we;
but sometimes his eye rest on she, and he fall into a brooding
state, and be silent. she always observe this quickly, I
think, and always rouse he with a question or caress. then he
come out of his meditation, and drank more wine.
Agnes make the tea, and preside over it; and the time pass away
after it, as after dinner, until she go to bed; when her father
take she in his arm and kiss she, and, she be go, order
candle in his office. then I go to bed too.
but in the course of the evening I have ramble down to the door,
and a little way along the street, that I might have another peep
at the old house, and the grey Cathedral; and might think of my
come through that old city on my journey, and of my pass the
very house I live in, without know it. as I come back, I see
Uriah Heep shut up the office; and feel friendly towards
everybody, go in and speak to he, and at parting, give he my
hand. but oh, what a clammy hand his be! as ghostly to the touch
as to the sight! I rub mine afterwards, to warm it, and to RUB
his OFF.
it be such an uncomfortable hand, that, when I go to my room, it
be still cold and wet upon my memory. lean out of the window,
and see one of the face on the beam-end look at I
sideways, I fancy it be Uriah Heep get up there somehow, and
shut he out in a hurry.
CHAPTER 16
I be a new boy in MORE sens than one
next morning, after breakfast, I enter on school life again. I
go, accompany by Mr. Wickfield, to the scene of my future
study - a grave building in a courtyard, with a learn air about
it that seem very well suited to the stray rook and jackdaw who
come down from the cathedral tower to walk with a clerkly bearing
on the grass-plot - and be introduce to my new master, Doctor
Strong.
Doctor Strong look almost as rusty, to my thinking, as the tall
iron rail and gate outside the house; and almost as stiff and
heavy as the great stone urn that flank they, and be set up,
on the top of the red-brick wall, at regular distance all round
the court, like sublimated skittle, for Time to play at. he be
in his library (I mean Doctor Strong be), with his clothe not
particularly well brush, and his hair not particularly well

hearth-rug. turn upon I a lustreless eye, that remind I of

but, sit at work, not far from Doctor Strong, be a very pretty

himself unconsciously enlighten I.
'by the by, Wickfield,' he say, stop in a passage with his

my wife's cousin yet?'
'no,' say Mr. Wickfield. 'no. not yet.'
'I could wish it do as soon as it can be do, Wickfield,' say

two bad thing, bad thing sometimes come. what do Doctor
watt say,' he add, look at I, and move his head to the
time of his quotation, '"Satan find some mischief still, for idle
hand to do."'
'Egad, doctor,' return Mr. Wickfield, 'if Doctor Watts know
mankind, he might have write, with as much truth, "Satan find
some mischief still, for busy hand to do." the busy people achieve
their full share of mischief in the world, you may rely upon it.
what have the people be about, who have be the busy in
get money, and in get power, this century or two? no
mischief?'
'Jack Maldon will never be very busy in get either, I expect,'
say Doctor Strong, rub his chin thoughtfully.
'perhaps not,' say Mr. Wickfield; 'and you bring I back to the

to dispose of Mr. Jack Maldon yet. I believe,' he say this with

more difficult.'
'my motive,' return Doctor Strong, 'be to make some suitable
provision for a cousin, and an old playfellow, of Annie's.'
'yes, I know,' say Mr. Wickfield; 'at home or abroad.'
'aye!' reply the Doctor, apparently wonder why he emphasize
those word so much. 'at home or abroad.'
'your own expression, you know,' say Mr. Wickfield. 'or abroad.'
'surely,' the Doctor answer. 'surely. one or other.'
'one or other? have you no choice?' ask Mr. Wickfield.
'no,' return the Doctor.
'no?' with astonishment.
'not the least.'
'no motive,' say Mr. Wickfield, 'for meaning abroad, and not at

'no,' return the Doctor.
'I be bind to believe you, and of course I do believe you,' say

I have know it before. but I confess I entertain another
impression.'
Doctor Strong regard he with a puzzled and doubt look, which
almost immediately subside into a smile that give I great

the schoolroom be a pretty large hall, on the quiet side of the

belong to the Doctor, where the peach be ripen on the

about five-and-twenty boy be studiously engage at their book

one Adams, who be the head-boy, then step out of his place and

that would have put I at my ease, if anything could.
it seem to I so long, however, since I have be among such boy,

would think, if they know of my familiar acquaintance with the
King's Bench Prison? be there anything about I which would

those pawning, and selling, and suppers - in spite of myself?
suppose some of the boy have see I come through Canterbury,

halfpence together, for the purchase of my daily saveloy and beer,

but there be such an influence in Mr. Wickfield's old house, that

Agnes be in the drawing-room, wait for her father, who be

'you have never be to school,' I say, 'have you?'
'oh yes! every day.'
'ah, but you mean here, at your own home?'

she nod 'yes,' and go to the door to listen for his come up,

but, see a light in the little round office, and immediately

he certainly do look uncommonly like the carve face on the beam

he have a way of writhing when he want to express enthusiasm,
