'oh, indeed!' exclaim Uriah. 'I should think you would come into
the business at last, Master Copperfield!'
I protest that I have no view of that sort, and that no such
scheme be entertain in my behalf by anybody; but Uriah insist
on blandly reply to all my assurance, 'oh, yes, Master
Copperfield, I should think you would, indeed!' and, 'oh, indeed,
Master Copperfield, I should think you would, certainly!' over and
over again. be, at last, ready to leave the office for the
night, he ask I if it would suit my convenience to have the
light put out; and on my answering 'yes,' instantly extinguish
it. after shake hand with I - his hand feel like a fish, in
the dark - he open the door into the street a very little, and
creep out, and shut it, leave I to grope my way back into the
house: which cost I some trouble and a fall over his stool. this
be the proximate cause, I suppose, of my dream about he, for
what appear to I to be half the night; and dream, among other
thing, that he have launch Mr. Peggotty's house on a piratical
expedition, with a black flag at the masthead, bear the
inscription 'Tidd's Practice', under which diabolical ensign he be
carry I and little Em'ly to the spanish Main, to be drown.
I get a little the well of my uneasiness when I go to school
next day, and a good deal the well next day, and so shake it off
by degree, that in less than a fortnight I be quite at home, and
happy, among my new companion. I be awkward enough in their
game, and backward enough in their study; but custom would
improve I in the first respect, I hop, and hard work in the
second. accordingly, I go to work very hard, both in play and in
earnest, and gain great commendation. and, in a very little
while, the Murdstone and Grinby life become so strange to I that
I hardly believe in it, while my present life grow so familiar,
that I seem to have be lead it a long time.
Doctor Strong's be an excellent school; as different from Mr.
Creakle's as good be from evil. it be very gravely and decorously
order, and on a sound system; with an appeal, in everything, to
the honour and good faith of the boy, and an avow intention to
rely on their possession of those quality unless they prove
themselves unworthy of it, which work wonder. we all feel that
we have a part in the management of the place, and in sustain its
character and dignity. hence, we soon become warmly attach to it
- I be sure I do for one, and I never know, in all my time, of any
other boy be otherwise - and learn with a good will, desire
to do it credit. we have noble game out of hour, and plenty of
liberty; but even then, as I remember, we be well speak of in
the town, and rarely do any disgrace, by our appearance or manner,
to the reputation of Doctor Strong and Doctor Strong's boy.
some of the high scholar board in the Doctor's house, and
through they I learn, at second hand, some particular of the
Doctor's history - as, how he have not yet be marry twelve
month to the beautiful young lady I have see in the study, whom he
have marry for love; for she have not a sixpence, and have a world
of poor relation (so our fellow say) ready to swarm the doctor
out of house and home. also, how the Doctor's cogitate manner
be attributable to his being always engage in look out for
greek root; which, in my innocence and ignorance, I suppose to be
a botanical furor on the doctor's part, especially as he always
look at the ground when he walk about, until I understand that
they be root of word, with a view to a new Dictionary which he
have in contemplation. Adams, our head-boy, who have a turn for
mathematic, have make a calculation, I be inform, of the time
this Dictionary would take in completing, on the Doctor's plan, and
at the Doctor's rate of go. he consider that it might be do
in one thousand six hundred and forty-nine year, count from the
Doctor's last, or sixty-second, birthday.
but the doctor himself be the idol of the whole school: and it
must have be a badly compose school if he have be anything
else, for he be the kind of man; with a simple faith in he
that might have touch the stone heart of the very urn upon the
wall. as he walk up and down that part of the courtyard which
be at the side of the house, with the stray rook and jackdaw
look after he with their head cock slyly, as if they know
how much more know they be in worldly affair than he, if any
sort of vagabond could only get near enough to his creak shoe
to attract his attention to one sentence of a tale of distress,
that vagabond be make for the next two day. it be so notorious
in the house, that the master and head-boy take pain to cut
these marauder off at angle, and to get out of window, and turn
they out of the courtyard, before they could make the Doctor aware
of their presence; which be sometimes happily effect within a
few yard of he, without his know anything of the matter, as he
jog to and fro. outside his own domain, and unprotected, he be
a very sheep for the shearer. he would have take his gaiter off
his leg, to give away. in fact, there be a story current among
we (I have no idea, and never have, on what authority, but I have
believe it for so many year that I feel quite certain it be
true), that on a frosty day, one winter-time, he actually do
bestow his gaiter on a beggar-woman, who occasion some scandal
in the neighbourhood by exhibit a fine infant from door to door,
wrap in those garment, which be universally recognize, be
as well know in the vicinity as the Cathedral. the legend add
that the only person who do not identify they be the Doctor
himself, who, when they be shortly afterwards display at the
door of a little second-hand shop of no very good repute, where
such thing be take in exchange for gin, be more than once
observe to handle they approvingly, as if admire some curious
novelty in the pattern, and consider they an improvement on his
own.
it be very pleasant to see the Doctor with his pretty young wife.
he have a fatherly, benignant way of show his fondness for she,
which seem in itself to express a good man. I often see they
walk in the garden where the peach be, and I sometimes have
a near observation of they in the study or the parlour. she
appear to I to take great care of the Doctor, and to like he
very much, though I never think she vitally interested in the
Dictionary: some cumbrous fragment of which work the Doctor always
carry in his pocket, and in the lining of his hat, and generally
seem to be expound to she as they walk about.
I see a good deal of Mrs. Strong, both because she have take a
liking for I on the morning of my introduction to the Doctor, and
be always afterwards kind to I, and interested in I; and because
she be very fond of Agnes, and be often backwards and forwards at
our house. there be a curious constraint between she and Mr.
Wickfield, I think (of whom she seem to be afraid), that never
wear off. when she come there of an evening, she always shrunk
from accept his escort home, and run away with I instead. and
sometimes, as we be run gaily across the Cathedral yard
together, expect to meet nobody, we would meet Mr. Jack Maldon,
who be always surprised to see we.
Mrs. Strong's mama be a lady I take great delight in. her name
be Mrs. Markleham; but our boy use to call she the Old Soldier,
on account of her generalship, and the skill with which she
marshal great force of relation against the Doctor. she be
a little, sharp-eyed woman, who use to wear, when she be dress,
one unchangeable cap, ornament with some artificial flower, and
two artificial butterfly suppose to be hover above the
flower. there be a superstition among we that this cap have come
from France, and could only originate in the workmanship of that
ingenious nation: but all I certainly know about it, be, that it
always make its appearance of an evening, wheresoever Mrs.
Markleham make she appearance; that it be carry about to
friendly meeting in a Hindoo basket; that the butterfly have the
gift of tremble constantly; and that they improve the shin
hour at Doctor Strong's expense, like busy bee.
I observe the Old Soldier - not to adopt the name disrespectfully
- to pretty good advantage, on a night which be make memorable to
I by something else I shall relate. it be the night of a little
party at the Doctor's, which be give on the occasion of Mr. Jack
Maldon's departure for India, whither he be go as a cadet, or
something of that kind: Mr. Wickfield have at length arrange the
business. it happen to be the Doctor's birthday, too. we have
have a holiday, have make present to he in the morning, have make a
speech to he through the head-boy, and have cheer he until we
be hoarse, and until he have shed tear. and now, in the evening,
Mr. Wickfield, Agnes, and I, go to have tea with he in his
private capacity.
Mr. Jack Maldon be there, before we. Mrs. Strong, dress in
white, with cherry-colour ribbon, be play the piano, when we
go in; and he be lean over she to turn the leaf. the clear
red and white of her complexion be not so bloom and flower-like
as usual, I think, when she turn round; but she look very
pretty, wonderfully pretty.
'I have forget, Doctor,' say Mrs. Strong's mama, when we be
seat, 'to pay you the compliment of the day - though they be,
as you may suppose, very far from be mere compliment in my
case. allow I to wish you many happy return.'
'I thank you, ma'am,' reply the Doctor.
'many, many, many, happy return,' say the Old Soldier. 'not only
for your own sake, but for Annie's, and John Maldon's, and many
other people's. it seem but yesterday to I, John, when you be
a little creature, a head short than Master Copperfield, make
baby love to Annie behind the gooseberry bush in the
back-garden.'
'my dear mama,' say Mrs. Strong, 'never mind that now.'
'Annie, donot be absurd,' return her mother. 'if you be to
blush to hear of such thing now you be an old married woman, when
be you not to blush to hear of they?'
'old?' exclaim Mr. Jack Maldon. 'Annie? come!'
'yes, John,' return the soldier. 'virtually, an old married
woman. although not old by year - for when do you ever hear I
say, or who have ever hear I say, that a girl of twenty be old by
year! - your cousin be the wife of the Doctor, and, as such, what
I have describe she. it be well for you, John, that your cousin
be the wife of the Doctor. you have find in he an influential
and kind friend, who will be kind yet, I venture to predict, if
you deserve it. I have no false pride. I never hesitate to admit,
frankly, that there be some member of our family who want a
friend. you be one yourself, before your cousin's influence
raise up one for you.'
the Doctor, in the goodness of his heart, wave his hand as if to
make light of it, and save Mr. Jack Maldon from any further
reminder. but Mrs. Markleham change her chair for one next the
Doctor's, and put her fan on his coat-sleeve, say:
'no, really, my dear doctor, you must excuse I if I appear to
dwell on this rather, because I feel so very strongly. I call it
quite my monomania, it be such a subject of mine. you be a
blessing to we. you really be a Boon, you know.'
'nonsense, nonsense,' say the Doctor.
'no, no, I beg your pardon,' retort the Old Soldier. 'with
nobody present, but our dear and confidential friend Mr. Wickfield,
I cannot consent to be put down. I shall begin to assert the
privilege of a mother-in-law, if you go on like that, and scold
you. I be perfectly honest and outspoken. what I be say, be
what I say when you first overpower I with surprise - you
remember how surprised I be? - by propose for Annie. not that
there be anything so very much out of the way, in the mere fact of
the proposal - it would be ridiculous to say that! - but because,
you have know her poor father, and have know she from a baby
six month old, I havenot think of you in such a light at all, or
indeed as a marry man in any way, - simply that, you know.'
'aye, aye,' return the Doctor, good-humouredly. 'never mind.'
'but I do mind,' say the Old Soldier, lay her fan upon his
lip. 'I mind very much. I recall these thing that I may be
contradict if I be wrong. well! then I speak to Annie, and I
tell she what have happen. I say, "my dear, herebe Doctor Strong
have positively be and make you the subject of a handsome
declaration and an offer." do I press it in the least? no. I
say, "now, Annie, tell I the truth this moment; be your heart
free?" "Mama," she say cry, "I be extremely young" - which be
perfectly true - "and I hardly know if I have a heart at all."
"then, my dear," I say, "you may rely upon it, itbe free. at all
event, my love," say I, "Doctor Strong be in an agitated state of
mind, and must be answer. he cannot be keep in his present state
of suspense." "Mama," say Annie, still cry, "would he be
unhappy without I? if he would, I honour and respect he so much,
that I think I will have he." so it be settle. and then, and
not till then, I say to Annie, "Annie, Doctor Strong will not only
be your husband, but he will represent your late father: he will
represent the head of our family, he will represent the wisdom and
station, and I may say the mean, of our family; and will be, in
short, a Boon to it." I use the word at the time, and I have use
it again, today. if I have any merit it be consistency.'
the daughter have sit quite silent and still during this speech,
with her eye fix on the ground; her cousin stand near she,
and look on the ground too. she now say very softly, in a
trembling voice:
'Mama, I hope you have finish?'
'no, my dear Annie,' return the Old Soldier, 'I have not quite
finish. since you ask I, my love, I reply that I have not. I
complain that you really be a little unnatural towards your own
family; and, as it be of no use complain to you. I mean to
complain to your husband. now, my dear doctor, do look at that
silly wife of your.'
as the doctor turn his kind face, with its smile of simplicity
and gentleness, towards she, she droop her head more. I notice
that Mr. Wickfield look at she steadily.
'when I happen to say to that naughty thing, the other day,'
pursue her mother, shake her head and her fan at she, playfully,
'that there be a family circumstance she might mention to you -
indeed, I think, be bind to mention - she say, that to mention
it be to ask a favour; and that, as you be too generous, and as
for she to ask be always to have, she wouldnot.'
'Annie, my dear,' say the Doctor. 'that be wrong. it rob I
of a pleasure.'
'almost the very word I say to she!' exclaim her mother. 'now
really, another time, when I know what she would tell you but for
this reason, and willnot, I have a great mind, my dear Doctor, to
tell you myself.'
'I shall be glad if you will,' return the Doctor.
'shall I?'
'certainly.'
'well, then, I will!' say the Old Soldier. 'thatbe a bargain.'
and have, I suppose, carry her point, she tap the Doctor's
hand several time with her fan (which she kiss first), and
return triumphantly to her former station.
some more company come in, among whom be the two master and
Adams, the talk become general; and it naturally turn on Mr. Jack
Maldon, and his voyage, and the country he be go to, and his
various plan and prospect. he be to leave that night, after
supper, in a post-chaise, for Gravesend; where the ship, in which
he be to make the voyage, lay; and be to be go - unless he come
home on leave, or for his health - I donot know how many year. I
recollect it be settle by general consent that India be quite a
misrepresented country, and have nothing objectionable in it, but a
tiger or two, and a little heat in the warm part of the day. for
my own part, I look on Mr. Jack Maldon as a modern Sindbad, and
picture he the bosom friend of all the Rajahs in the East,
sit under canopy, smoke curly golden pipe - a mile long,
if they could be straighten out.
Mrs. Strong be a very pretty singer: as I know, who often hear
her singing by herself. but, whether she be afraid of sing
before people, or be out of voice that evening, it be certain
that she couldnot sing at all. she try a duet, once, with her
cousin Maldon, but could not so much as begin; and afterwards, when
she try to sing by herself, although she begin sweetly, her voice
die away on a sudden, and leave she quite distressed, with her head
hang down over the key. the good doctor say she be nervous,
and, to relieve she, propose a round game at card; of which he
know as much as of the art of play the trombone. but I remark
that the Old Soldier take he into custody directly, for her
partner; and instruct he, as the first preliminary of
initiation, to give she all the silver he have in his pocket.
we have a merry game, not make the less merry by the Doctor's
mistake, of which he commit an innumerable quantity, in spite
of the watchfulness of the butterfly, and to their great
aggravation. Mrs. Strong have decline to play, on the ground of
not feel very well; and her cousin Maldon have excuse himself
because he have some packing to do. when he have do it, however,
he return, and they sit together, talk, on the sofa. from
time to time she come and look over the Doctor's hand, and tell
he what to play. she be very pale, as she bend over he, and I
think her finger tremble as she point out the card; but the
Doctor be quite happy in her attention, and take no notice of
this, if it be so.
at supper, we be hardly so gay. everyone appear to feel that
a parting of that sort be an awkward thing, and that the nearer it
approach, the more awkward it be. Mr. Jack Maldon try to be
very talkative, but be not at his ease, and make matter bad.
and they be not improve, as it appear to I, by the Old
soldier: who continually recall passage of Mr. Jack Maldon's
youth.
the Doctor, however, who feel, I be sure, that he be make
everybody happy, be well pleased, and have no suspicion but that we
be all at the utmost height of enjoyment.
'Annie, my dear,' say he, look at his watch, and fill his
glass, 'it be past your cousin jack's time, and we must not detain
he, since time and tide - both concerned in this case - wait for
no man. Mr. Jack Maldon, you have a long voyage, and a strange
country, before you; but many man have have both, and many man will
have both, to the end of time. the wind you be go to tempt,
have waft thousand upon thousand to fortune, and bring
thousand upon thousand happily back.'
'itbe an affect thing,' say Mrs. Markleham - 'however itbe
view, itbe affect, to see a fine young man one have know from
an infant, go away to the other end of the world, leave all he
know behind, and not know whatbe before he. a young man
really well deserve constant support and patronage,' look at
the doctor, 'who make such sacrifice.'
'time will go fast with you, Mr. Jack Maldon,' pursue the Doctor,
'and fast with all of we. some of we can hardly expect, perhaps,
in the natural course of thing, to greet you on your return. the
next good thing be to hope to do it, and thatbe my case. I shall
not weary you with good advice. you have long have a good model
before you, in your cousin Annie. imitate her virtue as nearly as
you can.'
Mrs. Markleham fan herself, and shake her head.
'Farewell, Mr. Jack,' say the Doctor, stand up; on which we all
stand up. 'a prosperous voyage out, a thriving career abroad, and
a happy return home!'
we all drink the toast, and all shake hand with Mr. Jack Maldon;
after which he hastily take leave of the lady who be there, and
hurry to the door, where he be receive, as he get into the
chaise, with a tremendous broadside of cheer discharge by our
boy, who have assemble on the lawn for the purpose. run in
among they to swell the rank, I be very near the chaise when it
roll away; and I have a lively impression make upon I, in the
midst of the noise and dust, of have see Mr. Jack Maldon rattle
past with an agitated face, and something cherry-colour in his
hand.
after another broadside for the Doctor, and another for the
Doctor's wife, the boy disperse, and I go back into the house,
where I find the guest all stand in a group about the Doctor,
discuss how Mr. Jack Maldon have go away, and how he have bear
it, and how he have feel it, and all the rest of it. in the midst
of these remark, Mrs. Markleham cry: 'wherebe Annie?'
no Annie be there; and when they call to she, no Annie reply.
but all press out of the room, in a crowd, to see what be the
matter, we find she lie on the hall floor. there be great
alarm at first, until it be find that she be in a swoon, and
that the swoon be yield to the usual mean of recovery; when
the Doctor, who have lift her head upon his knee, put her curl
aside with his hand, and say, look around:
'Poor Annie! shebe so faithful and tender-hearted! itbe the
parting from her old playfellow and friend - her favourite cousin
- that have do this. ah! itbe a pity! I be very sorry!'
when she open her eye, and see where she be, and that we be
all stand about she, she arise with assistance: turn she
head, as she do so, to lay it on the Doctor's shoulder - or to
hide it, I donot know which. we go into the drawing-room, to
leave she with the Doctor and her mother; but she say, it seem,
that she be well than she have be since morning, and that she
would rather be bring among we; so they bring she in, look
very white and weak, I think, and sit she on a sofa.
'Annie, my dear,' say her mother, do something to her dress.
'see here! you have lose a bow. will anybody be so good as find
a ribbon; a cherry-colour ribbon?'
it be the one she have wear at her bosom. we all look for it; I
myself look everywhere, I be certain - but nobody could find it.
'do you recollect where you have it last, Annie?' say her mother.
I wonder how I could have think she look white, or anything
but burn red, when she answer that she have have it safe, a
little while ago, she think, but it be not worth look for.
nevertheless, it be look for again, and still not find. she
entreat that there might be no more searching; but it be still
seek for, in a desultory way, until she be quite well, and the
company take their departure.
we walk very slowly home, Mr. Wickfield, Agnes, and I - Agnes and
I admire the moonlight, and Mr. Wickfield scarcely raise his
eye from the ground. when we, at last, reach our own door,
Agnes discover that she have leave she little reticule behind.
delighted to be of any service to she, I run back to fetch it.
I go into the supper-room where it have be leave, which be
deserted and dark. but a door of communication between that and
the Doctor's study, where there be a light, be open, I pass
on there, to say what I want, and to get a candle.
the Doctor be sit in his easy-chair by the fireside, and his
young wife be on a stool at his foot. the Doctor, with a
complacent smile, be read aloud some manuscript explanation or
statement of a theory out of that interminable Dictionary, and she
be look up at he. but with such a face as I never see. it
be so beautiful in its form, it be so ashy pale, it be so fix
in its abstraction, it be so full of a wild, sleep-walk, dreamy
horror of I donot know what. the eye be wide open, and her
brown hair fall in two rich cluster on her shoulder, and on her
white dress, disorder by the want of the lose ribbon. distinctly
as I recollect her look, I cannot say of what it be expressive, I
cannot even say of what it be expressive to I now, rise again
before my old judgement. Penitence, humiliation, shame, pride,
love, and trustfulness - I see they all; and in they all, I see
that horror of I donot know what.
my entrance, and my say what I want, rouse she. it disturb
the Doctor too, for when I go back to replace the candle I have
take from the table, he be pat her head, in his fatherly way,
and say he be a merciless drone to let she tempt he into
reading on; and he would have she go to bed.
but she ask he, in a rapid, urgent manner, to let she stay - to
let she feel assured (I hear she murmur some break word to this
effect) that she be in his confidence that night. and, as she
turn again towards he, after glance at I as I leave the room
and go out at the door, I see she cross her hand upon his knee,
and look up at he with the same face, something quiet, as he
resume his reading.
it make a great impression on I, and I remember it a long time
afterwards; as I shall have occasion to narrate when the time
come.
CHAPTER 17
SOMEBODY turn up
it have not occur to I to mention Peggotty since I run away;
but, of course, I write she a letter almost as soon as I be house
at Dover, and another, and a long letter, contain all
particular fully related, when my aunt take I formally under her
protection. on my being settle at Doctor Strong's I write to she
again, detail my happy condition and prospect. I never could
have derive anything like the pleasure from spend the money Mr.
Dick have give I, that I feel in send a gold half-guinea to
Peggotty, per post, enclose in this last letter, to discharge the
sum I have borrow of she: in which epistle, not before, I
mention about the young man with the donkey-cart.
to these communication Peggotty reply as promptly, if not as
concisely, as a merchant's clerk. her utmost power of expression
(which be certainly not great in ink) be exhaust in the
attempt to write what she feel on the subject of my journey. four
side of incoherent and interjectional beginning of sentence,
that have no end, except blot, be inadequate to afford she any
relief. but the blot be more expressive to I than the good
composition; for they show I that Peggotty have be cry all
over the paper, and what could I have desire more?
I make out, without much difficulty, that she could not take quite
kindly to my aunt yet. the notice be too short after so long a
prepossession the other way. we never know a person, she write;
but to think that Miss Betsey should seem to be so different from
what she have be think to be, be a Moral! - that be her word.
she be evidently still afraid of Miss Betsey, for she send she
grateful duty to she but timidly; and she be evidently afraid of
I, too, and entertain the probability of my run away again
soon: if I might judge from the repeat hint she throw out, that
the coach-fare to Yarmouth be always to be have of she for the
asking.
she give I one piece of intelligence which affect I very much,
namely, that there have be a sale of the furniture at our old
home, and that Mr. and Miss Murdstone be go away, and the house
be shut up, to be let or sell. God know I have no part in it
while they remain there, but it pain I to think of the dear
old place as altogether abandon; of the weed grow tall in the
garden, and the fallen leaf lie thick and wet upon the path.
I imagine how the wind of winter would howl round it, how the
cold rain would beat upon the window-glass, how the moon would make
ghost on the wall of the empty room, watch their solitude all
night. I think afresh of the grave in the churchyard, underneath
the tree: and it seem as if the house be dead too, now, and all
connect with my father and mother be fade away.
there be no other news in Peggotty's letter. Mr. Barkis be an
excellent husband, she say, though still a little near; but we all
have our fault, and she have plenty (though I be sure I donot know
what they be); and he send his duty, and my little bedroom be
always ready for I. Mr. Peggotty be well, and Ham be well, and
Mrs.. Gummidge be but poorly, and little Em'ly wouldnot send she
love, but say that Peggotty might send it, if she like.
all this intelligence I dutifully impart to my aunt, only
reserve to myself the mention of little Em'ly, to whom I
instinctively feel that she would not very tenderly incline. while
I be yet new at Doctor Strong's, she make several excursion over
to Canterbury to see I, and always at unseasonable hour: with the
view, I suppose, of take I by surprise. but, find I well
employ, and bear a good character, and hear on all hand
that I rise fast in the school, she soon discontinue these visit.
I see she on a Saturday, every third or fourth week, when I go
over to Dover for a treat; and I see Mr. Dick every alternate
Wednesday, when he arrive by stage-coach at noon, to stay until
next morning.
on these occasion Mr. Dick never travel without a leathern
writing-desk, contain a supply of stationery and the Memorial;
in relation to which document he have a notion that time be
begin to press now, and that it really must be get out of hand.
Mr. Dick be very partial to gingerbread. to render his visit the
more agreeable, my aunt have instruct I to open a credit for he
at a cake shop, which be hamper with the stipulation that he
should not be serve with more than one shilling's-worth in the
course of any one day. this, and the reference of all his little
bill at the county inn where he sleep, to my aunt, before they
be pay, induce I to suspect that he be only allow to rattle
his money, and not to spend it. I find on further investigation
that this be so, or at least there be an agreement between he
and my aunt that he should account to she for all his
disbursement. as he have no idea of deceive she, and always
desire to please she, he be thus make chary of launch into
expense. on this point, as well as on all other possible point,
Mr. Dick be convince that my aunt be the wise and most
wonderful of woman; as he repeatedly tell I with infinite secrecy,
and always in a whisper.
'trotwood,' say Mr. Dick, with an air of mystery, after impart
this confidence to I, one Wednesday; 'whobe the man that hide
near our house and frighten she?'
'frightens my aunt, sir?'
Mr. Dick nod. 'I think nothing would have frighten she,' he
say, 'for shebe -' here he whisper softly, 'donot mention it -
the wise and most wonderful of woman.' have say which, he
draw back, to observe the effect which this description of she make
upon I.
'the first time he come,' say Mr. Dick, 'was- let I see- sixteen
hundred and forty-nine be the date of King Charles's execution.
I think you say sixteen hundred and forty-nine?'
'yes, sir.'
'I donot know how it can be,' say Mr. Dick, sorely puzzle and
shake his head. 'I donot think I be as old as that.'
'be it in that year that the man appear, sir?' I ask.
'why, really' say Mr. Dick, 'I donot see how it can have be in
that year, Trotwood. do you get that date out of history?'
'yes, sir.'
'I suppose history never lie, do it?' say Mr. Dick, with a
gleam of hope.
'oh dear, no, sir!' I reply, most decisively. I be ingenuous
and young, and I think so.
'I cannot make it out,' say Mr. Dick, shake his head. 'therebe
something wrong, somewhere. however, it be very soon after the
mistake be make of put some of the trouble out of King
Charles's head into my head, that the man first come. I be
walk out with Miss Trotwood after tea, just at dark, and there
he be, close to our house.'
'walk about?' I inquire.
'walk about?' repeat Mr. Dick. 'let I see, I must recollect
a bit. n-no, no; he be not walk about.'
I ask, as the short way to get at it, what he be do.
'well, he benot there at all,' say Mr. Dick, 'until he come up
behind she, and whisper. then she turn round and faint, and
I stand still and look at he, and he walk away; but that he
should have be hide ever since (in the ground or somewhere), be
the most extraordinary thing!'
'has he be hide ever since?' I ask.
'to be sure he have,' retort Mr. Dick, nod his head gravely.
'never come out, till last night! we be walk last night, and
he come up behind she again, and I know he again.'
'and do he frighten my aunt again?'
'all of a shiver,' say Mr. Dick, counterfeit that affection and
make his tooth chatter. 'hold by the paling. cry. but,
trotwood, come here,' get I close to he, that he might
whisper very softly; 'why do she give he money, boy, in the
moonlight?'
'he be a beggar, perhaps.'
Mr. Dick shake his head, as utterly renounce the suggestion; and
have reply a great many time, and with great confidence, 'no
beggar, no beggar, no beggar, sir!' go on to say, that from his
window he have afterwards, and late at night, see my aunt give this
person money outside the garden rail in the moonlight, who then
slunk away - into the ground again, as he think probable - and
be see no more: while my aunt come hurriedly and secretly back
into the house, and have, even that morning, be quite different
from her usual self; which prey on Mr. Dick's mind.
I have not the least belief, in the outset of this story, that the
unknown be anything but a delusion of Mr. Dick's, and one of the
line of that ill-fat Prince who occasion he so much
difficulty; but after some reflection I begin to entertain the
question whether an attempt, or threat of an attempt, might have
be twice make to take poor Mr. Dick himself from under my aunt's
protection, and whether my aunt, the strength of whose kind feel
towards he I know from herself, might have be induce to pay a
price for his peace and quiet. as I be already much attach to
Mr. Dick, and very solicitous for his welfare, my fear favour
this supposition; and for a long time his Wednesday hardly ever
come round, without my entertain a misgiving that he would not
be on the coach-box as usual. there he always appear, however,
grey-head, laughing, and happy; and he never have anything more to
tell of the man who could frighten my aunt.
these wednesday be the happy day of Mr. Dick's life; they
be far from be the least happy of mine. he soon become know
to every boy in the school; and though he never take an active part
in any game but kite-flying, be as deeply interested in all our
sport as anyone among we. how often have I see he, intent upon
a match at marble or pegtop, look on with a face of unutterable
interest, and hardly breathe at the critical time! how often,
at hare and hound, have I see he mount on a little knoll,
cheer the whole field on to action, and wave his hat above his
grey head, oblivious of King Charles the Martyr's head, and all
belong to it! how many a summer hour have I know to be but
blissful minute to he in the cricket-field! how many winter day
have I see he, stand blue-nosed, in the snow and east wind,
look at the boy go down the long slide, and clap his
worst glove in rapture!
he be an universal favourite, and his ingenuity in little thing
be transcendent. he could cut orange into such device as none
of we have an idea of. he could make a boat out of anything, from
a skewer upwards. he could turn cramp-bone into chessman; fashion
roman chariot from old court card; make spoked wheel out of
cotton reel, and bird-cage of old wire. but he be great of
all, perhaps, in the article of string and straw; with which we
be all persuade he could do anything that could be do by
hand.
Mr. Dick's renown be not long confine to we. after a few
wednesday, Doctor Strong himself make some inquiry of I about
he, and I tell he all my aunt have tell I; which interest the
doctor so much that he request, on the occasion of his next
visit, to be present to he. this ceremony I perform; and the
Doctor beg Mr. Dick, whensoever he should not find I at the
coach office, to come on there, and rest himself until our
morning's work be over, it soon pass into a custom for Mr. Dick
to come on as a matter of course, and, if we be a little late, as
often happen on a Wednesday, to walk about the courtyard, wait
for I. here he make the acquaintance of the Doctor's beautiful
young wife (pale than formerly, all this time; more rarely see by
I or anyone, I think; and not so gay, but not less beautiful), and
so become more and more familiar by degree, until, at last, he
would come into the school and wait. he always sit in a particular
corner, on a particular stool, which be call 'Dick', after he;
here he would sit, with his grey head bend forward, attentively
listen to whatever might be go on, with a profound veneration
for the learning he have never be able to acquire.
this veneration Mr. Dick extend to the Doctor, whom he think
the most subtle and accomplished philosopher of any age. it be
long before Mr. Dick ever speak to he otherwise than bareheade;
and even when he and the Doctor have strike up quite a friendship,
and would walk together by the hour, on that side of the courtyard
which be know among we as the Doctor's Walk, Mr. Dick would pull
off his hat at interval to show his respect for wisdom and
knowledge. how it ever come about that the Doctor begin to read
out scrap of the famous Dictionary, in these walk, I never know;
perhaps he feel it all the same, at first, as read to himself.
however, it pass into a custom too; and Mr. Dick, listen with
a face shin with pride and pleasure, in his heart of heart
believe the Dictionary to be the most delightful book in the
world.
as I think of they go up and down before those schoolroom
window - the Doctor read with his complacent smile, an
occasional flourish of the manuscript, or grave motion of his head;
and Mr. Dick listening, enchain by interest, with his poor wit
calmly wander God know where, upon the wing of hard word - I
think of it as one of the pleasantest thing, in a quiet way, that
I have ever see. I feel as if they might go walk to and fro
for ever, and the world might somehow be the well for it - as if
a thousand thing it make a noise about, be not one half so good
for it, or I.
Agnes be one of Mr. Dick's friend, very soon; and in often come
to the house, he make acquaintance with Uriah. the friendship
between himself and I increase continually, and it be maintain
on this odd footing: that, while Mr. Dick come professedly to look
after I as my guardian, he always consult I in any little
matter of doubt that arise, and invariably guide himself by my
advice; not only have a high respect for my native sagacity, but
consider that I inherit a good deal from my aunt.
one Thursday morning, when I be about to walk with Mr. Dick from
the hotel to the coach office before go back to school (for we
have an hour's school before breakfast), I meet Uriah in the street,
who remind I of the promise I have make to take tea with himself
and his mother: add, with a writhe, 'but I donot expect you to
keep it, Master Copperfield, webe so very umble.'
I really have not yet be able to make up my mind whether I like
Uriah or detest he; and I be very doubtful about it still, as
I stand look he in the face in the street. but I feel it quite
an affront to be suppose proud, and say I only want to be
ask.
'oh, if thatbe all, Master Copperfield,' say Uriah, 'and it
really benot our umbleness that prevent you, will you come this
evening? but if it be our umbleness, I hope you willnot mind own
to it, Master Copperfield; for we be well aware of our condition.'
I say I would mention it to Mr. Wickfield, and if he approve, as
I have no doubt he would, I would come with pleasure. so, at six
o'clock that evening, which be one of the early office evening,
I announce myself as ready, to Uriah.
'Mother will be proud, indeed,' he say, as we walk away
together. 'or she would be proud, if it benot sinful, Master
Copperfield.'
'yet you donot mind suppose I be proud this morning,' I
return.
'oh dear, no, Master Copperfield!' return Uriah. 'oh, believe
I, no! such a thought never come into my head! I shouldnot have
deem it at all proud if you have think US too umble for you.
because we be so very umble.'
'have you be study much law lately?' I ask, to change the
subject.
'oh, Master Copperfield,' he say, with an air of self-denial, 'my
reading be hardly to be call study. I have pass an hour or two
in the evening, sometimes, with Mr. Tidd.'
'rather hard, I suppose?' say I.
'he be hard to I sometimes,' return Uriah. 'but I donot know
what he might be to a gifted person.'
after beat a little tune on his chin as he walk on, with the
two forefinger of his skeleton right hand, he add:
'there be expression, you see, Master Copperfield - Latin word
and term - in Mr. Tidd, that be try to a reader of my umble
attainment.'
'would you like to be teach Latin?' I say briskly. 'I will teach
it you with pleasure, as I learn it.'
'oh, thank you, Master Copperfield,' he answer, shake his head.
'I be sure itbe very kind of you to make the offer, but I be much
too umble to accept it.'
'what nonsense, Uriah!'
'oh, indeed you must excuse I, Master Copperfield! I be greatly
oblige, and I should like it of all thing, I assure you; but I be
far too umble. there be people enough to tread upon I in my
lowly state, without my do outrage to their feeling by
possessing learning. learning benot for I. a person like myself
have well not aspire. if he be to get on in life, he must get on
umbly, Master Copperfield!'
I never see his mouth so wide, or the crease in his cheek so
deep, as when he deliver himself of these sentiment: shake his
head all the time, and writhe modestly.
'I think you be wrong, Uriah,' I say. 'I dare say there be
several thing that I could teach you, if you would like to learn
they.'
'oh, I donot doubt that, Master Copperfield,' he answer; 'not in
the least. but not be umble yourself, you donot judge well,
perhaps, for they that be. I willnot provoke my better with
knowledge, thank you. Ibe much too umble. here be my umble
dwelling, Master Copperfield!'
we enter a low, old-fashioned room, walk straight into from the
street, and find there Mrs. Heep, who be the dead image of Uriah,
only short. she receive I with the utmost humility, and
apologize to I for give her son a kiss, observe that, lowly
as they be, they have their natural affection, which they hop
would give no offence to anyone. it be a perfectly decent room,
half parlour and half kitchen, but not at all a snug room. the
tea-thing be set upon the table, and the kettle be boil on
the hob. there be a chest of drawer with an escritoire top, for
Uriah to read or write at of an evening; there be Uriah's blue bag
lie down and vomit paper; there be a company of Uriah's
book command by Mr. Tidd; there be a corner cupboard: and there
be the usual article of furniture. I donot remember that any
individual object have a bare, pinch, spare look; but I do
remember that the whole place have.
it be perhaps a part of Mrs. Heep's humility, that she still wear
weed. notwithstanding the lapse of time that have occur since
Mr. Heep's decease, she still wear weed. I think there be some
compromise in the cap; but otherwise she be as weedy as in the
early day of her mourning.
'this be a day to be remember, my Uriah, I be sure,' say Mrs.
Heep, make the tea, 'when Master Copperfield pay we a visit.'
'I say youwould think so, mother,' say Uriah.
'if I could have wish father to remain among we for any reason,'
say Mrs. Heep, 'it would have be, that he might have know his
company this afternoon.'
I feel embarrassed by these compliment; but I be sensible, too,
of be entertain as an honour guest, and I think Mrs. Heep
an agreeable woman.
'my Uriah,' say Mrs. Heep, 'have look forward to this, sir, a
long while. he have his fear that our umbleness stand in the way,
and I join in they myself. umble we be, umble we have be,
umble we shall ever be,' say Mrs. Heep.
'I be sure you have no occasion to be so, ma'am,' I say, 'unless
you like.'
'thank you, sir,' retort Mrs. Heep. 'we know our station and be
thankful in it.'
I find that Mrs. Heep gradually get near to I, and that Uriah
gradually get opposite to I, and that they respectfully ply I
with the choicest of the eatable on the table. there be nothing
particularly choice there, to be sure; but I take the will for the
deed, and feel that they be very attentive. presently they begin
to talk about aunt, and then I tell they about mine; and about
father and mother, and then I tell they about mine; and then Mrs.
Heep begin to talk about father-in-law, and then I begin to tell
she about mine - but stop, because my aunt have advise I to
observe a silence on that subject. a tender young cork, however,
would have have no more chance against a pair of corkscrew, or a
tender young tooth against a pair of dentist, or a little
shuttlecock against two battledore, than I have against Uriah and
Mrs. Heep. they do just what they like with I; and worm
thing out of I that I have no desire to tell, with a certainty I
blush to think of, the more especially, as in my juvenile
frankness, I take some credit to myself for be so confidential
and feel that I be quite the patron of my two respectful
entertainer.
they be very fond of one another: that be certain. I take it,
that have its effect upon I, as a touch of nature; but the skill
with which the one follow up whatever the other say, be a touch
of art which I be still less proof against. when there be
nothing more to be get out of I about myself (for on the Murdstone
and Grinby life, and on my journey, I be dumb), they begin about
Mr. Wickfield and Agnes. Uriah throw the ball to Mrs. Heep, Mrs.
Heep catch it and throw it back to Uriah, Uriah keep it up a
little while, then send it back to Mrs. Heep, and so they go on
toss it about until I have no idea who have get it, and be quite
bewilder. the ball itself be always change too. now it be
Mr. Wickfield, now Agnes, now the excellence of Mr. Wickfield, now
my admiration of Agnes; now the extent of Mr. Wickfield's business
and resource, now our domestic life after dinner; now, the wine
that Mr. Wickfield take, the reason why he take it, and the pity
that it be he take so much; now one thing, now another, then
everything at once; and all the time, without appear to speak
very often, or to do anything but sometimes encourage they a
little, for fear they should be overcome by their humility and the
honour of my company, I find myself perpetually let out
something or other that I have no business to let out and see the
effect of it in the twinkling of Uriah's dinted nostril.
I have begin to be a little uncomfortable, and to wish myself well
out of the visit, when a figure come down the street pass the
door - it stand open to air the room, which be warm, the weather
be close for the time of year - come back again, look in, and
walk in, exclaim loudly, 'Copperfield! be it possible?'
it be Mr. Micawber! it be Mr. Micawber, with his eye-glass, and
his walking-stick, and his shirt-collar, and his genteel air, and
the condescend roll in his voice, all complete!
'my dear copperfield,' say Mr. Micawber, put out his hand,
'this be indeed a meeting which be calculate to impress the mind
with a sense of the instability and uncertainty of all human - in
short, it be a most extraordinary meeting. walk along the
street, reflect upon the probability of something turn up (of
which I be at present rather sanguine), I find a young but value
friend turn up, who be connect with the most eventful period of
my life; I may say, with the turning-point of my existence.
Copperfield, my dear fellow, how do you do?'
I cannot say - I really cannot say - that I be glad to see Mr.
Micawber there; but I be glad to see he too, and shake hand with
he, heartily, inquire how Mrs. Micawber be.
'thank you,' say Mr. Micawber, wave his hand as of old, and
settle his chin in his shirt-collar. 'she be tolerably
convalescent. the twin no long derive their sustenance from
Nature's fount - in short,' say Mr. Micawber, in one of his
burst of confidence, 'they be wean - and Mrs. Micawber be, at
present, my travel companion. she will be rejoice,
Copperfield, to renew her acquaintance with one who have prove
himself in all respect a worthy minister at the sacred altar of
friendship.'
I say I should be delighted to see she.
'you be very good,' say Mr. Micawber.
Mr. Micawber then smile, settle his chin again, and look about
he.
'I have discover my friend Copperfield,' say Mr. Micawber
genteelly, and without address himself particularly to anyone,
'not in solitude, but partaking of a social meal in company with a
widow lady, and one who be apparently her offspring - in short,'
say Mr. Micawber, in another of his burst of confidence, 'her
son. I shall esteem it an honour to be present.'
I could do no less, under these circumstance, than make Mr.
Micawber know to Uriah Heep and his mother; which I accordingly
do. as they abase themselves before he, Mr. Micawber take a
seat, and wave his hand in his most courtly manner.
'any friend of my friend Copperfield's,' say Mr. Micawber, 'have a
personal claim upon myself.'
'we be too umble, sir,' say Mrs. Heep, 'my son and I, to be the
friend of Master Copperfield. he have be so good as take his tea
with we, and we be thankful to he for his company, also to you,
sir, for your notice.'
'Ma'am,' return Mr. Micawber, with a bow, 'you be very obliging:
and what be you do, Copperfield? still in the wine trade?'
I be excessively anxious to get Mr. Micawber away; and reply,
with my hat in my hand, and a very red face, I have no doubt, that
I be a pupil at Doctor Strong's.
'a pupil?' say Mr. Micawber, raise his eyebrow. 'I be
extremely happy to hear it. although a mind like my friend
Copperfield's' - to Uriah and Mrs. Heep - 'do not require that
cultivation which, without his knowledge of man and thing, it
would require, still it be a rich soil teem with latent
vegetation - in short,' say Mr. Micawber, smile, in another
burst of confidence, 'it be an intellect capable of get up the
classic to any extent.'
Uriah, with his long hand slowly twin over one another, make a
ghastly writhe from the waist upwards, to express his concurrence
in this estimation of I.
'shall we go and see Mrs. Micawber, sir?' I say, to get Mr.
Micawber away.
'if you will do she that favour, Copperfield,' reply Mr.
Micawber, rise. 'I have no scruple in say, in the presence of
our friend here, that I be a man who have, for some year,
contend against the pressure of pecuniary difficulty.' I know
he be certain to say something of this kind; he always would be so
boastful about his difficulty. 'sometimes I have rise superior
to my difficulty. sometimes my difficulty have - in short,
have floor I. there have be time when I have administer a
succession of facer to they; there have be time when they have
be too many for I, and I have give in, and say to Mrs.
Micawber, in the word of Cato, "Plato, thou reason well. itbe
all up now. I can show fight no more." but at no time of my life,'
say Mr. Micawber, 'have I enjoy a high degree of satisfaction
than in pour my grief (if I may describe difficulty, chiefly
arise out of warrant of attorney and promissory note at two and
four month, by that word) into the bosom of my friend
Copperfield.'
Mr. Micawber close this handsome tribute by say, 'Mr. Heep!
good evening. Mrs. Heep! your servant,' and then walk out with
I in his most fashionable manner, make a good deal of noise on
the pavement with his shoe, and hum a tune as we go.
it be a little inn where Mr. Micawber put up, and he occupy a
little room in it, partition off from the commercial room, and
strongly flavour with tobacco-smoke. I think it be over the
kitchen, because a warm greasy smell appear to come up through
the chink in the floor, and there be a flabby perspiration on the
wall. I know it be near the bar, on account of the smell of
spirit and jingling of glass. here, recumbent on a small sofa,
underneath a picture of a race-horse, with her head close to the
fire, and her foot push the mustard off the dumb-waiter at the
other end of the room, be Mrs. Micawber, to whom Mr. Micawber
enter first, say, 'my dear, allow I to introduce to you a
pupil of Doctor Strong's.'
I notice, by the by, that although Mr. Micawber be just as much
confuse as ever about my age and standing, he always remember,
as a genteel thing, that I be a pupil of Doctor Strong's.
Mrs. Micawber be amazed, but very glad to see I. I be very glad
to see she too, and, after an affectionate greeting on both side,
sit down on the small sofa near she.
'my dear,' say Mr. Micawber, 'if you will mention to Copperfield
what our present position be, which I have no doubt he will like to
know, I will go and look at the paper the while, and see whether
anything turn up among the advertisement.'
'I think you be at Plymouth, ma'am,' I say to Mrs. Micawber,
as he go out.
'my dear Master Copperfield,' she reply, 'we go to Plymouth.'
'to be on the spot,' I hint.
'just so,' say Mrs. Micawber. 'to be on the spot. but, the truth
be, talent be not want in the Custom House. the local influence
of my family be quite unavaile to obtain any employment in that
department, for a man of Mr. Micawber's ability. they would
rather not have a man of Mr. Micawber's ability. he would only
show the deficiency of the other. apart from which,' say Mrs.
Micawber, 'I will not disguise from you, my dear Master
Copperfield, that when that branch of my family which be settle in
Plymouth, become aware that Mr. Micawber be accompany by myself,
and by little wilkin and his sister, and by the twin, they do
not receive he with that ardour which he might have expect,
be so newly release from captivity. in fact,' say Mrs.
Micawber, lower her voice, - 'this be between ourselves - our
reception be cool.'
'dear I!' I say.
'yes,' say Mrs. Micawber. 'it be truly painful to contemplate
mankind in such an aspect, Master Copperfield, but our reception
be, decidedly, cool. there be no doubt about it. in fact, that
branch of my family which be settle in Plymouth become quite
personal to Mr. Micawber, before we have be there a week.'
I say, and think, that they ought to be ashamed of themselves.
'still, so it be,' continue Mrs. Micawber. 'under such
circumstance, what could a man of Mr. Micawber's spirit do? but
one obvious course be leave. to borrow, of that branch of my
family, the money to return to London, and to return at any
sacrifice.'
'then you all come back again, ma'am?' I say.
'we all come back again,' reply Mrs. Micawber. 'since then, I
have consult other branch of my family on the course which it
be most expedient for Mr. Micawber to take - for I maintain that he
must take some course, Master Copperfield,' say Mrs. Micawber,
argumentatively. 'it be clear that a family of six, not include
a domestic, cannot live upon air.'
'certainly, ma'am,' say I.
'the opinion of those other branch of my family,' pursue Mrs.
Micawber, 'be, that Mr. Micawber should immediately turn his
attention to coal.'
'to what, ma'am?'
'to coal,' say Mrs. Micawber. 'to the coal trade. Mr. Micawber
be induce to think, on inquiry, that there might be an opening
for a man of his talent in the Medway Coal Trade. then, as Mr.
Micawber very properly say, the first step to be take clearly
be, to come and see the Medway. which we come and see. I say
"we", Master Copperfield; for I never will,' say Mrs. Micawber
with emotion, 'I never will desert Mr. Micawber.'
I murmur my admiration and approbation.
'we come,' repeat Mrs. Micawber, 'and see the Medway. my opinion
of the coal trade on that river be, that it may require talent, but
that it certainly require capital. talent, Mr. Micawber have;
capital, Mr. Micawber have not. we see, I think, the great part
of the Medway; and that be my individual conclusion. be so near
here, Mr. Micawber be of opinion that it would be rash not to come
on, and see the Cathedral. firstly, on account of its being so
well worth seeing, and our never have see it; and secondly, on
account of the great probability of something turn up in a
cathedral town. we have be here,' say Mrs. Micawber, 'three
day. nothing have, as yet, turn up; and it may not surprise you,
my dear Master Copperfield, so much as it would a stranger, to know
that we be at present wait for a remittance from London, to
discharge our pecuniary obligation at this hotel. until the
arrival of that remittance,' say Mrs. Micawber with much feeling,
'I be cut off from my home (I allude to lodging in Pentonville),
from my boy and girl, and from my twin.'
I feel the utmost sympathy for Mr. and Mrs. Micawber in this
anxious extremity, and say as much to Mr. Micawber, who now
return: add that I only wish I have money enough, to lend
they the amount they need. Mr. Micawber's answer express the
disturbance of his mind. he say, shake hand with I,
'copperfield, you be a true friend; but when the bad come to
the bad, no man be without a friend who be possess of shaving
material.' at this dreadful hint Mrs. Micawber throw her arm
round Mr. Micawber's neck and entreat he to be calm. he weep;
but so far recover, almost immediately, as to ring the bell for
the waiter, and bespeak a hot kidney pudding and a plate of shrimp
for breakfast in the morning.
when I take my leave of they, they both press I so much to come
and dine before they go away, that I could not refuse. but, as
I know I could not come next day, when I should have a good deal to
prepare in the evening, Mr. Micawber arrange that he would call at
Doctor Strong's in the course of the morning (have a presentiment
that the remittance would arrive by that post), and propose the day
after, if it would suit I well. accordingly I be call out of
school next forenoon, and find Mr. Micawber in the parlour; who
have call to say that the dinner would take place as propose.
when I ask he if the remittance have come, he press my hand and
depart.
as I be look out of window that same evening, it surprise I,
and make I rather uneasy, to see Mr. Micawber and Uriah Heep walk
past, arm in arm: Uriah humbly sensible of the honour that be do
he, and Mr. Micawber take a bland delight in extend his
patronage to Uriah. but I be still more surprised, when I go to
the little hotel next day at the appoint dinner-hour, which be
four o'clock, to find, from what Mr. Micawber say, that he have
go home with Uriah, and have drunk brandy-and-water at Mrs.
Heep's.
'and Iwill tell you what, my dear copperfield,' say Mr. Micawber,
'your friend Heep be a young fellow who might be attorney-general.
if I have know that young man, at the period when my difficulty
come to a crisis, all I can say be, that I believe my creditor
would have be a great deal well manage than they be.'
I hardly understand how this could have be, see that Mr.
Micawber have pay they nothing at all as it be; but I do not like
to ask. neither do I like to say, that I hop he have not be
too communicative to Uriah; or to inquire if they have talk much
about I. I be afraid of hurt Mr. Micawber's feeling, or, at
all event, Mrs. Micawber's, she be very sensitive; but I be
uncomfortable about it, too, and often think about it afterwards.
we have a beautiful little dinner. quite an elegant dish of fish;
the kidney-end of a loin of veal, roasted; fried sausage-meat; a
partridge, and a pudding. there be wine, and there be strong
ale; and after dinner Mrs. Micawber make we a bowl of hot punch
with her own hand.
Mr. Micawber be uncommonly convivial. I never see he such good
company. he make his face shine with the punch, so that it look
as if it have be varnish all over. he get cheerfully
sentimental about the town, and propose success to it; observe
that Mrs. Micawber and himself have be make extremely snug and
comfortable there and that he never should forget the agreeable
hour they have pass in Canterbury. he propose I afterwards;
and he, and Mrs. Micawber, and I, take a review of our past
acquaintance, in the course of which we sell the property all over
again. then I propose Mrs. Micawber: or, at least, say,
modestly, 'if youwill allow I, Mrs. Micawber, I shall now have the
pleasure of drink your health, ma'am.' on which Mr. Micawber
deliver an eulogium on Mrs. Micawber's character, and say she
have ever be his guide, philosopher, and friend, and that he would
recommend I, when I come to a marrying time of life, to marry such
another woman, if such another woman could be find.
as the punch disappear, Mr. Micawber become still more friendly
and convivial. Mrs. Micawber's spirit become elevate, too, we
sing 'Auld Lang Syne'. when we come to 'herebe a hand, my trusty
frere', we all join hand round the table; and when we declare
we would 'take a right gude Willie Waught', and havenot the least
idea what it mean, we be really affect.
in a word, I never see anybody so thoroughly jovial as Mr. Micawber
be, down to the very last moment of the evening, when I take a
hearty farewell of himself and his amiable wife. consequently, I
be not prepare, at seven o'clock next morning, to receive the
follow communication, date half past nine in the evening; a
quarter of an hour after I have leave he: -
'my DEAR YOUNG FRIEND,
'the die be cast - all be over. hide the ravage of care with a
sickly mask of mirth, I have not inform you, this evening, that
there be no hope of the remittance! under these circumstance,
alike humiliating to endure, humiliating to contemplate, and
humiliating to relate, I have discharge the pecuniary liability
contract at this establishment, by give a note of hand, make
payable fourteen day after date, at my residence, Pentonville,
London. when it become due, it will not be take up. the result
be destruction. the bolt be impend, and the tree must fall.
'let the wretched man who now address you, my dear Copperfield,
be a beacon to you through life. he write with that intention,
and in that hope. if he could think himself of so much use, one
gleam of day might, by possibility, penetrate into the cheerless
dungeon of his remain existence - though his longevity be, at
present (to say the least of it), extremely problematical.
'this be the last communication, my dear copperfield, you will ever
receive
'from
'the
'Beggared Outcast,
'WILKINS MICAWBER.'
I be so shocked by the content of this heart-rend letter, that
I run off directly towards the little hotel with the intention of
take it on my way to Doctor Strong's, and try to soothe Mr.
Micawber with a word of comfort. but, half-way there, I meet the
London coach with Mr. and Mrs. Micawber up behind; Mr. Micawber,
the very picture of tranquil enjoyment, smile at Mrs. Micawber's
conversation, eat walnut out of a paper bag, with a bottle
stick out of his breast pocket. as they do not see I, I
think it well, all thing consider, not to see they. so, with
a great weight take off my mind, I turn into a by-street that
be the near way to school, and feel, upon the whole, relieve
that they be go; though I still like they very much,
nevertheless.
CHAPTER 18
a retrospect
my school-day! the silent gliding on of my existence - the
unseen, unfelt progress of my life - from childhood up to youth!
let I think, as I look back upon that flow water, now a dry
channel overgrown with leaf, whether there be any mark along
its course, by which I can remember how it run.
a moment, and I occupy my place in the Cathedral, where we all go
together, every Sunday morning, assemble first at school for that
purpose. the earthy smell, the sunless air, the sensation of the
world be shut out, the resounding of the organ through the black
and white arch gallery and aisle, be wing that take I back,
and hold I hover above those day, in a half-sleep and
half-wake dream.
I be not the last boy in the school. I have rise in a few month,
over several head. but the first boy seem to I a mighty
creature, dwell afar off, whose giddy height be unattainable.
Agnes say 'no,' but I say 'yes,' and tell she that she little
think what store of knowledge have be master by the wonderful
being, at whose place she think I, even I, weak aspirant, may
arrive in time. he be not my private friend and public patron, as
Steerforth be, but I hold he in a reverential respect. I chiefly
wonder what hewill be, when he leave Doctor Strong's, and what
mankind will do to maintain any place against he.
but who be this that break upon I? this be Miss Shepherd, whom
I love.
Miss Shepherd be a boarder at the Misses Nettingalls'
establishment. I adore Miss Shepherd. she be a little girl, in a
spencer, with a round face and curly flaxen hair. the Misses
Nettingalls' young lady come to the Cathedral too. I cannot look
upon my book, for I must look upon Miss Shepherd. when the
chorister chaunt, I hear Miss Shepherd. in the service I mentally
insert Miss Shepherd's name - I put she in among the Royal Family.
at home, in my own room, I be sometimes move to cry out, 'oh, Miss
Shepherd!' in a transport of love.
for some time, I be doubtful of Miss Shepherd's feeling, but, at
length, fate be propitious, we meet at the dancing-school. I
have Miss Shepherd for my partner. I touch Miss Shepherd's glove,
and feel a thrill go up the right arm of my jacket, and come out at
my hair. I say nothing to Miss Shepherd, but we understand each
other. Miss Shepherd and myself live but to be unite.
why do I secretly give Miss Shepherd twelve Brazil nut for a
present, I wonder? they be not expressive of affection, they be
difficult to pack into a parcel of any regular shape, they be hard
to crack, even in room door, and they be oily when crack; yet
I feel that they be appropriate to Miss Shepherd. soft, seedy
biscuit, also, I bestow upon Miss Shepherd; and orange
innumerable. once, I kiss Miss Shepherd in the cloak-room.
Ecstasy! what be my agony and indignation next day, when I hear
a fly rumour that the Misses Nettingall have stand Miss Shepherd
in the stock for turn in her toe!
Miss Shepherd be the one pervade theme and vision of my life,
how do I ever come to break with she? I cannot conceive. and yet
a coolness grow between Miss Shepherd and myself. whisper reach
I of Miss Shepherd have say she wish I wouldnot stare so, and
have avow a preference for Master Jones - for Jones! a boy of
no merit whatever! the gulf between I and Miss Shepherd widen.
at last, one day, I meet the Misses Nettingalls' establishment out
walk. Miss Shepherd make a face as she go by, and laugh to
her companion. all be over. the devotion of a life - it seem a
life, it be all the same - be at an end; Miss Shepherd come out of
the morning service, and the Royal Family know she no more.
I be high in the school, and no one break my peace. I be not at
all polite, now, to the Misses Nettingalls' young lady, and
shouldnot dote on any of they, if they be twice as many and
twenty time as beautiful. I think the dancing-school a tiresome
affair, and wonder why the girl cannot dance by themselves and
leave we alone. I be grow great in Latin verse, and neglect
the lace of my boot. Doctor Strong refer to I in public as a
promising young scholar. Mr. Dick be wild with joy, and my aunt
remit I a guinea by the next post.
the shade of a young butcher rise, like the apparition of an armed
head in Macbeth. who be this young butcher? he be the terror of
the youth of Canterbury. there be a vague belief abroad, that the
beef suet with which he anoint his hair give he unnatural
strength, and that he be a match for a man. he be a broad-face,
bull-neck, young butcher, with rough red cheek, an
ill-condition mind, and an injurious tongue. his main use of
this tongue, be, to disparage Doctor Strong's young gentleman. he
say, publicly, that if they want anything hewill give it them. he
name individual among they (myself include), whom he could
undertake to settle with one hand, and the other tie behind he.
he waylay the small boy to punch their unprotected head, and
call challenge after I in the open street. for these
sufficient reason I resolve to fight the butcher.
it be a summer evening, down in a green hollow, at the corner of a
wall. I meet the butcher by appointment. I be attend by a
select body of our boy; the butcher, by two other butcher, a
young publican, and a sweep. the preliminary be adjust, and
the butcher and myself stand face to face. in a moment the butcher
light ten thousand candle out of my left eyebrow. in another
moment, I donot know where the wall be, or where I be, or where
anybody be. I hardly know which be myself and which the butcher,
we be always in such a tangle and tussle, knock about upon the
trodden grass. sometimes I see the butcher, bloody but confident;
sometimes I see nothing, and sit gasp on my second's knee;
sometimes I go in at the butcher madly, and cut my knuckle open
against his face, without appear to discompose he at all. at
last I awake, very queer about the head, as from a giddy sleep, and
see the butcher walk off, congratulate by the two other
butcher and the sweep and publican, and put on his coat as he
go; from which I augur, justly, that the victory be his.
I be take home in a sad plight, and I have beef-steak put to my
eye, and be rub with vinegar and brandy, and find a great puffy
place burst out on my upper lip, which swell immoderately. for
three or four day I remain at home, a very ill-look subject,
with a green shade over my eye; and I should be very dull, but
that Agnes be a sister to I, and condole with I, and read to
I, and make the time light and happy. Agnes have my confidence
completely, always; I tell she all about the butcher, and the
wrong he have heap upon I; she think I couldnot have do
otherwise than fight the butcher, while she shrink and tremble at
my have fight he.
Time have steal on unobserved, for Adams be not the head-boy in the
day that be come now, nor have he be this many and many a day.
Adams have leave the school so long, that when he come back, on a
visit to Doctor Strong, there be not many there, besides myself,
who know he. Adams be go to be call to the bar almost
directly, and be to be an advocate, and to wear a wig. I be
surprised to find he a meeker man than I have think, and less
impose in appearance. he have not stagger the world yet,
either; for it go on (as well as I can make out) pretty much the
same as if he have never join it.
a blank, through which the warrior of poetry and history march on
in stately host that seem to have no end - and what come next!
I be the head-boy, now! I look down on the line of boy below I,
with a condescend interest in such of they as bring to my mind

seem to be no part of I; I remember he as something leave behind

and the little girl I see on that first day at Mr. Wickfield's,

what other change have come upon I, besides the change in my

the eld Miss Larkins know officer. it be an awful thing to

my passion take away my appetite, and make I wear my new silk

I think continually about my age. say I be seventeen, and say that

besides, I shall be one-and-twenty in no time almost. I regularly

generally, but not always. sometimes bright vision rise before

I repair to the enchanted house, where there be light,

but after I have stand in the doorway for some time, and feast my

I stammer, with a bow, 'with you, Miss Larkins.'

Miss Larkins laugh and blush (or I think she blush), and say,

the time arrive. 'it be a waltz, I think,' Miss Larkins

not, Captain Bailey -'
but I do waltz (pretty well, too, as it happen), and I take Miss

he be wretche, I have no doubt; but he be nothing to I. I have

'I ask an inestimable price for it, Miss Larkins.'
'indeed! what be that?' return Miss Larkins.
'a flower of your, that I may treasure it as a miser do gold.'
'yoube a bold boy,' say Miss Larkins. 'there.'
she give it I, not displeased; and I put it to my lip, and then

I be lose in the recollection of this delicious interview, and the

'oh! here be my bold friend! Mr. Chestle want to know you, Mr.

I feel at once that he be a friend of the family, and be much

'I admire your taste, sir,' say Mr. Chestle. 'it do you credit.
I suppose you donot take much interest in hop; but I be a pretty
large grower myself; and if you ever like to come over to our
neighbourhood - neighbourhood of Ashford - and take a run about our
place, -we shall be glad for you to stop as long as you like.'
I thank Mr. Chestle warmly, and shake hand. I think I be in a

say I waltz so well! I go home in a state of unspeakable bliss,

nor when I call. I be imperfectly console for this disappointment
by the sacred pledge, the perished flower.
'trotwood,' say Agnes, one day after dinner. 'who do you think be
go to be marry tomorrow? someone you admire.'
'not you, I suppose, Agnes?'
'not I!' raise her cheerful face from the music she be copy.

'to - to Captain Bailey?' I have just enough power to ask.

this, and the resumption of my ring, as well as of the bear's

CHAPTER 19
I look about ME, and make a discovery
I be doubtful whether I be at heart glad or sorry, when my
school-day draw to an end, and the time come for my leave Doctor
Strong's. I have be very happy there, I have a great attachment
for the Doctor, and I be eminent and distinguish in that little
world. for these reason I be sorry to go; but for other reason,
unsubstantial enough, I be glad. misty idea of be a young man
at my own disposal, of the importance attach to a young man at
his own disposal, of the wonderful thing to be see and do by
that magnificent animal, and the wonderful effect he could not
fail to make upon society, lure I away. so powerful be these
visionary consideration in my boyish mind, that I seem, accord
to my present way of thinking, to have leave school without natural
regret. the separation have not make the impression on I, that
other separation have. I try in vain to recall how I feel about
it, and what its circumstance be; but it be not momentous in my
recollection. I suppose the opening prospect confuse I. I know
that my juvenile experience go for little or nothing then; and
that life be more like a great fairy story, which I be just about
to begin to read, than anything else.
my aunt and I have hold many grave deliberation on the calling to
which I should be devote. for a year or more I have endeavour to
find a satisfactory answer to her often-repeat question, 'what I
would like to be?' but I have no particular liking, that I could
discover, for anything. if I could have be inspire with a
knowledge of the science of navigation, take the command of a
fast-sailing expedition, and go round the world on a triumphant
voyage of discovery, I think I might have consider myself
completely suit. but, in the absence of any such miraculous
provision, my desire be to apply myself to some pursuit that would
not lie too heavily upon her purse; and to do my duty in it,
whatever it might be.
Mr. Dick have regularly assist at our council, with a meditative
and sage demeanour. he never make a suggestion but once; and on
that occasion (I donot know what put it in his head), he suddenly
propose that I should be 'a Brazier'. my aunt receive this
proposal so very ungraciously, that he never venture on a second;
but ever afterwards confine himself to look watchfully at she
for her suggestion, and rattle his money.
'Trot, I tell you what, my dear,' say my aunt, one morning in the
Christmas season when I leave school: 'as this knotty point be still
unsettled, and as we must not make a mistake in our decision if we
can help it, I think we have well take a little breathing-time.
in the meanwhile, you must try to look at it from a new point of
view, and not as a schoolboy.'
'I will, aunt.'
'it have occur to I,' pursue my aunt, 'that a little change,
and a glimpse of life out of door, may be useful in help you to
know your own mind, and form a cool judgement. suppose you be
to go down into the old part of the country again, for instance,
and see that - that out-of-the-way woman with the savage of
name,' say my aunt, rub her nose, for she could never
thoroughly forgive Peggotty for be so call.
'of all thing in the world, aunt, I should like it well!'
'well,' say my aunt, 'thatbe lucky, for I should like it too. but
itbe natural and rational that you should like it. and I be very
well persuade that whatever you do, Trot, will always be natural
and rational.'
'I hope so, aunt.'
'your sister, Betsey Trotwood,' say my aunt, 'would have be as
natural and rational a girl as ever breathe. youwill be worthy of
she, willnot you?'
'I hope I shall be worthy of you, aunt. that will be enough for
I.'
'itbe a mercy that poor dear baby of a mother of yours donot
live,' say my aunt, look at I approvingly, 'or shewould have be
so vain of her boy by this time, that her soft little head would
have be completely turn, if there be anything of it leave to
turn.' (my aunt always excuse any weakness of her own in my
behalf, by transfer it in this way to my poor mother.) 'bless
I, Trotwood, how you do remind I of she!'
'pleasantly, I hope, aunt?' say I.
'hebe as like she, Dick,' say my aunt, emphatically, 'hebe as like
she, as she be that afternoon before she begin to fret - bless my
heart, hebe as like she, as he can look at I out of his two eye!'
'be he indeed?' say Mr. Dick.
'and hebe like David, too,' say my aunt, decisively.
'he be very like David!' say Mr. Dick.
'but what I want you to be, trot,' resume my aunt, '- I donot mean
physically, but morally; you be very well physically - be, a firm
fellow. a fine firm fellow, with a will of your own. with
resolution,' say my aunt, shake her cap at I, and clench she
hand. 'with determination. with character, trot - with strength
of character that be not to be influence, except on good reason,
by anybody, or by anything. thatbe what I want you to be. thatbe
what your father and mother might both have be, Heaven know, and
be the well for it.'
I intimate that I hop I should be what she describe.
'that you may begin, in a small way, to have a reliance upon
yourself, and to act for yourself,' say my aunt, 'I shall send you
upon your trip, alone. I do think, once, of Mr. Dick's go with
you; but, on second thought, I shall keep he to take care of I.'
Mr. Dick, for a moment, look a little disappointed; until the
honour and dignity of have to take care of the most wonderful
woman in the world, restore the sunshine to his face.
'besides,' say my aunt, 'therebe the Memorial -'
'oh, certainly,' say Mr. Dick, in a hurry, 'I intend, Trotwood, to
get that do immediately - it really must be do immediately!
and then it will go in, you know - and then -' say Mr. Dick, after
check himself, and pause a long time, 'therewill be a pretty
kettle of fish!'
in pursuance of my aunt's kind scheme, I be shortly afterwards
fit out with a handsome purse of money, and a portmanteau, and
tenderly dismiss upon my expedition. at parting, my aunt give I
some good advice, and a good many kiss; and say that as she
object be that I should look about I, and should think a little,
she would recommend I to stay a few day in London, if I like it,
either on my way down into Suffolk, or in come back. in a word,
I be at liberty to do what I would, for three week or a month;
and no other condition be impose upon my freedom than the
before-mention thinking and look about I, and a pledge to
write three time a week and faithfully report myself.
I go to Canterbury first, that I might take leave of Agnes and
Mr. Wickfield (my old room in whose house I have not yet
relinquish), and also of the good Doctor. Agnes be very glad to
see I, and tell I that the house have not be like itself since
I have leave it.
'I be sure I be not like myself when I be away,' say I. 'I seem
to want my right hand, when I miss you. though thatbe not say
much; for therebe no head in my right hand, and no heart. everyone
who know you, consult with you, and be guide by you, Agnes.'
'everyone who know I, spoil I, I believe,' she answer,
smile.
'no. itbe because you be like no one else. you be so good, and
so sweet-temper. you have such a gentle nature, and you be
always right.'

'come! itbe not fair to abuse my confidence,' I answer,
redden at the recollection of my blue enslaver. 'but I shall

that. whenever I fall into trouble, or fall in love, I shall

we be to drink tea at the Doctor's. we go there at the usual

'Ill!' reply the Old Soldier. 'my dear sir, hebe all sort of

'oh! responsibility!' say the Old Soldier. 'everything be do

Mrs. Markleham be so overcome by this generous speech - which, I

I have no pleasure in thinking, any more, of the grave old

but morning bring with it my parting from the old house, which

I get away from Agnes and her father, somehow, with an indifferent

the main object on my mind, I remember, when we get fairly on the

grow-up sort of thing.
'you be go through, sir?' say the coachman.
'yes, William,' I say, condescendingly (I know he); 'I be go

'shooting, sir?' say the coachman.
he know as well as I do that it be just as likely, at that time

shall take a shot or not.'

'yes,' I say, with some importance. 'Suffolk's my county.'
