I be not aware of it myself, but I feel it necessary to uphold the

the gentleman speak of be a gentleman with a very unpromising

'that benot a sort of man to see sit behind a coach-box, be it

I construe this remark into an indication of a wish that he should

I have always consider this as the first fall I have in life.

a distrust of myself, which have often beset I in life on small

we go to the Golden Cross at Charing Cross, then a mouldy sort of

'well now,' say the waiter, in a tone of confidence, 'what would

I tell he, as majestically as I could, that I benot in the humour

I assent to this proposal, in default of be able to suggest

I command he, in my deep voice, to order a veal cutlet and

be then in a pleasant frame of mind (from which I infer that

I have emerge by another door, and stand in the street for a little

I be so fill with the play, and with the past - for it be, in

he look at I - just as he use to look, sometimes -but I see no

I grasp he by both hand, and could not let they go. but for

he laugh as he run his hand through the cluster curl of his

this be address to the waiter, who have be very attentive to

'of course it would be prefer,' say Steerforth. 'and do it at

the waiter immediately withdraw to make the exchange. Steerforth,
very much amuse at my have be put into forty-four, laugh

friendly heartiness at his door, and where I find my new room a
great improvement on my old one, it not be at all musty, and
have an immense four-post bedstead in it, which be quite a
little landed estate. here, among pillow enough for six, I soon
fall asleep in a blissful condition, and dream of ancient Rome,
Steerforth, and friendship, until the early morning coach,
rumble out of the archway underneath, make I dream of thunder
and the god.
CHAPTER 20
STEERFORTH's HOME
when the chambermaid tap at my door at eight o'clock, and
inform I that my shaving-water be outside, I feel severely the
have no occasion for it, and blush in my bed. the suspicion
that she laugh too, when she say it, prey upon my mind all the

I could hardly believe but that I be in a dream, and that I should

'thatbe a good fellow! my dear Daisy,' say Steerforth, laugh.

I be abash at have make so great a mistake, and be glad to

Lunch succeed to our sight-seeing, and the short winter day wear

it be a genteel old-fashioned house, very quiet and orderly. from

CHAPTER 21
LITTLE em'ly
there be a servant in that house, a man who, I understand, be
usually with Steerforth, and have come into his service at the
University, who be in appearance a pattern of respectability. I
believe there never exist in his station a more
respectable-look man. he be taciturn, soft-footed, very quiet
in his manner, deferential, observant, always at hand when want,
and never near when not want; but his great claim to
consideration be his respectability. he have not a pliant face, he
have rather a stiff neck, rather a tight smooth head with short hair
cling to it at the side, a soft way of speak, with a
peculiar habit of whisper the letter s so distinctly, that he
seem to use it oftener than any other man; but every peculiarity
that he have he make respectable. if his nose have be upside-down,
he would have make that respectable. he surround himself with an
atmosphere of respectability, and walk secure in it. it would
have be next to impossible to suspect he of anything wrong, he
be so thoroughly respectable. nobody could have think of
put he in a livery, he be so highly respectable. to have
impose any derogatory work upon he, would have be to inflict a
wanton insult on the feeling of a most respectable man. and of
this, I noticed- the woman-servant in the household be so
intuitively conscious, that they always do such work themselves,
and generally while he read the paper by the pantry fire.
such a self-contain man I never see. but in that quality, as in
every other he possess, he only seem to be the more
respectable. even the fact that no one know his christian name,
seem to form a part of his respectability. nothing could be
object against his surname, Littimer, by which he be know.
Peter might have be hang, or Tom transport; but Littimer be
perfectly respectable.
it be occasion, I suppose, by the reverend nature of
respectability in the abstract, but I feel particularly young in
this man's presence. how old he be himself, I could not guess -
and that again go to his credit on the same score; for in the
calmness of respectability he might have number fifty year as
well as thirty.
Littimer be in my room in the morning before I be up, to bring I
that reproachful shaving-water, and to put out my clothe. when I
undrew the curtain and look out of bed, I see he, in an equable
temperature of respectability, unaffected by the east wind of
January, and not even breathe frostily, stand my boot right
and leave in the first dancing position, and blow speck of dust
off my coat as he lay it down like a baby.
I give he good morning, and ask he what o'clock it be. he
take out of his pocket the most respectable hunting-watch I ever
see, and prevent the spring with his thumb from open far,
look in at the face as if he be consult an oracular oyster,
shut it up again, and say, if I please, it be half past eight.
'Mr. Steerforth will be glad to hear how you have rest, sir.'
'thank you,' say I, 'very well indeed. be Mr. Steerforth quite
well?'
'thank you, sir, Mr. Steerforth be tolerably well.' another of his
characteristic - no use of superlative. a cool calm medium
always.
'be there anything more I can have the honour of do for you,
sir? the warning-bell will ring at nine; the family take breakfast
at half past nine.'
'nothing, I thank you.'
'I thank you, sir, if you please'; and with that, and with a little
inclination of his head when he pass the bed-side, as an apology
for correct I, he go out, shut the door as delicately as
if I have just fall into a sweet sleep on which my life depend.
every morning we hold exactly this conversation: never any more,
and never any less: and yet, invariably, however far I might have
be lift out of myself over-night, and advance towards mature
year, by Steerforth's companionship, or Mrs. Steerforth's
confidence, or Miss Dartle's conversation, in the presence of this
most respectable man I became, as our small poet sing, 'a boy
again'.
he get horse for we; and Steerforth, who know everything, give I
lesson in rid. he provide foil for we, and Steerforth give
me lesson in fencing - glove, and I begin, of the same master, to
improve in boxing. it give I no manner of concern that Steerforth
should find I a novice in these science, but I never could bear
to show my want of skill before the respectable Littimer. I have no
reason to believe that Littimer understand such art himself; he
never lead I to suppose anything of the kind, by so much as the
vibration of one of his respectable eyelash; yet whenever he be
by, while we be practise, I feel myself the green and most
inexperienced of mortal.
I be particular about this man, because he make a particular effect
on I at that time, and because of what take place thereafter.
the week pass away in a most delightful manner. it pass
rapidly, as may be suppose, to one entrance as I be; and yet it
give I so many occasion for know Steerforth well, and
admire he more in a thousand respect, that at its close I
seem to have be with he for a much long time. a dashing way
he have of treat I like a plaything, be more agreeable to I
than any behaviour he could have adopt. it remind I of our
old acquaintance; it seem the natural sequel of it; it show I
that he be unchanged; it relieve I of any uneasiness I might
have feel, in compare my merit with his, and measure my claim
upon his friendship by any equal standard; above all, it be a
familiar, unrestrained, affectionate demeanour that he use towards
no one else. as he have treat I at school differently from all
the rest, I joyfully believe that he treat I in life unlike any
other friend he have. I believe that I be near to his heart
than any other friend, and my own heart warm with attachment to
he.
he make up his mind to go with I into the country, and the day
arrive for our departure. he have be doubtful at first whether
to take Littimer or not, but decide to leave he at home. the
respectable creature, satisfied with his lot whatever it be,
arrange our portmanteaux on the little carriage that be to take
we into London, as if they be intend to defy the shock of
age, and receive my modestly proffer donation with perfect
tranquillity.
we bade adieu to Mrs. Steerforth and Miss Dartle, with many thank
on my part, and much kindness on the devoted mother's. the last
thing I see be Littimer's unruffled eye; fraught, as I fancy,
with the silent conviction that I be very young indeed.
what I feel, in return so auspiciously to the old familiar

be in great spirit, have be stroll about the beach before I

sure must be the identical house of Mr. Peggotty, with smoke come
out of the chimney; and have have a great mind, he tell I, to walk
in and swear he be myself grow out of knowledge.
'when do you propose to introduce I there, Daisy?' he say. 'I be
at your disposal. make your own arrangement.'
'why, I be think that this evening would be a good time,
Steerforth, when they be all sit round the fire. I should
like you to see it when itbe snug, itbe such a curious place.'
'so be it!' return Steerforth. 'this evening.'
'I shall not give they any notice that we be here, you know,' say

'oh, of course! itbe no fun,' say Steerforth, 'unless we take

'though they be that sort of people that you mention,' I
return.
'aha! what! you recollect my skirmish with Rosa, do you?' he

be you go to do? you be go to see your nurse, I suppose?'
'why, yes,' I say, 'I must see Peggotty first of all.'
'well,' reply Steerforth, look at his watch. 'suppose I
deliver you up to be cry over for a couple of hour. be that
long enough?'
I answer, laugh, that I think we might get through it in
that time, but that he must come also; for he would find that his
renown have precede he, and that he be almost as great a
personage as I be.
'Iwill come anywhere you like,' say Steerforth, 'or do anything you
like. tell I where to come to; and in two hour Iwill produce
myself in any state you please, sentimental or comical.'
I give he minute direction for find the residence of Mr.
Barkis, carrier to Blunderstone and elsewhere; and, on this
understanding, go out alone. there be a sharp bracing air; the
ground be dry; the sea be crisp and clear; the sun be diffuse
abundance of light, if not much warmth; and everything be fresh
and lively. I be so fresh and lively myself, in the pleasure of
be there, that I could have stop the people in the street
and shake hand with they.
the street look small, of course. the street that we have only
see as child always do, I believe, when we go back to they.
but I have forget nothing in they, and find nothing change,
until I come to Mr. Omer's shop. OMER and Joram be now write
up, where OMER use to be; but the inscription, draper, TAILOR,
HABERDASHER, FUNERAL FURNISHER, &c., remain as it be.
my footstep seem to tend so naturally to the shop door, after I
have read these word from over the way, that I go across the road
and look in. there be a pretty woman at the back of the shop,
dance a little child in her arm, while another little fellow
clung to her apron. I have no difficulty in recognize either
Minnie or Minnie's child. the glass door of the parlour be not
open; but in the workshop across the yard I could faintly hear the
old tune playing, as if it have never leave off.
'be Mr. Omer at home?' say I, enter. 'I should like to see
he, for a moment, if he be.'
'oh yes, sir, he be at home,' say Minnie; 'the weather donot suit
his asthma out of door. Joe, call your grandfather!'
the little fellow, who be hold her apron, give such a lusty
shout, that the sound of it make he bashful, and he bury his
face in her skirt, to her great admiration. I hear a heavy
puffing and blow come towards we, and soon Mr. Omer,
shorter-wind than of yore, but not much older-look, stand
before I.
'Servant, sir,' say Mr. Omer. 'what can I do for you, sir?'
'you can shake hand with I, Mr. Omer, if you please,' say I,
put out my own. 'you be very good-natured to I once, when
I be afraid I donot show that I think so.'
'be I though?' return the old man. 'Ibe glad to hear it, but I
donot remember when. be you sure it be I?'
'quite.'
'I think my memory have get as short as my breath,' say Mr. Omer,
look at I and shake his head; 'for I donot remember you.'
'donot you remember your come to the coach to meet I, and my
have breakfast here, and our rid out to Blunderstone together:
you, and I, and Mrs. Joram, and Mr. Joram too - who benot her
husband then?'
'why, Lord bless my soul!' exclaim Mr. Omer, after be throw
by his surprise into a fit of cough, 'you donot say so! Minnie,
my dear, you recollect? dear I, yes; the party be a lady, I
think?'
'my mother,' I rejoin.
'to - be - sure,' say Mr. Omer, touch my waistcoat with his
forefinger, 'and there be a little child too! there be two
party. the little party be lay along with the other party.
over at Blunderstone it be, of course. dear I! and how have you
be since?'
very well, I thank he, as I hop he have be too.
'oh! nothing to grumble at, you know,' say Mr. Omer. 'I find my
breath get short, but it seldom get long as a man get old.
I take it as it come, and make the most of it. thatbe the good
way, benot it?'
Mr. Omer cough again, in consequence of laugh, and be
assist out of his fit by his daughter, who now stand close beside
we, dance she small child on the counter.
'dear I!' say Mr. Omer. 'yes, to be sure. two party! why, in
that very ride, if youwill believe I, the day be name for my
Minnie to marry Joram. "do name it, sir," say Joram. "yes, do,
father," say Minnie. and now hebe come into the business. and
look here! the young!'
Minnie laugh, and stroke her band hair upon her temple, as
her father put one of his fat finger into the hand of the child
she be dance on the counter.
'two party, of course!' say Mr. Omer, nod his head
retrospectively. 'ex-actly so! and Joram's at work, at this
minute, on a grey one with silver nail, not this measurement' -
the measurement of the dancing child upon the counter - 'by a good
two inch. - will you take something?'
I thank he, but decline.
'let I see,' say Mr. Omer. 'Barkis's the carrier's wife -
Peggotty's the boatman's sister - she have something to do with your
family? she be in service there, sure?'
my answering in the affirmative give he great satisfaction.
'I believe my breath will get long next, my memory's get so
much so,' say Mr. Omer. 'well, sir, wehave get a young relation of
her here, under article to we, that have as elegant a taste in the
dress-make business - I assure you I donot believe therebe a
Duchess in England can touch she.'
'not little Em'ly?' say I, involuntarily.
'Em'ly's her name,' say Mr. Omer, 'and shebe little too. but if
youwill believe I, she have such a face of her own that half the
woman in this town be mad against she.'
'Nonsense, father!' cry Minnie.
'my dear,' say Mr. Omer, 'I donot say itbe the case with you,'
wink at I, 'but I say that half the woman in Yarmouth - ah! and
in five mile round - be mad against that girl.'
'then she should have keep to her own station in life, father,'
say Minnie, 'and not have give they any hold to talk about she,
and then they couldnot have do it.'
'couldnot have do it, my dear!' retort Mr. Omer. 'couldnot
have do it! be that your knowledge of life? what be there that
any woman couldnot do, that she shouldnot do - especially on the
subject of another woman's good look?'
I really think it be all over with Mr. Omer, after he have
utter this libellous pleasantry. he cough to that extent, and
his breath elude all his attempt to recover it with that
obstinacy, that I fully expect to see his head go down behind the
counter, and his little black breech, with the rusty little
bunch of ribbon at the knee, come quiver up in a last
ineffectual struggle. at length, however, he get well, though he
still pant hard, and be so exhausted that he be oblige to sit
on the stool of the shop-desk.
'you see,' he say, wipe his head, and breathe with difficulty,
'she havenot take much to any companion here; she havenot take
kindly to any particular acquaintance and friend, not to mention
sweetheart. in consequence, an ill-natured story get about, that
Em'ly want to be a lady. now my opinion be, that it come into
circulation principally on account of she sometimes say, at the
school, that if she be a lady she would like to do so-and-so for
her uncle - donot you see? - and buy he such-and-such fine
thing.'
'I assure you, Mr. Omer, she have say so to I,' I return
eagerly, 'when we be both child.'
Mr. Omer nod his head and rub his chin. 'just so. then out
of a very little, she could dress herself, you see, well than
most other could out of a deal, and that make thing unpleasant.
moreover, she be rather what might be call wayward - Iwill go so
far as to say what I should call wayward myself,' say Mr. Omer; '-
donot know her own mind quite - a little spoiled - and couldnot,
at first, exactly bind herself down. no more than that be ever
say against she, Minnie?'
'no, father,' say Mrs. Joram. 'thatbe the bad, I believe.'
'so when she get a situation,' say Mr. Omer, 'to keep a fractious
old lady company, they donot very well agree, and she donot stop.
at last she come here, apprentice for three year. nearly two of
them be over, and she have be as good a girl as ever be. worth
any six! Minnie, be she worth any six, now?'
'yes, father,' reply Minnie. 'never say I detract from she!'
'very good,' say Mr. Omer. 'thatbe right. and so, young
gentleman,' he add, after a few moment' far rubbing of his
chin, 'that you may not consider I long-wind as well as
short-breathe, I believe thatbe all about it.'
as they have speak in a subdue tone, while speak of Em'ly, I
have no doubt that she be near. on my asking now, if that be not
so, Mr. Omer nod yes, and nod towards the door of the
parlour. my hurried inquiry if I might peep in, be answer with
a free permission; and, look through the glass, I see she
sit at her work. I see she, a most beautiful little creature,
with the cloudless blue eye, that have look into my childish
heart, turn laughingly upon another child of Minnie's who be
play near she; with enough of wilfulness in her bright face to
justify what I have hear; with much of the old capricious coyness
lurking in it; but with nothing in her pretty look, I be sure, but
what be mean for goodness and for happiness, and what be on a
good and happy course.
the tune across the yard that seem as if it never have leave off -
alas! it be the tune that never do leave off - be beat,
softly, all the while.
'wouldnot you like to step in,' say Mr. Omer, 'and speak to she?
walk in and speak to she, sir! make yourself at home!'
I be too bashful to do so then - I be afraid of confuse she,
and I be no less afraid of confuse myself.- but I inform
myself of the hour at which she leave of an evening, in order that
our visit might be time accordingly; and take leave of Mr. Omer,
and his pretty daughter, and her little child, go away to my
dear old Peggotty's.
here she be, in the tile kitchen, cook dinner! the moment I
knock at the door she open it, and ask I what I please to
want. I look at she with a smile, but she give I no smile in
return. I have never cease to write to she, but it must have be
seven year since we have meet.
'be Mr. Barkis at home, ma'am?' I say, feign to speak roughly
to she.
'hebe at home, sir,' return Peggotty, 'but hebe bad abe with the
rheumatic.'
'donot he go over to Blunderstone now?' I ask.
'when hebe well he do,' she answer.
'do you ever go there, Mrs. Barkis?'
she look at I more attentively, and I notice a quick movement
of her hand towards each other.
'because I want to ask a question about a house there, that they
call the - what be it? - the Rookery,' say I.
she take a step backward, and put out her hand in an undecided
frightened way, as if to keep I off.
'Peggotty!' I cry to she.
she cry, 'my darling boy!' and we both burst into tear, and be
lock in one another's arm.
what extravagance she commit; what laugh and cry over I;
what pride she show, what joy, what sorrow that she whose pride
and joy I might have be, could never hold I in a fond embrace;
I have not the heart to tell. I be trouble with no misgiving
that it be young in I to respond to her emotion. I have never
laugh and cry in all my life, I dare say - not even to her -
more freely than I do that morning.
'Barkis will be so glad,' say Peggotty, wipe her eye with her
apron, 'that itwill do he more good than pint of liniment. may I
go and tell he you be here? will you come up and see he, my
dear?'
of course I would. but Peggotty could not get out of the room as
easily as she mean to, for as often as she get to the door and
look round at I, she come back again to have another laugh and
another cry upon my shoulder. at last, to make the matter easy,
I go upstairs with she; and have wait outside for a minute,
while she say a word of preparation to Mr. Barkis, present
myself before that invalid.
he receive I with absolute enthusiasm. he be too rheumatic to
be shake hand with, but he beg I to shake the tassel on the
top of his nightcap, which I do most cordially. when I sit down
by the side of the bed, he say that it do he a world of good to
feel as if he be drive I on the Blunderstone road again. as he
lie in bed, face upward, and so cover, with that exception, that
he seem to be nothing but a face - like a conventional cherubim
- he look the queer object I ever beheld.
'what name be it, as I write up in the cart, sir?' say Mr.
Barkis, with a slow rheumatic smile.
'ah! Mr. Barkis, we have some grave talk about that matter, havenot
we?'
'I be willin' a long time, sir?' say Mr. Barkis.
'a long time,' say I.
'and I donot regret it,' say Mr. Barkis. 'do you remember what
you tell I once, about she make all the apple parstie and do
all the cooking?'
'yes, very well,' I return.
'it be as true,' say Mr. Barkis, 'as turnip be. it be as
true,' say Mr. Barkis, nod his nightcap, which be his only
mean of emphasis, 'as taxis be. and nothingbe truer than they.'
Mr. Barkis turn his eye upon I, as if for my assent to this
result of his reflection in bed; and I give it.
'nothingbe truer than they,' repeat Mr. Barkis; 'a man as poor as
I be, find that out in his mind when hebe lay up. Ibe a very
poor man, sir!'
'I be sorry to hear it, Mr. Barkis.'
'a very poor man, indeed I be,' say Mr. Barkis.
here his right hand come slowly and feebly from under the
bedclothe, and with a purposeless uncertain grasp take hold of a
stick which be loosely tie to the side of the bed. after some
poking about with this instrument, in the course of which his face
assume a variety of distracted expression, Mr. Barkis poke it
against a box, an end of which have be visible to I all the time.
then his face become compose.
'old clothe,' say Mr. Barkis.
'oh!' say I.
'I wish it be Money, sir,' say Mr. Barkis.
'I wish it be, indeed,' say I.
'but it ain't,' say Mr. Barkis, open both his eye as wide as
he possibly could.
I express myself quite sure of that, and Mr. Barkis, turn his
eye more gently to his wife, say:
'shebe the usefull and good of woman, C. P. Barkis. all the
praise that anyone can give to C. P. Barkis, she deserve, and
more! my dear, youwill get a dinner today, for company; something
good to eat and drink, will you?'
I should have protest against this unnecessary demonstration in
my honour, but that I see Peggotty, on the opposite side of the
bed, extremely anxious I should not. so I hold my peace.
'I have get a trifle of money somewhere about I, my dear,' say
Mr. Barkis, 'but Ibe a little tired. if you and Mr. David will
leave I for a short nap, Iwill try and find it when I wake.'
we leave the room, in compliance with this request. when we get
outside the door, Peggotty inform I that Mr. Barkis, be now
'a little nearer' than he use to be, always resort to this same
device before produce a single coin from his store; and that he
endure unheard-of agony in crawl out of bed alone, and take
it from that unlucky box. in effect, we presently hear he
uttering suppress groan of the most dismal nature, as this
magpie proceeding rack he in every joint; but while Peggotty's
eye be full of compassion for he, she say his generous impulse
would do he good, and it be well not to check it. so he
groan on, until he have get into bed again, suffer, I have no
doubt, a martyrdom; and then call we in, pretend to have just
wake up from a refreshing sleep, and to produce a guinea from under
his pillow. his satisfaction in which happy imposition on we, and
in have preserve the impenetrable secret of the box, appear to
be a sufficient compensation to he for all his torture.
I prepare Peggotty for Steerforth's arrival and it be not long
before he come. I be persuade she know no difference between his
have be a personal benefactor of her, and a kind friend to I,
and that she would have receive he with the utmost gratitude and
devotion in any case. but his easy, spirited good humour; his
genial manner, his handsome look, his natural gift of adapt
himself to whomsoever he please, and make direct, when he care
to do it, to the main point of interest in anybody's heart; bind
she to he wholly in five minute. his manner to I, alone, would
have win she. but, through all these cause combine, I sincerely
believe she have a kind of adoration for he before he leave the
house that night.
he stay there with I to dinner - if I be to say willingly, I
should not half express how readily and gaily. he go into Mr.
Barkis's room like light and air, brighten and refresh it as
if he be healthy weather. there be no noise, no effort, no
consciousness, in anything he do; but in everything an
indescribable lightness, a seem impossibility of do anything
else, or do anything well, which be so graceful, so natural,
and agreeable, that it overcome I, even now, in the remembrance.
we make merry in the little parlour, where the Book of Martyrs,
unthumbe since my time, be lay out upon the desk as of old, and
where I now turn over its terrific picture, remember the old
sensation they have awaken, but not feel they. when Peggotty
speak of what she call my room, and of its be ready for I at

case.
'of course,' he say. 'youwill sleep here, while we stay, and I
shall sleep at the hotel.'
'but to bring you so far,' I return, 'and to separate, seem bad
companionship, Steerforth.'
'why, in the name of Heaven, where do you naturally belong?' he
say. 'what be "seem", compare to that?' it be settle at
once.
he maintain all his delightful quality to the last, until we
start forth, at eight o'clock, for Mr. Peggotty's boat. indeed,
they be more and more brightly exhibit as the hour go on;
for I think even then, and I have no doubt now, that the
consciousness of success in his determination to please, inspire
he with a new delicacy of perception, and make it, subtle as it

be a brilliant game, play for the excitement of the moment, for
the employment of high spirit, in the thoughtless love of
superiority, in a mere wasteful careless course of win what be
worthless to he, and next minute throw away - I say, if anyone

receive it my indignation would have find a vent! probably only
in an increase, have that be possible, of the romantic feeling of
fidelity and friendship with which I walk beside he, over the
dark wintry sand towards the old boat; the wind sigh around we
even more mournfully, than it have sigh and moan upon the night
when I first darken Mr. Peggotty's door.
'this be a wild kind of place, Steerforth, be it not?'
'dismal enough in the dark,' he say: 'and the sea roar as if it
be hungry for we. be that the boat, where I see a light yonder?'
'thatbe the boat,' say I.
'and itbe the same I see this morning,' he return. 'I come
straight to it, by instinct, I suppose.'
we say no more as we approach the light, but make softly for the
door. I lay my hand upon the latch; and whisper Steerforth to
keep close to I, go in.
a murmur of voice have be audible on the outside, and, at the
moment of our entrance, a clapping of hand: which latter noise, I
be surprised to see, proceed from the generally disconsolate
Mrs. Gummidge. but Mrs. Gummidge be not the only person there who
be unusually excited. Mr. Peggotty, his face light up with
uncommon satisfaction, and laugh with all his might, hold his
rough arm wide open, as if for little Em'ly to run into they; Ham,
with a mixed expression in his face of admiration, exultation, and
a lumber sort of bashfulness that sit upon he very well, hold
little Em'ly by the hand, as if he be present she to Mr.
Peggotty; little Em'ly herself, blushing and shy, but delight
with Mr. Peggotty's delight, as her joyous eye express, be
stop by our entrance (for she see we first) in the very act of
spring from Ham to nestle in Mr. Peggotty's embrace. in the
first glimpse we have of they all, and at the moment of our passing
from the dark cold night into the warm light room, this be the way
in which they be all employ: Mrs. Gummidge in the background,
clap her hand like a madwoman.
the little picture be so instantaneously dissolve by our go
in, that one might have doubt whether it have ever be. I be in
the midst of the astonished family, face to face with Mr. Peggotty,
and hold out my hand to he, when Ham shout:
'Mas'r Davy! itbe Mas'r Davy!'
in a moment we be all shake hand with one another, and ask
one another how we do, and tell one another how glad we be to
meet, and all talk at once. Mr. Peggotty be so proud and
overjoyed to see we, that he do not know what to say or do, but
keep over and over again shake hand with I, and then with
Steerforth, and then with I, and then ruffle his shaggy hair all
over his head, and laugh with such glee and triumph, that it be
a treat to see he.
'why, that you two gent'lman - gent'lmen grow - should come to
this here roof tonight, of all night in my life,' say Mr.
Peggotty, 'be such a thing as never happen afore, I do rightly
believe! Em'ly, my darling, come here! come here, my little
witch! therebe Mas'r Davy's friend, my dear! therebe the
gent'lman as youhave heerd on, Em'ly. he come to see you, along
with Mas'r Davy, on the bright night of your uncle's life as
ever be or will be, gorm the t'other one, and horroar for it!'
after deliver this speech all in a breath, and with
extraordinary animation and pleasure, Mr. Peggotty put one of his
large hand rapturously on each side of his niece's face, and
kiss it a dozen time, lay it with a gentle pride and love upon
his broad chest, and pat it as if his hand have be a lady's.
then he let she go; and as she run into the little chamber where I
use to sleep, look round upon we, quite hot and out of breath
with his uncommon satisfaction.
'if you two gent'lman - gent'lmen grow now, and such gent'lman -'
say Mr. Peggotty.
'so th' be, so th' be!' cry Ham. 'well say! so th' be.
Mas'r Davy bor' - gent'lmen grow - so th' be!'
'if you two gent'lman, gent'lmen grow,' say Mr. Peggotty, 'donot
ex-cuse I for be in a state of mind, when you understand
matter, Iwill ark your pardon. Em'ly, my dear! - she know Ibe a
go to tell,' here his delight break out again, 'and have make
off. would you be so good as look arter she, Mawther, for a
minute?'
Mrs. Gummidge nod and disappear.
'if this benot,' say Mr. Peggotty, sit down among we by the
fire, 'the bright night o' my life, Ibe a shellfish - bile too
- and more I cannot say. this here little Em'ly, sir,' in a low
voice to Steerforth, '- she as you see a blushing here just now -'
Steerforth only nod; but with such a pleased expression of
interest, and of participation in Mr. Peggotty's feeling, that the
latter answer he as if he have speak.
'to be sure,' say Mr. Peggotty. 'thatbe she, and so she be.
Thankee, sir.'
Ham nod to I several time, as if he would have say so too.
'this here little Em'ly of ours,' say Mr. Peggotty, 'have be, in
our house, what I suppose (Ibe a ignorant man, but thatbe my
belief) no one but a little bright-eyed creetur can be in a house.
she benot my child; I never have one; but I couldnot love she more.
you understand! I couldnot do it!'
'I quite understand,' say Steerforth.
'I know you do, sir,' return Mr. Peggotty, 'and thankee again.
Mas'r Davy, he can remember what she be; you may judge for your
own self what she be; but neither of you cannot fully know what she
have be, be, and will be, to my love art. I be rough, sir,'
say Mr. Peggotty, 'I be as rough as a Sea Porkypine; but no one,
unless, mayhap, it be a woman, can know, I think, what our little
Em'ly be to I. and betwixt ourselves,' sink his voice lower
yet, 'that woman's name benot Missis Gummidge neither, though she
have a world of merit.'
Mr. Peggotty ruffle his hair again, with both hand, as a further
preparation for what he be go to say, and go on, with a hand
upon each of his knee:
'there be a certain person as have know'd our Em'ly, from the time
when her father be drownde; as have see her constant; when a
babby, when a young gal, when a woman. not much of a person to
look at, he warn't,' say Mr. Peggotty, 'something o' my own build
- rough - a good deal o' the sou'-wester in he - wery salt - but,
on the whole, a honest sort of a chap, with his art in the right
place.'
I think I have never see ham grin to anything like the extent to
which he sit grin at we now.
'what do this here bless tarpaulin go and do,' say Mr.
Peggotty, with his face one high noon of enjoyment, 'but he lose
that there art of his to our little Em'ly. he foller she about,
he make hisself a sort o' servant to she, he lose in a great
measure his relish for his wittle, and in the long-run he make it
clear to I wot's amiss. now I could wish myself, you see, that
our little Em'ly be in a fair way of be marry. I could wish
to see she, at all ewent, under article to a honest man as have a
right to defend she. I donot know how long I may live, or how soon
I may die; but I know that if I be capsize, any night, in a gale
of wind in Yarmouth Roads here, and be to see the town-light
shin for the last time over the roller as I couldnot make no
head against, I could go down quieter for think "therebe a man
ashore there, iron-true to my little Em'ly, God bless she, and no
wrong can touch my Em'ly while so be as that man live."'
Mr. Peggotty, in simple earnestness, wave his right arm, as if he
be wave it at the town-light for the last time, and then,
exchange a nod with Ham, whose eye he catch, proceed as
before.
'well! I counsel he to speak to Em'ly. hebe big enough, but hebe
bashfuller than a little un, and he donot like. so I speak.
"what! he!" say Em'ly. "he that Ihave know'd so intimate so
many year, and like so much. oh, Uncle! I never can have he.
hebe such a good fellow!" I give she a kiss, and I say no more to
she than, "my dear, yoube right to speak out, yoube to choose for
yourself, yoube as free as a little bird." then I away to he,
and I say, "I wish it could have be so, but it cannot. but you
can both be as you be, and wot I say to you be, be as you be with
she, like a man." he say to I, a-shaking of my hand, "I will!" he
say. and he be - honourable and manful - for two year go on,
and we be just the same at home here as afore.'
Mr. Peggotty's face, which have vary in its expression with the
various stage of his narrative, now resume all its former
triumphant delight, as he lay a hand upon my knee and a hand upon
Steerforth's (previously wet they both, for the great
emphasis of the action), and divide the follow speech between
us:
'all of a sudden, one evening - as it might be tonight - come
little Em'ly from her work, and he with she! there benot so much
in that, youwill say. no, because he take care on she, like a
brother, arter dark, and indeed afore dark, and at all time. but
this tarpaulin chap, he take hold of her hand, and he cry out to
I, joyful, "look here! this be to be my little wife!" and she
say, half bold and half shy, and half a laughing and half a
crying, "yes, Uncle! if you please." - if I please!' cry Mr.
Peggotty, roll his head in an ecstasy at the idea; 'Lord, as if
I should do anythink else! - "if you please, I be steady now, and
I have think well of it, and Iwill be as good a little wife as
I can to he, for hebe a dear, good fellow!" then Missis Gummidge,
she clap her hand like a play, and you come in. Theer! the
murderbe out!' say Mr. Peggotty - 'you come in! it take place
this here present hour; and herebe the man thatwill marry she, the
minute shebe out of her time.'
Ham stagger, as well he might, under the blow Mr. Peggotty deal
he in his unbounded joy, as a mark of confidence and friendship;
but feel call upon to say something to we, he say, with much
faltering and great difficulty:
'she warn't no high than you be, Mas'r Davy - when you first
come - when I think what shewould grow up to be. I see she grow up
- gent'lmen - like a flower. Iwould lay down my life for her - Mas'r
Davy - oh! most content and cheerful! shebe more to I - gent'lmen
- than - shebe all to I that ever I can want, and more than ever
i - than ever I could say. I - I love her true. there benot a
gent'lman in all the land - nor yet sail upon all the sea - that
can love his lady more than I love she, though therebe many a
common man - would say well - what he mean.'
I think it affect to see such a sturdy fellow as Ham be now,
tremble in the strength of what he feel for the pretty little
creature who have win his heart. I think the simple confidence
repose in we by Mr. Peggotty and by himself, be, in itself,
affect. I be affect by the story altogether. how far my

fill with pleasure by all this; but, at first, with an

change to pain.
therefore, if it have depend upon I to touch the prevail chord

so Mr. Peggotty go into my old room to fetch little Em'ly. at

lightly and easily he carry on, until he bring we, by degree,
into a charmed circle, and we be all talk away without any
reserve.
Em'ly, indeed, say little all the evening; but she look, and
listen, and her face get animate, and she be charming.
Steerforth tell a story of a dismal shipwreck (which arise out of
his talk with Mr. Peggotty), as if he see it all before he - and
little Em'ly's eye be fasten on he all the time, as if she
see it too. he tell we a merry adventure of his own, as a relief
to that, with as much gaiety as if the narrative be as fresh to
he as it be to we - and little Em'ly laugh until the boat ring
with the musical sound, and we all laugh (Steerforth too), in
irresistible sympathy with what be so pleasant and light-hearted.
he get Mr. Peggotty to sing, or rather to roar, 'when the stormy
wind do blow, do blow, do blow'; and he sing a sailor's song
himself, so pathetically and beautifully, that I could have almost
fancy that the real wind creep sorrowfully round the house,
and murmur low through our unbroken silence, be there to
listen.
as to Mrs. Gummidge, he rouse that victim of despondency with a
success never attain by anyone else (so Mr. Peggotty inform
I), since the decease of the old one. he leave she so little
leisure for be miserable, that she say next day she think she
must have be bewitch.
but he set up no monopoly of the general attention, or the
conversation. when little Em'ly grow more courageous, and talk

both laugh and redden, cast these look back on the pleasant

whether it be in her own little tormenting way, or in a maidenly
reserve before we, that she keep quite close to the wall, and away
from he; but I observe that she do so, all the evening.
as I remember, it be almost midnight when we take our leave. we
have have some biscuit and dry fish for supper, and Steerforth have
produce from his pocket a full flask of holland, which we man (I
may say we man, now, without a blush) have empty. we part

as far as they could upon our road, I see the sweet blue eye of
little Em'ly peep after we, from behind Ham, and hear her soft
voice call to we to be careful how we go.
'a most engaging little beauty!' say Steerforth, take my arm.
'well! itbe a quaint place, and they be quaint company, and itbe
quite a new sensation to mix with they.'
'how fortunate we be, too,' I return, 'to have arrive to

people so happy. how delightful to see it, and to be make the
sharer in their honest joy, as we have be!'
'thatbe rather a chuckle-head fellow for the girl; benot he?'
say Steerforth.
he have be so hearty with he, and with they all, that I feel a

'ah, Steerforth! itbe well for you to joke about the poor! you

jest from I, but I know well. when I see how perfectly you
understand they, how exquisitely you can enter into happiness like

know that there be not a joy or sorrow, not an emotion, of such
people, that can be indifferent to you. and I admire and love you

he stop, and, look in my face, say, 'Daisy, I believe you
be in earnest, and be good. I wish we all be!' next moment he
be gaily sing Mr. Peggotty's song, as we walk at a round pace
back to Yarmouth.
CHAPTER 22
some OLD SCENES, and some new PEOPLE
Steerforth and I stay for more than a fortnight in that part of
the country. we be very much together, I need not say; but
occasionally we be asunder for some hour at a time. he be a
good sailor, and I be but an indifferent one; and when he go out
boat with Mr. Peggotty, which be a favourite amusement of his,
I generally remain ashore. my occupation of Peggotty's
spare-room put a constraint upon I, from which he be free: for,
know how assiduously she attend on Mr. Barkis all day, I do
not like to remain out late at night; whereas Steerforth, lie at
the Inn, have nothing to consult but his own humour. thus it come
about, that I hear of his make little treat for the fisherman
at Mr. Peggotty's house of call, 'the willing mind', after I be in
bed, and of his be afloat, wrap in fisherman's clothe, whole
moonlight night, and come back when the morning tide be at
flood. by this time, however, I know that his restless nature and
bold spirit delight to find a vent in rough toil and hard
weather, as in any other mean of excitement that present itself
freshly to he; so none of his proceeding surprise I.
another cause of our be sometimes apart, be, that I have
naturally an interest in go over to Blunderstone, and revisit
the old familiar scene of my childhood; while Steerforth, after
be there once, have naturally no great interest in go there
again. hence, on three or four day that I can at once recall, we
go our several way after an early breakfast, and meet again at a
late dinner. I have no idea how he employ his time in the
interval, beyond a general knowledge that he be very popular in
the place, and have twenty mean of actively divert himself where
another man might not have find one.
for my own part, my occupation in my solitary pilgrimage be to
recall every yard of the old road as I go along it, and to haunt
the old spot, of which I never tire. I haunt they, as my
memory have often do, and linger among they as my young
thought have linger when I be far away. the grave beneath the
tree, where both my parent lay - on which I have look out, when
it be my father's only, with such curious feeling of compassion,
and by which I have stand, so desolate, when it be open to
receive my pretty mother and her baby - the grave which Peggotty's
own faithful care have ever since keep neat, and make a garden of,
I walk near, by the hour. it lie a little off the churchyard
path, in a quiet corner, not so far removed but I could read the
name upon the stone as I walk to and fro, startle by the sound
of the church-bell when it strike the hour, for it be like a
depart voice to I. my reflection at these time be always
associate with the figure I be to make in life, and the
distinguish thing I be to do. my echo footstep go to no
other tune, but be as constant to that as if I have come home to
build my castle in the air at a live mother's side.
there be great change in my old home. the ragged nest, so long
desert by the rook, be go; and the tree be lop and
top out of their remembered shape. the garden have run wild,
and half the window of the house be shut up. it be occupy,
but only by a poor lunatic gentleman, and the people who take care
of he. he be always sit at my little window, look out
into the churchyard; and I wonder whether his ramble thought
ever go upon any of the fancy that use to occupy mine, on the
rosy morning when I peep out of that same little window in my
night-clothe, and see the sheep quietly feed in the light of
the rise sun.
our old neighbour, Mr. and Mrs. Grayper, be go to South
America, and the rain have make its way through the roof of their
empty house, and stain the outer wall. Mr. Chillip be marry
again to a tall, raw-boned, high-nosed wife; and they have a weazen
little baby, with a heavy head that it couldnot hold up, and two
weak star eye, with which it seem to be always wonder why
it have ever be bear.
it be with a singular jumble of sadness and pleasure that I use
to linger about my native place, until the redden winter sun
admonish I that it be time to start on my return walk. but,
when the place be leave behind, and especially when Steerforth and
I be happily seat over our dinner by a blaze fire, it be
delicious to think of have be there. so it be, though in a
soften degree, when I go to my neat room at night; and, turn

a little table), remember with a grateful heart how bl I be
in have such a friend as Steerforth, such a friend as Peggotty,

generous aunt.
my near way to Yarmouth, in come back from these long walk,
be by a ferry. it land I on the flat between the town and the
sea, which I could make straight across, and so save myself a
considerable circuit by the high road. Mr. Peggotty's house be
on that waste-place, and not a hundred yard out of my track, I
always look in as I go by. Steerforth be pretty sure to be
there expect I, and we go on together through the frosty air
and gather fog towards the twinkling light of the town.
one dark evening, when I be later than usual - for I have, that

sit thoughtfully before the fire. he be so intent upon his

this, indeed, he might easily have be if he have be less

he give such a start when I put my hand upon his shoulder, that he

twenty year!'
'my dear Steerforth, what be the matter?'
'I wish with all my soul I have be well guide!' he exclaim.

there be a passionate dejection in his manner that quite amazed

his face be always full of expression, but I never see it express

Ham be a boat-builder in these day, have improve a natural

nose again, screw up her face, and twinkle her eye like an
imp of supernatural intelligence. 'never you mind! youwould like to
know whether I stop her hair from fall off, or dye it, or touch
up her complexion, or improve her eyebrow, wouldnot you? and so
you shall, my darling - when I tell you! do you know what my great
grandfather's name be?'
'no,' say Steerforth.
'it be Walker, my sweet pet,' reply Miss Mowcher, 'and he come
of a long line of walker, that I inherit all the Hookey estate
from.'
I never behold anything approach to Miss Mowcher's wink except
Miss Mowcher's self-possession. she have a wonderful way too, when
listen to what be say to she, or when wait for an answer to
what she have say herself, of pause with her head cunningly on
one side, and one eye turn up like a magpie's. altogether I be
lose in amazement, and sit star at she, quite oblivious, I be
afraid, of the law of politeness.
she have by this time draw the chair to her side, and be busily
engage in produce from the bag (plunge in her short arm to the
shoulder, at every dive) a number of small bottle, sponge, comb,
brush, bit of flannel, little pair of curling-iron, and other
instrument, which she tumble in a heap upon the chair. from this
employment she suddenly desist, and say to Steerforth, much to
my confusion:
'whobe your friend?'
'Mr. Copperfield,' say Steerforth; 'he want to know you.'
'well, then, he shall! I think he look as if he do!' return
Miss Mowcher, waddle up to I, bag in hand, and laugh on I as
she come. 'face like a peach!' stand on tiptoe to pinch my
cheek as I sit. 'quite tempting! Ibe very fond of peach. happy
to make your acquaintance, Mr. Copperfield, Ibe sure.'
I say that I congratulate myself on have the honour to make
her, and that the happiness be mutual.
'oh, my goodness, how polite we be!' exclaim Miss Mowcher,
make a preposterous attempt to cover her large face with her
morsel of a hand. 'what a world of gammon and spinnage it be,
though, benot it!'
this be address confidentially to both of we, as the morsel of
a hand come away from the face, and bury itself, arm and all, in
the bag again.
'what do you mean, Miss Mowcher?' say Steerforth.
'ha! ha! ha! what a refreshing set of humbug we be, to be sure,
benot we, my sweet child?' reply that morsel of a woman, feel
in the bag with her head on one side and her eye in the air. 'look
here!' take something out. 'scrap of the Russian Prince's
nail. Prince Alphabet turn topsy-turvy, I call he, for his
name's get all the letter in it, higgledy-piggledy.'
'the Russian Prince be a client of your, be he?' say Steerforth.
'I believe you, my pet,' reply Miss Mowcher. 'I keep his nail
in order for he. twice a week! finger and toe.'
'he pay well, I hope?' say Steerforth.
'pay, as he speak, my dear child - through the nose,' reply
Miss Mowcher. 'none of your close shaver the Prince benot. youwould
say so, if you see his moustachio. red by nature, black by art.'
'by your art, of course,' say Steerforth.
Miss Mowcher wink assent. 'force to send for I. couldnot help
it. the climate affect his dye; it do very well in Russia, but
it be no go here. you never see such a rusty Prince in all your
bear day as he be. like old iron!'
'be that why you call he a humbug, just now?' inquire
Steerforth.
'oh, yoube a broth of a boy, benot you?' return Miss Mowcher,
shake her head violently. 'I say, what a set of humbug we be
in general, and I show you the scrap of the Prince's nail to
prove it. the Prince's nail do more for I in private family of
the genteel sort, than all my talent put together. I always carry
them about. theybe the good introduction. if Miss Mowcher cut
the Prince's nail, she must be all right. I give them away to the
young lady. they put them in album, I believe. ha! ha! ha!
upon my life, "the whole social system" (as the man call it when
they make speech in Parliament) be a system of Prince's nail!'
say this least of woman, try to fold her short arm, and
nod her large head.
Steerforth laugh heartily, and I laugh too. Miss Mowcher
continue all the time to shake her head (which be very much on
one side), and to look into the air with one eye, and to wink with
the other.
'well, well!' she say, smite her small knee, and rise, 'this
be not business. come, Steerforth, letus explore the polar
region, and have it over.'
she then select two or three of the little instrument, and a
little bottle, and ask (to my surprise) if the table would bear.
on Steerforth's replying in the affirmative, she push a chair
against it, and beg the assistance of my hand, mount up,
pretty nimbly, to the top, as if it be a stage.
'if either of you see my ankle,' she say, when she be safely
elevated, 'say so, and Iwill go home and destroy myself!'
'I do not,' say Steerforth.
'I do not,' say I.
'well then,' cry Miss Mowcher,' Iwill consent to live. now,
ducky, ducky, ducky, come to Mrs. Bond and be kill.'
this be an invocation to Steerforth to place himself under her
hand; who, accordingly, sit himself down, with his back to the
table, and his laugh face towards I, and submit his head to
her inspection, evidently for no other purpose than our
entertainment. to see Miss Mowcher stand over he, look at
his rich profusion of brown hair through a large round magnifying
glass, which she take out of her pocket, be a most amazing
spectacle.
'yoube a pretty fellow!' say Miss Mowcher, after a brief
inspection. 'youwould be as bald as a friar on the top of your head
in twelve month, but for I. just half a minute, my young friend,
and wewill give you a polishing that shall keep your curl on for
the next ten year!'
with this, she tilt some of the content of the little bottle on
to one of the little bit of flannel, and, again impart some of
the virtue of that preparation to one of the little brush, begin
rub and scrap away with both on the crown of Steerforth's
head in the busy manner I ever witness, talk all the time.
'therebe Charley Pyegrave, the duke's son,' she say. 'you know
Charley?' peep round into his face.
'a little,' say Steerforth.
'what a man he be! therebe a whisker! as to Charley's leg, if
they be only a pair (which they benot), theywould defy competition.
would you believe he try to do without I - in the Life-Guards,
too?'
'mad!' say Steerforth.
'it look like it. however, mad or sane, he try,' return Miss
Mowcher. 'what do he do, but, lo and behold you, he go into a
perfumer's shop, and want to buy a bottle of the Madagascar
Liquid.'
'Charley do?' say Steerforth.
'Charley do. but they havenot get any of the Madagascar Liquid.'
'what be it? something to drink?' ask Steerforth.
'to drink?' return Miss Mowcher, stop to slap his cheek. 'to
doctor his own moustachio with, you know. there be a woman in
the shop - elderly female - quite a Griffin - who have never even
hear of it by name. "Begging pardon, sir," say the Griffin to
Charley, "itbe not - not - not ROUGE, be it?" "Rouge," say
Charley to the Griffin. "what the unmentionable to ear polite, do
you think I want with rouge?" "no offence, sir," say the Griffin;
"we have it ask for by so many name, I think it might be." now
that, my child,' continue Miss Mowcher, rub all the time as
busily as ever, 'be another instance of the refresh humbug I be
speak of. I do something in that way myself - perhaps a good
deal - perhaps a little - sharp's the word, my dear boy - never
mind!'
'in what way do you mean? in the rouge way?' say Steerforth.
'put this and that together, my tender pupil,' return the wary
Mowcher, touch her nose, 'work it by the rule of Secrets in all
trade, and the product will give you the desire result. I say I
do a little in that way myself. one Dowager, she call it
lip-salve. another, SHE call it glove. another, SHE call it
tucker-edging. another, SHE call it a fan. I call it whatever
they call it. I supply it for them, but we keep up the trick so, to
one another, and make believe with such a face, that theywould as soon
think of lay it on, before a whole drawing-room, as before I.
and when I wait upon them, theywill say to I sometimes - with it on
- thick, and no mistake - "how be I look, Mowcher? be I pale?"
ha! ha! ha! ha! benot that refreshing, my young friend!'
I never do in my day behold anything like Mowcher as she stand
upon the dining table, intensely enjoy this refreshment, rub
busily at Steerforth's head, and wink at I over it.
'ah!' she say. 'such thing be not much in demand hereabout.
that set I off again! I havenot see a pretty woman since Ihave
be here, jemmy.'
'no?' say Steerforth.
'not the ghost of one,' reply Miss Mowcher.
'we could show she the substance of one, I think?' say Steerforth,
address his eye to mine. 'eh, Daisy?'
'yes, indeed,' say I.
'aha?' cry the little creature, glance sharply at my face, and
then peep round at Steerforth's. 'umph?'
the first exclamation sound like a question put to both of we,
and the second like a question put to Steerforth only. she seem
to have find no answer to either, but continue to rub, with she
head on one side and her eye turn up, as if she be look for
an answer in the air and be confident of its appear presently.
'a sister of your, Mr. Copperfield?' she cry, after a pause, and
still keep the same look-out. 'aye, aye?'
'no,' say Steerforth, before I could reply. 'nothing of the sort.
on the contrary, Mr. Copperfield use - or I be much mistaken - to
have a great admiration for she.'
'why, havenot he now?' return Miss Mowcher. 'be he fickle? oh,
for shame! do he sip every flower, and change every hour, until
polly his passion requite? - be her name polly?'
the Elfin suddenness with which she pounce upon I with this
question, and a search look, quite disconcert I for a moment.
'no, Miss Mowcher,' I reply. 'her name be Emily.'
'aha?' she cry exactly as before. 'umph? what a rattle I be!
Mr. Copperfield, benot I volatile?'
her tone and look imply something that be not agreeable to I in
connexion with the subject. so I say, in a graver manner than any
of we have yet assume:
'she be as virtuous as she be pretty. she be engage to be marry
to a most worthy and deserving man in her own station of life. I
esteem she for her good sense, as much as I admire she for her good
look.'
'well say!' cry Steerforth. 'hear, hear, hear! now Iwill quench
the curiosity of this little Fatima, my dear Daisy, by leave she
nothing to guess at. she be at present apprentice, Miss Mowcher,
or article, or whatever it may be, to Omer and Joram,
haberdasher, Milliners, and so forth, in this town. do you
observe? Omer and Joram. the promise of which my friend have
speak, be make and enter into with her cousin; christian name,
Ham; surname, Peggotty; occupation, boat-builder; also of this
town. she live with a relative; christian name, unknown; surname,
Peggotty; occupation, seafaring; also of this town. she be the
prettiest and most engaging little fairy in the world. I admire
she - as my friend do - exceedingly. if it be not that I might
appear to disparage her Intended, which I know my friend would not
like, I would add, that to I she seem to be throw herself
away; that I be sure she might do well; and that I swear she be
bear to be a lady.'
Miss Mowcher listen to these word, which be very slowly and
distinctly speak, with her head on one side, and her eye in the
air as if she be still look for that answer. when he cease
she become brisk again in an instant, and rattle away with
surprising volubility.
'oh! and thatbe all about it, be it?' she exclaim, trim his
whisker with a little restless pair of scissor, that go
glance round his head in all direction. 'very well: very well!
quite a long story. ought to end "and they live happy ever
afterwards"; oughtnot it? ah! whatbe that game at forfeit? I
love my love with an e, because shebe entice; I hate she with an
E, because shebe engaged. I take she to the sign of the exquisite,
and treat she with an elopement, her name's Emily, and she live
in the east? ha! ha! ha! Mr. Copperfield, benot I volatile?'
merely look at I with extravagant slyness, and not wait for
any reply, she continue, without draw breath:
'there! if ever any scapegrace be trim and touch up to
perfection, you be, Steerforth. if I understand any noddle in the
world, I understand your. do you hear I when I tell you that, my
darling? I understand your,' peep down into his face. 'now
you may mizzle, jemmy (as we say at Court), and if Mr. Copperfield
will take the chair Iwill operate on he.'
'what do you say, Daisy?' inquire Steerforth, laughing, and
resign his seat. 'will you be improve?'
'thank you, Miss Mowcher, not this evening.'
'donot say no,' return the little woman, look at I with the
aspect of a connoisseur; 'a little bit more eyebrow?'
'thank you,' I return, 'some other time.'
'have it carry half a quarter of an inch towards the temple,'
say Miss Mowcher. 'we can do it in a fortnight.'
'no, I thank you. not at present.'
'go in for a tip,' she urge. 'no? letus get the scaffolding up,
then, for a pair of whisker. come!'
I could not help blush as I decline, for I feel we be on my
weak point, now. but Miss Mowcher, find that I be not at
present dispose for any decoration within the range of her art,
and that I be, for the time being, proof against the blandishment
of the small bottle which she hold up before one eye to enforce she
persuasion, say we would make a beginning on an early day, and
request the aid of my hand to descend from her elevated station.
thus assist, she skip down with much agility, and begin to tie
her double chin into her bonnet.
'the fee,' say Steerforth, 'be -'
'five bob,' reply Miss Mowcher, 'and dirt cheap, my chicken.
benot I volatile, Mr. Copperfield?'
I reply politely: 'not at all.' but I think she be rather so,
when she toss up his two half-crown like a goblin pieman, catch
they, drop they in her pocket, and give it a loud slap.
'thatbe the till!' observe Miss Mowcher, stand at the chair
again, and replace in the bag a miscellaneous collection of
little object she have empty out of it. 'have I get all my
trap? it seem so. it willnot do to be like long Ned Beadwood,
when they take he to church "to marry he to somebody", as he
say, and leave the bride behind. ha! ha! ha! a wicked rascal,
Ned, but droll! now, I know Ibe go to break your heart, but I
be force to leave you. you must call up all your fortitude, and
try to bear it. good-bye, Mr. Copperfield! take care of yourself,
jockey of Norfolk! how I have be rattle on! itbe all the
fault of you two wretch. I forgive you! "Bob swore!" - as the
Englishman say for "good night", when he first learn French, and
think it so like English. "Bob swore," my duck!'
with the bag sling over her arm, and rattle as she waddle away,
she waddle to the door, where she stop to inquire if she should
leave we a lock of her hair. 'benot I volatile?' she add, as a
commentary on this offer, and, with her finger on her nose,
depart.
Steerforth laugh to that degree, that it be impossible for I to
help laugh too; though I be not sure I should have do so, but
for this inducement. when we have have our laugh quite out, which
be after some time, he tell I that Miss Mowcher have quite an
extensive connexion, and make herself useful to a variety of people
in a variety of way. some people trifle with she as a mere
oddity, he say; but she be as shrewdly and sharply observant as
anyone he know, and as long-headed as she be short-armed. he tell
I that what she have say of be here, and there, and everywhere,
be true enough; for she make little dart into the province, and
seem to pick up customer everywhere, and to know everybody. I
ask he what her disposition be: whether it be at all
mischievous, and if her sympathy be generally on the right side
of thing: but, not succeed in attract his attention to these
question after two or three attempt, I forbore or forget to
repeat they. he tell I instead, with much rapidity, a good deal
about her skill, and her profit; and about she be a scientific
cupper, if I should ever have occasion for her service in that
capacity.
she be the principal theme of our conversation during the evening:
and when we part for the night Steerforth call after I over
the banister, 'Bob swore!' as I go downstairs.
I be surprised, when I come to Mr. Barkis's house, to find ham
walk up and down in front of it, and still more surprised to
learn from he that little Em'ly be inside. I naturally inquire
why he be not there too, instead of pace the street by himself?
'why, you see, Mas'r Davy,' he rejoin, in a hesitate manner,
'Em'ly, shebe talk to some 'un in here.'
'I should have think,' say I, smile, 'that that be a reason
for your be in here too, Ham.'
'well, Mas'r Davy, in a general way, so 't would be,' he return;
'but look'ee here, Mas'r Davy,' lower his voice, and speak
very gravely. 'itbe a young woman, sir - a young woman, that Em'ly
know once, and doen't ought to know no more.'
when I hear these word, a light begin to fall upon the figure I
have see follow they, some hour ago.
'itbe a poor wurem, Mas'r Davy,' say Ham, 'as be trod under foot
by all the town. up street and down street. the mowld o' the
churchyard donot hold any that the folk shrink away from, more.'
'do I see she tonight, Ham, on the sand, after we meet you?'
'keep we in sight?' say Ham. 'itbe like you do, Mas'r Davy.
not that I know'd then, she be theer, sir, but along of she
creep soon arterward under Em'ly's little winder, when she see
the light come, and whisper "Em'ly, Em'ly, for Christ's sake,
have a woman's heart towards I. I be once like you!" those be
solemn word, Mas'r Davy, fur to hear!'
'they be indeed, Ham. what do Em'ly do?'
'say Em'ly, "Martha, be it you? oh, Martha, can it be you?" - for
they have sit at work together, many a day, at Mr. Omer's.'
'I recollect she now!' cry I, recall one of the two girl I
have see when I first go there. 'I recollect she quite well!'
'Martha Endell,' say Ham. 'two or three year old than Em'ly,
but be at the school with she.'
'I never hear her name,' say I. 'I donot mean to interrupt
you.'
'for the matter o' that, Mas'r Davy,' reply Ham, 'all's tell
a'most in they word, "Em'ly, Em'ly, for Christ's sake, have a
woman's heart towards I. I be once like you!" she want to
speak to Em'ly. Em'ly couldnot speak to her theer, for her love
uncle be come home, and he wouldnot - no, Mas'r Davy,' say Ham,
with great earnestness, 'he couldnot, kind-natur'd, tender-hearted
as he be, see they two together, side by side, for all the
treasure thatbe wreck in the sea.'
I feel how true this be. I know it, on the instant, quite as well
as Ham.
'so Em'ly write in pencil on a bit of paper,' he pursue, 'and
give it to her out o' winder to bring here. "show that," she
say, "to my aunt, Mrs. Barkis, and shewill set you down by her
fire, for the love of I, till uncle be go out, and I can come."
by and by she tell I what I tell you, Mas'r Davy, and ask I to
bring she. what can I do? she doen't ought to know any such, but
I cannot deny she, when the tear be on her face.'
he put his hand into the breast of his shaggy jacket, and take out
with great care a pretty little purse.
'and if I could deny she when the tear be on her face, Mas'r
Davy,' say Ham, tenderly adjust it on the rough palm of his
hand, 'how could I deny she when she give I this to carry for her
- know what she bring it for? such a toy as it be!' say Ham,
thoughtfully look on it. 'with such a little money in it, Em'ly
my dear.'
I shake he warmly by the hand when he have put it away again - for
that be more satisfactory to I than say anything - and we
walk up and down, for a minute or two, in silence. the door
open then, and Peggotty appear, beckon to Ham to come in.
I would have keep away, but she come after I, entreat I to
come in too. even then, I would have avoid the room where they
all be, but for its be the neat-tile kitchen I have mention
more than once. the door open immediately into it, I find
myself among they before I consider whither I be go.
the girl - the same I have see upon the sand - be near the fire.
she be sit on the ground, with her head and one arm lie on
a chair. I fancy, from the disposition of her figure, that Em'ly
have but newly rise from the chair, and that the forlorn head might
perhaps have be lie on her lap. I see but little of the girl's
face, over which her hair fall loose and scatter, as if she have
be disorder it with her own hand; but I see that she be
young, and of a fair complexion. Peggotty have be cry. so have
little Em'ly. not a word be speak when we first go in; and the
dutch clock by the dresser seem, in the silence, to tick twice as
loud as usual. Em'ly speak first.
'Martha want,' she say to Ham, 'to go to London.'
'why to London?' return Ham.
he stand between they, look on the prostrate girl with a mixture
of compassion for she, and of jealousy of she hold any
companionship with she whom he love so well, which I have always
remember distinctly. they both speak as if she be ill; in a
soft, suppressed tone that be plainly hear, although it hardly
rise above a whisper.
'well there than here,' say a third voice aloud - Martha's,
though she do not move. 'no one know I there. everybody know
I here.'
'what will she do there?' inquire Ham.
she lift up her head, and look darkly round at he for a
moment; then lay it down again, and curve her right arm about she
neck, as a woman in a fever, or in an agony of pain from a shot,
might twist herself.
'she will try to do well,' say little Em'ly. 'you donot know what
she have say to we. do he - do they - aunt?'
Peggotty shake her head compassionately.
'Iwill try,' say Martha, 'if youwill help I away. I never can do
bad than I have do here. I may do well. oh!' with a
dreadful shiver, 'take I out of these street, where the whole
town know I from a child!'
as Em'ly hold out her hand to Ham, I see he put in it a little
canvas bag. she take it, as if she think it be her purse, and
make a step or two forward; but find her mistake, come back to
where he have retire near I, and show it to he.
'itbe all yourn, Em'ly,' I could hear he say. 'I havenot nowt in
all the wureld that benot yourn, my dear. it benot of no delight
to I, except for you!'
the tear rise freshly in her eye, but she turn away and go to
Martha. what she give she, I donot know. I see she stoop over
she, and put money in her bosom. she whisper something, as
she ask be that enough? 'More than enough,' the other say, and
take her hand and kiss it.
then Martha arise, and gather her shawl about she, cover she
face with it, and weep aloud, go slowly to the door. she
stop a moment before go out, as if she would have utter
something or turn back; but no word pass her lip. make the
same low, dreary, wretched moaning in her shawl, she go away.
as the door close, little Em'ly look at we three in a hurried
manner and then hide her face in her hand, and fall to sob.
'Doen't, Em'ly!' say Ham, tap she gently on the shoulder.
'Doen't, my dear! you doen't ought to cry so, pretty!'
'oh, Ham!' she exclaim, still weep pitifully, 'I be not so
good a girl as I ought to be! I know I have not the thankful
heart, sometimes, I ought to have!'
'yes, yes, you have, Ibe sure,' say Ham.
'no! no! no!' cry little Em'ly, sobbing, and shake her head.
'I be not as good a girl as I ought to be. not near! not near!'
and still she cry, as if her heart would break.
'I try your love too much. I know I do!' she sob. 'Ibe often
cross to you, and changeable with you, when I ought to be far
different. you be never so to I. why be I ever so to you, when
I should think of nothing but how to be grateful, and to make you
happy!'
'you always make I so,' say Ham, 'my dear! I be happy in the
sight of you. I be happy, all day long, in the thought of you.'
'ah! thatbe not enough!' she cry. 'that be because you be good;
not because I be! oh, my dear, it might have be a well fortune
for you, if you have be fond of someone else - of someone steady
and much worthy than I, who be all bind up in you, and never
vain and changeable like I!'
'poor little tender-heart,' say Ham, in a low voice. 'Martha have
overset she, altogether.'
'please, aunt,' sob Em'ly, 'come here, and let I lay my head
upon you. oh, I be very miserable tonight, aunt! oh, I be not as
good a girl as I ought to be. I be not, I know!'
Peggotty have hasten to the chair before the fire. Em'ly, with
her arm around her neck, kneel by she, look up most earnestly
into her face.
'oh, pray, aunt, try to help I! Ham, dear, try to help I! Mr.
David, for the sake of old time, do, please, try to help I! I
want to be a well girl than I be. I want to feel a hundred time
more thankful than I do. I want to feel more, what a blessed thing
it be to be the wife of a good man, and to lead a peaceful life.
oh I, oh I! oh my heart, my heart!'
she drop her face on my old nurse's breast, and, cease this
supplication, which in its agony and grief be half a woman's, half
a child's, as all her manner be (be, in that, more natural, and
well suit to her beauty, as I think, than any other manner
could have be), weep silently, while my old nurse hush she like
an infant.
she get calm by degree, and then we soothe she; now talk
encouragingly, and now jest a little with she, until she begin
to raise her head and speak to we. so we get on, until she be
able to smile, and then to laugh, and then to sit up, half ashamed;
while Peggotty recall her stray ringlet, dry her eye, and
make she neat again, lest her uncle should wonder, when she get
home, why his darling have be cry.
I see she do, that night, what I have never see she do before. I
see she innocently kiss her choose husband on the cheek, and creep
close to his bluff form as if it be her good support. when they
go away together, in the wan moonlight, and I look after
they, compare their departure in my mind with Martha's, I see
that she hold his arm with both her hand, and still keep close to
he.
CHAPTER 23
I corroborate Mr. DICK, and CHOOSE a profession
when I awake in the morning I think very much of little Em'ly,
and her emotion last night, after Martha have leave. I feel as if I
have come into the knowledge of those domestic weakness and
tenderness in a sacred confidence, and that to disclose they,
even to Steerforth, would be wrong. I have no gentler feeling
towards anyone than towards the pretty creature who have be my
playmate, and whom I have always be persuade, and shall always
be persuade, to my die day, I then devotedly love. the
repetition to any ear - even to Steerforth's - of what she have
be unable to repress when her heart lie open to I by an
accident, I feel would be a rough deed, unworthy of myself,
unworthy of the light of our pure childhood, which I always see
encircle her head. I make a resolution, therefore, to keep it in
my own breast; and there it give her image a new grace.
while we be at breakfast, a letter be deliver to I from my
aunt. as it contain matter on which I think Steerforth could
advise I as well as anyone, and on which I know I should be
delighted to consult he, I resolve to make it a subject of
discussion on our journey home. for the present we have enough to
do, in take leave of all our friend. Mr. Barkis be far from
be the last among they, in his regret at our departure; and I
believe would even have open the box again, and sacrifice
another guinea, if it would have keep we eight-and-forty hour in
Yarmouth. Peggotty and all her family be full of grief at our
go. the whole house of Omer and Joram turn out to bid we
good-bye; and there be so many seafare volunteer in attendance
on Steerforth, when our portmanteaux go to the coach, that if we
have have the baggage of a regiment with we, we should hardly have
want porter to carry it. in a word, we depart to the regret
and admiration of all concern, and leave a great many people very
sorry behind US.
do you stay long here, Littimer?' say I, as he stand wait to
see the coach start.
'no, sir,' he reply; 'probably not very long, sir.'
'he can hardly say, just now,' observed Steerforth, carelessly.
'he know what he have to do, and hewill do it.'
'that I be sure he will,' say I.
Littimer touch his hat in acknowledgement of my good opinion, and
I feel about eight year old. he touch it once more, wish we
a good journey; and we leave he stand on the pavement, as
respectable a mystery as any pyramid in Egypt.
for some little time we hold no conversation, Steerforth be
unusually silent, and I be sufficiently engage in wonder,
within myself, when I should see the old place again, and what new
change might happen to I or they in the meanwhile. at length
Steerforth, become gay and talkative in a moment, as he could
become anything he like at any moment, pull I by the arm:
'find a voice, David. what about that letter you be speak of
at breakfast?'
'oh!' say I, take it out of my pocket. 'itbe from my aunt.'
'and what do she say, require consideration?'
'why, she remind I, Steerforth,' say I, 'that I come out on
this expedition to look about I, and to think a little.'
'which, of course, you have do?'
'indeed I cannot say I have, particularly. to tell you the truth,
I be afraid I have forget it.'
'well! look about you now, and make up for your negligence,' say
Steerforth. 'look to the right, and youwill see a flat country,
with a good deal of marsh in it; look to the left, and youwill see
the same. look to the front, and youwill find no difference; look
to the rear, and there it be still.'
I laugh, and reply that I see no suitable profession in the
whole prospect; which be perhaps to be attribute to its flatness.
'what say our aunt on the subject?' inquire Steerforth, glance
at the letter in my hand. 'do she suggest anything?'
'why, yes,' say I. 'she ask I, here, if I think I should like
to be a proctor? what do you think of it?'
'well, I donot know,' reply Steerforth, coolly. 'you may as well
do that as anything else, I suppose?'
I could not help laugh again, at his balance all calling and
profession so equally; and I tell he so.
'what be a proctor, Steerforth?' say I.
'why, he be a sort of monkish attorney,' reply Steerforth. 'he
be, to some fade court hold in doctor' Commons, - a lazy old
nook near St. Paul's Churchyard - what solicitor be to the court
of law and equity. he be a functionary whose existence, in the
natural course of thing, would have terminate about two hundred
year ago. I can tell you well what he be, by tell you what
doctor' Commons be. itbe a little out-of-the-way place, where
they administer what be call ecclesiastical law, and play all
kind of trick with obsolete old monster of act of Parliament,
which three-fourth of the world know nothing about, and the other
fourth suppose to have be dig up, in a fossil state, in the day
of the Edwards. itbe a place that have an ancient monopoly in suit
about people's will and people's marriage, and dispute among
ship and boat.'
'Nonsense, Steerforth!' I exclaim. 'you donot mean to say that
there be any affinity between nautical matter and ecclesiastical
matter?'
'I donot, indeed, my dear boy,' he return; 'but I mean to say
that they be manage and decide by the same set of people, down
in that same doctor' Commons. you shall go there one day, and
find they blunder through half the nautical term in Young's
Dictionary, apropos of the "Nancy" have run down the "Sarah
Jane", or Mr. Peggotty and the Yarmouth boatman have put off in
a gale of wind with an anchor and cable to the "Nelson" Indiaman in
distress; and you shall go there another day, and find they deep in
the evidence, pro and con, respect a clergyman who have
misbehave himself; and you shall find the judge in the nautical
case, the advocate in the clergyman's case, or contrariwise. they
be like actor: now a man's a judge, and now he be not a judge;
now hebe one thing, now hebe another; now hebe something else,
change and change about; but itbe always a very pleasant,
profitable little affair of private theatrical, present to an
uncommonly select audience.'
'but advocate and proctor be not one and the same?' say I, a
little puzzle. 'be they?'
'no,' return Steerforth, 'the advocate be civilian - man who
have take a doctor's degree at college - which be the first reason
of my know anything about it. the proctor employ the
advocate. both get very comfortable fee, and altogether they
make a mighty snug little party. on the whole, I would recommend
you to take to doctor' Commons kindly, David. they plume them-
self on their gentility there, I can tell you, if thatbe any
satisfaction.'
I make allowance for Steerforth's light way of treat the
subject, and, consider it with reference to the staid air of

near St. Paul's Churchyard', do not feel indisposed towards my

'thatbe a laudable proceeding on the part of our aunt, at all

doctor' Commons.'
I quite make up my mind to do so. I then tell Steerforth that my

if I have be round the world since we part, we could hardly have
be well pleased to meet again. my aunt cry outright as she

have be alive, that silly little creature would have shed tear,
