'so you have leave Mr. Dick behind, aunt?' say I. 'I be sorry for
that. ah, Janet, how do you do?'
as Janet curtsy, hop I be well, I observe my aunt's visage
lengthen very much.
'I be sorry for it, too,' say my aunt, rub her nose. 'I have
have no peace of mind, Trot, since I have be here.'
before I could ask why, she tell I.
'I be convince,' say my aunt, lay her hand with melancholy

afternoon at four o'clock. a cold feeling come over I from head
to foot, and I know it be a donkey!'
I try to comfort she on this point, but she reject consolation.

come to my house.' this have be, ever since, the only name my

Janet venture to suggest that my aunt might be disturb herself

Supper be comfortably serve and hot, though my aunt's room be

'certainly not,' return my aunt. 'it would be no pleasure to a

I do not venture to controvert this opinion, but I make a good

'well, Trot,' she begin, 'what do you think of the proctor plan?

'I have think a good deal about it, my dear aunt, and I have

'come!' say my aunt. 'thatbe cheer!'
'I have only one difficulty, aunt.'
'say what it be, Trot,' she return.

my aunt finish eat the piece of toast on which she be then

it be the first time I have hear my aunt refer to her past

we have a long chat by the fire before we go to bed. I sleep in

at about mid-day, we set out for the office of Messrs Spenlow and

what Mr. Dick have tell I, and what I have suppose to be a delusion

doctor' Commons be approach by a little low archway. before we

accessible to pilgrim without the ceremony of knock, three or

'Mr. Spenlow's in Court, ma'am,' say the dry man; 'itbe an Arches
day; but itbe close by, and Iwill send for he directly.'
as we be leave to look about we while Mr. Spenlow be fetch, I
avail myself of the opportunity. the furniture of the room be
old-fashioned and dusty; and the green baize on the top of the
writing-table have lose all its colour, and be as withered and pale
as an old pauper. there be a great many bundle of paper on it,
some endorse as Allegations, and some (to my surprise) as Libels,
and some as be in the Consistory Court, and some in the Arches

Court, and some in the Delegates' court; give I occasion to

be sundry immense manuscript Books of Evidence take on

volume. all this look tolerably expensive, I think, and give

eye with increase complacency over these and many similar
object, when hasty footstep be hear in the room outside, and

in, take off his hat as he come.
he be a little light-haired gentleman, with undeniable boot, and
the stiff of white cravat and shirt-collar. he be button
up, mighty trim and tight, and must have take a great deal of
pain with his whisker, which be accurately curl. his gold

ought to have a sinewy golden arm, to draw it out with, like those
which be put up over the goldbeater' shop. he be get up with
such care, and be so stiff, that he could hardly bend himself;
be oblige, when he glance at some paper on his desk, after

of his spine, like Punch.
I have previously be present by my aunt, and have be

'and so, Mr. Copperfield, you think of enter into our

inclination of his body - punch again - 'that there be a vacancy

I bow my acknowledgement, and say, my aunt have mention to I

'oh surely! surely!' say Mr. Spenlow. 'we always, in this house,
propose a month - an initiatory month. I should be happy, myself,
to propose two month - three - an indefinite period, in fact - but
I have a partner. Mr. Jorkins.'
'and the premium, sir,' I return, 'be a thousand pound?'
'and the premium, Stamp include, be a thousand pound,' say Mr.

pound too little, in short.'
'I suppose, sir,' say I, still desire to spare my aunt, 'that it
be not the custom here, if an articled clerk be particularly
useful, and make himself a perfect master of his profession' - I
could not help blush, this look so like praise myself - 'I

allow he any -'
Mr. Spenlow, by a great effort, just lift his head far enough out

myself, Mr. Copperfield, if I be unfettered. Mr. Jorkins be

I be quite dismay by the idea of this terrible Jorkins. but I
find out afterwards that he be a mild man of a heavy temperament,
whose place in the business be to keep himself in the background,
and be constantly exhibit by name as the most obdurate and

it be settle that I should begin my month's probation as soon as

Mr. Spenlow conduct I through a paved courtyard form of grave

perfect library of evidence, and stop to put up, from time to

altogether, I have never, on any occasion, make one at such a
cosey, dosey, old-fashioned, time-forget, sleepy-head little

soothe opiate to belong to it in any character - except perhaps

we arrive at Lincoln's Inn Fields without any new adventure,

to take care of myself.
'I have not be here a week tomorrow, without consider that
too, my dear,' she return. 'there be a furnished little set of

a marvel.'
with this brief introduction, she produce from her pocket an

with a view of the river, a singularly desirable, and compact set

'why, this be the very thing, aunt!' say I, flush with the

they be on the top of the house - a great point with my aunt,

as I be delighted with the place, my aunt and Mrs. Crupp withdraw
into the pantry to discuss the term, while I remain on the
sit-room sofa, hardly dare to think it possible that I could
be destine to live in such a noble residence. after a single
combat of some duration they return, and I see, to my joy, both
in Mrs. Crupp's countenance and in my aunt's, that the deed be
do.
'be it the last occupant's furniture?' inquire my aunt.
'yes, it be, ma'am,' say Mrs. Crupp.
'whatbe become of he?' ask my aunt.
Mrs. Crupp be take with a troublesome cough, in the midst of
which she articulate with much difficulty. 'he be take ill here,
ma'am, and - ugh! ugh! ugh! dear I! - and he die!'
'hey! what do he die of?' ask my aunt.
'well, ma'am, he die of drink,' say Mrs. Crupp, in confidence.
'and smoke.'
'smoke? you donot mean chimney?' say my aunt.
'no, ma'am,' return Mrs. Crupp. 'cigar and pipe.'
'thatbe not catch, Trot, at any rate,' remark my aunt, turn
to I.
'no, indeed,' say I.
in short, my aunt, see how enraptured I be with the premise,
take they for a month, with leave to remain for twelve month when
that time be out. Mrs. Crupp be to find linen, and to cook;
every other necessary be already provide; and Mrs. Crupp
expressly intimated that she should always yearn towards I as a
son. I be to take possession the day after tomorrow, and Mrs.
Crupp say, thank Heaven she have now find summun she could care
for!
on our way back, my aunt inform I how she confidently trust
that the life I be now to lead would make I firm and
self-reliant, which be all I want. she repeat this several
time next day, in the interval of our arranging for the
transmission of my clothe and book from Mr. Wickfield's; relative
to which, and to all my late holiday, I write a long letter to
Agnes, of which my aunt take charge, as she be to leave on the
succeed day. not to lengthen these particular, I need only
add, that she make a handsome provision for all my possible want
during my month of trial; that Steerforth, to my great
disappointment and hers too, do not make his appearance before she
go away; that I see she safely seat in the Dover coach,
exult in the come discomfiture of the vagrant donkey, with
Janet at her side; and that when the coach be go, I turn my
face to the adelphi, ponder on the old day when I use to roam
about its subterranean arch, and on the happy change which have
bring I to the surface.
CHAPTER 24
MY first dissipation
it be a wonderfully fine thing to have that lofty castle to
myself, and to feel, when I shut my outer door, like Robinson
Crusoe, when he have get into his fortification, and pull his
ladder up after he. it be a wonderfully fine thing to walk about
town with the key of my house in my pocket, and to know that I
could ask any fellow to come home, and make quite sure of its being
inconvenient to nobody, if it be not so to I. it be a
wonderfully fine thing to let myself in and out, and to come and go
without a word to anyone, and to ring Mrs. Crupp up, gasp, from
the depth of the earth, when I want she - and when she be
disposed to come. all this, I say, be wonderfully fine; but I
must say, too, that there be time when it be very dreary.
it be fine in the morning, particularly in the fine morning. it
look a very fresh, free life, by daylight: still fresher, and
more free, by sunlight. but as the day decline, the life seem
to go down too. I donot know how it be; it seldom look well by
candle-light. I want somebody to talk to, then. I miss Agnes.
I find a tremendous blank, in the place of that smile repository
of my confidence. Mrs. Crupp appear to be a long way off. I
think about my predecessor, who have die of drink and smoke; and
I could have wish he have be so good as to live, and not bother
I with his decease.
after two day and night, I feel as if I have live there for a
year, and yet I be not an hour old, but be quite as much
torment by my own youthfulness as ever.
Steerforth not yet appear, which induce I to apprehend that he

Oxford friend.
as she press I to stay to dinner, I remain, and I believe we

I show he over the establishment, not omit the pantry, with

this be a delightful hearing. I tell he if he wait for that,
he would have to wait till doomsday.
'but you shall have some breakfast!' say I, with my hand on the

'then bring they here to dinner,' I return. 'do you think they

I would not by any mean consent to this, for it occur to I

when he be go, I rang for Mrs. Crupp, and acquaint she with my

it be a remarkable instance of want of forethought on the part of

I act on Mrs. Crupp's opinion, and give the order at the

I begin, by be singularly cheerful and light-hearted; all sort

a very foggy night, with great ring round the lamp in the

CHAPTER 25
good and bad angel
I be go out at my door on the morning after that deplorable day

mind relative to the date of my dinner-party, as if a body of
Titans have take an enormous lever and push the day before

look at he over the banister, he swing into a trot, and come
up pant as if he have run himself into a state of exhaustion.
'T. Copperfield, Esquire,' say the ticket-porter, touch his hat

I could scarcely lay claim to the name: I be so disturbed by the
conviction that the letter come from Agnes. however, I tell he I

if the day be half as tremendous to any other professional

the professional business of Mr. Waterbrook's establishment be

she smile rather sadly, I think, and shake her head.

I look at she inquiringly; but already with a foreknowledge of

there be always something in her modest voice that seem to touch

'when I recall it,' say Agnes.
she would have dismiss the subject so, but I be too full of it
to allow that, and insist on tell she how it happen that I
have disgrace myself, and what chain of accidental circumstance
have have the theatre for its final link. it be a great relief to
I to do this, and to enlarge on the obligation that I owe to
Steerforth for his care of I when I be unable to take care of
myself.
'you must not forget,' say Agnes, calmly change the conversation
as soon as I have conclude, 'that you be always to tell I, not
only when you fall into trouble, but when you fall in love. who
have succeed to Miss Larkins, Trotwood?'
'no one, Agnes.'
'someone, Trotwood,' say Agnes, laugh, and hold up her
finger.
'no, Agnes, upon my word! there be a lady, certainly, at Mrs.
Steerforth's house, who be very clever, and whom I like to talk to
- Miss Dartle - but I donot adore she.'
Agnes laugh again at her own penetration, and tell I that if I
be faithful to she in my confidence she think she should keep
a little register of my violent attachment, with the date,
duration, and termination of each, like the table of the reign of
the king and queen, in the history of England. then she ask I
if I have see Uriah.
'Uriah Heep?' say I. 'no. be he in London?'
'he come to the office downstairs, every day,' return Agnes.
'he be in London a week before I. I be afraid on disagreeable
business, Trotwood.'
'on some business that make you uneasy, Agnes, I see,' say I.
'what can that be?'
Agnes lay aside her work, and reply, fold her hand upon one
another, and look pensively at I out of those beautiful soft
eye of her:
'I believe he be go to enter into partnership with papa.'
'what? Uriah? that mean, fawn fellow, worm himself into such

about it, Agnes? consider what a connexion it be likely to be.
you must speak out. you must not allow your father to take such a
mad step. you must prevent it, Agnes, while therebe time.'
still look at I, Agnes shake her head while I be speak,
with a faint smile at my warmth: and then reply:
'you remember our last conversation about papa? it be not long

struggle between his desire to represent it to I as a matter of
choice on his part, and his inability to conceal that it be force
upon he. I feel very sorry.'
'force upon he, Agnes! who force it upon he?'
'Uriah,' she reply, after a moment's hesitation, 'have make
himself indispensable to papa. he be subtle and watchful. he have
master papa's weakness, foster they, and take advantage of
they, until - to say all that I mean in a word, Trotwood, - until
papa be afraid of he.'
there be more that she might have say; more that she know, or

spare her father. it have long be go on to this, I be

hard use of his power.'
I say he be a hound, which, at the moment, be a great
satisfaction to I.
'at the time I speak of, as the time when papa speak to I,'
pursue Agnes, 'he have tell papa that he be go away; that he

prospect. Papa be very much depressed then, and more bow down

seem hurt by it and ashamed of it.'
'and how do you receive it, Agnes?'

weaken his strength and energy, by turn they always upon one
idea. if I could ever set this right! if I could ever work out
his restoration, as I have so innocently be the cause of his
decline!'
I have never before see Agnes cry. I have see tear in her eye
when I have bring new honours home from school, and I have see

I have never see she grieve like this. it make I so sorry that I
could only say, in a foolish, helpless manner, 'pray, Agnes, donot!
donot, my dear sister!'
but Agnes be too superior to I in character and purpose, as I

I find Mr. Waterbrook to be a middle-aged gentleman, with a short

say his aunt.
Mrs. Henry Spiker be this lady's name; and her husband be there

I find Uriah Heep among the company, in a suit of black, and in

there be other guest - all iced for the occasion, as it strike

Traddles! my mind fly back to Salem House; and could it be Tommy,

I look for Mr. Traddles with unusual interest. he be a sober,

I make my way to Mr. Waterbrook, and say, that I believe I have

'oh! aye, aye! indeed!' say my host, with much diminished

I be much impressed by the extremely comfortable and satisfied

it occur to I several time that we should have get on well,

we might have be a party of ogre, the conversation assume such
a sanguine complexion.
'I confess I be of Mrs. Waterbrook's opinion,' say Mr. Waterbrook,
with his wine-glass at his eye. 'other thing be all very well in

'oh! there be nothing,' observe Hamlet's aunt, 'so satisfactory

positively Idols! before service, intellect, and so on. but these
be intangible point. blood be not so. we see blood in a nose,
and we know it. we meet with it in a chin, and we say, "there it
be! thatbe blood!" it be an actual matter of fact. we point it
out. it admit of no doubt.'
the simper fellow with the weak leg, who have take Agnes down,
state the question more decisively yet, I think.

myself, Iwould rather at any time be knock down by a man who have get

this sentiment, as compress the general question into a

'that affair of the first bond for four thousand five hundred

'the C. of B.'s!' say Mr. Gulpidge.

'Lord bless my soul!' cry Mr. Spiker.

Mr. Waterbrook be only too happy, as it appear to I, to have

'oh, really, Master Copperfield,' he rejoin - 'I beg your pardon,

'there be no constraint in the case,' say I. 'will you come?'

I lead he up the dark stair, to prevent his knock his head

that it seem to rain blessing on my ed. you have hear
something, I des-say, of a change in my expectation, Master

as he sit on my sofa, with his long knee draw up under his

'yes,' say I, 'something.'

I could have throw my bootjack at he (it lie ready on the rug),

he sit, with that carve grin on his face, look at the fire, as

something in the emphasis he lay upon the kindling of those

'so, Mr. Wickfield,' say I, at last, 'who be worth five hundred of

I recollect well how indignantly my heart beat, as I see his crafty

'Master Copperfield,' he begin - 'but be I keep you up?'
'you be not keep I up. I generally go to bed late.'

'oh, thank you! itbe so true!' he cry. 'oh, thank you very much

'not at all,' I say, loftily. 'there be no reason why you should

trust you with my secret, Master Copperfield, for I have always

I fathom the depth of the rascal's whole scheme, and understand

Dear Agnes! so much too loving and too good for anyone that I

'if it come to that,' I say, 'pray take my bed, and Iwill lie down

his repudiation of this offer be almost shrill enough, in the

I never shall forget that night. I never shall forget how I turn

CHAPTER 26
I fall into CAPTIVITY
I see no more of Uriah Heep, until the day when Agnes leave town.
I be at the coach office to take leave of she and see she go; and

I.
in the state of trouble into which his disclosure by my fire have

feel sure that it be necessary for papa's peace that the

well.

I have ample leisure to refine upon my uneasiness: for Steerforth

in the meantime, day and week slip away. I be article to

have sandwich and sherry into the office for the clerk, and

Mr. Spenlow be as good as his word. in a week or two, he refer

the phaeton be a very handsome affair; the horse arch their

we take thing much more easily in the Commons than they could be

I ask Mr. Spenlow what he consider the good sort of

complete idea of snugness. it lie in a nutshell. for example: you
bring a divorce case, or a restitution case, into the Consistory.
very good. you try it in the Consistory. you make a quiet
little round game of it, among a family group, and you play it
out at leisure. suppose you be not satisfied with the

what be the Arches? the same court, in the same room, with the
same bar, and the same practitioner, but another judge, for there
the consistory judge could plead any court-day as an advocate.
well, you play your round game out again. still you be not

high, the Commons have be busy; and a man might lay his hand

Commons, and down come the country!'
I listen to all this with attention; and though, I must say, I

change have take place in the weather. it be in the state of
my own feeling, I add bashfully: to clench the explanation.
I never see such curl - how could I, for there never be such
curl! - as those she shake out to hide her blush. as to the
straw hat and blue ribbon which be on the top of the curl, if I
could only have hang it up in my room in Buckingham Street, what a
priceless possession it would have be!
'you have just come home from Paris,' say I.
'yes,' say she. 'have you ever be there?'
'no.'
'oh! I hope youwill go soon! you would like it so much!'
trace of deep-seat anguish appear in my countenance. that she
should hope I would go, that she should think it possible I could
go, be insupportable. I depreciate Paris; I depreciate France.
I say I wouldnot leave England, under exist circumstance, for
any earthly consideration. nothing should induce I. in short,
she be shake the curl again, when the little dog come run
along the walk to our relief.
he be mortally jealous of I, and persist in bark at I. she
take he up in her arm - oh my goodness! - and caress he, but
he persist upon bark still. he wouldnot let I touch he,
when I try; and then she beat he. it increase my suffering
greatly to see the pat she give he for punishment on the bridge
of his blunt nose, while he wink his eye, and lick her hand,
and still growl within himself like a little double-bass. at
length he be quiet - well he might be with her dimpled chin upon
his head! - and we walk away to look at a greenhouse.
'you be not very intimate with Miss Murdstone, be you?' say
Dora. -'My pet.'
(the two last word be to the dog. oh, if they have only be to
I!)
'no,' I reply. 'not at all so.'
'she be a tiresome creature,' say Dora, pouting. 'I cannot think
what papa can have be about, when he choose such a vexatious thing
to be my companion. who want a protector? I be sure I donot want
a protector. Jip can protect I a great deal well than Miss
Murdstone, - cannot you, Jip, dear?'
he only wink lazily, when she kiss his ball of a head.
'papa call she my confidential friend, but I be sure she be no
such thing - be she, Jip? we be not go to confide in any such
cross people, Jip and I. we mean to bestow our confidence where we
like, and to find out our own friend, instead of have they find
out for we - donot we, Jip?'
Jip make a comfortable noise, in answer, a little like a tea-kettle
when it sing. as for I, every word be a new heap of fetter,
rivet above the last.
'it be very hard, because we have not a kind Mama, that we be to
have, instead, a sulky, gloomy old thing like Miss Murdstone,
always follow we about - benot it, Jip? never mind, Jip. we
willnot be confidential, and wewill make ourselves as happy as we can
in spite of she, and wewill tease she, and not please she - willnot
we, Jip?'
if it have last any long, I think I must have go down on my
knee on the gravel, with the probability before I of graze
they, and of be presently eject from the premise besides.
but, by good fortune the greenhouse be not far off, and these
word bring we to it.
it contain quite a show of beautiful geranium. we loiter
along in front of they, and Dora often stop to admire this one
or that one, and I stop to admire the same one, and Dora,
laughing, hold the dog up childishly, to smell the flower; and if
we be not all three in Fairyland, certainly I be. the scent of
a geranium leaf, at this day, strike I with a half comical half
serious wonder as to what change have come over I in a moment; and
then I see a straw hat and blue ribbon, and a quantity of curl,
and a little black dog be hold up, in two slender arm, against
a bank of blossom and bright leaf.
Miss Murdstone have be look for we. she find we here; and
present her uncongenial cheek, the little wrinkle in it fill
with hair powder, to Dora to be kiss. then she take Dora's arm
in her, and march we into breakfast as if it be a soldier's
funeral.
how many cup of tea I drank, because Dora make it, I donot know.
but, I perfectly remember that I sit swill tea until my whole
nervous system, if I have have any in those day, must have go by
the board. by and by we go to church. Miss Murdstone be
between Dora and I in the pew; but I hear her sing, and the
congregation vanish. a sermon be deliver - about Dora, of
course - and I be afraid that be all I know of the service.
we have a quiet day. no company, a walk, a family dinner of four,
and an evening of look over book and picture; Miss Murdstone
with a homily before she, and her eye upon we, keep guard
vigilantly. ah! little do Mr. Spenlow imagine, when he sit
opposite to I after dinner that day, with his pocket-handkerchief
over his head, how fervently I be embrace he, in my fancy, as
his son-in-law! little do he think, when I take leave of he at
night, that he have just give his full consent to my be engage
to Dora, and that I be invoke blessing on his head!
we depart early in the morning, for we have a salvage case come
on in the Admiralty Court, require a rather accurate knowledge of
the whole science of navigation, in which (as we couldnot be
expect to know much about those matter in the Commons) the judge
have entreat two old Trinity Masters, for charity's sake, to come
and help he out. Dora be at the breakfast-table to make the tea
again, however; and I have the melancholy pleasure of take off my
hat to she in the phaeton, as she stand on the door-step with Jip
in her arm.
what the Admiralty be to I that day; what nonsense I make of our
case in my mind, as I listen to it; how I see 'DORA' engrave
upon the blade of the silver oar which they lie upon the table, as
the emblem of that high jurisdiction; and how I feel when Mr.
Spenlow go home without I (I have have an insane hope that he
might take I back again), as if I be a mariner myself, and the
ship to which I belong have sail away and leave I on a desert
island; I shall make no fruitless effort to describe. if that
sleepy old court could rouse itself, and present in any visible
form the daydream I have have in it about Dora, it would reveal my
truth.
I donot mean the dream that I dream on that day alone, but day
after day, from week to week, and term to term. I go there, not
to attend to what be go on, but to think about Dora. if ever
I bestow a thought upon the case, as they drag their slow
length before I, it be only to wonder, in the matrimonial case
(remember Dora), how it be that married people could ever be
otherwise than happy; and, in the Prerogative case, to consider,
if the money in question have be leave to I, what be the
foremost step I should immediately have take in regard to Dora.
within the first week of my passion, I buy four sumptuous
waistcoat - not for myself; I have no pride in they; for Dora - and
take to wear straw-coloured kid glove in the street, and lay
the foundation of all the corn I have ever have. if the boot I
wear at that period could only be produce and compare with the
natural size of my foot, they would show what the state of my heart
be, in a most affect manner.
and yet, wretche cripple as I make myself by this act of homage to
Dora, I walk mile upon mile daily in the hope of see she.
not only be I soon as well know on the Norwood Road as the
postman on that beat, but I pervade London likewise. I walk
about the street where the good shop for lady be, I haunt
the Bazaar like an unquiet spirit, I fag through the Park again
and again, long after I be quite knock up. sometimes, at long
interval and on rare occasion, I see she. perhaps I see she
glove wave in a carriage window; perhaps I meet she, walk with
she and Miss Murdstone a little way, and speak to she. in the
latter case I be always very miserable afterwards, to think that
I have say nothing to the purpose; or that she have no idea of the
extent of my devotion, or that she care nothing about I. I be
always look out, as may be suppose, for another invitation to
Mr. Spenlow's house. I be always be disappoint, for I get
none.
Mrs. Crupp must have be a woman of penetration; for when this
attachment be but a few week old, and I have not have the courage
to write more explicitly even to Agnes, than that I have be to Mr.
Spenlow's house, 'whose family,' I add, 'consist of one
daughter'; - I say Mrs. Crupp must have be a woman of
penetration, for, even in that early stage, she find it out. she
come up to I one evening, when I be very low, to ask (she be
then afflict with the disorder I have mention) if I could
oblige she with a little tincture of cardamum mix with rhubarb,
and flavour with seven drop of the essence of clove, which be
the good remedy for her complaint; - or, if I have not such a thing
by I, with a little brandy, which be the next good. it be not,
she remark, so palatable to she, but it be the next good. as I
have never even hear of the first remedy, and always have the second
in the closet, I give Mrs. Crupp a glass of the second, which (that
I might have no suspicion of its be devoted to any improper use)
she begin to take in my presence.
'cheer up, sir,' say Mrs. Crupp. 'I cannot abear to see you so,
sir: Ibe a mother myself.'
I do not quite perceive the application of this fact to myself,
but I smile on Mrs. Crupp, as benignly as be in my power.
'come, sir,' say Mrs. Crupp. 'excuse I. I know what it be, sir.
therebe a lady in the case.'
'Mrs. Crupp?' I return, redden.
'oh, bless you! keep a good heart, sir!' say Mrs. Crupp, nodding
encouragement. 'never say die, sir! if she donot smile upon you,
therebe a many as will. you be a young gentleman to be smile on,
Mr. Copperfull, and you must learn your walue, sir.'
Mrs. Crupp always call I Mr. Copperfull: firstly, no doubt,
because it be not my name; and secondly, I be inclined to think,
in some indistinct association with a washing-day.
'what make you suppose there be any young lady in the case, Mrs.
Crupp?' say I.
'Mr. Copperfull,' say Mrs. Crupp, with a great deal of feeling,
'Ibe a mother myself.'
for some time Mrs. Crupp could only lay her hand upon her nankeen
bosom, and fortify herself against return pain with sip of her
medicine. at length she speak again.
'when the present set be take for you by your dear aunt, Mr.
Copperfull,' say Mrs. Crupp, 'my remark be, I have now find
summun I could care for. "Thank Ev'in!" be the expression, "I
have now find summun I can care for!" - you donot eat enough, sir,
nor yet drink.'
'be that what you find your supposition on, Mrs. Crupp?' say I.
'Sir,' say Mrs. Crupp, in a tone approach to severity, 'Ihave
laundresse other young gentleman besides yourself. a young
gentleman may be over-careful of himself, or he may be
under-careful of himself. he may brush his hair too regular, or
too un-regular. he may wear his boot much too large for he, or
much too small. that is accord as the young gentleman have his
original character form. but let he go to which extreme he may,
sir, therebe a young lady in both of them.'
Mrs. Crupp shake her head in such a determined manner, that I have
not an inch of vantage-ground leave.
'it be but the gentleman which die here before yourself,' say
Mrs. Crupp, 'that fall in love - with a barmaid - and have his
waistcoat take in directly, though much swell by drink.'
'Mrs. Crupp,' say I, 'I must beg you not to connect the young lady
in my case with a barmaid, or anything of that sort, if you
please.'
'Mr. Copperfull,' return Mrs. Crupp, 'Ibe a mother myself, and
not likely. I ask your pardon, sir, if I intrude. I should never
wish to intrude where I be not welcome. but you be a young
gentleman, Mr. Copperfull, and my adwice to you be, to cheer up,
sir, to keep a good heart, and to know your own walue. if you be
to take to something, sir,' say Mrs. Crupp, 'if you be to take to
skittle, now, which be healthy, you might find it divert your
mind, and do you good.'
with these word, Mrs. Crupp, affect to be very careful of the
brandy - which be all go - thank I with a majestic curtsey,
and retire. as her figure disappear into the gloom of the
entry, this counsel certainly present itself to my mind in the
light of a slight liberty on Mrs. Crupp's part; but, at the same
time, I be content to receive it, in another point of view, as a
word to the wise, and a warning in future to keep my secret well.
CHAPTER 27
TOMMY traddle
it may have be in consequence of Mrs. Crupp's advice, and,
perhaps, for no well reason than because there be a certain
similarity in the sound of the word skittle and traddle, that it
come into my head, next day, to go and look after traddle. the
time he have mention be more than out, and he live in a little
street near the Veterinary College at Camden Town, which be
principally tenant, as one of our clerk who live in that
direction inform I, by gentleman student, who buy live
donkey, and make experiment on those quadruped in their private
apartment. have obtain from this clerk a direction to the
academic grove in question, I set out, the same afternoon, to visit
my old schoolfellow.
I find that the street be not as desirable a one as I could have
wish it to be, for the sake of traddle. the inhabitant
appear to have a propensity to throw any little trifle they be
not in want of, into the road: which not only make it rank and
sloppy, but untidy too, on account of the cabbage-leaf. the
refuse be not wholly vegetable either, for I myself see a shoe, a
double-up saucepan, a black bonnet, and an umbrella, in various
stage of decomposition, as I be look out for the number I
want.
the general air of the place remind I forcibly of the day when
I live with Mr. and Mrs. Micawber. an indescribable character of
fade gentility that attach to the house I seek, and make it
unlike all the other house in the street - though they be all
build on one monotonous pattern, and look like the early copy
of a blundering boy who be learn to make house, and have not
yet get out of his cramp brick-and-mortar pothook - remind I
still more of Mr. and Mrs. Micawber. happen to arrive at the
door as it be open to the afternoon milkman, I be remind of
Mr. and Mrs. Micawber more forcibly yet.
'now,' say the milkman to a very youthful servant girl. 'have that
there little bill of mine be heerd on?'
'oh, master say hewill attend to it immediate,' be the reply.
'because,' say the milkman, go on as if he have receive no
answer, and speak, as I judge from his tone, rather for the
edification of somebody within the house, than of the youthful
servant - an impression which be strengthen by his manner of
glare down the passage - 'because that there little bill have be
run so long, that I begin to believe itbe run away altogether,
and never willnot be heerd of. now, Ibe not a go to stand it, you
know!' say the milkman, still throw his voice into the house,
and glare down the passage.
as to his dealing in the mild article of milk, by the by, there
never be a great anomaly. his deportment would have be fierce
in a butcher or a brandy-merchant.
the voice of the youthful servant become faint, but she seem to
I, from the action of her lip, again to murmur that it would be
attend to immediate.
'I tell you what,' say the milkman, look hard at she for the
first time, and take she by the chin, 'be you fond of milk?'
'yes, I like it,' she reply.
'good,' say the milkman. 'then you willnot have none tomorrow.
D'ye hear? not a fragment of milk you willnot have tomorrow.'
I think she seem, upon the whole, relieve by the prospect of
have any today. the milkman, after shake his head at she
darkly, release her chin, and with anything rather than good-will
open his can, and deposit the usual quantity in the family jug.
this do, he go away, mutter, and utter the cry of his
trade next door, in a vindictive shriek.
'do Mr. Traddles live here?' I then inquire.
a mysterious voice from the end of the passage reply 'yes.' upon
which the youthful servant reply 'yes.'
'be he at home?' say I.
again the mysterious voice reply in the affirmative, and again
the servant echo it. upon this, I walk in, and in pursuance of
the servant's direction walk upstairs; conscious, as I pass
the back parlour-door, that I be survey by a mysterious eye,
probably belong to the mysterious voice.
when I get to the top of the stair - the house be only a story
high above the ground floor - Traddles be on the landing to meet
I. he be delighted to see I, and give I welcome, with great
heartiness, to his little room. it be in the front of the house,
and extremely neat, though sparely furnish. it be his only
room, I see; for there be a sofa-bedstead in it, and his
blacking-brush and blacking be among his book - on the top
shelf, behind a dictionary. his table be cover with paper, and
he be hard at work in an old coat. I look at nothing, that I
know of, but I see everything, even to the prospect of a church
upon his china inkstand, as I sit down - and this, too, be a
faculty confirm in I in the old Micawber time. various
ingenious arrangement he have make, for the disguise of his chest
of drawer, and the accommodation of his boot, his shaving-glass,
and so forth, particularly impress themselves upon I, as
evidence of the same traddle who use to make model of
elephant' den in writing-paper to put fly in; and to comfort
himself under ill usage, with the memorable work of art I have so
often mention.
in a corner of the room be something neatly cover up with a
large white cloth. I could not make out what that be.
'traddle,' say I, shake hand with he again, after I have sit
down, 'I be delighted to see you.'
'I be delighted to see you, Copperfield,' he return. 'I be very
glad indeed to see you. it be because I be thoroughly glad to
see you when we meet in Ely Place, and be sure you be thoroughly
glad to see I, that I give you this address instead of my address
at chamber.'
'oh! you have chamber?' say I.
'why, I have the fourth of a room and a passage, and the fourth of
a clerk,' return traddle. 'three other and myself unite to
have a set of chamber - to look business-like - and we quarter the
clerk too. half-a-crown a week he cost I.'
his old simple character and good temper, and something of his old
unlucky fortune also, I think, smile at I in the smile with
which he make this explanation.
'itbe not because I have the least pride, Copperfield, you
understand,' say traddle, 'that I donot usually give my address
here. itbe only on account of those who come to I, who might not
like to come here. for myself, I be fight my way on in the
world against difficulty, and it would be ridiculous if I make a
pretence of do anything else.'
'you be read for the bar, Mr. Waterbrook inform I?' say I.
'why, yes,' say traddle, rub his hand slowly over one
another. 'I be read for the bar. the fact be, I have just
begin to keep my term, after rather a long delay. itbe some time
since I be article, but the payment of that hundred pound be a
great pull. a great pull!' say traddle, with a wince, as if he
have have a tooth out.
'do you know what I cannot help think of, Traddles, as I sit here
look at you?' I ask he.
'no,' say he.
'that sky-blue suit you use to wear.'
'Lord, to be sure!' cry traddle, laugh. 'tight in the arm
and leg, you know? dear I! well! those be happy time,
benot they?'
'I think our schoolmaster might have make they happy, without
do any harm to any of we, I acknowledge,' I return.
'perhaps he might,' say traddle. 'but dear I, there be a good
deal of fun go on. do you remember the night in the bedroom?
when we use to have the supper? and when you use to tell the
story? ha, ha, ha! and do you remember when I get can for
cry about Mr. Mell? Old Creakle! I should like to see he
again, too!'
'he be a brute to you, traddle,' say I, indignantly; for his
good humour make I feel as if I have see he beat but yesterday.
'do you think so?' return traddle. 'really? perhaps he be
rather. but itbe all over, a long while. Old Creakle!'
'you be bring up by an uncle, then?' say I.
'of course I be!' say traddle. 'the one I be always go to
write to. and always donot, eh! ha, ha, ha! yes, I have an uncle
then. he die soon after I leave school.'
'indeed!'
'yes. he be a retired - what do you call it! - draper -
cloth-merchant - and have make I his heir. but he donot like I
when I grow up.'
'do you really mean that?' say I. he be so composed, that I
fancy he must have some other meaning.
'oh dear, yes, Copperfield! I mean it,' reply traddle. 'it be
an unfortunate thing, but he donot like I at all. he say I
benot at all what he expect, and so he marry his housekeeper.'
'and what do you do?' I ask.
'I donot do anything in particular,' say traddle. 'I live with
they, wait to be put out in the world, until his gout
unfortunately fly to his stomach - and so he die, and so she
marry a young man, and so I benot provide for.'
'do you get nothing, traddle, after all?'
'oh dear, yes!' say traddle. 'I get fifty pound. I have never
be bring up to any profession, and at first I be at a loss
what to do for myself. however, I begin, with the assistance of
the son of a professional man, who have be to Salem House -
Yawler, with his nose on one side. do you recollect he?'
no. he have not be there with I; all the nose be straight in
my day.
'it donot matter,' say traddle. 'I begin, by mean of his
assistance, to copy law writing. that donot answer very well;
and then I begin to state case for they, and make abstract, and
that sort of work. for I be a plod kind of fellow,
Copperfield, and have learn the way of do such thing pithily.
well! that put it in my head to enter myself as a law student; and
that run away with all that be leave of the fifty pound. Yawler
recommend I to one or two other office, however - Mr.
Waterbrook's for one - and I get a good many job. I be fortunate
enough, too, to become acquainted with a person in the publishing
way, who be get up an encyclopaedia, and he set I to work;
and, indeed' (glance at his table), 'I be at work for he at this
minute. I be not a bad compiler, Copperfield,' say Traddles,
preserve the same air of cheerful confidence in all he say, 'but
I have no invention at all; not a particle. I suppose there never
be a young man with less originality than I have.'
as traddle seem to expect that I should assent to this as a
matter of course, I nod; and he go on, with the same sprightly
patience - I can find no well expression - as before.
'so, by little and little, and not live high, I manage to scrape
up the hundred pound at last,' say traddle; 'and thank Heaven
thatbe pay - though it be - though it certainly be,' say
traddle, wince again as if he have have another tooth out, 'a
pull. I be live by the sort of work I have mention, still, and
I hope, one of these day, to get connect with some newspaper:
which would almost be the making of my fortune. now, Copperfield,
you be so exactly what you use to be, with that agreeable face,
and itbe so pleasant to see you, that I sha'n't conceal anything.
therefore you must know that I be engaged.'
engage! oh, Dora!
'she be a curate's daughter,' say traddle; 'one of ten, down in
Devonshire. yes!' for he see I glance, involuntarily, at the

to the left, out of this gate,' trace his finger along the

the delight with which he enter into these particular, do not
fully present itself to I until afterwards; for my selfish

'she be such a dear girl!' say traddle; 'a little old than I,
but the dear girl! I tell you I be go out of town? I have
be down there. I walk there, and I walk back, and I have the
most delightful time! I dare say ours be likely to be a rather
long engagement, but our motto be "wait and hope!" we always say
that. "wait and hope," we always say. and she would wait,
Copperfield, till she be sixty - any age you can mention - for
I!'
traddle rise from his chair, and, with a triumphant smile, put his

(itbe two foot ten in circumference), I buy. you want to lay a
book down, you know, or somebody come to see you or your wife, and
want a place to stand a cup of tea upon, and - and there you be
again!' say traddle. 'itbe an admirable piece of workmanship -
firm as a rock!'
I praise they both, highly, and traddle replace the covering as
carefully as he have remove it.
'itbe not a great deal towards the furnishing,' say traddle, 'but

necessary - because those thing tell, and mount up. however,
"wait and hope!" and I assure you shebe the dear girl!'
'I be quite certain of it,' say I.
'in the meantime,' say traddle, come back to his chair; 'and

can. I donot make much, but I donot spend much. in general, I
board with the people downstairs, who be very agreeable people
indeed. both Mr. and Mrs. Micawber have see a good deal of life,
and be excellent company.'
'my dear traddle!' I quickly exclaim. 'what be you talk
about?'
traddle look at I, as if he wonder what I be talk about.
'Mr. and Mrs. Micawber!' I repeat. 'why, I be intimately
acquaint with they!'
an opportune double knock at the door, which I know well from old
experience in Windsor Terrace, and which nobody but Mr. Micawber
could ever have knock at that door, resolve any doubt in my mind
as to their be my old friend. I beg traddle to ask his

come into the room with a genteel and youthful air.
'I beg your pardon, Mr. Traddles,' say Mr. Micawber, with the old
roll in his voice, as he check himself in hum a soft tune.
'I be not aware that there be any individual, alien to this
tenement, in your sanctum.'
Mr. Micawber slightly bow to I, and pull up his shirt-collar.
'how do you do, Mr. Micawber?' say I.
'Sir,' say Mr. Micawber, 'you be exceedingly obliging. I be in
statu quo.'
'and Mrs. Micawber?' I pursue.
'Sir,' say Mr. Micawber, 'she be also, thank God, in statu quo.'
'and the child, Mr. Micawber?'
'Sir,' say Mr. Micawber, 'I rejoice to reply that they be,
likewise, in the enjoyment of salubrity.'
all this time, Mr. Micawber have not know I in the least, though
he have stand face to face with I. but now, see I smile, he
examine my feature with more attention, fall back, cry, 'be it
possible! have I the pleasure of again behold Copperfield!' and
shake I by both hand with the utmost fervour.
'Good Heaven, Mr. Traddles!' say Mr. Micawber, 'to think that I
should find you acquaint with the friend of my youth, the
companion of early day! my dear!' call over the banister to
Mrs. Micawber, while traddle look (with reason) not a little
amazed at this description of I. 'here be a gentleman in Mr.
Traddles's apartment, whom he wish to have the pleasure of

Mr. MICAWBER's GAUNTLET
until the day arrive on which I be to entertain my newly-find

with these word, and resist our entreaty that she would grace

poor traddle! I know enough of Mr. Micawber by this time, to

CHAPTER 29
I visit steerforth at his home, again
I mention to Mr. Spenlow in the morning, that I want leave of

we article clerk, as germ of the patrician order of proctor,

when I be ignorant. be it really, though?'
I reply that I like it well enough, but that I certainly could
not claim so much for it.

'well,' I reply; 'perhaps it be a little dry.'

a quick glance of her eye towards the spot where Steerforth be

'donot it - I donot say that it do, mind I want to know - donot

'no?'
'indeed, Miss Dartle, no!'
as she look full at I, I see her face grow sharp and paler,

as she still stand look fixedly at I, a twitching or throbbing,

she say at dinner:

'mysterious!' she cry. 'oh! really? do you consider I so?'

'I wish you would,' say Mrs. Steerforth, with a smile.

'you cannot learn frankness, Rosa,' say Mrs. Steerforth quickly -

'that I be sure of,' she answer, with uncommon fervour. 'if I be

be satisfied about?'
'that I want to be satisfied about?' she reply, with provoking
coldness. 'oh! it be only whether people, who be like each
other in their moral constitution - be that the phrase?'
'itbe as good a phrase as another,' say Steerforth.
'thank you: - whether people, who be like each other in their
moral constitution, be in great danger than people not so
circumstanced, suppose any serious cause of variance to arise
between they, of be divide angrily and deeply?'
'I should say yes,' say Steerforth.
'should you?' she retort. 'dear I! supposing then, for
instance - any unlikely thing will do for a supposition - that you
and your mother be to have a serious quarrel.'
'my dear Rosa,' interpose Mrs. Steerforth, laugh
good-naturedly, 'suggest some other supposition! James and I know
our duty to each other well, I pray Heaven!'
'oh!' say Miss Dartle, nod her head thoughtfully. 'to be
sure. that would prevent it? why, of course it would. exactly.
now, I be glad I have be so foolish as to put the case, for it be
so very good to know that your duty to each other would prevent it!
thank you very much.'
one other little circumstance connect with Miss Dartle I must not
omit; for I have reason to remember it thereafter, when all the
irremediable past be render plain. during the whole of this
day, but especially from this period of it, Steerforth exert
himself with his utmost skill, and that be with his utmost ease,
to charm this singular creature into a pleasant and please
companion. that he should succeed, be no matter of surprise to
I. that she should struggle against the fascinating influence of
his delightful art - delightful nature I think it then - do not
surprise I either; for I know that she be sometimes jaundice and
perverse. I see her feature and her manner slowly change; I see
her look at he with grow admiration; I see she try, more and
more faintly, but always angrily, as if she condemn a weakness in
herself, to resist the captivate power that he possess; and
finally, I see her sharp glance soften, and her smile become quite
gentle, and I cease to be afraid of she as I have really be all
day, and we all sit about the fire, talk and laugh together,
with as little reserve as if we have be child.
whether it be because we have sit there so long, or because
Steerforth be resolve not to lose the advantage he have gain, I
do not know; but we do not remain in the dining-room more than
five minute after her departure. 'she be play her harp,' say
Steerforth, softly, at the drawing-room door, 'and nobody but my
mother have hear she do that, I believe, these three year.' he
say it with a curious smile, which be go directly; and we go
into the room and find she alone.
'donot get up,' say Steerforth (which she have already do)' my
dear Rosa, donot! be kind for once, and sing we an irish song.'
'what do you care for an irish song?' she return.
'much!' say Steerforth. 'much more than for any other. here be
Daisy, too, love music from his soul. sing we an irish song,
Rosa! and let I sit and listen as I use to do.'
he do not touch she, or the chair from which she have rise, but
sit himself near the harp. she stand beside it for some little
while, in a curious way, go through the motion of play it
with her right hand, but not sound it. at length she sit down,
and draw it to she with one sudden action, and play and sang.
I donot know what it be, in her touch or voice, that make that
song the most unearthly I have ever hear in my life, or can
imagine. there be something fearful in the reality of it. it be
as if it have never be write, or set to music, but spring out of
passion within she; which find imperfect utterance in the low
sound of her voice, and crouch again when all be still. I be
dumb when she lean beside the harp again, play it, but not
sound it, with her right hand.
a minute more, and this have rouse I from my trance: - Steerforth
have leave his seat, and go to she, and have put his arm laughingly
about she, and have say, 'come, Rosa, for the future we will love
each other very much!' and she have strike he, and have throw he
off with the fury of a wild cat, and have burst out of the room.
'what be the matter with Rosa?' say Mrs. Steerforth, come in.
'she have be an angel, mother,' return Steerforth, 'for a little
while; and have run into the opposite extreme, since, by way of
compensation.'
'you should be careful not to irritate she, James. her temper have
be sour, remember, and ought not to be try.'
Rosa do not come back; and no other mention be make of she, until

laugh about she, and ask I if I have ever see such a fierce
little piece of incomprehensibility.
I express as much of my astonishment as be then capable of
expression, and ask if he could guess what it be that she have
take so much amiss, so suddenly.
'oh, Heaven know,' say Steerforth. 'anything you like - or
nothing! I tell you she take everything, herself include, to a
grindstone, and sharpen it. she be an edge-tool, and require
great care in deal with. she be always dangerous. good night!'
'good night!' say I, 'my dear Steerforth! I shall be go before
you wake in the morning. good night!'
he be unwilling to let I go; and stand, hold I out, with a
hand on each of my shoulder, as he have do in my own room.
'Daisy,' he say, with a smile - 'for though thatbe not the name
your godfather and godmother give you, itbe the name I like well
to call you by - and I wish, I wish, I wish, you could give it to
I!'
'why so I can, if I choose,' say I.
'Daisy, if anything should ever separate we, you must think of I
at my good, old boy. come! let we make that bargain. think of I
at my good, if circumstance should ever part we!'
'you have no good to I, Steerforth,' say I, 'and no bad. you
be always equally love, and cherish in my heart.'
so much compunction for have ever wrong he, even by a
shapeless thought, do I feel within I, that the confession of
have do so be rise to my lip. but for the reluctance I have
to betray the confidence of Agnes, but for my uncertainty how to
approach the subject with no risk of do so, it would have
reach they before he say, 'God bless you, Daisy, and good
night!' in my doubt, it do not reach they; and we shake hand, and
we part.
I be up with the dull dawn, and, have dress as quietly as I
could, look into his room. he be fast asleep; lie, easily,
with his head upon his arm, as I have often see he lie at school.
the time come in its season, and that be very soon, when I almost
wonder that nothing trouble his repose, as I look at he. but
he sleep - let I think of he so again - as I have often see he
sleep at school; and thus, in this silent hour, I leave he.
- never more, oh God forgive you, Steerforth! to touch that passive
hand in love and friendship. never, never more!
CHAPTER 30
a loss
I get down to Yarmouth in the evening, and go to the inn. I know
that Peggotty's spare room - my room - be likely to have
occupation enough in a little while, if that great Visitor, before
whose presence all the living must give place, be not already in
the house; so I betook myself to the inn, and din there, and
engage my bed.
it be ten o'clock when I go out. many of the shop be shut,
and the town be dull. when I come to Omer and Joram's, I find
the shutter up, but the shop door stand open. as I could
obtain a perspective view of Mr. Omer inside, smoke his pipe by
the parlour door, I enter, and ask he how he be.
'why, bless my life and soul!' say Mr. Omer, 'how do you find
yourself? take a seat. - smoke not disagreeable, I hope?'
'by no mean,' say I. 'I like it - in somebody else's pipe.'
'what, not in your own, eh?' Mr. Omer return, laugh. 'all the
well, sir. bad habit for a young man. take a seat. I smoke,
myself, for the asthma.'
Mr. Omer have make room for I, and place a chair. he now sit down
again very much out of breath, gasp at his pipe as if it
contain a supply of that necessary, without which he must perish.
'I be sorry to have hear bad news of Mr. Barkis,' say I.
Mr. Omer look at I, with a steady countenance, and shake his
head.
'do you know how he be tonight?' I ask.
'the very question I should have put to you, sir,' return Mr.

our line of business. when a party's ill, we cannot ask how the
party be.'
the difficulty have not occur to I; though I have have my
apprehension too, when I go in, of hear the old tune. on its
be mention, I recognize it, however, and say as much.
'yes, yes, you understand,' say Mr. Omer, nod his head. 'we

of party mightnot recover, to say "Omer and Joram's compliment,
and how do you find yourself this morning?" - or this afternoon -
as it may be.'
Mr. Omer and I nod at each other, and Mr. Omer recruit his

'itbe one of the thing that cut the trade off from attention they

forty year. but I cannot go and say, "how be he?"'
I feel it be rather hard on Mr. Omer, and I tell he so.
'Ibe not more self-interested, I hope, than another man,' say Mr.

under such circumstance. I say it benot likely, in a man who
know his wind will go, when it do go, as if a pair of bellow
be cut open; and that man a grandfather,' say Mr. Omer.
I say, 'not at all.'
'it benot that I complain of my line of business,' say Mr. Omer.

Mr. Omer, with a very complacent and amiable face, take several

'accordingly webe obleege, in ascertain how Barkis go on, to

passage, my dear."'
he really have no breath to spare, and it be very alarming to see

'well, sir,' say Mr. Omer, remove his pipe, that he might rub

take place.'
'why so?' I inquire.
'well, shebe unsettle at present,' say Mr. Omer. 'it benot that

she do. she be worth any six, and she be worth any six. but

general way by the expression, "a long pull, and a strong pull, and
a pull altogether, my heartie, hurrah!" I should say to you, that
that be - in a general way - what I miss in Em'ly.'
Mr. Omer's face and manner go for so much, that I could

'now I consider this be principally on account of she be in an

you must always recollect of Em'ly,' say Mr. Omer, shake his

thing. the proverb say, "you cannot make a silk purse out of a

'I be sure she have!' say I.

should it be make a long one than be needful?'
I listen attentively to the good old fellow, and acquiesce, with

their report be, that Mr. Barkis be 'as bad as bad could be';

'this be very kind of you, Mas'r Davy,' say Mr. Peggotty.

she clung the close to he, but neither lift up her face, nor

ham yield to this persuasion, and take his hat to go. even when

he be as mute and senseless as the box, from which his form

'hebe a go out with the tide,' say Mr. Peggotty to I, behind

my eye be dim and so be Mr. Peggotty's; but I repeat in a
