'Barkis be willin'!'
and, it be low water, he go out with the tide.
CHAPTER 31
a great loss
it be not difficult for I, on Peggotty's solicitation, to resolve
to stay where I be, until after the remain of the poor carrier
should have make their last journey to Blunderstone. she have long
ago buy, out of her own saving, a little piece of ground in our
old churchyard near the grave of 'her sweet girl', as she always
call my mother; and there they be to rest.
in keep Peggotty company, and do all I could for she (little
enough at the utmost), I be as grateful, I rejoice to think, as
even now I could wish myself to have be. but I be afraid I have
a supreme satisfaction, of a personal and professional nature, in
take charge of Mr. Barkis's will, and expound its content.
I may claim the merit of have originate the suggestion that the
will should be look for in the box. after some search, it be
find in the box, at the bottom of a horse's nose-bag; wherein
(besides hay) there be discover an old gold watch, with chain
and seal, which Mr. Barkis have wear on his wedding-day, and which
have never be see before or since; a silver tobacco-stopper, in
the form of a leg; an imitation lemon, full of minute cup and
saucer, which I have some idea Mr. Barkis must have purchase to
present to I when I be a child, and afterwards find himself
unable to part with; eighty-seven guineas and a half, in guineas
and half-guineas; two hundred and ten pound, in perfectly clean
Bank note; certain receipt for Bank of England stock; an old
horseshoe, a bad shilling, a piece of camphor, and an oyster-shell.
from the circumstance of the latter article have be much
polished, and display prismatic colour on the inside, I
conclude that Mr. Barkis have some general idea about pearl, which
never resolve themselves into anything definite.
for year and year, Mr. Barkis have carry this box, on all his
journey, every day. that it might the well escape notice, he
have invent a fiction that it belong to 'Mr. Blackboy', and be
'to be leave with Barkis till call for'; a fable he have
elaborately write on the lid, in character now scarcely legible.
he have hoard, all these year, I find, to good purpose. his
property in money amount to nearly three thousand pound. of
this he bequeath the interest of one thousand to Mr. Peggotty for
his life; on his decease, the principal to be equally divide
between Peggotty, little Emily, and I, or the survivor or
survivor of we, share and share alike. all the rest he die
possess of, he bequeath to Peggotty; whom he leave residuary
legatee, and sole executrix of that his last will and testament.
I feel myself quite a proctor when I read this document aloud with
all possible ceremony, and set forth its provision, any number of
time, to those whom they concern. I begin to think there be
more in the Commons than I have suppose. I examine the will with
the deep attention, pronounce it perfectly formal in all
respect, make a pencil-mark or so in the margin, and think it
rather extraordinary that I know so much.
in this abstruse pursuit; in make an account for Peggotty, of all
the property into which she have come; in arrange all the affair
in an orderly manner; and in be her referee and adviser on every
point, to our joint delight; I pass the week before the funeral.
I do not see little Emily in that interval, but they tell I she
be to be quietly marry in a fortnight.
I do not attend the funeral in character, if I may venture to say
so. I mean I be not dress up in a black coat and a streamer, to
frighten the bird; but I walk over to Blunderstone early in the
morning, and be in the churchyard when it come, attend only by
Peggotty and her brother. the mad gentleman look on, out of my
little window; Mr. Chillip's baby wag its heavy head, and roll
its goggle eye, at the clergyman, over its nurse's shoulder; Mr.
Omer breathe short in the background; no one else be there; and
it be very quiet. we walk about the churchyard for an hour,
after all be over; and pull some young leaf from the tree
above my mother's grave.
a dread fall on I here. a cloud be lower on the distant town,
towards which I retrace my solitary step. I fear to approach it.
I cannot bear to think of what do come, upon that memorable night;
of what must come again, if I go on.
it be no bad, because I write of it. it would be no well, if
I stop my most unwilling hand. it be do. nothing can undo
it; nothing can make it otherwise than as it be.
my old nurse be to go to London with I next day, on the business
of the will. little Emily be pass that day at Mr. Omer's. we
be all to meet in the old boathouse that night. ham would bring
Emily at the usual hour. I would walk back at my leisure. the
brother and sister would return as they have come, and be expect
we, when the day close in, at the fireside.
I part from they at the wicket-gate, where visionary Strap have
rest with Roderick Random's knapsack in the day of yore; and,
instead of go straight back, walk a little distance on the
road to Lowestoft. then I turn, and walk back towards
Yarmouth. I stay to dine at a decent alehouse, some mile or two
from the Ferry I have mention before; and thus the day wear away,
and it be evening when I reach it. rain be fall heavily by
that time, and it be a wild night; but there be a moon behind the
cloud, and it be not dark.
I be soon within sight of Mr. Peggotty's house, and of the light
within it shin through the window. a little flounder across
the sand, which be heavy, bring I to the door, and I go in.
it look very comfortable indeed. Mr. Peggotty have smoke his
evening pipe and there be preparation for some supper by and by.
the fire be bright, the ash be throw up, the locker be ready
for little Emily in her old place. in her own old place sit
Peggotty, once more, look (but for her dress) as if she have
never leave it. she have fall back, already, on the society of the
work-box with St. Paul's upon the lid, the yard-measure in the
cottage, and the bit of wax-candle; and there they all be, just
as if they have never be disturb. Mrs. Gummidge appear to be
fret a little, in her old corner; and consequently look quite
natural, too.
'yoube first of the lot, Mas'r Davy!' say Mr. Peggotty with a
happy face. 'Doen't keep in that coat, sir, if itbe wet.'
'thank you, Mr. Peggotty,' say I, give he my outer coat to hang
up. 'itbe quite dry.'
'so 'tis!' say Mr. Peggotty, feel my shoulder. 'as a chip!
sit ye down, sir. it benot o' no use say welcome to you, but
yoube welcome, kind and hearty.'
'thank you, Mr. Peggotty, I be sure of that. well, Peggotty!' say
I, give she a kiss. 'and how be you, old woman?'
'ha, ha!' laugh Mr. Peggotty, sit down beside we, and rub
his hand in his sense of relief from recent trouble, and in the
genuine heartiness of his nature; 'therebe not a woman in the
wureld, sir - as I tell she - that need to feel more easy in her
mind than she! she do she dooty by the depart, and the
depart know'd it; and the depart do what be right by she, as
she do what be right by the depart; - and - and - and itbe all
right!'
Mrs. Gummidge groan.
'cheer up, my pritty mawther!' say Mr. Peggotty. (but he shake
his head aside at we, evidently sensible of the tendency of the
late occurrence to recall the memory of the old one.) 'doen't be
down! cheer up, for your own self, on'y a little bit, and see if
a good deal more doen't come nat'ral!'
'not to I, Dan'l,' return Mrs. Gummidge. 'Nothink's nat'ral to
I but to be lone and lorn.'
'no, no,' say Mr. Peggotty, soothe her sorrow.
'yes, yes, Dan'l!' say Mrs. Gummidge. 'I benot a person to live
with they as have have money leave. think go too contrary with I.
I have well be a riddance.'
'why, how should I ever spend it without you?' say Mr. Peggotty,
with an air of serious remonstrance. 'what be you a talk on?
Doen't I want you more now, than ever I do?'
'I know'd I be never want before!' cry Mrs. Gummidge, with a
pitiable whimper, 'and now Ibe tell so! how could I expect to be
want, be so lone and lorn, and so contrary!'
Mr. Peggotty seem very much shocked at himself for have make a
speech capable of this unfeele construction, but be prevent
from reply, by Peggotty's pull his sleeve, and shake she
head. after look at Mrs. Gummidge for some moment, in sore
distress of mind, he glance at the dutch clock, rise, snuff the
candle, and put it in the window.
'theer!'said Mr. Peggotty, cheerily.'theer we be, Missis
Gummidge!' Mrs. Gummidge slightly groan. 'light up, accordin'
to custom! yoube a wonderin' what thatbe fur, sir! well, itbe
fur our little Em'ly. you see, the path benot over light or
cheerful arter dark; and when Ibe here at the hour as shebe a
comin' home, I put the light in the winder. that, you see,' say
Mr. Peggotty, bend over I with great glee, 'meet two object.
she say, say Em'ly, "Theer's home!" she say. and likewise, say
Em'ly, "my uncle's theer!" Fur if I benot theer, I never have no
light show.'
'yoube a baby!' say Peggotty; very fond of he for it, if she
think so.
'well,' return Mr. Peggotty, stand with his leg pretty wide
apart, and rub his hand up and down they in his comfortable
satisfaction, as he look alternately at we and at the fire. 'I
doen't know but I be. not, you see, to look at.'
'not azackly,' observe Peggotty.
'no,' laugh Mr. Peggotty, 'not to look at, but to - to consider
on, you know. I doen't care, bless you! now I tell you. when I
go a looking and look about that theer pritty house of our
Em'ly's, Ibe - Ibe gorme,' say Mr. Peggotty, with sudden emphasis
- 'theer! I cannot say more - if I doen't feel as if the little
thing be she, a'most. I take them up and I put them down, and I
touch of them as delicate as if they be our Em'ly. so 'tis with
her little bonnet and that. I couldnot see one on them rough use
a purpose - not fur the whole wureld. therebe a babby fur you, in
the form of a great Sea Porkypine!' say Mr. Peggotty, relieve
his earnestness with a roar of laughter.
Peggotty and I both laugh, but not so loud.
'itbe my opinion, you see,' say Mr. Peggotty, with a delighted
face, after some further rubbing of his leg, 'as this be along of
my havin' play with she so much, and make believe as we be
Turks, and French, and shark, and every wariety of forinner -
bless you, yes; and lion and whale, and I doen't know what all!
- when she warn't no high than my knee. Ihave get into the way on
it, you know. why, this here candle, now!' say Mr. Peggotty,
gleefully hold out his hand towards it, 'I know wery well that
arter shebe married and go, I shall put that candle theer, just
the same as now. I know wery well that when Ibe here o' night
(and where else should I live, bless your art, whatever fortun' I
come into!) and she benot here or I benot theer, I shall put the
candle in the winder, and sit afore the fire, pretend Ibe
expect of she, like Ibe a doing now. therebe a babby for you,'
say Mr. Peggotty, with another roar, 'in the form of a Sea
Porkypine! why, at the present minute, when I see the candle
sparkle up, I say to myself, "shebe a looking at it! Em'ly's a
come!" therebe a babby for you, in the form of a Sea Porkypine!
right for all that,' say Mr. Peggotty, stop in his roar, and
smite his hand together; 'fur here she be!'
it be only Ham. the night should have turn more wet since I
come in, for he have a large sou'wester hat on, slouch over his
face.
'Wheer's Em'ly?' say Mr. Peggotty.
Ham make a motion with his head, as if she be outside. Mr.
Peggotty take the light from the window, trim it, put it on the
table, and be busily stir the fire, when Ham, who have not
move, say:
'Mas'r Davy, will you come out a minute, and see what Em'ly and I
have get to show you?'
we go out. as I pass he at the door, I see, to my
astonishment and fright, that he be deadly pale. he push I
hastily into the open air, and close the door upon we. only upon
we two.
'ham! whatbe the matter?'
'Mas'r Davy! -' oh, for his broken heart, how dreadfully he weep!
I be paralyse by the sight of such grief. I donot know what I
think, or what I dread. I could only look at he.
'Ham! poor good fellow! for Heaven's sake, tell I whatbe the
matter!'
'my love, Mas'r Davy - the pride and hope of my art - she that Iwould
have die for, and would die for now - shebe go!'
'go!'
'Em'ly's run away! oh, Mas'r Davy, think how shebe run away, when
I pray my good and gracious God to kill she (she that be so dear
above all thing) soon than let she come to ruin and disgrace!'
the face he turn up to the troubled sky, the quivering of his
clasp hand, the agony of his figure, remain associated with the
lonely waste, in my remembrance, to this hour. it be always night
there, and he be the only object in the scene.
'yoube a scholar,' he say, hurriedly, 'and know whatbe right and
well. what be I to say, indoor? how be I ever to break it to
he, Mas'r Davy?'
I see the door move, and instinctively try to hold the latch on
the outside, to gain a moment's time. it be too late. Mr.
Peggotty thrust forth his face; and never could I forget the change
that come upon it when he see we, if I be to live five hundred
year.
I remember a great wail and cry, and the woman hang about he,
and we all stand in the room; I with a paper in my hand, which
Ham have give I; Mr. Peggotty, with his vest tear open, his hair
wild, his face and lip quite white, and blood trickle down his
bosom (it have spring from his mouth, I think), look fixedly at
I.
'read it, sir,' he say, in a low shivering voice. 'slow, please.
I doen't know as I can understand.'
in the midst of the silence of death, I read thus, from a blot
letter:
'"when you, who love I so much well than I ever have deserve,
even when my mind be innocent, see this, I shall be far away."'
'I shall be fur away,' he repeat slowly. 'stop! Em'ly fur away.
well!'
'"when I leave my dear home - my dear home - oh, my dear home! - in
the morning,"'
the letter bore date on the previous night:
'"- it will be never to come back, unless he bring I back a lady.
this will be find at night, many hour after, instead of I. oh,
if you know how my heart be tear. if even you, that I have wrong
so much, that never can forgive I, could only know what I suffer!
I be too wicked to write about myself! oh, take comfort in
think that I be so bad. oh, for mercy's sake, tell uncle that
I never love he half so dear as now. oh, donot remember how
affectionate and kind you have all be to I - donot remember we
be ever to be marry - but try to think as if I die when I be
little, and be bury somewhere. pray Heaven that I be go away
from, have compassion on my uncle! tell he that I never love he
half so dear. be his comfort. love some good girl that will be
what I be once to uncle, and be true to you, and worthy of you,
and know no shame but I. God bless all! Iwill pray for all,
often, on my knee. if he donot bring I back a lady, and I donot
pray for my own self, Iwill pray for all. my parting love to uncle.
my last tear, and my last thank, for uncle!"'
that be all.
he stand, long after I have cease to read, still look at I. at
length I venture to take his hand, and to entreat he, as well as
I could, to endeavour to get some command of himself. he reply,
'I thankee, sir, I thankee!' without move.
Ham speak to he. Mr. Peggotty be so far sensible of his
affliction, that he wring his hand; but, otherwise, he remain in
the same state, and no one dare to disturb he.
slowly, at last, he move his eye from my face, as if he be
wake from a vision, and cast they round the room. then he say,
in a low voice:
'whobe the man? I want to know his name.'
Ham glance at I, and suddenly I feel a shock that strike I back.
'therebe a man suspect,' say Mr. Peggotty. 'who be it?'
'Mas'r Davy!' implore Ham. 'go out a bit, and let I tell he
what I must. you doen't ought to hear it, sir.'
I feel the shock again. I sink down in a chair, and try to utter
some reply; but my tongue be fetter, and my sight be weak.
'I want to know his name!' I hear say once more.
'for some time past,' ham falter, 'therebe be a servant about
here, at odd time. therebe be a gen'lm'n too. both of them
belong to one another.'
Mr. Peggotty stand fix as before, but now look at he.
'the servant,' pursue Ham, 'be see along with - our poor girl -
last night. hebe be in hiding about here, this week or over. he
be think to have go, but he be hide. Doen't stay, Mas'r
Davy, doen't!'
I feel Peggotty's arm round my neck, but I could not have move if
the house have be about to fall upon I.
'a strange chay and hosse be outside town, this morning, on the
Norwich road, a'most afore the day break,' ham go on. 'the
servant go to it, and come from it, and go to it again. when
he go to it again, Em'ly be nigh he. the t'other be inside.
hebe the man.'
'for the Lord's love,' say Mr. Peggotty, fall back, and put
out his hand, as if to keep off what he dread. 'Doen't tell I
his name's Steerforth!'
'Mas'r Davy,' exclaim Ham, in a broken voice, 'it benot no fault
of yourn - and I be far from lay of it to you - but his name be
Steerforth, and hebe a damned villain!'
Mr. Peggotty utter no cry, and shed no tear, and move no more,
until he seem to wake again, all at once, and pull down his
rough coat from its peg in a corner.
'bear a hand with this! Ibe strike of a heap, and cannot do it,' he
say, impatiently. 'bear a hand and help I. well!' when somebody
have do so. 'now give I that theer hat!'
Ham ask he whither he be go.
'Ibe a go to seek my niece. Ibe a go to seek my Em'ly. Ibe
a going, first, to stave in that theer boat, and sink it where I
would have drownde he, as Ibe a live soul, if I have have one
thought of what be in he! as he sit afore I,' he say, wildly,
hold out his clenched right hand, 'as he sit afore I, face to
face, strike I down dead, but Iwould have drownde he, and think
it right! - Ibe a go to seek my niece.'
'where?' cry Ham, interpose himself before the door.
'anywhere! Ibe a go to seek my niece through the wureld. Ibe
a go to find my poor niece in her shame, and bring she back. no
one stop I! I tell you Ibe a go to seek my niece!'
'no, no!' cry Mrs. Gummidge, come between they, in a fit of
cry. 'no, no, Dan'l, not as you be now. seek she in a little
while, my lone lorn Dan'l, and thatwill be but right! but not as you
be now. sit ye down, and give I your forgiveness for have ever
be a worrit to you, Dan'l - what have my contrary ever be to
this! - and let we speak a word about they time when she be first
an orphan, and when Ham be too, and when I be a poor widder
woman, and you take I in. itwill soften your poor heart, Dan'l,'
lay her head upon his shoulder, 'and youwill bear your sorrow
well; for you know the promise, Dan'l, "as you have do it unto
one of the least of these, you have do it unto me",- and that can
never fail under this roof, thatbe be our shelter for so many,
many year!'
he be quite passive now; and when I hear he cry, the impulse
that have be upon I to go down upon my knee, and ask their
pardon for the desolation I have cause, and curse Steer- forth,
yield to a well feeling, my overcharged heart find the same
relief, and I cry too.
CHAPTER 32
the beginning of a long journey
what be natural in I, be natural in many other man, I infer, and
so I be not afraid to write that I never have love Steerforth
well than when the tie that bind I to he be break. in the
keen distress of the discovery of his unworthiness, I think more
of all that be brilliant in he, I soften more towards all that
be good in he, I do more justice to the quality that might
have make he a man of a noble nature and a great name, than ever
I have do in the height of my devotion to he. deeply as I feel
my own unconscious part in his pollution of an honest home, I
believe that if I have be bring face to face with he, I could
not have utter one reproach. I should have love he so well
still - though he fascinate I no long - I should have hold in
so much tenderness the memory of my affection for he, that I think
I should have be as weak as a spirit-wound child, in all but
the entertainment of a thought that we could ever be re-united.
that think I never have. I feel, as he have feel, that all be at
an end between we. what his remembrance of I be, I have never
know - they be light enough, perhaps, and easily dismiss - but
mine of he be as the remembrance of a cherished friend, who be
dead.
yes, Steerforth, long remove from the scene of this poor history!
my sorrow may bear involuntary witness against you at the judgement
Throne; but my angry thought or my reproach never will, I know!
the news of what have happen soon spread through the town;
insomuch that as I pass along the street next morning, I
overhear the people speak of it at their door. many be hard
upon she, some few be hard upon he, but towards her second
father and her lover there be but one sentiment. among all kind
of people a respect for they in their distress prevail, which be
full of gentleness and delicacy. the seafaring man keep apart,
when those two be see early, walk with slow step on the
beach; and stand in knot, talk compassionately among
themselves.
it be on the beach, close down by the sea, that I find they. it
would have be easy to perceive that they have not sleep all last
night, even if Peggotty have fail to tell I of their still
sit just as I leave they, when it be broad day. they look
worn; and I think Mr. Peggotty's head be bow in one night more
than in all the year I have know he. but they be both as grave
and steady as the sea itself, then lie beneath a dark sky,
waveless - yet with a heavy roll upon it, as if it breathe in its
rest - and touch, on the horizon, with a strip of silvery light
from the unseen sun.
'we have have a mort of talk, sir,' say Mr. Peggotty to I, when we
have all three walk a little while in silence, 'of what we ought
and doen't ought to do. but we see our course now.'
I happen to glance at Ham, then look out to sea upon the
distant light, and a frightful thought come into my mind - not that
his face be angry, for it be not; I recall nothing but an
expression of stern determination in it - that if ever he
encounter Steerforth, he would kill he.
'my dooty here, sir,' say Mr. Peggotty, 'be do. Ibe a go to
seek my -' he stop, and go on in a firm voice: 'Ibe a go
to seek she. thatbe my dooty evermore.'
he shake his head when I ask he where he would seek she, and
inquire if I be go to London tomorrow? I tell he I have not
go today, fear to lose the chance of be of any service to
he; but that I be ready to go when he would.
'Iwill go along with you, sir,' he rejoin, 'if yoube agreeable,
tomorrow.'
we walk again, for a while, in silence.
'Ham,'he presently resumed,'he'll hold to his present work, and go
and live along with my sister. the old boat yonder -'
'will you desert the old boat, Mr. Peggotty?' I gently interpose.
'my station, Mas'r Davy,' he return, 'benot there no long; and
if ever a boat founder, since there be darkness on the face of
the deep, that onebe go down. but no, sir, no; I doen't mean as
it should be desert. fur from that.'
we walk again for a while, as before, until he explain:
'my wish be, sir, as it shall look, day and night, winter and
summer, as it have always look, since she fust know'd it. if ever
she should come a wandering back, I wouldnot have the old place
seem to cast she off, you understand, but seem to tempt she to draw
nigh to 't, and to peep in, maybe, like a ghost, out of the wind
and rain, through the old winder, at the old seat by the fire.
then, maybe, Mas'r Davy, seein' none but Missis Gummidge there, she
might take heart to creep in, tremble; and might come to be lay
down in her old bed, and rest her weary head where it be once so
gay.'
I could not speak to he in reply, though I try.
'every night,' say Mr. Peggotty, 'as reg'lar as the night come,
the candle must be stand in its old pane of glass, that if ever she
should see it, it may seem to say "come back, my child, come back!"
if ever therebe a knock, Ham (partic'ler a soft knock), arter dark,
at your aunt's door, doen't you go nigh it. let it be she - not
you - that see my fall child!'
he walk a little in front of we, and keep before we for some
minute. during this interval, I glance at Ham again, and
observe the same expression on his face, and his eye still
direct to the distant light, I touch his arm.
twice I call he by his name, in the tone in which I might have
try to rouse a sleeper, before he heed I. when I at last
inquire on what his thought be so bent, he reply:
'on whatbe afore I, Mas'r Davy; and over yon.'
'on the life before you, do you mean?' he have point confusedly
out to sea.
'ay, Mas'r Davy. I doen't rightly know how 'tis, but from over yon
there seem to I to come - the end of it like,' look at I as
if he be wake, but with the same determined face.
'what end?' I ask, possess by my former fear.
'I doen't know,'he say, thoughtfully; 'I be call to mind that
the beginning of it all do take place here - and then the end
come. but itbe go! Mas'r Davy,' he add; answer, as I
think, my look; 'you han't no call to be afeerd of I: but Ibe
kiender muddle; I donot fare to feel no matter,' - which be as
much as to say that he be not himself, and quite confound.
Mr. Peggotty stop for we to join he: we do so, and say no
more. the remembrance of this, in connexion with my former
thought, however, haunt I at interval, even until the
inexorable end come at its appoint time.
we insensibly approach the old boat, and enter. Mrs. Gummidge,
no long mop in her especial corner, be busy prepare
breakfast. she take Mr. Peggotty's hat, and place his seat for
he, and speak so comfortably and softly, that I hardly know she.
'Dan'l, my good man,' say she, 'you must eat and drink, and keep
up your strength, for without it youwill do nowt. try, thatbe a
dear soul! an if I disturb you with my clicketten,' she mean she
chattering, 'tell I so, Dan'l, and I willnot.'
when she have serve we all, she withdraw to the window, where she
sedulously employ herself in repair some shirt and other
clothe belong to Mr. Peggotty, and neatly fold and pack
they in an old oilskin bag, such as sailor carry. meanwhile, she
continue talk, in the same quiet manner:
'all time and season, you know, Dan'l,' say Mrs. Gummidge, 'I
shall be allus here, and everythink will look accordin' to your
wish. Ibe a poor scholar, but I shall write to you, odd time,
when yoube away, and send my letter to Mas'r Davy. maybe youwill
write to I too, Dan'l, odd time, and tell I how you fare to feel
upon your lone lorn journie.'
'youwill be a solitary woman heer, Ibe afeerd!' say Mr. Peggotty.
'no, no, Dan'l,' she return, 'I shallnot be that. doen't you mind
I. I shall have enough to do to keep a Beein for you' (Mrs.
Gummidge mean a home), 'again you come back - to keep a Beein here
for any that may hap to come back, Dan'l. in the fine time, I
shall set outside the door as I use to do. if any should come
nigh, they shall see the old widder woman true to them, a long way
off.'
what a change in Mrs. Gummidge in a little time! she be another
woman. she be so devoted, she have such a quick perception of what
it would be well to say, and what it would be well to leave unsaid;
she be so forgetful of herself, and so regardful of the sorrow
about she, that I hold she in a sort of veneration. the work she
do that day! there be many thing to be bring up from the
beach and store in the outhouse - as oar, net, sail, cordage,
spar, lobster-pot, bag of ballast, and the like; and though
there be abundance of assistance render, there be not a pair
of work hand on all that shore but would have labour hard for
Mr. Peggotty, and be well pay in be ask to do it, yet she
persist, all day long, in toil under weight that she be
quite unequal to, and fag to and fro on all sort of
unnecessary errand. as to deplore her misfortune, she appear
to have entirely lose the recollection of ever have have any. she
preserve an equable cheerfulness in the midst of her sympathy,
which be not the least astonishing part of the change that have
come over she. Querulousness be out of the question. I do not
even observe her voice to falter, or a tear to escape from her
eye, the whole day through, until twilight; when she and I and Mr.
Peggotty be alone together, and he have fall asleep in
perfect exhaustion, she break into a half-suppress fit of sobbing
and cry, and take I to the door, say, 'ever bless you, Mas'r
Davy, be a friend to he, poor dear!' then, she immediately run out
of the house to wash her face, in order that she might sit quietly
beside he, and be find at work there, when he should awake. in
short I leave she, when I go away at night, the prop and staff of
Mr. Peggotty's affliction; and I could not meditate enough upon the
lesson that I read in Mrs. Gummidge, and the new experience she
unfold to I.
it be between nine and ten o'clock when, stroll in a melancholy
manner through the town, I stop at Mr. Omer's door. Mr. Omer
have take it so much to heart, his daughter tell I, that he have
be very low and poorly all day, and have go to bed without his
pipe.
'a deceitful, bad-hearted girl,' say Mrs. Joram. 'there be no
good in she, ever!'
'donot say so,' I return. 'you donot think so.'
'yes, I do!' cry Mrs. Joram, angrily.
'no, no,' say I.
Mrs. Joram toss her head, endeavour to be very stern and
cross; but she could not command her soft self, and begin to cry.
I be young, to be sure; but I think much the well of she for
this sympathy, and fancy it become she, as a virtuous wife and
mother, very well indeed.
'what will she ever do!' sob Minnie. 'where will she go! what
will become of she! oh, how could she be so cruel, to herself and
he!'
I remember the time when Minnie be a young and pretty girl; and
I be glad she remember it too, so feelingly.
'my little Minnie,' say Mrs. Joram, 'have only just now be get to
sleep. even in her sleep she be sob for Em'ly. all day long,
little Minnie have cry for she, and ask I, over and over again,
whether Em'ly be wicked? what can I say to she, when Em'ly tie
a ribbon off her own neck round little Minnie's the last night she
be here, and lay her head down on the pillow beside she till she
be fast asleep! the ribbon's round my little Minnie's neck now.
it ought not to be, perhaps, but what can I do? Em'ly be very bad,
but they be fond of one another. and the child know nothing!'
Mrs. Joram be so unhappy that her husband come out to take care of
she. leave they together, I go home to Peggotty's; more
melancholy myself, if possible, than I have be yet.
that good creature - I mean Peggotty - all untire by her late
anxiety and sleepless night, be at her brother's, where she
mean to stay till morning. an old woman, who have be employ
about the house for some week past, while Peggotty have be unable
to attend to it, be the house's only other occupant besides
myself. as I have no occasion for her service, I send she to bed,
by no mean against her will, and sit down before the kitchen fire
a little while, to think about all this.
I be blend it with the deathbed of the late Mr. Barkis, and be
drive out with the tide towards the distance at which Ham have
look so singularly in the morning, when I be recall from my
wandering by a knock at the door. there be a knocker upon the
door, but it be not that which make the sound. the tap be from
a hand, and low down upon the door, as if it be give by a child.
it make I start as much as if it have be the knock of a footman
to a person of distinction. I open the door; and at first look
down, to my amazement, on nothing but a great umbrella that
appear to be walk about of itself. but presently I discover
underneath it, Miss Mowcher.
I might not have be prepare to give the little creature a very
kind reception, if, on she remove the umbrella, which her utmost
effort be unable to shut up, she have show I the 'volatile'
expression of face which have make so great an impression on I at
our first and last meeting. but her face, as she turn it up to
mine, be so earnest; and when I relieve she of the umbrella
(which would have be an inconvenient one for the Irish Giant),
she wring she little hand in such an afflicted manner; that I
rather inclined towards she.
'Miss Mowcher!' say I, after glance up and down the empty
street, without distinctly know what I expect to see besides;
'how do you come here? what be the matter?'
she motion to I with her short right arm, to shut the umbrella
for she; and pass I hurriedly, go into the kitchen. when I
have close the door, and follow, with the umbrella in my hand, I
find she sit on the corner of the fender - it be a low iron
one, with two flat bar at top to stand plate upon - in the shadow
of the boiler, sway herself backwards and forwards, and chafing
her hand upon her knee like a person in pain.
quite alarmed at be the only recipient of this untimely visit,
and the only spectator of this portentous behaviour, I exclaim
again, 'pray tell I, Miss Mowcher, what be the matter! be you
ill?'
'my dear young soul,' return Miss Mowcher, squeeze her hand
upon her heart one over the other. 'I be ill here, I be very ill.
to think that it should come to this, when I might have know it
and perhaps prevent it, if I havenot be a thoughtless fool!'
again her large bonnet (very disproportionate to the figure) go
backwards and forwards, in her swaying of her little body to and
fro; while a most gigantic bonnet rock, in unison with it, upon
the wall.
'I be surprised,' I begin, 'to see you so distressed and serious'-
when she interrupt I.
'yes, itbe always so!' she say. 'they be all surprised, these
inconsiderate young people, fairly and full grown, to see any
natural feeling in a little thing like I! they make a plaything
of I, use I for their amusement, throw I away when they be
tired, and wonder that I feel more than a toy horse or a wooden
soldier! yes, yes, thatbe the way. the old way!'
'it may be, with other,' I return, 'but I do assure you it be
not with I. perhaps I ought not to be at all surprised to see you
as you be now: I know so little of you. I say, without
consideration, what I think.'
'what can I do?' return the little woman, stand up, and
hold out her arm to show herself. 'see! what I be, my father
be; and my sister be; and my brother be. I have work for sister
and brother these many year - hard, Mr. Copperfield - all day. I
must live. I do no harm. if there be people so unreflecting or
so cruel, as to make a jest of I, what be leave for I to do but to
make a jest of myself, they, and everything? if I do so, for the
time, whose fault be that? mine?'
no. not Miss Mowcher's, I perceive.
'if I have show myself a sensitive dwarf to your false friend,'
pursue the little woman, shake her head at I, with reproachful
earnestness, 'how much of his help or good will do you think I
should ever have have? if little Mowcher (who have no hand, young
gentleman, in the making of herself) address herself to he, or
the like of he, because of her misfortune, when do you suppose
her small voice would have be hear? little Mowcher would have
as much need to live, if she be the bitter and dull of
pigmy; but she couldnot do it. no. she might whistle for her
bread and butter till she die of Air.'
Miss Mowcher sit down on the fender again, and take out her
handkerchief, and wipe her eye.
'be thankful for I, if you have a kind heart, as I think you
have,' she say, 'that while I know well what I be, I can be
cheerful and endure it all. I be thankful for myself, at any rate,
that I can find my tiny way through the world, without be
beholden to anyone; and that in return for all that be throw at
I, in folly or vanity, as I go along, I can throw bubble back.
if I donot brood over all I want, it be the well for I, and not
the bad for anyone. if I be a plaything for you giant, be
gentle with I.'
Miss Mowcher replace her handkerchief in her pocket, look at I
with very intent expression all the while, and pursue:
'I see you in the street just now. you may suppose I be not able
to walk as fast as you, with my short leg and short breath, and I
couldnot overtake you; but I guess where you come, and come after
you. I have be here before, today, but the good woman benot at
home.'
'do you know she?' I demand.
'I know of she, and about she,' she reply, 'from Omer and Joram.
I be there at seven o'clock this morning. do you remember what
Steerforth say to I about this unfortunate girl, that time when
I see you both at the inn?'
the great bonnet on Miss Mowcher's head, and the great bonnet on
the wall, begin to go backwards and forwards again when she ask
this question.
I remember very well what she refer to, have have it in my
thought many time that day. I tell she so.
'may the Father of all evil confound he,' say the little woman,
hold up her forefinger between I and her sparkle eye, 'and
ten time more confound that wicked servant; but I believe it be
you who have a boyish passion for she!'
'I?' I repeat.
'child, child! in the name of blind ill-fortune,' cry Miss
Mowcher, wring her hand impatiently, as she go to and fro
again upon the fender, 'why do you praise she so, and blush, and
look disturbed?'
I could not conceal from myself that I have do this, though for a
reason very different from her supposition.
'what do I know?' say Miss Mowcher, take out her handkerchief
again, and give one little stamp on the ground whenever, at short
interval, she apply it to her eye with both hand at once. 'he
be cross you and wheedle you, I see; and you be soft wax in
his hand, I see. have I leave the room a minute, when his man tell
I that "Young Innocence" (so he call you, and you may call he
"Old Guilt" all the day of your life) have set his heart upon she,
and she be giddy and like he, but his master be resolve that
no harm should come of it - more for your sake than for hers - and
that that be their business here? how could I but believe he?
I see Steerforth soothe and please you by his praise of she! you
be the first to mention her name. you own to an old admiration
of she. you be hot and cold, and red and white, all at once when
I speak to you of she. what could I think - what do I think - but
that you be a young libertine in everything but experience, and
have fall into hand that have experience enough, and could manage
you (have the fancy) for your own good? oh! oh! oh! they be
afraid of my find out the truth,' exclaim Miss Mowcher,
get off the fender, and trot up and down the kitchen with
her two short arm distressfully lift up, 'because I be a sharp
little thing - I need be, to get through the world at all! - and
they deceive I altogether, and I give the poor unfortunate girl
a letter, which I fully believe be the beginning of she ever
speak to Littimer, who be leave behind on purpose!'
I stand amazed at the revelation of all this perfidy, look at
Miss Mowcher as she walk up and down the kitchen until she be
out of breath: when she sit upon the fender again, and, dry she
face with her handkerchief, shake her head for a long time, without
otherwise move, and without break silence.
'my country round,' she add at length, 'bring I to Norwich,
Mr. Copperfield, the night before last. what I happen to find
there, about their secret way of come and go, without you -
which be strange - lead to my suspecting something wrong. I get
into the coach from London last night, as it come through Norwich,
and be here this morning. oh, oh, oh! too late!'
poor little Mowcher turn so chilly after all her cry and
fretting, that she turn round on the fender, put her poor
little wet foot in among the ash to warm they, and sit look at
the fire, like a large doll. I sit in a chair on the other side of
the hearth, lose in unhappy reflection, and look at the fire
too, and sometimes at she.
'I must go,' she say at last, rise as she speak. 'itbe late.
you donot mistrust I?'
meet her sharp glance, which be as sharp as ever when she ask
I, I could not on that short challenge answer no, quite frankly.
'come!' say she, accept the offer of my hand to help she over
the fender, and look wistfully up into my face, 'you know you
wouldnot mistrust I, if I be a full-sized woman!'
I feel that there be much truth in this; and I feel rather ashamed
of myself.
'you be a young man,' she say, nod. 'take a word of advice,
even from three foot nothing. try not to associate bodily defect
with mental, my good friend, except for a solid reason.'
she have get over the fender now, and I have get over my suspicion.
I tell she that I believe she have give I a faithful account of
herself, and that we have both be hapless instrument in design
hand. she thank I, and say I be a good fellow.
'now, mind!' she exclaim, turn back on her way to the door,
and look shrewdly at I, with her forefinger up again.- 'I have
some reason to suspect, from what I have hear - my ear be always
open; I cannot afford to spare what power I have - that they be
go abroad. but if ever they return, if ever any one of they
return, while I be alive, I be more likely than another, go
about as I do, to find it out soon. whatever I know, you shall
know. if ever I can do anything to serve the poor betray girl,
I will do it faithfully, please Heaven! and Littimer have well
have a bloodhound at his back, than little Mowcher!'
I place implicit faith in this last statement, when I mark the
look with which it be accompany.
'trust I no more, but trust I no less, than you would trust a
full-sized woman,' say the little creature, touch I
appealingly on the wrist. 'if ever you see I again, unlike what
I be now, and like what I be when you first see I, observe what
company I be in. call to mind that I be a very helpless and
defenceless little thing. think of I at home with my brother like
myself and sister like myself, when my day's work be do. perhaps
you willnot, then, be very hard upon I, or surprised if I can be
distressed and serious. good night!'
I give Miss Mowcher my hand, with a very different opinion of she
from that which I have hitherto entertain, and open the door to
let she out. it be not a trifling business to get the great
umbrella up, and properly balanced in her grasp; but at last I
successfully accomplish this, and see it go bob down the
street through the rain, without the least appearance of have
anybody underneath it, except when a heavy fall than usual from
some over-charge water-spout send it topple over, on one side,
and discover Miss Mowcher struggle violently to get it right.
after make one or two sally to her relief, which be render
futile by the umbrella's hopping on again, like an immense bird,
before I could reach it, I come in, go to bed, and sleep till
morning.
in the morning I be join by Mr. Peggotty and by my old nurse,
and we go at an early hour to the coach office, where Mrs.
Gummidge and Ham be wait to take leave of we.
'Mas'r Davy,' ham whisper, draw I aside, while Mr. Peggotty
be stow his bag among the luggage, 'his life be quite broke up.
he doen't know wheer hebe go; he doen't know -whatbe afore he;
hebe bind upon a voyage thatwill last, on and off, all the rest of
his day, take my wure for 't, unless he find what hebe a seeking
of. I be sure youwill be a friend to he, Mas'r Davy?'
'trust I, I will indeed,' say I, shake hand with Ham
earnestly.
'Thankee. thankee, very kind, sir. one thing furder. Ibe in good
employ, you know, Mas'r Davy, and I han't no way now of spend
what I get. money's of no use to I no more, except to live. if
you can lay it out for he, I shall do my work with a well art.
though as to that, sir,' and he speak very steadily and mildly,
'yoube not to think but I shall work at all time, like a man, and
act the good that lay in my power!'
I tell he I be well convinced of it; and I hint that I hop
the time might even come, when he would cease to lead the lonely
life he naturally contemplate now.
'no, sir,' he say, shake his head, 'all thatbe past and over
with I, sir. no one can never fill the place thatbe empty. but
youwill bear in mind about the money, as theer's at all time some
lay by for he?'
remind he of the fact, that Mr. Peggotty derive a steady,
though certainly a very moderate income from the bequest of his
late brother-in-law, I promise to do so. we then take leave of
each other. I cannot leave he even now, without remember with
a pang, at once his modest fortitude and his great sorrow.
as to Mrs. Gummidge, if I be to endeavour to describe how she run
down the street by the side of the coach, see nothing but Mr.
Peggotty on the roof, through the tear she try to repress, and
dash herself against the people who be come in the opposite
direction, I should enter on a task of some difficulty. therefore
I have well leave she sit on a baker's door-step, out of
breath, with no shape at all remain in her bonnet, and one of
her shoe off, lie on the pavement at a considerable distance.
when we get to our journey's end, our first pursuit be to look
about for a little lodging for Peggotty, where her brother could
have a bed. we be so fortunate as to find one, of a very clean
and cheap description, over a chandler's shop, only two street
remove from I. when we have engage this domicile, I buy some
cold meat at an eating-house, and take my fellow-traveller home to
tea; a proceeding, I regret to state, which do not meet with Mrs.
Crupp's approval, but quite the contrary. I ought to observe,
however, in explanation of that lady's state of mind, that she be
much offend by Peggotty's tuck up her widow's gown before she
have be ten minute in the place, and set to work to dust my
bedroom. this Mrs. Crupp regard in the light of a liberty, and
a liberty, she say, be a thing she never allow.
Mr. Peggotty have make a communication to I on the way to London
for which I be not unprepared. it be, that he purpose first
see Mrs. Steerforth. as I feel bound to assist he in this, and
also to mediate between they; with the view of spar the mother's
feeling as much as possible, I write to she that night. I tell
she as mildly as I could what his wrong be, and what my own share
in his injury. I say he be a man in very common life, but of a
most gentle and upright character; and that I venture to express
a hope that she would not refuse to see he in his heavy trouble.
I mention two o'clock in the afternoon as the hour of our come,
and I send the letter myself by the first coach in the morning.
at the appoint time, we stand at the door - the door of that
house where I have be, a few day since, so happy: where my
youthful confidence and warmth of heart have be yield up so
freely: which be close against I henceforth: which be now a
waste, a ruin.
no Littimer appear. the pleasanter face which have replace his,
on the occasion of my last visit, answer to our summon, and go
before we to the drawing-room. Mrs. Steerforth be sit there.
Rosa Dartle glide, as we go in, from another part of the room
and stand behind her chair.
I see, directly, in his mother's face, that she know from himself
what he have do. it be very pale; and bear the trace of deep
emotion than my letter alone, weaken by the doubt her fondness
would have raise upon it, would have be likely to create. I
think she more like he than ever I have think she; and I feel,
rather than see, that the resemblance be not lose on my companion.
she sit upright in her arm-chair, with a stately, immovable,
passionless air, that it seem as if nothing could disturb. she
look very steadfastly at Mr. Peggotty when he stand before she;
and he look quite as steadfastly at she. Rosa Dartle's keen
glance comprehend all of we. for some moment not a word be
speak.
she motion to Mr. Peggotty to be seat. he say, in a low
voice, 'I shouldnot feel it nat'ral, ma'am, to sit down in this
house. Iwould soon stand.' and this be succeed by another
silence, which she break thus:
'I know, with deep regret, what have bring you here. what do you
want of I? what do you ask I to do?'
he put his hat under his arm, and feel in his breast for Emily's
letter, take it out, unfold it, and give it to she.
'please to read that, ma'am. thatbe my niece's hand!'
she read it, in the same stately and impassive way, - untouched by
its content, as far as I could see, - and return it to he.
'"unless he bring I back a lady,"' say Mr. Peggotty, trace out
that part with his finger. 'I come to know, ma'am, whether he will
keep his wure?'
'no,' she return.
'why not?' say Mr. Peggotty.
'it be impossible. he would disgrace himself. you cannot fail to
know that she be far below he.'
'raise she up!' say Mr. Peggotty.
'she be uneducated and ignorant.'
'maybe shebe not; maybe she be,' say Mr. Peggotty. 'I think not,
ma'am; but Ibe no judge of they thing. teach she well!'
'since you oblige I to speak more plainly, which I be very
unwilling to do, her humble connexion would render such a thing
impossible, if nothing else do.'
'Hark to this, ma'am,' he return, slowly and quietly. 'you know
what it be to love your child. so do I. if she be a hundred
time my child, I couldnot love she more. you doen't know what it
be to lose your child. I do. all the heap of rich in the
wureld would be nowt to I (if they be mine) to buy she back!
but, save she from this disgrace, and she shall never be disgrace
by we. not one of we that shebe grow up among, not one of we
thatbe live along with she and have she for their all in all, these
many year, will ever look upon her pritty face again. wewill be
content to let she be; wewill be content to think of she, far off,
as if she be underneath another sun and sky; wewill be content to
trust she to her husband, - to her little child, p'raps, - and
bide the time when all of we shall be alike in quality afore our
God!'
the rugged eloquence with which he speak, be not devoid of all
effect. she still preserve her proud manner, but there be a
touch of softness in her voice, as she answer:
'I justify nothing. I make no counter-accusation. but I be sorry
to repeat, it be impossible. such a marriage would irretrievably
blight my son's career, and ruin his prospect. nothing be more
certain than that it never can take place, and never will. if
there be any other compensation -'
'I be look at the likeness of the face,' interrupt Mr.
Peggotty, with a steady but a kindling eye, 'that have look at I,
in my home, at my fireside, in my boat - wheer not? - smile and
friendly, when it be so treacherous, that I go half wild when I
think of it. if the likeness of that face donot turn to burn
fire, at the thought of offer money to I for my child's blight
and ruin, itbe as bad. I doen't know, be a lady's, but what
itbe bad.'
she change now, in a moment. an angry flush overspread her
feature; and she say, in an intolerant manner, grasp the
arm-chair tightly with her hand:
'what compensation can you make to I for open such a pit
between I and my son? what be your love to mine? what be your
separation to our?'
Miss Dartle softly touch she, and bent down her head to whisper,
but she would not hear a word.
'no, Rosa, not a word! let the man listen to what I say! my son,
who have be the object of my life, to whom its every thought have
be devote, whom I have gratify from a child in every wish,
from whom I have have no separate existence since his birth, - to
take up in a moment with a miserable girl, and avoid I! to repay
my confidence with systematic deception, for her sake, and quit I
for she! to set this wretched fancy, against his mother's claim
upon his duty, love, respect, gratitude - claim that every day and
hour of his life should have strengthen into tie that nothing
could be proof against! be this no injury?'
again Rosa Dartle try to soothe she; again ineffectually.
'I say, Rosa, not a word! if he can stake his all upon the
light object, I can stake my all upon a great purpose. let
he go where he will, with the mean that my love have secure to
he! do he think to reduce I by long absence? he know his
mother very little if he do. let he put away his whim now, and
he be welcome back. let he not put she away now, and he never
shall come near I, live or die, while I can raise my hand to
make a sign against it, unless, be rid of she for ever, he come
humbly to I and beg for my forgiveness. this be my right. this
be the acknowledgement I will have. this be the separation that
there be between we! and be this,' she add, look at her
visitor with the proud intolerant air with which she have begin, 'no
injury?'
while I hear and see the mother as she say these word, I seem
to hear and see the son, defy they. all that I have ever see in
he of an unyielding, wilful spirit, I see in she. all the
understanding that I have now of his misdirect energy, become an
understanding of her character too, and a perception that it be,
in its strong spring, the same.
she now observe to I, aloud, resume her former restraint, that
it be useless to hear more, or to say more, and that she beg to
put an end to the interview. she rise with an air of dignity to
leave the room, when Mr. Peggotty signify that it be needless.
'Doen't fear I be any hindrance to you, I have no more to say,
ma'am,' he remark, as he move towards the door. 'I come beer
with no hope, and I take away no hope. I have do what I thowt
should be do, but I never look fur any good to come of my
stan'ning where I do. this have be too evil a house fur I and
mine, fur I to be in my right sens and expect it.'
with this, we depart; leave she standing by her elbow-chair, a
picture of a noble presence and a handsome face.
we have, on our way out, to cross a paved hall, with glass side and
roof, over which a vine be train. its leaf and shoot be
green then, and the day be sunny, a pair of glass door lead
to the garden be throw open. Rosa Dartle, enter this way
with a noiseless step, when we be close to they, address
herself to I:
'you do well,' she say, 'indeed, to bring this fellow here!'
such a concentration of rage and scorn as darken her face, and
flash in her jet-black eye, I could not have think
compressible even into that face. the scar make by the hammer be,
as usual in this excited state of her feature, strongly marked.
when the throbbing I have see before, come into it as I look at
she, she absolutely lift up her hand, and strike it.
'this be a fellow,' she say, 'to champion and bring here, be he
not? you be a true man!'
'Miss Dartle,' I return, 'you be surely not so unjust as to
condemn I!'
'why do you bring division between these two mad creature?' she
return. 'donot you know that they be both mad with their own
self-will and pride?'
'be it my doing?' I return.
'be it your doing!' she retort. 'why do you bring this man
here?'
'he be a deeply-injure man, Miss Dartle,' I reply. 'you may not
know it.'
'I know that James Steerforth,' she say, with her hand on her
bosom, as if to prevent the storm that be rag there, from be
loud, 'have a false, corrupt heart, and be a traitor. but what need
I know or care about this fellow, and his common niece?'
'Miss Dartle,' I return, 'you deepen the injury. it be
sufficient already. I will only say, at part, that you do he
a great wrong.'
'I do he no wrong,' she return. 'they be a depraved, worthless
set. I would have she whip!'
Mr. Peggotty pass on, without a word, and go out at the door.
'oh, shame, Miss Dartle! shame!' I say indignantly. 'how can you
bear to trample on his undeserved affliction!'
'I would trample on they all,' she answer. 'I would have his
house pull down. I would have she brand on the face, dress
in rag, and cast out in the street to starve. if I have the power
to sit in judgement on she, I would see it do. see it do? I
would do it! I detest she. if I ever could reproach she with her
infamous condition, I would go anywhere to do so. if I could hunt
she to her grave, I would. if there be any word of comfort that
would be a solace to she in her die hour, and only I possess
it, I wouldnot part with it for life itself.'
the mere vehemence of her word can convey, I be sensible, but a
weak impression of the passion by which she be possess, and
which make itself articulate in her whole figure, though her voice,
instead of be raise, be low than usual. no description I
could give of she would do justice to my recollection of she, or to
her entire deliverance of herself to her anger. I have see
passion in many form, but I have never see it in such a form as
that.
when I join Mr. Peggotty, he be walk slowly and thoughtfully
down the hill. he tell I, as soon as I come up with he, that
have now discharge his mind of what he have purpose do in
London, he mean 'to set out on his travel', that night. I ask
he where he mean to go? he only answer, 'Ibe a going, sir, to
seek my niece.'
we go back to the little lodge over the chandler's shop, and
there I find an opportunity of repeat to Peggotty what he have
say to I. she inform I, in return, that he have say the same
to she that morning. she know no more than I do, where he be
go, but she think he have some project shape out in his mind.
I do not like to leave he, under such circumstance, and we all
three din together off a beefsteak pie - which be one of the
many good thing for which Peggotty be famous - and which be
curiously flavour on this occasion, I recollect well, by a
miscellaneous taste of tea, coffee, butter, bacon, cheese, new
loaf, firewood, candle, and walnut ketchup, continually
ascend from the shop. after dinner we sit for an hour or so
near the window, without talk much; and then Mr. Peggotty get
up, and bring his oilskin bag and his stout stick, and lay they
on the table.
he accept, from his sister's stock of ready money, a small sum on
account of his legacy; barely enough, I should have think, to
keep he for a month. he promise to communicate with I, when
anything befall he; and he sling his bag about he, take his hat
and stick, and bid we both 'good-bye!'
'all good attend you, dear old woman,' he say, embrace Peggotty,
'and you too, Mas'r Davy!' shake hand with I. 'Ibe a-go to
seek she, fur and wide. if she should come home while Ibe away -
but ah, that benot like to be! - or if I should bring she back, my
meaning be, that she and I shall live and die where no one cannot
reproach she. if any hurt should come to I, remember that the
last word I leave for she be, "my unchanged love be with my
darling child, and I forgive she!"'
he say this solemnly, bare-headed; then, put on his hat, he
go down the stair, and away. we follow to the door. it be
a warm, dusty evening, just the time when, in the great main
thoroughfare out of which that by-way turn, there be a temporary
lull in the eternal tread of foot upon the pavement, and a strong
red sunshine. he turn, alone, at the corner of our shady street,
into a glow of light, in which we lose he.
rarely do that hour of the evening come, rarely do I wake at
night, rarely do I look up at the moon, or star, or watch the
fall rain, or hear the wind, but I think of his solitary
figure toil on, poor pilgrim, and recall the word:
'Ibe a go to seek she, fur and wide. if any hurt should come to
I, remember that the last word I leave for she be, "my unchanged
love be with my darling child, and I forgive she!"'
CHAPTER 33
BLISSFUL
all this time, I have go on love Dora, hard than ever. her
idea be my refuge in disappointment and distress, and make some
amend to I, even for the loss of my friend. the more I pity
myself, or pitied other, the more I seek for consolation in the
image of Dora. the great the accumulation of deceit and trouble
in the world, the bright and the purer shine the star of Dora
high above the world. I donot think I have any definite idea where
Dora come from, or in what degree she be relate to a high order
of being; but I be quite sure I should have scout the notion of
she be simply human, like any other young lady, with indignation
and contempt.
if I may so express it, I be steep in Dora. I be not merely
over head and ear in love with she, but I be saturate through
and through. enough love might have be wring out of I,
metaphorically speak, to drown anybody in; and yet there would
have remain enough within I, and all over I, to pervade my
entire existence.
the first thing I do, on my own account, when I come back, be to
take a night-walk to Norwood, and, like the subject of a venerable
riddle of my childhood, to go 'round and round the house, without
ever touch the house', think about Dora. I believe the theme
of this incomprehensible conundrum be the moon. no matter what it
be, I, the moon-strike slave of Dora, perambulate round and round
the house and garden for two hour, look through crevice in the

my love be so much in my mind and it be so natural to I to

'the young lady might think herself well off,' she observe, 'to
have such a beau. and as to she Pa,' she say, 'what do the
gentleman expect, for gracious sake!'
I observe, however, that Mr. Spenlow's proctorial gown and stiff

I despise they, to a man. freeze-out old gardener in the

take the management of Peggotty's affair into my own hand, with

we be a little like undertaker, in the Commons, as regard

the husband, whose name be Thomas Benjamin, have take out his

original will of all person leave effect within the immense
province of Canterbury, for three whole century, should be an
accidental building, never design for the purpose, lease by the
registrar for their own private emolument, unsafe, not even
ascertain to be fire-proof, choke with the important document
it hold, and positively, from the roof to the basement, a mercenary
speculation of the registrar, who take great fee from the public,
and cram the public's will away anyhow and anywhere, have no
other object than to get rid of they cheaply. that, perhaps, it
be a little unreasonable that these registrar in the receipt of
profit amount to eight or nine thousand pound a year (to say
nothing of the profit of the deputy registrar, and clerk of
seat), should not be oblige to spend a little of that money, in
find a reasonably safe place for the important document which
all class of people be compel to hand over to they, whether
they would or no. that, perhaps, it be a little unjust, that all
the great office in this great office should be magnificent
sinecure, while the unfortunate working-clerk in the cold dark
room upstairs be the worst reward, and the least consider

little indecent that the principal registrar of all, whose duty it
be to find the public, constantly resort to this place, all
needful accommodation, should be an enormous sinecurist in virtue
of that post (and might be, besides, a clergyman, a pluralist, the
holder of a staff in a cathedral, and what not), - while the public
be put to the inconvenience of which we have a speciman every
afternoon when the office be busy, and which we know to be quite

diocese of Canterbury be altogether such a pestilent job, and such

corner of St. Paul's Churchyard, which few people know, it must
have be turn completely inside out, and upside down, long ago.

oh dear no! not in the least.

that sagacious Miss Mills, too; that amiable, though quite use up,
recluse; that little patriarch of something less than twenty, who
have do with the world, and mustnot on any account have the
slumber echo in the cavern of memory awaken; what a kind
thing she do!
'Mr. Copperfield,' say Miss Mills, 'come to this side of the
carriage a moment - if you can spare a moment. I want to speak to
you.'
behold I, on my gallant grey, bend at the side of Miss Mills,
with my hand upon the carriage door!
'Dora be come to stay with I. she be come home with I the
day after tomorrow. if you would like to call, I be sure papa
would be happy to see you.'
what could I do but invoke a silent blessing on Miss Mills's head,
and store Miss Mills's address in the securest corner of my memory!
what could I do but tell Miss Mills, with grateful look and
fervent word, how much I appreciate her good office, and what an
inestimable value I set upon her friendship!
then Miss Mills benignantly dismiss I, say, 'go back to
Dora!' and I go; and Dora lean out of the carriage to talk to
I, and we talk all the rest of the way; and I ride my gallant
grey so close to the wheel that I graze his near fore leg against
it, and 'take the bark off', as his owner tell I, 'to the tune of
three pun' sivin' - which I pay, and think extremely cheap for
so much joy. what time Miss Mills sit look at the moon,
murmur verses- and recall, I suppose, the ancient day when
she and earth have anything in common.
Norwood be many mile too near, and we reach it many hour too
soon; but Mr. Spenlow come to himself a little short of it, and
say, 'you must come in, Copperfield, and rest!' and I consent,
we have sandwich and wine-and-water. in the light room, Dora
blushing look so lovely, that I could not tear myself away, but
sit there star, in a dream, until the snoring of Mr. Spenlow
inspire I with sufficient consciousness to take my leave. so we
part; I rid all the way to London with the farewell touch of
Dora's hand still light on mine, recall every incident and word
ten thousand time; lie down in my own bed at last, as enrapture
a young noodle as ever be carry out of his five wit by love.
when I awake next morning, I be resolute to declare my passion to
Dora, and know my fate. happiness or misery be now the question.
there be no other question that I know of in the world, and only
Dora could give the answer to it. I pass three day in a luxury
of wretchedness, torture myself by put every conceivable
variety of discourage construction on all that ever have take
place between Dora and I. at last, array for the purpose at a
vast expense, I go to Miss Mills's, fraught with a declaration.
how many time I go up and down the street, and round the square
- painfully aware of be a much well answer to the old riddle
than the original one - before I could persuade myself to go up the
step and knock, be no matter now. even when, at last, I have
knock, and be wait at the door, I have some flurry thought
of ask if that be Mr. Blackboy's (in imitation of poor
Barkis), beg pardon, and retreat. but I keep my ground.
Mr. Mills be not at home. I do not expect he would be. nobody
want HIM. Miss Mills be at home. Miss Mills would do.
I be show into a room upstairs, where Miss Mills and Dora be.
Jip be there. Miss Mills be copy music (I recollect, it be
a new song, call 'Affection's Dirge'), and Dora be paint
flower. what be my feeling, when I recognize my own flower;
the identical Covent Garden Market purchase! I cannot say that
they be very like, or that they particularly resemble any
flower that have ever come under my observation; but I know from
the paper round they which be accurately copy, what the
composition be.
Miss Mills be very glad to see I, and very sorry her papa be not
at home: though I think we all bear that with fortitude. Miss
Mills be conversational for a few minute, and then, lay down
her pen upon 'Affection's Dirge', get up, and leave the room.
I begin to think I would put it off till tomorrow.
'I hope your poor horse be not tired, when he get home at night,'
say Dora, lift up her beautiful eye. 'it be a long way for
he.'
I begin to think I would do it today.
'it be a long way for he,' say I, 'for he have nothing to uphold
he on the journey.'
'benot he feed, poor thing?' ask Dora.
I begin to think I would put it off till tomorrow.
'ye-yes,' I say, 'he be well take care of. I mean he have not
the unutterable happiness that I have in be so near you.'
Dora bend her head over her drawing and say, after a little while
- I have sit, in the interval, in a burn fever, and with my leg
in a very rigid state -
'you donot seem to be sensible of that happiness yourself, at one
time of the day.'
I see now that I be in for it, and it must be do on the spot.
'you donot care for that happiness in the least,' say Dora,
slightly raise her eyebrow, and shake her head, 'when you be
sit by Miss Kitt.'
Kitt, I should observe, be the name of the creature in pink, with
the little eye.
'though certainly I donot know why you should,' say Dora, or why
you should call it a happiness at all. but of course you donot
mean what you say. and I be sure no one doubt your be at
liberty to do whatever you like. Jip, you naughty boy, come here!'
I donot know how I do it. I do it in a moment. I intercept
Jip. I have Dora in my arm. I be full of eloquence. I never
stop for a word. I tell she how I love she. I tell she I
should die without she. I tell she that I idolize and worship
she. Jip bark madly all the time.
when Dora hang her head and cry, and tremble, my eloquence
increase so much the more. if she would like I to die for she,
she have but to say the word, and I be ready. life without Dora's
love be not a thing to have on any term. I couldnot bear it, and
I wouldnot. I have love she every minute, day and night, since I
first see she. I love she at that minute to distraction. I
should always love she, every minute, to distraction. lover have
love before, and lover would love again; but no lover have love,
might, could, would, or should ever love, as I love Dora. the
more I rave, the more Jip bark. each of we, in his own way, get
more mad every moment.
well, well! Dora and I be sit on the sofa by and by, quiet
enough, and Jip be lie in her lap, wink peacefully at I. it
be off my mind. I be in a state of perfect rapture. Dora and I
be engage.
I suppose we have some notion that this be to end in marriage. we
must have have some, because Dora stipulate that we be never to
be marry without her papa's consent. but, in our youthful
ecstasy, I donot think that we really look before we or behind
we; or have any aspiration beyond the ignorant present. we be to
keep our secret from Mr. Spenlow; but I be sure the idea never
enter my head, then, that there be anything dishonourable in
that.
Miss Mills be more than usually pensive when Dora, go to find
she, bring she back; - I apprehend, because there be a tendency
in what have pass to awaken the slumber echo in the cavern
of memory. but she give we her blessing, and the assurance of her
last friendship, and speak to we, generally, as become a voice
from the Cloister.
what an idle time it be! what an insubstantial, happy, foolish
time it be!
when I measure Dora's finger for a ring that be to be make of
forget-I-not, and when the jeweller, to whom I take the measure,
find I out, and laugh over his order-book, and charge I
anything he like for the pretty little toy, with its blue stone
- so associate in my remembrance with Dora's hand, that yesterday,
when I see such another, by chance, on the finger of my own
daughter, there be a momentary stirring in my heart, like pain!
when I walk about, exalt with my secret, and full of my own
interest, and feel the dignity of love Dora, and of be
beloved, so much, that if I have walk the air, I could not have
be more above the people not so situated, who be creep on
the earth!
when we have those meeting in the garden of the square, and sit
within the dingy summer-house, so happy, that I love the London
sparrow to this hour, for nothing else, and see the plumage of the
tropic in their smoky feather!
when we have our first great quarrel (within a week of our
betrothal), and when Dora send I back the ring, enclose in a
despair cock-hat note, wherein she use the terrible
expression that 'our love have begin in folly, and end in
madness!' which dreadful word occasion I to tear my hair, and
cry that all be over!
when, under cover of the night, I fly to Miss Mills, whom I see by
stealth in a back kitchen where there be a mangle, and implore
Miss Mills to interpose between we and avert insanity. when Miss
Mills undertake the office and return with Dora, exhort we,
from the pulpit of her own bitter youth, to mutual concession, and
the avoidance of the Desert of Sahara!
when we cry, and make it up, and be so blest again, that the
back kitchen, mangle and all, change to Love's own temple, where
we arrange a plan of correspondence through Miss Mills, always to
comprehend at least one letter on each side every day!
what an idle time! what an insubstantial, happy, foolish time! of
all the time of mine that Time have in his grip, there be none that
in one retrospect I can smile at half so much, and think of half so
tenderly.
CHAPTER 34
MY AUNT astonishes ME
I write to Agnes as soon as Dora and I be engage. I write she

be know.

after this, Mrs. Crupp confine herself to make pitfall on the

'my dear copperfield,' cry traddle, punctually appear at my

'mine, perhaps you recollect,' say traddle, with a serious look,

he see, I suppose, that I could not help smile at the simplicity

'I hope so, aunt.'
'what do you think?' inquire Miss Betsey.

'because,' say my aunt, 'itbe all I have. because Ibe ruin, my

if the house, and every one of we, have tumble out into the river

'we must meet revers boldly, and not suffer they to frighten we,

misfortune down, Trot!'
CHAPTER 35
DEPRESSION
as soon as I could recover my presence of mind, which quite
desert I in the first overpowering shock of my aunt's
intelligence, I propose to Mr. Dick to come round to the
chandler's shop, and take possession of the bed which Mr. Peggotty
have lately vacate. the chandler's shop be in Hungerford
Market, and Hungerford Market be a very different place in those
day, there be a low wooden colonnade before the door (not very
unlike that before the house where the little man and woman use to
live, in the old weather-glass), which please Mr. Dick mightily.
the glory of lodge over this structure would have compensate
he, I dare say, for many inconvenience; but, as there be really
few to bear, beyond the compound of flavour I have already

perfectly charm with his accommodation. Mrs. Crupp have
indignantly assure he that there benot room to swing a cat
there; but, as Mr. Dick justly observe to I, sit down on the
foot of the bed, nurse his leg, 'you know, Trotwood, I donot want
to swing a cat. I never do swing a cat. therefore, what do that
signify to I!'
I try to ascertain whether Mr. Dick have any understanding of the
cause of this sudden and great change in my aunt's affair. as I
might have expect, he have none at all. the only account he could
give of it be, that my aunt have say to he, the day before
yesterday, 'now, Dick, be you really and truly the philosopher I
take you for?' that then he have say, yes, he hop so. that then
my aunt have say, 'Dick, I be ruin.' that then he have say, 'oh,
indeed!' that then my aunt have praise he highly, which he be
glad of. and that then they have come to I, and have have bottle
porter and sandwich on the road.
Mr. Dick be so very complacent, sit on the foot of the bed,
nurse his leg, and tell I this, with his eye wide open and
a surprised smile, that I be sorry to say I be provoke into
explain to he that ruin mean distress, want, and starvation;
but I be soon bitterly reprove for this harshness, by see his
face turn pale, and tear course down his lengthened cheek, while

be so confident, merely because of his faith in the wise and
most wonderful of woman, and his unbounded reliance on my
intellectual resource. the latter, I believe, he consider a
match for any kind of disaster not absolutely mortal.
'what can we do, trotwood?' say Mr. Dick. 'therebe the Memorial
-'
'to be sure there be,' say I. 'but all we can do just now, Mr.
Dick, be to keep a cheerful countenance, and not let my aunt see
that we be think about it.'
he assent to this in the most earnest manner; and implore I, if

have reach an advanced stage of attenuation.
my aunt, on the other hand, be in a compose frame of mind, which

be so near the river, in case of a conflagration; and I suppose
really do find some satisfaction in that circumstance.
'Trot, my dear,' say my aunt, when she see I make preparation
for compound her usual night-draught, 'no!'
'nothing, aunt?'
'not wine, my dear. Ale.'
'but there be wine here, aunt. and you always have it make of
wine.'

I think Mr. Dick would have fall, insensible. my aunt being

monument of human misery.
my aunt be walk up and down the room when I return, crimp

'my dear,' say my aunt, after take a spoonful of it; 'itbe a
great deal well than wine. not half so bilious.'
I suppose I look doubtful, for she add:
'tut, tut, child. if nothing bad than Ale happen to we, we be
well off.'
'I should think so myself, aunt, I be sure,' say I.

my aunt go on with a quiet enjoyment, in which there be very

affect to laugh, she get an opportunity of put her hand to

I seriously believe it have never once enter my head for a single

this be not upon the whole very comforting to a rapturous lover;

how miserable I be, when I lie down! how I think and think

as to sleep, I have dream of poverty in all sort of shape, but I

now I be rag, want to sell Dora match, six bundle for a

my aunt be restless, too, for I frequently hear she walk to

it be difficult to believe that a night so long to I, could be

there be an old roman bath in those day at the bottom of one of

and through a pleasant smell of summer flower, grow in garden
and carry into town on huckster' head, intent on this first
effort to meet our altered circumstance.
I arrive at the office so soon, after all, that I have half an

'how be you, Copperfield?' say he. 'fine morning!'
'beautiful morning, sir,' say I. 'could I say a word to you

'by all mean,' say he. 'come into my room.'
I follow he into his room, and he begin put on his gown, and

'no!' say he. 'dear I! not paralysis, I hope?'
'it have no reference to her health, sir,' I reply. 'she have meet

'you as-tound I, Copperfield!' cry Mr. Spenlow.

what it cost I to make this proposal, nobody know. it be like

I explain with tolerable firmness, that I really do not know

avail myself of this permission, which be give with a warm

I be try to familiarize my mind with the bad, and to present

she be go to my room to see my aunt. the day be very fine,

'and now they be partner,' say I. 'confound he!'

'they?' say I.

I look at Agnes when she say these word, without detect in

a certain bright smile, which I never see on any other face, die

we begin to talk about my aunt's loss, and I tell they what I have
try to do that morning.
'which be injudicious, Trot,' say my aunt, 'but well mean. you

stand.'
I observe Agnes turn pale, as she look very attentively at my

that do very well, and return very good interest, till Betsey

right, she lose in the banking way. I donot know what the Bank
share be worth for a little while,' say my aunt; 'cent per cent
be the low of it, I believe; but the Bank be at the other end
of the world, and tumble into space, for what I know; anyhow, it
fall to piece, and never will and never can pay sixpence; and
Betsey's sixpence be all there, and therebe an end of they.
Least say, soon mend!'
my aunt conclude this philosophical summary, by fix her eye
with a kind of triumph on Agnes, whose colour be gradually
return.
'dear Miss Trotwood, be that all the history?' say Agnes.
'I hope itbe enough, child,' say my aunt. 'if there have be more
money to lose, it wouldnot have be all, I dare say. Betsey would
have contrive to throw that after the rest, and make another
chapter, I have little doubt. but there be no more money, and
therebe no more story.'
Agnes have listen at first with suspend breath. her colour

might be in some way to blame for what have happen. my aunt take

'be that all?' repeat my aunt. 'why, yes, thatbe all, except,
"and she live happy ever afterwards." perhaps I may add that of

'I say, aunt,' I interpose, 'that I must do something!'

I be about to explain that I be not desirous of introduce that

people out of six would die - of course - of that woman in nankeen
with the flannel petticoat. I have a little ready money; and I
agree with you, the good thing we can do, be, to live the term out
here, and get a bedroom hard by.'
I think it my duty to hint at the discomfort my aunt would

prepared to astonish Mrs. Crupp for the whole remainder of her
natural life.

and to and fro upon the Norwood Road, 'I have abundance of time.'

Wherever Agnes be, some agreeable token of her noiseless presence
