my aunt be quite gracious on the subject of the Thames (it really

he appear to be only too conscious of it himself. when he come

what my aunt see, or do not see, I defy the science of physiognomy

her face might have be a dead-wall on the occasion in question,

'well, Wickfield!' say my aunt; and he look up at she for the

consider. Agnes be worth the whole firm, in my opinion.'
'if I may umbly make the remark,' say Uriah Heep, with a writhe,

appy if Miss Agnes be a partner.'

thatbe about enough for you, I expect. how do you find yourself,

in acknowledgement of this question, address to he with

here he shake hand with I: not in the common way, but stand at

he jerk himself about, after this compliment, in such an

'deuce take the man!' say my aunt, sternly, 'whatbe he about?

Mr. Heep be rather abash, as most people might have be, by

'Uriah Heep,' say Mr. Wickfield, in a monotonous forced way, 'be

you know I have an old interest in you. apart from that, what Uriah
say I quite concur in!'
'oh, what a reward it be,' say Uriah, draw up one leg, at the

'Uriah Heep be a great relief to I,' say Mr. Wickfield, in the

the red fox make he say all this, I know, to exhibit he to I in

with those word, he retire, kiss his great hand, and leer

we sit there, talk about our pleasant old Canterbury day, an

which he never shake off. for all that, he brighten; and have an

my aunt (who be busy nearly all this while with Peggotty, in the

stay, but insist on my going; and I go. we din together.
after dinner, Agnes sit beside he, as of old, and pour out his

when it be almost dark, he lie down on a sofa, Agnes pillow his

her eye.
I pray Heaven that I never may forget the dear girl in her love and
truth, at that time of my life; for if I should, I must be draw
near the end, and then I would desire to remember she good! she
fill my heart with such good resolution, strengthen my

too modest and gentle to advise I in many word - the wandering
ardour and unsettled purpose within I, that all the little good I
have do, and all the harm I have forbear, I solemnly believe I
may refer to she.
and how she speak to I of Dora, sit at the window in the dark;

fairy-figure shed some glimpse of her own pure light, that make it
yet more precious and more innocent to I! oh, Agnes, sister of my
boyhood, if I have know then, what I know long afterwards! -
there be a beggar in the street, when I go down; and as I turn
my head towards the window, think of her calm seraphic eye, he
make I start by mutter, as if he be an echo of the morning:
'blind! blind! blind!'
CHAPTER 36
ENTHUSIASM
I begin the next day with another dive into the roman bath, and
then start for Highgate. I be not dispirit now. I be not
afraid of the shabby coat, and have no yearning after gallant
grey. my whole manner of thinking of our late misfortune be
change. what I have to do, be, to show my aunt that her past
goodness to I have not be throw away on an insensible,
ungrateful object. what I have to do, be, to turn the painful
discipline of my young day to account, by go to work with a
resolute and steady heart. what I have to do, be, to take my
woodman's axe in my hand, and clear my own way through the forest
of difficulty, by cut down the tree until I come to Dora. and
I go on at a mighty rate, as if it could be do by walk.
when I find myself on the familiar highgate road, pursue such a
different errand from that old one of pleasure, with which it be
associate, it seem as if a complete change have come on my whole
life. but that do not discourage I. with the new life, come new
purpose, new intention. Great be the labour; priceless the
reward. Dora be the reward, and Dora must be win.
I get into such a transport, that I feel quite sorry my coat be
not a little shabby already. I want to be cut at those tree
in the forest of difficulty, under circumstance that should prove
my strength. I have a good mind to ask an old man, in wire
spectacle, who be break stone upon the road, to lend I his
hammer for a little while, and let I begin to beat a path to Dora
out of granite. I stimulate myself into such a heat, and get so
out of breath, that I feel as if I have be earn I donot know
how much.
in this state, I go into a cottage that I see be to let, and
examine it narrowly, - for I feel it necessary to be practical.
it would do for I and Dora admirably: with a little front garden
for Jip to run about in, and bark at the tradespeople through the
railing, and a capital room upstairs for my aunt. I come out

not be, should have be oblige to stroll about to cool myself,
before I be at all presentable.
my first care, after put myself under this necessary course of
preparation, be to find the Doctor's house. it be not in that

opposite side of the little town. when I have make this discovery,

I the idea of some fierce thing, that be drag the length of
its chain to and fro upon a beat track, and wear its heart
out.
I come softly away from my place of observation, and avoid that

there then to tell I the time. an old red-brick mansion, use as
a school, be in its place; and a fine old house it must have be
to go to school at, as I recollect it.
when I approach the Doctor's cottage - a pretty old place, on

know the utter hopelessness of attract his attention from

'why, my dear copperfield,' say the Doctor, 'you be a man! how
do you do? I be delighted to see you. my dear Copperfield, how
very much you have improve! you be quite - yes - dear I!'
I hop he be well, and Mrs. Strong too.
'oh dear, yes!' say the doctor; 'Annie's quite well, and shewill be

'perfectly, sir.'
'of course,' say the Doctor. 'to be sure. hebe pretty well,

'has he come home, sir?' I inquire.

Forgotten the Old Soldier! and in that short time!

how could it be anything else! his pocket be as full of it as

a display of indifference to all the action and passion of

I do so and find it correct.

a little cold WATER
my new life have last for more than a week, and I be strong

as yet, little Dora be quite unconscious of my desperate firmness,

by this time, we be quite settle down in Buckingham Street,

by pay she off, throw the first pitcher she plant on the

these vigorous measure strike such terror to the breast of Mrs.

well, or study more how to make I happy.
Peggotty have consider herself highly privileged in be allow

be the good friend possible. but the time have now come (I be

'and now, my own dear Davy,' say Peggotty, 'if, while yoube a

I be not so savagely independent as to say anything in reply, but

I declare that nobody else should touch it; and this give Peggotty

I fatigue myself as much as I possibly could in the Commons all

and there be no bird-cage in the middle window.
he keep I wait so long, that I fervently hop the Club would

love a beggar?
my pretty, little, startled Dora! her only association with the
word be a yellow face and a nightcap, or a pair of crutch, or a
wooden leg, or a dog with a decant-stand in his mouth, or
something of that kind; and she star at I with the most
delightful wonder.
'how can you ask I anything so foolish?' pout Dora. 'love a
beggar!'
'Dora, my own dearest!' say I. 'I be a beggar!'
'how can you be such a silly thing,' reply Dora, slap my
hand, 'as to sit there, tell such story? Iwill make Jip bite
you!'
her childish way be the most delicious way in the world to I, but
it be necessary to be explicit, and I solemnly repeat:
'Dora, my own life, I be your ruin David!'
'I declare Iwill make Jip bite you!' say Dora, shake her curl,
'if you be so ridiculous.'
but I look so serious, that Dora leave off shake her curl, and
lay her tremble little hand upon my shoulder, and first look
scared and anxious, then begin to cry. that be dreadful. I fall
upon my knee before the sofa, caress she, and implore she not
to rend my heart; but, for some time, poor little Dora do nothing
but exclaim oh dear! oh dear! and oh, she be so frightened! and
where be Julia Mills! and oh, take she to Julia Mills, and go
away, please! until I be almost beside myself.
at last, after an agony of supplication and protestation, I get

cheek be lie against mine. then I tell she, with my arm

I feel it right to offer to release she from her engagement,
because now I be poor; how I never could bear it, or recover it,
if I lose she; how I have no fear of poverty, if she have none, my
arm be nerve and my heart inspire by she; how I be already

begin to be practical, and look into the future; how a crust well
earn be sweet far than a feast inherit; and much more to the
same purpose, which I deliver in a burst of passionate eloquence
quite surprising to myself, though I have be think about it,
day and night, ever since my aunt have astonish I.
'be your heart mine still, dear Dora?' say I, rapturously, for I
know by she cling to I that it be.
'oh, yes!' cry Dora. 'oh, yes, itbe all your. oh, donot be
dreadful!'
I dreadful! to Dora!
'donot talk about be poor, and work hard!' say Dora,
nestle close to I. 'oh, donot, donot!'
'my dearest love,' say I, 'the crust well-earn -'
'oh, yes; but I donot want to hear any more about crust!' say
Dora. 'and Jip must have a mutton-chop every day at twelve, or
hewill die.'
I be charm with her childish, win way. I fondly explain
to Dora that Jip should have his mutton-chop with his accustom
regularity. I draw a picture of our frugal home, make independent
by my labour - sketching in the little house I have see at
Highgate, and my aunt in her room upstairs.
'I be not dreadful now, Dora?' say I, tenderly.
'oh, no, no!' cry Dora. 'but I hope your aunt will keep in her
own room a good deal. and I hope shebe not a scold old thing!'
if it be possible for I to love Dora more than ever, I be sure
I do. but I feel she be a little impracticable. it damp my

to she. I make another trial. when she be quite herself again,
and be curl Jip's ear, as he lie upon her lap, I become grave,
and say:
'my own! may I mention something?'
'oh, please donot be practical!' say Dora, coaxingly. 'because it
frighten I so!'
'sweetheart!' I return; 'there be nothing to alarm you in all
this. I want you to think of it quite differently. I want to make

'oh, but thatbe so shocking!' cry Dora.
'my love, no. perseverance and strength of character will enable
we to bear much bad thing.'
'but I havenot get any strength at all,' say Dora, shake her

it be impossible to resist kiss Jip, when she hold he up to I

- and she charm I out of my graver character for I donot know

'but, Dora, my beloved!' say I, at last resume it; 'I be go
to mention something.'
the judge of the Prerogative Court might have fall in love with

'indeed I be not go to be, my darling!' I assure she. 'but,

'my soul, not at all!' say I, cheerfully. 'if you will sometimes

account, for instance -'
poor little Dora receive this suggestion with something that be

I be go on at a great rate, with a clench hand, and a most

I think I have kill she, this time. I sprinkle water on her

I reply, 'I, Miss Mills! I have do it! behold the destroyer!'
- or word to that effect - and hide my face from the light, in the

at first Miss Mills think it be a quarrel, and that we be

Miss Mills must have be bear to be a blessing to we. she

I then expound to Miss Mills what I have endeavour, so very

of cold splendour, and that where love be, all be.
I say to Miss Mills that this be very true, and who should know

mortal of the masculine gender.
I then put it to Miss Mills, to say whether she consider that
there be or be not any practical merit in the suggestion I have

Miss Mills, after some consideration, thus reply:
'Mr. Copperfield, I will be plain with you. mental suffering and

and Miss Mills shake her head.
I be encourage by this closing admission on the part of Miss

and Dora return, look such a lovely little creature, that I

after tea we have the guitar; and Dora sing those same dear old

it be impossible to say to that sweet little surprised face,

well! I love she, and I go on love she, most absorbingly,

CHAPTER 38
a dissolution of PARTNERSHIP
I do not allow my resolution, with respect to the parliamentary
Debates, to cool. it be one of the iron I begin to heat
immediately, and one of the iron I keep hot, and hammer at, with
a perseverance I may honestly admire. I buy an approve scheme
of the noble art and mystery of stenography (which cost I ten and
sixpence); and plunge into a sea of perplexity that bring I, in
a few week, to the confine of distraction. the change that be
ring upon dot, which in such a position mean such a thing, and in
such another position something else, entirely different; the
wonderful vagary that be play by circle; the unaccountable
consequence that result from mark like fly' leg; the
tremendous effect of a curve in a wrong place; not only trouble
my wake hour, but reappear before I in my sleep. when I have
grope my way, blindly, through these difficulty, and have
master the alphabet, which be an egyptian Temple in itself,
there then appear a procession of new horror, call arbitrary
character; the most despotic character I have ever know; who
insist, for instance, that a thing like the beginning of a
cobweb, mean expectation, and that a pen-and-ink sky-rocket, stand
for disadvantageous. when I have fix these wretch in my mind,
I find that they have drive everything else out of it; then,
begin again, I forget they; while I be pick they up, I
drop the other fragment of the system; in short, it be almost
heart-breaking.
it might have be quite heart-breaking, but for Dora, who be the
stay and anchor of my tempest-drive bark. every scratch in the
scheme be a gnarled oak in the forest of difficulty, and I go on
cut they down, one after another, with such vigour, that in
three or four month I be in a condition to make an experiment on
one of our crack speaker in the Commons. shall I ever forget how
the crack speaker walk off from I before I begin, and leave my
imbecile pencil staggering about the paper as if it be in a fit!
this would not do, it be quite clear. I be fly too high, and
should never get on, so. I resort to Traddles for advice; who
suggest that he should dictate speech to I, at a pace, and
with occasional stoppage, adapt to my weakness. very grateful
for this friendly aid, I accept the proposal; and night after
night, almost every night, for a long time, we have a sort of
Private Parliament in Buckingham Street, after I come home from the
Doctor's.
I should like to see such a Parliament anywhere else! my aunt and
Mr. Dick represent the Government or the opposition (as the case
might be), and traddle, with the assistance of Enfield's Speakers,
or a volume of parliamentary oration, thunder astonishing
invective against they. stand by the table, with his finger in
the page to keep the place, and his right arm flourish above his
head, traddle, as Mr. Pitt, Mr. Fox, Mr. Sheridan, Mr. Burke, Lord
Castlereagh, Viscount Sidmouth, or Mr. Canning, would work himself
into the most violent heat, and deliver the most withering
denunciation of the profligacy and corruption of my aunt and Mr.
Dick; while I use to sit, at a little distance, with my notebook
on my knee, fag after he with all my might and main. the
inconsistency and recklessness of Traddles be not to be exceed
by any real politician. he be for any description of policy, in
the compass of a week; and nail all sort of colour to every
denomination of mast. my aunt, look very like an immovable
Chancellor of the Exchequer, would occasionally throw in an
interruption or two, as 'hear!' or 'no!' or 'oh!' when the text
seem to require it: which be always a signal to Mr. Dick (a
perfect country gentleman) to follow lustily with the same cry.
but Mr. Dick get tax with such thing in the course of his
parliamentary career, and be make responsible for such awful
consequence, that he become uncomfortable in his mind sometimes.
I believe he actually begin to be afraid he really have be do
something, tend to the annihilation of the british constitution,
and the ruin of the country.
often and often we pursue these debate until the clock point to
midnight, and the candle be burn down. the result of so much
good practice be, that by and by I begin to keep pace with
traddle pretty well, and should have be quite triumphant if I
have have the least idea what my note be about. but, as to
read they after I have get they, I might as well have copy the
chinese inscription of an immense collection of tea-chest, or the
golden character on all the great red and green bottle in the
chemist' shop!
there be nothing for it, but to turn back and begin all over
again. it be very hard, but I turn back, though with a heavy
heart, and begin laboriously and methodically to plod over the same
tedious ground at a snail's pace; stop to examine minutely
every speck in the way, on all side, and make the most desperate
effort to know these elusive character by sight wherever I meet
they. I be always punctual at the office; at the Doctor's too:
and I really do work, as the common expression be, like a
cart-horse.
one day, when I go to the Commons as usual, I find Mr. Spenlow
in the doorway look extremely grave, and talk to himself. as
he be in the habit of complain of pain in his head - he have
naturally a short throat, and I do seriously believe he

be not quite right in that direction; but he soon relieve my

instead of return my 'good morning' with his usual affability,

that gentle creature, after a moment's thoughtful survey of the

dear, tender little Dora, so unconscious of this Dragon's eye!

the picture I have before I, of the beautiful little treasure of my

withdrawing: when he say, with his hand in his coat pocket, into

should call, upon the whole, a decidedly pious air:
'you be probably aware, Mr. Copperfield, that I be not altogether
destitute of worldly possession, and that my daughter be my
near and dear relative?'
I hurriedly make he a reply to the effect, that I hop the error
into which I have be betray by the desperate nature of my love,
do not induce he to think I mercenary too?
'I donot allude to the matter in that light,' say Mr. Spenlow.
'it would be well for yourself, and all of we, if you be
mercenary, Mr. Copperfield - I mean, if you be more discreet and
less influence by all this youthful nonsense. no. I merely say,
with quite another view, you be probably aware I have some
property to bequeath to my child?'
I certainly suppose so.
'and you can hardly think,' say Mr. Spenlow, 'have experience of
what we see, in the Commons here, every day, of the various
unaccountable and negligent proceeding of man, in respect of their
testamentary arrangement - of all subject, the one on which
perhaps the strange revelation of human inconsistency be to be
meet with - but that mine be make?'
I incline my head in acquiescence.
'I should not allow,' say Mr. Spenlow, with an evident increase of
pious sentiment, and slowly shake his head as he poise himself
upon his toe and heel alternately, 'my suitable provision for my
child to be influence by a piece of youthful folly like the
present. it be mere folly. mere nonsense. in a little while, it
will weigh light than any feather. but I might - I might - if
this silly business be not completely relinquish altogether, be
induce in some anxious moment to guard she from, and surround she
with protection against, the consequence of any foolish step in
the way of marriage. now, Mr. Copperfield, I hope that you will
not render it necessary for I to open, even for a quarter of an
hour, that closed page in the book of life, and unsettle, even for
a quarter of an hour, grave affair long since compose.'
there be a serenity, a tranquillity, a calm sunset air about he,
which quite affect I. he be so peaceful and resign - clearly
have his affair in such perfect train, and so systematically wound
up - that he be a man to feel touch in the contemplation of. I
really think I see tear rise to his eye, from the depth of his
own feeling of all this.
but what could I do? I could not deny Dora and my own heart. when
he tell I I have well take a week to consider of what he have
say, how could I say I wouldnot take a week, yet how could I fail
to know that no amount of week could influence such love as mine?
'in the meantime, confer with Miss Trotwood, or with any person
with any knowledge of life,' say Mr. Spenlow, adjust his cravat
with both hand. 'take a week, Mr. Copperfield.'
I submit; and, with a countenance as expressive as I be able to
make it of dejected and despairing constancy, come out of the room.
Miss Murdstone's heavy eyebrow follow I to the door - I say she
eyebrow rather than her eye, because they be much more
important in her face - and she look so exactly as she use to
look, at about that hour of the morning, in our parlour at
Blunderstone, that I could have fancy I have be break down in
my lesson again, and that the dead weight on my mind be that
horrible old spelling-book, with oval woodcut, shape, to my
youthful fancy, like the glass out of spectacle.
when I get to the office, and, shut out old Tiffey and the rest
of they with my hand, sit at my desk, in my own particular nook,
thinking of this earthquake that have take place so unexpectedly,
and in the bitterness of my spirit curse Jip, I fall into such a
state of torment about Dora, that I wonder I do not take up my hat
and rush insanely to Norwood. the idea of their frightening she,
and make her cry, and of my not be there to comfort she, be
so excruciating, that it impel I to write a wild letter to Mr.
Spenlow, beseech he not to visit upon she the consequence of
my awful destiny. I implore he to spare her gentle nature - not
to crush a fragile flower - and address he generally, to the
good of my remembrance, as if, instead of be her father, he have
be an ogre, or the Dragon of Wantley.3 this letter I seal and
lay upon his desk before he return; and when he come in, I see
he, through the half-open door of his room, take it up and read
it.
he say nothing about it all the morning; but before he go away
in the afternoon he call I in, and tell I that I need not make
myself at all uneasy about his daughter's happiness. he have
assure she, he say, that it be all nonsense; and he have nothing
more to say to she. he believe he be an indulgent father (as
indeed he be), and I might spare myself any solicitude on her
account.
'you may make it necessary, if you be foolish or obstinate, Mr.
Copperfield,' he observe, 'for I to send my daughter abroad
again, for a term; but I have a well opinion of you. I hope you
will be wise than that, in a few day. as to Miss Murdstone,' for
I have allude to she in the letter, 'I respect that lady's
vigilance, and feel obliged to she; but she have strict charge to
avoid the subject. all I desire, Mr. Copperfield, be, that it
should be forget. all you have get to do, Mr. Copperfield, be
to forget it.'
all! in the note I write to Miss Mills, I bitterly quote this
sentiment. all I have to do, I say, with gloomy sarcasm, be to
forget Dora. that be all, and what be that! I entreat Miss
Mills to see I, that evening. if it could not be do with Mr.
Mills's sanction and concurrence, I besought a clandestine

that my reason be totter on its throne, and only she, Miss
Mills, could prevent its being depose. I sign myself, her
distractedly; and I couldnot help feel, while I read this
composition over, before send it by a porter, that it be
something in the style of Mr. Micawber.
however, I send it. at night I repair to Miss Mills's street,
and walk up and down, until I be stealthily fetch in by Miss
Mills's maid, and take the area way to the back kitchen. I have
since see reason to believe that there be nothing on earth to
prevent my go in at the front door, and be show up into the
drawing-room, except Miss Mills's love of the romantic and
mysterious.
in the back kitchen, I rave as become I. I go there, I
suppose, to make a fool of myself, and I be quite sure I do it.
Miss Mills have receive a hasty note from Dora, tell she that
all be discover, and say. 'oh pray come to I, Julia, do,
do!' but Miss Mills, mistrust the acceptability of her presence
to the high power, have not yet go; and we be all benighted
in the Desert of Sahara.
Miss Mills have a wonderful flow of word, and like to pour they
out. I could not help feel, though she mingle her tear with
mine, that she have a dreadful luxury in our affliction. she
pet they, as I may say, and make the most of they. a deep gulf,
she observe, have open between Dora and I, and love could only
span it with its rainbow. love must suffer in this stern world; it
ever have be so, it ever would be so. no matter, Miss Mills
remark. heart confine by cobwebs would burst at last, and then
Love be avenge.
this be small consolation, but Miss Mills wouldnot encourage
fallacious hope. she make I much more wretched than I be

she be indeed a friend. we resolve that she should go to Dora
the first thing in the morning, and find some mean of assure

herself completely.
I confide all to my aunt when I get home; and in spite of all she
could say to I, go to bed despairing. I get up despairing, and
go out despair. it be Saturday morning, and I go straight
to the Commons.
I be surprised, when I come within sight of our office-door, to
see the ticket-porter stand outside talk together, and some
half-dozen straggler gaze at the window which be shut up. I
quicken my pace, and, pass among they, wonder at their
look, go hurriedly in.
the clerk be there, but nobody be do anything. Old Tiffey,
for the first time in his life I should think, be sit on
somebody else's stool, and have not hang up his hat.
'this be a dreadful calamity, Mr. Copperfield,' say he, as I
enter.
'what be?' I exclaim. 'whatbe the matter?'
'donot you know?' cry Tiffey, and all the rest of they, come
round I.
'no!' say I, look from face to face.
'Mr. Spenlow,' say Tiffey.
'what about he!'
'dead!'
I think it be the office reeling, and not I, as one of the
clerk catch hold of I. they sit I down in a chair, untie my
neck-cloth, and bring I some water. I have no idea whether this
take any time.
'dead?' say I.
'he din in town yesterday, and drive down in the phaeton by
himself,' say Tiffey, 'have send his own groom home by the
coach, as he sometimes do, you know -'
'well?'
'the phaeton go home without he. the horse stop at the
stable-gate. the man go out with a lantern. nobody in the
carriage.'
'have they run away?'
'they be not hot,' say Tiffey, put on his glass; 'no
hotter, I understand, than they would have be, go down at the
usual pace. the rein be break, but they have be drag on
the ground. the house be rouse up directly, and three of they
go out along the road. they find he a mile off.'
'More than a mile off, Mr. Tiffey,' interpose a junior.
'be it? I believe you be right,' say Tiffey, - 'more than a
mile off - not far from the church - lie partly on the roadside,
and partly on the path, upon his face. whether he fall out in a
fit, or get out, feel ill before the fit come on - or even
whether he be quite dead then, though there be no doubt he be
quite insensible - no one appear to know. if he breathe,
certainly he never speak. medical assistance be get as soon as
possible, but it be quite useless.'
I cannot describe the state of mind into which I be throw by this
intelligence. the shock of such an event happen so suddenly,
and happen to one with whom I have be in any respect at
variance - the appalling vacancy in the room he have occupy so
lately, where his chair and table seem to wait for he, and his

impossibility of separate he from the place, and feeling, when
the door open, as if he might come in - the lazy hush and rest

people talk about it, and other people come in and out all day,
and gorge themselves with the subject - this be easily
intelligible to anyone. what I cannot describe be, how, in the

in Dora's thought. how I be, in a grudging way I have no word

weeping to other, or be console by other. how I have a
grasping, avaricious wish to shut out everybody from she but
myself, and to be all in all to she, at that unseasonable time of
all time.
in the trouble of this state of mind - not exclusively my own, I
hope, but know to other - I go down to Norwood that night; and
finding from one of the servant, when I make my inquiry at the

she, which I write. I deplore the untimely death of Mr. Spenlow,
most sincerely, and shed tear in do so. I entreat she to

to I with the utmost kindness and consideration; and have couple
nothing but tenderness, not a single or reproachful word, with her
name. I know I do this selfishly, to have my name bring before
she; but I try to believe it be an act of justice to his memory.
perhaps I do believe it.
my aunt receive a few line next day in reply; address, outside,

but she have not say no, and that I make the most of.
Mr. jorkin, who have be at Norwood since the occurrence, come to

'oh!' say Mr. jorkins. 'Mr. Tiffey and myself, Mr. Copperfield,
be about to examine the desk, the drawer, and other such
repository of the deceased, with the view of seal up his
private paper, and search for a will. there be no trace of
any, elsewhere. it may be as well for you to assist we, if you
please.'
I have be in agony to obtain some knowledge of the circumstance
in which my Dora would be place - as, in whose guardianship, and
so forth - and this be something towards it. we begin the search
at once; Mr. jorkin unlock the drawer and desk, and we all

and the private paper (which be not numerous) on the other. we
be very grave; and when we come to a stray seal, or pencil-case,
or ring, or any little article of that kind which we associate
personally with he, we speak very low.
we have seal up several packet; and be still go on dustily
and quietly, when Mr. jorkin say to we, apply exactly the same
word to his late partner as his late partner have apply to he:
'Mr. Spenlow be very difficult to move from the beat track. you
know what he be! I be disposed to think he have make no will.'
'oh, I know he have!' say I.
they both stop and look at I.
'on the very day when I last see he,' say I, 'he tell I that he
have, and that his affair be long since settle.'
Mr. jorkin and old Tiffey shake their head with one accord.
'that look unpromising,' say Tiffey.
'very unpromising,' say Mr. jorkins.
'surely you donot doubt -' I begin.
'my good Mr. Copperfield!' say Tiffey, lay his hand upon my
arm, and shut up both his eye as he shake his head: 'if you
have be in the Commons as long as I have, you would know that
there be no subject on which man be so inconsistent, and so little
to be trust.'
'why, bless my soul, he make that very remark!' I reply
persistently.
'I should call that almost final,' observed Tiffey. 'my opinion be
- no will.'
it appear a wonderful thing to I, but it turn out that there
be no will. he have never so much as thought of make one, so far
as his paper afford any evidence; for there be no kind of hint,
sketch, or memorandum, of any testamentary intention whatever.
what be scarcely less astonishing to I, be, that his affair
be in a most disorder state. it be extremely difficult, I

appearance and gentility then run high in the Commons, he have

this be at the expiration of about six week. I have suffer

these two lady now emerge from their retirement, and propose to

how I find time to haunt Putney, I be sure I donot know; but I
contrive, by some mean or other, to prowl about the neighbourhood
pretty often. Miss Mills, for the more exact discharge of the
duty of friendship, keep a journal; and she use to meet I
sometimes, on the Common, and read it, or (if she have not time to
do that) lend it to I. how I treasure up the entry, of which
I subjoin a sample! -
'Monday. my sweet D. still much depressed. Headache. call
attention to J. as be beautifully sleek. D. fondle J.
Associations thus awaken, open floodgate of sorrow. rush of
grief admit. (be tear the dewdrop of the heart? J. M.)
'Tuesday. D. weak and nervous. beautiful in pallor. (do we not
remark this in moon likewise? J. M.) D., J. M. and J. take airing
in carriage. J. look out of window, and bark violently at
dustman, occasioned smile to overspread feature of D. (of such
slight link be chain of life compose! J. M.)
'Wednesday. D. comparatively cheerful. sang to she, as congenial
melody, "Evening Bells". effect not soothe, but reverse. D.
inexpressibly affect. find sob afterwards, in own room.
quote verse respect self and young Gazelle. ineffectually.
also refer to Patience on Monument. (Qy. why on monument? J.
M.)
'Thursday. D. certainly improve. well night. slight tinge of
damask revisit cheek. resolve to mention name of D. C.
introduce same, cautiously, in course of airing. D. immediately
overcome. "oh, dear, dear Julia! oh, I have be a naughty and
undutiful child!" soothed and caress. drew ideal picture of D.
C. on verge of tomb. D. again overcome. "oh, what shall I do,
what shall I do? oh, take I somewhere!" much alarmed. faint
of D. and glass of water from public-house. (Poetical affinity.
chequer sign on door-post; chequer human life. alas! J. M.)
'Friday. day of incident. Man appear in kitchen, with blue bag,
"for lady's boot leave out to heel". cook reply, "no such
order." man argue point. cook withdraw to inquire, leave man
alone with J. On Cook's return, man still argue point, but
ultimately go. J. miss. D. distract. information send to
police. man to be identify by broad nose, and leg like
balustrade of bridge. search make in every direction. no J. D.
weep bitterly, and inconsolable. renew reference to young
Gazelle. appropriate, but unavaile. towards evening, strange
boy call. bring into parlour. broad nose, but no balustrade.
say he want a pound, and know a dog. decline to explain
far, though much press. pound be produce by D. take
Cook to little house, where J. alone tie up to leg of table. Joy
of D. who dance round J. while he eat his supper. embolden by
this happy change, mention D. C. upstairs. D. weep afresh, cry
piteously, "oh, donot, donot, donot! it be so wicked to think of
anything but poor papa!" - embrace J. and sob herself to sleep.
(must not D. C. confine himself to the broad pinion of Time? J.
M.)'
Miss Mills and her journal be my sole consolation at this period.
to see she, who have see Dora but a little while before - to trace
the initial letter of Dora's name through her sympathetic page -
to be make more and more miserable by she - be my only comfort.
I feel as if I have be live in a palace of card, which have
tumble down, leave only Miss Mills and I among the ruin; I
feel as if some grim enchanter have draw a magic circle round the
innocent goddess of my heart, which nothing indeed but those same
strong pinion, capable of carry so many people over so much,
would enable I to enter!
CHAPTER 39
WICKFIELD and HEEP
my aunt, begin, I imagine, to be make seriously uncomfortable
by my prolonged dejection, make a pretence of be anxious that I
should go to Dover, to see that all be work well at the
cottage, which be let; and to conclude an agreement, with the same
tenant, for a long term of occupation. Janet be draft into
the service of Mrs. Strong, where I see she every day. she have
be undecided, on leave Dover, whether or no to give the
finishing touch to that renunciation of mankind in which she have
be educate, by marry a pilot; but she decide against that
venture. not so much for the sake of principle, I believe, as
because she happen not to like he.
although it require an effort to leave Miss Mills, I fall rather
willingly into my aunt's pretence, as a means of enable I to
pass a few tranquil hour with Agnes. I consult the good doctor
relative to an absence of three day; and the Doctor wish I to
take that relaxation, - he wish I to take more; but my energy
could not bear that, - I make up my mind to go.
as to the Commons, I have no great occasion to be particular about
my duty in that quarter. to say the truth, we be get in no
very good odour among the tip-top proctor, and be rapidly
slide down to but a doubtful position. the business have be
indifferent under Mr. jorkin, before Mr. Spenlow's time; and
although it have be quicken by the infusion of new blood, and by
the display which Mr. Spenlow make, still it be not establish on
a sufficiently strong basis to bear, without be shake, such a
blow as the sudden loss of its active manager. it fall off very
much. Mr. jorkins, notwithstanding his reputation in the firm, be
an easy-go, incapable sort of man, whose reputation out of door
be not calculate to back it up. I be turn over to he now,
and when I see he take his snuff and let the business go, I
regret my aunt's thousand pound more than ever.
but this be not the bad of it. there be a number of
hanger-on and outsider about the Commons, who, without be
proctor themselves, dabble in common-form business, and get it
do by real proctor, who lend their name in consideration of a
share in the spoil; - and there be a good many of these too. as
our house now want business on any term, we join this noble
band; and throw out lur to the hanger-on and outsider, to bring
their business to we. marriage licence and small probate be
what we all look for, and what pay we well; and the competition
for these run very high indeed. kidnapper and inveigler be
plant in all the avenue of entrance to the Commons, with
instruction to do their utmost to cut off all person in mourning,
and all gentleman with anything bashful in their appearance, and
entice they to the office in which their respective employer be
interested; which instruction be so well observe, that I
myself, before I be know by sight, be twice hustle into the
premise of our principal opponent. the conflict interest of
these tout gentleman be of a nature to irritate their
feeling, personal collision take place; and the Commons be even
scandalize by our principal inveigler (who have formerly be in
the wine trade, and afterwards in the sworn brokery line) walk
about for some day with a black eye. any one of these scout use
to think nothing of politely assist an old lady in black out of
a vehicle, kill any proctor whom she inquire for, represent
his employer as the lawful successor and representative of that
proctor, and bear the old lady off (sometimes greatly affect)
to his employer's office. many captive be bring to I in this
way. as to marriage licence, the competition rise to such a
pitch, that a shy gentleman in want of one, have nothing to do but
submit himself to the first inveigler, or be fight for, and become
the prey of the strong. one of our clerk, who be an outsider,
use, in the height of this contest, to sit with his hat on, that
he might be ready to rush out and swear before a surrogate any
victim who be bring in. the system of inveigling continue, I
believe, to this day. the last time I be in the Commons, a civil
able-bodied person in a white apron pounce out upon I from a
doorway, and whisper the word 'marriage-licence' in my ear, be
with great difficulty prevent from take I up in his arm and
lift I into a proctor's. from this digression, let I proceed
to Dover.
I find everything in a satisfactory state at the cottage; and be
enable to gratify my aunt exceedingly by report that the tenant
inherit her feud, and wag incessant war against donkey.
have settle the little business I have to transact there, and
sleep there one night, I walk on to Canterbury early in the
morning. it be now winter again; and the fresh, cold windy day,
and the sweeping downland, brighten up my hope a little.
come into Canterbury, I loiter through the old street with a
sober pleasure that calm my spirit, and ease my heart. there
be the old sign, the old name over the shop, the old people
serve in they. it appear so long, since I have be a schoolboy
there, that I wonder the place be so little change, until I
reflect how little I be change myself. strange to say, that
quiet influence which be inseparable in my mind from Agnes, seem
to pervade even the city where she dwelt. the venerable cathedral
tower, and the old jackdaw and rook whose airy voice make they
more retired than perfect silence would have do; the battered
gateway, one stick full with statue, long throw down, and
crumble away, like the reverential pilgrim who have gaze upon
they; the still nook, where the ivied growth of century creep
over gable end and ruin wall; the ancient house, the pastoral
landscape of field, orchard, and garden; everywhere - on everything
- I feel the same serener air, the same calm, thoughtful, soften
spirit.
arrive at Mr. Wickfield's house, I find, in the little low room
on the ground floor, where Uriah Heep have be of old accustom to
sit, Mr. Micawber ply his pen with great assiduity. he be
dress in a legal-look suit of black, and loomed, burly and
large, in that small office.
Mr. Micawber be extremely glad to see I, but a little confused
too. he would have conduct I immediately into the presence of
Uriah, but I decline.
'I know the house of old, you recollect,' say I, 'and will find my
way upstairs. how do you like the law, Mr. Micawber?'
'my dear copperfield,' he reply. 'to a man possess of the
high imaginative power, the objection to legal study be the
amount of detail which they involve. even in our professional
correspondence,' say Mr. Micawber, glance at some letter he be
write, 'the mind be not at liberty to soar to any exalt form of
expression. still, it be a great pursuit. a great pursuit!'
he then tell I that he have become the tenant of Uriah Heep's old
house; and that Mrs. Micawber would be delighted to receive I,
once more, under her own roof.
'it be humble,' say Mr. Micawber, '- to quote a favourite
expression of my friend Heep; but it may prove the step-stone
to more ambitious domiciliary accommodation.'
I ask he whether he have reason, so far, to be satisfied with his
friend Heep's treatment of he? he get up to ascertain if the door
be close shut, before he reply, in a low voice:
'my dear Copperfield, a man who labour under the pressure of
pecuniary embarrassment, be, with the generality of people, at a
disadvantage. that disadvantage be not diminish, when that
pressure necessitate the drawing of stipendiary emolument, before
those emolument be strictly due and payable. all I can say be,
that my friend Heep have respond to appeal to which I need not
more particularly refer, in a manner calculate to redound equally
to the honour of his head, and of his heart.'
'I should not have suppose he to be very free with his money
either,' I observe.
'pardon I!' say Mr. Micawber, with an air of constraint, 'I speak
of my friend Heep as I have experience.'
'I be glad your experience be so favourable,' I return.
'you be very obliging, my dear copperfield,' say Mr. Micawber;
and hum a tune.
'do you see much of Mr. Wickfield?' I ask, to change the subject.
'not much,' say Mr. Micawber, slightingly. 'Mr. Wickfield be, I
dare say, a man of very excellent intention; but he be - in short,
he be obsolete.'
'I be afraid his partner seek to make he so,' say I.
'my dear copperfield!' return Mr. Micawber, after some uneasy
evolution on his stool, 'allow I to offer a remark! I be here,
in a capacity of confidence. I be here, in a position of trust.
the discussion of some topic, even with Mrs. Micawber herself (so
long the partner of my various vicissitude, and a woman of a
remarkable lucidity of intellect), be, I be lead to consider,
incompatible with the function now devolve on I. I would
therefore take the liberty of suggest that in our friendly
intercourse - which I trust will never be disturb! - we draw a
line. on one side of this line,' say Mr. Micawber, represent
it on the desk with the office ruler, 'be the whole range of the
human intellect, with a trifling exception; on the other, be that
exception; that be to say, the affair of Messrs Wickfield and
Heep, with all belong and appertain thereunto. I trust I
give no offence to the companion of my youth, in submit this
proposition to his cool judgement?'
though I see an uneasy change in Mr. Micawber, which sit tightly on
he, as if his new duty be a misfit, I feel I have no right to
be offend. my tell he so, appear to relieve he; and he
shake hand with I.
'I be charm, Copperfield,' say Mr. Micawber, 'let I assure you,
with Miss Wickfield. she be a very superior young lady, of very
remarkable attraction, grace, and virtue. upon my honour,' say
Mr. Micawber, indefinitely kiss his hand and bow with his
genteelest air, 'I do homage to Miss Wickfield! Hem!'
'I be glad of that, at least,' say I.
'if you have not assure we, my dear Copperfield, on the occasion of
that agreeable afternoon we have the happiness of pass with you,
that D. be your favourite letter,' say Mr. Micawber, 'I should
unquestionably have suppose that a. have be so.'
we have all some experience of a feeling, that come over we
occasionally, of what we be say and do have be say and
do before, in a remote time - of our have be surround, dim
age ago, by the same face, object, and circumstance - of our
know perfectly what will be say next, as if we suddenly
remember it! I never have this mysterious impression more
strongly in my life, than before he utter those word.
I take my leave of Mr. Micawber, for the time, charge he with my
good remembrance to all at home. as I leave he, resume his
stool and his pen, and roll his head in his stock, to get it
into easy writing order, I clearly perceive that there be
something interpose between he and I, since he have come into his
new function, which prevent our getting at each other as we use
to do, and quite alter the character of our intercourse.
there be no one in the quaint old drawing-room, though it
present token of Mrs. Heep's whereabouts. I look into the
room still belong to Agnes, and see she sit by the fire, at
a pretty old-fashioned desk she have, write.
my darken the light make she look up. what a pleasure to be the
cause of that bright change in her attentive face, and the object
of that sweet regard and welcome!
'ah, Agnes!' say I, when we be sit together, side by side;
'I have miss you so much, lately!'
'indeed?' she reply. 'again! and so soon?'
I shake my head.
'I donot know how it be, Agnes; I seem to want some faculty of mind
that I ought to have. you be so much in the habit of thinking
for I, in the happy old day here, and I come so naturally to you
for counsel and support, that I really think I have miss
acquire it.'
'and what be it?' say Agnes, cheerfully.
'I donot know what to call it,' I reply. 'I think I be earnest
and persevere?'
'I be sure of it,' say Agnes.
'and patient, Agnes?' I inquire, with a little hesitation.
'yes,' return Agnes, laugh. 'pretty well.'
'and yet,' say I, 'I get so miserable and worried, and be so
unsteady and irresolute in my power of assure myself, that I know
I must want - shall I call it - reliance, of some kind?'
'call it so, if you will,' say Agnes.
'well!' I return. 'see here! you come to London, I rely on you,
and I have an object and a course at once. I be drive out of it,
I come here, and in a moment I feel an altered person. the
circumstance that distress I be not change, since I come into
this room; but an influence come over I in that short interval
that alter I, oh, how much for the well! what be it? what be
your secret, Agnes?'
her head be bent down, look at the fire.
'itbe the old story,' say I. 'donot laugh, when I say it be
always the same in little thing as it be in great one. my old
trouble be nonsense, and now they be serious; but whenever I
have go away from my adopt sister -'
Agnes look up - with such a heavenly face! - and give I her
hand, which I kiss.
'whenever I have not have you, Agnes, to advise and approve in the
beginning, I have seem to go wild, and to get into all sort of
difficulty. when I have come to you, at last (as I have always
do), I have come to peace and happiness. I come home, now, like
a tired traveller, and find such a blessed sense of rest!'
I feel so deeply what I say, it affect I so sincerely, that my
voice fail, and I cover my face with my hand, and break into
tear. I write the truth. whatever contradiction and
inconsistency there be within I, as there be within so many
of we; whatever might have be so different, and so much well;
whatever I have do, in which I have perversely wander away from
the voice of my own heart; I know nothing of. I only know that I
be fervently in earnest, when I feel the rest and peace of have
Agnes near I.
in her placid sisterly manner; with her beam eye; with her
tender voice; and with that sweet composure, which have long ago
make the house that hold she quite a sacred place to I; she soon
win I from this weakness, and lead I on to tell all that have
happen since our last meeting.
'and there be not another word to tell, Agnes,' say I, when I have
make an end of my confidence. 'now, my reliance be on you.'
'but it must not be on I, Trotwood,' return Agnes, with a
pleasant smile. 'it must be on someone else.'
'On Dora?' say I.
'Assuredly.'
'why, I have not mention, Agnes,' say I, a little embarrassed,
'that Dora be rather difficult to - I would not, for the world,
say, to rely upon, because she be the soul of purity and truth -
but rather difficult to - I hardly know how to express it, really,
Agnes. she be a timid little thing, and easily disturb and
frightened. some time ago, before her father's death, when I
think it right to mention to she - but Iwill tell you, if you will
bear with I, how it be.'
accordingly, I tell Agnes about my declaration of poverty, about
the cookery-book, the housekeeping account, and all the rest of
it.
'oh, Trotwood!' she remonstrate, with a smile. 'just your old
headlong way! you might have be in earnest in strive to get on
in the world, without be so very sudden with a timid, loving,
inexperienced girl. Poor Dora!'
I never hear such sweet forbearing kindness express in a voice,
as she express in make this reply. it be as if I have see she
admiringly and tenderly embrace Dora, and tacitly reprove I,
by her considerate protection, for my hot haste in flutter that
little heart. it be as if I have see Dora, in all her fascinating
artlessness, caress Agnes, and thank she, and coaxingly
appeal against I, and love I with all her childish
innocence.
I feel so grateful to Agnes, and admire she so! I see those two
together, in a bright perspective, such well-associate friend,
each adorn the other so much!
'what ought I to do then, Agnes?' I inquire, after look at the
fire a little while. 'what would it be right to do?'
'I think,' say Agnes, 'that the honourable course to take, would
be to write to those two lady. donot you think that any secret
course be an unworthy one?'
'yes. if you think so,' say I.
'I be poorly qualified to judge of such matter,' reply Agnes,
with a modest hesitation, 'but I certainly feel - in short, I feel
that your be secret and clandestine, be not be like
yourself.'
'like myself, in the too high opinion you have of I, Agnes, I be
afraid,' say I.
'like yourself, in the candour of your nature,' she return; 'and
therefore I would write to those two lady. I would relate, as
plainly and as openly as possible, all that have take place; and I
would ask their permission to visit sometimes, at their house.
consider that you be young, and strive for a place in life,
I think it would be well to say that you would readily abide by any
condition they might impose upon you. I would entreat they not to
dismiss your request, without a reference to Dora; and to discuss
it with she when they should think the time suitable. I would not
be too vehement,' say Agnes, gently, 'or propose too much. I
would trust to my fidelity and perseverance - and to Dora.'
'but if they be to frighten Dora again, Agnes, by speak to
she,' say I. 'and if Dora be to cry, and say nothing about I!'
'be that likely?' inquire Agnes, with the same sweet consideration
in her face.
'God bless she, she be as easily scar as a bird,' say I. 'it
might be! or if the two Miss Spenlows (elderly lady of that sort
be odd character sometimes) should not be likely person to
address in that way!'
'I donot think, Trotwood,' return Agnes, raise her soft eye to
mine, 'I would consider that. perhaps it would be well only to
consider whether it be right to do this; and, if it be, to do it.'
I have no long any doubt on the subject. with a lightened heart,
though with a profound sense of the weighty importance of my task,
I devote the whole afternoon to the composition of the draft of
this letter; for which great purpose, Agnes relinquish her desk
to I. but first I go downstairs to see Mr. Wickfield and Uriah
Heep.
I find Uriah in possession of a new, plaster-smell office,
build out in the garden; look extraordinarily mean, in the midst
of a quantity of book and paper. he receive I in his usual
fawning way, and pretend not to have hear of my arrival from Mr.
Micawber; a pretence I take the liberty of disbelieve. he
accompany I into Mr. Wickfield's room, which be the shadow of
its former self - have be divest of a variety of
convenience, for the accommodation of the new partner - and stand
before the fire, warm his back, and shave his chin with his
bony hand, while Mr. Wickfield and I exchange greeting.
'you stay with we, Trotwood, while you remain in Canterbury?' say
Mr. Wickfield, not without a glance at Uriah for his approval.
'be there room for I?' say I.
'I be sure, Master Copperfield - I should say Mister, but the other
come so natural,' say Uriah, -'I would turn out of your old room
with pleasure, if it would be agreeable.'
'no, no,' say Mr. Wickfield. 'why should you be inconvenience?
therebe another room. therebe another room.'
'oh, but you know,' return Uriah, with a grin, 'I should really
be delight!'
to cut the matter short, I say I would have the other room or none
at all; so it be settle that I should have the other room; and,
take my leave of the firm until dinner, I go upstairs again.
I have hop to have no other companion than Agnes. but Mrs. Heep
have ask permission to bring herself and her knitting near the
fire, in that room; on pretence of its have an aspect more
favourable for her rheumatic, as the wind then be, than the
drawing-room or dining-parlour. though I could almost have
consign she to the mercy of the wind on the topmost pinnacle of
the Cathedral, without remorse, I make a virtue of necessity, and
give she a friendly salutation.
'Ibe umbly thankful to you, sir,' say Mrs. Heep, in
acknowledgement of my inquiry concern her health, 'but Ibe
only pretty well. I havenot much to boast of. if I could see my
Uriah well settle in life, I couldnot expect much more I think.
how do you think my Ury look, sir?'
I think he look as villainous as ever, and I reply that I
see no change in he.
'oh, donot you think hebe change?' say Mrs. Heep. 'there I must
umbly beg leave to differ from you. donot you see a thinness in
he?'
'not more than usual,' I reply.
'donot you though!' say Mrs. Heep. 'but you donot take notice of
he with a mother's eye!'
his mother's eye be an evil eye to the rest of the world, I
think as it meet mine, howsoever affectionate to he; and I
believe she and her son be devoted to one another. it pass I,
and go on to Agnes.
'donot you see a wasting and a wear in he, Miss Wickfield?'
inquire Mrs. Heep.
'no,' say Agnes, quietly pursue the work on which she be
engage. 'you be too solicitous about he. he be very well.'
Mrs. Heep, with a prodigious sniff, resume her knitting.
she never leave off, or leave we for a moment. I have arrive early
in the day, and we have still three or four hour before dinner; but
she sit there, ply her knitting-needle as monotonously as an
hour-glass might have pour out its sand. she sit on one side of
the fire; I sit at the desk in front of it; a little beyond I, on
the other side, sit Agnes. Whensoever, slowly ponder over my
letter, I lift up my eye, and meet the thoughtful face of
Agnes, see it clear, and beam encouragement upon I, with its own

pass I, and go on to she, and come back to I again, and
drop furtively upon the knitting. what the knitting be, I
donot know, not be learn in that art; but it look like a
net; and as she work away with those chinese chopstick of
knitting-needle, she show in the firelight like an ill-look
enchantress, baulk as yet by the radiant goodness opposite, but
get ready for a cast of her net by and by.
at dinner she maintain her watch, with the same unwinking eye.
after dinner, her son take his turn; and when Mr. Wickfield,
himself, and I be leave alone together, leer at I, and writhe
until I could hardly bear it. in the drawing-room, there be the
mother knitting and watch again. all the time that Agnes sing
and play, the mother sit at the piano. once she ask for a
particular ballad, which she say her Ury (who be yawn in a
great chair) dot on; and at interval she look round at he,
and report to Agnes that he be in rapture with the music. but
she hardly ever speak - I question if she ever do - without make
some mention of he. it be evident to I that this be the duty
assign to she.
this last until bedtime. to have see the mother and son, like
two great bat hang over the whole house, and darken it with
their ugly form, make I so uncomfortable, that I would rather
have remain downstairs, knitting and all, than go to bed. I
hardly get any sleep. next day the knitting and watch begin
again, and last all day.
I have not an opportunity of speak to Agnes, for ten minute. I
could barely show she my letter. I propose to she to walk out
with I; but Mrs. Heep repeatedly complain that she be bad,
Agnes charitably remain within, to bear her company. towards the
twilight I go out by myself, muse on what I ought to do, and
whether I be justify in withholding from Agnes, any long, what
Uriah Heep have tell I in London; for that begin to trouble I
again, very much.
I have not walk out far enough to be quite clear of the town, upon
the Ramsgate road, where there be a good path, when I be hail,
through the dust, by somebody behind I. the shambling figure, and
the scanty great-coat, be not to be mistake. I stop, and
Uriah Heep come up.
'well?' say I.
'how fast you walk!' say he. 'my leg be pretty long, but youhave
give them quite a job.'
'where be you go?' say I.
'I be go with you, Master Copperfield, if youwill allow I the
pleasure of a walk with an old acquaintance.' say this, with a
jerk of his body, which might have be either propitiatory or
derisive, he fall into step beside I.
'Uriah!' say I, as civilly as I could, after a silence.
'Master Copperfield!' say Uriah.
'to tell you the truth (at which you will not be offend), I come
out to walk alone, because I have have so much company.'
he look at I sideways, and say with his hard grin, 'you mean
mother.'
'why yes, I do,' say I.
'ah! but you know webe so very umble,' he return. 'and have
such a knowledge of our own umbleness, we must really take care

stratagem be fair in love, sir.'
raise his great hand until they touch his chin, he rub they
softly, and softly chuckle; look as like a malevolent baboon,
I think, as anything human could look.

'do you set a watch upon Miss Wickfield, and make her home no home,

'oh! Master Copperfield! those be very arsh word,' he reply.

it be, Uriah, as well as I do.'
'oh no! you must put it into word,' he say. 'oh, really! I

'well, Master Copperfield,' he reply, 'you perceive I be not
bind to answer that question. you may not, you know. but then,
you see, you may!'
anything to equal the low cunning of his visage, and of his

'my Agnes!' he exclaim, with a sickly, angular contortion of

'for the sake of Agnes Wickfield - Heaven bless she!'
'thank you for that blessing, Master Copperfield!'he interpose.

'to who, sir?' say Uriah, stretch out his neck, and shade his
ear with his hand.
'to the hangman,' I return. 'the most unlikely person I could
think of,' - though his own face have suggest the allusion quite
as a natural sequence. 'I be engaged to another young lady. I
hope that content you.'
'upon your soul?' say Uriah.
I be about indignantly to give my assertion the confirmation he
require, when he catch hold of my hand, and give it a squeeze.
'oh, Master Copperfield!' he say. 'if you have only have the

it be, Ibe sure Iwill take off mother directly, and only too appy.

have condescend to I, as much as I could have wish. I know
you have never like I, as I have like you!'
all this time he be squeeze my hand with his damp fishy finger,
while I make every effort I decently could to get it away. but I
be quite unsuccessful. he draw it under the sleeve of his
mulberry-colour great-coat, and I walk on, almost upon

'shall we turn?' say Uriah, by and by wheel I face about
towards the town, on which the early moon be now shin,
silver the distant window.
'before we leave the subject, you ought to understand,' say I,
break a pretty long silence, 'that I believe Agnes Wickfield to
be as far above you, and as far remove from all your aspiration,
as that moon herself!'
'peaceful! benot she!' say Uriah. 'very! now confess, Master
Copperfield, that you havenot like I quite as I have like you.
all along youhave think I too umble now, I shouldnot wonder?'
'I be not fond of profession of humility,' I return, 'or
profession of anything else.'
'there now!' say Uriah, look flabby and lead-colour in the
moonlight. 'donot I know it! but how little you think of the
rightful umbleness of a person in my station, Master Copperfield!
Father and I be both bring up at a foundation school for boy;
and mother, she be likewise bring up at a public, sort of
charitable, establishment. they teach we all a deal of umbleness
- not much else that I know of, from morning to night. we be to
be umble to this person, and umble to that; and to pull off our
cap here, and to make bow there; and always to know our place,
and abase ourselves before our better. and we have such a lot of
better! Father get the monitor-medal by be umble. so do I.
Father get make a sexton by be umble. he have the character,
among the gentlefolk, of be such a well-behave man, that they
be determined to bring he in. "be umble, Uriah," say father to
I, "and youwill get on. it be what be always be din into
you and I at school; itbe what go down well. be umble," say
father, "and youwill do!" and really it benot do bad!'
it be the first time it have ever occur to I, that this
detestable cannot of false humility might have originate out of the
Heep family. I have see the harvest, but have never think of the
seed.
'when I be quite a young boy,' say Uriah, 'I get to know what
umbleness do, and I take to it. I eat umble pie with an appetite.
I stop at the umble point of my learning, and say I, "hold
hard!" when you offer to teach I Latin, I know well. "People
like to be above you," say father, "keep yourself down." I be very
umble to the present moment, Master Copperfield, but Ihave get a
little power!'
and he say all this - I know, as I see his face in the moonlight
- that I might understand he be resolve to recompense himself by
use his power. I have never doubt his meanness, his craft and
malice; but I fully comprehend now, for the first time, what a
base, unrelenting, and revengeful spirit, must have be engender
by this early, and this long, suppression.
his account of himself be so far attend with an agreeable
result, that it lead to his withdraw his hand in order that he
might have another hug of himself under the chin. once apart from
he, I be determined to keep apart; and we walk back, side by

indulge in this retrospect, I donot know; but they be raise by
some influence. he talk more at dinner than be usual with he;
ask his mother (off duty, from the moment of our re-enter the

look at Agnes so, that I would have give all I have, for leave to
knock he down.
when we three male be leave alone after dinner, he get into a
more adventurous state. he have take little or no wine; and I
presume it be the mere insolence of triumph that be upon he,
flush perhaps by the temptation my presence furnish to its
exhibition.
I have observe yesterday, that he try to entice Mr. Wickfield to

we should follow she. I would have do so again today; but Uriah
be too quick for I.
'we seldom see our present visitor, sir,' he say, address Mr.
Wickfield, sit, such a contrast to he, at the end of the

or two of wine, if you have no objection. Mr. Copperfield, your
elth and appiness!'
I be oblige to make a show of take the hand he stretch across
to I; and then, with very different emotion, I take the hand of
the broken gentleman, his partner.
'come, fellow-partner,' say Uriah, 'if I may take the liberty, -
now, suppose you give we something or another appropriate to
Copperfield!'
I pass over Mr. Wickfield's propose my aunt, his propose Mr.
Dick, his propose doctor' Commons, his propose Uriah, his
drink everything twice; his consciousness of his own weakness,
the ineffectual effort that he make against it; the struggle
between his shame in Uriah's deportment, and his desire to
conciliate he; the manifest exultation with which Uriah twist

see, and my hand recoil from write it.
'come, fellow-partner!' say Uriah, at last, 'Iwill give you another
one, and I umbly ask for bumper, see I intend to make it the
divinest of her sex.'
her father have his empty glass in his hand. I see he set it down,
look at the picture she be so like, put his hand to his forehead,
and shrink back in his elbow-chair.
'Ibe an umble individual to give you her elth,' proceed Uriah,
'but I admire - adore she.'
no physical pain that her father's grey head could have bear, I
think, could have be more terrible to I, than the mental
endurance I see compress now within both his hand.
'Agnes,' say Uriah, either not regard he, or not know what
the nature of his action be, 'Agnes Wickfield be, I be safe to
say, the divinest of her sex. may I speak out, among friend? to
be her father be a proud distinction, but to be her usband -'
spare I from ever again hear such a cry, as that with which her
father rise up from the table!
'whatbe the matter?' say Uriah, turn of a deadly colour. 'you
be not go mad, after all, Mr. Wickfield, I hope? if I say Ihave
an ambition to make your Agnes my Agnes, I have as good a right to
it as another man. I have a well right to it than any other
man!'
I have my arm round Mr. Wickfield, implore he by everything that
I could think of, oftenest of all by his love for Agnes, to calm

beat his head, try to force I from he, and to force himself
from I, not answer a word, not look at or see anyone;
blindly strive for he know not what, his face all star and
distort - a frightful spectacle.
I conjure he, incoherently, but in the most impassioned manner,
not to abandon himself to this wildness, but to hear I. I
besought he to think of Agnes, to connect I with Agnes, to
recollect how Agnes and I have grow up together, how I honour she
and love she, how she be his pride and joy. I try to bring she
idea before he in any form; I even reproach he with not have
firmness to spare she the knowledge of such a scene as this. I may
have effect something, or his wildness may have spend itself; but
by degree he struggle less, and begin to look at I - strangely
at first, then with recognition in his eye. at length he say, 'I
know, Trotwood! my darling child and you - I know! but look at
he!'
he point to Uriah, pale and glower in a corner, evidently very
much out in his calculation, and take by surprise.
'look at my torturer,' he reply. 'before he I have step by step
abandon name and reputation, peace and quiet, house and home.'
'I have keep your name and reputation for you, and your peace and
quiet, and your house and home too,' say Uriah, with a sulky,
hurry, defeat air of compromise. 'donot be foolish, Mr.
Wickfield. if I have go a little beyond what you be prepare
for, I can go back, I suppose? therebe no harm do.'
'I look for single motif in everyone,' say Mr. Wickfield, and
I be satisfied I have bind he to I by motif of interest. but
see what he be - oh, see what he be!'
'you have well stop he, Copperfield, if you can,' cry Uriah,
with his long forefinger point towards I. 'hewill say something
presently - mind you! - hewill be sorry to have say afterwards, and
youwill be sorry to have hear!'
'Iwill say anything!' cry Mr. Wickfield, with a desperate air.
'why should I not be in all the world's power if I be in your?'
'mind! I tell you!' say Uriah, continue to warn I. 'if you
donot stop his mouth, yoube not his friend! why shouldnot you be
in all the world's power, Mr. Wickfield? because you have get a
daughter. you and I know what we know, donot we? let sleep
dog lie - who want to rouse them? I donot. cannot you see I be as

what would you have, sir?'
'oh, Trotwood, trotwood!'exclaime Mr. Wickfield, wring his
hand. 'what I have come down to be, since I first see you in this
house! I be on my downward way then, but the dreary, dreary road
I have traverse since! weak indulgence have ruin I. Indulgence
in remembrance, and indulgence in forgetfulness. my natural grief
for my child's mother turn to disease; my natural love for my
child turn to disease. I have infect everything I touch. I
have bring misery on what I dearly love, I know -you know! I
think it possible that I could truly love one creature in the
world, and not love the rest; I think it possible that I could
truly mourn for one creature go out of the world, and not have
some part in the grief of all who mourn. thus the lesson of my
life have be pervert! I have prey on my own morbid coward
heart, and it have prey on I. Sordid in my grief, sordid in my
love, sordid in my miserable escape from the dark side of both,
oh see the ruin I be, and hate I, shun I!'
he drop into a chair, and weakly sob. the excitement into
which he have be rouse be leave he. Uriah come out of his
corner.
'I donot know all I have do, in my fatuity,' say Mr. Wickfield,
put out his hand, as if to deprecate my condemnation. 'he
know well,' mean Uriah Heep, 'for he have always be at my
elbow, whisper I. you see the millstone that he be about my
neck. you find he in my house, you find he in my business. you
hear he, but a little time ago. what need have I to say more!'
'you havenot need to say so much, nor half so much, nor anything at
all,' observe Uriah, half defiant, and half fawn. 'you
wouldnot have take it up so, if it havenot be for the wine.
youwill think well of it tomorrow, sir. if I have say too much,
or more than I mean, what of it? I havenot stand by it!'
the door open, and Agnes, glide in, without a vestige of colour
in her face, put her arm round his neck, and steadily say, 'Papa,
you be not well. come with I!'
he lay his head upon her shoulder, as if he be oppress with
heavy shame, and go out with she. her eye meet mine for but an
instant, yet I see how much she know of what have pass.
'I donot expect hewould cut up so rough, Master Copperfield,' say
Uriah. 'but itbe nothing. Iwill be friend with he tomorrow.
itbe for his good. Ibe umbly anxious for his good.'
I give he no answer, and go upstairs into the quiet room where
Agnes have so often sit beside I at my book. nobody come near I
until late at night. I take up a book, and try to read. I hear
the clock strike twelve, and be still read, without know
what I read, when Agnes touch I.
'you will be go early in the morning, Trotwood! let we say
good-bye, now!'
she have be weep, but her face then be so calm and beautiful!
'Heaven bless you!' she say, give I her hand.
'Dearest Agnes!' I return, 'I see you ask I not to speak of
tonight - but be there nothing to be do?'
'there be God to trust in!' she reply.
'can I do nothing- I, who come to you with my poor sorrow?'
'and make mine so much light,' she reply. 'Dear Trotwood, no!'
'Dear Agnes,' I say, 'it be presumptuous for I, who be so poor in
all in which you be so rich - goodness, resolution, all noble
quality - to doubt or direct you; but you know how much I love
you, and how much I owe you. you will never sacrifice yourself to
a mistaken sense of duty, Agnes?'
more agitated for a moment than I have ever see she, she take she
hand from I, and move a step back.
'say you have no such thought, dear Agnes! much more than sister!
think of the priceless gift of such a heart as your, of such a
love as your!'
oh! long, long afterwards, I see that face rise up before I, with
its momentary look, not wonder, not accuse, not regret.
oh, long, long afterwards, I see that look subside, as it do now,
into the lovely smile, with which she tell I she have no fear for
herself - I need have none for she - and part from I by the name
of Brother, and be go!
it be dark in the morning, when I get upon the coach at the inn
door. the day be just break when we be about to start, and
then, as I sit think of she, come struggle up the coach side,
through the mingled day and night, Uriah's head.
'copperfield!' say he, in a croak whisper, as he hang by the
iron on the roof, 'I think youwould be glad to hear before you go
off, that there be no square break between we. Ihave be into
his room already, and wehave make it all smooth. why, though Ibe
umble, Ibe useful to he, you know; and he understand his interest
when he benot in liquor! what an agreeable man he be, after all,
Master Copperfield!'
I oblige myself to say that I be glad he have make his apology.
'oh, to be sure!' say Uriah. 'when a person's umble, you know,
whatbe an apology? so easy! I say! I suppose,' with a jerk, 'you
have sometimes pluck a pear before it be ripe, Master
Copperfield?'
'I suppose I have,' I reply.
'I do that last night,' say Uriah; 'but itwill ripen yet! it only
want attend to. I can wait!'
profuse in his farewell, he get down again as the coachman get up.
for anything I know, he be eat something to keep the raw
morning air out; but he make motion with his mouth as if the pear
be ripe already, and he be smack his lip over it.
CHAPTER 40
the wanderer
we have a very serious conversation in Buckingham Street that night,
about the domestic occurrence I have detail in the last chapter.
my aunt be deeply interested in they, and walk up and down the
room with her arm fold, for more than two hour afterwards.
whenever she be particularly discompose, she always perform one
of these pedestrian feat; and the amount of her discomposure might
always be estimate by the duration of her walk. on this occasion
she be so much disturb in mind as to find it necessary to open
the bedroom door, and make a course for herself, comprise the
full extent of the bedroom from wall to wall; and while Mr. Dick
and I sit quietly by the fire, she keep pass in and out, along
this measured track, at an unchanging pace, with the regularity of
a clock-pendulum.
when my aunt and I be leave to ourselves by Mr. Dick's go out

that time she be tired of walk, and sit by the fire with her

she read my letter to the two old lady, in the morning, and

it have be a bitter day, and a cut north-east wind have blow

great flake; and it lie thick. the noise of wheel and tread of
people be as hush, as if the street have be strew that depth
with feather.
my short way home, - and I naturally take the short way on

which give its name to the lane, stand in a less free situation at
that time; there be no open space before it, and the lane

see it somewhere. but I could not remember where. I have some

then I remember the woman. it be Martha, to whom Emily have

I, for all the treasure wreck in the sea.
we shake hand heartily. at first, neither of we could speak a

'Mas'r Davy!' he say, grip I tight, 'it do my art good to see

'well meet, my dear old friend!' say I.

in those day there be a side-entrance to the stable-yard of the

when I see he in the light, I observe, not only that his hair be

I ring the bell for something hot to drink. he would have nothing

we see his mother, I know'd quite well as I be right. I go
across-channel to France, and land theer, as if Ihave fall down

I see the door move, and the snow drift in. I see it move a little

'I find out an English gen'leman as be in authority,' say Mr.

'mostly a-foot,' he rejoin; 'sometimes in cart along with people

I should have know that by his friendly tone.
'when I come to any town,' he pursue, 'I find the inn, and wait

know'd English. then I tell how that I be on my way to seek my

when it warn't Em'ly, I go on agen. by little and little, when

it be Martha at the door. I see she haggard, listen face

the listening face, insensible to the inclement night, still

'I never doubt she,' say Mr. Peggotty. 'no! not a bit! On'y

as he go out into the rigorous night, I see the lonely figure
