CHAPTER 41
dora's aunt
at last, an answer come from the two old lady. they present
their compliment to Mr. Copperfield, and inform he that they
have give his letter their good consideration, 'with a view to the
happiness of both party' - which I think rather an alarming
expression, not only because of the use they have make of it in
relation to the family difference before-mention, but because I
have (and have all my life) observe that conventional phrase be
a sort of firework, easily let off, and liable to take a great
variety of shape and colour not at all suggest by their
original form. the Misses Spenlow add that they beg to
forbear express, 'through the medium of correspondence', an
opinion on the subject of Mr. Copperfield's communication; but that
if Mr. Copperfield would do they the favour to call, upon a certain
day (accompany, if he think proper, by a confidential friend),
they would be happy to hold some conversation on the subject.
to this favour, Mr. Copperfield immediately reply, with his
respectful compliment, that he would have the honour of wait on
the Misses Spenlow, at the time appoint; accompany, in
accordance with their kind permission, by his friend Mr. Thomas
traddle of the Inner Temple. have dispatch which missive, Mr.
Copperfield fall into a condition of strong nervous agitation; and
so remain until the day arrive.
it be a great augmentation of my uneasiness to be bereave, at
this eventful crisis, of the inestimable service of Miss Mills.
but Mr. Mills, who be always do something or other to annoy I
- or I feel as if he be, which be the same thing - have bring
his conduct to a climax, by take it into his head that he would
go to India. why should he go to India, except to harass I? to
be sure he have nothing to do with any other part of the world, and
have a good deal to do with that part; be entirely in the India
trade, whatever that be (I have float dream myself concern
golden shawl and elephant' tooth); have be at Calcutta in his
youth; and design now to go out there again, in the capacity of
resident partner. but this be nothing to I. however, it be so
much to he that for India he be bind, and Julia with he; and
Julia go into the country to take leave of her relation; and the
house be put into a perfect suit of bill, announce that it be
to be let or sell, and that the furniture (Mangle and all) be to
be take at a valuation. so, here be another earthquake of which
I become the sport, before I have recover from the shock of its
predecessor!
I be in several mind how to dress myself on the important day;
be divide between my desire to appear to advantage, and my
apprehension of put on anything that might impair my severely
practical character in the eye of the Misses Spenlow. I
endeavour to hit a happy medium between these two extreme; my
aunt approve the result; and Mr. Dick throw one of his shoe after
traddle and I, for luck, as we go downstairs.
excellent fellow as I know traddle to be, and warmly attach to
he as I be, I could not help wish, on that delicate occasion,
that he have never contract the habit of brush his hair so very
upright. it give he a surprised look - not to say a hearth-broomy
kind of expression - which, my apprehension whisper, might be
fatal to we.
I take the liberty of mention it to traddle, as we be walk
to Putney; and say that if he would smooth it down a little -
'my dear copperfield,' say traddle, lift off his hat, and
rub his hair all kind of way, 'nothing would give I great
pleasure. but it willnot.'
'willnot be smooth down?' say I.
'no,' say traddle. 'nothing will induce it. if I be to carry
a half-hundred-weight upon it, all the way to Putney, it would be
up again the moment the weight be take off. you have no idea
what obstinate hair mine be, Copperfield. I be quite a fretful
porcupine.'
I be a little disappointed, I must confess, but thoroughly charm
by his good-nature too. I tell he how I esteem his good-nature;
and say that his hair must have take all the obstinacy out of his
character, for he have none.
'oh!' return traddle, laugh. 'I assure you, itbe quite an
old story, my unfortunate hair. my uncle's wife couldnot bear it.
she say it exasperate she. it stand very much in my way, too,
when I first fall in love with Sophy. very much!'
'do she object to it?'
'she donot,' rejoin traddle; 'but her eld sister - the one

'perfectly!'

his honest face, as he look at I with a serio-comic shake of his

'my dear Dora! now, indeed, my own for ever!'

the only member of our small society who positively refuse to
adapt himself to circumstance, be Jip. he never see my aunt
without immediately display every tooth in his head, retire
under a chair, and growl incessantly: with now and then a
doleful howl, as if she really be too much for his feeling. all
kind of treatment be try with he, coax, scold,

never could prevail upon himself to bear my aunt's society. he
would sometimes think he have get the well of his objection, and

report at the door.

when we pass a butcher's shop, I would say:
'now suppose, my pet, that we be married, and you be go to
buy a shoulder of mutton for dinner, would you know how to buy it?'
my pretty little Dora's face would fall, and she would make she
mouth into a bud again, as if she would very much prefer to shut
mine with a kiss.
'would you know how to buy it, my darling?' I would repeat,
perhaps, if I be very inflexible.
Dora would think a little, and then reply, perhaps, with great
triumph:
'why, the butcher would know how to sell it, and what need I know?
oh, you silly boy!'
so, when I once ask Dora, with an eye to the cookery-book, what
she would do, if we be marry, and I be to say I should like
a nice irish stew, she reply that she would tell the servant to
make it; and then clap she little hand together across my arm,
and laugh in such a charming manner that she be more delightful
than ever.
consequently, the principal use to which the cookery-book be
devote, be be put down in the corner for Jip to stand upon.
but Dora be so pleased, when she have train he to stand upon it
without offer to come off, and at the same time to hold the
pencil-case in his mouth, that I be very glad I have buy it.
and we fall back on the guitar-case, and the flower-painting, and
the song about never leave off dancing, Ta ra la! and be as
happy as the week be long. I occasionally wish I could venture
to hint to Miss Lavinia, that she treat the darling of my heart
a little too much like a plaything; and I sometimes awake, as it
be, wonder to find that I have fall into the general fault,
and treat she like a playthe too - but not often.
CHAPTER 42
MISCHIEF
I feel as if it be not for I to record, even though this
manuscript be intend for no eye but mine, how hard I work at
that tremendous short-hand, and all improvement appertaining to it,
in my sense of responsibility to Dora and her aunt. I will only
add, to what I have already write of my perseverance at this time
of my life, and of a patient and continuous energy which then begin
to be mature within I, and which I know to be the strong part of
my character, if it have any strength at all, that there, on
look back, I find the source of my success. I have be very
fortunate in worldly matter; many man have work much hard, and
not succeed half so well; but I never could have do what I have
do, without the habit of punctuality, order, and diligence,
without the determination to concentrate myself on one object at a
time, no matter how quickly its successor should come upon its
heel, which I then form. Heaven know I write this, in no
spirit of self-laudation. the man who review his own life, as I
do mine, in go on here, from page to page, have need to have be
a good man indeed, if he would be spar the sharp consciousness of
many talent neglect, many opportunity waste, many erratic and
pervert feeling constantly at war within his breast, and
defeat he. I do not hold one natural gift, I dare say, that I
have not abuse. my meaning simply be, that whatever I have try
to do in life, I have try with all my heart to do well; that
whatever I have devote myself to, I have devote myself to
completely; that in great aim and in small, I have always be
thoroughly in earnest. I have never believe it possible that any
natural or improved ability can claim immunity from the
companionship of the steady, plain, hard-work quality, and
hope to gain its end. there be no such thing as such fulfilment on
this earth. some happy talent, and some fortunate opportunity, may
form the two side of the ladder on which some man mount, but the
round of that ladder must be make of stuff to stand wear and tear;
and there be no substitute for thorough-go, ardent, and sincere
earnestness. never to put one hand to anything, on which I could
throw my whole self; and never to affect depreciation of my work,
whatever it be; I find, now, to have be my golden rule.
how much of the practice I have just reduce to precept, I owe to
Agnes, I will not repeat here. my narrative proceed to Agnes,
with a thankful love.
she come on a visit of a fortnight to the Doctor's. Mr. Wickfield

his eye look green now, as they watch mine with a rascally

young. I take she to myself when her character be scarcely

affection; I ask pardon of that lady, in my heart!'
he walk across the room, and come back to the same place; hold
the chair with a grasp that tremble, like his subdued voice, in
its earnestness.
'I regard myself as a refuge, for she, from the danger and
vicissitude of life. I persuade myself that, unequal though we
be in year, she would live tranquilly and contentedly with I.
I do not shut out of my consideration the time when I should leave
she free, and still young and still beautiful, but with she
judgement more mature - no, gentleman - upon my truth!'
his homely figure seem to be lighten up by his fidelity and
generosity. every word he utter have a force that no other grace
could have impart to it.
'my life with this lady have be very happy. until tonight, I have
have uninterrupted occasion to bless the day on which I do she
great injustice.'
his voice, more and more faltering in the utterance of these word,

'once awaken from my dream - I have be a poor dreamer, in one
way or other, all my life - I see how natural it be that she should

equal. that she do regard he with some innocent regret, with
some blameless thought of what might have be, but for I, be, I
fear, too true. much that I have see, but not note, have come

beyond this, gentleman, the dear lady's name never must be couple
with a word, a breath, of doubt.'
for a little while, his eye kindle and his voice be firm; for a

'it only remain for I, to bear the knowledge of the unhappiness
I have occasion, as submissively as I can. it be she who should

misconstruction, that even my friend have not be able to avoid,
become my duty. the more retired we live, the well I shall

his merciful pleasure! - when my death shall release she from
constraint, I shall close my eye upon her honoured face, with
unbounded confidence and love; and leave she, with no sorrow then,
to happy and bright day.'
I could not see he for the tear which his earnestness and

he add:

it. what we have say tonight be never to be say more.
Wickfield, give I an old friend's arm upstairs!'
Mr. Wickfield hasten to he. without interchange a word they

brickbat; but this familybe out of the cart, I think!'
I need but the sound of his voice to be so madly enrage as I

'you villain,' say I, 'what do you mean by entrap I into your
scheme? how dare you appeal to I just now, you false rascal, as
if we have be in discussion together?'
as we stand, front to front, I see so plainly, in the stealthy

he force his confidence upon I, expressly to make I miserable,

my finger tingle as if I have burn they.
he catch the hand in his, and we stand in that connexion, look

and leave it a deep red.
'copperfield,' he say at length, in a breathless voice, 'have you

dog, Iwill know no more of you.'

in the morning, when I come out, the early church-bell be ring,

the Doctor give out that he be not quite well; and remain alone,

Neither, I feel convinced, have Mrs. Strong then. several week

as this change steal on Annie, once like sunshine in the Doctor's

cannot say how sorrowfully.
sometimes, after that, I fancy that she try to speak even to

each know that the other like he, and that he love both; and he

when I think of he, with his impenetrably wise face, walk up

have know that he be not quite in his wit, take account of
the utmost I have do with mine.
'nobody but myself, Trot, know what that man be!' my aunt would
proudly remark, when we converse about it. 'Dick will distinguish
himself yet!'
I must refer to one other topic before I close this chapter. while
the visit at the Doctor's be still in progress, I observe that
the postman bring two or three letter every morning for Uriah
Heep, who remain at Highgate until the rest go back, it be

'CANTERBURY, Monday Evening.
'you will doubtless be surprised, my dear Mr. Copperfield, to
receive this communication. still more so, by its content. still
more so, by the stipulation of implicit confidence which I beg to
impose. but my feeling as a wife and mother require relief; and
as I do not wish to consult my family (already obnoxious to the
feeling of Mr. Micawber), I know no one of whom I can well ask
advice than my friend and former lodger.
'you may be aware, my dear Mr. Copperfield, that between myself and
Mr. Micawber (whom I will never desert), there have always be
preserve a spirit of mutual confidence. Mr. Micawber may have
occasionally give a bill without consult I, or he may have
mislead I as to the period when that obligation would become due.
this have actually happen. but, in general, Mr. Micawber have have
no secret from the bosom of affection - I allude to his wife - and
have invariably, on our retirement to rest, recall the event of
the day.
'you will picture to yourself, my dear Mr. Copperfield, what the
poignancy of my feeling must be, when I inform you that Mr.
Micawber be entirely change. he be reserve. he be secret. his
life be a mystery to the partner of his joy and sorrow - I again
allude to his wife - and if I should assure you that beyond know
that it be pass from morning to night at the office, I now know
less of it than I do of the man in the south, connect with whose
mouth the thoughtless child repeat an idle tale respect cold
plum porridge, I should adopt a popular fallacy to express an
actual fact.
'but this be not all. Mr. Micawber be morose. he be severe. he
be estranged from our eld son and daughter, he have no pride in
his twin, he look with an eye of coldness even on the unoffending
stranger who last become a member of our circle. the pecuniary
mean of meet our expense, keep down to the utmost farthing,
be obtain from he with great difficulty, and even under fearful
threat that he will settle himself (the exact expression); and he
inexorably refuse to give any explanation whatever of this
distracting policy.
'this be hard to bear. this be heart-breaking. if you will advise
I, know my feeble power such as they be, how you think it
will be good to exert they in a dilemma so unwonted, you will add
another friendly obligation to the many you have already render
I. with love from the child, and a smile from the
happily-unconscious stranger, I remain, dear Mr. Copperfield,
your afflict,
'EMMA MICAWBER.'
I do not feel justified in give a wife of Mrs. Micawber's
experience any other recommendation, than that she should try to
reclaim Mr. Micawber by patience and kindness (as I know she would
in any case); but the letter set I think about he very much.
CHAPTER 43
another retrospect
once again, let I pause upon a memorable period of my life. let
I stand aside, to see the phantom of those day go by I,
accompany the shadow of myself, in dim procession.
week, month, season, pass along. they seem little more than a
summer day and a winter evening. now, the Common where I walk with
Dora be all in bloom, a field of bright gold; and now the unseen
heather lie in mound and bunch underneath a covering of snow.
in a breath, the river that flow through our Sunday walk be
sparkle in the summer sun, be ruffle by the winter wind, or
thicken with drift heap of ice. fast than ever river run
towards the sea, it flash, darken, and roll away.
not a thread change, in the house of the two little bird-like
lady. the clock tick over the fireplace, the weather-glass
hang in the hall. neither clock nor weather-glass be ever right;
but we believe in both, devoutly.
I have come legally to man's estate. I have attain the dignity
of twenty-one. but this be a sort of dignity that may be thrust
upon one. let I think what I have achieve.
I have tame that savage stenographic mystery. I make a
respectable income by it. I be in high repute for my
accomplishment in all pertain to the art, and be join with
eleven other in report the debate in Parliament for a Morning
Newspaper. night after night, I record prediction that never come
to pass, profession that be never fulfil, explanation that
be only mean to mystify. I wallow in word. Britannia, that
unfortunate female, be always before I, like a truss fowl:
skewer through and through with office-pen, and bind hand and
foot with red tape. I be sufficiently behind the scene to know
the worth of political life. I be quite an Infidel about it, and
shall never be convert.
my dear old traddle have try his hand at the same pursuit, but it
be not in Traddles's way. he be perfectly good-humour respect
his failure, and remind I that he always do consider himself

get up the fact of dry subject, to be write about and

pound together, to fee a conveyancer whose chamber he attend.
a great deal of very hot port wine be consume at his call; and,
consider the figure, I should think the Inner Temple must have
make a profit by it.
I have come out in another way. I have take with fear and

and send it to a magazine, and it be publish in the magazine.

the third finger and take in the fourth to the middle joint.
we have remove, from Buckingham Street, to a pleasant little

yes! I be go to be marry to Dora! Miss Lavinia and Miss

Miss Clarissa and my aunt roam all over London, to find out
article of furniture for Dora and I to look at. it would be

Peggotty come up to make herself useful, and fall to work

why do traddle look so important when he call upon I this

of the clergyman and clerk appear; of a few boatman and some

of our kneeling down together, side by side; of Dora's trembling

of my walk so proudly and lovingly down the aisle with my sweet

CHAPTER 44
our housekeeping
it be a strange condition of thing, the honeymoon be over, and
the bridesmaid go home, when I find myself sit down in my
own small house with Dora; quite throw out of employment, as I may
say, in respect of the delicious old occupation of make love.
it seem such an extraordinary thing to have Dora always there.
it be so unaccountable not to be oblige to go out to see she, not
to have any occasion to be torment myself about she, not to have
to write to she, not to be scheme and devise opportunity of
be alone with she. sometimes of an evening, when I look up
from my writing, and see her seat opposite, I would lean back in
my chair, and think how queer it be that there we be, alone
together as a matter of course - nobody's business any more - all
the romance of our engagement put away upon a shelf, to rust - no
one to please but one another - one another to please, for life.
when there be a debate, and I be keep out very late, it seem so
strange to I, as I be walk home, to think that Dora be at
home! it be such a wonderful thing, at first, to have she come
softly down to talk to I as I eat my supper. it be such a
stupendous thing to know for certain that she put her hair in
paper. it be altogether such an astonishing event to see she do
it!
I doubt whether two young bird could have know less about keep
house, than I and my pretty Dora do. we have a servant, of course.
she keep house for we. I have still a latent belief that she must
have be Mrs. Crupp's daughter in disguise, we have such an awful
time of it with Mary Anne.
her name be Paragon. her nature be represent to we, when we
engage she, as be feebly express in her name. she have a
write character, as large as a proclamation; and, accord to
this document, could do everything of a domestic nature that ever
I hear of, and a great many thing that I never do hear of. she
be a woman in the prime of life; of a severe countenance; and
subject (particularly in the arm) to a sort of perpetual measle
or fiery rash. she have a cousin in the Life-Guards, with such long
leg that he look like the afternoon shadow of somebody else.
his shell-jacket be as much too little for he as he be too big
for the premise. he make the cottage small than it need have
be, by be so very much out of proportion to it. besides
which, the wall be not thick, and, whenever he pass the
evening at our house, we always know of it by hear one continual
growl in the kitchen.
our treasure be warrant sober and honest. I be therefore
willing to believe that she be in a fit when we find she under
the boiler; and that the deficient tea-spoon be attributable to
the dustman.
but she prey upon our mind dreadfully. we feel our
inexperience, and be unable to help ourselves. we should have
be at her mercy, if she have have any; but she be a remorseless
woman, and have none. she be the cause of our first little
quarrel.
'my dearest life,' I say one day to Dora, 'do you think Mary Anne
have any idea of time?'
'why, Doady?' inquire Dora, look up, innocently, from she
drawing.
'my love, because itbe five, and we be to have din at four.'
Dora glance wistfully at the clock, and hint that she think it
be too fast.
'on the contrary, my love,' say I, refer to my watch, 'itbe a
few minute too slow.'
my little wife come and sit upon my knee, to coax I to be quiet,
and draw a line with her pencil down the middle of my nose; but I
couldnot dine off that, though it be very agreeable.
'donot you think, my dear,' say I, 'it would be well for you to
remonstrate with Mary Anne?'
'oh no, please! I couldnot, Doady!' say Dora.
'why not, my love?' I gently ask.
'oh, because I be such a little goose,' say Dora, 'and she know
I be!'
I think this sentiment so incompatible with the establishment of
any system of check on Mary Anne, that I frown a little.
'oh, what ugly wrinkle in my bad boy's forehead!' say Dora, and
still be on my knee, she trace they with her pencil; put it
to her rosy lip to make it mark blacker, and work at my
forehead with a quaint little mockery of be industrious, that
quite delight I in spite of myself.
'therebe a good child,' say Dora, 'it make its face so much
pretty to laugh.'
'but, my love,' say I.
'no, no! please!' cry Dora, with a kiss, 'donot be a naughty Blue
Beard! donot be serious!'
'my precious wife,' say I, 'we must be serious sometimes. come!

there! now let we talk sensibly. you know, dear'; what a little
hand it be to hold, and what a tiny wedding-ring it be to see!

'n-n-no!' reply Dora, faintly.
'my love, how you tremble!'

'my sweet, I be only go to reason.'
'oh, but reasoning be bad than scold!' exclaim Dora, in

tell I so, you cruel boy!'
I try to pacify Dora, but she turn away her face, and shake she

I feel so injure by the inconsequential nature of this charge,

wickedness.
it be two or three hour past midnight when I get home. I find

'nothing, Trot,' she reply. 'sit down, sit down. little Blossom
have be rather out of spirit, and I have be keep her

I lean my head upon my hand; and feel more sorry and downcast, as

'trot,' return my aunt, with some emotion, 'no! donot ask I

her tone be so very earnest that I raise my eye in surprise.

the next domestic trial we go through, be the Ordeal of

everybody we have anything to do with seem to cheat we. our

I have reason to believe that in accomplish these failure we

as to the washerwoman pawn the clothe, and come in a state of

this appeal of Dora's make a strong impression on I. I look back

over the page, wag his tail, and smear they all out. her own
little right-hand middle finger get steep to the very bone in
ink; and I think that be the only decide result obtain.
sometimes, of an evening, when I be at home and at work - for I
write a good deal now, and be begin in a small way to be know
as a writer - I would lay down my pen, and watch my child-wife
try to be good. first of all, she would bring out the immense
account-book, and lie it down upon the table, with a deep sigh.
then she would open it at the place where Jip have make it illegible
last night, and call Jip up, to look at his misdeed. this would
occasion a diversion in Jip's favour, and some inking of his nose,
perhaps, as a penalty. then she would tell Jip to lie down on the

though I cannot say the likeness be strike - and, if he be in
an obedient humour, he would obey. then she would take up a pen,
and begin to write, and find a hair in it. then she would take up
another pen, and begin to write, and find that it splutter. then
she would take up another pen, and begin to write, and say in a low
voice, 'oh, itbe a talk pen, and will disturb Doady!' and then
she would give it up as a bad job, and put the account-book away,
after pretend to crush the lion with it.
or, if she be in a very sedate and serious state of mind, she
would sit down with the tablet, and a little basket of bill and
other document, which look more like curl-paper than anything
else, and endeavour to get some result out of they. after severely
compare one with another, and make entry on the tablet, and
blot they out, and count all the finger of her left hand
over and over again, backwards and forwards, she would be so vexed
and discourage, and would look so unhappy, that it give I pain to
see her bright face clouded - and for I! - and I would go softly
to she, and say:
'whatbe the matter, Dora?'
Dora would look up hopelessly, and reply, 'they willnot come right.
they make my head ache so. and they willnot do anything I want!'
then I would say, 'now let we try together. let I show you,
Dora.'
then I would commence a practical demonstration, to which Dora
would pay profound attention, perhaps for five minute; when she
would begin to be dreadfully tired, and would lighten the subject
by curl my hair, or try the effect of my face with my
shirt-collar turn down. if I tacitly check this playfulness,

become more and more bewildered, that the remembrance of her
natural gaiety when I first stray into her path, and of her be
my child-wife, would come reproachfully upon I; and I would lay
the pencil down, and call for the guitar.
I have a great deal of work to do, and have many anxiety, but the
same consideration make I keep they to myself. I be far from
sure, now, that it be right to do this, but I do it for my
child-wife's sake. I search my breast, and I commit its secret,
if I know they, without any reservation to this paper. the old
unhappy loss or want of something have, I be conscious, some place
in my heart; but not to the embitterment of my life. when I walk
alone in the fine weather, and thought of the summer day when all
the air have be fill with my boyish enchantment, I do miss
something of the realization of my dream; but I think it be a
soften glory of the Past, which nothing could have throw upon
the present time. I do feel, sometimes, for a little while, that
I could have wish my wife have be my counsellor; have have more
character and purpose, to sustain I and improve I by; have be
endow with power to fill up the void which somewhere seem to be
about I; but I feel as if this be an unearthly consummation of
my happiness, that never have be mean to be, and never could have
be.
I be a boyish husband as to year. I have know the soften
influence of no other sorrow or experience than those record in
these leaf. if I do any wrong, as I may have do much, I do
it in mistaken love, and in my want of wisdom. I write the exact
truth. it would avail I nothing to extenuate it now.
thus it be that I take upon myself the toil and care of our
life, and have no partner in they. we live much as before, in
reference to our scramble household arrangement; but I have get
use to those, and Dora I be pleased to see be seldom vex now.
she be bright and cheerful in the old childish way, love I
dearly, and be happy with her old trifle.
when the debate be heavy - I mean as to length, not quality, for
in the last respect they be not often otherwise - and I go home

always come downstairs to meet I. when my evening be
unoccupied by the pursuit for which I have qualify myself with so

would often think she have drop asleep. but generally, when I
raise my head, I see her blue eye look at I with the quiet
attention of which I have already speak.
'oh, what a weary boy!' say Dora one night, when I meet her eye as
I be shut up my desk.
'what a weary girl!' say I. 'thatbe more to the purpose. you
must go to bed another time, my love. itbe far too late for you.'
'no, donot send I to bed!' plead Dora, come to my side.
'pray, donot do that!'
'Dora!' to my amazement she be sob on my neck. 'not well, my
dear! not happy!'
'yes! quite well, and very happy!' say Dora. 'but say youwill let
I stop, and see you write.'
'why, what a sight for such bright eye at midnight!' I reply.
'be they bright, though?' return Dora, laugh. 'Ibe so glad
theybe bright.'
'Little vanity!' say I.
but it be not vanity; it be only harmless delight in my
admiration. I know that very well, before she tell I so.
'if you think they pretty, say I may always stop, and see you
write!' say Dora. 'do you think they pretty?'
'very pretty.'
'then let I always stop and see you write.'
'I be afraid that willnot improve their brightness, Dora.'
'yes, it will! because, you clever boy, youwill not forget I then,

peep over my shoulder into my face.
'what wonderful thing be that?' say I.
'please let I hold the pen,' say Dora. 'I want to have

the remembrance of her pretty joy when I say yes, bring tear

neck, be touch recollection to I, simple as they might appear
to other man.
she take possession of the key soon after this, and go jingle

belong be lock, or that they be of any use except as a

be quite satisfied that a good deal be effect by this
make-belief of housekeeping; and be as merry as if we have be

so we go on. Dora be hardly less affectionate to my aunt than

'a cross old thing'. I never see my aunt unbend more

'wherebe Little Blossom?'
CHAPTER 45
MR. DICK FULFILS MY AUNT's prediction
it be some time now, since I have leave the Doctor. live in his
neighbourhood, I see he frequently; and we all go to his house
on two or three occasion to dinner or tea. the Old Soldier be in
permanent quarter under the doctor's roof. she be exactly the
same as ever, and the same immortal butterfly hover over her
cap.
like some other mother, whom I have know in the course of my
life, Mrs. Markleham be far more fond of pleasure than her
daughter be. she require a great deal of amusement, and, like a
deep old soldier, pretend, in consult her own inclination, to
be devote herself to her child. the Doctor's desire that Annie
should be entertain, be therefore particularly acceptable to
this excellent parent; who express unqualified approval of his
discretion.
I have no doubt, indeed, that she probe the Doctor's wound without
know it. mean nothing but a certain mature frivolity and

she confirm he in his fear that he be a constraint upon his

'my dear soul,' she say to he one day when I be present, 'you

the Doctor nod his benevolent head. 'when she come to her

call an italian-iron, a bedstead. but we cannot expect a

even the calm and patient face of Doctor Strong express some

she be as good as her word. she be one of those people who can

this be usually say in the Doctor's presence, and appear to I

my aunt rub her nose sometimes when she happen to be alone

but her abide reliance be on Mr. Dick. that man have evidently

Unconscious of this prediction, Mr. Dick continue to occupy

thus earnestly besought, I make no reference to the Doctor for his

description.
when I have finish, Annie remain, for some few moment, silent,

'there be great need,' she answer, in the same way, 'that I

- 'I must be permit to observe that it cannot be requisite to

'upon my word!' gasp Mrs. Markleham.

she raise her eye, and clasp her hand, and look as beautiful

CHAPTER 46
INTELLIGENCE
I must have be marry, if I may trust to my imperfect memory for
date, about a year or so, when one evening, as I be return
from a solitary walk, think of the book I be then write - for
my success have steadily increase with my steady application, and
I be engage at that time upon my first work of fiction - I come
past Mrs. Steerforth's house. I have often pass it before, during
my residence in that neighbourhood, though never when I could
choose another road. Howbeit, it do sometimes happen that it be
not easy to find another, without make a long circuit; and so I
have pass that way, upon the whole, pretty often.
I have never do more than glance at the house, as I go by with
a quickened step. it have be uniformly gloomy and dull. none of
the good room abut on the road; and the narrow, heavily-frame
old-fashioned window, never cheerful under any circumstance,
look very dismal, close shut, and with their blind always draw
down. there be a covered way across a little paved court, to an
entrance that be never use; and there be one round staircase
window, at odd with all the rest, and the only one unshade by a
blind, which have the same unoccupied blank look. I do not remember
that I ever see a light in all the house. if I have be a casual
passer-by, I should have probably suppose that some childless
person lie dead in it. if I have happily possess no knowledge of
the place, and have see it often in that changeless state, I should
have please my fancy with many ingenious speculation, I dare say.
as it be, I think as little of it as I might. but my mind could
not go by it and leave it, as my body do; and it usually awaken
a long train of meditation. come before I, on this particular
evening that I mention, mingle with the childish recollection and
later fancy, the ghost of half-form hope, the broken shadow
of disappointment dimly see and understand, the blending of
experience and imagination, incidental to the occupation with which
my thought have be busy, it be more than commonly suggestive.
I fall into a brown study as I walk on, and a voice at my side

it be a woman's voice, too. I be not long in recollect Mrs.

'if you please, sir, would you have the goodness to walk in, and

pass

he keep a lodging over the little chandler's shop in Hungerford

'Mas'r Davy! Thankee, sir! thankee hearty, for this visit! sit ye

I recall to his remembrance the morning after her departure, when

we have come, through Temple Bar, into the city. converse no more

CHAPTER 47
MARTHA
we be now down in Westminster. we have turn back to follow she,
have encounter she come towards we; and Westminster Abbey be
the point at which she pass from the light and noise of the

that, between this and the advance she have of we when she strike

avoid the footstep that she hear so close behind; and, without
look back, pass on even more rapidly.
a glimpse of the river through a dull gateway, where some waggon
be house for the night, seem to arrest my foot. I touch my
companion without speak, and we both forbear to cross after she,
and both follow on that opposite side of the way; keep as

there be, and be when I write, at the end of that low-lie
street, a dilapidated little wooden building, probably an obsolete
old ferry-house. its position be just at that point where the
street cease, and the road begin to lie between a row of house
and the river. as soon as she come here, and see the water, she
stop as if she have come to her destination; and presently go
slowly along by the brink of the river, look intently at it.
all the way here, I have suppose that she be go to some house;
indeed, I have vaguely entertain the hope that the house might be
in some way associate with the lose girl. but that one dark
glimpse of the river, through the gateway, have instinctively
prepare I for she go no far.
the neighbourhood be a dreary one at that time; as oppressive,
sad, and solitary by night, as any about London. there be
neither wharf nor house on the melancholy waste of road near the
great blank Prison. a sluggish ditch deposit its mud at the
prison wall. coarse grass and rank weed straggle over all the
marshy land in the vicinity. in one part, carcase of house,
inauspiciously begin and never finish, rot away. in another,
the ground be cumber with rusty iron monster of steam-boiler,
wheel, crank, pipe, furnace, paddle, anchor, diving-bell,
windmill-sail, and I know not what strange object, accumulate by
some speculator, and grovel in the dust, underneath which -
have sink into the soil of their own weight in wet weather - they
have the appearance of vainly try to hide themselves. the clash
and glare of sundry fiery work upon the river-side, arise by night
to disturb everything except the heavy and unbroken smoke that
pour out of their chimney. Slimy gap and causeway, wind
among old wooden pile, with a sickly substance cling to the
latter, like green hair, and the rag of last year's handbill
offer reward for drowned man flutter above high-water mark,
lead down through the ooze and slush to the ebb-tide. there be a
story that one of the pit dig for the dead in the time of the
Great Plague be hereabout; and a blight influence seem to
have proceed from it over the whole place. or else it look as
if it have gradually decompose into that nightmare condition, out
of the overflowing of the polluted stream.
as if she be a part of the refuse it have cast out, and leave to
corruption and decay, the girl we have follow stray down to the
river's brink, and stand in the midst of this night-picture, lonely
and still, look at the water.
there be some boat and barge astrand in the mud, and these
enable we to come within a few yard of she without be see.
I then sign to Mr. Peggotty to remain where he be, and emerge

I think she be talk to herself. I be sure, although absorb

at the same moment I say 'Martha!'

but she still repeat the same word, continually exclaim, 'oh,

'I know itbe like I!' she exclaim. 'I know that I belong to it.
I know that itbe the natural company of such as I be! it come from
country place, where there be once no harm in it - and it creep
through the dismal street, defile and miserable - and it go

I feel that I must go with it!'
I have never know what despair be, except in the tone of those
word.

and night. itbe the only thing in all the world that I be fit for,
or thatbe fit for I. oh, the dreadful river!'
the thought pass through my mind that in the face of my

sink on the stone, she take some in each hand, and clench

inexorable.

try.'

can all do some good, if we will.'
she tremble, and her lip shake, and her face be pale, as she

'it have be put into your heart, perhaps, to save a wretched

bold. if any good should come of I, I might begin to hope; for
nothing but harm have ever come of my deed yet. I be to be

think that my aunt might have relapse into one of her old

he have a glass and bottle in his hand, and be in the act of

he be eat as well as drinking, and seem to eat with a hungry

the light in the passage be obscure for a moment, and my aunt

hear it chink.
'whatbe the use of this?' he demand.
'I can spare no more,' return my aunt.
'then I cannot go,' say he. 'here! you may take it back!'
'you bad man,' return my aunt, with great emotion; 'how can you
use I so? but why do I ask? it be because you know how weak I
be! what have I to do, to free myself for ever of your visit, but
to abandon you to your desert?'
'and why donot you abandon I to my desert?' say he.
'you ask I why!' return my aunt. 'what a heart you must have!'
he stand moodily rattle the money, and shake his head, until at
length he say:
'be this all you mean to give I, then?'

loss, and be poor than I use to be. I have tell you so.
have get it, why do you give I the pain of look at you for
another moment, and see what you have become?'
'I have become shabby enough, if you mean that,' he say. 'I lead
the life of an owl.'
'you strip I of the great part of all I ever have,' say my

repent of it. donot add new injury to the long, long list of
injury you have do I!'
'aye!' he return. 'itbe all very fine - well! I must do the good
I can, for the present, I suppose.'
in spite of himself, he appear abash by my aunt's indignant

'child,' return my aunt, take my arm, 'come in, and donot speak

we sit down in her little parlour. my aunt retire behind the

'your husband, aunt? I think he have be dead!'
'dead to I,' return my aunt, 'but live.'
I sit in silent amazement.
'Betsey Trotwood donot look a likely subject for the tender

'my dear, good aunt!'

she give my hand a squeeze, and shake her head.

wouldnot have even this shadow of my idle fancy hardly deal with.
for I be in earnest, Trot, if ever a woman be.'
my aunt dismiss the matter with a heavy sigh, and smooth she
dress.

and end, and all about it. we willnot mention the subject to one
another any more; neither, of course, will you mention it to
anybody else. this be my grumpy, frumpy story, and wewill keep it
to ourselves, Trot!'
CHAPTER 48
DOMESTIC
I labour hard at my book, without allow it to interfere with
the punctual discharge of my newspaper duty; and it come out and
be very successful. I be not stun by the praise which sound
in my ear, notwithstanding that I be keenly alive to it, and
think well of my own performance, I have little doubt, than
anybody else do. it have always be in my observation of human
nature, that a man who have any good reason to believe in himself
never flourish himself before the face of other people in order
that they may believe in he. for this reason, I retain my
modesty in very self-respect; and the more praise I get, the more
I try to deserve.
it be not my purpose, in this record, though in all other
essential it be my write memory, to pursue the history of my own
fiction. they express themselves, and I leave they to themselves.
when I refer to they, incidentally, it be only as a part of my
progress.
have some foundation for believe, by this time, that nature and
accident have make I an author, I pursue my vocation with
confidence. without such assurance I should certainly have leave it
alone, and bestow my energy on some other endeavour. I should
have try to find out what nature and accident really have make I,
and to be that, and nothing else.
I have be write, in the newspaper and elsewhere, so
prosperously, that when my new success be achieve, I consider
myself reasonably entitle to escape from the dreary debate. one
joyful night, therefore, I note down the music of the
parliamentary bagpipe for the last time, and I have never hear it
since; though I still recognize the old drone in the newspaper,
without any substantial variation (except, perhaps, that there be
more of it), all the livelong session.
I now write of the time when I have be marry, I suppose, about
a year and a half. after several variety of experiment, we have
give up the housekeeping as a bad job. the house keep itself, and
we keep a page. the principal function of this retainer be to
quarrel with the cook; in which respect he be a perfect
Whittington, without his cat, or the remote chance of be make
Lord Mayor.
he appear to I to have live in a hail of saucepan-lid. his
whole existence be a scuffle. he would shriek for help on the
most improper occasion, - as when we have a little dinner-party, or
a few friend in the evening, - and would come tumble out of the
kitchen, with iron missile fly after he. we want to get rid
of he, but he be very much attach to we, and wouldnot go. he
be a tearful boy, and break into such deplorable lamentation,
when a cessation of our connexion be hint at, that we be
oblige to keep he. he have no mother - no anything in the way of
a relative, that I could discover, except a sister, who flee to
America the moment we have take he off her hand; and he become
quarter on we like a horrible young changeling. he have a lively
perception of his own unfortunate state, and be always rub his
eye with the sleeve of his jacket, or stoop to blow his nose on
the extreme corner of a little pocket-handkerchief, which he never
would take completely out of his pocket, but always economize and
secrete.
this unlucky page, engage in an evil hour at six pound ten per
annum, be a source of continual trouble to I. I watch he as
he grow - and he grow like scarlet bean - with painful
apprehension of the time when he would begin to shave; even of the
day when he would be bald or grey. I see no prospect of ever
get rid of he; and, project myself into the future, use to
think what an inconvenience he would be when he be an old man.
I never expect anything less, than this unfortunate's manner of
get I out of my difficulty. he steal Dora's watch, which,
like everything else belong to we, have no particular place of
its own; and, convert it into money, spend the produce (he be
always a weak-minded boy) in incessantly rid up and down between
London and Uxbridge outside the coach. he be take to Bow Street,
as well as I remember, on the completion of his fifteenth journey;
when four-and-sixpence, and a second-hand fife which he couldnot
play, be find upon his person.
the surprise and its consequence would have be much less
disagreeable to I if he have not be penitent. but he be very
penitent indeed, and in a peculiar way - not in the lump, but by
instalment. for example: the day after that on which I be
oblige to appear against he, he make certain revelation touch
a hamper in the cellar, which we believe to be full of wine, but
which have nothing in it except bottle and cork. we suppose he
have now ease his mind, and tell the bad he know of the cook;
but, a day or two afterwards, his conscience sustain a new
twinge, and he disclose how she have a little girl, who, early
every morning, take away our bread; and also how he himself have
be suborn to maintain the milkman in coal. in two or three
day more, I be inform by the authority of his have lead to
the discovery of sirloin of beef among the kitchen-stuff, and
sheet in the rag-bag. a little while afterwards, he break out in
an entirely new direction, and confess to a knowledge of
burglarious intention as to our premise, on the part of the
pot-boy, who be immediately take up. I get to be so ashamed of
be such a victim, that I would have give he any money to hold
his tongue, or would have offer a round bribe for his being
permit to run away. it be an aggravating circumstance in the
case that he have no idea of this, but conceive that he be make
I amend in every new discovery: not to say, heap obligation
on my head.
at last I run away myself, whenever I see an emissary of the police
approach with some new intelligence; and live a stealthy life
until he be try and order to be transport. even then he
couldnot be quiet, but be always write we letter; and want so
much to see Dora before he go away, that Dora go to visit he,
and faint when she find herself inside the iron bar. in short,
I have no peace of my life until he be expatriate, and make (as I
afterwards hear) a shepherd of, 'up the country' somewhere; I have
no geographical idea where.
all this lead I into some serious reflection, and present our

'my love,' say I, 'it be very painful to I to think that our want

'no, my dear, indeed! let I explain to you what I mean.'

I begin to be afraid that the fault be not entirely on one side,

Dora would not allow I, for a long time, to remove the

what other course be leave to take? to 'form her mind'? this be

I begin immediately. when Dora be very childish, and I would have

I press traddle into the service without his knowledge; and

she put her arm round my neck, and laugh, and call herself by

between these two irreconcilable conclusion: the one, that what I

'the first mistaken impulse of an undisciplined heart.' those

fellow!'

doubt. but if you want a dog to race with, Little Blossom, he have
live too well for that, and Iwill give you one.'
'thank you, aunt,' say Dora, faintly. 'but donot, please!'
'no?' say my aunt, take off her spectacle.
'I couldnot have any other dog but Jip,' say Dora. 'it would be
so unkind to Jip! besides, I couldnot be such friend with any
other dog but Jip; because he wouldnot have know I before I be
married, and wouldnot have bark at Doady when he first come to
our house. I couldnot care for any other dog but Jip, I be afraid,
aunt.'
'to be sure!' say my aunt, pat her cheek again. 'you be
right.'
'you be not offend,' say Dora. 'be you?'
'why, what a sensitive pet it be!' cry my aunt, bend over she

'no, no, I donot really think so,' return Dora; 'but I be a

little altered - could I, Jip?'
Jip nestle close to his mistress, and lazily lick her hand.

yet?' say Dora. 'we may keep one another company a little
long!'
my pretty Dora! when she come down to dinner on the ensue Sunday,
and be so glad to see old traddle (who always din with we on
Sunday), we think she would be 'run about as she use to do',
in a few day. but they say, wait a few day more; and then, wait

very pretty, and be very merry; but the little foot that use to

I begin to carry she downstairs every morning, and upstairs every

if I do it for a wager. Jip would bark and caper round we, and go

that we be come. my aunt, the good and most cheerful of

but, sometimes, when I take she up, and feel that she be light

I avoid the recognition of this feeling by any name, or by any
communing with myself; until one night, when it be very strong

night, Little Blossom,' I sit down at my desk alone, and cry to
think, oh what a fatal name it be, and how the blossom wither in
its bloom upon the tree!

I be involve in MYSTERY

'MY DEAR SIR,

well than his own. but even then his passion for write letter
be too strong to be resist; for while we be yet in the height
of our excitement, hope, and wonder, the follow pastoral note
be bring to I from a neighbouring tavern, at which he have
call to write it: -
'most secret and confidential.
'MY DEAR SIR,
'I beg to be allow to convey, through you, my apology to your
excellent aunt for my late excitement. an explosion of a
smoulder volcano long suppress, be the result of an internal
contest more easily conceive than describe.
'I trust I render tolerably intelligible my appointment for the
morning of this day week, at the house of public entertainment at
Canterbury, where Mrs. Micawber and myself have once the honour of
unite our voice to your, in the well-know strain of the
immortal exciseman nurture beyond the Tweed.
'the duty do, and act of reparation perform, which can alone
enable I to contemplate my fellow mortal, I shall be know no
more. I shall simply require to be deposit in that place of
universal resort, where
each in his narrow cell for ever lay,
the rude forefather of the hamlet sleep,
'- with the plain Inscription,
'WILKINS MICAWBER.'
CHAPTER 50
Mr. PEGGOTTY'S DREAM come true
by this time, some month have pass since our interview on the

his conviction remain unchanged. so far as I know - and I

one evening, at this hour, he tell I that he have find Martha

'do she tell you why?' I inquire.

CHAPTER 51
the beginning of a long journey
it be yet early in the morning of the follow day, when, as I

have happen overnight. without say a word, she walk up with

it be so expressively do, that she have no need to say a word.
Mr. Peggotty understand she quite as well as if she have say a
thousand.
'Iwill go in now, Trot,' say my aunt, 'and look after little

'not along of my being heer, ma'am, I hope?' say Mr. Peggotty.
'unless my wit be go a bahd's neezing' - by which Mr. Peggotty

kind, pervise you doen't mind my clicketten, if youwould bide heer.'
'would you?' say my aunt, with short good-nature. 'then I be sure
I will!'
so, she draw her arm through Mr. Peggotty's, and walk with he to

what power and force of character his sinewy hand express, and
what a good and trusty companion it be to his honest brow and

be expect of she and prepare fur she. it be hour afore

he draw his sleeve across his face, without any pretence of

mind a minute ago, to say a word about myself; but it come up so

'you be a self-deny soul,' say my aunt, 'and will have your

Mr. Peggotty, with the shadow of the leaf play athwart his

have relinquish.
'when my Em'ly take flight,' he say, in stern wrath for the

GOD confound he! - she take flight in the night. it be a dark

be fire afore her eye, and roaring in her ear. of a sudden -
or so she thowt, you unnerstand - the day break, wet and windy, and
she be lie b'low a heap of stone upon the shore, and a woman be

be it as have go so much amiss?'
he see everything he relate. it pass before he, as he speak,

express. I can hardly believe, write now long afterwards, but
that I be actually present in these scene; they be impress

'as Em'ly's eye - which be heavy - see this woman well,' Mr.

helpful of she at the last; a Angel to her heer, and heerafter!'
