he affect by anything since the night she go away. my aunt
and I do not attempt to disturb he.
'it be a little cottage, you may suppose,' he say, presently,
'but she find space for Em'ly in it, - her husband be away at
sea, - and she kep it secret, and prevail upon such neighbour as
she have (they be not many near) to keep it secret too. Em'ly be
take bad with fever, and, what be very strange to I be, - maybe
'tis not so strange to scholar, - the language of that country
go out of her head, and she could only speak her own, that no one
unnerstood. she recollect, as if she have dream it, that she lie
there always a-talk her own tongue, always believe as the old
boat be round the next pint in the bay, and beg and imploring
of them to send theer and tell how she be die, and bring back a
message of forgiveness, if it be on'y a wure. a'most the whole
time, she thowt, - now, that he as I make mention on just now be
lurk for her unnerneath the winder; now that he as have bring
she to this be in the room, - and cry to the good young woman
not to give she up, and know'd, at the same time, that she couldnot
unnerstand, and dread that she must be take away. likewise the
fire be afore her eye, and the roaring in her ear; and theer
be no today, nor yesterday, nor yet tomorrow; but everything in
her life as ever have be, or as ever could be, and everything as
never have be, and as never could be, be a crowding on she all at
once, and nothing clear nor welcome, and yet she sing and laugh
about it! how long this last, I doen't know; but then theer come
a sleep; and in that sleep, from be a many time strong than
her own self, she fall into the weakness of the little child.'
here he stop, as if for relief from the terror of his own
description. after be silent for a few moment, he pursue his
story.
'it be a pleasant arternoon when she awake; and so quiet, that
there warn't a sound but the rippling of that blue sea without a
tide, upon the shore. it be her belief, at first, that she be at
home upon a Sunday morning; but the vine leave as she see at the
winder, and the hill beyond, warn't home, and contradict of she.
then, come in her friend to watch alongside of her bed; and then
she know'd as the old boat warn't round that next pint in the bay
no more, but be fur off; and know'd where she be, and why; and
break out a-cry on that good young woman's bosom, wheer I hope
her baby be a-lie now, a-cheering of she with its pretty eye!'
he could not speak of this good friend of Emily's without a flow of
tear. it be in vain to try. he break down again, endeavour
to bless she!
'that do my Em'ly good,' he resume, after such emotion as I
could not behold without share in; and as to my aunt, she weep
with all her heart; 'that do Em'ly good, and she begin to mend.
but, the language of that country be quite go from she, and she
be force to make sign. so she go on, get well from day
to day, slow, but sure, and try to learn the name of common
thing - name as she seem never to have heerd in all her life -
till one evening come, when she be a-setting at her window,
look at a little girl at play upon the beach. and of a sudden
this child hold out her hand, and say, what would be in English,
"Fisherman's daughter, herebe a shell!" - for you be to unnerstand
that they use at first to call she "Pretty lady", as the general
way in that country be, and that she have teach them to call she
"Fisherman's daughter" instead. the child say of a sudden,
"Fisherman's daughter, herebe a shell!" then Em'ly unnerstand she;
and she answer, burst out a-crying; and it all come back!
'when Em'ly get strong again,' say Mr. Peggotty, after another
short interval of silence, 'she cast about to leave that good young
creetur, and get to her own country. the husband be come home,
then; and the two together put she aboard a small trader bind to
Leghorn, and from that to France. she have a little money, but it
be less than little as they would take for all they do. Ibe
a'most glad on it, though they be so poor! what they do, be lay
up wheer neither moth or rust doth corrupt, and wheer thief do
not break through nor steal. Mas'r Davy, itwill outlast all the
treasure in the wureld.
'Em'ly get to France, and take service to wait on travel lady
at a inn in the port. Theer, theer come, one day, that snake. -
let he never come nigh I. I doen't know what hurt I might do
he! - soon as she see he, without he see she, all her fear
and wildness return upon she, and she flee afore the very breath
he draw'd. she come to England, and be set ashore at Dover.
'I doen't know,' say Mr. Peggotty, 'for sure, when her 'art begin
to fail she; but all the way to England she have thowt to come to
her dear home. soon as she get to England she turn her face
tow'rd it. but, fear of not be forgiv, fear of be pinte
at, fear of some of we be dead along of she, fear of many
thing, turn she from it, kiender by force, upon the road:
"Uncle, uncle," she say to I, "the fear of not be worthy to do
what my torn and bleed breast so long to do, be the most
fright'ning fear of all! I turn back, when my 'art be full of
prayer that I might crawl to the old door-step, in the night, kiss
it, lie my wicked face upon it, and theer be find dead in the
morning."
'she come,' say Mr. Peggotty, drop his voice to an
awe-stricken whisper, 'to London. she - as have never see it in
her life - alone - without a penny - young - so pretty - come to
London. a'most the moment as she light heer, all so desolate,
she find (as she believe) a friend; a decent woman as speak to
she about the needle-work as she have be bring up to do, about
find plenty of it fur she, about a lodge fur the night, and
make secret inquiration concern of I and all at home,
tomorrow. when my child,' he say aloud, and with an energy of
gratitude that shake he from head to foot, 'stand upon the brink
of more than I can say or think on - Martha, trew to her promise,
save she.'
I could not repress a cry of joy.
'Mas'r Davy!' say he, grip my hand in that strong hand of his,
'it be you as first make mention of she to I. I thankee, sir!
she be arn. she have know'd of her bitter knowledge wheer to
watch and what to do. she have do it. and the Lord be above
all! she come, white and hurried, upon Em'ly in her sleep. she
say to she, "rise up from bad than death, and come with I!"
they belong to the house would have stop she, but they might
as soon have stop the sea. "stand away from I," she say, "I
be a ghost that call she from beside her open grave!" she tell
Em'ly she have see I, and know'd I love she, and forgive she.
she wrap she, hasty, in her clothe. she take she, faint and
trembling, on her arm. she heed no more what they say, than if
she have have no ear. she walk among them with my child, mind
only she; and bring she safe out, in the dead of the night, from
that black pit of ruin!
'she attend on Em'ly,' say Mr. Peggotty, who have release my
hand, and put his own hand on his heaving chest; 'she attend to
my Em'ly, lie weary out, and wander betwixt while, till
late next day. then she go in search of I; then in search of
you, Mas'r Davy. she donot tell Em'ly what she come out fur, lest
her 'art should fail, and she should think of hiding of herself.
how the cruel lady know'd of her be theer, I cannot say. whether
he as I have speak so much of, chance to see them go theer, or
whether (which be most like, to my thinking) he have heerd it from
the woman, I doen't greatly ask myself. my niece be find.
'all night long,' say Mr. Peggotty, 'we have be together, Em'ly
and I. 'tis little (consider the time) as she have say, in
wured, through they break-hearted tear; 'tis less as I have see
of her dear face, as grow'd into a woman's at my hearth. but, all
night long, her arm have be about my neck; and her head have lay
heer; and we know full well, as we can put our trust in one
another, ever more.'
he cease to speak, and his hand upon the table rest there in
perfect repose, with a resolution in it that might have conquer
lion.
'it be a gleam of light upon I, Trot,' say my aunt, dry her
eye, 'when I form the resolution of be godmother to your
sister Betsey Trotwood, who disappoint I; but, next to that,
hardly anything would have give I great pleasure, than to be
godmother to that good young creature's baby!'
Mr. Peggotty nod his understanding of my aunt's feeling, but
could not trust himself with any verbal reference to the subject of
her commendation. we all remain silent, and occupy with our
own reflection (my aunt dry her eye, and now sob
convulsively, and now laugh and call herself a fool); until
I speak.
'you have quite make up your mind,' say I to Mr. Peggotty, 'as to
the future, good friend? I need scarcely ask you.'
'quite, Mas'r Davy,' he return; 'and tell Em'ly. Theer's mighty
country, fur from heer. our future life lay over the sea.'
'they will emigrate together, aunt,' say I.
'yes!' say Mr. Peggotty, with a hopeful smile. 'no one cannot
reproach my darling in Australia. we will begin a new life over
theer!'
I ask he if he yet propose to himself any time for go away.
'I be down at the dock early this morning, sir,' he return, 'to
get information concern of they ship. in about six week or
two month from now, therewill be one sailing - I see she this
morning - go aboard - and we shall take our passage in she.'
'quite alone?' I ask.
'Aye, Mas'r Davy!' he return. 'my sister, you see, shebe that
fond of you and yourn, and that accustomed to think on'y of her own
country, that it wouldnot be hardly fair to let she go. besides
which, theer's one she have in charge, Mas'r Davy, as doen't ought
to be forget.'
'Poor ham!' say I.
'my good sister take care of his house, you see, ma'am, and he
take kindly to she,' Mr. Peggotty explain for my aunt's well
information. 'hewill set and talk to she, with a calm spirit, wen
itbe like he couldnot bring himself to open his lip to another.
poor fellow!' say Mr. Peggotty, shake his head, 'theerbe not so
much leave he, that he could spare the little as he have!'
'and Mrs. Gummidge?' say I.
'well, Ihave have a mort of consideration, I do tell you,' return
Mr. Peggotty, with a perplexed look which gradually clear as he
go on, 'concern of Missis Gummidge. you see, wen Missis
Gummidge fall a-thinking of the old 'un, she an't what you may
call good company. Betwixt you and I, Mas'r Davy - and you, ma'am
- wen Mrs. Gummidge take to wimicking,' - our old country word for
cry, - 'shebe liable to be consider to be, by they as donot
know the old 'un, peevish-like. now I do know the old 'un,' say
Mr. Peggotty, 'and I know'd his merit, so I unnerstan' she; but
'tan't entirely so, you see, with other - nat'rally cannot be!'
my aunt and I both acquiesce.
'Wheerby,' say Mr. Peggotty, 'my sister might - I doen't say she
would, but might - find Missis Gummidge give she a leetle trouble
now-and-again. Theerfur 'tan't my intention to moor Missis
Gummidge 'long with they, but to find a Beein' fur her wheer she
can fisherate for herself.' (a Beein' signifie, in that dialect,
a home, and to fisherate be to provide.) 'fur which purpose,' say
Mr. Peggotty, 'I mean to make she a 'lowance afore I go, as'll
leave she pretty comfort'ble. shebe the faithfull of creetur.
'tan't to be expect, of course, at her time of life, and be
lone and lorn, as the good old Mawther be to be knock about
aboardship, and in the wood and wild of a new and fur-away
country. so thatbe what Ibe a-go to do with she.'
he forget nobody. he think of everybody's claim and striving,
but his own.
'Em'ly,' he continue, 'will keep along with I - poor child, shebe
sore in need of peace and rest! - until such time as we go upon
our voyage. shewill work at they clothe, as must be make; and I
hope her trouble will begin to seem long ago than they be, wen
she find herself once more by her rough but love uncle.'
my aunt nod confirmation of this hope, and impart great
satisfaction to Mr. Peggotty.
'Theer's one thing furder, Mas'r Davy,' say he, put his hand
in his breast-pocket, and gravely take out the little paper
bundle I have see before, which he unroll on the table. 'Theer's
these here banknote - fifty pound, and ten. to they I wish to add
the money as she come away with. Ihave ask she about that (but
not say why), and have add of it up. I an't a scholar. would
you be so kind as see how 'tis?'
he hand I, apologetically for his scholarship, a piece of paper,
and observe I while I look it over. it be quite right.
'Thankee, sir,' he say, take it back. 'this money, if you
doen't see objection, Mas'r Davy, I shall put up jest afore I go,
in a cover direct to he; and put that up in another, direct to
his mother. I shall tell she, in no more wured than I speak to
you, what itbe the price on; and that Ibe go, and past receive
of it back.'
I tell he that I think it would be right to do so - that I be
thoroughly convince it would be, since he feel it to be right.
'I say that theer be on'y one thing furder,' he proceed with a
grave smile, when he have make up his little bundle again, and put
it in his pocket; 'but theer be two. I warn't sure in my mind,
wen I come out this morning, as I could go and break to Ham, of my
own self, what have so thankfully happen. so I writ a letter
while I be out, and put it in the post-office, tell of them how
all be as 'tis; and that I should come down tomorrow to unload my
mind of what little need a-doing of down theer, and, most-like,
take my farewell leave of Yarmouth.'
'and do you wish I to go with you?' say I, see that he leave
something unsaid.
'if you could do I that kind favour, Mas'r Davy,' he reply. 'I
know the sight on you would cheer them up a bit.'
my little Dora be in good spirit, and very desirous that I
should go - as I find on talk it over with she - I readily
pledge myself to accompany he in accordance with his wish. next
morning, consequently, we be on the Yarmouth coach, and again
travel over the old ground.
as we pass along the familiar street at night - Mr. Peggotty, in
despite of all my remonstrance, carry my bag - I glance into
Omer and Joram's shop, and see my old friend Mr. Omer there,
smoke his pipe. I feel reluctant to be present, when Mr.
Peggotty first meet his sister and Ham; and make Mr. Omer my excuse
for linger behind.
'how be Mr. Omer, after this long time?' say I, go in.
he fan away the smoke of his pipe, that he might get a well
view of I, and soon recognize I with great delight.
'I should get up, sir, to acknowledge such an honour as this
visit,' say he, 'only my limb be rather out of sort, and I be
wheel about. with the exception of my limb and my breath,
howsoever, I be as hearty as a man can be, Ibe thankful to say.'
I congratulate he on his contented look and his good spirit,
and see, now, that his easy-chair go on wheel.
'itbe an ingenious thing, benot it?' he inquire, follow the
direction of my glance, and polish the elbow with his arm. 'it
run as light as a feather, and track as true as a mail-coach.
bless you, my little Minnie - my grand-daughter you know, Minnie's
child - put she little strength against the back, give it a
shove, and away we go, as clever and merry as ever you see
anything! and I tell you what - itbe a most uncommon chair to smoke
a pipe in.'
I never see such a good old fellow to make the good of a thing, and
find out the enjoyment of it, as Mr. Omer. he be as radiant, as
if his chair, his asthma, and the failure of his limb, be the
various branch of a great invention for enhance the luxury of
a pipe.
'I see more of the world, I can assure you,' say Mr. Omer, 'in
this chair, than ever I see out of it. youwould be surprised at the
number of people that look in of a day to have a chat. you really
would! therebe twice as much in the newspaper, since Ihave take to
this chair, as there use to be. as to general reading, dear I,
what a lot of it I do get through! thatbe what I feel so strong,
you know! if it have be my eye, what should I have do? if it
have be my ear, what should I have do? be my limb, what
do it signify? why, my limb only make my breath short when I
use them. and now, if I want to go out into the street or down to
the sand, Ihave only get to call Dick, Joram's young 'prentice,
and away I go in my own carriage, like the Lord Mayor of London.'
he half suffocate himself with laugh here.
'Lord bless you!' say Mr. Omer, resume his pipe, 'a man must
take the fat with the lean; thatbe what he must make up his mind
to, in this life. Joram do a fine business. ex-cellent
business!'
'I be very glad to hear it,' say I.
'I know you would be,' say Mr. Omer. 'and Joram and Minnie be
like valentine. what more can a man expect? whatbe his limb to
that!'
his supreme contempt for his own limb, as he sit smoking, be one
of the pleasantest oddity I have ever encounter.
'and since Ihave take to general reading, youhave take to general
writing, eh, sir?' say Mr. Omer, survey I admiringly. 'what
a lovely work that be of your! what expression in it! I read it
every word - every word. and as to feel sleepy! not at all!'
I laughingly express my satisfaction, but I must confess that I
think this association of ideas significant.
'I give you my word and honour, sir,' say Mr. Omer, 'that when I
lie that book upon the table, and look at it outside; compact in
three separate and indiwidual wollume - one, two, three; I be as
proud as Punch to think that I once have the honour of be
connect with your family. and dear I, itbe a long time ago,
now, benot it? over at Blunderstone. with a pretty little party
lay along with the other party. and you quite a small party then,
yourself. dear, dear!'
I change the subject by refer to Emily. after assure he
that I do not forget how interested he have always be in she, and
how kindly he have always treat she, I give he a general account
of her restoration to her uncle by the aid of Martha; which I know
would please the old man. he listen with the utmost attention,
and say, feelingly, when I have do:
'I be rejoice at it, sir! itbe the good news I have hear for many
a day. dear, dear, dear! and whatbe go to be undertake for that
unfortunate young woman, Martha, now?'
'you touch a point that my thought have be dwell on since
yesterday,' say I, 'but on which I can give you no information
yet, Mr. Omer. Mr. Peggotty have not allude to it, and I have a
delicacy in do so. I be sure he have not forget it. he
forget nothing that be disintereste and good.'
'because you know,' say Mr. Omer, take himself up, where he have
leave off, 'whatever be do, I should wish to be a member of. put
I down for anything you may consider right, and let I know. I
never could think the girl all bad, and I be glad to find shebe
not. so will my daughter Minnie be. young woman be contradictory
creature in some thing - her mother be just the same as she -
but their heart be soft and kind. itbe all show with Minnie,
about Martha. why she should consider it necessary to make any
show, I donot undertake to tell you. but itbe all show, bless you.
shewould do she any kindness in private. so, put I down for whatever
you may consider right, will you be so good? and drop I a line
where to forward it. dear I!' say Mr. Omer, 'when a man be
draw on to a time of life, where the two end of life meet; when
he find himself, however hearty he be, be wheel about for the
second time, in a speech of go-cart; he should be over-rejoice
to do a kindness if he can. he want plenty. and I donot speak of
myself, particular,' say Mr. Omer, 'because, sir, the way I look
at it be, that we be all draw on to the bottom of the hill,
whatever age we be, on account of time never stand still for a
single moment. so let we always do a kindness, and be
over-rejoice. to be sure!'
he knock the ash out of his pipe, and put it on a ledge in the
back of his chair, expressly make for its reception.
'therebe Em'ly's cousin, he that she be to have be marry to,'
say Mr. Omer, rub his hand feebly, 'as fine a fellow as there
be in Yarmouth! hewill come and talk or read to I, in the evening,
for an hour together sometimes. thatbe a kindness, I should call
it! all his lifebe a kindness.'
'I be go to see he now,' say I.
'be you?' say Mr. Omer. 'tell he I be hearty, and send my
respect. Minnie and Jorambe at a ball. they would be as proud to
see you as I be, if they be at home. Minnie willnot hardly go out
at all, you see, "on account of father", as she say. so I swear
tonight, that if she donot go, Iwould go to bed at six. in
consequence of which,' Mr. Omer shake himself and his chair with
laughter at the success of his device, 'she and Jorambe at a ball.'
I shake hand with he, and wish he good night.
'half a minute, sir,' say Mr. Omer. 'if you be to go without
see my little elephant, youwould lose the good of sight. you
never see such a sight! minnie!'
a musical little voice answer, from somewhere upstairs, 'I be
come, grandfather!' and a pretty little girl with long, flaxen,
curl hair, soon come run into the shop.
'this be my little elephant, sir,' say Mr. Omer, fondle the
child. 'siamese breed, sir. now, little elephant!'
the little elephant set the door of the parlour open, enable I
to see that, in these latter day, it be convert into a bedroom
for Mr. Omer who could not be easily convey upstairs; and then
hide her pretty forehead, and tumble her long hair, against the
back of Mr. Omer's chair.
'the elephant butt, you know, sir,' say Mr. Omer, winking, 'when
he go at a object. once, elephant. twice. three time!'
at this signal, the little elephant, with a dexterity that be next
to marvellous in so small an animal, whisk the chair round with
Mr. Omer in it, and rattle it off, pell-mell, into the parlour,
without touch the door-post: Mr. Omer indescribably enjoy the
performance, and look back at I on the road as if it be the
triumphant issue of his life's exertion.
after a stroll about the town I go to Ham's house. Peggotty have
now remove here for good; and have let her own house to the
successor of Mr. Barkis in the carrying business, who have pay she
very well for the good-will, cart, and horse. I believe the very
same slow horse that Mr. Barkis drive be still at work.
I find they in the neat kitchen, accompany by Mrs. Gummidge, who
have be fetch from the old boat by Mr. Peggotty himself. I
doubt if she could have be induce to desert her post, by anyone
else. he have evidently tell they all. both Peggotty and Mrs.
Gummidge have their apron to their eye, and Ham have just step
out 'to take a turn on the beach'. he presently come home, very
glad to see I; and I hope they be all the well for my being
there. we speak, with some approach to cheerfulness, of Mr.
Peggotty's grow rich in a new country, and of the wonder he
would describe in his letter. we say nothing of Emily by name,
but distantly refer to she more than once. Ham be the serene
of the party.
but, Peggotty tell I, when she light I to a little chamber
where the Crocodile book be lie ready for I on the table, that
he always be the same. she believe (she tell I, cry) that he
be break-hearted; though he be as full of courage as of
sweetness, and work hard and well than any boat-builder in
any yard in all that part. there be time, she say, of an
evening, when he talk of their old life in the boat-house; and
then he mention Emily as a child. but, he never mention she as
a woman.
I think I have read in his face that he would like to speak to I
alone. I therefore resolve to put myself in his way next evening,
as he come home from his work. have settle this with myself, I
fall asleep. that night, for the first time in all those many
night, the candle be take out of the window, Mr. Peggotty swing
in his old hammock in the old boat, and the wind murmur with the
old sound round his head.
all next day, he be occupy in dispose of his fishing-boat and
tackle; in pack up, and send to London by waggon, such of his
little domestic possession as he think would be useful to he;
and in part with the rest, or bestow they on Mrs. Gummidge.
she be with he all day. as I have a sorrowful wish to see the old
place once more, before it be lock up, I engage to meet they
there in the evening. but I so arrange it, as that I should meet
Ham first.
it be easy to come in his way, as I know where he work. I meet
he at a retired part of the sand, which I know he would cross,
and turn back with he, that he might have leisure to speak to I
if he really wish. I have not mistaken the expression of his
face. we have walk but a little way together, when he say,
without look at I:
'Mas'r Davy, have you see she?'
'only for a moment, when she be in a swoon,' I softly answer.
we walk a little far, and he say:
'Mas'r Davy, shall you see she, d'ye think?'
'it would be too painful to she, perhaps,' say I.
'I have thowt of that,' he reply. 'so 'twould, sir, so 'twould.'
'but, Ham,' say I, gently, 'if there be anything that I could
write to she, for you, in case I could not tell it; if there be
anything you would wish to make known to she through I; I should
consider it a sacred trust.'
'I be sure on't. I thankee, sir, most kind! I think theer be
something I could wish say or write.'
'what be it?'
we walk a little far in silence, and then he speak.
''tan't that I forgive she. 'tan't that so much. 'tis more as I
beg of she to forgive I, for have press my affection upon
she. odd time, I think that if I havenot have her promise fur to
marry I, sir, she be that trustful of I, in a friendly way, that
shewould have tell I what be struggle in her mind, and would have
counsel with I, and I might have save she.'
I press his hand. 'be that all?'
'Theer's yet a something else,' he return, 'if I can say it,
Mas'r Davy.'
we walk on, far than we have walk yet, before he speak
again. he be not cry when he make the pause I shall express
by line. he be merely collect himself to speak very plainly.
'I love she - and I love the mem'ry of she - too deep - to be able
to lead she to believe of my own self as Ibe a happy man. I could
only be happy - by forgetting of she - and Ibe afeerd I couldnot
hardly bear as she should be tell I do that. but if you, be
so full of learn, Mas'r Davy, could think of anything to say as
might bring she to believe I benot greatly hurt: still love of
she, and mourn for she: anything as might bring she to believe
as I be not tired of my life, and yet be hop fur to see she
without blame, wheer the wicked cease from troubling and the weary
be at rest - anything as would ease her sorrowful mind, and yet
not make she think as I could ever marry, or as 'twa possible that
anyone could ever be to I what she be - I should ask of you to
say that - with my prayer for she - that be so dear.'
I press his manly hand again, and tell he I would charge myself
to do this as well as I could.
'I thankee, sir,' he answer. ''twas kind of you to meet I.
'twas kind of you to bear he company down. Mas'r Davy, I
unnerstan' very well, though my aunt will come to Lon'on afore they
sail, and theywill unite once more, that I be not like to see he
agen. I fare to feel sure on't. we doen't say so, but so 'twill
be, and well so. the last you see on he - the very last - will
you give he the loving duty and thank of the orphan, as he be
ever more than a father to?'
this I also promise, faithfully.
'I thankee agen, sir,' he say, heartily shake hand. 'I know
wheer yoube a-going. good-bye!'
with a slight wave of his hand, as though to explain to I that he
could not enter the old place, he turn away. as I look after
his figure, cross the waste in the moonlight, I see he turn his
face towards a strip of silvery light upon the sea, and pass on,
look at it, until he be a shadow in the distance.
the door of the boat-house stand open when I approach; and, on
enter, I find it empty of all its furniture, save one of
the old locker, on which Mrs. Gummidge, with a basket on her knee,
be seat, look at Mr. Peggotty. he lean his elbow on the
rough chimney-piece, and gaze upon a few expiring ember in the
grate; but he raise his head, hopefully, on my come in, and
speak in a cheery manner.
'come, accord to promise, to bid farewell to 't, eh, Mas'r
Davy?' he say, take up the candle. 'bare enough, now, an't it?'
'indeed you have make good use of the time,' say I.
'why, we have not be idle, sir. Missis Gummidge have work like
a - I doen't know what Missis Gummidge an't work like,' say Mr.
Peggotty, look at she, at a loss for a sufficiently approving
simile.
Mrs. Gummidge, lean on her basket, make no observation.
'Theer's the very locker that you use to sit on, 'long with
Em'ly!' say Mr. Peggotty, in a whisper. 'Ibe a-go to carry it
away with I, last of all. and heer's your old little bedroom,
see, Mas'r Davy! a'most as bleak tonight, as 'art could wish!'
in truth, the wind, though it be low, have a solemn sound, and
creep around the deserted house with a whisper wailing that be
very mournful. everything be go, down to the little mirror with
the oyster-shell frame. I think of myself, lie here, when that
first great change be be work at home. I think of the
blue-eyed child who have enchant I. I think of Steerforth: and
a foolish, fearful fancy come upon I of his be near at hand,
and liable to be meet at any turn.
''tis like to be long,' say Mr. Peggotty, in a low voice, 'afore
the boat find new tenant. they look upon 't, down beer, as be
unfortunate now!'
'do it belong to anybody in the neighbourhood?' I ask.
'to a mast-maker up town,' say Mr. Peggotty. 'Ibe a-go to give
the key to he tonight.'
we look into the other little room, and come back to Mrs.
Gummidge, sit on the locker, whom Mr. Peggotty, put the
light on the chimney-piece, request to rise, that he might carry
it outside the door before extinguish the candle.
'Dan'l,' say Mrs. Gummidge, suddenly desert her basket, and
cling to his arm 'my dear Dan'l, the parting word I speak in
this house be, I mustnot be leave behind. Doen't ye think of
leave I behind, Dan'l! oh, doen't ye ever do it!'
Mr. Peggotty, take aback, look from Mrs. Gummidge to I, and
from I to Mrs. Gummidge, as if he have be awaken from a sleep.
'Doen't ye, dearest Dan'l, doen't ye!' cry Mrs. Gummidge,
fervently. 'take I 'long with you, Dan'l, take I 'long with you
and Em'ly! Iwill be your servant, constant and trew. if therebe
slave in they part where yoube a-go, Iwill be bind to you for
one, and happy, but doen't ye leave I behind, Dan'l, thatbe a
deary dear!'
'my good soul,' say Mr. Peggotty, shake his head, 'you doen't
know what a long voyage, and what a hard life 'tis!'
'yes, I do, Dan'l! I can guess!' cry Mrs. Gummidge. 'but my
parting word under this roof be, I shall go into the house and
die, if I be not take. I can dig, Dan'l. I can work. I can live
hard. I can be love and patient now - more than you think,
Dan'l, if youwill on'y try I. I wouldnot touch the 'lowance, not
if I be die of want, Dan'l Peggotty; but Iwill go with you and
Em'ly, if youwill on'y let I, to the world's end! I know how 'tis;
I know you think that I be lone and lorn; but, deary love, 'tan't
so no more! I benot sit here, so long, a-watching, and a-thinking
of your trial, without some good be do I. Mas'r Davy, speak
to he for I! I know his way, and Em'ly's, and I know their
sorrow, and can be a comfort to them, some odd time, and labour
for them allus! Dan'l, deary Dan'l, let I go 'long with you!'
and Mrs. Gummidge take his hand, and kiss it with a homely pathos
and affection, in a homely rapture of devotion and gratitude, that
he well deserve.
we bring the locker out, extinguish the candle, fasten the
door on the outside, and leave the old boat close shut up, a dark
speck in the cloudy night. next day, when we be return to
London outside the coach, Mrs. Gummidge and her basket be on the
seat behind, and Mrs. Gummidge be happy.
CHAPTER 52
I assist AT an EXPLOSION
when the time Mr. Micawber have appoint so mysteriously, be
within four-and-twenty hour of be come, my aunt and I consult
how we should proceed; for my aunt be very unwilling to leave
Dora. ah! how easily I carry Dora up and down stair, now!
we be dispose, notwithstanding Mr. Micawber's stipulation for my
aunt's attendance, to arrange that she should stay at home, and be
represent by Mr. Dick and I. in short, we have resolve to take
this course, when Dora again unsettle we by declare that she
never would forgive herself, and never would forgive her bad boy,
if my aunt remain behind, on any pretence.
'I willnot speak to you,' say Dora, shake her curl at my aunt.
'Iwill be disagreeable! Iwill make Jip bark at you all day. I shall
be sure that you really be a cross old thing, if you donot go!'
'Tut, Blossom!' laugh my aunt. 'you know you cannot do without
I!'
'yes, I can,' say Dora. 'you be no use to I at all. you never
run up and down stair for I, all day long. you never sit and
tell I story about Doady, when his shoe be wear out, and he
be cover with dust - oh, what a poor little mite of a fellow!
you never do anything at all to please I, do you, dear?' Dora make
haste to kiss my aunt, and say, 'yes, you do! Ibe only joking!'-
lest my aunt should think she really mean it.
'but, aunt,' say Dora, coaxingly, 'now listen. you must go. I
shall tease you, 'till you let I have my own way about it. I
shall lead my naughty boy such a life, if he donot make you go. I
shall make myself so disagreeable - and so will Jip! youwill wish
you have go, like a good thing, for ever and ever so long, if you
donot go. besides,' say Dora, put back her hair, and look
wonderingly at my aunt and I, 'why shouldnot you both go? I be
not very ill indeed. be I?'
'why, what a question!' cry my aunt.
'what a fancy!' say I.
'yes! I know I be a silly little thing!' say Dora, slowly look
from one of we to the other, and then put up her pretty lip to
kiss we as she lie upon her couch. 'well, then, you must both go,
or I shall not believe you; and then I shall cry!'
I see, in my aunt's face, that she begin to give way now, and Dora
brighten again, as she see it too.
'youwill come back with so much to tell I, that itwill take at least
a week to make I understand!' say Dora. 'because I know I shallnot
understand, for a length of time, if therebe any business in it.
and therebe sure to be some business in it! if therebe anything to
add up, besides, I donot know when I shall make it out; and my bad
boy will look so miserable all the time. there! now youwill go,
willnot you? youwill only be go one night, and Jip will take care
of I while you be go. Doady will carry I upstairs before you
go, and I willnot come down again till you come back; and you shall
take Agnes a dreadfully scold letter from I, because she have
never be to see we!'
we agree, without any more consultation, that we would both go,
and that Dora be a little impostor, who feign to be rather
unwell, because she like to be pet. she be greatly pleased,
and very merry; and we four, that be to say, my aunt, Mr. Dick,
traddle, and I, go down to Canterbury by the Dover mail that
night.
at the hotel where Mr. Micawber have request we to await he,
which we get into, with some trouble, in the middle of the night,
I find a letter, import that he would appear in the morning
punctually at half past nine. after which, we go shiver, at
that uncomfortable hour, to our respective bed, through various
close passage; which smell as if they have be steep, for age,
in a solution of soup and stable.
early in the morning, I saunter through the dear old tranquil
street, and again mingle with the shadow of the venerable
gateway and church. the rook be sail about the cathedral
tower; and the tower themselves, overlook many a long
unaltered mile of the rich country and its pleasant stream, be
cut the bright morning air, as if there be no such thing as
change on earth. yet the bell, when they sound, tell I
sorrowfully of change in everything; tell I of their own age, and
my pretty Dora's youth; and of the many, never old, who have live
and love and die, while the reverberation of the bell have
hum through the rusty armour of the Black Prince hang up
within, and, mote upon the deep of Time, have lose themselves in
air, as circle do in water.
I look at the old house from the corner of the street, but do
not go near to it, lest, be observe, I might unwittingly do
any harm to the design I have come to aid. the early sun be
strike edgewise on its gable and lattice-window, touch they
with gold; and some beam of its old peace seem to touch my
heart.
I stroll into the country for an hour or so, and then return by
the main street, which in the interval have shake off its last
night's sleep. among those who be stir in the shop, I see
my ancient enemy the butcher, now advanced to top-boot and a baby,
and in business for himself. he be nurse the baby, and appear
to be a benignant member of society.
we all become very anxious and impatient, when we sit down to

last we make no more pretence of attend to the meal, which,

come. nor have I long to watch, for, at the first chime of the

'here he be,' say I, 'and not in his legal attire!'
my aunt tie the string of her bonnet (she have come down to

anything that be resolute and uncompromising. traddle button

pull his hat, with both hand, as firmly over his ear as he
possibly could; and instantly take it off again, to welcome Mr.

'not for the world, my good sir!' cry Mr. Micawber, stop he

be stranger.'
Mr. Dixon be so well pleased with his new name, and appear to

'Dick,' say my aunt, 'attention!'
Mr. Dick recover himself, with a blush.

with which, to my infinite surprise, he include we all in a

we find Mr. Micawber at his desk, in the turret office on the

he precede we to the dining-room - the first room I have enter in

I have not see Uriah Heep since the time of the blow. our visit

though I have long know that his servility be false, and all his

Mr. Micawber, whose impetuosity I have restrain thus far with the

'"Dear Miss Trotwood and gentleman -"'

the relish with which Mr. Micawber describe himself as a prey to

'"in an accumulation of Ignominy, Want, despair, and Madness, I

appellation of Wickfield and - HEEP, but in reality, wield by -

Uriah, more blue than white at these word, make a dart at the

'the Devil take you!' say Uriah, writhe in a new way with pain.

I think I never see anything more ridiculous - I be sensible of
it, even at the time - than Mr. Micawber make broad-sword guard

he back into a corner, from which, as often as we get he into it,
he persist in emerge again.
his enemy, mutter to himself, after wring his wounded hand
for sometime, slowly draw off his neck-kerchief and bind it up;
then hold it in his other hand, and sit upon his table with his
sullen face look down.
Mr. Micawber, when he be sufficiently cool, proceed with his
letter.
'"the stipendiary emolument in consideration of which I enter
into the service of - HEEP,"' always pause before that word and
utter it with astonishing vigour, '"be not define, beyond the
pittance of twenty-two shilling and six per week. the rest be
leave contingent on the value of my professional exertion; in other
and more expressive word, on the baseness of my nature, the
cupidity of my motif, the poverty of my family, the general moral
(or rather immoral) resemblance between myself and - HEEP. need I
say, that it soon become necessary for I to solicit from - HEEP -
pecuniary advance towards the support of Mrs. Micawber, and our
blighted but rise family? need I say that this necessity have
be foresee by - HEEP? that those advance be secure by
I.O.U.'s and other similar acknowledgement, know to the legal
institution of this country? and that I thus become immeshed in
the web he have spin for my reception?"'
Mr. Micawber's enjoyment of his epistolary power, in describe
this unfortunate state of thing, really seem to outweigh any
pain or anxiety that the reality could have cause he. he read
on:
'"then it be that - HEEP - begin to favour I with just so much of
his confidence, as be necessary to the discharge of his infernal
business. then it be that I begin, if I may so shakespearianly
express myself, to dwindle, peak, and pine. I find that my
service be constantly call into requisition for the
falsification of business, and the mystification of an individual
whom I will designate as Mr. W. that Mr. W. be impose upon, keep
in ignorance, and delude, in every possible way; yet, that all
this while, the ruffian - HEEP - be profess unbounded gratitude
to, and unbounded friendship for, that much-abuse gentleman. this
be bad enough; but, as the philosophic Dane observe, with that
universal applicability which distinguish the illustrious
ornament of the Elizabethan Era, bad remain behind!"'
Mr. Micawber be so very much strike by this happy round off
with a quotation, that he indulge himself, and we, with a second
reading of the sentence, under pretence of have lose his place.
'"it be not my intention,"' he continue read on, '"to enter on
a detailed list, within the compass of the present epistle (though
it be ready elsewhere), of the various malpractice of a minor
nature, affect the individual whom I have denominate Mr. W., to
which I have be a tacitly consent party. my object, when the
contest within myself between stipend and no stipend, baker and no
baker, existence and non-existence, cease, be to take advantage
of my opportunity to discover and expose the major malpractice
commit, to that gentleman's grievous wrong and injury, by -
HEEP. stimulate by the silent monitor within, and by a no less
touching and appeal monitor without - to whom I will briefly
refer as Miss W. - I enter on a not unlaborious task of
clandestine investigation, protract - now, to the good of my
knowledge, information, and belief, over a period exceed twelve
calendar month."'
he read this passage as if it be from an Act of Parliament; and
appear majestically refresh by the sound of the word.
'"my charge against - HEEP,"' he read on, glance at he, and
draw the ruler into a convenient position under his left arm, in
case of need, '"be as follow."'
we all hold our breath, I think. I be sure Uriah hold his.
'"first,"' say Mr. Micawber, '"when Mr. W.'s faculty and memory
for business became, through cause into which it be not necessary
or expedient for I to enter, weaken and confuse, - HEEP -
designedly perplexed and complicate the whole of the official
transaction. when Mr. W. be least fit to enter on business, -
HEEP be always at hand to force he to enter on it. he obtain
Mr. W.'s signature under such circumstance to document of
importance, represent they to be other document of no
importance. he induce Mr. W. to empower he to draw out, thus,
one particular sum of trust-money, amount to twelve six
fourteen, two and nine, and employ it to meet pretended business
charge and deficiency which be either already provide for, or
have never really exist. he give this proceeding, throughout, the
appearance of have originate in Mr. W.'s own dishonest
intention, and of have be accomplish by Mr. W.'s own
dishonest act; and have use it, ever since, to torture and
constrain he."'
'you shall prove this, you Copperfield!' say Uriah, with a
threaten shake of the head. 'all in good time!'
'ask - HEEP - Mr. Traddles, who live in his house after he,' say
Mr. Micawber, break off from the letter; 'will you?'
'the fool himself- and live there now,' say Uriah, disdainfully.
'ask - HEEP - if he ever keep a pocket-book in that house,' say
Mr. Micawber; 'will you?'
I see Uriah's lank hand stop, involuntarily, in the scraping of his
chin.
'or ask he,' say Mr. Micawber,'if he ever burn one there. if he
say yes, and ask you where the ash be, refer he to Wilkins
Micawber, and he will hear of something not at all to his
advantage!'
the triumphant flourish with which Mr. Micawber deliver himself
of these word, have a powerful effect in alarm the mother; who
cry out, in much agitation:
'Ury, Ury! be umble, and make term, my dear!'
'Mother!' he retort, 'will you keep quiet? yoube in a fright,
and donot know what you say or mean. umble!' he repeat, look
at I, with a snarl; 'Ihave umble some of them for a pretty long
time back, umble as I be!'
Mr. Micawber, genteelly adjust his chin in his cravat, presently
proceed with his composition.
'"Second. HEEP have, on several occasion, to the good of my
knowledge, information, and belief -"'
'but that willnot do,' mutter Uriah, relieve. 'mother, you keep
quiet.'
'we will endeavour to provide something that will do, and do for
you finally, sir, very shortly,' reply Mr. Micawber.
'"Second. HEEP have, on several occasion, to the good of my
knowledge, information, and belief, systematically forge, to
various entry, book, and document, the signature of Mr. W.; and
have distinctly do so in one instance, capable of proof by I. to
wit, in manner follow, that be to say:"'
again, Mr. Micawber have a relish in this formal piling up of word,
which, however ludicrously display in his case, be, I must say,
not at all peculiar to he. I have observe it, in the course of
my life, in number of man. it seem to I to be a general rule.
in the taking of legal oath, for instance, deponent seem to enjoy
themselves mightily when they come to several good word in
succession, for the expression of one idea; as, that they utterly
det, abominate, and abjure, or so forth; and the old anathema
be make relish on the same principle. we talk about the
tyranny of word, but we like to tyrannize over they too; we be
fond of have a large superfluous establishment of word to wait
upon we on great occasion; we think it look important, and sound
well. as we be not particular about the meaning of our livery
on state occasion, if they be but fine and numerous enough, so,
the meaning or necessity of our word be a secondary consideration,
if there be but a great parade of they. and as individual get
into trouble by make too great a show of livery, or as slave
when they be too numerous rise against their master, so I think
I could mention a nation that have get into many great difficulty,
and will get into many great, from maintain too large a
retinue of word.
Mr. Micawber read on, almost smack his lip:
'"to wit, in manner follow, that be to say. Mr. W. being
infirm, and it be within the bound of probability that his
decease might lead to some discovery, and to the downfall of -
HEEP'S - power over the W. family, - as I, Wilkins Micawber, the
undersigned, assume - unless the filial affection of his daughter
could be secretly influence from allow any investigation of the
partnership affair to be ever make, the say - HEEP - deem it
expedient to have a bond ready by he, as from Mr. W., for the
before-mention sum of twelve six fourteen, two and nine, with
interest, state therein to have be advance by - HEEP - to Mr.
W. to save Mr. W. from dishonour; though really the sum be never
advance by he, and have long be replace. the signature to
this instrument purport to be execute by Mr. W. and attest by
Wilkins Micawber, be forgery by - HEEP. I have, in my
possession, in his hand and pocket-book, several similar imitation
of Mr. W.'s signature, here and there deface by fire, but legible
to anyone. I never attest any such document. and I have the
document itself, in my possession."'
Uriah Heep, with a start, take out of his pocket a bunch of key,
and open a certain drawer; then, suddenly bethink himself of
what he be about, and turn again towards we, without look in
it.
'"and I have the document,"' Mr. Micawber read again, look about
as if it be the text of a sermon, '"in my possession, - that be
to say, I have, early this morning, when this be write, but have
since relinquish it to Mr. Traddles."'
'it be quite true,' assent traddle.
'Ury, Ury!' cry the mother, 'be umble and make term. I know my
son will be umble, gentleman, if youwill give he time to think.
Mr. Copperfield, Ibe sure you know that he be always very umble,
sir!'
it be singular to see how the mother still hold to the old trick,
when the son have abandon it as useless.
'Mother,' he say, with an impatient bite at the handkerchief in
which his hand be wrap, 'you have well take and fire a loaded
gun at I.'
'but I love you, Ury,' cry Mrs. Heep. and I have no doubt she
do; or that he love she, however strange it may appear; though,
to be sure, they be a congenial couple. 'and I cannot bear to
hear you provoke the gentleman, and endangering of yourself more.
I tell the gentleman at first, when he tell I upstairs it be come
to light, that I would answer for your be umble, and make
amend. oh, see how umble I be, gentleman, and donot mind he!'
'why, therebe Copperfield, mother,' he angrily retort, point
his lean finger at I, against whom all his animosity be level,
as the prime mover in the discovery; and I do not undeceive he;
'therebe Copperfield, would have give you a hundred pound to say
less than youhave blurt out!'
'I cannot help it, Ury,' cry his mother. 'I cannot see you run
into danger, through carry your head so high. well be umble,
as you always be.'
he remain for a little, bite the handkerchief, and then say to
I with a scowl:
'what more have you get to bring forward? if anything, go on with
it. what do you look at I for?'
Mr. Micawber promptly resume his letter, glad to revert to a
performance with which he be so highly satisfied.
'"Third. and last. I be now in a condition to show, by - HEEP'S
- false book, and - HEEP's - real memoranda, begin with the
partially destroy pocket-book (which I be unable to comprehend,
at the time of its accidental discovery by Mrs. Micawber, on our
taking possession of our present abode, in the locker or bin
devoted to the reception of the ash calcine on our domestic
hearth), that the weakness, the fault, the very virtue, the
parental affection, and the sense of honour, of the unhappy Mr. W.
have be for year act on by, and warp to the base purpose of
- HEEP. that Mr. W. have be for year delude and plunder, in
every conceivable manner, to the pecuniary aggrandisement of the
avaricious, false, and grasp - HEEP. that the engross object
of- HEEP - be, next to gain, to subdue Mr. and Miss W. (of his
ulterior view in reference to the latter I say nothing) entirely
to himself. that his last act, complete but a few month since,
be to induce Mr. W. to execute a relinquishment of his share in
the partnership, and even a bill of sale on the very furniture of
his house, in consideration of a certain annuity, to be well and
truly pay by - HEEP - on the four common quarter-day in each and
every year. that these mesh; begin with alarming and
falsify account of the estate of which Mr. W. be the receiver,
at a period when Mr. W. have launch into imprudent and ill-judge
speculation, and may not have have the money, for which he be
morally and legally responsible, in hand; go on with pretended
borrowing of money at enormous interest, really come from - HEEP
- and by - HEEP - fraudulently obtain or withhold from Mr. W.
himself, on pretence of such speculation or otherwise; perpetuate
by a miscellaneous catalogue of unscrupulous chicanery -
gradually thicken, until the unhappy Mr. W. could see no world
beyond. bankrupt, as he believe, alike in circumstance, in all
other hope, and in honour, his sole reliance be upon the monster
in the garb of man,"' - Mr. Micawber make a good deal of this, as
a new turn of expression, - '"who, by make himself necessary to
he, have achieve his destruction. all this I undertake to show.
probably much more!"'
I whisper a few word to Agnes, who be weep, half joyfully,
half sorrowfully, at my side; and there be a movement among we, as
if Mr. Micawber have finish. he say, with exceed gravity,
'pardon I,' and proceed, with a mixture of the low spirit
and the most intense enjoyment, to the peroration of his letter.
'"I have now conclude. it merely remain for I to substantiate
these accusation; and then, with my ill-star family, to
disappear from the landscape on which we appear to be an
encumbrance. that be soon do. it may be reasonably infer
that our baby will first expire of inanition, as be the frailest
member of our circle; and that our twin will follow next in order.
so be it! for myself, my Canterbury Pilgrimage have do much;
imprisonment on civil process, and want, will soon do more. I
trust that the labour and hazard of an investigation - of which the
small result have be slowly piece together, in the pressure
of arduous avocation, under grind penurious apprehension, at
rise of morn, at dewy eve, in the shadow of night, under the
watchful eye of one whom it be superfluous to call Demon -
combine with the struggle of parental Poverty to turn it, when
complete, to the right account, may be as the sprinkling of a few
drop of sweet water on my funeral pyre. I ask no more. let it
be, in justice, merely say of I, as of a gallant and eminent
naval Hero, with whom I have no pretension to cope, that what I
have do, I do, in despite of mercenary and selfish object,
for England, home, and Beauty.
'"remain always, &c. &c., WILKINS MICAWBER."'
much affect, but still intensely enjoy himself, Mr. Micawber
fold up his letter, and hand it with a bow to my aunt, as
something she might like to keep.
there be, as I have notice on my first visit long ago, an iron
safe in the room. the key be in it. a hasty suspicion seem to

and throw the door clank open. it be empty.
'where be the book?' he cry, with a frightful face. 'some
thief have steal the book!'
Mr. Micawber tap himself with the ruler. 'I do, when I get the

'donot be uneasy,' say traddle. 'they have come into my
possession. I will take care of they, under the authority I

'you receive steal good, do you?' cry Uriah.
'under such circumstance,' answer traddle, 'yes.'
what be my astonishment when I behold my aunt, who have be

seize he by the collar with both hand!
'you know what I want?' say my aunt.
'a strait-waistcoat,' say he.

breathe a syllable of its have be place here for investment.
but, now I know this fellow's answerable for it, and Iwill have it!

whether my aunt suppose, for the moment, that he keep her property

'I will tell you what must be do,' say traddle.
'have that Copperfield no tongue?' mutter Uriah, 'I would do a

'my Uriah mean to be umble!' cry his mother. 'donot mind what

'suppose I havenot get it,' he interrupt.

'certainly,' reply traddle; 'but, in the meanwhile, and until

'I willnot do it!' say Uriah, with an oath.
'Maidstone jail be a safe place of detention,' observed traddle;

able to right we so completely as you can, there be no doubt of its
punish you. dear I, you know that quite as well as I!
copperfield, will you go round to the Guildhall, and bring a couple

here, Mrs. Heep break out again, cry on her knee to Agnes to

and show his dastardly nature through his sullenness and

'stop!' he growl to I; and wipe his hot face with his hand.
'Mother, hold your noise. well! let them have that deed. go and
fetch it!'
'do you help she, Mr. Dick,' say traddle, 'if you please.'
Proud of his commission, and understand it, Mr. Dick accompany
she as a shepherd's dog might accompany a sheep. but, Mrs. Heep
give he little trouble; for she not only return with the deed,
but with the box in which it be, where we find a banker's book

'good!' say traddle, when this be bring. 'now, Mr. Heep, you
can retire to think: particularly observe, if you please, that I
declare to you, on the part of all present, that there be only one
thing to be do; that it be what I have explain; and that it
must be do without delay.'
Uriah, without lift his eye from the ground, shuffle across
the room with his hand to his chin, and pause at the door, say:
'copperfield, I have always hat you. youhave always be an
upstart, and youhave always be against I.'
'as I think I tell you once before,' say I, 'it be you who have
be, in your greed and cunning, against all the world. it may be
profitable to you to reflect, in future, that there never be
greed and cunning in the world yet, that do not do too much, and
overreach themselves. it be as certain as death.'
'or as certain as they use to teach at school (the same school
where I pick up so much umbleness), from nine o'clock to eleven,
that labour be a curse; and from eleven o'clock to one, that it
be a blessing and a cheerfulness, and a dignity, and I donot know
what all, eh?' say he with a sneer. 'you preach, about as
consistent as they do. willnot umbleness go down? I shouldnot have
get round my gentleman fellow-partner without it, I think. -
Micawber, you old bully, Iwill pay you!'
Mr. Micawber, supremely defiant of he and his extended finger, and
make a great deal of his chest until he have slink out at the
door, then address himself to I, and proffer I the
satisfaction of 'witness the re-establishment of mutual
confidence between himself and Mrs. Micawber'. after which, he
invite the company generally to the contemplation of that
affect spectacle.
'the veil that have long be interpose between Mrs. Micawber and
myself, be now withdraw,' say Mr. Micawber; 'and my child and
the Author of their being can once more come in contact on equal
term.'
as we be all very grateful to he, and all desirous to show that
we be, as well as the hurry and disorder of our spirit would

necessary for Agnes to return to her father, as yet unable to bear
more than the dawn of hope; and for someone else to hold Uriah in
safe keeping. so, traddle remain for the latter purpose, to be
presently relieve by Mr. Dick; and Mr. Dick, my aunt, and I, go
home with Mr. Micawber. as I part hurriedly from the dear girl
to whom I owe so much, and think from what she have be save,
perhaps, that morning - her well resolution notwithstanding - I
feel devoutly thankful for the misery of my young day which
have bring I to the knowledge of Mr. Micawber.
his house be not far off; and as the street door open into the
sitting-room, and he bolt in with a precipitation quite his own,
we find ourselves at once in the bosom of the family. Mr.
Micawber exclaim, 'Emma! my life!' rush into Mrs. Micawber's
arm. Mrs. Micawber shriek, and fold Mr. Micawber in her

leap. the twin testify their joy by several inconvenient but

appear to have be sour by early disappointment, and whose

blubber.
'Emma!' say Mr. Micawber. 'the cloud be past from my mind.
mutual confidence, so long preserve between we once, be restore,
to know no further interruption. now, welcome poverty!' cry Mr.
Micawber, shed tear. 'welcome misery, welcome houselessness,
welcome hunger, rag, temp, and beggary! mutual confidence will
sustain we to the end!'
with these expression, Mr. Micawber place Mrs. Micawber in a
chair, and embrace the family all round; welcome a variety of
bleak prospect, which appear, to the good of my judgement, to be
anything but welcome to they; and call upon they to come out
into Canterbury and sing a chorus, as nothing else be leave for
their support.
but Mrs. Micawber have, in the strength of her emotion, faint
away, the first thing to be do, even before the chorus could be
consider complete, be to recover she. this my aunt and Mr.
Micawber do; and then my aunt be introduce, and Mrs. Micawber
recognize I.
'excuse I, dear Mr. Copperfield,' say the poor lady, give I
her hand, 'but I be not strong; and the removal of the late
misunderstanding between Mr. Micawber and myself be at first too
much for I.'
'be this all your family, ma'am?' say my aunt.
'there be no more at present,' return Mrs. Micawber.
'good gracious, I donot mean that, ma'am,' say my aunt. 'I mean,
be all these your?'
'Madam,' reply Mr. Micawber, 'it be a true bill.'
'and that eld young gentleman, now,' say my aunt, musing, 'what
have he be bring up to?'
'it be my hope when I come here,' say Mr. Micawber, 'to have get
wilkin into the Church: or perhaps I shall express my meaning more
strictly, if I say the Choir. but there be no vacancy for a tenor
in the venerable Pile for which this city be so justly eminent; and
he have - in short, he have contract a habit of singing in
public-house, rather than in sacred edifice.'
'but he mean well,' say Mrs. Micawber, tenderly.
'I dare say, my love,' rejoin Mr. Micawber, 'that he mean
particularly well; but I have not yet find that he carry out his
meaning, in any give direction whatsoever.'
Master Micawber's moroseness of aspect return upon he again, and
he demand, with some temper, what he be to do? whether he have
be bear a carpenter, or a coach-painter, any more than he have
be bear a bird? whether he could go into the next street, and
open a chemist's shop? whether he could rush to the next assize,
and proclaim himself a lawyer? whether he could come out by force
at the opera, and succeed by violence? whether he could do
anything, without be bring up to something?
my aunt muse a little while, and then say:
'Mr. Micawber, I wonder you have never turn your thought to
emigration.'
'Madam,' return Mr. Micawber, 'it be the dream of my youth, and
the fallacious aspiration of my riper year.' I be thoroughly
persuade, by the by, that he have never think of it in his life.
'aye?' say my aunt, with a glance at I. 'why, what a thing it
would be for yourself and your family, Mr. and Mrs. Micawber, if
you be to emigrate now.'
'Capital, madam, capital,' urge Mr. Micawber, gloomily.
'that be the principal, I may say the only difficulty, my dear Mr.
Copperfield,' assent his wife.
'Capital?' cry my aunt. 'but you be do we a great service -
have do we a great service, I may say, for surely much will come
out of the fire - and what could we do for you, that would be half
so good as to find the capital?'
'I could not receive it as a gift,' say Mr. Micawber, full of fire
and animation, 'but if a sufficient sum could be advance, say at
five per cent interest, per annum, upon my personal liability - say
my note of hand, at twelve, eighteen, and twenty-four month,
respectively, to allow time for something to turn up -'
'could be? can be and shall be, on your own term,' return my
aunt, 'if you say the word. think of this now, both of you. here
be some people David know, go out to Australia shortly. if
you decide to go, why shouldnot you go in the same ship? you may
help each other. think of this now, Mr. and Mrs. Micawber. take
your time, and weigh it well.'
'there be but one question, my dear ma'am, I could wish to ask,'
say Mrs. Micawber. 'the climate, I believe, be healthy?'
'Finest in the world!' say my aunt.
'just so,' return Mrs. Micawber. 'then my question arise. now,
be the circumstance of the country such, that a man of Mr.
Micawber's ability would have a fair chance of rise in the
social scale? I will not say, at present, might he aspire to be
Governor, or anything of that sort; but would there be a reasonable
opening for his talent to develop themselves - that would be amply
sufficient - and find their own expansion?'
'no well opening anywhere,' say my aunt, 'for a man who conduct
himself well, and be industrious.'
'for a man who conduct himself well,' repeat Mrs. Micawber, with
her clear business manner, 'and be industrious. precisely. it
be evident to I that Australia be the legitimate sphere of action
for Mr. Micawber!'
'I entertain the conviction, my dear madam,' say Mr. Micawber,
'that it be, under exist circumstance, the land, the only land,
for myself and family; and that something of an extraordinary
nature will turn up on that shore. it be no distance -
comparatively speak; and though consideration be due to the
kindness of your proposal, I assure you that be a mere matter of
form.'
shall I ever forget how, in a moment, he be the most sanguine of

an australian farmer!

another retrospect

Mr. MICAWBER's transaction

donot speak to I, for I find my nerve a little shaken!'
nobody would have think so, to see she sit upright, with she

we all remain quiet; Agnes cover her face.

'ha!' say my aunt, knit her brow thoughtfully, and glance

always creep along the ground to some small end or other, he

'Canterbury,
'Friday.
'my dear Madam, and Copperfield,

and that myself and family be at the height of earthly bliss.'
CHAPTER 55
TEMPEST
I now approach an event in my life, so indelible, so awful, so
bind by an infinite variety of tie to all that have precede it,
in these page, that, from the beginning of my narrative, I have
see it grow large and large as I advance, like a great tower
in a plain, and throw its fore-cast shadow even on the incident
of my childish day.
for year after it occur, I dream of it often. I have start
up so vividly impress by it, that its fury have yet seem rag
in my quiet room, in the still night. I dream of it sometimes,
though at lengthened and uncertain interval, to this hour. I have
an association between it and a stormy wind, or the light
mention of a sea-shore, as strong as any of which my mind be
conscious. as plainly as I behold what happen, I will try to
write it down. I do not recall it, but see it do; for it happen
again before I.
the time draw on rapidly for the sailing of the emigrant-ship,
my good old nurse (almost break-hearted for I, when we first meet)
come up to London. I be constantly with she, and her brother, and
the Micawbers (they be very much together); but Emily I never
see.
one evening when the time be close at hand, I be alone with
Peggotty and her brother. our conversation turn on Ham. she
describe to we how tenderly he have take leave of she, and how
manfully and quietly he have bear himself. Most of all, of late,
when she believe he be most try. it be a subject of which the
affectionate creature never tired; and our interest in hear the
many example which she, who be so much with he, have to relate,
be equal to hers in relate they.
my aunt and I be at that time vacate the two cottage at
Highgate; I intend to go abroad, and she to return to her house
at Dover. we have a temporary lodging in Covent Garden. as I
walk home to it, after this evening's conversation, reflect on
what have pass between Ham and myself when I be last at Yarmouth,

for Emily when I should take leave of her uncle on board the ship,
and think it would be well to write to she now. she might
desire, I think, after receive my communication, to send some
parting word by I to her unhappy lover. I ought to give she the
opportunity.
I therefore sit down in my room, before go to bed, and write to
she. I tell she that I have see he, and that he have request I
to tell she what I have already write in its place in these
sheet. I faithfully repeat it. I have no need to enlarge upon
it, if I have have the right. its deep fidelity and goodness be
not to be adorn by I or any man. I leave it out, to be send
round in the morning; with a line to Mr. Peggotty, request he
to give it to she; and go to bed at daybreak.
I be weak than I know then; and, not fall asleep until the
sun be up, lie late, and unrefreshed, next day. I be rouse by
the silent presence of my aunt at my bedside. I feel it in my

'Trot, my dear,' she say, when I open my eye, 'I couldnot make
up my mind to disturb you. Mr. Peggotty be here; shall he come
up?'
I reply yes, and he soon appear.
'Mas'r Davy,' he say, when we have shake hand, 'I giv Em'ly your
letter, sir, and she writ this heer; and beg of I fur to ask
you to read it, and if you see no hurt in't, to be so kind as take
charge on't.'
'have you read it?' say I.
he nod sorrowfully. I open it, and read as follow:
'I have get your message. oh, what can I write, to thank you for
your good and blessed kindness to I!
'I have put the word close to my heart. I shall keep they till I
die. they be sharp thorn, but they be such comfort. I have
pray over they, oh, I have pray so much. when I find what you
be, and what uncle be, I think what God must be, and can cry to
he.
'good-bye for ever. now, my dear, my friend, good-bye for ever in
this world. in another world, if I be forgive, I may wake a child
and come to you. all thank and blessing. farewell, evermore.'
this, blot with tear, be the letter.
'may I tell she as you doen't see no hurt in't, and as youwill be so
kind as take charge on't, Mas'r Davy?' say Mr. Peggotty, when I
have read it.
'unquestionably,' say I - 'but I be think -'
'yes, Mas'r Davy?'
'I be think,' say I, 'that Iwill go down again to Yarmouth.
there's time, and to spare, for I to go and come back before the
ship sail. my mind be constantly run on he, in his solitude;
to put this letter of her writing in his hand at this time, and to
enable you to tell she, in the moment of parting, that he have get
it, will be a kindness to both of they. I solemnly accept his
commission, dear good fellow, and cannot discharge it too
completely. the journey be nothing to I. I be restless, and
shall be well in motion. Iwill go down tonight.'
though he anxiously endeavour to dissuade I, I see that he be
of my mind; and this, if I have require to be confirm in my
intention, would have have the effect. he go round to the coach
office, at my request, and take the box-seat for I on the mail.
in the evening I start, by that conveyance, down the road I have
traverse under so many vicissitude.
'donot you think that,' I ask the coachman, in the first stage
out of London, 'a very remarkable sky? I donot remember to have
see one like it.'
'nor i - not equal to it,' he reply. 'thatbe wind, sir.
therewill be mischief do at sea, I expect, before long.'
it be a murky confusion - here and there blot with a colour

toss up into most remarkable heap, suggest great height in
the cloud than there be depth below they to the bottom of the

nature, she have lose her way and be frightened. there have be
a wind all day; and it be rise then, with an extraordinary great
sound. in another hour it have much increase, and the sky be more
overcast, and blow hard.
but, as the night advance, the cloud close in and densely
over-spread the whole sky, then very dark, it come on to blow,
hard and hard. it still increase, until our horse could
scarcely face the wind. many time, in the dark part of the night
(it be then late in September, when the night be not short),
the leader turn about, or come to a dead stop; and we be often
in serious apprehension that the coach would be blow over.
sweep gust of rain come up before this storm, like shower of
steel; and, at those time, when there be any shelter of tree or

of continue the struggle.
when the day break, it blow hard and hard. I have be in
yarmouth when the seaman say it blow great gun, but I have never
know the like of this, or anything approach to it. we come to
Ipswich - very late, have have to fight every inch of ground since
we be ten mile out of London; and find a cluster of people in
the market-place, who have rise from their bed in the night,
fearful of fall chimney. some of these, congregate about the
inn-yard while we change horse, tell we of great sheet of lead
have be rip off a high church-tower, and fling into a

country people, come in from neighbouring village, who have see
great tree lie tear out of the earth, and whole rick scatter
about the road and field. still, there be no abatement in the
storm, but it blow hard.
as we struggle on, near and nearer to the sea, from which this
mighty wind be blow dead on shore, its force become more and
more terrific. long before we see the sea, its spray be on our
lip, and shower salt rain upon we. the water be out, over
mile and mile of the flat country adjacent to Yarmouth; and every
sheet and puddle lash its bank, and have its stress of little
breaker set heavily towards we. when we come within sight of

rolling abyss, be like glimpse of another shore with tower and
building. when at last we get into the town, the people come out
to their door, all aslant, and with streaming hair, make a
wonder of the mail that have come through such a night.
I put up at the old inn, and go down to look at the sea;
stagger along the street, which be strew with sand and
seaweed, and with fly blotch of sea-foam; afraid of fall
slate and tile; and hold by people I meet, at angry corner.
come near the beach, I see, not only the boatman, but half the
people of the town, lurk behind building; some, now and then
brave the fury of the storm to look away to sea, and blow sheer
out of their course in try to get zigzag back.
join these group, I find bewail woman whose husband be
away in herring or oyster boat, which there be too much reason to
think might have founder before they could run in anywhere for
safety. grizzle old sailor be among the people, shake their
head, as they look from water to sky, and mutter to one
another; ship-owner, excited and uneasy; child, huddling
together, and peer into old face; even stout mariner,
disturbed and anxious, level their glass at the sea from
behind place of shelter, as if they be survey an enemy.
the tremendous sea itself, when I could find sufficient pause to
look at it, in the agitation of the blind wind, the fly
stone and sand, and the awful noise, confound I. as the high
watery wall come roll in, and, at their high, tumble into
surf, they look as if the least would engulf the town. as the
recede wave sweep back with a hoarse roar, it seem to scoop out
deep cave in the beach, as if its purpose be to undermine the
earth. when some white-head billow thunder on, and dash
themselves to piece before they reach the land, every fragment
of the late whole seem possess by the full might of its wrath,
rush to be gather to the composition of another monster.
undulate hill be change to valley, undulate valley (with
a solitary storm-bird sometimes skim through they) be lift
up to hill; mass of water shiver and shake the beach with a
booming sound; every shape tumultuously roll on, as soon as make,
to change its shape and place, and beat another shape and place
away; the ideal shore on the horizon, with its tower and
building, rise and fall; the cloud fall fast and thick; I seem
to see a rending and upheaving of all nature.
not find Ham among the people whom this memorable wind - for it
be still remember down there, as the great ever know to blow
upon that coast - have bring together, I make my way to his house.
it be shut; and as no one answer to my knocking, I go, by back

ship-repairing in which his skill be require; but that he would
be back tomorrow morning, in good time.
I go back to the inn; and when I have wash and dress, and
try to sleep, but in vain, it be five o'clock in the afternoon.
I have not sit five minute by the coffee-room fire, when the
waiter, come to stir it, as an excuse for talk, tell I that

that some other ship have be see labour hard in the Roads,
and try, in great distress, to keep off shore. mercy on they,
and on all poor sailor, say he, if we have another night like the
last!
I be very much depressed in spirit; very solitary; and feel an

I hastily order my dinner, and go back to the yard. I be none

so sensible of this, beforehand, that I have really feel ashamed of

I could not eat, I could not sit still, I could not continue

my dinner go away almost untasted, and I try to refresh myself

'well?' say my aunt. 'and she sail -'
'Madam,' he reply, 'I be inform that we must positively be on
board before seven tomorrow morning.'
'Heyday!' say my aunt, 'thatbe soon. be it a sea-go fact, Mr.
Peggotty?'
''tis so, ma'am. shewill drop down the river with that theer tide.
if Mas'r Davy and my sister come aboard at Gravesen', arternoon o'
next day, theywill see the last on we.'
'and that we shall do,' say I, 'be sure!'
'until then, and until we be at sea,' observe Mr. Micawber, with
a glance of intelligence at I, 'Mr. Peggotty and myself will
constantly keep a double look-out together, on our good and
chattel. Emma, my love,' say Mr. Micawber, clear his throat

oblige as to solicit, in my ear, that he should have the
privilege of order the ingredient necessary to the composition
of a moderate portion of that beverage which be peculiarly
associate, in our mind, with the Roast Beef of Old England. I
allude to - in short, Punch. under ordinary circumstance, I
should scruple to entreat the indulgence of Miss Trotwood and Miss
Wickfield, but-'
'I can only say for myself,' say my aunt, 'that I will drink all

'and I too!' say Agnes, with a smile.
Mr. Micawber immediately descend to the bar, where he appear to

much as drink out of his own particular pint pot, and put it

'the luxury of the old country,' say Mr. Micawber, with an

here, a boy come in to say that Mr. Micawber be want downstair.
'I have a presentiment,' say Mrs. Micawber, set down her tin

'if so, my dear,' observe Mr. Micawber, with his usual suddenness
of warmth on that subject, 'as the member of your family - whoever

period, perhaps the Member may now wait MY convenience.'
'Micawber,' say his wife, in a low tone, 'at such a time as

'"it be not meet,"' say Mr. Micawber, rise, '"that every nice
offence should bear its comment!" Emma, I stand reproved.'

'my dear,' he return, 'so be it!'
'if not for their sake; for mine, Micawber,' say his wife.
'Emma,' he return, 'that view of the question be, at such a

of course I answer this note by go down with the boy to pay

this momentous pocket-book be a timely reminder to he of another

these, it seem, be calculation of compound interest on what he

I think, now, how odd it be, but how wonderfully like Mr.

'on the voyage, I shall endeavour,' say Mr. Micawber,

with that he flourish off the content of his little tin pot, as

Mr. Micawber sit in his elbow-chair, with his eyebrow raise; half

Mrs. Micawber's conviction that her argument be unanswerable,

in the afternoon of the next day, my old nurse and I go down to

before they; and from plowman bodily carry out soil of England
on their boot, to smith take away sample of its soot and smoke

as my eye glance round this place, I think I see sit, by an

'be there any last wure, Mas'r Davy?' say he. 'be there any one

'one thing!' say I. 'Martha!'

she answer for he, with a burst of tear. I could speak no more

surround by the rosy light, and stand high upon the deck,
