CHAPTER 58
ABSENCE
it be a long and gloomy night that gather on I, haunt by the
ghost of many hope, of many dear remembrance, many error, many
unavaile sorrow and regret.
I go away from England; not know, even then, how great the
shock be, that I have to bear. I leave all who be dear to I, and
go away; and believe that I have bear it, and it be past. as
a man upon a field of battle will receive a mortal hurt, and
scarcely know that he be strike, so I, when I be leave alone with
my undisciplined heart, have no conception of the wound with which
it have to strive.
the knowledge come upon I, not quickly, but little by little, and
grain by grain. the desolate feeling with which I go abroad,
deepen and widen hourly. at first it be a heavy sense of loss
and sorrow, wherein I could distinguish little else. by
imperceptible degree, it become a hopeless consciousness of all
that I have lose - love, friendship, interest; of all that have be
shatter - my first trust, my first affection, the whole airy
castle of my life; of all that remain - a ruin blank and waste,
lie wide around I, unbroken, to the dark horizon.
if my grief be selfish, I do not know it to be so. I mourn
for my child-wife, take from her bloom world, so young. I
mourn for he who might have win the love and admiration of
thousand, as he have win mine long ago. I mourn for the break
heart that have find rest in the stormy sea; and for the wandering
remnant of the simple home, where I have hear the night-wind
blowing, when I be a child.
from the accumulate sadness into which I fall, I have at length no
hope of ever issue again. I roam from place to place, carry
my burden with I everywhere. I feel its whole weight now; and I
droop beneath it, and I say in my heart that it could never be
lightened.
when this despondency be at its bad, I believe that I should
die. sometimes, I think that I would like to die at home; and
actually turn back on my road, that I might get there soon. at
other time, I pass on far away, -from city to city, seek
I know not what, and try to leave I know not what behind.
it be not in my power to retrace, one by one, all the weary phase
of distress of mind through which I pass. there be some dream
that can only be imperfectly and vaguely describe; and when I
oblige myself to look back on this time of my life, I seem to be
recall such a dream. I see myself pass on among the
novelty of foreign town, palace, cathedral, temple, picture,
castle, tomb, fantastic street - the old abide place of
history and Fancy - as a dreamer might; bear my painful load
through all, and hardly conscious of the object as they fade
before I. Listlessness to everything, but brood sorrow, be
the night that fall on my undisciplined heart. let I look up from
it - as at last I do, thank Heaven! - and from its long, sad,
wretched dream, to dawn.
for many month I travel with this ever-darken cloud upon my
mind. some blind reason that I have for not return home -
reason then struggle within I, vainly, for more distinct
expression - keep I on my pilgrimage. sometimes, I have proceed
restlessly from place to place, stop nowhere; sometimes, I have
linger long in one spot. I have have no purpose, no sustain
soul within I, anywhere.
I be in Switzerland. I have come out of Italy, over one of the
great pass of the Alps, and have since wander with a guide among
the by-way of the mountain. if those awful solitude have speak
to my heart, I do not know it. I have find sublimity and wonder
in the dread height and precipice, in the roar torrent, and
the waste of ice and snow; but as yet, they have teach I nothing
else.
I come, one evening before sunset, down into a valley, where I be
to rest. in the course of my descent to it, by the wind track
along the mountain-side, from which I see it shin far below, I
think some long-unwonted sense of beauty and tranquillity, some
soften influence awaken by its peace, move faintly in my
breast. I remember pause once, with a kind of sorrow that be
not all oppressive, not quite despairing. I remember almost hop
that some well change be possible within I.
I come into the valley, as the evening sun be shin on the
remote height of snow, that close it in, like eternal cloud.
the basis of the mountain form the gorge in which the little
village lay, be richly green; and high above this gentler
vegetation, grow forest of dark fir, cleave the wintry
snow-drift, wedge-like, and stem the avalanche. above these,
be range upon range of craggy steep, grey rock, bright ice, and
smooth verdure-speck of pasture, all gradually blend with the
crowning snow. dot here and there on the mountain's-side, each
tiny dot a home, be lonely wooden cottage, so dwarf by the
tower height that they appear too small for toy. so do
even the cluster village in the valley, with its wooden bridge
across the stream, where the stream tumble over break rock, and
roar away among the tree. in the quiet air, there be a sound
of distant singing - shepherd voice; but, as one bright evening
cloud float midway along the mountain's-side, I could almost have
believe it come from there, and be not earthly music. all at
once, in this serenity, great Nature speak to I; and soothe I to
lay down my weary head upon the grass, and weep as I have not weep
yet, since Dora die!
I have find a packet of letter await I but a few minute
before, and have stroll out of the village to read they while my
supper be make ready. other packet have miss I, and I have
receive none for a long time. beyond a line or two, to say that
I be well, and have arrive at such a place, I have not have
fortitude or constancy to write a letter since I leave home.
the packet be in my hand. I open it, and read the writing of
Agnes.
she be happy and useful, be prosper as she have hop. that
be all she tell I of herself. the rest refer to I.
she give I no advice; she urge no duty on I; she only tell I,
in her own fervent manner, what her trust in I be. she know (she
say) how such a nature as mine would turn affliction to good. she
know how trial and emotion would exalt and strengthen it. she be
sure that in my every purpose I should gain a firm and a high
tendency, through the grief I have undergo. she, who so glory
in my fame, and so look forward to its augmentation, well know
that I would labour on. she know that in I, sorrow could not be
weakness, but must be strength. as the endurance of my childish
day have do its part to make I what I be, so great calamity
would nerve I on, to be yet well than I be; and so, as they have
teach I, would I teach other. she commend I to God, who have
take my innocent darling to his rest; and in her sisterly
affection cherish I always, and be always at my side go where
I would; proud of what I have do, but infinitely prouder yet of
what I be reserve to do.
I put the letter in my breast, and think what have I be an hour
ago! when I hear the voice die away, and see the quiet evening
cloud grow dim, and all the colour in the valley fade, and the
golden snow upon the mountain-top become a remote part of the pale
night sky, yet feel that the night be pass from my mind, and
all its shadow clearing, there be no name for the love I bear
she, dearer to I, henceforward, than ever until then.
I read her letter many time. I write to she before I sleep. I
tell she that I have be in sore need of her help; that without she
I be not, and I never have be, what she think I; but that she
inspire I to be that, and I would try.
I do try. in three month more, a year would have pass since
the beginning of my sorrow. I determine to make no resolution
until the expiration of those three month, but to try. I live in
that valley, and its neighbourhood, all the time.
the three month go, I resolve to remain away from home for some
time long; to settle myself for the present in Switzerland, which
be grow dear to I in the remembrance of that evening; to
resume my pen; to work.
I resort humbly whither Agnes have commend I; I seek out
nature, never seek in vain; and I admit to my breast the human
interest I have lately shrink from. it be not long, before I have
almost as many friend in the valley as in Yarmouth: and when I
leave it, before the winter set in, for Geneva, and come back in the
spring, their cordial greeting have a homely sound to I, although
they be not convey in english word.
I work early and late, patiently and hard. I write a Story, with
a purpose grow, not remotely, out of my experience, and send it
to traddle, and he arrange for its publication very
advantageously for I; and the tiding of my grow reputation
begin to reach I from traveller whom I encounter by chance.
after some rest and change, I fall to work, in my old ardent way,
on a new fancy, which take strong possession of I. as I advance
in the execution of this task, I feel it more and more, and rouse
my utmost energy to do it well. this be my third work of
fiction. it be not half write, when, in an interval of rest, I
think of return home.
for a long time, though study and work patiently, I have
accustom myself to robust exercise. my health, severely impair
when I leave England, be quite restore. I have see much. I have
be in many country, and I hope I have improve my store of
knowledge.
I have now recall all that I think it needful to recall here, of
this term of absence - with one reservation. I have make it, thus
far, with no purpose of suppress any of my thought; for, as I
have elsewhere say, this narrative be my write memory. I have
desire to keep the most secret current of my mind apart, and to
the last. I enter on it now. I cannot so completely penetrate the
mystery of my own heart, as to know when I begin to think that I
might have set its early and bright hope on Agnes. I cannot
say at what stage of my grief it first become associate with the
reflection, that, in my wayward boyhood, I have throw away the
treasure of her love. I believe I may have hear some whisper of
that distant thought, in the old unhappy loss or want of something
never to be realize, of which I have be sensible. but the
thought come into my mind as a new reproach and new regret, when I
be leave so sad and lonely in the world.
if, at that time, I have be much with she, I should, in the
weakness of my desolation, have betray this. it be what I
remotely dread when I be first impel to stay away from
England. I could not have bear to lose the small portion of
her sisterly affection; yet, in that betrayal, I should have set a
constraint between we hitherto unknown.
I could not forget that the feeling with which she now regard I
have grow up in my own free choice and course. that if she have
ever love I with another love - and I sometimes think the time
be when she might have do so - I have cast it away. it be
nothing, now, that I have accustom myself to think of she, when we
be both mere child, as one who be far remove from my wild
fancy. I have bestow my passionate tenderness upon another
object; and what I might have do, I have not do; and what Agnes
be to I, I and her own noble heart have make she.
in the beginning of the change that gradually work in I, when I
try to get a well understanding of myself and be a well man,
I do glance, through some indefinite probation, to a period when
I might possibly hope to cancel the mistaken past, and to be so
bless as to marry she. but, as time wear on, this shadowy
prospect fade, and depart from I. if she have ever love I,
then, I should hold she the more sacred; remember the
confidence I have repose in she, her knowledge of my errant heart,
the sacrifice she must have make to be my friend and sister, and
the victory she have win. if she have never love I, could I
believe that she would love I now?
I have always feel my weakness, in comparison with her constancy and
fortitude; and now I feel it more and more. whatever I might have
be to she, or she to I, if I have be more worthy of her long
ago, I be not now, and she be not. the time be past. I have let
it go by, and have deservedly lose she.
that I suffer much in these contention, that they fill I with
unhappiness and remorse, and yet that I have a sustaining sense that
it be require of I, in right and honour, to keep away from
myself, with shame, the thought of turn to the dear girl in the
withering of my hope, from whom I have frivolously turn when they
be bright and fresh - which consideration be at the root of
every thought I have concern she - be all equally true. I make
no effort to conceal from myself, now, that I love she, that I be
devoted to she; but I bring the assurance home to myself, that it
be now too late, and that our long-subsist relation must be
undisturbed.
I have think, much and often, of my Dora's shadow out to I
what might have happen, in those year that be destine not to
try we; I have consider how the thing that never happen, be
often as much reality to we, in their effect, as those that be
accomplish. the very year she speak of, be reality now, for
my correction; and would have be, one day, a little later
perhaps, though we have part in our early folly. I endeavour
to convert what might have be between myself and Agnes, into a
mean of make I more self-deny, more resolved, more conscious
of myself, and my defect and error. thus, through the reflection
that it might have be, I arrive at the conviction that it could
never be.
these, with their perplexity and inconsistency, be the
shifting quicksand of my mind, from the time of my departure to
the time of my return home, three year afterwards. three year
have elapse since the sailing of the emigrant ship; when, at that
same hour of sunset, and in the same place, I stand on the deck of
the packet vessel that bring I home, look on the rosy water
where I have see the image of that ship reflect.
three year. long in the aggregate, though short as they go by.
and home be very dear to I, and Agnes too - but she be not mine
- she be never to be mine. she might have be, but that be
past!
CHAPTER 59
RETURN
I land in London on a wintry autumn evening. it be dark and
raining, and I see more fog and mud in a minute than I have see in
a year. I walk from the Custom House to the Monument before I
find a coach; and although the very house-front, look on the
swollen gutter, be like old friend to I, I could not but admit
that they be very dingy friend.
I have often remark - I suppose everybody have - that onebe go
away from a familiar place, would seem to be the signal for change
in it. as I look out of the coach window, and observe that an
old house on Fish-street Hill, which have stand untouched by
painter, carpenter, or bricklayer, for a century, have be pull
down in my absence; and that a neighbouring street, of
time-honour insalubrity and inconvenience, be be drain and
widen; I half expect to find St. Paul's Cathedral look
old.
for some change in the fortune of my friend, I be prepare. my
aunt have long be re-establish at Dover, and traddle have begin

tell I, in his last letter, that he be not without hope of
be soon unite to the dear girl in the world.
they expect I home before Christmas; but have no idea of my

the well-know shop, however, with their cheerful light, do

that be natural.
'do you know where Mr. Traddles live in the Inn?' I ask the

'Holborn Court, sir. number two.'
'Mr. Traddles have a rise reputation among the lawyer, I

'well, sir,' return the waiter, 'probably he have, sir; but I be

this waiter, who be middle-aged and spare, look for help to a
waiter of more authority - a stout, potential old man, with a

I feel I be in England again, and really be quite cast down on

I come down again to my dinner; and even the slow comfort of the

I have see nothing like this since I go away, and it quite dash

little Mr. Chillip the Doctor, to whose good office I be indebte
in the very first chapter of this history, sit read a newspaper
in the shadow of an opposite corner. he be tolerably stricken in
year by this time; but, be a mild, meek, calm little man, have
wear so easily, that I think he look at that moment just as he
might have look when he sit in our parlour, wait for I to be
bear.
Mr. Chillip have leave Blunderstone six or seven year ago, and I have
never see he since. he sit placidly peruse the newspaper, with
his little head on one side, and a glass of warm sherry negus at
his elbow. he be so extremely conciliatory in his manner that he
seem to apologize to the very newspaper for take the liberty of
read it.
I walk up to where he be sit, and say, 'how do you do, Mr.
Chillip?'
he be greatly flutter by this unexpected address from a
stranger, and reply, in his slow way, 'I thank you, sir, you be
very good. thank you, sir. I hope you be well.'
'you donot remember I?' say I.
'well, sir,' return Mr. Chillip, smile very meekly, and shake
his head as he survey I, 'I have a kind of an impression that
something in your countenance be familiar to I, sir; but I
couldnot lay my hand upon your name, really.'
'and yet you know it, long before I know it myself,' I return.
'do I indeed, sir?' say Mr. Chillip. 'be it possible that I have
the honour, sir, of officiate when -?'
'yes,' say I.
'dear I!' cry Mr. Chillip. 'but no doubt you be a good deal
change since then, sir?'
'probably,' say I.
'well, sir,' observe Mr. Chillip, 'I hope youwill excuse I, if I
be compel to ask the favour of your name?'
on my tell he my name, he be really move. he quite shake
hand with I - which be a violent proceeding for he, his usual
course be to slide a tepid little fish-slice, an inch or two in
advance of his hip, and evince the great discomposure when
anybody grapple with it. even now, he put his hand in his
coat-pocket as soon as he could disengage it, and seem relieve
when he have get it safe back.
'dear I, sir!' say Mr. Chillip, survey I with his head on one
side. 'and itbe Mr. Copperfield, be it? well, sir, I think I
should have know you, if I have take the liberty of look more
closely at you. therebe a strong resemblance between you and your
poor father, sir.'
'I never have the happiness of see my father,' I observe.
'very true, sir,' say Mr. Chillip, in a soothe tone. 'and very
much to be deplore it be, on all account! we be not ignorant,
sir,' say Mr. Chillip, slowly shake his little head again, 'down
in our part of the country, of your fame. there must be great
excitement here, sir,' say Mr. Chillip, tap himself on the
forehead with his forefinger. 'you must find it a try
occupation, sir!'
'what be your part of the country now?' I ask, seat myself
near he.
'I be establish within a few mile of Bury St. Edmund's, sir,'
say Mr. Chillip. 'Mrs. Chillip, come into a little property in
that neighbourhood, under her father's will, I buy a practice
down there, in which you will be glad to hear I be do well. my
daughter be grow quite a tall lass now, sir,' say Mr. Chillip,
give his little head another little shake. 'her mother let down
two tuck in her frock only last week. such be time, you see,
sir!'
as the little man put his now empty glass to his lip, when he make
this reflection, I propose to he to have it refill, and I would
keep he company with another. 'well, sir,' he return, in his
slow way, 'itbe more than I be accustom to; but I cannot deny
myself the pleasure of your conversation. it seem but yesterday
that I have the honour of attend you in the measle. you come
through they charmingly, sir!'
I acknowledge this compliment, and order the negus, which be
soon produce. 'quite an uncommon dissipation!' say Mr. Chillip,
stir it, 'but I cannot resist so extraordinary an occasion. you
have no family, sir?'
I shake my head.
'I be aware that you sustain a bereavement, sir, some time ago,'
say Mr. Chillip. 'I hear it from your father-in-law's sister.
very decide character there, sir?'
'why, yes,' say I, 'decide enough. where do you see she, Mr.
Chillip?'
'be you not aware, sir,' return Mr. Chillip, with his placid
smile, 'that your father-in-law be again a neighbour of mine?'
'no,' say I.
'he be indeed, sir!' say Mr. Chillip. 'marry a young lady of
that part, with a very good little property, poor thing. - and
this action of the brain now, sir? donot you find it fatigue you?'
say Mr. Chillip, look at I like an admire Robin.
I waive that question, and return to the Murdstones. 'I be
aware of his be marry again. do you attend the family?' I
ask.
'not regularly. I have be call in,' he reply. 'strong
phrenological development of the organ of firmness, in Mr.
Murdstone and his sister, sir.'
I reply with such an expressive look, that Mr. Chillip be
embolden by that, and the negus together, to give his head
several short shake, and thoughtfully exclaim, 'ah, dear I! we
remember old time, Mr. Copperfield!'
'and the brother and sister be pursue their old course, be
they?' say I.
'well, sir,' reply Mr. Chillip, 'a medical man, be so much in
family, ought to have neither eye nor ear for anything but his
profession. still, I must say, they be very severe, sir: both as
to this life and the next.'
'the next will be regulate without much reference to they, I dare
say,' I return: 'what be they do as to this?'
Mr. Chillip shake his head, stir his negus, and sip it.
'she be a charming woman, sir!' he observe in a plaintive manner.
'the present Mrs. Murdstone?'
a charming woman indeed, sir,' say Mr. Chillip; 'as amiable, I be
sure, as it be possible to be! Mrs. Chillip's opinion be, that her
spirit have be entirely break since her marriage, and that she be
all but melancholy mad. and the lady,' observe Mr. Chillip,
timorously, 'be great observer, sir.'
'I suppose she be to be subdue and break to their detestable
mould, Heaven help she!' say I. 'and she have be.'
'well, sir, there be violent quarrel at first, I assure you,'
say Mr. Chillip; 'but she be quite a shadow now. would it be
consider forward if I be to say to you, sir, in confidence, that
since the sister come to help, the brother and sister between they
have nearly reduce she to a state of imbecility?'
I tell he I could easily believe it.
'I have no hesitation in say,' say Mr. Chillip, fortify
himself with another sip of negus, 'between you and I, sir, that
her mother die of it - or that tyranny, gloom, and worry have make
Mrs. Murdstone nearly imbecile. she be a lively young woman, sir,
before marriage, and their gloom and austerity destroy she. they
go about with she, now, more like her keeper than her husband and
sister-in-law. that be Mrs. Chillip's remark to I, only last
week. and I assure you, sir, the lady be great observer. Mrs.
Chillip herself be a great observer!'
'do he gloomily profess to be (I be ashamed to use the word in
such association) religious still?' I inquire.
'you anticipate, sir,' say Mr. Chillip, his eyelid get quite
red with the unwonted stimulus in which he be indulge. 'one of
Mrs. Chillip's most impressive remark. Mrs. Chillip,' he
proceed, in the calm and slow manner, 'quite electrify
I, by point out that Mr. Murdstone set up an image of himself,
and call it the Divine Nature. you might have knock I down on
the flat of my back, sir, with the feather of a pen, I assure you,
when Mrs. Chillip say so. the lady be great observer, sir?'
'intuitively,' say I, to his extreme delight.
'I be very happy to receive such support in my opinion, sir,' he
rejoin. 'it be not often that I venture to give a non-medical
opinion, I assure you. Mr. Murdstone deliver public address
sometimes, and it be say, - in short, sir, it be say by Mrs.
Chillip, - that the dark tyrant he have lately be, the more
ferocious be his doctrine.'
'I believe Mrs. Chillip to be perfectly right,' say I.
'Mrs. Chillip do go so far as to say,' pursue the meek of
little man, much encouraged, 'that what such people miscall their
religion, be a vent for their bad humour and arrogance. and do
you know I must say, sir,' he continue, mildly lay his head on
one side, 'that I don't find authority for Mr. and Miss Murdstone
in the New Testament?'
'I never find it either!' say I.
'in the meantime, sir,' say Mr. Chillip, 'they be much dislike;
and as they be very free in consign everybody who dislike they
to perdition, we really have a good deal of perdition go on in
our neighbourhood! however, as Mrs. Chillip say, sir, they undergo
a continual punishment; for they be turn inward, to feed upon
their own heart, and their own heart be very bad feeding. now,
sir, about that brain of your, if youwill excuse my return to
it. donot you expose it to a good deal of excitement, sir?'
I find it not difficult, in the excitement of Mr. Chillip's own
brain, under his potation of negus, to divert his attention from
this topic to his own affair, on which, for the next half-hour, he
be quite loquacious; give I to understand, among other piece
of information, that he be then at the Gray's Inn Coffee-house to
lay his professional evidence before a Commission of Lunacy,
touch the state of mind of a patient who have become derange
from excessive drinking.
'and I assure you, sir,' he say, 'I be extremely nervous on such
occasion. I could not support be what be call Bullied, sir.
it would quite unman I. do you know it be some time before I
recover the conduct of that alarming lady, on the night of your
birth, Mr. Copperfield?'
I tell he that I be go down to my aunt, the Dragon of that
night, early in the morning; and that she be one of the most
tender-hearted and excellent of woman, as he would know full well
if he know she well. the mere notion of the possibility of his
ever see she again, appear to terrify he. he reply with a
small pale smile, 'be she so, indeed, sir? really?' and almost
immediately call for a candle, and go to bed, as if he be not
quite safe anywhere else. he do not actually stagger under the
negus; but I should think his placid little pulse must have make
two or three more beat in a minute, than it have do since the
great night of my aunt's disappointment, when she strike at he
with her bonnet.
thoroughly tired, I go to bed too, at midnight; pass the next
day on the Dover coach; burst safe and sound into my aunt's old
parlour while she be at tea (she wear spectacle now); and be
receive by she, and Mr. Dick, and dear old Peggotty, who act as
housekeeper, with open arm and tear of joy. my aunt be mightily
amuse, when we begin to talk composedly, by my account of my
meeting with Mr. Chillip, and of his hold she in such dread
remembrance; and both she and Peggotty have a great deal to say
about my poor mother's second husband, and 'that murder woman of
a sister', - on whom I think no pain or penalty would have induce
my aunt to bestow any christian or Proper name, or any other
designation.
CHAPTER 60
AGNES
my aunt and I, when we be leave alone, talk far into the night.
how the emigrant never write home, otherwise than cheerfully and
hopefully; how Mr. Micawber have actually remit diver small sum
of money, on account of those 'pecuniary liability', in reference
to which he have be so business-like as between man and man; how
Janet, return into my aunt's service when she come back to
Dover, have finally carry out her renunciation of mankind by
enter into wedlock with a thriving tavern-keeper; and how my
aunt have finally set her seal on the same great principle, by
aid and abet the bride, and crown the marriage-ceremony
with her presence; be among our topic - already more or less
familiar to I through the letter I have have. Mr. Dick, as usual,
be not forget. my aunt inform I how he incessantly occupy
himself in copy everything he could lay his hand on, and keep
King Charles the First at a respectful distance by that semblance
of employment; how it be one of the main joy and reward of her
life that he be free and happy, instead of pin in monotonous
restraint; and how (as a novel general conclusion) nobody but she
could ever fully know what he be.
'and when, Trot,' say my aunt, pat the back of my hand, as we
sit in our old way before the fire, 'when be you go over to
Canterbury?'
'I shall get a horse, and ride over tomorrow morning, aunt, unless
you will go with I?'
'no!' say my aunt, in her short abrupt way. 'I mean to stay where
I be.'
then, I should ride, I say. I could not have come through
Canterbury today without stop, if I have be come to anyone
but she.
she be pleased, but answer, 'Tut, Trot; my old bone would have
keep till tomorrow!' and softly pat my hand again, as I sit
look thoughtfully at the fire.
thoughtfully, for I could not be here once more, and so near Agnes,
without the revival of those regret with which I have so long be
occupy. soften regret they might be, teach I what I have
fail to learn when my young life be all before I, but not the
less regret. 'oh, Trot,' I seem to hear my aunt say once more;
and I understand she well now - 'blind, blind, blind!'
we both keep silence for some minute. when I raise my eye, I
find that she be steadily observant of I. perhaps she have
follow the current of my mind; for it seem to I an easy one to
track now, wilful as it have be once.
'you will find her father a white-haired old man,' say my aunt,
'though a well man in all other respect - a reclaim man.
neither will you find he measure all human interest, and joy,
and sorrow, with his one poor little inch-rule now. trust I,
child, such thing must shrink very much, before they can be
measure off in that way.'
'indeed they must,' say I.
'you will find she,' pursue my aunt, 'as good, as beautiful, as
earnest, as disintereste, as she have always be. if I know
high praise, Trot, I would bestow it on she.'
there be no high praise for she; no high reproach for I. oh,
how have I stray so far away!
'if she train the young girl whom she have about she, to be like
herself,' say my aunt, earnest even to the filling of her eye
with tear, 'Heaven know, her life will be well employ! useful
and happy, as she say that day! how could she be otherwise than
useful and happy!'
'have Agnes any -' I be think aloud, rather than speak.
'well? hey? any what?' say my aunt, sharply.
'any lover,' say I.
'a score,' cry my aunt, with a kind of indignant pride. 'she
might have marry twenty time, my dear, since you have be
go!'
'no doubt,' say I. 'no doubt. but have she any lover who be
worthy of she? Agnes could care for no other.'
my aunt sit musing for a little while, with her chin upon her hand.
slowly raise her eye to mine, she say:
'I suspect she have an attachment, Trot.'
'a prosperous one?' say I.
'trot,' return my aunt gravely, 'I cannot say. I have no right to
tell you even so much. she have never confide it to I, but I
suspect it.'
she look so attentively and anxiously at I (I even see her
tremble), that I feel now, more than ever, that she have follow my
late thought. I summon all the resolution I have make, in all
those many day and night, and all those many conflict of my
heart.
'if it should be so,' I begin, 'and I hope it is-'
'I donot know that it be,' say my aunt curtly. 'you must not be
rule by my suspicion. you must keep they secret. they be very
slight, perhaps. I have no right to speak.'
'if it should be so,' I repeat, 'Agnes will tell I at her own
good time. a sister to whom I have confide so much, aunt, will
not be reluctant to confide in I.'
my aunt withdraw her eye from mine, as slowly as she have turn
they upon I; and cover they thoughtfully with her hand. by and
by she put her other hand on my shoulder; and so we both sit,
look into the past, without say another word, until we part
for the night.
I ride away, early in the morning, for the scene of my old
school-day. I cannot say that I be yet quite happy, in the hope
that I be gain a victory over myself; even in the prospect of
so soon look on her face again.
the well-remember ground be soon traverse, and I come into the
quiet street, where every stone be a boy's book to I. I go on
foot to the old house, and go away with a heart too full to
enter. I return; and look, as I pass, through the low
window of the turret-room where first Uriah Heep, and afterwards
Mr. Micawber, have be willnot to sit, see that it be a little
parlour now, and that there be no office. otherwise the staid old
house be, as to its cleanliness and order, still just as it have
be when I first see it. I request the new maid who admit
I, to tell Miss Wickfield that a gentleman who wait on she from
a friend abroad, be there; and I be show up the grave old
staircase (caution of the step I know so well), into the
unchanged drawing-room. the book that Agnes and I have read
together, be on their shelf; and the desk where I have labour
at my lesson, many a night, stand yet at the same old corner of
the table. all the little change that have creep in when the Heeps
be there, be change again. everything be as it use to be,
in the happy time.
I stand in a window, and look across the ancient street at the
opposite house, recall how I have watch they on wet
afternoon, when I first come there; and how I have use to
speculate about the people who appear at any of the window, and
have follow they with my eye up and down stair, while woman go
click along the pavement in patten, and the dull rain fall in
slanting line, and pour out of the water-spout yonder, and
flow into the road. the feeling with which I use to watch the
tramp, as they come into the town on those wet evening, at dusk,
and limp past, with their bundle droop over their shoulder
at the end of stick, come freshly back to I; fraught, as then,
with the smell of damp earth, and wet leaf and briar, and the
sensation of the very air that blow upon I in my own toilsome
journey.
the opening of the little door in the panel wall make I start
and turn. her beautiful serene eye meet mine as she come towards
I. she stop and lay her hand upon her bosom, and I catch she
in my arm.
'Agnes! my dear girl! I have come too suddenly upon you.'
'no, no! I be so rejoice to see you, Trotwood!'
'Dear Agnes, the happiness it be to I, to see you once again!'
I fold she to my heart, and, for a little while, we be both
silent. presently we sit down, side by side; and her angel-face
be turn upon I with the welcome I have dream of, wake and
sleeping, for whole year.
she be so true, she be so beautiful, she be so good, - I owe
she so much gratitude, she be so dear to I, that I could find no
utterance for what I feel. I try to bless she, try to thank
she, try to tell she (as I have often do in letter) what an
influence she have upon I; but all my effort be in vain. my
love and joy be dumb.
with her own sweet tranquillity, she calm my agitation; lead I
back to the time of our parting; speak to I of Emily, whom she have
visit, in secret, many time; speak to I tenderly of Dora's
grave. with the unerring instinct of her noble heart, she touch
the chord of my memory so softly and harmoniously, that not one
jar within I; I could listen to the sorrowful, distant music,
and desire to shrink from nothing it awake. how could I, when,
blend with it all, be her dear self, the well angel of my
life?
'and you, Agnes,' I say, by and by. 'tell I of yourself. you
have hardly ever tell I of your own life, in all this lapse of
time!'
'what should I tell?' she answer, with her radiant smile. 'papa
be well. you see we here, quiet in our own home; our anxiety set
at rest, our home restore to we; and know that, dear Trotwood,
you know all.'
'all, Agnes?' say I.
she look at I, with some flutter wonder in her face.
'be there nothing else, Sister?' I say.
her colour, which have just now fade, return, and fade again.
she smile; with a quiet sadness, I think; and shake her head.
I have seek to lead she to what my aunt have hint at; for,
sharply painful to I as it must be to receive that confidence, I
be to discipline my heart, and do my duty to she. I see, however,
that she be uneasy, and I let it pass.
'you have much to do, dear Agnes?'
'with my school?' say she, look up again, in all her bright
composure.
'yes. it be laborious, be it not?'
'the labour be so pleasant,' she return, 'that it be scarcely
grateful in I to call it by that name.'
'nothing good be difficult to you,' say I.
her colour come and go once more; and once more, as she bent she
head, I see the same sad smile.
'you will wait and see papa,' say Agnes, cheerfully, 'and pass the
day with we? perhaps you will sleep in your own room? we always
call it yours.'
I could not do that, have promise to ride back to my aunt's at
night; but I would pass the day there, joyfully.
'I must be a prisoner for a little while,' say Agnes, 'but here
be the old book, Trotwood, and the old music.'
'even the old flower be here,' say I, look round; 'or the old
kind.'
'I have find a pleasure,' return Agnes, smile, 'while you have
be absent, in keep everything as it use to be when we be
child. for we be very happy then, I think.'
'Heaven know we be!' say I.
'and every little thing that have remind I of my brother,' say
Agnes, with her cordial eye turn cheerfully upon I, 'have be
a welcome companion. even this,' show I the basket-trifle,
full of key, still hang at her side, 'seem to jingle a kind of
old tune!'
she smile again, and go out at the door by which she have come.
it be for I to guard this sisterly affection with religious care.
it be all that I have leave myself, and it be a treasure. if I
once shake the foundation of the sacred confidence and usage, in
virtue of which it be give to I, it be lose, and could never be
recover. I set this steadily before myself. the well I love
she, the more it behove I never to forget it.
I walk through the street; and, once more see my old
adversary the butcher - now a constable, with his staff hang up
in the shop - go down to look at the place where I have fight
he; and there meditate on Miss Shepherd and the eld Miss
Larkins, and all the idle love and liking, and disliking, of
that time. nothing seem to have survive that time but Agnes;
and she, ever a star above I, be bright and high.
when I return, Mr. Wickfield have come home, from a garden he have,
a couple of mile or so out of town, where he now employ himself
almost every day. I find he as my aunt have describe he. we
sit down to dinner, with some half-dozen little girl; and he
seem but the shadow of his handsome picture on the wall.
the tranquillity and peace belong, of old, to that quiet ground
in my memory, pervade it again. when dinner be do, Mr.
Wickfield take no wine, and I desire none, we go up-stair;
where Agnes and her little charge sing and play, and work.
after tea the child leave we; and we three sit together, talk
of the bygone day.
'my part in they,' say Mr. Wickfield, shake his white head, 'have
much matter for regret - for deep regret, and deep contrition,
Trotwood, you well know. but I would not cancel it, if it be in
my power.'
I could readily believe that, look at the face beside he.
'I should cancel with it,' he pursue, 'such patience and devotion,
such fidelity, such a child's love, as I must not forget, no! even
to forget myself.'
'I understand you, sir,' I softly say. 'I hold it - I have always
hold it - in veneration.'
'but no one know, not even you,' he return, 'how much she have
do, how much she have undergo, how hard she have strive. dear
Agnes!'
she have put her hand entreatingly on his arm, to stop he; and be
very, very pale.
'well, well!' he say with a sigh, dismissing, as I then see, some
trial she have bear, or be yet to bear, in connexion with what my
aunt have tell I. 'well! I have never tell you, Trotwood, of her
mother. have anyone?'
'never, sir.'
'itbe not much - though it be much to suffer. she marry I in
opposition to her father's wish, and he renounce she. she pray
he to forgive she, before my Agnes come into this world. he be
a very hard man, and her mother have long be dead. he repulse
she. he break her heart.'
Agnes lean upon his shoulder, and steal her arm about his neck.
'she have an affectionate and gentle heart,' he say; 'and it be
break. I know its tender nature very well. no one could, if I
do not. she love I dearly, but be never happy. she be always
labour, in secret, under this distress; and be delicate and
downcast at the time of his last repulse - for it be not the
first, by many - pined away and die. she leave I Agnes, two week
old; and the grey hair that you recollect I with, when you first
come.' he kiss Agnes on her cheek.
'my love for my dear child be a diseased love, but my mind be all
unhealthy then. I say no more of that. I be not speak of
myself, Trotwood, but of her mother, and of she. if I give you any
clue to what I be, or to what I have be, you will unravel it, I
know. what Agnes be, I need not say. I have always read something
of her poor mother's story, in her character; and so I tell it you
tonight, when we three be again together, after such great
change. I have tell it all.'
his bowed head, and her angel-face and filial duty, derive a more
pathetic meaning from it than they have have before. if I have want
anything by which to mark this night of our re-union, I should have
find it in this.
Agnes rise up from her father's side, before long; and go softly
to her piano, play some of the old air to which we have often
listen in that place.
'have you any intention of go away again?' Agnes ask I, as I
be stand by.
'what do my sister say to that?'
'I hope not.'
'then I have no such intention, Agnes.'
'I think you ought not, Trotwood, since you ask I,' she say,
mildly. 'your grow reputation and success enlarge your power of
do good; and if I could spare my brother,' with her eye upon
I, 'perhaps the time could not.'
'what I be, you have make I, Agnes. you should know well.'
'I make you, trotwood?'
'yes! Agnes, my dear girl!' I say, bend over she. 'I try to
tell you, when we meet today, something that have be in my thought
since Dora die. you remember, when you come down to I in our
little room - point upward, Agnes?'
'oh, Trotwood!' she return, her eye fill with tear. 'so
loving, so confiding, and so young! can I ever forget?'
'as you be then, my sister, I have often think since, you have
ever be to I. ever point upward, Agnes; ever lead I to
something well; ever direct I to high thing!'
she only shake her head; through her tear I see the same sad quiet
smile.
'and I be so grateful to you for it, Agnes, so bound to you, that
there be no name for the affection of my heart. I want you to
know, yet donot know how to tell you, that all my life long I shall
look up to you, and be guide by you, as I have be through the
darkness that be past. whatever betide, whatever new tie you may
form, whatever change may come between we, I shall always look to
you, and love you, as I do now, and have always do. you will
always be my solace and resource, as you have always be. until
I die, my dear sister, I shall see you always before I,
point upward!'
she put her hand in mine, and tell I she be proud of I, and of
what I say; although I praise she very far beyond her worth.
then she go on softly play, but without remove her eye from
I.
'do you know, what I have hear tonight, Agnes,' say I, strangely
seem to be a part of the feeling with which I regard you when I
see you first - with which I sit beside you in my rough
school-day?'

a light shines on MY WAY
the year come round to Christmas-time, and I have be at home above

at least once a week, and sometimes oftener, I ride over there, and

unhappy sense be always hover about I now - most sorrowfully
when I leave she - and I be glad to be up and out, rather than

I wear away the long part of many wild sad night, in those

long absence.

thought, I should well express the truth. they speak to I from

place. when I read to Agnes what I write; when I see her listening

could have wish my wife to be.
