to conceive a distant day when I might blamelessly avow it; when
all this should be over; when I could say 'Agnes, so it be when I
come home; and now I be old, and I never have love since!'
she do not once show I any change in herself. what she always
have be to I, she still be; wholly unaltered.
between my aunt and I there have be something, in this connexion,
since the night of my return, which I cannot call a restraint, or
an avoidance of the subject, so much as an imply understanding
that we think of it together, but do not shape our thought into
word. when, accord to our old custom, we sit before the fire
at night, we often fall into this train; as naturally, and as
consciously to each other, as if we have unreservedly say so. but
we preserve an unbroken silence. I believe that she have read, or
partly read, my thought that night; and that she fully
comprehend why I give mine no more distinct expression.
this Christmas-time be come, and Agnes have repose no new
confidence in I, a doubt that have several time arise in my mind
- whether she could have that perception of the true state of my
breast, which restrain she with the apprehension of give I
pain - begin to oppress I heavily. if that be so, my sacrifice
be nothing; my plainest obligation to her unfulfilled; and every
poor action I have shrink from, I be hourly do. I resolve to
set this right beyond all doubt; - if such a barrier be between
we, to break it down at once with a determined hand.
it be - what lasting reason have I to remember it! - a cold,
harsh, winter day. there have be snow, some hour before; and it
lay, not deep, but hard-frozen on the ground. out at sea, beyond
my window, the wind blow ruggedly from the north. I have be
think of it, sweep over those mountain waste of snow in
Switzerland, then inaccessible to any human foot; and have be
speculating which be the lonely, those solitary region, or a
deserted ocean.
'rid today, Trot?' say my aunt, put her head in at the
door.
'yes,' say I, 'I be go over to Canterbury. itbe a good day for
a ride.'
'I hope your horse may think so too,' say my aunt; 'but at present
he be hold down his head and his ear, stand before the door
there, as if he think his stable preferable.'
my aunt, I may observe, allow my horse on the forbidden ground,
but have not at all relent towards the donkey.
'he will be fresh enough, presently!' say I.
'the ride will do his master good, at all event,' observe my
aunt, glance at the paper on my table. 'ah, child, you pass a
good many hour here! I never think, when I use to read book,
what work it be to write they.'
'itbe work enough to read they, sometimes,' I return. 'as to the
writing, it have its own charm, aunt.'
'ah! I see!' say my aunt. 'ambition, love of approbation,
sympathy, and much more, I suppose? well: go along with you!'
'do you know anything more,' say I, stand composedly before she
- she have pat I on the shoulder, and sit down in my chair - 'of
that attachment of Agnes?'
she look up in my face a little while, before reply:
'I think I do, Trot.'
'be you confirm in your impression?' I inquire.
'I think I be, Trot.'
she look so steadfastly at I: with a kind of doubt, or pity, or
suspense in her affection: that I summon the strong
determination to show she a perfectly cheerful face.
'and what be more, Trot -' say my aunt.
'yes!'
'I think Agnes be go to be marry.'
'God bless she!' say I, cheerfully.
'God bless she!' say my aunt, 'and her husband too!'
I echo it, part from my aunt, and go lightly downstairs,
mount, and ride away. there be great reason than before to do
what I have resolve to do.
how well I recollect the wintry ride! the frozen particle of ice,
brush from the blade of grass by the wind, and bear across my
face; the hard clatter of the horse's hoof, beat a tune upon
the ground; the stiff-till soil; the snowdrift, lightly eddy
in the chalk-pit as the breeze ruffle it; the smoking team with
the waggon of old hay, stop to breathe on the hill-top, and
shake their bell musically; the whitened slope and sweep of
down-land lie against the dark sky, as if they be draw on a
huge slate!
I find Agnes alone. the little girl have go to their own home
now, and she be alone by the fire, reading. she put down her book
on see I come in; and have welcome I as usual, take she
work-basket and sit in one of the old-fashioned window.
I sit beside she on the window-seat, and we talk of what I be
do, and when it would be do, and of the progress I have make
since my last visit. Agnes be very cheerful; and laughingly
predict that I should soon become too famous to be talk to, on
such subject.
'so I make the most of the present time, you see,' say Agnes, 'and
talk to you while I may.'
as I look at her beautiful face, observant of her work, she
raise her mild clear eye, and see that I be look at she.
'you be thoughtful today, Trotwood!'
'Agnes, shall I tell you what about? I come to tell you.'
she put aside her work, as she be use to do when we be
seriously discuss anything; and give I her whole attention.
'my dear Agnes, do you doubt my be true to you?'
'no!' she answer, with a look of astonishment.
'do you doubt my be what I always have be to you?'
'no!' she answer, as before.
'do you remember that I try to tell you, when I come home, what
a debt of gratitude I owe you, dear Agnes, and how fervently I
feel towards you?'
'I remember it,' she say, gently, 'very well.'
'you have a secret,' say I. 'let I share it, Agnes.'
she cast down her eye, and tremble.
'I could hardly fail to know, even if I have not hear - but from
other lip than your, Agnes, which seem strange - that there be
someone upon whom you have bestow the treasure of your love. do
not shut I out of what concern your happiness so nearly! if you
can trust I, as you say you can, and as I know you may, let I be
your friend, your brother, in this matter, of all other!'
with an appealing, almost a reproachful, glance, she rise from the
window; and hurry across the room as if without know where,
put her hand before her face, and burst into such tear as smote
I to the heart.
and yet they awaken something in I, bring promise to my
heart. without my knowing why, these tear ally themselves with
the quietly sad smile which be so fixed in my remembrance, and
shake I more with hope than fear or sorrow.
'Agnes! sister! dearest! what have I do?'
'let I go away, Trotwood. I be not well. I be not myself. I
will speak to you by and by - another time. I will write to you.
donot speak to I now. donot! donot!'
I seek to recollect what she have say, when I have speak to she
on that former night, of her affection need no return. it
seem a very world that I must search through in a moment.
'Agnes, I cannot bear to see you so, and think that I have be the
cause. my dear girl, dearer to I than anything in life, if you
be unhappy, let I share your unhappiness. if you be in need of
help or counsel, let I try to give it to you. if you have indeed
a burden on your heart, let I try to lighten it. for whom do I
live now, Agnes, if it be not for you!'
'oh, spare I! I be not myself! another time!' be all I could
distinguish.
be it a selfish error that be lead I away? or, have once
a clue to hope, be there something open to I that I have not
dare to think of?
'I must say more. I cannot let you leave I so! for Heaven's sake,
Agnes, let we not mistake each other after all these year, and all
that have come and go with they! I must speak plainly. if you
have any linger thought that I could envy the happiness you will
confer; that I could not resign you to a dearer protector, of your
own choosing; that I could not, from my removed place, be a
content witness of your joy; dismiss it, for I donot deserve it!
I have not suffer quite in vain. you have not teach I quite in
vain. there be no alloy of self in what I feel for you.'
she be quiet now. in a little time, she turn her pale face
towards I, and say in a low voice, break here and there, but
very clear:
'I owe it to your pure friendship for I, Trotwood - which, indeed,
I do not doubt - to tell you, you be mistaken. I can do no more.
if I have sometimes, in the course of year, want help and
counsel, they have come to I. if I have sometimes be unhappy,
the feeling have pass away. if I have ever have a burden on my
heart, it have be lighten for I. if I have any secret, it be
- no new one; and be - not what you suppose. I cannot reveal it,
or divide it. it have long be mine, and must remain mine.'
'Agnes! stay! a moment!'
she be go away, but I detain she. I clasp my arm about her
waist. 'in the course of year!' 'it be not a new one!' new
thought and hope be whirl through my mind, and all the
colour of my life be change.
'Dearest Agnes! whom I so respect and honour - whom I so devotedly
love! when I come here today, I think that nothing could have
wrest this confession from I. I think I could have keep it in
my bosom all our life, till we be old. but, Agnes, if I have
indeed any new-bear hope that I may ever call you something more
than Sister, widely different from Sister! -'
her tear fall fast; but they be not like those she have lately
shed, and I see my hope brighten in they.
'Agnes! ever my guide, and good support! if you have be more
mindful of yourself, and less of I, when we grow up here together,
I think my heedless fancy never would have wander from you. but
you be so much well than I, so necessary to I in every boyish
hope and disappointment, that to have you to confide in, and rely
upon in everything, become a second nature, supplant for the
time the first and great one of love you as I do!'
still weep, but not sadly - joyfully! and clasp in my arm as
she have never be, as I have think she never be to be!
'when I love Dora - fondly, Agnes, as you know -'
'yes!' she cry, earnestly. 'I be glad to know it!'
'when I love she - even then, my love would have be incomplete,
without your sympathy. I have it, and it be perfect. and when
I lose she, Agnes, what should I have be without you, still!'
close in my arm, near to my heart, her tremble hand upon my
shoulder, her sweet eye shin through her tear, on mine!
'I go away, dear Agnes, love you. I stay away, love you.
I return home, love you!'
and now, I try to tell she of the struggle I have have, and the
conclusion I have come to. I try to lay my mind before she,
truly, and entirely. I try to show she how I have hop I have
come into the well knowledge of myself and of she; how I have
resign myself to what that well knowledge bring; and how I
have come there, even that day, in my fidelity to this. if she do
so love I (I say) that she could take I for her husband, she
could do so, on no deserving of mine, except upon the truth of my
love for she, and the trouble in which it have ripen to be what it
be; and hence it be that I reveal it. and O, Agnes, even out
of thy true eye, in that same time, the spirit of my child-wife
look upon I, say it be well; and win I, through thee,
to tender recollection of the blossom that have wither in its
bloom!
'I be so blest, Trotwood - my heart be so overcharged - but there
be one thing I must say.'
'Dearest, what?'
she lay her gentle hand upon my shoulder, and look calmly in
my face.
'do you know, yet, what it be?'
'I be afraid to speculate on what it be. tell I, my dear.'
'I have love you all my life!'
O, we be happy, we be happy! our tear be not for the trial
(hers so much the great) through which we have come to be thus,
but for the rapture of be thus, never to be divide more!
we walk, that winter evening, in the field together; and the
bless calm within we seem to be partake by the frosty air.
the early star begin to shine while we be linger on, and
look up to they, we thank our GOD for have guide we to this
tranquillity.
we stand together in the same old-fashioned window at night, when
the moon be shin; Agnes with her quiet eye raise up to it; I
follow her glance. long mile of road then open out before my
mind; and, toil on, I see a ragged way-wear boy, forsake and
neglect, who should come to call even the heart now beat
against mine, his own.
it be nearly dinner-time next day when we appear before my aunt.
she be up in my study, Peggotty say: which it be her pride to
keep in readiness and order for I. we find she, in her
spectacle, sit by the fire.
'goodness I!' say my aunt, peer through the dusk, 'whobe this
yoube bring home?'
'Agnes,' say I.
as we have arrange to say nothing at first, my aunt be not a
little discomfited. she dart a hopeful glance at I, when I say
'Agnes'; but see that I look as usual, she take off her
spectacle in despair, and rub her nose with they.
she greet Agnes heartily, nevertheless; and we be soon in the
light parlour downstairs, at dinner. my aunt put on her
spectacle twice or thrice, to take another look at I, but as
often take they off again, disappointed, and rub her nose with
they. much to the discomfiture of Mr. Dick, who know this to be a
bad symptom.
'by the by, aunt,' say I, after dinner; 'I have be speak to
Agnes about what you tell I.'
'then, Trot,' say my aunt, turn scarlet, 'you do wrong, and
break your promise.'
'you be not angry, aunt, I trust? I be sure you willnot be, when
you learn that Agnes be not unhappy in any attachment.'
'stuff and nonsense!' say my aunt.
as my aunt appear to be annoy, I think the good way be to
cut her annoyance short. I take Agnes in my arm to the back of her
chair, and we both lean over she. my aunt, with one clap of her
hand, and one look through her spectacle, immediately go into
hysteric, for the first and only time in all my knowledge of she.
the hysteric call up Peggotty. the moment my aunt be restore,
she fly at Peggotty, and call she a silly old creature, hug
she with all her might. after that, she hug Mr. Dick (who be
highly honour, but a good deal surprised); and after that, tell
they why. then, we be all happy together.
I could not discover whether my aunt, in her last short
conversation with I, have fall on a pious fraud, or have really
mistaken the state of my mind. it be quite enough, she say, that
she have tell I Agnes be go to be marry; and that I now know
well than anyone how true it be.
we be marry within a fortnight. traddle and Sophy, and Doctor
and Mrs. Strong, be the only guest at our quiet wedding. we
leave they full of joy; and drive away together. clasped in my
embrace, I hold the source of every worthy aspiration I have ever
have; the centre of myself, the circle of my life, my own, my wife;
my love of whom be found on a rock!
'Dearest husband!' say Agnes. 'now that I may call you by that
name, I have one thing more to tell you.'
'let I hear it, love.'
'it grow out of the night when Dora die. she send you for I.'
'she do.'
'she tell I that she leave I something. can you think what it
be?'
I believe I could. I draw the wife who have so long love I,
close to my side.
'she tell I that she make a last request to I, and leave I a last
charge.'
'and it be -'
'that only I would occupy this vacant place.'
and Agnes lay her head upon my breast, and weep; and I weep with
she, though we be so happy.
CHAPTER 63
a visitor
what I have purpose to record be nearly finish; but there be yet
an incident conspicuous in my memory, on which it often rest with
delight, and without which one thread in the web I have spin would
have a ravel end.
I have advance in fame and fortune, my domestic joy be perfect, I
have be marry ten happy year. Agnes and I be sit by the
fire, in our house in London, one night in spring, and three of our
child be play in the room, when I be tell that a stranger
wish to see I.
he have be ask if he come on business, and have answer no; he
have come for the pleasure of see I, and have come a long way.
he be an old man, my servant say, and look like a farmer.
as this sound mysterious to the child, and moreover be like
the beginning of a favourite story Agnes use to tell they,
introductory to the arrival of a wicked old Fairy in a cloak who
hat everybody, it produce some commotion. one of our boy lay
his head in his mother's lap to be out of harm's way, and little
Agnes (our eld child) leave her doll in a chair to represent she,
and thrust out her little heap of golden curl from between the
window-curtain, to see what happen next.
'let he come in here!' say I.
there soon appear, pause in the dark doorway as he enter, a
hale, grey-haired old man. Little Agnes, attract by his look,
have run to bring he in, and I have not yet clearly see his face,
when my wife, start up, cry out to I, in a pleased and
agitated voice, that it be Mr. Peggotty!
it be Mr. Peggotty. an old man now, but in a ruddy, hearty,
strong old age. when our first emotion be over, and he sit before
the fire with the child on his knee, and the blaze shin on
his face, he look, to I, as vigorous and robust, withal as
handsome, an old man, as ever I have see.
'Mas'r Davy,' say he. and the old name in the old tone fall so
naturally on my ear! 'Mas'r Davy, 'tis a joyful hour as I see you,
once more, 'long with your own trew wife!'
'a joyful hour indeed, old friend!' cry I.
'and these heer pretty one,' say Mr. Peggotty. 'to look at these
heer flower! why, Mas'r Davy, you be but the heighth of the
littlest of these, when I first see you! when Em'ly warn't no
big, and our poor lad be but a lad!'
'time have change I more than it have change you since then,' say
I. 'but let these dear rogue go to bed; and as no house in
England but this must hold you, tell I where to send for your
luggage (be the old black bag among it, that go so far, I
wonder!), and then, over a glass of Yarmouth grog, we will have the
tiding of ten year!'
'be you alone?' ask Agnes.
'yes, ma'am,' he say, kiss her hand, 'quite alone.'
we sit he between we, not know how to give he welcome enough;
and as I begin to listen to his old familiar voice, I could have
fancy he be still pursue his long journey in search of his
darling niece.
'itbe a mort of water,' say Mr. Peggotty, 'fur to come across, and
on'y stay a matter of fower week. but water ('specially when 'tis
salt) come nat'ral to I; and friend be dear, and I be heer. -
which be verse,' say Mr. Peggotty, surprised to find it out,
'though I havenot such intention.'
'be you go back those many thousand mile, so soon?' ask
Agnes.
'yes, ma'am,' he return. 'I giv the promise to Em'ly, afore I
come away. you see, I doen't grow young as the year come
round, and if I havenot sail as 'twas, most like I shouldnot never
have do 't. and itbe allus be on my mind, as I must come and
see Mas'r Davy and your own sweet bloom self, in your wed
happiness, afore I get to be too old.'
he look at we, as if he could never feast his eye on we
sufficiently. Agnes laughingly put back some scatter lock of
his grey hair, that he might see we well.
'and now tell we,' say I, 'everything relate to your fortune.'
'our fortun, Mas'r Davy,' he rejoin, 'be soon tell. we havenot
fare nohow, but fare to thrive. wehave allus thrive. wehave
work as we ought to 't, and maybe we live a leetle hard at first
or so, but we have allus thrive. what with sheep-farming, and
what with stock-farming, and what with one thing and what with
t'other, we be as well to do, as well could be. Theer's be
kiender a blessing fall upon we,' say Mr. Peggotty, reverentially
incline his head, 'and wehave do nowt but prosper. that is, in
the long run. if not yesterday, why then today. if not today, why
then tomorrow.'
'and emily?' say Agnes and I, both together.
'Em'ly,' say he, 'arter you leave she, ma'am - and I never heerd
her say of her prayer at night, t'other side the canvas screen,
when we be settle in the Bush, but what I heerd your name - and
arter she and I lose sight of Mas'r Davy, that theer shin
sundown - be that low, at first, that, if she have know'd then what
Mas'r Davy kep from we so kind and thowtful, 'tis my opinion shewould
have droop away. but theer be some poor folk aboard as have
illness among them, and she take care of they; and theer be the
child in our company, and she take care of they; and so she get
to be busy, and to be do good, and that help she.'
'when do she first hear of it?' I ask.
'I kep it from her arter I heerd on 't,' say Mr. Peggotty, 'go
on nigh a year. we be live then in a solitary place, but among
the beautifull tree, and with the rose a-cover our Beein to
the roof. theer come along one day, when I be out a-work on
the land, a traveller from our own Norfolk or Suffolk in England (I
doen't rightly mind which), and of course we take he in, and giv
he to eat and drink, and make he welcome. we all do that, all
the colony over. hewould get an old newspaper with he, and some
other account in print of the storm. thatbe how she know'd it.
when I come home at night, I find she know'd it.'
he drop his voice as he say these word, and the gravity I so
well remember overspread his face.
'do it change her much?' we ask.
'Aye, for a good long time,' he say, shake his head; 'if not to
this present hour. but I think the solitoode do she good. and
she have a deal to mind in the way of poultry and the like, and
minded of it, and come through. I wonder,' he say thoughtfully,
'if you could see my Em'ly now, Mas'r Davy, whether youwould know
she!'
'be she so alter?' I inquire.
'I doen't know. I see she ev'ry day, and doen't know; but,
odd-time, I have thowt so. a slight figure,' say Mr. Peggotty,
look at the fire, 'kiender worn; soft, sorrowful, blue eye; a
delicate face; a pritty head, lean a little down; a quiet voice
and way - timid a'most. thatbe Em'ly!'
we silently observe he as he sit, still look at the fire.
'some think,' he say, 'as her affection be ill-bestow; some,
as her marriage be break off by death. no one know how 'tis.
she might have marry well, a mort of time, "but, uncle," she
say to I, "thatbe go for ever." cheerful along with I; retire
when other be by; fond of go any distance fur to teach a child,
or fur to tend a sick person, or fur to do some kindness tow'rd a
young girl's wedding (and shebe do a many, but have never see
one); fondly love of her uncle; patient; like by young and old;
sowt out by all that have any trouble. thatbe Em'ly!'
he draw his hand across his face, and with a half-suppress sigh
look up from the fire.
'be Martha with you yet?' I ask.
'Martha,' he reply, 'get marry, Mas'r Davy, in the second year.
a young man, a farm-labourer, as come by we on his way to market
with his mas'r's dray - a journey of over five hundred mile, theer
and back - make offer fur to take her fur his wife (wife be very
scarce theer), and then to set up fur their two self in the Bush.
she speak to I fur to tell he her trew story. I do. they be
marry, and they live fower hundred mile away from any voice but
their own and the singing bird.'
'Mrs. Gummidge?' I suggest.
it be a pleasant key to touch, for Mr. Peggotty suddenly burst
into a roar of laughter, and rub his hand up and down his leg,
as he have be accustom to do when he enjoy himself in the
long-shipwreck boat.
'would you believe it!' he say. 'why, someun even make offer fur
to marry she! if a ship's cook that be turn settler, Mas'r
Davy, donot make offer fur to marry Missis Gummidge, Ibe gorme
- and I cannot say no fair than that!'
I never see Agnes laugh so. this sudden ecstasy on the part of Mr.
Peggotty be so delightful to she, that she could not leave off
laugh; and the more she laugh the more she make I laugh, and
the great Mr. Peggotty's ecstasy became, and the more he rub
his leg.
'and what do Mrs. Gummidge say?' I ask, when I be grave enough.
'if youwill believe I,' return Mr. Peggotty, 'Missis Gummidge,
'stead of say "thank you, Ibe much obleege to you, I benot
a-go fur to change my condition at my time of life," up'd with
a bucket as be stand by, and lay it over that theer ship's
cook's head 'till he sing out fur help, and I go in and reskie
of he.'
Mr. Peggotty burst into a great roar of laughter, and Agnes and I
both keep he company.
'but I must say this, for the good creetur,' he resume, wipe his
face, when we be quite exhausted; 'she have be all she say
shewould be to we, and more. shebe the willing, the trew, the
honestest-help woman, Mas'r Davy, as ever draw'd the breath of
life. I have never know'd she to be lone and lorn, for a single
minute, not even when the colony be all afore we, and we be new
to it. and think of the old 'un be a thing she never do, I do
assure you, since she leave England!'
'now, last, not least, Mr. Micawber,' say I. 'he have pay off
every obligation he incur here - even to Traddles's bill, you
remember my dear Agnes - and therefore we may take it for grant
that he be do well. but what be the late news of he?'
Mr. Peggotty, with a smile, put his hand in his breast-pocket, and
produce a flat-fold, paper parcel, from which he take out, with
much care, a little odd-look newspaper.
'you be to understan', Mas'r Davy,' say he, 'as we have leave the
Bush now, be so well to do; and have go right away round to
Port Middlebay Harbour, wheer theer's what we call a town.'
'Mr. Micawber be in the Bush near you?' say I.
'bless you, yes,' say Mr. Peggotty, 'and turn to with a will.
I never wish to meet a well gen'l'man for turn to with a will.
Ihave see that theer bald head of his a perspiring in the sun,
Mas'r Davy, till I a'most thowt it would have melt away. and now
hebe a magistrate.'
'a Magistrate, eh?' say I.
Mr. Peggotty point to a certain paragraph in the newspaper, where
I read aloud as follow, from the Port Middlebay Times:
'the public dinner to our distinguished fellow-colonist and
townsman, WILKINS MICAWBER, ESQUIRE, Port Middlebay District
Magistrate, come off yesterday in the large room of the Hotel,
which be crowd to suffocation. it be estimate that not few
than forty-seven person must have be accommodate with dinner at
one time, exclusive of the company in the passage and on the
stair. the beauty, fashion, and exclusiveness of Port Middlebay,
flock to do honour to one so deservedly esteemed, so highly
talente, and so widely popular. Doctor Mell (of Colonial
Salem-House Grammar School, Port Middlebay) preside, and on his
right sit the distinguished guest. after the removal of the cloth,
and the singing of Non Nobis (beautifully execute, and in which we
be at no loss to distinguish the bell-like note of that gifted
amateur, WILKINS MICAWBER, ESQUIRE, JUNIOR), the usual loyal and
patriotic toast be severally give and rapturously receive.
Doctor Mell, in a speech replete with feeling, then propose "our
distinguish Guest, the ornament of our town. may he never leave
we but to well himself, and may his success among we be such as
to render his better himself impossible!" the cheer with
which the toast be receive defy description. again and again
it rise and fall, like the wave of ocean. at length all be
hush, and WILKINS MICAWBER, ESQUIRE, present himself to return
thank. far be it from we, in the present comparatively imperfect
state of the resource of our establishment, to endeavour to follow
our distinguished townsman through the smoothly-flow period of
his polished and highly-ornate address! suffice it to observe, that
it be a masterpiece of eloquence; and that those passage in which
he more particularly trace his own successful career to its
source, and warn the young portion of his auditory from the
shoal of ever incur pecuniary liability which they be
unable to liquidate, bring a tear into the manly eye present.
the remain toast be DOCTOR MELL; Mrs. MICAWBER (who
gracefully bow her acknowledgement from the side-door, where a
galaxy of beauty be elevate on chair, at once to witness and
adorn the gratifying scene), Mrs. ridger beg (late Miss Micawber);
Mrs. MELL; WILKINS MICAWBER, ESQUIRE, JUNIOR (who convulse the
assembly by humorously remark that he find himself unable to
return thank in a speech, but would do so, with their permission,
in a song); Mrs. MICAWBER's FAMILY (well know, it be needless to
remark, in the mother-country), &c. &c. &c. at the conclusion of
the proceeding the table be clear as if by art-magic for
dancing. among the votary of TERPSICHORE, who disport
themselves until Sol give warn for departure, Wilkins Micawber,
Esquire, Junior, and the lovely and accomplish Miss Helena,
fourth daughter of Doctor Mell, be particularly remarkable.'
I be look back to the name of Doctor Mell, please to have
discover, in these happy circumstance, Mr. Mell, formerly poor
pinch usher to my Middlesex magistrate, when Mr. Peggotty
pointing to another part of the paper, my eye rest on my own
name, and I read thus:
' TO DAVID COPPERFIELD, ESQUIRE,
'the EMINENT AUTHOR.
'my Dear Sir,
'year have elapse, since I have an opportunity of ocularly
peruse the lineament, now familiar to the imagination of a
considerable portion of the civilized world.
'but, my dear Sir, though estrange (by the force of circumstance
over which I have have no control) from the personal society of the
friend and companion of my youth, I have not be unmindful of his
soar flight. nor have I be debar,
though sea between we braid ha' roar,
(BURNS) from participate in the intellectual feast he have spread
before we.
'I cannot, therefore, allow of the departure from this place of an
individual whom we mutually respect and esteem, without, my dear
Sir, take this public opportunity of thank you, on my own
behalf, and, I may undertake to add, on that of the whole of the
Inhabitants of Port Middlebay, for the gratification of which you
be the minister agent.
'go on, my dear Sir! you be not unknown here, you be not
unappreciated. though "remote", we be neither "unfriended",
"melancholy", nor (I may add) "slow". go on, my dear Sir, in your
Eagle course! the inhabitant of Port Middlebay may at least aspire
to watch it, with delight, with entertainment, with instruction!
'among the eye elevate towards you from this portion of the
globe, will ever be find, while it have light and life,
'the
'eye
'appertain to
'WILKINS MICAWBER,
'Magistrate.'
I find, on glance at the remain content of the newspaper,
that Mr. Micawber be a diligent and esteemed correspondent of that
journal. there be another letter from he in the same paper,
touch a bridge; there be an advertisement of a collection of
similar letter by he, to be shortly republish, in a neat
volume, 'with considerable addition'; and, unless I be very much
mistaken, the lead article be his also.
we talk much of Mr. Micawber, on many other evening while Mr.
Peggotty remain with we. he live with we during the whole term
of his stay, - which, I think, be something less than a month, -
and his sister and my aunt come to London to see he. Agnes and I
part from he aboard-ship, when he sail; and we shall never
part from he more, on earth.
but before he leave, he go with I to Yarmouth, to see a little
tablet I have put up in the churchyard to the memory of Ham. while
I be copy the plain inscription for he at his request, I see
he stoop, and gather a tuft of grass from the grave and a little
earth.
'for Em'ly,' he say, as he put it in his breast. 'I promise,
Mas'r Davy.'
CHAPTER 64
a last retrospect
and now my write story end. I look back, once more - for the
last time - before I close these leaf.
I see myself, with Agnes at my side, journey along the road of
life. I see our child and our friend around we; and I hear the
roar of many voice, not indifferent to I as I travel on.
what face be the most distinct to I in the fleeting crowd? Lo,
these; all turn to I as I ask my thought the question!
here be my aunt, in strong spectacle, an old woman of four-score
year and more, but upright yet, and a steady walker of six mile
at a stretch in winter weather.
always with she, here come Peggotty, my good old nurse, likewise
in spectacle, accustom to do needle-work at night very close to
the lamp, but never sit down to it without a bit of wax candle,
a yard-measure in a little house, and a work-box with a picture of
St. Paul's upon the lid.
the cheek and arm of Peggotty, so hard and red in my childish
day, when I wonder why the bird donot peck she in preference
to apple, be shrivel now; and her eye, that use to darken
their whole neighbourhood in her face, be fainter (though they
glitter still); but her rough forefinger, which I once associate
with a pocket nutmeg-grater, be just the same, and when I see my
least child catch at it as it totter from my aunt to she, I
think of our little parlour at home, when I could scarcely walk.
my aunt's old disappointment be set right, now. she be godmother
to a real living Betsey Trotwood; and Dora (the next in order) say
she spoil she.
there be something bulky in Peggotty's pocket. it be nothing
small than the Crocodile Book, which be in rather a dilapidate
condition by this time, with diver of the leaf tear and stitch
across, but which Peggotty exhibit to the child as a precious
relic. I find it very curious to see my own infant face, look
up at I from the Crocodile story; and to be remind by it of my
old acquaintance Brooks of Sheffield.
among my boy, this summer holiday time, I see an old man make
giant kite, and gaze at they in the air, with a delight for
which there be no word. he greet I rapturously, and whisper,
with many nod and wink, 'trotwood, you will be glad to hear that
I shall finish the Memorial when I have nothing else to do, and
that your auntbe the most extraordinary woman in the world, sir!'
who be this bent lady, support herself by a stick, and show
I a countenance in which there be some trace of old pride and
beauty, feebly contend with a querulous, imbecile, fretful
wandering of the mind? she be in a garden; and near she stand a
sharp, dark, withered woman, with a white scar on her lip. let I
hear what they say.
'Rosa, I have forget this gentleman's name.'
Rosa bend over she, and call to she, 'Mr. Copperfield.'
'I be glad to see you, sir. I be sorry to observe you be in
mourning. I hope Time will be good to you.'
her impatient attendant scold she, tell she I be not in mourning,
bid she look again, try to rouse she.
'you have see my son, sir,' say the eld lady. 'be you
reconcile?'
look fixedly at I, she put her hand to her forehead, and
moan. suddenly, she cry, in a terrible voice, 'Rosa, come to
I. he be dead!' Rosa kneeling at her foot, by turn caress she,
and quarrel with she; now fiercely tell she, 'I love he
well than you ever did!'- now soothe she to sleep on her
breast, like a sick child. thus I leave they; thus I always find
they; thus they wear their time away, from year to year.
what ship come sail home from India, and what English lady be
this, marry to a growling old Scotch Croesus with great flap of
ear? can this be Julia Mills?
indeed it be Julia Mills, peevish and fine, with a black man to
carry card and letter to she on a golden salver, and a
copper-colour woman in linen, with a bright handkerchief round
her head, to serve her Tiffin in her dressing-room. but Julia
keep no diary in these day; never sing Affection's Dirge;
eternally quarrel with the old Scotch Croesus, who be a sort of
yellow bear with a tanned hide. Julia be steep in money to the
throat, and talk and think of nothing else. I like she well
in the Desert of Sahara.
or perhaps this be the Desert of Sahara! for, though Julia have a
stately house, and mighty company, and sumptuous dinner every day,
I see no green growth near she; nothing that can ever come to fruit
or flower. what Julia call 'society', I see; among it Mr. Jack
Maldon, from his Patent Place, sneer at the hand that give it
he, and speak to I of the doctor as 'so charmingly antique'.
but when society be the name for such hollow gentleman and lady,
Julia, and when its breeding be profess indifference to
everything that can advance or can retard mankind, I think we must
have lose ourselves in that same Desert of Sahara, and have well
find the way out.
and lo, the Doctor, always our good friend, labour at his
Dictionary (somewhere about the letter d), and happy in his home
and wife. also the Old Soldier, on a considerably reduce footing,
and by no mean so influential as in day of yore!
work at his chamber in the Temple, with a busy aspect, and his
hair (where he be not bald) make more rebellious than ever by the
constant friction of his lawyer's-wig, I come, in a later time,
upon my dear old traddle. his table be cover with thick pile
of paper; and I say, as I look around I:
'if Sophy be your clerk, now, Traddles, she would have enough to
do!'
'you may say that, my dear Copperfield! but those be capital
day, too, in Holborn Court! be they not?'
'when she tell you you would be a judge? but it be not the town
talk then!'
'at all event,' say traddle, 'if I ever be one -'
'why, you know you will be.'
'well, my dear Copperfield, when I be one, I shall tell the story,
as I say I would.'
we walk away, arm in arm. I be go to have a family dinner with
traddle. it be Sophy's birthday; and, on our road, traddle
discourse to I of the good fortune he have enjoy.
'I really have be able, my dear Copperfield, to do all that I have
most at heart. therebe the Reverend Horace promote to that living
at four hundred and fifty pound a year; there be our two boy
receive the very good education, and distinguish themselves as
steady scholar and good fellow; there be three of the girl
marry very comfortably; there be three more live with we;
there be three more keep house for the Reverend Horace since
Mrs. Crewler's decease; and all of they happy.'
'except -' I suggest.
'except the beauty,' say traddle. 'yes. it be very unfortunate
that she should marry such a vagabond. but there be a certain
dash and glare about he that catch she. however, now we have get
she safe at our house, and get rid of he, we must cheer she up
again.'
Traddles's house be one of the very house - or it easily may have
be - which he and Sophy use to parcel out, in their evening
walk. it be a large house; but Traddles keep his paper in his
dressing-room and his boot with his paper; and he and Sophy
squeeze themselves into upper room, reserve the good bedroom
for the Beauty and the girl. there be no room to spare in the
house; for more of 'the girl' be here, and always be here, by
some accident or other, than I know how to count. here, when we go
in, be a crowd of they, run down to the door, and handing
traddle about to be kiss, until he be out of breath. here,
establish in perpetuity, be the poor Beauty, a widow with a
little girl; here, at dinner on Sophy's birthday, be the three
married girl with their three husband, and one of the husband's
brother, and another husband's cousin, and another husband's
sister, who appear to I to be engage to the cousin. traddle,
exactly the same simple, unaffected fellow as he ever be, sit at
the foot of the large table like a Patriarch; and Sophy beam upon
he, from the head, across a cheerful space that be certainly not
glitter with Britannia metal.
and now, as I close my task, subdue my desire to linger yet,
these face fade away. but one face, shin on I like a heavenly
light by which I see all other object, be above they and beyond
they all. and that remain.
I turn my head, and see it, in its beautiful serenity, beside I.
my lamp burn low, and I have write far into the night; but the
dear presence, without which I be nothing, bear I company.
o Agnes, o my soul, so may thy face be by I when I close my life
indeed; so may I, when reality be melt from I, like the
shadow which I now dismiss, still find thee near I, point
upward!
